$$f(t) \;=\; -\frac{dR(t)}{dt}$$

Substituting and applying integration by parts with u = t, dv = −dR, we obtain the canonical result:

$$\text{MTTF} \;=\; \int_{0}^{\infty} R(t)\, dt$$

**Step 4 — Specialize for the exponential distribution.**
For an exponential reliability function R(t) = e^(−λt), the integral evaluates to:

$$\text{MTTF} \;=\; \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-\lambda t}\, dt \;=\; \left[-\frac{1}{\lambda}e^{-\lambda t}\right]_{0}^{\infty} \;=\; \frac{1}{\lambda}$$

This is the single most-tested formula in KTU Module 1.

**Step 5 — Recognize the failure-rate duality.**
For *any* system with constant failure rate λ (i.e., operating in its useful-life period, the flat region of the bathtub curve), **MTTF = 1/λ**. This is not coincidence — it is a defining property of the exponential distribution.

## 2.2 Distinguishing MTTF, MTBF, and MTTR

| Metric | Full Name | Applies To | Formula | KTU Tag |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **MTTF** | Mean Time To Failure | Non-repairable items | $E[T] = \int R(t)\, dt$ | Module 1 Core |
| **MTBF** | Mean Time Between Failures | Repairable items | MTTF + MTTR | Module 2/3 |
| **MTTR** | Mean Time To Repair | Repairable items | $\int_{0}^{\infty}[1-A(t)]\,dt$ form | Module 3 |
| **λ(t)** | Instantaneous Failure Rate | All systems | $f(t)/R(t)$ | Module 1 Core |
| **R(t)** | Reliability Function | All systems | $\Pr(T > t)$ | Module 1 Core |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet (Exhaustive)

> [!IMPORTANT] > **Memorize this entire table for the KTU End Semester Exam.**

| # | Distribution | f(t) | R(t) | MTTF | MTTF Units / Form |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Exponential** | $\lambda e^{-\lambda t}$ | $e^{-\lambda t}$ | $\frac{1}{\lambda}$ | Most tested |
| 2 | **Weibull (2-parameter)** | $\frac{\beta}{\eta}\left(\frac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta-1} e^{-(t/\eta)^\beta}$ | $e^{-(t/\eta)^\beta}$ | $\eta \cdot \Gamma\!\left(1+\frac{1}{\beta}\right)$ | $\eta$ = scale, $\beta$ = shape |
| 3 | **Rayleigh** | $\frac{t}{\sigma^2}e^{-t^2/(2\sigma^2)}$ | $e^{-t^2/(2\sigma^2)}$ | $\sigma\sqrt{\frac{\pi}{2}}$ | Special case of Weibull ($\beta = 2$) |
| 4 | **Normal (approx.)** | $\frac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}}e^{-(t-\mu)^2/(2\sigma^2)}$ | $1 - \Phi\!\left(\frac{t-\mu}{\sigma}\right)$ | $\mu$ | Useful-life approximations |
| 5 | **Lognormal** | $\frac{1}{t\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}}\exp\!\left[-\frac{(\ln t - \mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right]$ | $1 - \Phi\!\left(\frac{\ln t - \mu}{\sigma}\right)$ | $e^{\mu + \sigma^2/2}$ | Fatigue, semiconductor wear |
| 6 | **Gamma** | $\frac{\lambda^\alpha t^{\alpha-1} e^{-\lambda t}}{\Gamma(\alpha)}$ | $1 - \Gamma_{\text{inc}}(\alpha, \lambda t)$ | $\frac{\alpha}{\lambda}$ | Erlang-k systems |
| 7 | **Uniform [0, a]** | $\frac{1}{a}$ | $1 - \frac{t}{a}$ | $\frac{a}{2}$ | Rare, but appears in 2-mark MCQs |

> ⚠️ **Critical LaTeX Rule:** In the table above, vertical bars inside cell expressions (like $\vert x \vert$) were replaced with parentheses to avoid breaking the markdown table parser. In your own handwritten KTU answers, use the original notation.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Electronics Manufacturing**: MTTF of an IC chip at 25°C is published in datasheets; derating curves give MTTF at higher junction temperatures.
- **Automotive Industry**: Warranty cost models depend critically on the MTTF of airbags, brake pads, and ECU components.
- **Aerospace**: Mission-critical components (turbine blades, flight-control sensors) require MTTF exceeding $10^6$ hours before scheduled replacement.
- **Telecommunications**: Fiber-optic laser transmitters are characterized by MTTF to plan optical-link budgets and redundancy.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation #1 — MTTF from the Probability Density Function

We begin with the formal definition of the expected value of a non-negative continuous random variable **T** (the failure time).

**Starting Statement:**
MTTF is the first moment of f(t) about the origin:

$$\text{MTTF} \;\equiv\; E[T] \;=\; \int_{0}^{\infty} t \cdot f(t)\, dt \quad \text{...(1)}$$

**Step 1 — Express f(t) using the reliability function.**
By the fundamental relationship between pdf and reliability:

$$R(t) \;=\; \Pr(T > t) \;=\; \int_{t}^{\infty} f(u)\, du$$

Differentiating both sides with respect to t (using Leibniz rule):

$$\frac{dR(t)}{dt} \;=\; -f(t) \quad \Longrightarrow \quad f(t) \;=\; -\frac{dR(t)}{dt} \quad \text{...(2)}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute (2) into (1).**

$$\text{MTTF} \;=\; \int_{0}^{\infty} t \cdot \left[-\frac{dR(t)}{dt}\right]\, dt \;=\; -\int_{0}^{\infty} t \cdot dR(t)$$

**Step 3 — Apply integration by parts.**
Recall the standard identity: $\int u\, dv = uv - \int v\, du$.
Let $u = t$ and $dv = dR(t)$, so $du = dt$ and $v = R(t)$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{MTTF} &= -\Bigl[\, t \cdot R(t) \Bigr]_{0}^{\infty} \;+\; \int_{0}^{\infty} R(t)\, dt \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the boundary term $[t \cdot R(t)]_{0}^{\infty}$.**
- At $t = 0$: $t \cdot R(t) = 0 \cdot R(0) = 0 \cdot 1 = 0$.
- At $t \to \infty$: For *any physically meaningful* reliability function, R(t) decays faster than 1/t, so $\lim_{t\to\infty} t \cdot R(t) = 0$.

Therefore, the boundary term is identically zero.

**Step 5 — Final result.**

$$\boxed{\;\text{MTTF} \;=\; \int_{0}^{\infty} R(t)\, dt\;}$$

This derivation is worth **7 marks** in any KTU 14-mark derivation question.

## 3.2 Derivation #2 — MTTF for the Exponential Distribution

**Given:** $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$, with $\lambda > 0$ being the constant failure rate.

**Step 1 — Substitute into the canonical MTTF formula.**

$$\text{MTTF} \;=\; \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-\lambda t}\, dt$$

**Step 2 — Apply the standard exponential integral.**
For $\lambda > 0$, the integral is elementary:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{MTTF} &= \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-\lambda t}\, dt \\
&= \left[ \frac{e^{-\lambda t}}{-\lambda} \right]_{0}^{\infty} \\
&= \frac{e^{-\lambda \cdot 0}}{-\lambda} \;-\; \lim_{t \to \infty} \frac{e^{-\lambda t}}{\lambda} \\
&= \frac{1}{-\lambda}(0 - 1) \\
&= \frac{1}{\lambda}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Conclude with the dual interpretation.**

$$\boxed{\;\text{MTTF}_{\text{exp}} \;=\; \frac{1}{\lambda}\;}$$

> [!NOTE] > **Dual Meaning:** In the exponential distribution, MTTF and MTBF are *numerically equal* because the memoryless property means repair resets the clock. Hence $\text{MTTF} = \text{MTBF} = 1/\lambda$.

## 3.3 Derivation #3 — MTTF for the Weibull Distribution

**Given:** $R(t) = \exp\!\left[-\left(\dfrac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta}\right]$, with $\eta > 0$ (scale) and $\beta > 0$ (shape).

**Step 1 — Substitute into the canonical MTTF formula.**

$$\text{MTTF} \;=\; \int_{0}^{\infty} \exp\!\left[-\left(\frac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta}\right]\, dt$$

**Step 2 — Apply the substitution $u = (t/\eta)^{\beta}$.**
Then $t = \eta \cdot u^{1/\beta}$ and $dt = \eta \cdot \frac{1}{\beta}\, u^{(1/\beta) - 1}\, du$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{MTTF} &= \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-u} \cdot \eta \cdot \frac{1}{\beta}\, u^{(1/\beta) - 1}\, du \\
&= \frac{\eta}{\beta} \int_{0}^{\infty} u^{(1/\beta) - 1} e^{-u}\, du
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Recognize the Gamma function integral.**
The integral $\int_{0}^{\infty} u^{a-1} e^{-u}\, du = \Gamma(a)$ for $\text{Re}(a) > 0$.

Here, $a = 1/\beta$, so:

$$\text{MTTF} \;=\; \frac{\eta}{\beta}\, \Gamma\!\left(\frac{1}{\beta}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Apply the Gamma function identity $\Gamma(x+1) = x\,\Gamma(x)$.**

$$
\boxed{\;\text{MTTF}_{\text{Weibull}} \;=\; \eta\, \Gamma\!\left(1 + \frac{1}{\beta}\right)\;}
$$

**Special Cases to Memorize:**
- $\beta = 1$ (exponential): $\text{MTTF} = \eta \cdot \Gamma(2) = \eta \cdot 1! = \eta$. And since $\lambda = 1/\eta$, this recovers $\text{MTTF} = 1/\lambda$. ✓
- $\beta = 2$ (Rayleigh): $\text{MTTF} = \eta \cdot \Gamma(1.5) = \eta \cdot \frac{\sqrt{\pi}}{2}$.
- $\beta = 3$ (approx. normal): $\text{MTTF} = \eta \cdot \Gamma(4/3) \approx 0.8930\, \eta$.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A non-repairable electronic component has a constant failure rate of λ = 0.0002 failures per hour. Compute (a) the reliability for a 1000-hour mission, and (b) the MTTF.

**Solution:**

(a) Reliability for 1000-hour mission:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R(1000) &= e^{-\lambda \cdot 1000} \\
&= e^{-0.0002 \times 1000} \\
&= e^{-0.2} \\
&= 0.8187 \quad \text{(approximately)}
\end{aligned}
$$

(b) Mean Time To Failure:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{MTTF} &= \frac{1}{\lambda} \\
&= \frac{1}{0.0002} \\
&= 5000 \text{ hours}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** Out of 1000 such components operated continuously, on average 500 will have failed by t = 5000 hours. Only ≈ 81.87% will survive a 1000-hour mission.

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — MTTF Across Distributions

| Property | Exponential | Weibull (β > 1) | Weibull (β < 1) | Lognormal |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **MTTF behavior** | $1/\lambda$ | $\eta\,\Gamma(1+1/\beta)$ | $\eta\,\Gamma(1+1/\beta)$ | $e^{\mu + \sigma^2/2}$ |
| **Failure rate** | Constant | Increasing | Decreasing | Initially inc., then dec. |
| **Physical meaning** | Random failures | Wear-out (fatigue) | Infant mortality | Crack propagation |
| **KTU difficulty** | ⭐ Easy | ⭐⭐ Moderate | ⭐⭐ Moderate | ⭐⭐⭐ Hard |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 MTTF Derivation Logic Flow (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Start: Define Random Variable T = Failure Time"] --> B["T is non-negative continuous"]
    B --> C["Compute E of T using pdf f of t"]
    C --> D["Apply relationship f equals negative dR by dt"]
    D --> E["Substitute into MTTF integral"]
    E --> F["Use integration by parts: u equals t, dv equals dR"]
    F --> G["Boundary term vanishes at zero and infinity"]
    G --> H["Result: MTTF equals integral of R of t from zero to infinity"]
    H --> I{"Choose Distribution"}
    I -- Exponential R of t equals e power minus lambda t --> J["MTTF equals 1 over lambda"]
    I -- Weibull R of t equals e power minus t over eta to the beta --> K["MTTF equals eta times Gamma of 1 plus 1 over beta"]
    I -- Rayleigh special case beta equals 2 --> L["MTTF equals sigma times square root of pi over 2"]
    J --> M["End: Numerical Evaluation"]
    K --> M
    L --> M
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — MTTF Computation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT["INPUT LAYER"]
        A1["Field Failure Data"] --> A2["Histogram of Failure Times"]
        A2 --> A3["Empirical pdf f of t"]
    end

    subgraph PROCESSING["PROCESSING LAYER"]
        B1["Reliability Function R of t"] --> B2["Integration Engine"]
        B3["Failure Rate Lambda of t"] --> B2
        A3 --> B2
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT["OUTPUT LAYER"]
        B2 --> C1["MTTF in hours"]
        B2 --> C2["Standard Deviation Sigma"]
        B2 --> C3["Confidence Intervals"]
    end

    subgraph APPLICATION["APPLICATION LAYER"]
        C1 --> D1["Warranty Cost Models"]
        C1 --> D2["Spare Parts Inventory"]
        C1 --> D3["Predictive Maintenance Schedule"]
        C1 --> D4["System Availability A equals MTBF over MTBF plus MDT"]
    end

    style INPUT fill:#e6f3ff,stroke:#0066cc
    style PROCESSING fill:#fff4e6,stroke:#cc6600
    style OUTPUT fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#2e7d32
    style APPLICATION fill:#fce4ec,stroke:#c2185b
```

## 4.3 Decision Tree — Which MTTF Formula to Use?

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START["Given a Reliability Problem"] --> Q1{"Is the item REPAIRABLE?"}
    Q1 -- NO --> Q2{"Is failure rate CONSTANT?"}
    Q1 -- YES --> STOP["Use MTBF equals MTTF plus MTTR — out of scope for Module 1"]
    Q2 -- YES --> Q3["Use MTTF equals 1 over lambda — Exponential"]
    Q2 -- NO --> Q4{"What is the shape parameter beta?"}
    Q4 -- beta less than 1 --> Q5["Infant mortality — use Weibull with beta less than 1"]
    Q4 -- beta greater than 1 --> Q6["Wear-out — use Weibull with beta greater than 1"]
    Q4 -- beta equals 2 --> Q7["Rayleigh — MTTF equals sigma times square root of pi over 2"]
    Q4 -- unknown beta --> Q8["Fit Weibull via Maximum Likelihood — advanced"]
    Q5 --> DONE["Compute MTTF using eta Gamma 1 plus 1 over beta"]
    Q6 --> DONE
    Q7 --> DONE
    Q8 --> DONE
    Q3 --> DONE
    DONE --> FINAL["Report MTTF in hours with units and confidence bounds"]
```

> [!NOTE] > **Diagram-to-Concept Mapping:**
> The first diagram maps the **theoretical derivation path** from random variable definition → integration by parts → final formula. The second diagram shows the **engineering data pipeline** from raw field data to actionable maintenance schedules. The third diagram is your **exam-decision aid** for picking the right formula.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. Define Mean Time To Failure (MTTF). Mention its unit.** *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question Paper, Module 1]*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
MTTF is the *expected (mean) time* until the first failure of a **non-repairable** item, computed as the first moment of the failure-time pdf:

$$\text{MTTF} \;=\; \int_{0}^{\infty} t \cdot f(t)\, dt \;=\; \int_{0}^{\infty} R(t)\, dt$$

For an exponential distribution, MTTF simplifies to $1/\lambda$. The unit is **hours** (or cycles/km/years depending on application).

**[Mark Allocation:** *Definition — 1.5 marks, Formula — 1 mark, Unit — 0.5 mark***]**

---

### **Q2. State the relationship between MTTF and failure rate for an exponentially distributed failure time.** *[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Retest Paper]*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
For an exponential distribution with constant failure rate λ, the reliability is $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$. Substituting into the MTTF integral:

$$\text{MTTF} \;=\; \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-\lambda t}\, dt \;=\; \frac{1}{\lambda}$$

Thus, **MTTF is the reciprocal of the constant failure rate**, i.e., $\text{MTTF} = 1/\lambda$. This duality holds *only* for the exponential case; for Weibull or other distributions, MTTF depends on shape and scale parameters jointly.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU 2024 Internal Choice Rule:** *Answer ANY ONE full question from the two alternatives. Each question has sub-parts (a) and (b) for 7 marks each.*

---

### **Question A — 14 Marks** *[KTU University Exam — May 2024, ESE Paper, Module 1, Q1(b)]*

**(a)** Derive an expression for the Mean Time To Failure (MTTF) of a non-reparable component in terms of its reliability function R(t). **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a non-repairable system whose time-to-failure follows the Weibull distribution with scale parameter η = 5000 hours and shape parameter β = 2.5, compute the MTTF. Given $\Gamma(1.4) = 0.8873$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Q-A(a) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1 — State the definition using pdf.** [1 mark]

$$\text{MTTF} \;=\; E[T] \;=\; \int_{0}^{\infty} t \cdot f(t)\, dt$$

**Step 2 — Use the relation $f(t) = -dR/dt$.** [1 mark]

Substituting:

$$\text{MTTF} \;=\; -\int_{0}^{\infty} t \cdot \frac{dR(t)}{dt}\, dt$$

**Step 3 — Apply integration by parts with u = t, dv = dR.** [3 marks]

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{MTTF} &= -\left[t \cdot R(t)\right]_{0}^{\infty} + \int_{0}^{\infty} R(t)\, dt
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Justify that the boundary term vanishes.** [1 mark]
- At $t=0$: $0 \cdot R(0) = 0$
- At $t \to \infty$: $t \cdot R(t) \to 0$ because $R(t)$ decays faster than $1/t$.

**Step 5 — State final result.** [1 mark]

$$\boxed{\;\text{MTTF} \;=\; \int_{0}^{\infty} R(t)\, dt\;}$$

---

#### **Model Solution to Q-A(b) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1 — Recall Weibull MTTF formula.** [1 mark]

$$\text{MTTF}_{\text{Weibull}} \;=\; \eta \cdot \Gamma\!\left(1 + \frac{1}{\beta}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values η = 5000, β = 2.5.** [2 marks]

$$
\begin{aligned}
1 + \frac{1}{\beta} &= 1 + \frac{1}{2.5} = 1 + 0.4 = 1.4 \\
\text{MTTF} &= 5000 \cdot \Gamma(1.4)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Insert the given Gamma value.** [1 mark]

$$\text{MTTF} \;=\; 5000 \times 0.8873$$

**Step 4 — Compute final numeric answer.** [2 marks]

$$\boxed{\;\text{MTTF} \;=\; 4436.5 \text{ hours}\;}$$

**Step 5 — Physical interpretation.** [1 mark]
On average, this component will operate for 4436.5 hours before failure. The shape parameter β = 2.5 > 1 indicates *wear-out* failure mode (increasing failure rate over time).

---

### **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)** *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Retest]*

**(a)** Show that for an exponentially distributed time-to-failure with constant failure rate λ, the MTTF is $1/\lambda$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A non-repairable pump used in a chemical plant has an exponential time-to-failure with MTTF = 8000 hours. Find: (i) the failure rate λ, (ii) the reliability for a 2000-hour mission, and (iii) the probability that it will fail between 2000 and 4000 hours of operation. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Q-B(a) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1 — State the exponential reliability function.** [1 mark]

$$R(t) \;=\; e^{-\lambda t}, \quad \lambda > 0$$

**Step 2 — Substitute into the canonical MTTF integral.** [1 mark]

$$\text{MTTF} \;=\; \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-\lambda t}\, dt$$

**Step 3 — Perform the integration.** [3 marks]

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{MTTF} &= \left[-\frac{1}{\lambda}\, e^{-\lambda t}\right]_{0}^{\infty} \\
&= -\frac{1}{\lambda}\,(0 - 1) \\
&= \frac{1}{\lambda}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — State the result and physical meaning.** [2 marks]

$$\boxed{\;\text{MTTF} \;=\; \frac{1}{\lambda}\;}$$

*Physical meaning:* When failure rate is constant, the average lifetime is exactly the reciprocal of the rate. Doubling the failure rate halves the MTTF.

---

#### **Model Solution to Q-B(b) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1 — Compute λ from MTTF.** [1 mark]

$$\lambda \;=\; \frac{1}{\text{MTTF}} \;=\; \frac{1}{8000} \;=\; 1.25 \times 10^{-4} \text{ per hour}$$

**Step 2 — Compute R(2000).** [2 marks]

$$
\begin{aligned}
R(2000) &= e^{-\lambda \cdot 2000} \\
&= e^{-1.25 \times 10^{-4} \times 2000} \\
&= e^{-0.25} \\
&\approx 0.7788
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute P(failure between 2000 and 4000 hours).** [3 marks]

This probability is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(2000 < T \leq 4000) &= R(2000) - R(4000) \\
R(4000) &= e^{-1.25 \times 10^{-4} \times 4000} = e^{-0.5} \approx 0.6065 \\
P(2000 < T \leq 4000) &= 0.7788 - 0.6065 \\
&\approx 0.1723
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Final answers.** [1 mark]

$$
\boxed{\;\lambda = 1.25 \times 10^{-4}/\text{hr}, \quad R(2000) \approx 0.7788, \quad P(2000 < T \leq 4000) \approx 0.1723\;}
$$

---

> [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting units** in the final answer (deduct 0.5 mark per missing unit).
> 2. **Confusing MTTF with MTBF** — if the question says *non-repairable*, you MUST use MTTF, not MTBF (deduct 2 marks).
> 3. **Forgetting the boundary-term justification** in the integration-by-parts derivation — examiners specifically test this (deduct 1.5 marks).
> 4. **Wrong Gamma function argument** — students often write $\Gamma(1/\beta)$ instead of $\Gamma(1 + 1/\beta)$ — this is a classic 1-mark trap.
> 5. **Not interpreting the result** — physical interpretation of MTTF (wear-out vs infant mortality) carries 1 mark and is often skipped.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

- ✅ **MTTF = Expected time to first failure for a NON-repairable item.**
- ✅ **Two equivalent formulas:** $\text{MTTF} = \int_{0}^{\infty} t \cdot f(t)\, dt = \int_{0}^{\infty} R(t)\, dt$
- ✅ **Exponential:** $\text{MTTF} = 1/\lambda$ — the single most-tested identity.
- ✅ **Weibull:** $\text{MTTF} = \eta \cdot \Gamma(1 + 1/\beta)$
- ✅ **Rayleigh (β=2):** $\text{MTTF} = \sigma\sqrt{\pi/2}$
- ✅ **Lognormal:** $\text{MTTF} = e^{\mu + \sigma^2/2}$
- ✅ **Units:** hours, cycles, km, or operations — *always state explicitly.*
- ✅ **MTBF ≠ MTTF** for repairable items; use MTBF only when repair is possible.
- ✅ **Constant failure rate** is a *defining* property of the exponential distribution — not a coincidence.
- ✅ **Derivation by parts** step needs the *boundary justification* at both 0 and ∞.
- ✅ **Always interpret** the MTTF value physically (wear-out? infant mortality? random?).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hazard Rate Function
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET632 - RELIABILITY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Hazard Rate Function

# Hazard Rate Function – Foundational Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Hazard Rate Function** (also called the **Failure Rate Function** or **Instantaneous Failure Rate**) is the most fundamental conditional probability density used in reliability engineering. It quantifies the *instantaneous rate of failure* of a component, system, or item at time $t$, **given that it has survived up to time $t$ without failure**.

Mathematically, the hazard rate function $h(t)$ is defined as the ratio of the probability density function (PDF) of failure to the reliability function (survival function):

$$h(t) = \frac{f(t)}{R(t)} = \frac{f(t)}{1 - F(t)}$$

where the conditional probability interpretation is preserved by the survival function $R(t)$ in the denominator.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The hazard rate is **NOT** a probability. It is a **rate** (failures per unit time), and its unit is the reciprocal of the time unit (e.g., $\text{failures/hour}$, $\text{failures/cycle}$).

The **Cumulative Hazard Function** $H(t)$ is obtained by integrating the hazard rate over the interval $[0, t]$:

$$H(t) = \int_{0}^{t} h(u)\,du$$

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy – Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are 25 years old and have never had a serious cardiac event. The doctor wants to know your **instantaneous risk of having a heart attack right now, in the next instant**, knowing you have been heart-attack-free until this moment. That conditional risk is precisely what $h(t)$ measures.

**Another everyday analogy:** A car tire has been running safely for 30,000 km. The hazard rate at 30,000 km tells you: *"How likely is this tire to fail in the very next kilometre, given it has survived 30,000 km already?"* It is **NOT** "How many kilometres has the tire survived?" — that would be cumulative history. It is the **conditional forward-looking risk** at exactly that instant.

> [!NOTE]
> **Reliability Interpretation:** A high hazard rate means the component is "wearing out" rapidly. A low (or decreasing) hazard rate often indicates infant mortality has passed (e.g., burn-in phase) or the component is in its useful life phase.

## 1.3 Physical Significance in Engineering Systems

- **Electronics:** Tracks degradation of capacitors, solder joints, semiconductor chips.
- **Mechanical Systems:** Monitors bearing wear, fatigue crack propagation in turbine blades.
- **Civil Engineering:** Evaluates structural fatigue in bridges, pipelines under cyclic loading.
- **Power Systems:** Forecasts transformer insulation breakdown probability.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why the Hazard Rate Matters – The "Why" Behind the Math

Standard reliability functions $R(t)$ and $F(t)$ only tell you *whether* a failure has occurred by time $t$. They are **cumulative** in nature. The hazard rate answers a fundamentally different and more practical engineering question:

> *"What is the **instantaneous** probability of failure per unit time at age $t$?"*

This matters because engineers need to schedule **preventive maintenance**, plan **replacement intervals**, and predict **remaining useful life (RUL)** — all of which depend on *instantaneous* failure propensity, not historical cumulative behavior.

## 2.2 Theoretical Breakdown – Layered Logic

**Layer 1 — Conditional Probability Foundation:**
The probability that a component fails in the small interval $[t, t+\Delta t]$, given it has survived to time $t$, is:

$$P(t \leq T \leq t+\Delta t \mid T > t) = \frac{F(t+\Delta t) - F(t)}{R(t)}$$

**Layer 2 — Passage to the Limit (Definition of $h(t)$):**
Dividing by $\Delta t$ and taking the limit as $\Delta t \to 0$:

$$h(t) = \lim_{\Delta t \to 0} \frac{F(t+\Delta t) - F(t)}{\Delta t \cdot R(t)} = \frac{1}{R(t)} \cdot \frac{dF(t)}{dt} = \frac{f(t)}{R(t)}$$

**Layer 3 — Differential Equation for $R(t)$:**
Starting from $h(t) = -\dfrac{d}{dt}\ln R(t)$, integration yields the closed-form survival function:

$$R(t) = \exp\!\left(-\int_{0}^{t} h(u)\,du\right) = e^{-H(t)}$$

**Layer 4 — Conditional Reliability (Survival over an Interval):**
The probability that a component of age $t$ survives an additional duration $\Delta t$:

$$R(t+\Delta t \mid t) = \frac{R(t+\Delta t)}{R(t)} = \exp\!\left(-\int_{t}^{t+\Delta t} h(u)\,du\right)$$

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| Formula | Expression | Meaning / Use |
|---|---|---|
| Hazard Rate (Definition) | $h(t) = \dfrac{f(t)}{R(t)}$ | Instantaneous failure rate at age $t$ |
| Hazard Rate (Log-Form) | $h(t) = -\dfrac{d}{dt}\ln R(t)$ | Differential form for derivation |
| Cumulative Hazard | $H(t) = \displaystyle\int_{0}^{t} h(u)\,du$ | Total accumulated hazard up to $t$ |
| Reliability from $H(t)$ | $R(t) = e^{-H(t)}$ | Survival function in terms of hazard |
| PDF from Hazard | $f(t) = h(t)\,e^{-H(t)}$ | Failure density via hazard rate |
| Conditional Reliability | $R(t+\Delta t \mid t) = \dfrac{R(t+\Delta t)}{R(t)}$ | Survival over next interval |
| Mean Time To Failure | $\text{MTTF} = \displaystyle\int_{0}^{\infty} R(t)\,dt$ | Expected life of non-repairable item |
| MTTF via Hazard | $\text{MTTF} = \displaystyle\int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-H(t)}\,dt$ | Alternative MTTF form |
| Failure in $[t,t+\Delta t]$ | $\approx h(t)\,\Delta t$ | Approximation for small $\Delta t$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Unit Check:** Since $h(t)$ has units of $\text{time}^{-1}$, the product $h(t)\,\Delta t$ is dimensionless — correctly representing a probability for small intervals.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The hazard rate function forms the backbone of:

- **Bathtub Curve Analysis:** $h(t)$ decreases (infant mortality), stabilizes (useful life), increases (wear-out).
- **Preventive Maintenance Scheduling:** Replace components **before** $h(t)$ escalates in the wear-out phase.
- **Warranty & Spare-Part Forecasting:** Predicting failures per million hours (FPMH).
- **Aerospace & Nuclear Safety:** Mission reliability computations for critical one-shot systems.
- **Insurance & Actuarial Science:** Mortality modeling directly uses hazard-rate mathematics.

## 2.5 Key Properties of $h(t)$

1. **Non-negative:** $h(t) \geq 0$ for all $t \geq 0$ (a rate cannot be negative).
2. **No upper bound:** $h(t)$ can theoretically be infinite (e.g., deterministic failure at $t_0$).
3. **Dimension:** $\text{time}^{-1}$ (e.g., per hour, per cycle, per km).
4. **Not a probability:** Despite the conditional probability origin, $h(t)$ is a rate density.
5. **No probability conservation:** $\int_{0}^{\infty} h(t)\,dt$ is **not** constrained to equal 1; it equals $\infty$ for any non-degenerate distribution.

## 2.6 The Bathtub Curve – Qualitative Hazard Behavior

| Phase | Time Interval | Hazard Behavior | Physical Cause |
|---|---|---|---|
| Infant Mortality (Burn-in) | $0 \le t < t_1$ | $h(t)$ is **decreasing** | Manufacturing defects, weak components failing early |
| Useful Life (Random Failures) | $t_1 \le t < t_2$ | $h(t) \approx \text{constant}$ | Random external shocks, environmental stresses |
| Wear-Out Phase | $t \ge t_2$ | $h(t)$ is **increasing** | Material degradation, fatigue, corrosion |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — From Conditional Probability to $h(t) = f(t)/R(t)$

**Step 1:** Write the conditional probability that a component fails in the small interval $[t, t+\Delta t]$, given survival up to $t$:

$$P(t \leq T \leq t+\Delta t \mid T > t) = \frac{P(t < T \leq t+\Delta t)}{P(T > t)} = \frac{F(t+\Delta t) - F(t)}{R(t)}$$

*Reasoning:* We divide the joint event "fails in $[t, t+\Delta t]$" by the conditioning event "survives to $t$". The numerator is the change in cumulative failure probability over the small interval.

**Step 2:** Convert this conditional probability into a **rate** by dividing by the interval length $\Delta t$:

$$\text{Average Hazard} = \frac{F(t+\Delta t) - F(t)}{\Delta t \cdot R(t)}$$

*Reasoning:* A rate is "events per unit time". Dividing a probability by $\Delta t$ yields a density.

**Step 3:** Take the limit as $\Delta t \to 0$ to obtain the **instantaneous** hazard rate:

$$h(t) = \lim_{\Delta t \to 0} \frac{F(t+\Delta t) - F(t)}{\Delta t \cdot R(t)}$$

**Step 4:** Recognize the limit in the numerator as the derivative of $F(t)$, which is by definition the PDF $f(t)$:

$$h(t) = \frac{1}{R(t)} \cdot \lim_{\Delta t \to 0} \frac{F(t+\Delta t) - F(t)}{\Delta t} = \frac{f(t)}{R(t)}$$

*Result:* $\boxed{h(t) = \dfrac{f(t)}{R(t)}}$ — the foundational definition.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Logarithmic Form $h(t) = -\dfrac{d}{dt}\ln R(t)$

**Step 1:** Substitute $f(t) = -\dfrac{dR(t)}{dt}$ (since $R(t) + F(t) = 1 \Rightarrow f(t) = F'(t) = -R'(t)$):

$$h(t) = \frac{f(t)}{R(t)} = \frac{-R'(t)}{R(t)} = -\frac{dR(t)/dt}{R(t)}$$

**Step 2:** Recognize that $\dfrac{d}{dt}\ln R(t) = \dfrac{R'(t)}{R(t)}$ by the chain rule applied to $\ln$:

$$\frac{d}{dt}\ln R(t) = \frac{1}{R(t)} \cdot \frac{dR(t)}{dt} = \frac{R'(t)}{R(t)}$$

**Step 3:** Combine:

$$h(t) = -\frac{d}{dt}\ln R(t)$$

*Result:* $\boxed{h(t) = -\dfrac{d}{dt}\ln R(t)}$ — useful for differential-equation style derivations.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Reliability in Terms of Cumulative Hazard $R(t) = e^{-H(t)}$

**Step 1:** From Derivation 2:

$$h(t) = -\frac{d}{dt}\ln R(t)$$

**Step 2:** Integrate both sides from $0$ to $t$:

$$\int_{0}^{t} h(u)\,du = -\int_{0}^{t} \frac{d}{du}\ln R(u)\,du$$

**Step 3:** Evaluate the right-hand side by the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus:

$$\int_{0}^{t} h(u)\,du = -\bigl[\ln R(t) - \ln R(0)\bigr]$$

**Step 4:** Apply the boundary condition $R(0) = 1$ (component is operational at $t = 0$), so $\ln R(0) = \ln 1 = 0$:

$$\int_{0}^{t} h(u)\,du = -\ln R(t)$$

**Step 5:** Define $H(t) = \int_{0}^{t} h(u)\,du$, then solve for $R(t)$:

$$H(t) = -\ln R(t) \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \ln R(t) = -H(t) \quad \Longrightarrow \quad R(t) = e^{-H(t)}$$

*Result:* $\boxed{R(t) = e^{-H(t)}}$ — the most compact reliability representation.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — PDF from Hazard Function $f(t) = h(t)\,e^{-H(t)}$

**Step 1:** Start from the definition:

$$f(t) = h(t) \cdot R(t)$$

**Step 2:** Substitute $R(t) = e^{-H(t)}$ from Derivation 3:

$$f(t) = h(t) \cdot e^{-H(t)}$$

*Result:* $\boxed{f(t) = h(t)\,e^{-H(t)}}$ — useful for density computations given any $h(t)$.

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Conditional Reliability over $[t, t+\Delta t]$

**Step 1:** The probability that a component aged $t$ survives an additional $\Delta t$:

$$R(\Delta t \mid t) = \frac{P(T > t + \Delta t)}{P(T > t)} = \frac{R(t+\Delta t)}{R(t)}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute the exponential form $R(s) = e^{-H(s)}$:

$$R(\Delta t \mid t) = \frac{e^{-H(t+\Delta t)}}{e^{-H(t)}} = e^{-[H(t+\Delta t) - H(t)]}$$

**Step 3:** Recognize that $H(t+\Delta t) - H(t) = \int_{t}^{t+\Delta t} h(u)\,du$:

$$R(\Delta t \mid t) = \exp\!\left(-\int_{t}^{t+\Delta t} h(u)\,du\right)$$

*Result:* $\boxed{R(\Delta t \mid t) = \exp\!\left(-\int_{t}^{t+\Delta t} h(u)\,du\right)}$ — mission reliability formula.

## 3.6 Worked Numerical Example — Exponential Distribution

Let $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ for $t \geq 0$, where $\lambda = 0.001$ failures/hour (a typical electronic component).

**Step 1:** Compute the hazard rate:

$$h(t) = -\frac{d}{dt}\ln R(t) = -\frac{d}{dt}\ln(e^{-\lambda t}) = -\frac{d}{dt}(-\lambda t) = \lambda = 0.001 \text{ hr}^{-1}$$

**Step 2:** Compute cumulative hazard at $t = 500$ hours:

$$H(500) = \int_{0}^{500} 0.001\,du = 0.001 \times 500 = 0.5$$

**Step 3:** Verify reliability:

$$R(500) = e^{-0.5} \approx 0.6065$$

**Step 4:** Compute MTTF:

$$\text{MTTF} = \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-\lambda t}\,dt = \frac{1}{\lambda} = \frac{1}{0.001} = 1000 \text{ hours}$$

**Step 5:** Probability of failure in the next 100 hours, given survival to $t = 500$:

$$\text{Prob} \approx h(500) \cdot \Delta t = 0.001 \times 100 = 0.10 \;\; (10\%)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Insight:** For an exponential distribution, $h(t)$ is **constant** — memoryless property. The component "does not age" in any statistical sense.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Relationship Block Diagram

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the functional dependencies between all the key reliability functions, with the hazard rate function as the central hub:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Time Variable t"] --> B["Cumulative Distribution F of t"]
    B --> C["Probability Density f of t"]
    A --> D["Reliability Function R of t"]
    D --> C
    C --> E["Hazard Rate h of t = f divided by R"]
    D --> E
    E --> F["Cumulative Hazard H of t = integral of h"]
    F --> G["Exponential Form R of t = exp of minus H of t"]
    G --> D
    E --> H["Conditional Survival R delta t given t"]
    F --> H
    D --> H
    E --> I["MTTF = integral of R of t dt"]
    D --> I
    F --> I
```

## 4.2 Bathtub Curve Hazard Behavior — Block Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P1["Phase 1: Infant Mortality 0 to t1"] -->|h decreasing| P2["Phase 2: Useful Life t1 to t2"]
    P2 -->|h approximately constant| P3["Phase 3: Wear Out t2 to infinity"]
    P3 -->|h increasing rapidly| P4["System End of Life"]
    P1 --> Q1["Cause: Manufacturing defects, weak parts"]
    P2 --> Q2["Cause: Random shocks, environmental loads"]
    P3 --> Q3["Cause: Fatigue, corrosion, material aging"]
    P4 --> Q4["Action: Preventive replacement scheduled here"]
```

## 4.3 Hazard Rate Categorization Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S["Start: Choose Hazard Model"] --> Q1{"Is h constant?"}
    Q1 -->|Yes| EX["Exponential Distribution - Memoryless"]
    Q1 -->|No| Q2{"Is h linearly increasing?"}
    Q2 -->|Yes| WE["Weibull Distribution - Shape parameter beta greater than 1"]
    Q2 -->|No| Q3{"Is h decreasing?"}
    Q3 -->|Yes| GM["Gamma or Weibull beta less than 1"]
    Q3 -->|No| NL["Non-standard model - Empirical fit required"]
    EX --> A1["R of t = exp of minus lambda t"]
    EX --> A2["MTTF = 1 divided by lambda"]
    WE --> B1["R of t = exp of minus t divided by eta to power beta"]
    WE --> B2["MTTF = eta times Gamma 1 plus 1 over beta"]
    GM --> C1["R of t computed via incomplete gamma function"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Tip:** Each rectangular node represents a reliability quantity, and each arrow represents a deterministic mathematical mapping. For KTU board exams, students are often asked to draw the **functional dependency** of these quantities — understanding this block diagram is essential.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Style]**  
Define the **Hazard Rate Function**. Explain in one sentence why it is **not** a probability.

**Model Answer:**

The hazard rate function $h(t)$ is defined as the instantaneous failure rate of a component at time $t$, conditioned on its survival up to that time:

$$h(t) = \frac{f(t)}{R(t)}$$

It is **not** a probability because its units are $\text{failures per unit time}$ (i.e., $\text{time}^{-1}$), not dimensionless. A probability must lie in $[0,1]$; a rate can exceed 1 and has no upper bound.

---

### Question A2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]**  
State the relationship between the **Reliability Function** $R(t)$ and the **Cumulative Hazard Function** $H(t)$.

**Model Answer:**

$$R(t) = e^{-H(t)} \quad \text{where} \quad H(t) = \int_{0}^{t} h(u)\,du$$

This follows from integrating $h(t) = -\dfrac{d}{dt}\ln R(t)$ and applying $R(0) = 1$.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) — [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Style]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the expression for the hazard rate function $h(t)$ starting from the definition of conditional probability. Show clearly the passage to the limit and explain each step.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A component has reliability function $R(t) = e^{-0.002\,t}$ for $t \geq 0$ (time in hours).  
(i) Compute the hazard rate $h(t)$.  
(ii) Find the probability of survival up to $t = 1000$ hours.  
(iii) Find the MTTF of the component.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **Bathtub Curve** in reliability engineering with a neat sketch. Discuss the three phases and the corresponding behavior of the hazard rate $h(t)$ in each phase. Give one engineering example for each phase.

**(b) [7 Marks]** For a system with hazard rate $h(t) = 0.0005 + 0.0001\,t$ (per hour), find:  
(i) The cumulative hazard $H(t)$.  
(ii) The reliability function $R(t)$.  
(iii) The probability that the system survives its first 2000 hours of operation.

---

## 5.3 Complete Model Solutions — Part B

### Solution to Question A (Choice 1)

#### Part (a) — Derivation of $h(t)$

**Step 1:** Conditional probability formulation:

$$P(t < T \leq t+\Delta t \mid T > t) = \frac{F(t+\Delta t) - F(t)}{R(t)}$$

**Step 2:** Convert to an average rate by dividing by $\Delta t$:

$$\text{Average rate} = \frac{F(t+\Delta t) - F(t)}{\Delta t \cdot R(t)}$$

**Step 3:** Take the limit $\Delta t \to 0$:

$$h(t) = \lim_{\Delta t \to 0} \frac{F(t+\Delta t) - F(t)}{\Delta t \cdot R(t)} = \frac{f(t)}{R(t)}$$

**Step 4:** Using $f(t) = -\dfrac{dR(t)}{dt}$:

$$h(t) = -\frac{1}{R(t)} \cdot \frac{dR(t)}{dt} = -\frac{d}{dt}\ln R(t)$$

**Conclusion:** $\boxed{h(t) = \dfrac{f(t)}{R(t)} = -\dfrac{d}{dt}\ln R(t)}$

#### Part (b) — Numerical Computations

Given: $R(t) = e^{-0.002 t}$, where $\lambda = 0.002$ /hr.

**(i) Hazard rate:**

$$h(t) = -\frac{d}{dt}\ln(e^{-0.002 t}) = -\frac{d}{dt}(-0.002 t) = 0.002 \;\text{hr}^{-1}$$

**(ii) Survival probability at $t = 1000$ hours:**

$$R(1000) = e^{-0.002 \times 1000} = e^{-2} \approx 0.1353$$

**(iii) Mean Time To Failure:**

$$\text{MTTF} = \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-0.002 t}\,dt = \frac{1}{0.002} = 500 \;\text{hours}$$

---

### Solution to Question B (Choice 2)

#### Part (a) — Bathtub Curve Explanation

| Phase | Time Range | $h(t)$ Behavior | Cause | Engineering Example |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Infant Mortality | $0 \le t < t_1$ | Decreasing | Defects, poor workmanship | Capacitor with weak dielectric failing early |
| Useful Life | $t_1 \le t < t_2$ | Approximately constant | Random shocks | Electronic component under normal operating stress |
| Wear-Out | $t \ge t_2$ | Increasing | Material degradation | Bearing fatigue in rotating machinery |

#### Part (b) — Hazard Integration

Given: $h(t) = 0.0005 + 0.0001\,t$ (per hour).

**(i) Cumulative Hazard:**

$$H(t) = \int_{0}^{t} (0.0005 + 0.0001\,u)\,du = 0.0005\,t + 0.0001 \cdot \frac{t^{2}}{2} = 0.0005\,t + 0.00005\,t^{2}$$

**(ii) Reliability Function:**

$$R(t) = e^{-H(t)} = \exp\!\left(-0.0005\,t - 0.00005\,t^{2}\right)$$

**(iii) Survival probability up to $t = 2000$ hours:**

$$H(2000) = 0.0005 \times 2000 + 0.00005 \times (2000)^{2}$$
$$= 1.0 + 0.00005 \times 4{,}000{,}000$$
$$= 1.0 + 200 = 201$$

$$R(2000) = e^{-201} \approx 0$$

**Interpretation:** With a linearly increasing hazard, the system is essentially guaranteed to fail before 2000 hours under this model — the hazard rate itself becomes very high ($h(2000) = 0.0005 + 0.0001 \times 2000 = 0.2005$/hr).

---

## 5.4 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in Hazard-Rate Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the boundary condition** $R(0) = 1$ when deriving $R(t) = e^{-H(t)}$. Students often lose **2 marks** here.
> 2. **Confusing units:** Writing $h(t)$ as a probability (dimensionless) instead of a rate ($\text{time}^{-1}$). Examiners deduct **1–2 marks** for unit errors.
> 3. **Skipping the limit passage** in derivations. The board expects the explicit step:
>    $$\lim_{\Delta t \to 0} \frac{F(t+\Delta t) - F(t)}{\Delta t} = f(t)$$
>    Omitting this costs **1 mark**.
> 4. **Incorrect sign in $h(t) = -\dfrac{d}{dt}\ln R(t)$:** A missing negative sign flips the inequality direction. This is the **#1 most common error** in KTU answer scripts.
> 5. **Forgetting to apply the chain rule** when differentiating $\ln R(t)$. Always write:
>    $$\frac{d}{dt}\ln R(t) = \frac{R'(t)}{R(t)}$$
> 6. **Treating $h(t)$ as a probability density** and attempting to normalize it. Recall: $\int_{0}^{\infty} h(t)\,dt \neq 1$ in general.
> 7. **For exponential model confusion:** Students often mis-state that $h(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$. The correct form is $h(t) = \lambda$ (constant), not exponential.

## 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Hazard Rate Function**

- **Definition:** $h(t) = \dfrac{f(t)}{R(t)}$ — instantaneous conditional failure rate.
- **Log-form:** $h(t) = -\dfrac{d}{dt}\ln R(t)$ — useful for derivations.
- **Cumulative Hazard:** $H(t) = \displaystyle\int_{0}^{t} h(u)\,du$.
- **Reliability in Hazard Form:** $R(t) = e^{-H(t)}$ — most compact form.
- **PDF in Hazard Form:** $f(t) = h(t)\,e^{-H(t)}$.
- **Conditional Reliability:** $R(t+\Delta t \mid t) = \exp\!\left(-\displaystyle\int_{t}^{t+\Delta t} h(u)\,du\right)$.
- **MTTF:** $\text{MTTF} = \displaystyle\int_{0}^{\infty} R(t)\,dt = \displaystyle\int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-H(t)}\,dt$.
- **Units:** $h(t)$ has units of $\text{time}^{-1}$, **not** dimensionless probability.
- **Properties:** $h(t) \geq 0$ always; $\int h(t)\,dt$ can be $\infty$ (not normalized).
- **Exponential Special Case:** $h(t) = \lambda$ (constant) — memoryless property.
- **Bathtub Curve:** $h(t)$ decreases → constant → increases over the three life phases.
- **Boundary Conditions:** $R(0) = 1$, $F(0) = 0$, $R(\infty) = 0$, $F(\infty) = 1$, $H(0) = 0$.
- **Maintenance Rule of Thumb:** Schedule preventive replacement **before** $h(t)$ escalates in the wear-out phase.
- **Frequent Exam Trap:** Forgetting the negative sign in $h(t) = -\dfrac{d}{dt}\ln R(t)$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bathtub Curve
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET632 - RELIABILITY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Bathtub Curve

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Bathtub Curve** is a classic reliability engineering graphical model that depicts the time-dependent **hazard rate** (instantaneous failure rate) of a population of non-repairable components, systems, or items, plotted as a function of operating time or cycles. The curve derives its name from its characteristic shape resembling the longitudinal cross-section of a traditional bathtub, exhibiting three distinct operational regimes that map the dominant failure mechanisms across the operational lifespan of a system.

In rigorous reliability terms, the Bathtub Curve plots the **conditional failure intensity** function:

$$h(t) = \frac{f(t)}{R(t)} = \frac{f(t)}{1 - F(t)}$$

where $f(t)$ is the **probability density function (pdf)** of time-to-failure, $F(t)$ is the **cumulative distribution function (cdf)** of failure, and $R(t) = 1 - F(t)$ is the **reliability function** (survival function).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Mandate:** The Bathtub Curve is the foundational qualitative model of Module 1 — *Introduction to System Reliability*. Students must be able to (a) identify the three regions, (b) explain the underlying failure physics of each, and (c) sketch and label the curve correctly in descriptive and numerical problems.

### 1.2 The Three Phases — Syllabus Definition

| Phase | Operational Name | Failure Rate Behaviour | Dominant Failure Physics |
|---|---|---|---|
| I | **Infant Mortality** (Burn-in / Debugging / Early-life) | Monotonically **decreasing** | Manufacturing defects, poor workmanship, latent material flaws, quality control escapes |
| II | **Useful Life** (Intrinsic / Normal Operating) | Approximately **constant** | Random, stochastic overstress events — no dominant wear mechanism |
| III | **Wear-out** (Aging / Senescence) | Monotonically **increasing** | Fatigue, corrosion, creep, wear, oxidation, dielectric breakdown, cumulative damage |

### 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **The Human Life Analogy (Pedagogically Powerful):**
> 
> Think of a brand-new car the day it rolls out of the factory:
> - **Infant Mortality (Phase I):** In the first few weeks, the car is *more* likely to fail — a loose wire, a faulty sensor, a defective bolt. These are **birth defects** of the manufacturing process. As the weak components fail and are replaced, the failure rate drops.
> - **Useful Life (Phase II):** Once the "lemons" are weeded out, the remaining fleet experiences a **flat, low, random failure rate**. Failures occur due to unpredictable events — a stray nail in a tyre, a freak electrical surge. Nothing structural. This is the long, stable "valley" of the bathtub.
> - **Wear-out (Phase III):** As the car ages past its design life, components begin to **fatigue, corrode, and degrade**. Timing belts snap, gaskets leak, bearings seize. The failure rate now **rises steeply**. The car is "worn out."
> 
> The bathtub is simply this lifecycle story, quantified.

A second analogy: **Light Bulbs (Incandescent)**. A defective batch fails in the first hours (Phase I), most bulbs burn for their rated 1000 hours at a low random rate (Phase II), and as the filament thins near 1000 hours, mass burnouts occur (Phase III).

### 1.4 Why the Bathtub Curve Matters in Engineering

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industrial Significance:**
> - **Design phase:** Engineers use Phase II's constant failure rate to size redundancy, compute MTBF, and certify Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF).
> - **Quality phase:** Phase I failures drive **burn-in testing** — factories intentionally operate components under stress to eliminate early failures *before* shipment.
> - **Maintenance phase:** Phase III failures drive **preventive replacement policies** (e.g., replace aircraft engine turbines at TBO — Time Between Overhauls).
> - **Procurement:** Buyers demand low Phase II failure rate (FIT — Failures In Time) data in vendor datasheets.

**Key Constants and Standard Metrics (Bolded for Board Emphasis):**
- **Failure rate unit:** failures per $10^9$ device-hours = **1 FIT (Failures In Time)**.
- **Mean Time To Failure (MTTF):** $E[T] = \int_{0}^{\infty} t \cdot f(t)\, dt$.
- **For Phase II constant rate $\lambda$:** $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$, **MTTF** $= \frac{1}{\lambda}$, and **MTBF** $= \frac{1}{\lambda}$.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Underlying Reliability Mathematical Framework

The fundamental reliability relationships that generate the bathtub curve are:

**Step 1 — Survival Function $R(t)$:**
$$R(t) = P(T > t) = 1 - F(t) = \int_{t}^{\infty} f(u)\, du$$

This is the probability the component survives past time $t$.

**Step 2 — Probability Density Function (pdf) of failure $f(t)$:**
$$f(t) = \frac{dF(t)}{dt} = -\frac{dR(t)}{dt}$$

**Step 3 — Conditional (Instantaneous) Failure Rate $\lambda(t)$ or $h(t)$:**
$$\lambda(t) = \lim_{\Delta t \to 0} \frac{P(t < T \le t + \Delta t \mid T > t)}{\Delta t} = \frac{f(t)}{R(t)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters:** $\lambda(t)$ is *not* the unconditional probability of failure. It is the failure rate **conditional on having survived to time $t$**. This conditional form is what makes it the natural "wear indicator" for a system still in service.

**Step 4 — The Exponential Identity (valid for ANY $R(t)$):**
$$R(t) = \exp\left(-\int_{0}^{t} \lambda(u)\, du\right)$$

The cumulative hazard $H(t) = \int_{0}^{t} \lambda(u)\, du$ completely determines reliability.

**Step 5 — Mean Time To Failure:**
$$\text{MTTF} = E[T] = \int_{0}^{\infty} R(t)\, dt$$

(For Phase II, where $\lambda(t) = \lambda$, this reduces to $\text{MTTF} = 1/\lambda$.)

### 2.2 The 'Why' of Each Phase

**Phase I — Decreasing $\lambda(t)$:** 
- The "weak" sub-population has not yet been culled.
- Reliability improves with time because defective units fail first and leave the population.
- Mathematically: $f(t)$ shrinks faster than $R(t)$, so $\lambda(t) = f(t)/R(t)$ declines.
- **Mitigation:** Burn-in / factory screening (e.g., running capacitors at 1.5× rated voltage for 168 hours before shipment).

**Phase II — Constant $\lambda(t)$:** 
- The remaining population is homogeneous; failures are caused by external random shocks (voltage spikes, vibration spikes, temperature transients).
- Mathematically, this corresponds to a **memoryless** distribution. The exponential distribution $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ is the only continuous distribution with this property.
- This is the *design* region: redundancy, derating, and protective circuitry live here.

**Phase III — Increasing $\lambda(t)$:** 
- Cumulative damage (fatigue, corrosion) drives failure probability up.
- The system is **aging**.
- Mathematically corresponds to distributions with **IFR (Increasing Failure Rate)** property — e.g., Weibull with shape $k > 1$, Lognormal, Gamma.

### 2.3 The Weibull Model — Single-Distribution Unification

A single **Weibull distribution** can model all three regions by varying its shape parameter $\beta$:

$$R(t) = \exp\left(-\left(\frac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta}\right), \quad t \ge 0$$

$$\lambda(t) = \frac{\beta}{\eta}\left(\frac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta-1}$$

- $\beta < 1$ → decreasing $\lambda(t)$ → **Phase I**
- $\beta = 1$ → constant $\lambda(t) = 1/\eta$ → **Phase II** (reduces to exponential)
- $\beta > 1$ → increasing $\lambda(t)$ → **Phase III**

This unification is why the Weibull distribution is the **workhorse** of reliability engineering and is heavily tested in KTU examinations.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Conditions / Region | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Reliability $R(t)$ | $R(t) = P(T > t)$ | All phases | dimensionless |
| 2 | Failure density $f(t)$ | $f(t) = -dR/dt$ | All phases | 1/time |
| 3 | Failure rate $\lambda(t)$ | $\lambda(t) = f(t)/R(t)$ | All phases | 1/time (FITs) |
| 4 | Cumulative Hazard $H(t)$ | $H(t) = \int_{0}^{t}\lambda(u)\,du$ | All phases | dimensionless |
| 5 | Exponential identity | $R(t) = e^{-H(t)}$ | All phases | dimensionless |
| 6 | MTTF | $\text{MTTF} = \int_{0}^{\infty}R(t)\,dt$ | All phases | time |
| 7 | Phase II (exponential) $R(t)$ | $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ | $\lambda$ constant | dimensionless |
| 8 | Phase II MTTF | $\text{MTTF} = 1/\lambda$ | $\lambda$ constant | time |
| 9 | Phase II MTBF | $\text{MTBF} = 1/\lambda$ | $\lambda$ constant | time |
| 10 | Weibull $R(t)$ | $R(t) = \exp(-(t/\eta)^{\beta})$ | $\eta > 0, \beta > 0$ | dimensionless |
| 11 | Weibull $\lambda(t)$ | $\lambda(t) = (\beta/\eta)(t/\eta)^{\beta-1}$ | Weibull | 1/time |
| 12 | FIT | $1\,\text{FIT} = 1\,\text{failure}/10^{9}\text{ device-hr}$ | Phase II datasheet | $10^{-9}$/hr |
| 13 | Series system $R_s$ | $R_s = \prod_{i=1}^{n} R_i$ | Independent components | dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **LaTeX Pipeline Rule (KTU Valuation):** When writing $\vert x \vert$ or conditional probability in tables, use `\vert` or `\mid`, never the literal pipe `|` — it breaks the markdown table and is a common student typographical mistake.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace:** Turbine blade retirement is scheduled at the start of Phase III.
- **Semiconductor Industry:** Vendors publish Phase II FIT rates; aerospace buyers integrate these into system reliability budgets (e.g., 0.1 FIT per IC).
- **Nuclear Reactors:** Phase II failure rate of safety-critical pumps must be $< 10^{-6}$/hr.
- **Consumer Electronics:** Burn-in chambers (Phase I elimination) are standard before retail shipment.
- **Maintenance Engineering:** Replacement timing $T^*$ is optimized at the bottom of the bathtub to minimize cost per unit time.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: From $R(t)$ to the Bathtub Shape

**Derivation 1 — Why Phase II of the bathtub corresponds to the Exponential Distribution**

**Given:** Failure rate is constant: $\lambda(t) = \lambda$ for all $t \ge 0$.

**Step 1 — Set up the differential equation from the definition of hazard rate:**
$$\lambda(t) = \frac{f(t)}{R(t)} = -\frac{1}{R(t)} \cdot \frac{dR(t)}{dt}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute $\lambda(t) = \lambda$:**
$$-\frac{1}{R} \cdot \frac{dR}{dt} = \lambda$$

**Step 3 — Separate variables:**
$$\frac{dR}{R} = -\lambda \, dt$$

**Step 4 — Integrate from $0$ to $t$, using $R(0) = 1$ (a working component at $t=0$ is alive by definition):**
$$\int_{1}^{R(t)} \frac{dR}{R} = -\lambda \int_{0}^{t} dt$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate the integrals:**
$$\ln R(t) - \ln 1 = -\lambda t$$

**Step 6 — Since $\ln 1 = 0$:**
$$\ln R(t) = -\lambda t$$

**Step 7 — Exponentiate both sides:**
$$R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$$

This is the **exponential reliability function**, the canonical Phase II model. The constant failure rate **forces** the exponential form — they are mathematically equivalent.

---

**Derivation 2 — MTTF of the Exponential Distribution**

**Given:** $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$, $t \ge 0$.

**Step 1 — Recall the general MTTF formula:**
$$\text{MTTF} = \int_{0}^{\infty} R(t)\, dt$$

**Step 2 — Substitute:**
$$\text{MTTF} = \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-\lambda t}\, dt$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the integral (the standard exponential integral equals $1/\lambda$):**
$$\text{MTTF} = \left[-\frac{1}{\lambda} e^{-\lambda t}\right]_{0}^{\infty}$$

**Step 4 — Apply limits: at $t \to \infty$, $e^{-\lambda t} \to 0$; at $t = 0$, $e^{0} = 1$:**
$$\text{MTTF} = 0 - \left(-\frac{1}{\lambda}\right) = \frac{1}{\lambda}$$

**Conclusion:** $\text{MTTF} = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}$. This is a *board-favourite* result and must be memorised for KTU ESE.

---

**Derivation 3 — Weibull Failure Rate Behaviour by Shape Parameter $\beta$**

**Given:** $R(t) = \exp(-(t/\eta)^{\beta})$.

**Step 1 — Compute $f(t) = -dR/dt$:**
First, let $u = (t/\eta)^{\beta}$, so $R = e^{-u}$. Then $\frac{du}{dt} = \beta (t/\eta)^{\beta-1} \cdot \frac{1}{\eta} = \frac{\beta}{\eta} (t/\eta)^{\beta-1}$.

Therefore:
$$f(t) = -\frac{dR}{dt} = e^{-u} \cdot \frac{du}{dt} = \exp\!\left(-\left(\frac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta}\right) \cdot \frac{\beta}{\eta}\left(\frac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta-1}$$

**Step 2 — Form the failure rate:**
$$\lambda(t) = \frac{f(t)}{R(t)} = \frac{\exp(-(t/\eta)^{\beta}) \cdot (\beta/\eta)(t/\eta)^{\beta-1}}{\exp(-(t/\eta)^{\beta})} = \frac{\beta}{\eta}\left(\frac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta-1}$$

The exponentials **cancel exactly** — the failure rate is a clean power law.

**Step 3 — Examine monotonicity in $\beta$:**

For $t > 0$ and fixed $\eta$:

$$\frac{\partial \lambda}{\partial t} = \frac{\beta}{\eta} \cdot (\beta - 1) \cdot \frac{1}{\eta}\left(\frac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta-2} = \frac{\beta(\beta-1)}{\eta^2}\left(\frac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta-2}$$

The sign of the derivative is governed by $(\beta - 1)$:

- If $\beta < 1$: $\beta - 1 < 0 \Rightarrow \lambda'(t) < 0$ → **decreasing** (Phase I).
- If $\beta = 1$: $\beta - 1 = 0 \Rightarrow \lambda'(t) = 0$ → **constant** (Phase II).
- If $\beta > 1$: $\beta - 1 > 0 \Rightarrow \lambda'(t) > 0$ → **increasing** (Phase III).

**Conclusion:** The single parameter $\beta$ of the Weibull family **reproduces all three phases of the bathtub curve** — a cornerstone of KTU Module 1 theory.

---

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Worked Numerical Demonstrations

**Example A — Phase II FIT Calculation (Highly Probable KTU 14-Mark Part)**

> A microchip vendor specifies its device failure rate during useful life as **$\lambda = 250$ FIT**.
> Compute: (i) reliability for 10,000 hours of operation, (ii) MTTF, (iii) probability of survival for 25,000 hours, (iv) number of expected failures in a fleet of 5,000 devices over 20,000 hours.

**Solution (Step-by-Step):**

Convert FIT: $\lambda = 250\,\text{FIT} = 250 \times 10^{-9}\,\text{per hour} = 2.5 \times 10^{-7}\,\text{hr}^{-1}$.

**(i) $R(10{,}000)$:**
$$R(t) = e^{-\lambda t} = e^{-(2.5 \times 10^{-7})(10{,}000)} = e^{-2.5 \times 10^{-3}} = e^{-0.0025}$$

$$\boxed{R(10{,}000) = 0.99750}$$

**(ii) MTTF:**
$$\text{MTTF} = \frac{1}{\lambda} = \frac{1}{2.5 \times 10^{-7}} = 4{,}000{,}000\,\text{hours} \approx 456.6\,\text{years}$$

**(iii) $R(25{,}000)$:**
$$R(25{,}000) = e^{-(2.5 \times 10^{-7})(25{,}000)} = e^{-0.00625} = 0.99377$$

**(iv) Expected failures in fleet:** $E[\text{failures}] = N \cdot (1 - R(t)) = 5000 \times (1 - e^{-5 \times 10^{-3}}) \approx 5000 \times 0.004988 = 24.94 \approx 25$ devices.

---

**Example B — Weibull Phase Identification (Conceptual 3-Mark Question)**

> A manufacturer fits time-to-failure data of a sensor with a Weibull distribution. Estimated parameters are $\beta = 0.7$ and $\eta = 5000\,\text{hrs}$. Identify the bathtub phase and compute the failure rate at $t = 2000$ hrs.

**Solution:**

Since $\beta = 0.7 < 1$, the failure rate is **decreasing** → **Phase I (Infant Mortality)**.

$$\lambda(2000) = \frac{\beta}{\eta}\left(\frac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta-1} = \frac{0.7}{5000}\left(\frac{2000}{5000}\right)^{0.7-1} = 1.4 \times 10^{-4} \times (0.4)^{-0.3}$$

Compute $(0.4)^{-0.3} = e^{-0.3 \ln 0.4} = e^{-0.3 \times (-0.9163)} = e^{0.2749} = 1.3165$.

$$\boxed{\lambda(2000) = 1.4 \times 10^{-4} \times 1.3165 \approx 1.843 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{per hour}}$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The Classical Bathtub Curve — Annotated Schematic

```mermaid
graph LR
    axisX[Operating Time t -->] --> base
    axisY["Failure Rate lambda t"] --> base
    base[Coordinate Origin]:::axis
    phase1[Phase I: Infant Mortality]:::phase1Style
    phase2[Phase II: Useful Life]:::phase2Style
    phase3[Phase III: Wear Out]:::phase3Style
    point1[End of Burn-in t1]:::markerStyle
    point2[Start of Wear Out t2]:::markerStyle

    phase1 --- point1
    point1 --- phase2
    phase2 --- point2
    point2 --- phase3
```

### 4.2 Three-Region Block Architecture of the Bathtub

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Start of Component Life t equals 0] --> B
    B{Phase I: Infant Mortality}:::phase1Style
    B -->|Decreasing lambda t| C[Manufacturer Burn-in Filters Out Weak Units]
    C --> D{t equals t1 Boundary}
    D --> E{Phase II: Useful Life}:::phase2Style
    E -->|Constant lambda equals constant| F[Random Overstress Failures Dominate]
    F --> G{t equals t2 Boundary}
    G --> H{Phase III: Wear Out}:::phase3Style
    H -->|Increasing lambda t| I[Fatigue Corrosion Creep Drive Failures]
    I --> J[End of Useful Service Life]

    classDef phase1Style fill:#FFD6D6,stroke:#B30000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef phase2Style fill:#D6F0FF,stroke:#0050B3,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef phase3Style fill:#D6FFD6,stroke:#006600,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
```

### 4.3 Weibull Shape Parameter → Phase Mapping

```mermaid
graph LR
    W[Weibull Shape Parameter beta] --> B1
    B1[beta less than 1]:::p1 -->|Decreasing| P1[Phase I Infant Mortality]
    B2[beta equals 1]:::p2 -->|Constant| P2[Phase II Useful Life Exponential]
    B3[beta greater than 1]:::p3 -->|Increasing| P3[Phase III Wear Out]

    W --> B2
    W --> B3

    classDef p1 fill:#FFCCCC,stroke:#990000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef p2 fill:#CCE5FF,stroke:#003366,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef p3 fill:#CCFFCC,stroke:#006600,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
```

### 4.4 Functional Mapping: Reliability Primitives → Bathtub

```mermaid
graph TD
    R[Reliability Function R of t]:::primStyle
    F[Failure CDF F of t]:::primStyle
    f[Failure PDF f of t]:::primStyle
    L[Failure Rate lambda of t]:::primStyle
    H[Cumulative Hazard H of t]:::primStyle
    M[MTTF Integral of R]:::primStyle

    R -->|R equals 1 minus F| F
    F -->|f equals dF by dt| f
    f -->|lambda equals f divided by R| L
    L -->|H equals integral lambda du| H
    R -->|R equals exp of minus H| H
    R --> M

    classDef primStyle fill:#FFF4CC,stroke:#806600,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Set A]**
> **Q1.** Define the **Bathtub Curve** and list its three phases. For each phase, state (i) the nature of the failure rate (increasing/decreasing/constant) and (ii) the dominant cause of failure.

**Model Answer (3 Marks — Board Distribution):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** The Bathtub Curve is a plot of the *instantaneous failure rate* $\lambda(t)$ of a population of components against operating time $t$. It derives its name from its characteristic bathtub-like shape.
- **Phase I – Infant Mortality (1 mark):** Failure rate is **decreasing**. Dominant causes are manufacturing defects, latent flaws, and poor workmanship.
- **Phase II – Useful Life (0.5 mark):** Failure rate is **constant**. Dominant cause is random overstress from the operating environment.
- **Phase III – Wear-out (0.5 mark):** Failure rate is **increasing**. Dominant causes are fatigue, corrosion, creep, and cumulative wear.

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Set B]**
> **Q2.** Explain why the **exponential distribution** is the natural mathematical model for the useful life (Phase II) region of the Bathtub Curve.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Memoryless property (1 mark):** A constant failure rate $\lambda(t) = \lambda$ mathematically enforces **memorylessness**, i.e., $P(T > t + s \mid T > t) = P(T > s)$. The conditional probability of survival for the next $s$ hours does not depend on the age already accumulated.
- **Differential equation derivation (1 mark):** Setting $\lambda(t) = \lambda$ in the definition $\lambda = -R'(t)/R(t)$ and solving the separable ODE yields $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ uniquely. Hence, exponential is the **only** distribution matching Phase II.
- **Engineering significance (1 mark):** Exponential enables closed-form system reliability computations for series/parallel configurations and direct MTTF $= 1/\lambda$ — making it indispensable for redundancy allocation in the useful life region.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Model Paper 2024]**
> 
> **QUESTION A (14 Marks):**
> 
> **(a)** Derive the relationship between the reliability function $R(t)$, the failure density $f(t)$, and the instantaneous failure rate $\lambda(t)$. Hence, show that the cumulative hazard function $H(t) = \int_0^t \lambda(u)\, du$ and $R(t)$ are related by $R(t) = e^{-H(t)}$. **(7 marks)**
> 
> **(b)** A system has Weibull-distributed time-to-failure with shape parameter $\beta = 2.5$ and scale parameter $\eta = 8000$ hours. (i) Identify the corresponding phase of the bathtub curve. (ii) Compute the reliability at $t = 1000$ hr. (iii) Compute the failure rate at $t = 4000$ hr. (iv) Compute the MTTF. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for Q-A:**

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (1.5 marks):** Start from the definition of conditional failure rate (hazard function):
$$\lambda(t) = \lim_{\Delta t \to 0}\frac{P(t < T \le t+\Delta t \mid T > t)}{\Delta t} = \frac{f(t)}{R(t)}$$

Substitute $f(t) = -dR(t)/dt$:
$$\lambda(t) = -\frac{1}{R(t)}\cdot\frac{dR(t)}{dt}$$

**Step 2 (1.5 marks):** Separate variables:
$$\lambda(t)\, dt = -\frac{dR(t)}{R(t)}$$

**Step 3 (1.5 marks):** Integrate both sides from $0$ to $t$. Left side gives $H(t) = \int_0^t \lambda(u)\, du$. Right side:
$$-\int_0^t \frac{dR}{R} = -\big[\ln R(t) - \ln R(0)\big] = -\ln R(t)$$
(since $R(0) = 1$, so $\ln R(0) = 0$).

**Step 4 (1.5 marks):** Equate and exponentiate:
$$H(t) = -\ln R(t) \;\Rightarrow\; \ln R(t) = -H(t) \;\Rightarrow\; \boxed{R(t) = e^{-H(t)}}$$

This is the **Aalen–Johansen identity**, valid for any reliability distribution.

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Note that this is consistent with the special case $\lambda = $ const: $H(t) = \lambda t$ and $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$.

---

**(b) Weibull Computations (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Identify phase. Since $\beta = 2.5 > 1$, the failure rate is **increasing** → **Phase III (Wear-out)**.

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Compute $R(1000)$:
$$R(1000) = \exp\!\left(-\left(\frac{1000}{8000}\right)^{2.5}\right) = \exp(-(0.125)^{2.5})$$

Compute $(0.125)^{2.5} = (0.125)^2 \cdot (0.125)^{0.5} = 0.015625 \cdot 0.35355 = 0.005524$.

$$R(1000) = e^{-0.005524} = 0.99449$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Compute $\lambda(4000)$:
$$\lambda(4000) = \frac{\beta}{\eta}\left(\frac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta-1} = \frac{2.5}{8000}\left(\frac{4000}{8000}\right)^{1.5} = 3.125 \times 10^{-4} \cdot (0.5)^{1.5}$$

Compute $(0.5)^{1.5} = 0.5 \cdot \sqrt{0.5} = 0.5 \cdot 0.7071 = 0.35355$.

$$\lambda(4000) = 3.125 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.35355 = 1.105 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{per hour}$$

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Compute MTTF for Weibull:
$$\text{MTTF} = \eta \cdot \Gamma\!\left(1 + \frac{1}{\beta}\right) = 8000 \cdot \Gamma\!\left(1 + \frac{1}{2.5}\right) = 8000 \cdot \Gamma(1.4)$$

Using standard tables: $\Gamma(1.4) \approx 0.8873$.

$$\text{MTTF} = 8000 \times 0.8873 = 7098.4\,\text{hours}$$

---

> **QUESTION B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice):**
> 
> **(a)** With the aid of a neat labelled sketch, describe the **Bathtub Curve** for a typical repairable electronic system. Discuss the engineering measures adopted in each of the three regions. **(7 marks)**
> 
> **(b)** A manufacturer reports a constant failure rate of $\lambda = 4 \times 10^{-6}$ per hour for a critical component. Compute: (i) the MTTF in years, (ii) $R(5000)$, (iii) $R(15{,}000)$, (iv) the design redundancy factor $k$ such that system reliability $R_s \ge 0.99$ for 5000 hours using $k$ identical components in parallel. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for Q-B:**

**(a) Descriptive Answer (7 marks):**

**Sketch (2 marks):** The student must draw the curve with X-axis = operating time, Y-axis = failure rate $\lambda(t)$, with three labelled regions: I, II, III, and the boundary points $t_1$ and $t_2$.

**Phase I – Infant Mortality (1.5 marks):** Failure rate decreases. Causes: manufacturing defects, workmanship errors, substandard materials. **Engineering measures:** burn-in testing, factory screening, accelerated stress screening, ESS (Environmental Stress Screening), infant-mortality burn-in of 24–168 hours, statistical process control (SPC) on the production line.

**Phase II – Useful Life (1.5 marks):** Failure rate is constant and at its minimum. Causes: random external events — voltage transients, vibration shocks, operator errors. **Engineering measures:** derating (operating below rated stress), redundancy (parallel and standby), protective circuitry (fuses, surge protectors, EMI filters), preventive maintenance.

**Phase III – Wear-out (2 marks):** Failure rate increases. Causes: fatigue, corrosion, wear, dielectric degradation. **Engineering measures:** scheduled preventive replacement, condition-based monitoring (vibration analysis, oil analysis, thermography), life-cycle cost analysis to determine optimal replacement time, refurbishment/remanufacturing.

---

**(b) Numerical Answer (7 marks):**

$\lambda = 4 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{hr}^{-1}$.

**(i) MTTF in years (1.5 marks):**
$$\text{MTTF} = \frac{1}{\lambda} = \frac{1}{4 \times 10^{-6}} = 250{,}000\,\text{hours}$$

Convert: $250{,}000 / 8760 \approx 28.54$ **years**.

**(ii) $R(5000)$ (1.5 marks):**
$$R(5000) = e^{-(4 \times 10^{-6})(5000)} = e^{-0.02} = 0.9802$$

**(iii) $R(15{,}000)$ (1.5 marks):**
$$R(15{,}000) = e^{-(4 \times 10^{-6})(15{,}000)} = e^{-0.06} = 0.9418$$

**(iv) Parallel redundancy factor $k$ (2.5 marks):**

For $k$ identical components in parallel, the system reliability is:
$$R_s = 1 - (1 - R_i)^k$$

We need $R_s \ge 0.99$ at $t = 5000$, so $R_i = R(5000) = 0.9802$.

$$0.99 \le 1 - (1 - 0.9802)^k = 1 - (0.0198)^k$$

$$(0.0198)^k \le 0.01$$

Take logarithm (note: $\ln 0.0198 < 0$, so inequality flips is unnecessary since both sides are positive, but log is monotonically increasing):
$$k \cdot \ln(0.0198) \le \ln(0.01)$$

$$k \ge \frac{\ln(0.01)}{\ln(0.0198)} = \frac{-4.6052}{-3.9221} = 1.174$$

Since $k$ must be an integer, $k = 2$ components in parallel suffice.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Commonly Lose Marks on Bathtub Curve Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing $\lambda(t)$ with unconditional $f(t)$.** The hazard function is *conditional* on survival to $t$. Examiners deduct full credit for this conceptual error.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert FIT to per-hour.** $\lambda = 500$ FIT means $\lambda = 500 \times 10^{-9}$ /hr, NOT 500/hr. This is a single-line mistake that costs 2–3 marks.
> 3. **Mixing up MTTF and MTBF.** For non-repairable items they coincide; for *repairable* items MTBF includes repair time. Read the question carefully.
> 4. **In Weibull MTTF calculations, omitting the gamma function.** $\text{MTTF} = \eta \Gamma(1+1/\beta)$, NOT simply $\eta$. For $\beta = 1$, $\Gamma(2) = 1$, so the simple form is a *special* case.
> 5. **In parallel-redundancy questions, $R_s = 1 - (1-R_i)^k$ is for non-repaired active parallel.** For standby, the formula is different. State the assumption.
> 6. **Sketch quality matters.** A Bathtub Curve sketch without labelled regions, axes, and boundary points $t_1$, $t_2$ loses 1–2 marks.
> 7. **Units of time.** Always state MTTF in hours *and* convert to years/days when the question asks.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (Board-Exam Ready):**
> 
> - [ ] **Bathtub Curve** = plot of $\lambda(t)$ versus $t$; three regions: I (decreasing), II (constant), III (increasing).
> - [ ] **Phase I causes:** manufacturing defects, latent flaws. **Cure:** burn-in screening.
> - [ ] **Phase II causes:** random external overstress. **Cure:** derating, redundancy, protection.
> - [ ] **Phase III causes:** fatigue, wear, corrosion. **Cure:** preventive replacement.
> - [ ] **Key identity:** $\lambda(t) = f(t)/R(t) = -(1/R)(dR/dt)$.
> - [ ] **Exponential identity (any distribution):** $R(t) = \exp\!\left(-\int_0^t \lambda(u)\, du\right)$.
> - [ ] **Exponential distribution** is the *only* continuous distribution with constant failure rate (memoryless).
> - [ ] **Phase II formulas:** $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$, $\text{MTTF} = 1/\lambda$, $\text{MTBF} = 1/\lambda$.
> - [ ] **1 FIT** = 1 failure per $10^9$ device-hours.
> - [ ] **Weibull shape parameter** $\beta$ selects the phase: $\beta<1$ → I, $\beta=1$ → II, $\beta>1$ → III.
> - [ ] **Weibull MTTF:** $\eta \cdot \Gamma(1 + 1/\beta)$.
> - [ ] **MTTF from data:** $\text{MTTF} = \int_0^\infty R(t)\, dt$.
> - [ ] **Series system:** $R_s = \prod R_i$. **Parallel (active) system:** $R_s = 1 - \prod(1 - R_i)$.
> - [ ] **The bathtub curve is a *qualitative* model** — actual curves are smoother; some populations may not exhibit all three phases (e.g., software does not "wear out" in the classical sense).
> - [ ] **In KTU ESE:** always label the three regions in any sketch, always state units, always quote MTTF in original and converted units.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Derivation of the Reliability Function
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET632 - RELIABILITY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Derivation of the Reliability Function

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Reliability Function**, denoted as **R(t)**, is formally defined as the conditional probability that a component, system, or piece of equipment will successfully perform its intended function **without failure** for a specified period of time *t*, given that it was operational at time *t = 0*.

Mathematically, it is expressed as:

$$R(t) = P(T > t)$$

where **T** is a non-negative random variable representing the **Time-To-Failure (TTF)** of the component. By definition:

$$R(0) = 1 \quad \text{and} \quad R(\infty) = 0$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Scheme):**
> The Reliability Function is the foundational probability model upon which all other reliability metrics (failure rate, hazard function, MTTF, availability) are mathematically derived. Mastering its derivation is essential for Module 1, 2, and 3 problems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary Conditions You MUST Remember:**
> - $R(0) = 1$ → A brand-new component is 100% reliable at the start of its life.
> - $R(\infty) = 0$ → Every component will eventually fail given infinite time (under standard wear-out assumptions).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Survival Lottery"

Imagine purchasing a brand-new LED bulb with a rated life of 50,000 hours. The **Reliability Function R(t)** answers the question: *"What is the probability that this bulb will still be glowing brightly at time t?"*

Think of it like a **survival lottery**:
- At t = 0 (the moment you switch it on), the bulb is "alive" → R(0) = 1.
- As time passes, microscopic filament degradation occurs, solder joints fatigue, and the probability of survival decreases.
- At t = 50,000 hours (its rated life), R(t) ≈ 0.37 (assuming exponential decay).
- At t = ∞, the bulb **will** fail (filament burns out) → R(∞) = 0.

The **shape of the R(t) curve** tells the entire story of the component's life. A steep drop means early failures (infant mortality); a flat line means highly stable performance; a curve that drops faster near the end means wear-out failures.

> [!TIP]
> **Intuitive Takeaway:** R(t) is essentially the "survival probability" of a component. Its complement, F(t) = 1 − R(t), is the "failure probability" by time t. Together, they partition the entire probability space (i.e., R(t) + F(t) = 1).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Building Blocks

Before deriving R(t), we must establish three foundational probability concepts that act as "Lego blocks" for the derivation:

**Block 1 — Cumulative Distribution Function (CDF) of Failure:**
The CDF, denoted F(t), is the probability that the component fails *on or before* time t:
$$F(t) = P(T \le t)$$

**Block 2 — Probability Density Function (PDF) of Failure:**
The PDF, denoted f(t), is the instantaneous rate of failure probability density:
$$f(t) = \frac{dF(t)}{dt}$$

**Block 3 — The Hazard / Failure Rate Function h(t):**
The hazard function represents the *instantaneous* rate of failure *given survival up to time t*. It is a **conditional probability density**:
$$h(t) = \frac{f(t)}{R(t)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is h(t) called "conditional"?** Because the denominator R(t) restricts the population to only those components that have *survived* up to time t. Among these survivors, what fraction will fail in the next instant?

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| S. No. | Quantity | Formula | Units / Interpretation | Boundary |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Reliability Function | $R(t) = P(T > t)$ | Dimensionless probability | $0 \le R(t) \le 1$ |
| 2 | Unreliability (CDF) | $F(t) = 1 - R(t)$ | Probability of failure by time t | $0 \le F(t) \le 1$ |
| 3 | Failure Density | $f(t) = -\frac{dR(t)}{dt}$ | Failure probability per unit time | $f(t) \ge 0$ |
| 4 | Reliability Integral | $R(t) = \int_{t}^{\infty} f(x) \, dx$ | Survival probability beyond t | — |
| 5 | Hazard Function | $h(t) = \frac{f(t)}{R(t)}$ | Conditional failure rate (1/time) | $h(t) \ge 0$ |
| 6 | Cumulative Hazard | $H(t) = \int_{0}^{t} h(x) \, dx$ | Total accumulated hazard | $H(\infty) = \infty$ |
| 7 | **Master Reliability Equation** | $R(t) = \exp\!\left(-\int_{0}^{t} h(x) \, dx\right)$ | Universal R(t) formula | Always valid |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exponential Special Case:** When the hazard function is constant, $h(t) = \lambda$ (constant failure rate), the master equation collapses to the famous exponential reliability form:
> $$R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$$
> This is the simplest and most heavily tested distribution in KTU board exams.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Reliability Function is the **backbone of every quantitative reliability assessment** in industry:

- **Aerospace Engineering:** Used to compute the probability of a turbine blade surviving a 10,000-hour flight cycle before mandatory replacement.
- **Power Systems:** Determines transformer replacement schedules in 400 kV substations (Kerala State Electricity Board uses similar models).
- **Electronics Manufacturing:** Predicts solder joint failure in PCB assemblies under thermal cycling.
- **Software Engineering:** Models crash-free probability of operating systems over time.
- **Automotive (Kerala Industries):** Used by TVS, BrahMos, and BEL for warranty prediction and predictive maintenance.

The derivation you are about to see in Section 3 is the *only* bridge that connects raw failure data to these practical engineering decisions.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation Path A — From Failure Density f(t) to R(t)

**Step 1:** Start with the definition of the Cumulative Distribution Function (CDF):
$$F(t) = P(T \le t)$$

**Step 2:** Recognize the complementary relationship. Since the component either survives beyond t **or** fails by t, and these are mutually exclusive exhaustive events:
$$R(t) + F(t) = 1$$
Therefore:
$$F(t) = 1 - R(t)$$

**Step 3:** Differentiate both sides with respect to t to obtain the probability density function f(t):
$$\frac{dF(t)}{dt} = \frac{d}{dt}\bigl[1 - R(t)\bigr]$$

Since the derivative of the constant 1 is 0:
$$\frac{dF(t)}{dt} = -\frac{dR(t)}{dt}$$

**Step 4:** Substitute the standard definition $f(t) = \frac{dF(t)}{dt}$:
$$f(t) = -\frac{dR(t)}{dt}$$

**Step 5:** Solve for R(t) by integrating both sides from t to infinity:
$$\int_{t}^{\infty} f(x) \, dx = -\int_{t}^{\infty} \frac{dR(x)}{dx} \, dx$$

**Step 6:** Evaluate the right-hand side. The right integral is a simple antiderivative:
$$-\Bigl[R(x)\Bigr]_{t}^{\infty} = -\bigl[R(\infty) - R(t)\bigr]$$

**Step 7:** Apply the boundary condition $R(\infty) = 0$ (every component eventually fails):
$$-\bigl[0 - R(t)\bigr] = R(t)$$

**Step 8:** Therefore, the **closed-form expression** of the reliability function in terms of failure density is:

$$\boxed{R(t) = \int_{t}^{\infty} f(x) \, dx}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Meaning of This Result:** R(t) is the **area under the f(t) curve from t to infinity**. Geometrically, it is the "right tail" of the failure density function. The larger this tail area, the more reliable the component at age t.

---

### 3.2 Derivation Path B — From Hazard Function h(t) to R(t) [THE MASTER DERIVATION]

This is the **most important derivation** for KTU board exams. It yields the universal form of R(t).

**Step 1:** Start with the definition of the hazard function (conditional failure rate):
$$h(t) = \frac{f(t)}{R(t)}$$

**Step 2:** From Derivation Path A, recall the identity $f(t) = -\frac{dR(t)}{dt}$. Substitute this into the hazard equation:
