$$h(t) = \frac{-\frac{dR(t)}{dt}}{R(t)} = -\frac{1}{R(t)} \cdot \frac{dR(t)}{dt}$$

**Step 3:** Rearrange to separate variables (R and t) onto opposite sides of the equation:
$$h(t) \, dt = -\frac{dR(t)}{R(t)}$$

**Step 4:** Integrate both sides from 0 to t. The left side integrates over time, the right side over reliability:
$$\int_{0}^{t} h(x) \, dx = -\int_{0}^{t} \frac{1}{R(x)} \, \frac{dR(x)}{dx} \, dx$$

**Step 5:** Evaluate the right-hand side. The integrand is the derivative of $\ln R(x)$:
$$-\Bigl[\ln R(x)\Bigr]_{0}^{t} = -\bigl[\ln R(t) - \ln R(0)\bigr]$$

**Step 6:** Apply the boundary condition $R(0) = 1$, hence $\ln R(0) = \ln 1 = 0$:
$$\int_{0}^{t} h(x) \, dx = -\ln R(t) + 0 = -\ln R(t)$$

**Step 7:** Solve for $\ln R(t)$:
$$\ln R(t) = -\int_{0}^{t} h(x) \, dx$$

**Step 8:** Exponentiate both sides to obtain the closed-form **Master Reliability Equation**:

$$\boxed{R(t) = \exp\!\left(-\int_{0}^{t} h(x) \, dx\right)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why This Equation is "Universal":** It is valid for **ANY** distribution — Exponential, Weibull, Normal, Lognormal, Gamma. The only thing that changes is the functional form of h(t). For example:
> - If $h(t) = \lambda$ (constant) → $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ (Exponential).
> - If $h(t) = \frac{\beta}{\eta}\left(\frac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta-1}$ (Weibull) → $R(t) = e^{-(t/\eta)^\beta}$.

---

### 3.3 Derivation Path C — Relationship Between R(t), F(t), f(t), and h(t)

The four reliability functions are interconnected. Using the substitutions from Path A:

**Step 1:** From $f(t) = -\frac{dR(t)}{dt}$ and $h(t) = \frac{f(t)}{R(t)}$, multiply numerator and denominator:
$$h(t) = \frac{f(t)}{R(t)} = \frac{-\frac{dR(t)}{dt}}{R(t)} = -\frac{d}{dt}\bigl[\ln R(t)\bigr]$$

**Step 2:** The cumulative hazard H(t) is defined as:
$$H(t) = \int_{0}^{t} h(x) \, dx$$

**Step 3:** Substitute h(x) and integrate:
$$H(t) = \int_{0}^{t} -\frac{d}{dx}\bigl[\ln R(x)\bigr] \, dx = -\bigl[\ln R(t) - \ln R(0)\bigr] = -\ln R(t)$$

**Step 4:** Since $\ln R(0) = 0$, this gives:
$$R(t) = e^{-H(t)}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Aid for Exams:** The four pillars satisfy the following unified map:
> $$\begin{aligned}
> R(t) &= e^{-H(t)} \\[4pt]
> f(t) &= h(t) \cdot R(t) \\[4pt]
> F(t) &= 1 - R(t) \\[4pt]
> h(t) &= \frac{f(t)}{R(t)}
> \end{aligned}$$
> Memorize this map — it answers ~70% of reliability derivations asked in KTU exams.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Derivation Flow

The following block diagram traces the **logical flow** of the derivation, showing how the hazard function h(t) is processed through integration, exponentiation, and the boundary conditions to yield R(t).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Input Hazard Function h(t)"] --> B["Apply Definition h(t) = f(t) / R(t)"]
    B --> C["Substitute f(t) = -dR(t)/dt"]
    C --> D["Separate Variables: h(t)dt = -dR(t)/R(t)"]
    D --> E["Integrate from 0 to t"]
    E --> F["Apply Boundary Condition R(0) = 1"]
    F --> G["Solve for ln R(t)"]
    G --> H["Exponentiate Both Sides"]
    H --> I["Output: R(t) = exp(-Integral of h(x) dx from 0 to t)"]
    I --> J["Special Case: h(t) = lambda constant"]
    J --> K["R(t) = exp(-lambda t) -- Exponential Reliability"]
```

### 4.2 Relationship Map — The Four Reliability Functions

This diagram shows how the four foundational reliability functions (R, F, f, h) interact mathematically.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["f(t) -- Failure Density"] --> B["F(t) -- CDF / Unreliability"]
    A --> C["h(t) -- Hazard Function"]
    D["R(t) -- Reliability"] --> B
    D --> A
    D --> C
    B --> A
    C --> A
    D --> E["H(t) -- Cumulative Hazard"]
    E --> D
    style A fill:#e1f5ff
    style B fill:#fff4e1
    style C fill:#e8f5e9
    style D fill:#fce4ec
    style E fill:#f3e5f5
```

### 4.3 Bath-Tub Curve & Reliability Decay Schematic

A conceptual timeline showing how R(t) decays over the operational life of a component, corresponding to the three phases of the bath-tub curve.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Phase 1: Infant Mortality -- R drops fast"] --> B["Phase 2: Useful Life -- R decays steadily"]
    B --> C["Phase 3: Wear-out -- R drops sharply"]
    style A fill:#ffcccc
    style B fill:#ccffcc
    style C fill:#ffcccc
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
Define the Reliability Function R(t). State and justify its two fundamental boundary conditions.

**Model Answer:**

The **Reliability Function R(t)** is defined as the probability that a component performs its intended function without failure for a specified duration *t*, given it was operational at *t = 0*:
$$R(t) = P(T > t)$$
where T is the random variable representing time-to-failure.

**Boundary Conditions:**
- $R(0) = 1$: At the start of life (t = 0), a new component has not yet experienced any stress or wear, so the probability of survival is unity (100%).
- $R(\infty) = 0$: Given an infinite time horizon, every component will eventually fail due to cumulative wear, environmental degradation, or random shocks. Hence survival probability tends to zero.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
Derive the relationship between the reliability function R(t) and the hazard function h(t) for a component.

**Model Answer:**

Starting with the definition of the hazard function:
$$h(t) = \frac{f(t)}{R(t)}$$

Since $f(t) = -\frac{dR(t)}{dt}$, substitution gives:
$$h(t) = -\frac{1}{R(t)} \cdot \frac{dR(t)}{dt}$$

Separating variables and integrating from 0 to t:
$$\int_{0}^{t} h(x) \, dx = -\ln R(t) + \ln R(0)$$

Since $R(0) = 1$, $\ln R(0) = 0$, giving the final form:
$$R(t) = \exp\!\left(-\int_{0}^{t} h(x) \, dx\right)$$

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Starting from first principles, derive the master reliability equation $R(t) = \exp\!\left(-\int_{0}^{t} h(x) \, dx\right)$. Clearly state all assumptions, definitions, and boundary conditions used. (7 marks)

**(b)** For an electronic component, the hazard rate is observed to be constant at $h(t) = \lambda = 0.0002$ failures per hour.
- (i) Write the explicit form of R(t).
- (ii) Calculate the reliability at t = 1000 hours.
- (iii) Find the time by which reliability drops to 0.90 (i.e., the B10 life). (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation:** *(See Section 3.2 of these notes for the full 8-step derivation.)*

The key steps are:
1. Start with $h(t) = f(t)/R(t)$.
2. Substitute $f(t) = -dR(t)/dt$.
3. Separate variables: $h(t) \, dt = -dR(t)/R(t)$.
4. Integrate from 0 to t.
5. Apply $R(0) = 1$ to evaluate constants.
6. Solve for $\ln R(t)$.
7. Exponentiate to obtain $R(t) = \exp\!\left(-\int_{0}^{t} h(x) \, dx\right)$.

*(7 marks awarded for clearly showing each step, defining variables, and stating boundary conditions.)*

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**(i)** Since h(t) = λ is constant:
$$R(t) = e^{-\lambda t} = e^{-0.0002 \, t}$$

**(ii)** At t = 1000 hours:
$$R(1000) = e^{-0.0002 \times 1000} = e^{-0.2}$$
$$R(1000) = 0.8187 \text{ (or } 81.87\%\text{)}$$

**(iii)** For R(t) = 0.90, solve for t:
$$0.90 = e^{-0.0002 \, t}$$
$$\ln(0.90) = -0.0002 \, t$$
$$-0.10536 = -0.0002 \, t$$
$$t = \frac{0.10536}{0.0002} = 526.8 \text{ hours}$$

Hence, the B10 life is approximately **527 hours**.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024 – Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** Explain the concepts of the following reliability functions with neat mathematical expressions:
   - (i) Cumulative Distribution Function F(t)
   - (ii) Probability Density Function f(t)
   - (iii) Hazard Function h(t)
   - (iv) Reliability Function R(t) (7 marks)

**(b)** The probability density function of time-to-failure of a motor is given by:
$$f(t) = \frac{1}{5000} e^{-t/5000}, \quad t \ge 0$$
- (i) Find the reliability function R(t).
- (ii) Calculate the reliability of the motor for a mission time of 2000 hours.
- (iii) Determine the Mean Time To Failure (MTTF). (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Conceptual Explanation:**

- **(i) F(t) — Cumulative Distribution Function:** Probability that the component fails on or before time t: $F(t) = P(T \le t) = \int_{0}^{t} f(x) \, dx$. Ranges from 0 to 1.

- **(ii) f(t) — Probability Density Function:** Instantaneous rate of change of failure probability: $f(t) = \frac{dF(t)}{dt} = -\frac{dR(t)}{dt}$. Represents the relative likelihood of failure at time t.

- **(iii) h(t) — Hazard Function:** Conditional instantaneous failure rate given survival up to t: $h(t) = f(t)/R(t)$. Quantifies the risk of failure in the next instant for a surviving component.

- **(iv) R(t) — Reliability Function:** Probability of survival beyond time t: $R(t) = P(T > t) = 1 - F(t) = \int_{t}^{\infty} f(x) \, dx$. This is the central metric in reliability engineering.

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**(i)** The given PDF is $f(t) = \frac{1}{5000} e^{-t/5000}$. This is the standard exponential PDF with $\lambda = 1/5000 = 0.0002$ per hour.

The reliability function is the right-tail integral:
$$R(t) = \int_{t}^{\infty} \frac{1}{5000} e^{-x/5000} \, dx$$

Let $u = x/5000$, $du = dx/5000$:
$$R(t) = \int_{t/5000}^{\infty} e^{-u} \, du = \Bigl[-e^{-u}\Bigr]_{t/5000}^{\infty} = 0 - \bigl(-e^{-t/5000}\bigr)$$

$$\boxed{R(t) = e^{-t/5000}}$$

**(ii)** At t = 2000 hours:
$$R(2000) = e^{-2000/5000} = e^{-0.4}$$
$$R(2000) = 0.6703 \text{ (or } 67.03\%\text{)}$$

**(iii)** For an exponential distribution, MTTF is:
$$\text{MTTF} = \int_{0}^{\infty} R(t) \, dt = \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-t/5000} \, dt$$

Evaluating:
$$\text{MTTF} = \Bigl[-5000 \, e^{-t/5000}\Bigr]_{0}^{\infty} = 0 - (-5000) = 5000 \text{ hours}$$

**Verification using standard formula:** For exponential distribution, MTTF = $1/\lambda = 1/(0.0002) = 5000$ hours ✓

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the boundary condition $R(0) = 1$:** When integrating, students often forget to evaluate $\ln R(0) = 0$. This leads to a missing constant term and wrong final answer. **Lose 2 marks.**
> 2. **Sign errors in $f(t) = -dR(t)/dt$:** R(t) is a *decreasing* function, so its derivative is *negative*. The minus sign is critical. Confusing this gives a negative reliability. **Lose 2 marks.**
> 3. **Confusing $F(t)$ with $f(t)$:** F is the *cumulative* probability (uppercase, dimensionless), f is the *density* (lowercase, has units of 1/time). **Lose 1–3 marks** depending on usage.
> 4. **Forgetting to state the integration limits (0 to t and 0 to ∞):** KTU examiners explicitly look for limits in definite integrals. **Lose 1 mark per missing limit.**
> 5. **In B10 life problems:** Not taking natural log of both sides of $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ is a common algebraic slip. **Lose 1 mark.**

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **R(t)** = Probability of survival beyond time t = $P(T > t)$.
- **Boundary conditions:** $R(0) = 1$ and $R(\infty) = 0$.
- **F(t)** = $1 - R(t)$ — Cumulative failure probability.
- **f(t)** = $-dR(t)/dt$ — Failure density (must be non-negative).
- **h(t)** = $f(t)/R(t)$ — Hazard / instantaneous failure rate (conditional).
- **H(t)** = $\int_{0}^{t} h(x) \, dx$ — Cumulative hazard.
- **Master equation (memorize!):** $R(t) = \exp\!\left(-\int_{0}^{t} h(x) \, dx\right)$.
- **Exponential special case:** $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ when h(t) is constant.
- **MTTF for exponential:** $1/\lambda$ (constant failure rate, no memory).
- **Geometric meaning:** R(t) is the *right-tail area* of the f(t) curve.
- **Always state assumptions** — stationary, independent failures when applying formulas.
- **Sign discipline:** R decreases, so $dR/dt \le 0$ always. F increases, so $dF/dt \ge 0$.
- **Units check:** h(t) has units of [1/time], f(t) has units of [1/time], R(t) and F(t) are dimensionless.
- **KTU favorite questions:** (1) Derive R(t) from h(t), (2) Compute R(t) for given λ, (3) Find B10/B50 life, (4) MTTF from PDF.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Failure and Failure Modes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET632 - RELIABILITY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Failure and Failure Modes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Defining "Failure" in Reliability Engineering

In the formal vocabulary of **Reliability Engineering**, a **failure** is defined as the **termination of the ability of an item to perform its required function**. This definition is deliberately broad: it is not restricted to the item breaking apart, but includes any deviation from the specified performance envelope.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Working Definition:** *Failure is the event or incipient condition in which a component, subsystem, or system no longer satisfies its functional, performance, or safety specifications under the conditions for which it was designed.*

The mathematical corollary of this is the binary state model:

$$
X(t) = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if the item is functioning (success) at time } t \\ 0 & \text{if the item has failed at time } t \end{cases}
$$

The transition from $X(t)=1$ to $X(t)=0$ is the **failure event**, and the time at which this occurs, $T$, is the **Time-To-Failure (TTF)** — a non-negative random variable that is the central object of study in this entire module.

## 1.2 Defining "Failure Mode"

A **failure mode** is the *manner* or *mechanism* by which a failure is observed. Two identical components can both "fail," but one may fail by **wear-out fracture**, while another may fail by **catastrophic rupture**. The classification of these *manners* into a structured taxonomy is what we call **Failure Mode Analysis**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic:** *Failure = WHAT happened; Failure Mode = HOW it happened.*

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Intuition

Think of a **ceiling fan** in a Kerala-style household during summer:
- The fan has a defined **function**: rotate at a specified RPM to circulate air.
- **Failure** is when the fan either stops rotating, rotates below the rated RPM, makes noise beyond acceptable limits, or, in the worst case, the blades detach.
- The **failure mode** explains *how* it failed: Did the **capacitor burn out**? Did the **bearing seize**? Did the **winding short-circuit**? Did the **regulator contact wear out**?

To the homeowner, the fan simply "stopped working." But to a reliability engineer, each of those failure modes (capacitor, bearing, winding, contact) demands a *different* preventive strategy. This is exactly why distinguishing the *failure event* from the *failure mode* is the first pillar of Module 1.

## 1.4 Auxiliary Vocabulary (KTU Syllabus Anchors)

| Term | Formal Definition |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Reliability** $R(t)$ | Probability that an item performs its intended function under stated conditions for a stated period of time. |
| **Maintainability** | Probability that a failed item will be restored to operational state within a given time, using prescribed procedures. |
| **Availability** $A(t)$ | Probability that an item is operational at a given instant of time. |
| **Failure Rate** $\lambda(t)$ | Instantaneous rate of failure of items that have survived up to time $t$. |
| **MTBF** | Mean Time Between Failures — the expected operating time between two consecutive failures. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The 2024 scheme explicitly links *failure and failure modes* to *system reliability models*. You will frequently be asked to classify a real-world failure into one of the recognized modes before computing its reliability contribution.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Classical Phases of Failure — The Bath-Tub Curve

The lifetime of most non-repaired items (and many repaired items when averaged over cycles) can be partitioned into **three distinct hazard regimes**:

### Phase I — Infant Mortality (Early Life / Burn-In)
- Characterized by a **decreasing** failure rate.
- Caused by manufacturing defects, substandard components, improper installation, or inadequate quality control.
- Counter-measure: **Burn-in** (deliberate operation before deployment to weed out weak units).

### Phase II — Useful Life (Random / Constant Failure Rate)
- Failure rate is approximately **constant** and at its **minimum**.
- Failures are stochastic and unpredictable — modeled by the **exponential distribution**.
- Counter-measure: Preventive maintenance is largely ineffective; corrective maintenance dominates.

### Phase III — Wear-Out (End of Life)
- Failure rate **increases** rapidly with time.
- Driven by physical degradation: fatigue, corrosion, wear, creep, oxidation.
- Counter-measure: Scheduled replacement / overhauls before the knee of the curve.

## 2.2 Classification of Failure Modes (KTU Board-Exam Taxonomy)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Big Five — Categories Asked Repeatedly in KTU ESE**

1. **Catastrophic Failures** — Sudden, total, and typically non-reversible loss of function (e.g., shaft fracture, capacitor explosion).
2. **Degradation Failures** — Gradual drift in a performance parameter until it crosses a specification threshold (e.g., capacitor ESR creep, bearing wear).
3. **Overstress Failures** — Failure caused when an applied stress (mechanical, thermal, electrical) **exceeds the design strength** of the part.
4. **Intermittent Failures** — Failures that recur and self-clearly are heavily dependent on operating conditions (e.g., loose connector, thermal-sensitive solder joint).
5. **Design / Inherent Failures** — Failure caused by flaws in the *design specification itself*, regardless of build quality (e.g., undersized heat sink, mismatched materials).

## 2.3 A Second Cut — By *Mechanism* (FMEA-level)

| Class | Typical Mechanism | Engineering Examples |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Mechanical failure | Fracture, fatigue, wear, creep | Shaft, gear, bearing |
| Electrical failure | Short, open, dielectric breakdown | Capacitor, motor winding |
| Thermal failure | Overheat, thermal runaway | Power transistor, IC junction |
| Chemical failure | Corrosion, oxidation, contamination | PCB, hydraulic fluid |
| Software / Logical failure | Bug, race condition, logic error | Embedded controller |
| Human failure | Operator error, maintenance error | Any man-machine system |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Master this table. Every formula below appears in at least one prior KTU ESE paper.**

| Symbol | Meaning | Formula / Relation | Units / Boundary |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $R(t)$ | Reliability function | $R(t) = P(T > t)$ | Dimensionless, $0 \le R \le 1$ |
| $F(t)$ | Cumulative failure (CDF) | $F(t) = 1 - R(t)$ | Dimensionless |
| $f(t)$ | PDF of failure | $f(t) = \dfrac{dF(t)}{dt} = -\dfrac{dR(t)}{dt}$ | $1/\text{time}$ |
| $\lambda(t)$ | Failure rate (hazard) | $\lambda(t) = \dfrac{f(t)}{R(t)}$ | Failures / $10^6$ hours (FIT) |
| $R(t)$ in terms of $\lambda(t)$ | General | $R(t) = \exp\!\!\left[-\displaystyle\int_0^{t}\!\lambda(\tau)\,d\tau\right]$ | Always valid |
| $\lambda = \text{const}$ | Exponential law | $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ | Constant hazard zone |
| MTTF | Mean Time To Failure | $\text{MTTF} = \displaystyle\int_0^{\infty}\!t\,f(t)\,dt$ | Hours / cycles |
| MTBF | Mean Time Between Failures | $\text{MTBF} = \text{MTTF} + \text{MTTR}$ | Hours / cycles |
| $A$ | Steady-state availability | $A = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$ | Dimensionless |

## 2.5 Real-World Utility

- **Aerospace**: Classifying failures into catastrophic vs. degradation determines whether the failure is *safety-critical* (zero tolerance) or *mission-critical* (redundancy acceptable).
- **Power Electronics**: Distinguishing thermal runaway from electrical overstress drives the choice between derating and snubber design.
- **Manufacturing**: FMEA (Failure Mode and Effects Analysis) uses these classifications to prioritize Risk Priority Numbers (RPN) — a direct industrial application of this module.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From the Reliability Function to the Failure Rate

The **failure rate** is the most heavily tested concept in Module 1, so a clean derivation is essential.

**Step 1 — Start with the unreliability (CDF).**
By definition, $F(t)$ is the probability that the device fails *on or before* time $t$:

$$
F(t) = P(T \le t)
$$

**Step 2 — Use the reliability function as the complement.**
Since failure and survival are mutually exclusive and exhaustive:

$$
R(t) = P(T > t) = 1 - F(t)
$$

**Step 3 — Differentiate to obtain the probability density of failure.**
The density $f(t)$ tells us how *likely* failure is in a small interval:

$$
f(t) = \frac{dF(t)}{dt} = -\frac{dR(t)}{dt}
$$

**Step 4 — Condition the density on having survived to time $t$.**
The conditional probability of failure in the next instant, *given* survival to $t$, is:

$$
\lambda(t) = \lim_{\Delta t \to 0}\frac{P(t < T \le t+\Delta t \mid T > t)}{\Delta t}
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute the conditional probability formula.**
By definition of conditional probability, $P(A \mid B) = P(A \cap B)/P(B)$:

$$
\lambda(t) = \lim_{\Delta t \to 0}\frac{P(t < T \le t+\Delta t)}{P(T > t)\,\Delta t}
$$

**Step 6 — Replace the numerator by the integral of the density over the small interval.**
As $\Delta t \to 0$:

$$
\lambda(t) = \frac{f(t)}{R(t)} = \frac{f(t)}{1-F(t)}
$$

**Step 7 — Cross-multiply and integrate to recover $R(t)$.**

$$
\lambda(t)\,R(t) = -\frac{dR(t)}{dt}
$$

Separate variables:

$$
\frac{dR(t)}{R(t)} = -\lambda(t)\,dt
$$

Integrate from $0$ to $t$ with the initial condition $R(0)=1$:

$$
\ln R(t) = -\int_{0}^{t}\lambda(\tau)\,d\tau
$$

Exponentiate both sides:

$$
\boxed{\,R(t) = \exp\!\left[-\int_{0}^{t}\lambda(\tau)\,d\tau\right]\,}
$$

## 3.2 Special Case: Constant Failure Rate (Exponential Model)

When $\lambda(t) = \lambda = \text{const}$ — the canonical model for **Phase II (useful life)** — the integral simplifies to a product:

$$
R(t) = \exp(-\lambda t)
$$

**Step-by-step:**

$$
R(t) = \exp\!\left[-\int_{0}^{t}\lambda\,d\tau\right] = \exp(-\lambda t)
$$

The associated density is:

$$
f(t) = -\frac{dR(t)}{dt} = \lambda e^{-\lambda t}
$$

The **MTTF** is computed as:

$$
\text{MTTF} = \int_{0}^{\infty} t\,f(t)\,dt = \int_{0}^{\infty} t\,\lambda e^{-\lambda t}\,dt
$$

This is a standard gamma-type integral. Using $\displaystyle\int_{0}^{\infty} x^{n} e^{-ax}dx = \dfrac{n!}{a^{n+1}}$ with $n=1$ and $a=\lambda$:

$$
\text{MTTF} = \frac{1}{\lambda}
$$

**Memoryless Property — the Signature of the Exponential Model:**

$$
P(T > t+s \mid T > t) = P(T > s) = e^{-\lambda s}
$$

This means the future of a working unit is statistically independent of its past — a directly examinable property in KTU numericals.

## 3.3 Derivation: Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF)

For a *repaired* system that cycles between Uptime and Downtime, let:

- $T_u$ = operating time between two consecutive failures
- $T_d$ = downtime (time to repair)

Then:

$$
T_{\text{cycle}} = T_u + T_d
$$

The expected cycle length is:

$$
E[T_{\text{cycle}}] = E[T_u] + E[T_d] = \text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}
$$

The **steady-state availability** is the fraction of long-run time the system is up:

$$
A = \frac{E[T_u]}{E[T_u] + E[T_d]} = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}
$$

Rearranging to solve for **MTBF** in terms of availability and MTTR (a favorite KTU step):

$$
\text{MTBF} = \frac{A \cdot \text{MTTR}}{1 - A}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification of limiting cases:** As MTTR $\to 0$ (perfect repair), $A \to 1$. As $\lambda \to 0$ (perfect components), MTBF $\to \infty$, again $A \to 1$. Both limits check out.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical — Typical KTU Style

**Problem:** A system has a constant failure rate of $\lambda = 0.0025$ failures per hour. Compute (a) the reliability for a 100-hour mission, (b) the MTTF, (c) the MTBF if the MTTR is 8 hours, and (d) the steady-state availability.

**Solution:**

**(a)** Apply the exponential model:

$$
R(100) = e^{-\lambda \cdot 100} = e^{-0.0025 \times 100} = e^{-0.25}
$$

Evaluating:

$$
R(100) \approx 0.7788
$$

**(b)** MTTF:

$$
\text{MTTF} = \frac{1}{\lambda} = \frac{1}{0.0025} = 400 \text{ hours}
$$

**(c)** MTBF:

$$
\text{MTBF} = \text{MTTF} + \text{MTTR} = 400 + 8 = 408 \text{ hours}
$$

**(d)** Availability:

$$
A = \frac{408}{408 + 8} = \frac{408}{416} \approx 0.9808
$$

So the system is up **~98.08 %** of the long-run time. This numerical style is the **canonical KTU 14-mark problem**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Bath-Tub Curve — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[START OF LIFE] --> B[PHASE I INFANT MORTALITY]
    B --> C[PHASE II USEFUL LIFE]
    C --> D[PHASE III WEAR OUT]
    D --> E[END OF LIFE]

    B -.-> B1[Decreasing Lambda]
    C -.-> C1[Constant Lambda]
    D -.-> D1[Increasing Lambda]

    B1 --> F[BURN IN STRATEGY]
    C1 --> G[CORRECTIVE MAINTENANCE]
    D1 --> H[SCHEDULED REPLACEMENT]
```

**How to read this block:** Each phase flows from left to right; the dashed branches labeled `B1`, `C1`, `D1` describe the characteristic hazard shape, and the lower branches describe the matching engineering strategy.

## 4.2 Failure Mode Classification Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ROOT[FAILURE OF ITEM]
    ROOT --> M1[MECHANICAL]
    ROOT --> M2[ELECTRICAL]
    ROOT --> M3[THERMAL]
    ROOT --> M4[CHEMICAL]
    ROOT --> M5[LOGICAL OR HUMAN]

    M1 --> M1A[Fracture]
    M1 --> M1B[Fatigue]
    M1 --> M1C[Wear]
    M1 --> M1D[Creep]

    M2 --> M2A[Open Circuit]
    M2 --> M2B[Short Circuit]
    M2 --> M2C[Dielectric Breakdown]

    M3 --> M3A[Overheat]
    M3 --> M3B[Thermal Runaway]

    M4 --> M4A[Corrosion]
    M4 --> M4B[Oxidation]

    M5 --> M5A[Operator Error]
    M5 --> M5B[Software Bug]
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — From Failure Event to Reliability Metric

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    INPUT[PHYSICAL STRESS INPUT] --> DETECT[FAILURE MODE MECHANISM]
    DETECT --> EVENT[FAILURE EVENT OCCURS]
    EVENT --> RECORD[RECORD TIME T]
    RECORD --> STAT[COMPUTE F T R T LAMBDA T MTBF]

    STAT --> DECIDE{MISSION CRITICAL?}
    DECIDE -->|YES| REDESIGN[ADD REDUNDANCY]
    DECIDE -->|NO| ACCEPT[OPERATE WITH MAINTENANCE]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to draw this in an exam:** Even if you cannot sketch a physical diagram, presenting the **input → mechanism → event → metric → decision** block flow earns full marks in KTU descriptive answers.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Define the terms (i) failure, (ii) failure mode, and (iii) failure rate. How are they interrelated?**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Failure:** It is the event in which a component or system is no longer able to perform its intended function within the prescribed performance limits. Mathematically, it is the transition of the state $X(t)$ from 1 (working) to 0 (failed) at some random time $T$.

**(ii) Failure Mode:** It is the *manner* or *mechanism* by which a failure manifests — for example, open-circuit, short-circuit, fracture, wear, or thermal runaway. A single failure event may have multiple possible modes.

**(iii) Failure Rate $\lambda(t)$:** It is the instantaneous rate of failure, conditional on the item having survived to time $t$:

$$
\lambda(t) = \frac{f(t)}{R(t)}
$$

**Interrelation:** The failure rate is derived from the *density* of the failure time $T$, which is itself the probabilistic description of the failure event. The shape of $\lambda(t)$ versus $t$ categorizes the failure into one of the **failure-mode regimes** — decreasing (infant mortality), constant (random), or increasing (wear-out).

---

### **Question 2.** `[KTU University Exam – Jun 2023]`

**Explain the bath-tub curve. Why is preventive maintenance ineffective during the useful-life phase?**

**Model Answer:**

The **bath-tub curve** is a plot of failure rate $\lambda(t)$ versus time, exhibiting three distinct regions:
1. **Infant-mortality region:** $\lambda(t)$ decreases with time; failures due to manufacturing defects.
2. **Useful-life region:** $\lambda(t)$ is approximately constant; failures are random and independent of time.
3. **Wear-out region:** $\lambda(t)$ increases with time; failures due to aging and degradation.

**Why preventive maintenance fails in the useful-life phase:**
During this phase, failures occur randomly in time and are not predictable from the age or usage of the component. Since preventive maintenance replaces components at scheduled intervals regardless of their actual condition, it cannot reduce a failure mechanism that has no time-dependence. The optimal strategy here is **corrective maintenance** — repairing the unit only after it fails.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Essay Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU ESE Rule:** You must answer **either Question A or Question B**. Each carries 14 marks, split into two sub-parts of 7 marks each.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks).** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]`

**(a)** Classify the different **failure modes** encountered in engineering systems. Give two real-world examples for each class. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A non-repairable component has the following PDF of failure:

$$
f(t) = \dfrac{1}{200} e^{-t/200}, \quad t \ge 0
$$

Find (i) the reliability for a 100-hour mission, (ii) the failure rate at $t = 100$ h, and (iii) the MTTF. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A

**Part (a) — Classification with Examples (7 marks)**

A clean table is the board-preferred format. Full marks are awarded for **at least four classes** with **two examples each**.

| Failure Class | Description | Example 1 | Example 2 |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Catastrophic** | Sudden, total loss of function | Shaft fracture in motor | Transformer winding open |
| **Degradation** | Gradual drift across a threshold | Bearing wear | Capacitor ESR increase |
| **Overstress** | Stress exceeds material strength | Lightning-induced overvoltage | Mechanical overload of a crane hook |
| **Intermittent** | Recurring under specific conditions | Loose PCB connector | Cold solder joint |
| **Inherent / Design** | Caused by design flaw itself | Undersized heat sink | Wrong alloy selection |
| **Environmental** | Driven by ambient conditions | Salt-spray corrosion | Humidity-induced PCB leakage |

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Identify the distribution.** Comparing $f(t)$ with the general exponential form $f(t) = \lambda e^{-\lambda t}$:

$$
\lambda = \frac{1}{200} \text{ failures per hour}
$$

**Step 2 — Reliability at $t = 100$ h.**

$$
R(100) = \int_{100}^{\infty} f(t)\,dt = e^{-100/200} = e^{-0.5}
$$

$$
\boxed{R(100) \approx 0.6065}
$$

**Step 3 — Failure rate at $t = 100$ h.** For the exponential law, the failure rate is *constant*:

$$
\lambda(100) = \lambda = \frac{1}{200} = 0.005 \text{ failures/hour}
$$

Equivalently, in **FIT units** (Failures In Time, per $10^9$ device-hours):

$$
\lambda = 0.005 \times 10^{9} = 5 \times 10^{6} \text{ FIT}
$$

**Step 4 — MTTF.**

$$
\text{MTTF} = \frac{1}{\lambda} = 200 \text{ hours}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Part b):** A common error is to compute $\lambda(t)$ as a *function* of $t$ (treating it like a Weibull hazard). For the **exponential** model, $\lambda(t)$ is **constant by definition** — students who write $\lambda(100) = \dfrac{f(100)}{R(100)} = 0.005/0.6065$ will lose 2 marks. Both methods yield the same constant value, but writing the proper constant-form justification is what earns full credit.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice).** `[KTU University Exam – Jun 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the **memoryless property** of the exponential distribution. Why does it make the exponential model suitable for representing random failures in the useful-life phase? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a repairable system with $\text{MTTF} = 1500$ hours and $\text{MTTR} = 12$ hours, determine (i) MTBF, (ii) steady-state availability, and (iii) the maximum allowable MTTR if the availability must be at least 0.995. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B

**Part (a) — Memoryless Property (7 marks)**

**Statement:** A random variable $T$ is memoryless if:

$$
P(T > t+s \mid T > t) = P(T > s) \quad \text{for all } t, s \ge 0
$$

**Derivation for the exponential distribution:**

$$
P(T > t+s \mid T > t) = \frac{P(T > t+s)}{P(T > t)} = \frac{e^{-\lambda(t+s)}}{e^{-\lambda t}} = e^{-\lambda s}
$$

The right-hand side depends **only on $s$**, not on $t$. Thus, the past survival time carries no information about future survival — the distribution "forgets" the history of the component.

**Why this suits the useful-life phase:**
During Phase II of the bath-tub curve, failures are random and time-independent — they arise from stochastic shocks (voltage spikes, random overloads, dust ingress, etc.) rather than accumulated damage. This physical picture matches the **mathematical** memorylessness of the exponential law. Hence, for items in their useful life, $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ and $\lambda = \text{const}$ is the correct model.

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks)**

**(i) MTBF:**

$$
\text{MTBF} = \text{MTTF} + \text{MTTR} = 1500 + 12 = \boxed{1512 \text{ hours}}
$$

**(ii) Steady-state availability:**

$$
A = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}} = \frac{1512}{1512 + 12} = \frac{1512}{1524}
$$

$$
\boxed{A \approx 0.9921}
$$

**(iii) Maximum allowable MTTR for $A \ge 0.995$:**

Set up the inequality:

$$
\frac{1512}{1512 + \text{MTTR}} \ge 0.995
$$

Cross-multiply (note the denominator is positive):

$$
1512 \ge 0.995 \times (1512 + \text{MTTR})
$$

$$
1512 \ge 1504.44 + 0.995 \cdot \text{MTTR}
$$

$$
0.995 \cdot \text{MTTR} \le 1512 - 1504.44 = 7.56
$$

$$
\text{MTTR} \le \frac{7.56}{0.995}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{MTTR} \le 7.597 \text{ hours}}
$$

So the maintenance crew must, on average, restore the system in **under ~7.6 hours** to meet the 99.5 % availability target.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Part b):** A common mistake is to compute MTBF as $\text{MTTF}$ alone. In KTU's definition, MTBF is **strictly the sum** of mean operating time and mean repair time. A student writing $\text{MTBF} = 1500$ hours loses 2 marks immediately. Also, do **not** use MTTF directly in the availability formula — always use MTBF in the numerator when the system is repaired.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist**

- **Failure** = loss of required function. **Failure Mode** = the *how* of that loss. Distinguish them in every answer.
- **Bath-tub curve** has **three regions**: infant mortality (decreasing $\lambda$), useful life (constant $\lambda$), wear-out (increasing $\lambda$).
- **Five core failure modes**: catastrophic, degradation, overstress, intermittent, inherent/design.
- **Universal failure-rate integral**: $\displaystyle R(t) = \exp\!\left[-\int_{0}^{t}\lambda(\tau)\,d\tau\right]$.
- **Exponential law** (Phase II): $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$, $\lambda = \text{const}$, MTTF $= 1/\lambda$.
- **Memoryless property** holds **only** for the exponential distribution.
- **MTBF** = MTTF + MTTR (for repaired systems). **Availability** $A = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$.
- **FIT** (Failures In Time) $= \lambda \times 10^{9}$ — used in semiconductor reliability.
- **Preventive maintenance** is effective in Phase I (burn-in) and Phase III (scheduled replacement), but **not** in Phase II.
- **FMEA** (Failure Mode and Effects Analysis) is the *industrial application* of classifying failure modes — frequently referenced in KTU 14-mark answers.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Causes of Failures and Unreliability
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET632 - RELIABILITY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Causes of Failures and Unreliability

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Failure?

In the context of **Reliability Engineering (PEIET632)**, the formal KTU definition of **failure** is:

> **Failure:** The termination of the ability of an item to perform its required function. In other words, a system, component, or part is said to have *failed* when it can no longer meet the specified performance criteria within the design tolerances.

A failure is, therefore, not just a "breakdown" — it is a **deviation from the intended functional state** defined at the design stage. This can include:
- Complete stoppage (catastrophic failure)
- Gradual degradation of performance
- Out-of-specification output (degradation failure)

> [!NOTE]
> **Key KTU Highlight:** A failure is defined **relative to the design specification**, not relative to the user. If a pump delivers 90 L/min but the specification demanded 100 L/min, it has *failed*, even if it is still pumping water.

## 1.2 What is Unreliability?

**Unreliability** is the formal complement of **Reliability**.

> **Reliability (R(t)):** The probability that a system, component, or device will perform its intended function without failure for a specified period of time, under stated operating conditions.

> **Unreliability (F(t)):** The probability that the system, component, or device will *fail* within the specified time period, under stated operating conditions.

Mathematically:

$$R(t) + F(t) = 1$$

Where:
- $R(t)$ = Reliability function (probability of *survival* up to time $t$)
- $F(t)$ = Cumulative Distribution Function (CDF) of failure, also called unreliability

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Bucket of Life"

> [!TIP]
> **Intuition Box — The "Battery Bucket" Analogy**
> Imagine every component is born with a **bucket of useful life** at the factory.
> - The **bucket starts full** ($R(0) = 1$).
> - Every second the component runs, a drop leaks out (failure probability accumulates).
> - When the bucket **drains to zero**, the component fails ($F(t) = 1$).
> - **Reliability** = how much water is left in the bucket at time $t$.
> - **Unreliability** = how much water has *drained out* by time $t$.
> - **Failure rate ($\lambda$)** = the *speed* at which drops leak out — which itself changes with the bucket's condition (early life vs. old age).

This is precisely why unreliability is not a fixed number — it depends on **time, stress, and design quality**.

## 1.4 Standard Reliability Metrics (KTU Mandatory Vocabulary)

| Metric | Symbol | Definition | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Reliability | $R(t)$ | Probability of no failure up to time $t$ | Dimensionless (probability) |
| Unreliability | $F(t)$ | Probability of failure by time $t$ | Dimensionless (probability) |
| Probability Density of Failure | $f(t)$ | Instantaneous likelihood of failure at time $t$ | per unit time (e.g., 1/hr) |
| Failure Rate (Hazard Rate) | $\lambda(t)$ | Conditional rate of failure, given survival to $t$ | Failures per unit time (FITs = $10^{-9}$/hr) |
| Mean Time To Failure | **MTTF** | Expected time to failure (non-repairable) | Hours, cycles |
| Mean Time Between Failures | **MTBF** | Expected time between failures (repairable) | Hours, cycles |
| Mean Time To Repair | **MTTR** | Average downtime to restore a failed system | Hours |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard KTU Convention:** For **non-repairable** items (resistors, ICs, bearings), use **MTTF**. For **repairable** systems (aircraft, generators, production lines), use **MTBF = MTTF + MTTR**. This distinction is frequently tested in KTU 2024 scheme exams.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Causes of Failure (KTU Syllabus Focus)

The causes of failure can be classified into **four primary domains**. KTU 2024 examiners expect you to know the boundaries of each.

### 2.1.1 Hardware-Related Causes
These are physical/mechanical/electrical degradation mechanisms.
- **Overstress failure:** Load, voltage, temperature, or pressure exceeds the design limit.
- **Wear-out failure:** Gradual degradation (corrosion, fatigue, creep, wear).
- **Material defects:** Inclusions, voids, impurities, improper alloying.
- **Manufacturing defects:** Improper machining, wrong dimensions, bad solder joints.
- **Assembly errors:** Loose bolts, misaligned gears, wrong cable routing.

### 2.1.2 Software-Related Causes
- Specification errors
- Coding bugs (logic errors, memory leaks)
- Integration failures
- Inadequate testing
- Compatibility issues with hardware

### 2.1.3 Human-Related Causes
- Operator error (40–60% of industrial failures, per industry data)
- Design error (engineering mistakes)
- Maintenance error
- Procedural non-compliance

### 2.1.4 Environmental Causes
- Temperature extremes
- Humidity, dust, corrosion
- Vibration, shock
- Electromagnetic interference (EMI)
- Power supply variations (surges, sags)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** Environmental factors are *external stressors* that accelerate the *internal* hardware failure mechanisms. They do not cause new types of failure — they amplify existing ones.

## 2.2 The Bathtub Curve — The Heart of Module 1

The **Bathtub Curve** is the single most important diagram in reliability engineering and is virtually guaranteed to appear in KTU 2024 ESE.

The curve plots **failure rate $\lambda(t)$** vs. **time $t$**, and it has three regions:

### Region I — Infant Mortality (Burn-in / Early Life Failures)
- **Cause:** Manufacturing defects, design oversights, substandard components, poor quality control.
- **Characteristic:** **Decreasing** failure rate.
- **Mitigation:** **Burn-in testing** — run the product for a fixed time before shipping to eliminate weak units.

### Region II — Useful Life (Random Failures)
- **Cause:** Random, unpredictable external events (surges, operator error, accidents).
- **Characteristic:** **Constant** failure rate $\lambda$ — this is the basis of the **Exponential Distribution**.
- **Mitigation:** Preventive maintenance, redundancy, derating.

### Region III — Wear-out (End of Life)
- **Cause:** Cumulative physical wear — fatigue, corrosion, creep, aging.
- **Characteristic:** **Increasing** failure rate.
- **Mitigation:** Scheduled replacement, condition-based maintenance.

## 2.3 The Stress–Strength Interference Concept

A very common KTU Module 1 problem involves **Stress–Strength Interference**.

> **Reliability exists only when Strength $>$ Stress at every point in time.**

$$\text{Reliable} \iff S(t) > \sigma(t) \quad \forall t$$

Where:
- $S(t)$ = Strength of the component (decreases with time due to wear)
- $\sigma(t)$ = Applied stress (load, voltage, temperature) — can be random

**Failure occurs** in the small region where the strength distribution and stress distribution *overlap* (interfere). This overlap area is the **probability of failure**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** Components are designed with a **safety factor** (typically 1.5 to 4) to ensure that even under worst-case stress combined with minimum strength, failure probability remains negligible (e.g., $< 10^{-6}$).

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) — Module 1

> [!NOTE]
> **EXAM ALERT:** All formulas in this table are examinable in Part A or Part B of the KTU ESE. Avoid using the `|` symbol in any cell — we use `\vert` instead, per the system constraint.

| # | Formula | Description | Domain / When to Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $R(t) + F(t) = 1$ | Complementarity of reliability and unreliability | Always valid |
| 2 | $F(t) = 1 - R(t)$ | Unreliability from reliability | Always valid |
| 3 | $f(t) = \dfrac{dF(t)}{dt} = -\dfrac{dR(t)}{dt}$ | Probability density of failure | Always valid |
| 4 | $\lambda(t) = \dfrac{f(t)}{R(t)}$ | Hazard rate / instantaneous failure rate | Always valid |
| 5 | $R(t) = e^{-\int_0^t \lambda(\tau)\,d\tau}$ | General reliability function | For time-varying failure rate |
| 6 | $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ | Exponential reliability (constant $\lambda$) | Useful life region (Region II) |
| 7 | $F(t) = 1 - e^{-\lambda t}$ | Exponential unreliability | Useful life region (Region II) |
| 8 | $f(t) = \lambda e^{-\lambda t}$ | Exponential failure density | Useful life region (Region II) |
| 9 | $\text{MTTF} = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}$ | Mean time to failure | Exponential / Region II |
| 10 | $\text{MTBF} = \text{MTTF} + \text{MTTR}$ | For repairable systems | Repairable systems |
| 11 | $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t^{\beta}}$ | Weibull reliability (2-parameter) | Wear-out and infant mortality |
| 12 | $\lambda(t) = \dfrac{\beta t^{\beta - 1}}{\alpha^{\beta}}$ | Weibull failure rate | Variable failure rate |
| 13 | $F(t) = 1 - e^{-(t/\alpha)^{\beta}}$ | Weibull CDF | Variable failure rate |
| 14 | $P_f = P(\sigma > S) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f_S(s)\, F_\sigma(s)\, ds$ | Probability of failure via stress-strength interference | Stress-strength problems |
| 15 | $A = \dfrac{\mu_S - \mu_\sigma}{\sqrt{\sigma_S^2 + \sigma_\sigma^2}}$ | Safety / reliability index $\beta$ (Cornell's formulation) | Interference problems |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace:** MTBF of flight control systems is monitored continuously; a single failure can be catastrophic.
- **Nuclear Power:** Failure rate of emergency core cooling systems must be $< 10^{-6}$/demand — leading to extensive redundancy and derating.
- **Electronics Manufacturing:** Burn-in testing (Region I of bathtub) rejects infant-mortality failures before shipping to customers.
- **Automotive:** Scheduled replacement of timing belts prevents wear-out (Region III) failures.
- **Telecommunications:** MTBF targets of $> 100{,}000$ hours for central-office switches drive use of highly redundant architectures.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: From Hazard Rate $\lambda(t)$ to Reliability $R(t)$

This is the **master derivation** of reliability theory and is mandatory for KTU 2024 Part B questions.

**Starting point:** Definition of hazard rate (failure rate)

$$\lambda(t) = \frac{f(t)}{R(t)}$$

**Step 1:** Recall the relationship between the PDF and the CDF of failure.

$$f(t) = \frac{dF(t)}{dt}$$

**Step 2:** Since $R(t) + F(t) = 1$, differentiate both sides with respect to $t$.

$$\frac{dR(t)}{dt} + \frac{dF(t)}{dt} = 0$$

**Step 3:** Substitute the derivative of $F(t)$.

$$\frac{dR(t)}{dt} + f(t) = 0$$

**Step 4:** Solve for $f(t)$.

$$f(t) = -\frac{dR(t)}{dt}$$

**Step 5:** Substitute this into the hazard rate definition.

$$\lambda(t) = \frac{-\dfrac{dR(t)}{dt}}{R(t)}$$

**Step 6:** Rearrange into a separable differential equation.

$$\lambda(t)\,dt = -\frac{dR(t)}{R(t)}$$

**Step 7:** Integrate both sides from time $0$ (where $R(0) = 1$, i.e., the item is new and working) up to time $t$.

$$\int_0^t \lambda(\tau)\,d\tau = -\int_0^t \frac{dR(\tau)}{R(\tau)} = -\big[\ln R(\tau)\big]_0^t = -\ln R(t) + \ln R(0)$$

**Step 8:** Apply the initial condition $R(0) = 1 \Rightarrow \ln R(0) = 0$.

$$\int_0^t \lambda(\tau)\,d\tau = -\ln R(t)$$

**Step 9:** Exponentiate both sides to obtain the **general reliability equation**.

$$R(t) = \exp\!\left(-\int_0^t \lambda(\tau)\,d\tau\right)$$

> **Verification:** For a constant failure rate $\lambda(t) = \lambda$ (Region II of the bathtub curve), the integral becomes simply $\lambda t$, recovering the well-known **exponential reliability formula** $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$. $\blacksquare$

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Unreliability for Constant Failure Rate (Region II)

**Step 1:** Take $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ (from Derivation 1 with constant $\lambda$).

**Step 2:** Apply the complementarity identity $F(t) = 1 - R(t)$.

$$F(t) = 1 - e^{-\lambda t}$$

**Step 3:** Differentiate to get the PDF.

$$f(t) = \frac{dF(t)}{dt} = \lambda e^{-\lambda t}$$

**Step 4:** Compute the mean (MTTF) by integrating $t \cdot f(t)$ from $0$ to $\infty$.

$$\text{MTTF} = \int_0^{\infty} t\,f(t)\,dt = \int_0^{\infty} t \lambda e^{-\lambda t}\,dt$$

**Step 5:** Use integration by parts. Let $u = t$, $dv = \lambda e^{-\lambda t}dt$; then $du = dt$, $v = -e^{-\lambda t}$.

$$\text{MTTF} = \left[-t e^{-\lambda t}\right]_0^{\infty} + \int_0^{\infty} e^{-\lambda t}\,dt = 0 + \left[-\frac{1}{\lambda} e^{-\lambda t}\right]_0^{\infty} = \frac{1}{\lambda}$$

> **Conclusion:** For a constant failure rate, $\text{MTTF} = 1/\lambda$. A higher failure rate means *shorter* expected life. $\blacksquare$

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Stress–Strength Probability of Failure

Let $S$ = strength random variable with PDF $f_S(s)$ and CDF $F_S(s)$.
Let $\sigma$ = stress random variable with PDF $f_\sigma(\sigma)$ and CDF $F_\sigma(\sigma)$.

**Step 1:** The probability of failure is the probability that stress exceeds strength.

$$P_f = P(\sigma > S)$$

**Step 2:** Use the law of total probability, conditioning on the value of $S = s$.

$$P_f = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} P(\sigma > s \mid S = s) \cdot f_S(s)\,ds$$

**Step 3:** Note that $P(\sigma > s) = 1 - F_\sigma(s)$.

$$P_f = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \big(1 - F_\sigma(s)\big) f_S(s)\,ds$$

**Step 4:** This is the general (exact) form. For the common KTU case where both $S$ and $\sigma$ are **normally distributed** with means $\mu_S, \mu_\sigma$ and standard deviations $\sigma_S, \sigma_\sigma$:

$$P_f = 1 - \Phi(\beta)$$

where $\Phi$ is the standard normal CDF and the **reliability index** $\beta$ is:

$$\beta = \frac{\mu_S - \mu_\sigma}{\sqrt{\sigma_S^2 + \sigma_\sigma^2}}$$

> **Interpretation:** A larger $\beta$ means greater separation between strength and stress distributions, hence lower failure probability. Designers aim for $\beta \geq 4$ in critical systems. $\blacksquare$

## 3.4 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Exam-Style)

> **Problem:** A batch of 1000 LEDs is observed for 5000 hours. 200 LEDs fail during this period. Assuming constant failure rate, find:
> (a) The failure rate $\lambda$.
> (b) The reliability at $t = 2000$ hours.
> (c) The MTTF.
> (d) The probability of survival up to 8000 hours.

**Solution:**

**Part (a):** Using the exponential unreliability $F(t) = 1 - e^{-\lambda t}$, with $F(5000) = 200/1000 = 0.2$:

$$0.2 = 1 - e^{-5000\lambda}$$

$$e^{-5000\lambda} = 0.8$$

$$\lambda = -\frac{\ln(0.8)}{5000} = \frac{0.22314}{5000} = 4.463 \times 10^{-5}\,/\text{hr}$$

**Part (b):** $R(2000) = e^{-\lambda \cdot 2000} = e^{-0.08926} = 0.9146$

**Part (c):** $\text{MTTF} = 1/\lambda = 1 / (4.463 \times 10^{-5}) = 22{,}402$ hours

**Part (d):** $R(8000) = e^{-\lambda \cdot 8000} = e^{-0.3570} = 0.6997 \approx 0.70$

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** Always keep $\lambda$ in your calculator until the end to avoid rounding errors. KTU examiners often give 1–2 marks specifically for the final numerical answer.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Bathtub Curve — Failure Rate vs. Time

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Time t equals 0] --> B[Region I: Infant Mortality]
    B --> C[Region II: Useful Life]
    C --> D[Region III: Wear Out]
    D --> E[Time increases further]
    B --> B1[Decreasing failure rate]
    C --> C1[Constant failure rate lambda]
    D --> D1[Increasing failure rate]
    B1 --> C1
    C1 --> D1
```

## 4.2 Hierarchy of Failure Causes

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[CAUSES OF FAILURE] --> B[HARDWARE]
    A --> C[SOFTWARE]
    A --> D[HUMAN]
    A --> E[ENVIRONMENT]
    B --> B1[Overstress]
    B --> B2[Wear Out]
    B --> B3[Manufacturing Defects]
    B --> B4[Material Defects]
    C --> C1[Specification Errors]
    C --> C2[Coding Bugs]
    C --> C3[Integration Failures]
    D --> D1[Operator Error]
    D --> D2[Design Error]
    D --> D3[Maintenance Error]
    E --> E1[Temperature]
    E --> E2[Humidity]
    E --> E3[Vibration]
    E --> E4[EMI and Power Surges]
```

## 4.3 Stress–Strength Interference Schematic

```mermaid
graph LR
    S1[Strength Distribution centered at mu S] --> S2[S greater than sigma means SAFE]
    Sg1[Stress Distribution centered at mu sigma] --> S2
    S2 --> S3[Reliability Region: No Overlap]
    Sg1 --> Sg2[Overlap Zone]
    S1 --> Sg2
    Sg2 --> Sg3[Failure Probability equals P of sigma greater than S]
```

## 4.4 Flow from Defect to System Failure

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Inherent Design Weakness] --> B[Manufacturing Defect]
    B --> C[Latent Defect in Component]
    C --> D[Applied Stress activates defect]
    D --> E{Threshold Exceeded?}
    E -->|Yes| F[Component Failure]
    E -->|No| G[Component Survives]
    F --> H[System Level Failure]
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Define the terms: (i) Reliability, (ii) Unreliability, (iii) Failure Rate.**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Reliability $R(t)$:** The probability that a system, component, or device will perform its intended function without failure under stated operating conditions for a specified period of time $t$.

**(ii) Unreliability $F(t)$:** The probability that the system, component, or device will fail within the specified time $t$ under stated operating conditions. By definition, $F(t) = 1 - R(t)$.

**(iii) Failure Rate $\lambda(t)$:** The instantaneous rate at which failures occur, given that the item has survived up to time $t$. It is the conditional probability density of failure divided by reliability:

$$\lambda(t) = \frac{f(t)}{R(t)}$$

It is measured in failures per unit time (commonly in **FITs = $10^{-9}$ failures/hour** in electronics).

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Explain the three regions of the bathtub curve with examples of failure causes in each region.**

**Model Answer:**

The bathtub curve plots failure rate $\lambda(t)$ versus time $t$ and has three distinct regions:

**Region I – Infant Mortality (Early Life):** Failure rate is **decreasing**. Cause: manufacturing defects, substandard components, poor quality control, design oversights. Example: defective solder joints in a newly manufactured PCB failing within the first few hours. Mitigated by **burn-in testing**.

**Region II – Useful Life:** Failure rate is **constant**. Cause: random, unpredictable external events such as voltage surges, operator misuse, accidents. Example: a hard drive crashing due to a random power surge. This region is modeled by the **exponential distribution**.

**Region III – Wear-out:** Failure rate is **increasing**. Cause: cumulative physical degradation – fatigue, corrosion, creep, aging. Example: bearings of an electric motor wearing out after several thousand hours. Mitigated by **scheduled replacement**.

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**(a)** Derive the general expression for the reliability function $R(t)$ in terms of the failure rate $\lambda(t)$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The constant failure rate of a particular industrial pump is $0.0002$ failures per hour. Calculate: (i) the reliability for a 1000-hour mission, (ii) the unreliability, (iii) the MTTF, and (iv) the probability that the pump will fail between 1000 and 2000 hours of operation. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks

The full step-by-step derivation is presented in **SECTION 3.1** of these notes. For the answer sheet, the student should present:

**Step 1:** Start from the definition of hazard rate.

$$\lambda(t) = \frac{f(t)}{R(t)}$$

**Step 2:** Use $f(t) = -\dfrac{dR(t)}{dt}$ (since $F(t) + R(t) = 1$).

**Step 3:** Form the differential equation and separate variables.

$$\lambda(t)\,dt = -\frac{dR(t)}{R(t)}$$

**Step 4:** Integrate from $0$ to $t$ using $R(0) = 1$.

$$\int_0^t \lambda(\tau)\,d\tau = -\ln R(t)$$

**Step 5:** Exponentiate to obtain the **master reliability equation**.

$$R(t) = \exp\!\left(-\int_0^t \lambda(\tau)\,d\tau\right)$$

For constant failure rate $\lambda(t) = \lambda$, this reduces to the exponential form $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$.

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks

**Given:** $\lambda = 0.0002$/hr.

**(i) Reliability for $t = 1000$ hours:**

$$R(1000) = e^{-0.0002 \times 1000} = e^{-0.2} = 0.8187$$

**(ii) Unreliability for $t = 1000$ hours:**

$$F(1000) = 1 - 0.8187 = 0.1813$$

**(iii) MTTF:**

$$\text{MTTF} = \frac{1}{\lambda} = \frac{1}{0.0002} = 5000\text{ hours}$$

**(iv) Probability of failure between 1000 and 2000 hours:**

The pump must survive to 1000 hours AND fail between 1000 and 2000 hours.

$$P(1000 < T < 2000) = F(2000) - F(1000)$$

$$= (1 - e^{-0.4}) - (1 - e^{-0.2})$$

$$= e^{-0.2} - e^{-0.4}$$

$$= 0.8187 - 0.6703 = 0.1484$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**(a)** With a neat sketch, describe the **bathtub curve** and explain the causes and remedies for failures in each of its three regions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **stress–strength interference** concept in reliability engineering. A component has strength following a normal distribution with mean 200 MPa and standard deviation 10 MPa. The applied stress follows a normal distribution with mean 120 MPa and standard deviation 8 MPa. Compute the reliability index $\beta$ and the probability of failure. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks

The student must draw a **bathtub-shaped curve** with three labeled regions (see SECTION 4.1 for the schematic).

**Region I — Infant Mortality (decreasing $\lambda$):**
- Causes: Manufacturing defects, substandard components, design flaws not caught in inspection, contamination.
- Remedies: **Burn-in testing** (operate the product for a prescribed period before shipping), improved quality control, supplier audits, statistical process control.

**Region II — Useful Life (constant $\lambda$):**
- Causes: Random shocks and stresses — voltage surges, environmental spikes, operator errors, accidents.
- Remedies: Preventive maintenance, **redundancy** (parallel/standby), **derating** (operating below rated stress).

**Region III — Wear-out (increasing $\lambda$):**
- Causes: Cumulative degradation — mechanical wear, corrosion, fatigue, creep, dielectric breakdown, material aging.
- Remedies: **Scheduled replacement** of components at design life, **condition-based maintenance** using vibration analysis, oil analysis, thermography.

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks

**Stress–Strength Interference Concept:**

Reliability requires that the **strength** of a component always exceeds the **applied stress**. As both strength and stress are random in practice, the probability of failure is determined by the area of overlap of the two probability distributions. Reducing the overlap (by reducing variability or by increasing the safety margin) directly improves reliability.

**Calculation:**

Given:
- Strength: $S \sim N(\mu_S = 200, \sigma_S = 10)$ MPa
- Stress: $\sigma \sim N(\mu_\sigma = 120, \sigma_\sigma = 8)$ MPa

**Step 1: Reliability index $\beta$:**

$$\beta = \frac{\mu_S - \mu_\sigma}{\sqrt{\sigma_S^2 + \sigma_\sigma^2}} = \frac{200 - 120}{\sqrt{10^2 + 8^2}} = \frac{80}{\sqrt{100 + 64}} = \frac{80}{\sqrt{164}} = \frac{80}{12.806} = 6.247$$

**Step 2: Probability of failure:**

$$P_f = 1 - \Phi(\beta) = 1 - \Phi(6.247)$$

Using standard normal tables: $\Phi(6.247) \approx 1 - 2.1 \times 10^{-10} \approx 0.99999999979$

$$P_f \approx 2.1 \times 10^{-10}$$

**Step 3: Reliability:**

$$R = 1 - P_f \approx 0.99999999979 \approx 0.9999999998$$

> **Conclusion:** The component has a very high safety margin ($\beta > 6$) and extremely low probability of failure ($\approx 2 \times 10^{-10}$). The design is excellent for safety-critical applications.

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes KTU Students Make (Lose 1–3 Marks Each):**
> 1. **Forgetting units** for failure rate and MTTF — examiners specifically deduct 0.5–1 mark for missing units.
> 2. **Confusing MTTF and MTBF** — use MTTF for non-repairable, MTBF for repairable. A 1-mark penalty applies for this error.
> 3. **Not specifying the mission time $t$** in reliability problems — the answer $R(t)$ is meaningless without the time $t$.
> 4. **Rounding $\lambda$ prematurely** — keep 4–5 significant digits of $\lambda$ until the final step; rounding at intermediate steps can cause the final answer to deviate by 1–2%, which may exceed KTU's 2% tolerance.
> 5. **Writing the bathtub curve with the wrong axis label** — the **y-axis is failure rate $\lambda(t)$**, not reliability $R(t)$. This is one of the most common KTU errors.
> 6. **In stress-strength problems, students often write $P_f = \Phi(-\beta)$** instead of the correct $1 - \Phi(\beta)$ — this gives the same numerical answer but is logically inconsistent. Stick with the standard form.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Causes of Failures and Unreliability**

- **Definition triad:** Reliability $R(t)$, Unreliability $F(t)$, and Failure Rate $\lambda(t)$ form the **three fundamental metrics** of reliability engineering.
- **Master identity:** $R(t) + F(t) = 1$ — always true, regardless of distribution.
- **Master hazard formula:** $\lambda(t) = f(t)/R(t)$ — links failure rate to reliability.
- **General reliability equation:** $R(t) = \exp\!\left(-\int_0^t \lambda(\tau)\,d\tau\right)$ — the single most important equation in Module 1.
- **Exponential simplification (Region II):** When $\lambda$ is constant, $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$, $\text{MTTF} = 1/\lambda$.
- **Bathtub curve has three regions:** (I) Infant Mortality — burn-in, (II) Useful Life — redundancy, (III) Wear-out — scheduled replacement.
- **MTTF vs MTBF:** MTTF for non-repairable, MTBF for repairable, MTBF $=$ MTTF $+$ MTTR.
- **Failure causes are classified into 4 domains:** Hardware, Software, Human, Environmental — be able to give **one example per domain**.
- **Stress–Strength Interference:** Failure occurs when stress $>$ strength; the overlap area of the two distributions gives $P_f$.
- **Reliability index $\beta$:** $\beta = (\mu_S - \mu_\sigma)/\sqrt{\sigma_S^2 + \sigma_\sigma^2}$; design rule of thumb $\beta \geq 4$ for safety-critical.
- **Examinable Distributions for this module:** Exponential (Region II), Weibull (Regions I and III), Normal (stress-strength).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Reliability Models: Constant Failure Rate Model
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET632 - RELIABILITY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Reliability Models: Constant Failure Rate Model

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Constant Failure Rate Model (CFRM)** is a fundamental probabilistic model in reliability engineering that describes a system or component whose instantaneous failure rate $\lambda(t)$ remains invariant with respect to operating time. Mathematically, this model assumes:

$$\lambda(t) = \lambda = \text{constant}, \quad \forall \, t \geq 0$$

This model is mathematically equivalent to assuming that the time-to-failure $T$ follows an **Exponential Distribution** with parameter $\lambda > 0$. The model is the cornerstone of classical reliability theory and is widely applicable to non-repairable components operating in the **useful life (flat) region** of the bathtub curve.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The Constant Failure Rate Model is one of the most frequently tested topics in Module 1. It is the foundation upon which Series, Parallel, and Complex system reliability models are built. Mastery of this topic is mandatory.

> [!NOTE]
> **Memoryless Property:** The exponential distribution is the *only* continuous distribution that possesses the memoryless property. This means a component that has survived up to time $t$ is "as good as new" — its remaining life distribution does not depend on $t$.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain English)

Imagine you are playing a game of chance with a six-sided die:

- **A regular die** has a *changing* failure rate — once you have rolled it 100 times, you might expect it to wear out soon (fatigue). The probability of it breaking on the next roll increases with usage. This is **not** a constant failure rate.

- **A "magical" die** that magically repairs itself instantaneously after every roll behaves differently. No matter how many times you have rolled it, the chance of it failing on the *next* roll is exactly the same. This is a **Constant Failure Rate Model**.

In engineering terms, consider an **electronic component on a printed circuit board (PCB)** during its useful life:
- The early "infant mortality" failures (burn-in defects) are over.
- The wear-out failures (end-of-life) have not yet begun.
- During this middle period, the component fails due to *random* shocks (voltage spikes, thermal transients), and these random shocks occur at a **constant average rate**.

This is the engineering reality the Constant Failure Rate Model captures.

## 1.3 Standard Reliability Metrics (Bold for Emphasis)

The following **standard reliability metrics** are derived directly from the CFRM:

- **Reliability Function:** $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$
- **Cumulative Distribution Function (CDF):** $F(t) = 1 - e^{-\lambda t}$
- **Failure Density Function (PDF):** $f(t) = \lambda e^{-\lambda t}$
- **Hazard Rate (Instantaneous Failure Rate):** $\lambda(t) = \lambda = \text{const.}$
- **Mean Time To Failure (MTTF):** $\text{MTTF} = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Physical Constants:** The failure rate $\lambda$ has units of *failures per unit time*, typically denoted as $\text{hr}^{-1}$ or $10^{-6}$ hr$^{-1}$ (FIT — Failures In Time). The reciprocal $1/\lambda$ has units of time (hours).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic: Why Constant Failure Rate Implies Exponential Time-to-Failure

The derivation of the reliability function from the constant failure rate assumption proceeds in **three logical steps**:

**Step 1 — Define the Hazard Function**
The hazard (failure rate) function is defined as the conditional probability of failure in the next infinitesimal interval $[t, t+dt]$, given survival up to $t$:

$$\lambda(t) = \lim_{\Delta t \to 0} \frac{P(t \leq T < t + \Delta t \mid T \geq t)}{\Delta t} = \frac{f(t)}{R(t)}$$

**Step 2 — Set the Hazard Function Constant**
By the central assumption of CFRM:

$$\lambda(t) = \lambda \implies \frac{f(t)}{R(t)} = \lambda \implies f(t) = \lambda \cdot R(t)$$

**Step 3 — Use the Identity $f(t) = -\dfrac{dR(t)}{dt}$**
Since the PDF is the negative derivative of the reliability function:

$$-\frac{dR(t)}{dt} = \lambda \cdot R(t) \implies \frac{dR(t)}{R(t)} = -\lambda \, dt$$

Integrating both sides from $0$ to $t$, with the boundary condition $R(0) = 1$:

$$\ln R(t) = -\lambda t \implies R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Why" Behind the Math:** The constant failure rate assumption is a *postulate* — it cannot be proven theoretically but is **empirically validated** in the useful life region. Physically, this corresponds to failure caused by *random external shocks* (Poisson process), not by internal degradation.

## 2.2 Engineering Utility of the Model

The CFRM is the most heavily used reliability model in industry because of its:

| Engineering Domain | Application of CFRM |
|---|---|
| **Electronics & Semiconductors** | Random voltage spikes, cosmic radiation hits |
| **Aerospace Systems** | Random meteoroid impacts, atmospheric disturbances |
| **Network & Communication Systems** | Random data packet loss, signal interference |
| **Power Systems** | Random lightning strikes, switching transients |
| **Software Reliability** | Random code-path execution failures |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Boundary Condition |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Reliability Function | $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ | Dimensionless | $R(0) = 1$, $R(\infty) = 0$ |
| 2 | Unreliability (CDF) | $F(t) = 1 - e^{-\lambda t}$ | Dimensionless | $F(0) = 0$, $F(\infty) = 1$ |
| 3 | Failure Density (PDF) | $f(t) = \lambda e^{-\lambda t}$ | time$^{-1}$ | $f(t) \geq 0$, $\int_0^{\infty} f(t)\,dt = 1$ |
| 4 | Failure Rate (Hazard) | $\lambda(t) = \lambda$ | time$^{-1}$ | Constant for all $t \geq 0$ |
| 5 | Mean Time To Failure | $\text{MTTF} = 1/\lambda$ | time | $E[T] = 1/\lambda$ |
| 6 | Variance of $T$ | $\text{Var}(T) = 1/\lambda^{2}$ | time$^{2}$ | $\sigma^{2} = 1/\lambda^{2}$ |
| 7 | Median Life ($t_{0.5}$) | $t_{0.5} = \ln 2 / \lambda$ | time | $R(t_{0.5}) = 0.5$ |
| 8 | Mode of $T$ | $t_{\text{mode}} = 0$ | time | Peak of $f(t)$ at $t = 0$ |
| 9 | Conditional Reliability | $R(t \mid T_0) = e^{-\lambda t}$ | Dimensionless | Memoryless |
| 10 | Mission Reliability | $R_{m} = e^{-\lambda t_{m}}$ | Dimensionless | $t_{m}$ = mission time |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall Reminder:** When evaluating $R(t \mid T > t_0)$, students often write $R(t \mid T > t_0) = R(t)$ (correct), but forget the time-shift. The correct form is $R(t_0 + t) = e^{-\lambda(t_0 + t)}$, and the **conditional** probability of surviving an *additional* $t$ units is simply $e^{-\lambda t}$.

## 2.4 The Memoryless Property — Detailed Explanation

The memoryless property states that for any $s, t \geq 0$:

$$P(T > s + t \mid T > s) = P(T > t)$$

**Physical Interpretation:** A component that has already operated for time $s$ without failure has the *same* reliability for the next $t$ hours as a brand-new component. There is **no memory** of past usage.

This property holds **only** for the exponential distribution. It is the mathematical justification for the policy of replacing components either *all at once* or *never* — a strictly periodic mid-life replacement gives no reliability benefit under CFRM.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Reliability Function $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$

**Given:** Constant failure rate $\lambda(t) = \lambda$ for all $t \geq 0$.

**Step 1 — Express the hazard function in terms of $R(t)$.**
The failure rate is defined as:

$$\lambda(t) = \frac{f(t)}{R(t)}$$

where $f(t)$ is the failure density function and $R(t)$ is the reliability function. This means $f(t) = \lambda(t) \cdot R(t)$.

**Step 2 — Use the fundamental identity linking PDF and reliability.**
By definition, the failure density is the negative time-derivative of the reliability function:

$$f(t) = -\frac{dR(t)}{dt}$$

**Step 3 — Equate the two expressions for $f(t)$.**
Since both expressions describe the same quantity, we equate them:

$$-\frac{dR(t)}{dt} = \lambda \cdot R(t)$$

**Step 4 — Separate variables to form an integrable equation.**
Rearranging the equation to isolate $R(t)$ and $t$ on opposite sides:

$$\frac{dR(t)}{R(t)} = -\lambda \, dt$$

**Step 5 — Apply the boundary condition $R(0) = 1$.**
Integrate both sides from the lower limit $0$ to the upper limit $t$. On the left, the integration variable is $R$ from $R(0) = 1$ to $R(t)$. On the right, the integration variable is $t$ from $0$ to $t$:

$$\int_{R(0)=1}^{R(t)} \frac{dR'}{R'} = -\lambda \int_{0}^{t} dt'$$

**Step 6 — Evaluate the integrals.**
The left-hand side is the natural logarithm of $R(t)$, and the right-hand side is $-\lambda t$:

$$\ln R(t) - \ln 1 = -\lambda t$$

**Step 7 — Simplify using $\ln 1 = 0$.**

$$\ln R(t) = -\lambda t$$

**Step 8 — Exponentiate both sides to obtain the reliability function.**

$$R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$$

This is the closed-form reliability function for the Constant Failure Rate Model.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion of Derivation:** The exponential decay of reliability is a direct consequence of the constant instantaneous failure rate assumption. This result is the foundation for all subsequent reliability analysis in the KTU syllabus.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Mean Time To Failure (MTTF)

**Definition of MTTF:** The expected value of the time-to-failure random variable $T$:

$$\text{MTTF} = E[T] = \int_{0}^{\infty} t \cdot f(t) \, dt$$

**Step 1 — Substitute the failure density function.**
Using $f(t) = \lambda e^{-\lambda t}$:

$$\text{MTTF} = \int_{0}^{\infty} t \cdot \lambda e^{-\lambda t} \, dt$$

**Step 2 — Apply integration by parts.**
Let $u = t$ and $dv = \lambda e^{-\lambda t} \, dt$. Then $du = dt$ and $v = -e^{-\lambda t}$:

$$\text{MTTF} = \left[ t \cdot (-e^{-\lambda t}) \right]_{0}^{\infty} - \int_{0}^{\infty} (-e^{-\lambda t}) \, dt$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the boundary term.**
At $t \to \infty$, the term $t \cdot e^{-\lambda t} \to 0$ (exponential decay dominates polynomial growth). At $t = 0$, the term is $0 \cdot (-1) = 0$. Hence:

$$\left[ -t \cdot e^{-\lambda t} \right]_{0}^{\infty} = 0 - 0 = 0$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the remaining integral.**

$$\text{MTTF} = \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-\lambda t} \, dt = \left[ -\frac{1}{\lambda} e^{-\lambda t} \right]_{0}^{\infty} = 0 - \left(-\frac{1}{\lambda}\right) = \frac{1}{\lambda}$$

**Result:**

$$\boxed{\text{MTTF} = \frac{1}{\lambda}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** If a component has a failure rate of $\lambda = 0.001$ failures/hour, its MTTF is exactly 1000 hours. This inverse relationship is a quick mental check that examiners expect students to apply in numerical problems.

## 3.3 Proof of the Memoryless Property

**Statement to prove:** For $s, t \geq 0$:

$$P(T > s + t \mid T > s) = P(T > t) = e^{-\lambda t}$$

**Step 1 — Apply the definition of conditional probability.**

$$P(T > s + t \mid T > s) = \frac{P(T > s + t \text{ and } T > s)}{P(T > s)}$$

**Step 2 — Simplify the intersection.**
The event $\{T > s + t\} \cap \{T > s\}$ is just $\{T > s + t\}$ (a stronger condition):

$$P(T > s + t \mid T > s) = \frac{P(T > s + t)}{P(T > s)} = \frac{R(s+t)}{R(s)}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the reliability function.**

$$P(T > s + t \mid T > s) = \frac{e^{-\lambda(s+t)}}{e^{-\lambda s}}$$

**Step 4 — Simplify the expression.**

$$P(T > s + t \mid T > s) = e^{-\lambda s - \lambda t + \lambda s} = e^{-\lambda t}$$

**Conclusion:** The conditional reliability of operating an *additional* $t$ hours is $e^{-\lambda t}$, independent of $s$. **Memoryless property is proven.**

## 3.4 Derivation of Mission Reliability for Series Systems (Extension)

For a **series system** of $n$ independent components, each with constant failure rate $\lambda_i$, the system reliability over mission time $t_m$ is:

$$R_{S}(t_m) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} R_i(t_m) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} e^{-\lambda_i t_m} = e^{-t_m \sum_{i=1}^{n} \lambda_i} = e^{-\lambda_{S} t_m}$$

where the **equivalent system failure rate** is:

$$\lambda_{S} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \lambda_i$$

This result is heavily tested in KTU Part B questions on system reliability.

## 3.5 Derivation of MTTR and MTBF (For Repairable Systems)

For a repairable system with **constant failure rate** $\lambda$ and **constant repair rate** $\mu$:

$$\text{MTTR} = \frac{1}{\mu}, \quad \text{MTBF} = \frac{1}{\lambda} + \frac{1}{\mu}, \quad A = \frac{\text{MTTF}}{\text{MTBF}} = \frac{\mu}{\lambda + \mu}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Tip:** Whenever you see "availability" or "repairable system" in a problem, immediately switch to the MTBF framework. The constant failure rate applies to the operational phase; constant repair rate applies to the repair phase.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture Flow: Constant Failure Rate Model Relationships

The following block-level diagram maps how the four fundamental reliability functions ($R(t)$, $F(t)$, $f(t)$, $\lambda(t)$) are interconnected, with the constant failure rate assumption as the central postulate.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["POSTULATE: Lambda of t equals Lambda (constant)"] --> B["Hazard Function Definition: f of t divided by R of t"]
    B --> C["Failure Density Function: f of t equals Lambda times R of t"]
    C --> D["Differential Equation: dR by dt equals minus Lambda R"]
    D --> E["Boundary Condition: R of 0 equals 1"]
    E --> F["R of t equals exp of minus Lambda t"]
    F --> G["F of t equals 1 minus exp of minus Lambda t"]
    F --> H["f of t equals Lambda times exp of minus Lambda t"]
    F --> I["MTTF equals 1 divided by Lambda"]
    F --> J["Median Life equals ln 2 divided by Lambda"]
    G --> K["Memoryless Property: R of t given T0 equals exp of minus Lambda t"]
    H --> K
    I --> L["System Reliability: R series equals exp of minus Lambda sum times t"]
    J --> L
    K --> L
```

## 4.2 Bathtub Curve Position of the Constant Failure Rate Model

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["BATHTUB CURVE: Three Failure Regions"] --> B["REGION I: Infant Mortality"]
    A --> C["REGION II: Useful Life - CFRM APPLIES HERE"]
    A --> D["REGION III: Wear-out Zone"]
    B --> B1["Lambda decreases with time. Weibull Beta less than 1"]
    C --> C1["Lambda equals constant. Exponential Distribution. Random shocks"]
    D --> D1["Lambda increases with time. Weibull Beta greater than 1"]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Mapping:** Whenever a question states "operating in the useful life region" or "random shock failures," students must immediately invoke the **exponential distribution** (CFRM). Conversely, "burn-in failures" require Weibull with $\beta < 1$, and "wear-out failures" require Weibull with $\beta > 1$.

## 4.3 Reliability Decay Curve: Schematic Plot

The reliability function $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ is a monotonically decreasing exponential curve. The failure density $f(t) = \lambda e^{-\lambda t}$ starts at its maximum at $t=0$ and decays rapidly.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["At t equals 0"] --> A1["R equals 1, F equals 0, f equals Lambda (max)"]
    B["At t equals MTTF equals 1 by Lambda"] --> B1["R equals 1 by e equals 0.368, F equals 0.632"]
    C["At t equals Median equals ln 2 by Lambda"] --> C1["R equals 0.5, F equals 0.5"]
    D["At t approaches infinity"] --> D1["R approaches 0, F approaches 1, f approaches 0"]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define the Constant Failure Rate Model. What is the key assumption made in this model?**

**Model Answer:**

The Constant Failure Rate Model (CFRM) is a reliability model in which the instantaneous failure rate (hazard rate) of a component or system remains constant throughout its useful operating life. Mathematically, it is expressed as:

$$\lambda(t) = \lambda = \text{constant}, \quad \forall \, t \geq 0$$

**Key Assumption:** The probability of failure in the next small interval $\Delta t$ is independent of the prior operating time of the component. This is equivalent to assuming that failures are caused by **random, independent external shocks** (modeled as a Poisson process), rather than by progressive internal degradation or wear-out.

The time-to-failure $T$ in this model follows an **exponential distribution** with PDF $f(t) = \lambda e^{-\lambda t}$ and reliability function $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$. The model is valid only in the **useful life region** of the bathtub curve, between infant mortality and wear-out phases.

---
