### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**State and prove the memoryless property of the exponential distribution.**

**Model Answer:**

**Statement:** The exponential distribution is the only continuous distribution possessing the memoryless property. For any $s, t \geq 0$:

$$P(T > s + t \mid T > s) = P(T > t) = e^{-\lambda t}$$

**Proof:** Using the definition of conditional probability:

$$P(T > s + t \mid T > s) = \frac{P(T > s + t)}{P(T > s)}$$

Substituting $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$:

$$= \frac{e^{-\lambda(s+t)}}{e^{-\lambda s}} = e^{-\lambda s - \lambda t + \lambda s} = e^{-\lambda t}$$

This equals $P(T > t)$. Hence, the residual life distribution is independent of the elapsed time $s$. **Q.E.D.**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** In Part B questions on CFRM, students most commonly lose marks by:
> 1. Forgetting to specify **units** of failure rate and time.
> 2. Confusing MTTF (non-repairable) with MTBF (repairable).
> 3. Using $\ln 2 / \lambda$ where $1/\lambda$ is required, and vice versa.
> 4. Not explicitly stating the assumption of "useful life region" before applying the model.
> 5. Skipping intermediate derivation steps in the reliability function derivation.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) Derive the reliability function $R(t)$ for a component undergoing the Constant Failure Rate Model. Hence obtain the expressions for the failure density function, CDF, and MTTF.** (7 Marks)

**(b) A digital communication system has a constant failure rate of $\lambda = 5 \times 10^{-4}$ failures per hour. Calculate: (i) the reliability for a 1000-hour mission, (ii) the MTTF in hours, (iii) the time by which 10% of the components are expected to have failed, and (iv) the probability that a component which has already operated for 500 hours will survive an additional 1000 hours.** (7 Marks)

#### Model Solution for Part (a):

**Step 1 — Assumption.** In the useful life region, the hazard rate is constant:

$$\lambda(t) = \lambda, \quad t \geq 0$$

**Step 2 — Use the definition of hazard rate:**

$$\lambda = \frac{f(t)}{R(t)} \implies f(t) = \lambda R(t)$$

**Step 3 — Use the identity $f(t) = -dR(t)/dt$:**

$$-\frac{dR(t)}{dt} = \lambda R(t) \implies \frac{dR(t)}{R(t)} = -\lambda \, dt$$

**Step 4 — Integrate with $R(0) = 1$:**

$$\int_1^{R(t)} \frac{dR'}{R'} = -\lambda \int_0^t dt' \implies \ln R(t) = -\lambda t$$

**Step 5 — Reliability function:**

$$R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$$

**Step 6 — Failure density function:**

$$f(t) = -\frac{dR(t)}{dt} = \lambda e^{-\lambda t}$$

**Step 7 — CDF (Unreliability):**

$$F(t) = 1 - R(t) = 1 - e^{-\lambda t}$$

**Step 8 — MTTF (using integration by parts as derived in Section 3.2):**

$$\text{MTTF} = \int_0^\infty t \cdot \lambda e^{-\lambda t} \, dt = \frac{1}{\lambda}$$

#### Model Solution for Part (b):

**Given:** $\lambda = 5 \times 10^{-4}$ failures/hour, $t_m = 1000$ hr, $t_{\text{elapsed}} = 500$ hr, $t_{\text{additional}} = 1000$ hr.

**(i) Reliability for 1000-hour mission:**

$$R(1000) = e^{-(5 \times 10^{-4})(1000)} = e^{-0.5} = 0.6065$$

**(ii) MTTF:**

$$\text{MTTF} = \frac{1}{5 \times 10^{-4}} = 2000 \text{ hours}$$

**(iii) Time by which 10% of components fail (i.e., $F(t) = 0.10$):**

$$1 - e^{-\lambda t} = 0.10 \implies e^{-\lambda t} = 0.90$$

$$t = \frac{-\ln(0.90)}{\lambda} = \frac{0.10536}{5 \times 10^{-4}} = 210.72 \text{ hours}$$

**(iv) Conditional reliability using memoryless property:**

$$R(1000 \mid T > 500) = e^{-\lambda \cdot 1000} = e^{-0.5} = 0.6065$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Answers:** (i) $R(1000) = 0.6065$, (ii) MTTF = 2000 hours, (iii) $t_{10\%} = 210.72$ hours, (iv) 0.6065.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) What is the bathtub curve? Explain the Constant Failure Rate Model with reference to the useful life region. Derive the relationship between MTTF and the failure rate $\lambda$.** (7 Marks)

**(b) A system consists of 3 components in series, each with a constant failure rate: $\lambda_1 = 0.002$/hr, $\lambda_2 = 0.003$/hr, $\lambda_3 = 0.005$/hr. Find: (i) the system reliability for a 100-hour mission, (ii) the MTTF of the system, and (iii) the system failure rate.** (7 Marks)

#### Model Solution for Part (a):

**The Bathtub Curve:** The bathtub curve is a graphical representation of the failure rate $\lambda(t)$ versus time, showing three distinct regions:

1. **Region I — Infant Mortality:** $\lambda(t)$ decreases with time. Caused by manufacturing defects, poor quality control. Modeled by Weibull with $\beta < 1$.

2. **Region II — Useful Life:** $\lambda(t)$ is approximately **constant**. Random external shocks dominate. Modeled by the **exponential distribution** (CFRM).

3. **Region III — Wear-out:** $\lambda(t)$ increases with time. Aging, fatigue, corrosion. Modeled by Weibull with $\beta > 1$.

**CFRM in Region II:** In the useful life region, infant mortality has been eliminated (via burn-in), and wear-out has not yet begun. Failures are random and independent. The model assumes:

$$\lambda(t) = \lambda = \text{const.}$$

The reliability function is $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ and the PDF is $f(t) = \lambda e^{-\lambda t}$.

**Derivation of MTTF = 1/λ:**

$$\text{MTTF} = \int_0^\infty t \cdot f(t) \, dt = \int_0^\infty \lambda t e^{-\lambda t} \, dt$$

Using integration by parts with $u = t$, $dv = \lambda e^{-\lambda t} dt$, $du = dt$, $v = -e^{-\lambda t}$:

$$\text{MTTF} = \left[ -t e^{-\lambda t} \right]_0^\infty + \int_0^\infty e^{-\lambda t} \, dt = 0 + \frac{1}{\lambda} = \frac{1}{\lambda}$$

#### Model Solution for Part (b):

**Given:** Series system with $n = 3$ components, $\lambda_1 = 0.002$/hr, $\lambda_2 = 0.003$/hr, $\lambda_3 = 0.005$/hr, $t_m = 100$ hr.

**(i) System reliability for 100-hour mission:**

The system failure rate is the sum (for series, independent components):

$$\lambda_S = \lambda_1 + \lambda_2 + \lambda_3 = 0.002 + 0.003 + 0.005 = 0.010 \text{ per hour}$$

$$R_S(100) = e^{-\lambda_S \cdot 100} = e^{-0.010 \times 100} = e^{-1} = 0.3679$$

**(ii) MTTF of the system:**

$$\text{MTTF}_S = \frac{1}{\lambda_S} = \frac{1}{0.010} = 100 \text{ hours}$$

**(iii) System failure rate:**

$$\lambda_S = 0.010 \text{ failures/hour} = 10 \times 10^{-3} \text{ per hour}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Answers:** (i) $R_S(100) = 0.3679$, (ii) MTTF$_S$ = 100 hours, (iii) $\lambda_S = 0.01$/hr.
> 
> **Verification shortcut:** $\lambda_S \cdot t_m = 0.010 \times 100 = 1$ nats, and $e^{-1} = 0.3679$. This matches the standard one-$\lambda$ mission reliability.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ⭐ **CFRM = Exponential Distribution**: The constant failure rate assumption leads uniquely to the exponential distribution via the differential equation $dR/R = -\lambda \, dt$.

- ⭐ **Reliability Function**: $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ — the single most important formula in this topic.

- ⭐ **Failure Density**: $f(t) = \lambda e^{-\lambda t}$ — starts at its maximum $\lambda$ at $t = 0$ and decays monotonically.

- ⭐ **MTTF = $1/\lambda$**: Inverse relationship. Higher failure rate means shorter mean life.

- ⭐ **Variance = $1/\lambda^2$**: Standard deviation $\sigma = 1/\lambda =$ MTTF. This means the standard deviation equals the mean for the exponential distribution — a unique property.

- ⭐ **Median Life = $\ln 2 / \lambda \approx 0.693/\lambda$**: Not equal to MTTF. Common exam trap.

- ⭐ **Memoryless Property**: $R(t_0 + t \mid T > t_0) = e^{-\lambda t}$ — independent of $t_0$. The only continuous distribution with this property is the exponential.

- ⭐ **Series System (CFRM)**: $R_S(t) = \prod e^{-\lambda_i t} = e^{-t \sum \lambda_i}$, so $\lambda_S = \sum \lambda_i$.

- ⭐ **Parallel System (CFRM)**: $R_S(t) = 1 - \prod (1 - e^{-\lambda_i t})$. No simple $\lambda_S$ form; compute each term separately.

- ⭐ **Repairable Systems**: MTTR = $1/\mu$, MTBF = $1/\lambda + 1/\mu$, Availability $A = \mu/(\lambda + \mu)$.

- ⭐ **Bathtub Curve Position**: CFRM is valid in **Region II (Useful Life)** only — never at the start (infant mortality) or at the end (wear-out) of a component's life.

- ⭐ **Units**: Always quote failure rate in failures/hour (or FIT = $10^{-9}$/hr) and time in consistent units.

- ⭐ **Mission Reliability Shortcut**: For a single component, $R(t_m) = e^{-\lambda t_m}$. If $\lambda t_m = 1$ (one "nat"), then $R = 0.368$ — useful mental check.

- ⭐ **KTU-Specific Tip**: When asked to "derive" $R(t)$, always start from the hazard function definition, not from the exponential PDF directly. Marks are awarded for the derivation path, not just the final answer.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Time Dependent Failure Models
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET632 - RELIABILITY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Time Dependent Failure Models

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 Module 1: Time Dependent Failure Models

## 1.1 Core Technical Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Time Dependent Failure Models** are probabilistic and physical-statistical models used in reliability engineering to characterize the failure behavior of components, devices, or systems as a function of operating time, environmental stress, and accumulated degradation. Unlike static reliability models, these explicitly capture the time-varying nature of failure rates caused by wear-out, fatigue, corrosion, electromigration, dielectric breakdown, and other progressive degradation mechanisms.

Formally, a time dependent failure model describes the **instantaneous failure rate** $\lambda(t)$, also called the **hazard function** $h(t)$, which represents the conditional probability density of failure at time $t$, given survival up to that time:

$$
\lambda(t) = \lim_{\Delta t \to 0} \frac{P(t \leq T < t + \Delta t \mid T \geq t)}{\Delta t} = \frac{f(t)}{R(t)}
$$

where:
- $f(t)$ = probability density function (pdf) of failure
- $R(t) = P(T \geq t)$ = reliability (survival) function
- $F(t) = 1 - R(t)$ = cumulative distribution function (cdf) of failure

### 1.2 Intuitive Overview & Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Aging Human Body:** A newly manufactured semiconductor behaves like a young adult — very few random "infant mortality" failures occur after burn-in, and the body is robust. As time progresses (analogous to wear-out region), the heart, joints, and tissues degrade. The probability of a health event (failure) per year **increases with age**. Time dependent failure models mathematically formalize this increasing hazard.

In engineering, every component has a "life story":
- **Infant mortality period (decreasing $\lambda$)** — early failures due to manufacturing defects
- **Useful life period (constant $\lambda$)** — random failures
- **Wear-out period (increasing $\lambda$)** — time-dependent failures due to material degradation

> **Key Insight:** Time dependent failure models predominantly characterize the **wear-out region**, where $\lambda(t)$ is no longer constant but is a function of stress (temperature, voltage, humidity, vibration) and time.

### 1.3 Standard Reliability Metrics (Recall)

For a KTU-ready foundation, the following standard metrics are essential:

| Metric | Symbol | Formula | Unit |
| :--- | :---: | :--- | :---: |
| Reliability Function | $R(t)$ | $P(T \geq t)$ | dimensionless |
| Cumulative Failure Probability | $F(t)$ | $1 - R(t)$ | dimensionless |
| Probability Density Function | $f(t)$ | $\dfrac{dF(t)}{dt}$ | $\text{time}^{-1}$ |
| Instantaneous Failure Rate (Hazard) | $\lambda(t)$ | $\dfrac{f(t)}{R(t)}$ | $\text{time}^{-1}$ |
| Mean Time To Failure | $\text{MTTF}$ | $\int_{0}^{\infty} R(t)\, dt$ | time |
| Conditional Reliability | $R(t \mid t_0)$ | $\dfrac{R(t_0 + t)}{R(t_0)}$ | dimensionless |

> [!TIP]
> For KTU problems, the most frequently tested identities are the relationships $\lambda(t) = f(t)/R(t)$ and the exponential form $R(t) = \exp\!\left(-\int_{0}^{t}\lambda(x)\, dx\right)$.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Time Dependent Failure Models

Time dependent failure models are broadly classified into three families based on the physical/chemical degradation mechanism they represent:

### A. Physical-Stressed (Accelerated) Models
These relate failure rate to **applied stress** (temperature, voltage, current, humidity):

1. **Arrhenius Model** — temperature-driven chemical/physical reactions
2. **Eyring Model** — quantum-mechanical, generalizes Arrhenius
3. **Inverse Power Law (IPL) Model** — non-thermal stresses (voltage, pressure, vibration)
4. **Temperature-Humidity Model** — combined stress
5. **Coffin–Manson Model** — thermal cycling fatigue

### B. Statistical Lifetime Distribution Models
These describe the **distribution of time-to-failure**:

1. **Exponential Distribution** — constant $\lambda$ (memoryless)
2. **Weibull Distribution** — variable shape parameter $\beta$
3. **Lognormal Distribution** — multiplicative degradation
4. **Gamma Distribution** — multi-stage degradation

### C. Cumulative Damage Models
These account for **accumulated damage** under cyclic or varying loads:

1. **Miner's Rule** (linear damage accumulation)
2. **Paris Law** (crack propagation)
3. **Strain-life / Stress-life approaches**

## 2.2 The Arrhenius Model (Most Tested in KTU)

The Arrhenius model is the cornerstone of time-temperature-dependent failure modeling. It expresses the **reaction rate** (or equivalently, the failure rate / degradation rate) as an exponential function of absolute temperature:

$$
\lambda(T) = A \, e^{-\dfrac{E_a}{k T}}
$$

where:
- $A$ = pre-exponential constant (frequency factor)
- $E_a$ = **activation energy** of the failure mechanism (in eV or J/mol)
- $k$ = **Boltzmann constant** $= 8.617 \times 10^{-5}$ eV/K
- $T$ = absolute temperature in Kelvin

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key observation:** As temperature $T$ increases, the failure rate $\lambda(T)$ **increases exponentially**. This is the basis of **Accelerated Life Testing (ALT)** — by elevating temperature, failures are induced in compressed time, and the result is extrapolated to normal-use temperature.

For the **time-to-failure** $L$ (often called "life" or "longevity"), the Arrhenius form is often expressed as:

$$
L(T) = C \, e^{\dfrac{B}{T}}
$$

where $B = E_a/k$ and $C$ is a constant. Note that $L \propto 1/\lambda$.

### Acceleration Factor (AF)

The **Acceleration Factor** between two temperatures $T_1$ (use) and $T_2$ (accelerated) is:

$$
AF = \dfrac{L(T_1)}{L(T_2)} = \exp\!\left[\dfrac{E_a}{k}\left(\dfrac{1}{T_1} - \dfrac{1}{T_2}\right)\right]
$$

> **Real-world utility:** In IC packaging reliability, the Arrhenius model with $E_a \approx 0.3$–$0.5$ eV is used to predict solder joint failure, metallization electromigration, and dielectric breakdown.

## 2.3 Inverse Power Law (IPL) Model

For non-thermal stresses (voltage $V$, current $I$, vibration $G$, pressure $P$):

$$
L = K \cdot V^{-n}
$$

where $K$ and $n$ are empirical constants. The acceleration factor between two stress levels is:

$$
AF = \left(\dfrac{V_2}{V_1}\right)^{n}
$$

## 2.4 The Weibull Distribution (Statistical Time-Dependent Model)

The Weibull model is the workhorse distribution for time-dependent reliability because its **shape parameter $\beta$** controls the time-dependence of $\lambda(t)$:

$$
f(t) = \frac{\beta}{\eta}\left(\frac{t - \gamma}{\eta}\right)^{\beta - 1} e^{-\left(\dfrac{t-\gamma}{\eta}\right)^{\beta}}
$$

$$
R(t) = e^{-\left(\dfrac{t-\gamma}{\eta}\right)^{\beta}}
$$

$$
\lambda(t) = \frac{\beta}{\eta}\left(\frac{t-\gamma}{\eta}\right)^{\beta - 1}
$$

where:
- $\beta$ = **shape parameter** (slope of hazard)
- $\eta$ = **scale parameter** (characteristic life, 63.2% failures)
- $\gamma$ = **location parameter** (typically 0)

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation of $\beta$:**
> - $\beta < 1$ → decreasing failure rate (infant mortality)
> - $\beta = 1$ → constant failure rate (exponential — useful life)
> - $\beta > 1$ → **increasing failure rate (time-dependent / wear-out)**

The **Mean Time To Failure (MTTF)** for Weibull ($\gamma=0$) is:

$$
\text{MTTF} = \eta \, \Gamma\!\left(1 + \frac{1}{\beta}\right)
$$

where $\Gamma(\cdot)$ is the gamma function.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!TIP]
> **Master these formulas — they appear in 90% of KTU 2024 Scheme problems on this topic.**

### Foundational Reliability Identities

$$
R(t) = e^{-\int_0^t \lambda(x)\, dx} \qquad
f(t) = \lambda(t) R(t) \qquad
\text{MTTF} = \int_0^\infty R(t)\, dt
$$

### Arrhenius Family

$$
L(T) = C \, e^{\dfrac{B}{T}} \qquad
\ln L = \ln C + \frac{B}{T} \qquad
AF = \exp\!\left[\frac{E_a}{k}\left(\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}\right)\right]
$$

### Inverse Power Law Family

$$
L = K \cdot V^{-n} \qquad
AF = \left(\frac{V_2}{V_1}\right)^{n} \qquad
\log L = \log K - n \log V
$$

### Eyring Model (Generalized Thermal)

$$
L = A T^{\alpha} \exp\!\left(\frac{B}{kT}\right)
$$

### Weibull Distribution

$$
R(t) = e^{-(t/\eta)^{\beta}} \qquad
\lambda(t) = \frac{\beta}{\eta}\left(\frac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta-1} \qquad
\text{MTTF} = \eta \, \Gamma\!\left(1 + \frac{1}{\beta}\right)
$$

### Exponential Distribution (Special Case of Weibull with $\beta=1$)

$$
R(t) = e^{-\lambda t} \qquad
f(t) = \lambda e^{-\lambda t} \qquad
\text{MTTF} = \frac{1}{\lambda}
$$

### Lognormal Distribution

$$
f(t) = \frac{1}{t \sigma \sqrt{2\pi}} \exp\!\left[-\frac{(\ln t - \mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right] \qquad
R(t) = 1 - \Phi\!\left(\frac{\ln t - \mu}{\sigma}\right)
$$

where $\Phi$ is the standard normal CDF.

## 2.6 Why These Models Matter in Engineering

| Field | Typical Application |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Microelectronics / VLSI** | Dielectric breakdown (Arrhenius), electromigration |
| **Power Electronics** | Solder fatigue (Coffin–Manson), capacitor aging |
| **Aerospace** | Crack growth (Paris Law), turbine creep (Arrhenius) |
| **Nuclear Systems** | Embrittlement, radiation damage (Weibull) |
| **Batteries** | Capacity fade (Eyring, lognormal) |
| **Mechanical Components** | Bearing wear (Weibull with $\beta > 1$) |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📘 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — From Hazard Function to Reliability Function

**Goal:** Derive $R(t) = \exp\!\left(-\int_0^t \lambda(x)\,dx\right)$ from first principles.

**Step 1.** Start with the definition of the conditional failure probability over a small interval $[t, t+dt]$:

$$
P(t \leq T < t+dt \mid T \geq t) = \lambda(t)\, dt
$$

**Step 2.** Express the unconditional probability of failure in $[t, t+dt]$ using conditional probability:

$$
P(t \leq T < t+dt) = P(T \geq t) \cdot \lambda(t)\, dt = R(t)\, \lambda(t)\, dt
$$

**Step 3.** Recognize that the pdf $f(t)$ is the unconditional failure probability density:

$$
f(t) = R(t) \cdot \lambda(t)
$$

**Step 4.** Note the relationship between $R(t)$ and $F(t)$:

$$
R(t) = 1 - F(t) \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \frac{dF(t)}{dt} = -\frac{dR(t)}{dt}
$$

**Step 5.** Since $f(t) = dF(t)/dt$, combine with Step 3:

$$
-\frac{dR(t)}{dt} = R(t) \cdot \lambda(t)
$$

**Step 6.** Separate variables and integrate, with the initial condition $R(0) = 1$:

$$
\frac{dR(t)}{R(t)} = -\lambda(t)\, dt
$$

$$
\int_{1}^{R(t)} \frac{dR}{R} = -\int_{0}^{t} \lambda(x)\, dx
$$

**Step 7.** Evaluate the left side:

$$
\ln R(t) = -\int_{0}^{t} \lambda(x)\, dx
$$

**Step 8.** Exponentiate to obtain the final result:

$$
\boxed{R(t) = \exp\!\left(-\int_{0}^{t} \lambda(x)\, dx\right)}
$$

> **Special case:** If $\lambda(t) = \lambda = $ constant, then $\int_0^t \lambda\, dx = \lambda t$, giving the **exponential reliability** $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — MTTF for the Arrhenius Model

**Goal:** Show that if $L(T) = C e^{B/T}$, then the average life at temperature $T$ is exactly $L(T)$ (i.e., for the deterministic Arrhenius life, MTTF = $L(T)$).

**Step 1.** Treat $L(T)$ as the **time to failure** for a single deterministic degradation event at temperature $T$. The unreliability function is:

$$
F(t; T) = 
\begin{cases}
0, & t < L(T) \\
1, & t \geq L(T)
\end{cases}
$$

**Step 2.** The reliability is $R(t; T) = 1 - F(t; T) = u[L(T) - t]$, the unit step function.

**Step 3.** Apply the MTTF integral:

$$
\text{MTTF}(T) = \int_{0}^{\infty} R(t; T)\, dt = \int_{0}^{L(T)} 1\, dt = L(T)
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{MTTF}(T) = C\, e^{B/T}}
$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — MTTF for the Weibull Distribution

**Goal:** Derive $\text{MTTF} = \eta\, \Gamma\!\left(1 + 1/\beta\right)$ for Weibull with $\gamma = 0$.

**Step 1.** Start from the Weibull reliability:

$$
R(t) = \exp\!\left[-\left(\frac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta}\right]
$$

**Step 2.** Substitute into the MTTF integral:

$$
\text{MTTF} = \int_{0}^{\infty} \exp\!\left[-\left(\frac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta}\right] dt
$$

**Step 3.** Use the substitution $u = (t/\eta)^{\beta}$, which gives $t = \eta u^{1/\beta}$ and $dt = \frac{\eta}{\beta} u^{(1/\beta) - 1} du$:

**Step 4.** The integration limits: $t = 0 \Rightarrow u = 0$; $t \to \infty \Rightarrow u \to \infty$.

**Step 5.** Substituting:

$$
\text{MTTF} = \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-u} \cdot \frac{\eta}{\beta} \, u^{(1/\beta) - 1}\, du
$$

**Step 6.** Factor out the constants:

$$
\text{MTTF} = \frac{\eta}{\beta} \int_{0}^{\infty} u^{(1/\beta) - 1} e^{-u}\, du
$$

**Step 7.** Recognize the integral as the **gamma function** $\Gamma(s) = \int_0^\infty u^{s-1} e^{-u} du$ with $s = 1/\beta$:

$$
\text{MTTF} = \frac{\eta}{\beta} \cdot \Gamma\!\left(\frac{1}{\beta}\right)
$$

**Step 8.** Apply the gamma function identity $\Gamma(x+1) = x\, \Gamma(x)$, with $x = 1/\beta$:

$$
\Gamma\!\left(\frac{1}{\beta}\right) = \frac{1}{\beta}\, \Gamma\!\left(1 + \frac{1}{\beta}\right)
$$

Wait — let's verify: $\Gamma(x+1) = x\Gamma(x)$ implies $\Gamma(x) = \Gamma(x+1)/x$. So $\Gamma(1/\beta) = \Gamma(1/\beta + 1) \div (1/\beta) = \beta\, \Gamma(1 + 1/\beta)$.

**Step 9.** Re-substitute:

$$
\text{MTTF} = \frac{\eta}{\beta} \cdot \beta \cdot \Gamma\!\left(1 + \frac{1}{\beta}\right)
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{MTTF} = \eta\, \Gamma\!\left(1 + \frac{1}{\beta}\right)}
$$

> **Numerical recall values:**
> - $\Gamma(1) = 1$, $\Gamma(2) = 1$, $\Gamma(3) = 2$, $\Gamma(1.5) = \sqrt{\pi}/2 \approx 0.886$
> - For $\beta = 2$ (Rayleigh): $\text{MTTF} = \eta \Gamma(1.5) = 0.886\,\eta$
> - For $\beta = 3$: $\text{MTTF} = \eta \Gamma(4/3) = \eta \cdot 0.8930$

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Acceleration Factor for Arrhenius

**Goal:** Derive $AF = \exp\!\left[\dfrac{E_a}{k}\left(\dfrac{1}{T_1} - \dfrac{1}{T_2}\right)\right]$.

**Step 1.** Define $L_1$ = life at use temperature $T_1$ and $L_2$ = life at accelerated temperature $T_2 > T_1$:

$$
L_1 = C \exp\!\left(\frac{E_a}{k T_1}\right), \qquad L_2 = C \exp\!\left(\frac{E_a}{k T_2}\right)
$$

**Step 2.** The acceleration factor is defined as the ratio of use-life to accelerated-life (how much "faster" we are testing):

$$
AF = \frac{L_1}{L_2}
$$

**Step 3.** Substitute the Arrhenius expressions:

$$
AF = \frac{C \exp\!\left(\frac{E_a}{k T_1}\right)}{C \exp\!\left(\frac{E_a}{k T_2}\right)}
$$

**Step 4.** Cancel $C$ and combine the exponentials:

$$
AF = \exp\!\left(\frac{E_a}{k T_1} - \frac{E_a}{k T_2}\right)
$$

**Step 5.** Factor and finalize:

$$
\boxed{AF = \exp\!\left[\frac{E_a}{k}\left(\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}\right)\right]}
$$

> **Engineering interpretation:** $AF$ tells you "one hour at $T_2$ is equivalent to $AF$ hours at $T_1$." If a device must last 10 years at $T_1 = 323\,\text{K}$ ($50^\circ$C) and we test at $T_2 = 423\,\text{K}$ ($150^\circ$C) with $E_a = 0.7$ eV, then $AF \approx 1065$. So a 1000-hour test is equivalent to ~118 years of use.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Model Selection Quick Reference

| Model | When to Use | Key Parameter | Hazard Trend |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Exponential | Random failures, no wear-out | $\lambda$ (constant) | Constant |
| Weibull ($\beta<1$) | Infant mortality | $\beta$ | Decreasing |
| Weibull ($\beta=1$) | Useful life | $\eta$ | Constant |
| Weibull ($\beta>1$) | Wear-out | $\beta$ | Increasing (power-law) |
| Lognormal | Repair times, semi-conductor failures | $\mu$, $\sigma$ | Hump-shaped |
| Arrhenius | Thermal degradation, chemical reaction | $E_a$ | Exponential in $1/T$ |
| Inverse Power | Voltage / mechanical stress | $n$ | Power-law in stress |
| Eyring | Quantum effects, low-temperature | $\alpha$ | Generalized Arrhenius |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📘 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Bathtub Curve — Failure Rate vs. Time

This is the canonical schematic showing the three life phases of a component and where time-dependent models apply.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PHASE1["PHASE 1: INFANT MORTALITY"]
        A1["Decreasing lambda t"]
        A2["Burn-in removes defects"]
    end

    subgraph PHASE2["PHASE 2: USEFUL LIFE"]
        B1["Constant lambda"]
        B2["Random shocks dominate"]
    end

    subgraph PHASE3["PHASE 3: WEAR-OUT"]
        C1["Increasing lambda t"]
        C2["Time dependent models apply"]
    end

    T0["Time t equals 0"] --> PHASE1
    PHASE1 --> PHASE2
    PHASE2 --> PHASE3
    PHASE3 --> TEND["End of useful life"]

    style PHASE1 fill:#FFD9D9,stroke:#990000,color:#000000
    style PHASE2 fill:#D9F2D9,stroke:#006600,color:#000000
    style PHASE3 fill:#FFD9B3,stroke:#994D00,color:#000000
    style T0 fill:#E0E0E0,stroke:#000000,color:#000000
    style TEND fill:#E0E0E0,stroke:#000000,color:#000000
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Time dependent failure models (Arrhenius, Weibull $\beta>1$, IPL) characterize **PHASE 3** where $\lambda(t)$ grows with time. PHASE 2 is modeled by exponential distribution. PHASE 1 is modeled by Weibull with $\beta<1$.

---

## 4.2 Functional Architecture — Time Dependent Reliability Modeling Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    INPUT["Identify Failure Mechanism: Wear, Corrosion, Fatigue, EMigration"] --> STRESS["Quantify Applied Stress: Temperature, Voltage, Current, Humidity"]
    STRESS --> MODEL{"Select Time Dependent Model"}

    MODEL -- Thermal Degradation --> ARR["Arrhenius Model L equals C exp B over T"]
    MODEL -- Non Thermal Stress --> IPL["Inverse Power Law L equals K V power n"]
    MODEL -- General Thermal --> EYR["Eyring Model L equals A T alpha exp B over kT"]
    MODEL -- Statistical Distribution --> WEI["Weibull Distribution R equals exp minus t over eta power beta"]
    MODEL -- Multiplicative Degradation --> LN["Lognormal Distribution"]

    ARR --> FIT["Fit Parameters from ALT Data: Linearize and Regress"]
    IPL --> FIT
    EYR --> FIT
    WEI --> FIT
    LN --> FIT

    FIT --> PRED["Predict Reliability at Use Conditions R of t and MTTF"]
    PRED --> DEC["Decision: Replace, Reject, Accept, or Redesign"]

    style INPUT fill:#CCE5FF,stroke:#003366,color:#000000
    style STRESS fill:#CCE5FF,stroke:#003366,color:#000000
    style MODEL fill:#FFFFCC,stroke:#666600,color:#000000
    style ARR fill:#FFD9B3,stroke:#994D00,color:#000000
    style IPL fill:#FFD9B3,stroke:#994D00,color:#000000
    style EYR fill:#FFD9B3,stroke:#994D00,color:#000000
    style WEI fill:#D9F2D9,stroke:#006600,color:#000000
    style LN fill:#D9F2D9,stroke:#006600,color:#000000
    style FIT fill:#E0CCFF,stroke:#330099,color:#000000
    style PRED fill:#FFCCCC,stroke:#990000,color:#000000
    style DEC fill:#CCCCCC,stroke:#000000,color:#000000
```

> **Reading the diagram:** This pipeline is exactly the workflow used in industrial ALT (Accelerated Life Testing) labs. The path **Stress → Arrhenius → Fit → Predict** is the most commonly tested route in KTU 2024 Scheme examinations.

---

## 4.3 Weibull Hazard Function Behavior vs. Shape Parameter $\beta$

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph BETA1["BETA LESS THAN 1 INFANT MORTALITY"]
        W1["lambda t decreases with t"]
    end

    subgraph BETA2["BETA EQUALS 1 USEFUL LIFE"]
        W2["lambda t is constant"]
    end

    subgraph BETA3["BETA GREATER THAN 1 WEAR OUT TIME DEPENDENT"]
        W3["lambda t increases with t power beta minus 1"]
    end

    W1 --> W2 --> W3

    style BETA1 fill:#FFD9D9,stroke:#990000,color:#000000
    style BETA2 fill:#D9F2D9,stroke:#006600,color:#000000
    style BETA3 fill:#FFD9B3,stroke:#994D00,color:#000000
    style W1 fill:#FFEEEE,stroke:#990000,color:#000000
    style W2 fill:#EEFFEE,stroke:#006600,color:#000000
    style W3 fill:#FFEFD9,stroke:#994D00,color:#000000
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📘 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]

**Define the Arrhenius model for time dependent failure. What is the physical significance of the activation energy $E_a$?**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> The Arrhenius model is an empirical-physical model that relates the rate of a temperature-activated failure process to absolute temperature:
> $$\lambda(T) = A \, e^{-E_a/(kT)} \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad L(T) = C\, e^{B/T}$$
> where $B = E_a/k$.
>
> **Physical significance of $E_a$:** The activation energy represents the minimum energy threshold (per molecule or atom) that must be overcome for the failure mechanism to proceed — such as breaking a chemical bond, diffusing an atom across a grain boundary, or initiating a crack. A **larger $E_a$** implies a stronger temperature dependence (failure rate is more sensitive to temperature). Typical values:
> - $E_a \approx 0.3$–$0.5$ eV → metallization electromigration
> - $E_a \approx 0.7$–$1.0$ eV → dielectric breakdown
> - $E_a \approx 1.0$–$1.5$ eV → corrosion, oxidation

---

### Question 2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]

**Explain the Weibull distribution as a time-dependent failure model. How does the shape parameter $\beta$ influence the failure rate $\lambda(t)$?**

**Model Answer:**

> The Weibull distribution is a versatile statistical model for time-to-failure, with reliability:
> $$R(t) = \exp\!\left[-\left(\frac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta}\right]$$
> and time-varying failure rate:
> $$\lambda(t) = \frac{\beta}{\eta}\left(\frac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta-1}$$
>
> **Influence of $\beta$:**
> - $\beta < 1$: $\lambda(t)$ is **decreasing** — models infant mortality / debugging phase.
> - $\beta = 1$: $\lambda(t) = 1/\eta =$ constant — reduces to exponential; useful-life phase.
> - $\beta > 1$: $\lambda(t)$ is **increasing** as $t^{\beta-1}$ — models wear-out / time-dependent failures.
>
> Thus the Weibull model captures **all three phases** of the bathtub curve in a single, unified framework, making it the most widely used time-dependent failure distribution in reliability engineering.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A. [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model — 14 Marks]

**(a)** Derive the relationship $R(t) = \exp\!\left(-\int_0^t \lambda(x)\,dx\right)$ from the definition of the hazard function. Explain each step clearly. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a certain component, accelerated life tests were conducted at three elevated temperatures. The following median-times-to-failure (in hours) were recorded:

| Temperature $T$ (K) | $1/T$ (K$^{-1}$) | Median life $L_{50}$ (h) | $\ln L_{50}$ |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 348 | $2.874 \times 10^{-3}$ | 8000 | 8.987 |
| 378 | $2.645 \times 10^{-3}$ | 1500 | 7.313 |
| 408 | $2.451 \times 10^{-3}$ | 350 | 5.858 |

Assuming the Arrhenius model applies, determine:
- (i) The activation energy $E_a$ in eV
- (ii) The pre-exponential constant $C$ in the life equation $L = C e^{B/T}$
- (iii) The predicted MTTF at a use temperature of 323 K (50 °C)

**(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution to Question A

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks):**

Starting from the definition of the instantaneous failure rate (hazard function):

$$
\lambda(t) = \frac{f(t)}{R(t)} = \frac{1}{R(t)} \cdot \frac{dF(t)}{dt}
$$

Since $R(t) = 1 - F(t)$, we have $\dfrac{dF}{dt} = -\dfrac{dR}{dt}$. Substituting:

$$
\lambda(t) = -\frac{1}{R(t)} \cdot \frac{dR(t)}{dt}
$$

Rearranging and separating variables:

$$
\lambda(t)\, dt = -\frac{dR(t)}{R(t)}
$$

Integrate both sides from $0$ to $t$, with the initial condition $R(0) = 1$ (the component is reliable at $t=0$):

$$
\int_{0}^{t} \lambda(x)\, dx = -\int_{1}^{R(t)} \frac{dR}{R} = -\ln R(t) + \ln 1
$$

Since $\ln 1 = 0$:

$$
\ln R(t) = -\int_{0}^{t} \lambda(x)\, dx
$$

Exponentiating both sides:

$$
\boxed{R(t) = \exp\!\left[-\int_{0}^{t} \lambda(x)\, dx\right]}
$$

This is the most general reliability expression. For the **special case** of a constant failure rate $\lambda(t) = \lambda_0$, the integral reduces to $\lambda_0 t$, giving the **exponential reliability** $R(t) = e^{-\lambda_0 t}$.

**Part (b) — Arrhenius parameter estimation (7 marks):**

The Arrhenius life model in logarithmic form is:

$$
\ln L = \ln C + \frac{B}{T} = \ln C + \frac{E_a}{k} \cdot \frac{1}{T}
$$

This is a linear equation $y = a + b x$ where $y = \ln L$, $x = 1/T$, intercept $a = \ln C$, slope $b = E_a/k$.

**Computing the slope (linear regression on the data):**

Let $x_i = 1/T_i$ and $y_i = \ln L_i$:

$$
x_1 = 2.874 \times 10^{-3}, \quad y_1 = 8.987
$$
$$
x_2 = 2.645 \times 10^{-3}, \quad y_2 = 7.313
$$
$$
x_3 = 2.451 \times 10^{-3}, \quad y_3 = 5.858
$$

Using least-squares slope formula $b = \dfrac{n\sum x_i y_i - \sum x_i \sum y_i}{n\sum x_i^2 - (\sum x_i)^2}$:

First, compute the sums (with $n = 3$):

$$
\sum x_i = (2.874 + 2.645 + 2.451) \times 10^{-3} = 7.970 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{K}^{-1}
$$

$$
\sum y_i = 8.987 + 7.313 + 5.858 = 22.158
$$

$$
\sum x_i y_i = (2.874 \times 8.987 + 2.645 \times 7.313 + 2.451 \times 5.858) \times 10^{-3}
$$

Compute each term:

- $2.874 \times 8.987 = 25.83$  → $25.83 \times 10^{-3}$
- $2.645 \times 7.313 = 19.34$  → $19.34 \times 10^{-3}$
- $2.451 \times 5.858 = 14.36$  → $14.36 \times 10^{-3}$

$$
\sum x_i y_i = (25.83 + 19.34 + 14.36) \times 10^{-3} = 59.53 \times 10^{-3}
$$

$$
\sum x_i^2 = (2.874^2 + 2.645^2 + 2.451^2) \times 10^{-6} = (8.260 + 6.996 + 6.007) \times 10^{-6} = 21.263 \times 10^{-6}
$$

Numerator:

$$
n \sum x_i y_i - \sum x_i \sum y_i = 3 \times 59.53 \times 10^{-3} - 7.970 \times 10^{-3} \times 22.158
$$

$$
= 178.59 \times 10^{-3} - 176.60 \times 10^{-3} = 1.99 \times 10^{-3}
$$

Denominator:

$$
n \sum x_i^2 - (\sum x_i)^2 = 3 \times 21.263 \times 10^{-6} - (7.970 \times 10^{-3})^2
$$

$$
= 63.789 \times 10^{-6} - 63.520 \times 10^{-6} = 0.269 \times 10^{-6}
$$

Slope $b$:

$$
b = \frac{1.99 \times 10^{-3}}{0.269 \times 10^{-6}} = 7398\,\text{K}
$$

**(i) Activation energy:**

$$
E_a = b \cdot k = 7398 \times 8.617 \times 10^{-5} = 0.637\,\text{eV}
$$

**Intercept** $a = \bar{y} - b \bar{x}$ where $\bar{y} = 22.158/3 = 7.386$ and $\bar{x} = 7.970 \times 10^{-3}/3 = 2.657 \times 10^{-3}$:

$$
a = 7.386 - 7398 \times 2.657 \times 10^{-3} = 7.386 - 19.66 = -12.27
$$

**(ii) Pre-exponential constant:**

$$
C = e^{a} = e^{-12.27} = 4.65 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{h}
$$

So the Arrhenius life equation is:

$$
L(T) = 4.65 \times 10^{-6} \cdot \exp\!\left(\frac{7398}{T}\right)\ \text{hours}
$$

**(iii) MTTF at 323 K:**

$$
L(323) = 4.65 \times 10^{-6} \cdot \exp\!\left(\frac{7398}{323}\right)
$$

$$
\frac{7398}{323} = 22.90
$$

$$
L(323) = 4.65 \times 10^{-6} \cdot e^{22.90} = 4.65 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 8.86 \times 10^{9}
$$

$$
\boxed{L(323) \approx 41{,}200\,\text{hours} \approx 4.7\,\text{years}}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Students often forget to convert $E_a$ from K·k (slope·Boltzmann) to eV using $k = 8.617 \times 10^{-5}$ eV/K — **NOT** $1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K unless the problem explicitly asks for SI units. Also, **temperature must always be in Kelvin**, never Celsius, in the Arrhenius exponent. Many students lose 1–2 marks here per year.

---

### Question B. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model — 14 Marks — Alternative to Question A]

**(a)** Define the Weibull distribution. Starting from $R(t)$, derive the failure rate $\lambda(t)$ and the MTTF for a Weibull model with location parameter $\gamma = 0$. Clearly state the role of the shape parameter $\beta$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A ball bearing has been tested and found to follow a Weibull distribution with shape parameter $\beta = 2.5$ and characteristic life $\eta = 8000$ hours. Compute:
- (i) The reliability $R(2000)$
- (ii) The probability of failure in the interval $[2000, 5000]$ hours, i.e. $F(5000) - F(2000)$
- (iii) The mean time to failure (MTTF)
- (iv) The instantaneous failure rate at $t = 4000$ hours, $\lambda(4000)$

Given: $\Gamma(1.4) \approx 0.887$. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution to Question B

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks):**

The Weibull distribution (with location parameter $\gamma$) is defined as:

$$
R(t) = e^{-\left(\frac{t-\gamma}{\eta}\right)^{\beta}}, \quad t \geq \gamma
$$

**Derivation of $\lambda(t)$:** From the fundamental identity $f(t) = -\dfrac{dR}{dt}$:

$$
f(t) = -R(t) \cdot \frac{d}{dt}\left[-\left(\frac{t-\gamma}{\eta}\right)^{\beta}\right]
$$

Compute the derivative of the exponent:

$$
\frac{d}{dt}\left[\left(\frac{t-\gamma}{\eta}\right)^{\beta}\right] = \frac{\beta (t-\gamma)^{\beta-1}}{\eta^{\beta}}
$$

Therefore:

$$
f(t) = R(t) \cdot \frac{\beta (t-\gamma)^{\beta-1}}{\eta^{\beta}}
$$

Using $\lambda(t) = f(t)/R(t)$:

$$
\boxed{\lambda(t) = \frac{\beta}{\eta}\left(\frac{t-\gamma}{\eta}\right)^{\beta-1}}
$$

**Derivation of MTTF:** The MTTF is:

$$
\text{MTTF} = \int_{0}^{\infty} R(t)\, dt
$$

For $\gamma = 0$:

$$
\text{MTTF} = \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-(t/\eta)^{\beta}} dt
$$

Substitute $u = (t/\eta)^{\beta}$, so $t = \eta u^{1/\beta}$ and $dt = \dfrac{\eta}{\beta} u^{(1/\beta)-1} du$:

$$
\text{MTTF} = \frac{\eta}{\beta} \int_{0}^{\infty} u^{(1/\beta)-1} e^{-u} du = \frac{\eta}{\beta} \Gamma\!\left(\frac{1}{\beta}\right)
$$

Using $\Gamma(x+1) = x\,\Gamma(x)$ with $x = 1/\beta$:

$$
\boxed{\text{MTTF} = \eta\, \Gamma\!\left(1 + \frac{1}{\beta}\right)}
$$

**Role of $\beta$:** The shape parameter $\beta$ controls the **monotonicity of the failure rate**:
- $\beta < 1$: decreasing $\lambda(t)$ (early-life / infant mortality)
- $\beta = 1$: constant $\lambda(t)$ (random / useful life)
- $\beta > 1$: increasing $\lambda(t)$ (wear-out / time-dependent failure)

**Part (b) — Numerical computation (7 marks):**

Given $\beta = 2.5$, $\eta = 8000$ h.

**(i) Reliability at $t = 2000$ h:**

$$
R(2000) = \exp\!\left[-\left(\frac{2000}{8000}\right)^{2.5}\right] = \exp\!\left[-(0.25)^{2.5}\right]
$$

Compute $(0.25)^{2.5}$:

$$
(0.25)^{2.5} = (0.25)^2 \cdot (0.25)^{0.5} = 0.0625 \times 0.5 = 0.03125
$$

$$
R(2000) = e^{-0.03125} = 0.9693
$$

$$
\boxed{R(2000) \approx 0.9693}
$$

**(ii) Probability of failure in [2000, 5000]:**

$$
P(2000 \leq T \leq 5000) = F(5000) - F(2000) = R(2000) - R(5000)
$$

Compute $R(5000)$:

$$
\left(\frac{5000}{8000}\right)^{2.5} = (0.625)^{2.5}
$$

Calculate $(0.625)^{2.5}$: $\ln(0.625) = -0.4700$, so $2.5 \times (-0.4700) = -1.1750$, giving $(0.625)^{2.5} = e^{-1.1750} = 0.3088$.

Alternatively: $(0.625)^2 = 0.3906$, $(0.625)^{0.5} = 0.7906$, product $= 0.3906 \times 0.7906 = 0.3088$.

$$
R(5000) = e^{-0.3088} = 0.7344
$$

$$
P(2000 \leq T \leq 5000) = 0.9693 - 0.7344 = 0.2349
$$

$$
\boxed{P(2000 \leq T \leq 5000) \approx 0.2349}
$$

**(iii) MTTF:**

For $\beta = 2.5$, $1 + 1/\beta = 1 + 0.4 = 1.4$. Given $\Gamma(1.4) \approx 0.887$:

$$
\text{MTTF} = 8000 \times 0.887 = 7096\,\text{hours}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{MTTF} \approx 7096\,\text{hours}}
$$

**(iv) Failure rate at $t = 4000$ h:**

$$
\lambda(4000) = \frac{2.5}{8000}\left(\frac{4000}{8000}\right)^{2.5-1} = \frac{2.5}{8000} \cdot (0.5)^{1.5}
$$

Compute $(0.5)^{1.5} = 0.5 \cdot \sqrt{0.5} = 0.5 \times 0.7071 = 0.3536$:

$$
\lambda(4000) = \frac{2.5 \times 0.3536}{8000} = \frac{0.8839}{8000}
$$

$$
\boxed{\lambda(4000) \approx 1.105 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{per hour}}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Many students compute $F(5000) - F(2000)$ as $F(5000) - F(2000) = [1-R(5000)] - [1-R(2000)] = R(2000) - R(5000)$ — this is **correct** but is often miswritten. A more common error is computing $F(5000) - F(2000)$ as $1 - [R(5000) - R(2000)]$, which is **wrong**. Always remember: $F(t)$ is monotonically increasing, and $F(t_2) - F(t_1) = R(t_1) - R(t_2)$ for $t_2 > t_1$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Exam — Module 1: Time Dependent Failure Models**

### Core Concepts
- Time dependent failure models characterize **wear-out phase** of the bathtub curve.
- Key function triplet: $R(t)$, $f(t)$, $\lambda(t)$ — all derivable from one another.
- Universal identity: $\lambda(t) = f(t)/R(t)$.

### Master Formulas
- $R(t) = e^{-\int_0^t \lambda(x)\, dx}$
- MTTF $= \int_0^\infty R(t)\, dt$
- Arrhenius: $L = C e^{B/T}$  →  plot $\ln L$ vs. $1/T$ is linear
- $AF = \exp\!\left[\frac{E_a}{k}\left(\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}\right)\right]$
- Weibull: $R(t) = e^{-(t/\eta)^\beta}$, $\lambda(t) = \frac{\beta}{\eta}(t/\eta)^{\beta-1}$
- Weibull MTTF: $\eta\,\Gamma(1+1/\beta)$
- Inverse Power Law: $L = K V^{-n}$, $AF = (V_2/V_1)^n$

### Numerical Values to Memorize
- Boltzmann constant $k = 8.617 \times 10^{-5}$ eV/K
- $\Gamma(1) = 1$, $\Gamma(1.5) = \sqrt{\pi}/2 \approx 0.886$, $\Gamma(2) = 1$
- Characteristic life $\eta$ → 63.2% of population has failed by $t = \eta$

### Key Engineering Insights
- $\beta > 1$ → wear-out (use Weibull or lognormal)
- $\beta < 1$ → infant mortality (improve burn-in)
- $\beta = 1$ → constant hazard (use exponential)
- High $E_a$ → failure is more temperature-sensitive (use Arrhenius)
- Low $E_a$ → failure is weakly temperature-dependent

### Common KTU Pitfalls to Avoid
1. ❌ Using Celsius instead of Kelvin in the Arrhenius exponent
2. ❌ Forgetting to convert $k$ to eV/K when computing $E_a$
3. ❌ Confusing $F(t_2) - F(t_1)$ with $1 - [R(t_2) - R(t_1)]$
4. ❌ Using $\Gamma(1/\beta)$ instead of $\Gamma(1+1/\beta)$ in MTTF
5. ❌ Forgetting that $\beta < 1$ Weibull **decreases** in $\lambda(t)$, not increases
6. ❌ Using $L_1/L_2$ vs. $L_2/L_1$ inverted in the acceleration factor

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Weibull Distribution
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET632 - RELIABILITY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Weibull Distribution

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Weibull Distribution — Core Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Weibull Distribution** is a continuous probability distribution widely used in reliability engineering, life data analysis, and failure-time modelling. It is a versatile, three-parameter distribution capable of modelling a wide spectrum of failure behaviours — from **infant mortality** (decreasing failure rate) to **wear-out** (increasing failure rate), and even constant failure rate scenarios.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Standard Form):**
> A non-negative random variable $T$ representing the *time-to-failure* follows a **Weibull Distribution** if its cumulative distribution function (CDF) is given by:
>
> $$F(t) = 1 - e^{-(t/\eta)^\beta}, \quad t \ge 0$$
>
> where:
> - $\beta$ = **Shape Parameter** (dimensionless), $\beta > 0$
> - $\eta$ = **Scale Parameter** (units of time), $\eta > 0$
> - $\gamma$ = **Location Parameter** (optional, third parameter), $\gamma \ge 0$

When $\gamma = 0$ (as assumed in most KTU problems), it becomes a **Two-Parameter Weibull Distribution**.

## 1.2 The Three Parameters — Physical Intuition

| Parameter | Symbol | Range | Physical Meaning |
|-----------|--------|-------|------------------|
| Shape | $\beta$ | $> 0$ | Controls the *failure rate behaviour* (the "personality" of the failure) |
| Scale | $\eta$ | $> 0$ | Stretches/compresses the time axis; **63.2%** of units fail by $t = \eta$ |
| Location | $\gamma$ | $\ge 0$ | Shifts the distribution along the time axis (guaranteed survival period) |

## 1.3 Real-World Analogy — "The Lifetime Personality Test"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy: The Battery Analogy 🔋**
>
> Imagine you test 1000 rechargeable batteries to find their lifetime:
> - If $\beta < 1$ → Batteries fail *more* when they are **new** (defects, poor manufacturing). Failure rate **decreases** with time. (Infant Mortality)
> - If $\beta = 1$ → Batteries fail at a **constant rate** regardless of age. Memoryless behaviour — like radioactive decay. (Useful Life / Exponential)
> - If $\beta > 1$ → Batteries fail *more* as they **age and wear out**. Failure rate **increases** with time. (Wear-out Phase)
> - If $\beta = 2$ → Special "Rayleigh" case — used in vibration and wind-speed analysis.
> - If $\beta = 3.44$ → Weibull closely **approximates the Normal Distribution**.

The **Scale Parameter** $\eta$ simply answers: "How long, on average, do these units last?" — it stretches the curve along the time axis without changing its *shape*.

## 1.4 Why Weibull is the "Workhorse" of Reliability

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Weibull is the **most widely used distribution in reliability engineering** because:
> 1. It can model **all three phases** of the bathtub curve (infant mortality, useful life, wear-out).
> 2. Its CDF has a **closed-form, simple mathematical structure**.
> 3. It generalises the **Exponential Distribution** ($\beta=1$) and **Rayleigh Distribution** ($\beta=2$).
> 4. Empirical failure data from bearings, ballasts, capacitors, and mechanical components often fit Weibull well.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Weibull Family of Functions — Step-by-Step Logic

Starting from the cumulative distribution function (probability of failure by time $t$):

$$F(t) = 1 - e^{-(t/\eta)^\beta}$$

We can derive every other reliability function through elementary calculus operations:

### Step 1 — Reliability (Survival) Function $R(t)$
The probability that a unit **survives** beyond time $t$:

$$R(t) = 1 - F(t) = e^{-(t/\eta)^\beta}$$

### Step 2 — Probability Density Function (PDF) $f(t)$
Differentiating the CDF with respect to $t$:

$$f(t) = \frac{dF(t)}{dt} = \frac{\beta}{\eta}\left(\frac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta-1} e^{-(t/\eta)^\beta}$$

### Step 3 — Hazard (Failure) Rate Function $h(t)$
The instantaneous rate of failure at time $t$, given survival up to $t$:

$$h(t) = \frac{f(t)}{R(t)} = \frac{\beta}{\eta}\left(\frac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta-1}$$

### Step 4 — Cumulative Hazard Function $H(t)$

$$H(t) = \int_0^t h(x)\,dx = \left(\frac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta}$$

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **HIGH-YIELD TABLE — MEMORISE FOR KTU ESE**

| Function | Formula | Engineering Interpretation |
|----------|---------|----------------------------|
| **CDF** $F(t)$ | $1 - e^{-(t/\eta)^{\beta}}$ | Probability of failure by time $t$ |
| **Reliability** $R(t)$ | $e^{-(t/\eta)^{\beta}}$ | Probability of survival beyond $t$ |
| **PDF** $f(t)$ | $\dfrac{\beta}{\eta}\left(\dfrac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta-1} e^{-(t/\eta)^{\beta}}$ | Failure density at time $t$ |
| **Hazard Rate** $h(t)$ | $\dfrac{\beta}{\eta}\left(\dfrac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta-1}$ | Instantaneous failure rate at $t$ |
| **Cum. Hazard** $H(t)$ | $\left(\dfrac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta}$ | Accumulated hazard up to $t$ |
| **Mean (MTTF)** $E(T)$ | $\eta \cdot \Gamma\left(1 + \dfrac{1}{\beta}\right)$ | Expected lifetime |
| **Median Life** $t_{med}$ | $\eta \cdot (\ln 2)^{1/\beta}$ | 50% failure time |
| **Characteristic Life** $t_{0.632}$ | $\eta$ | 63.2% failure time |
| **Mode** $t_{mode}$ | $\eta \cdot \left(\dfrac{\beta-1}{\beta}\right)^{1/\beta}$, $\beta>1$ | Most likely failure time |
| **Variance** $\text{Var}(T)$ | $\eta^{2}\left[\Gamma\left(1 + \dfrac{2}{\beta}\right) - \left\{\Gamma\left(1 + \dfrac{1}{\beta}\right)\right\}^{2}\right]$ | Spread of lifetimes |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on Gamma Function**: $\Gamma(k) = \int_0^{\infty} x^{k-1} e^{-x} dx$
> Key values: $\Gamma(1)=1$, $\Gamma(2)=1$, $\Gamma(1.5)=\frac{\sqrt{\pi}}{2}\approx 0.886$

## 2.3 Behaviour of Hazard Rate with Shape Parameter $\beta$

| Value of $\beta$ | Hazard Rate Behaviour | Distribution Reduced To |
|------------------|----------------------|-------------------------|
| $\beta < 1$ | $h(t)$ **decreasing** — infant mortality | Weibull (decreasing hazard) |
| $\beta = 1$ | $h(t) = \frac{1}{\eta}$ **constant** | Exponential Distribution |
| $1 < \beta < 2$ | $h(t)$ **increasing** — early wear-out | Weibull (I.D. hazard) |
| $\beta = 2$ | $h(t) = \frac{2t}{\eta^{2}}$ **linearly increasing** | Rayleigh Distribution |
| $\beta = 3.44$ | $h(t)$ rapidly increasing | Approximates Normal |
| $\beta > 1$ | $h(t)$ **increasing** — wear-out | Weibull (wear-out) |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Bearings & Rotating Machinery** — Weibull is the *de-facto* standard for ball-bearing fatigue life.
- **Electronics** — Capacitor, IC, and solder-joint failure time modelling.
- **Aerospace** — Turbine blade and structural component life prediction.
- **Civil Engineering** — Concrete strength and material fatigue analysis.
- **Renewable Energy** — Wind speed distributions are classically Rayleigh ($\beta=2$).
- **Biomedical Engineering** — Survival analysis of implants and prostheses.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — PDF from the CDF (with full calculus)

We start from the given CDF:

$$F(t) = 1 - e^{-(t/\eta)^{\beta}}$$

**Step 1:** The PDF is defined as the derivative of the CDF with respect to $t$:

$$f(t) = \frac{dF(t)}{dt}$$

**Step 2:** Differentiate using the chain rule. Let $u = (t/\eta)^{\beta}$. Then:

$$\frac{d}{dt}\left[1 - e^{-u}\right] = -e^{-u} \cdot \frac{du}{dt}$$

**Step 3:** Compute $\frac{du}{dt}$ for $u = (t/\eta)^{\beta}$:

$$\frac{du}{dt} = \beta \cdot \left(\frac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta - 1} \cdot \frac{1}{\eta} = \frac{\beta}{\eta}\left(\frac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta - 1}$$

**Step 4:** Combine Steps 2 and 3:

$$f(t) = e^{-(t/\eta)^{\beta}} \cdot \frac{\beta}{\eta}\left(\frac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta - 1}$$

**Step 5:** Reorder the multiplicative factors into standard form:

$$f(t) = \frac{\beta}{\eta}\left(\frac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta - 1} e^{-(t/\eta)^{\beta}}, \quad t \ge 0$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification of a valid PDF**:
> $\int_0^{\infty} f(t)\, dt = \left[-e^{-(t/\eta)^{\beta}}\right]_0^{\infty} = 0 - (-1) = 1$ ✓

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Hazard Rate from $f(t)$ and $R(t)$

The hazard rate is defined as:

$$h(t) = \frac{f(t)}{R(t)}$$

**Step 1:** Write both functions explicitly:

$$f(t) = \frac{\beta}{\eta}\left(\frac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta - 1} e^{-(t/\eta)^{\beta}}$$

$$R(t) = e^{-(t/\eta)^{\beta}}$$

**Step 2:** Form the ratio. The exponential terms cancel:

$$h(t) = \frac{\dfrac{\beta}{\eta}\left(\dfrac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta - 1} e^{-(t/\eta)^{\beta}}}{e^{-(t/\eta)^{\beta}}}$$

**Step 3:** Cancel the common exponential factor:

$$h(t) = \frac{\beta}{\eta}\left(\frac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta - 1}$$

**Step 4:** Interpretation — The hazard rate is a pure **power function** of $t$, with exponent $(\beta - 1)$:
- If $\beta = 1$: $h(t) = \frac{1}{\eta}$ → **constant** (memoryless)
- If $\beta = 2$: $h(t) = \frac{2t}{\eta^{2}}$ → **linearly increasing**
- If $\beta = 3$: $h(t) = \frac{3t^{2}}{\eta^{3}}$ → **quadratically increasing**

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Mean Time To Failure (MTTF)

The mean of a continuous random variable is:

$$E(T) = \int_0^{\infty} t \cdot f(t)\, dt$$

**Step 1:** Substitute the Weibull PDF:

$$E(T) = \int_0^{\infty} t \cdot \frac{\beta}{\eta}\left(\frac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta - 1} e^{-(t/\eta)^{\beta}} dt$$

**Step 2:** Substitute $u = (t/\eta)^{\beta}$. Then $du = \frac{\beta}{\eta}\left(\frac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta - 1} dt$.

So the integrand simplifies to $t \cdot du$. We also need $t$ in terms of $u$:
$t = \eta \cdot u^{1/\beta}$.

**Step 3:** Rewrite the integral:

$$E(T) = \int_0^{\infty} \eta \cdot u^{1/\beta} \cdot e^{-u}\, du$$

**Step 4:** Factor out the constant $\eta$:

$$E(T) = \eta \int_0^{\infty} u^{1/\beta} e^{-u}\, du$$

**Step 5:** Recognise the **Gamma Function** form $\Gamma(k) = \int_0^{\infty} u^{k-1} e^{-u} du$ with $k - 1 = 1/\beta$, i.e. $k = 1 + 1/\beta$:

$$E(T) = \eta \cdot \Gamma\left(1 + \frac{1}{\beta}\right)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Numerical Examples of MTTF**:
> - $\beta = 1$: $MTTF = \eta \cdot \Gamma(2) = \eta \cdot 1 = \eta$ (Exponential case)
> - $\beta = 2$: $MTTF = \eta \cdot \Gamma(1.5) = \eta \cdot \frac{\sqrt{\pi}}{2} \approx 0.886\,\eta$ (Rayleigh)
> - $\beta = 3$: $MTTF = \eta \cdot \Gamma(1.333) \approx 0.893\,\eta$

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Special Case: $\beta = 1$ yields Exponential

**Step 1:** Substitute $\beta = 1$ into the Weibull CDF:

$$F(t) = 1 - e^{-(t/\eta)^{1}} = 1 - e^{-t/\eta}$$

**Step 2:** This is exactly the **Exponential CDF** with rate $\lambda = 1/\eta$.

**Step 3:** The hazard rate becomes:

$$h(t) = \frac{1}{\eta} = \lambda \quad \text{(constant)}$$

**Conclusion:** Weibull **reduces to the Exponential Distribution** when $\beta = 1$. This is the foundation of the bathtub curve's *useful life* phase.

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Special Case: $\beta = 2$ yields Rayleigh

**Step 1:** Substitute $\beta = 2$ into the Weibull PDF:

$$f(t) = \frac{2}{\eta}\left(\frac{t}{\eta}\right) e^{-(t/\eta)^{2}}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute $u = t/\eta$:

$$f(t) = \frac{2t}{\eta^{2}} e^{-(t/\eta)^{2}}$$

**Step 3:** The hazard rate becomes:

$$h(t) = \frac{2t}{\eta^{2}} \quad \text{(linearly increasing from zero)}$$

**Conclusion:** This is the **Rayleigh Distribution**, foundational in modelling **wind speed**, **vibration amplitudes**, and **surface roughness**.

## 3.6 Numerical Worked Example (KTU-style)

> **Problem:** A component's lifetime follows a Weibull distribution with $\beta = 2.5$ and $\eta = 1000$ hours. Find:
> (a) Reliability at $t = 500$ hours
> (b) MTTF
> (c) Probability of failure between 200 and 800 hours

**Solution:**

**(a) Reliability at $t = 500$:**

$$R(500) = e^{-(500/1000)^{2.5}} = e^{-(0.5)^{2.5}} = e^{-0.1768} \approx 0.8379$$

**(b) MTTF:**

$$MTTF = 1000 \cdot \Gamma(1 + 1/2.5) = 1000 \cdot \Gamma(1.4)$$

Using $\Gamma(1.4) \approx 0.8873$:

$$MTTF \approx 887.3 \text{ hours}$$

**(c) Probability of failure between 200 and 800:**

$$P(200 \le T \le 800) = F(800) - F(200)$$

Compute $F(800) = 1 - e^{-(0.8)^{2.5}} = 1 - e^{-0.5724} = 1 - 0.5641 = 0.4359$

Compute $F(200) = 1 - e^{-(0.2)^{2.5}} = 1 - e^{-0.01789} = 1 - 0.9823 = 0.0177$

$$P(200 \le T \le 800) = 0.4359 - 0.0177 = 0.4182$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is a high-yield numerical problem pattern — KTU frequently tests $R(t)$, MTTF, and conditional probability in Part B questions.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Weibull Function Family — Functional Block Diagram

The diagram below shows the **functional derivation flow** of all Weibull functions from the base CDF, illustrating the engineering information flow used in reliability block analysis.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Weibull CDF F of t equals 1 minus exp of negative t over eta to the power beta] --> B[Reliability R of t equals exp of negative t over eta to the power beta]
    A --> C[PDF f of t is dF by dt]
    C --> D[Hazard Rate h of t equals f of t divided by R of t]
    B --> D
    D --> E[Cumulative Hazard H of t equals integral of h from 0 to t]
    C --> F[Mean MTTF equals eta times Gamma of 1 plus 1 over beta]
    B --> G[Median Life equals eta times ln 2 to the power 1 over beta]
    D --> H[Bathtub Curve Region Identification]
    H --> I[Beta less than 1 Infant Mortality Decreasing]
    H --> J[Beta equals 1 Constant Failure Exponential]
    H --> K[Beta greater than 1 Wear Out Increasing]
```

```mermaid

        R1["Region 1: Infant Mortality - Beta less than 1 - h of t DECREASING"] --> R2["Region 2: Useful Life - Beta equals 1 - h of t CONSTANT - Exponential Distribution"]
        R2 --> R3["Region 3: Wear Out - Beta greater than 1 - h of t INCREASING"]
    end
    Bathtub --> W["Weibull Distribution is the ONLY distribution that models all three regions"]
```
