# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]**

**Q1. Define the Weibull distribution. State the significance of its shape and scale parameters.**

**Model Answer:**

The Weibull distribution is a continuous probability distribution with CDF:

$$F(t) = 1 - e^{-(t/\eta)^{\beta}}, \quad t \ge 0$$

The **shape parameter** $\beta$ controls the failure rate behaviour — for $\beta < 1$ the hazard rate is decreasing (infant mortality), for $\beta = 1$ it is constant (exponential case), and for $\beta > 1$ it is increasing (wear-out).

The **scale parameter** $\eta$ characterises the spread of lifetimes along the time axis. It represents the time at which **63.2%** of the population has failed, since $F(\eta) = 1 - e^{-1} = 0.632$.

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Q2. Show that the Weibull distribution with $\beta = 1$ reduces to the exponential distribution.**

**Model Answer:**

Substituting $\beta = 1$ into the Weibull CDF:

$$F(t) = 1 - e^{-(t/\eta)^{1}} = 1 - e^{-t/\eta}$$

This is the **exponential CDF** with parameter $\lambda = 1/\eta$.

The corresponding PDF is:

$$f(t) = \frac{1}{\eta} e^{-t/\eta} = \lambda e^{-\lambda t}$$

The hazard rate becomes:

$$h(t) = \frac{1}{\eta} = \lambda \quad \text{(constant)}$$

Since the exponential distribution is the only continuous distribution with a constant hazard rate, Weibull with $\beta = 1$ is **exactly the exponential distribution**. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 1]**

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Derive the Weibull probability density function from the cumulative distribution function. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The lifetime of a certain type of electric motor follows a Weibull distribution with $\beta = 2$ and $\eta = 5000$ hours. Calculate: (i) the reliability at $t = 2000$ hours, (ii) the mean time to failure (MTTF), and (iii) the median life. **(7 marks)**

### **Model Solution for Question A:**

**(a) Derivation of PDF from CDF:**

Given CDF:

$$F(t) = 1 - e^{-(t/\eta)^{\beta}}$$

Differentiating with respect to $t$ using the chain rule:

$$f(t) = \frac{dF}{dt} = \frac{d}{dt}\left[1 - e^{-(t/\eta)^{\beta}}\right]$$

Let $u = (t/\eta)^{\beta}$. Then:

$$f(t) = e^{-u} \cdot \frac{du}{dt}$$

Computing the derivative:

$$\frac{du}{dt} = \frac{\beta}{\eta} \left(\frac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta - 1}$$

Substituting back:

$$f(t) = \frac{\beta}{\eta}\left(\frac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta - 1} e^{-(t/\eta)^{\beta}}, \quad t \ge 0$$

This is the Weibull PDF. **Verification:** $\int_0^{\infty} f(t)\, dt = 1$ by substitution. **(7 marks — full steps required)**

**(b) Numerical Computations:**

Given: $\beta = 2$, $\eta = 5000$ hours.

**(i) Reliability at $t = 2000$ hours:**

$$R(2000) = e^{-(2000/5000)^{2}} = e^{-(0.4)^{2}} = e^{-0.16}$$

$$R(2000) = 0.8521 \text{ (or } 85.21\%)$$

**(ii) Mean Time to Failure:**

$$MTTF = \eta \cdot \Gamma\left(1 + \frac{1}{\beta}\right) = 5000 \cdot \Gamma(1.5)$$

Using $\Gamma(1.5) = \frac{\sqrt{\pi}}{2} \approx 0.8862$:

$$MTTF = 5000 \times 0.8862 = 4431.1 \text{ hours}$$

**(iii) Median Life:**

$$t_{med} = \eta \cdot (\ln 2)^{1/\beta} = 5000 \cdot (\ln 2)^{1/2}$$

$$t_{med} = 5000 \cdot \sqrt{0.6931} = 5000 \times 0.8326 = 4162.8 \text{ hours}$$

**Summary:** $R(2000) \approx 0.8521$, $MTTF \approx 4431.1$ hrs, $t_{med} \approx 4162.8$ hrs. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative**

**(a)** Explain the role of the shape parameter $\beta$ in determining the hazard rate behaviour of a Weibull distribution. Sketch the hazard rate curves for $\beta < 1$, $\beta = 1$, and $\beta > 1$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a Weibull distribution with $\beta = 1.5$ and $\eta = 800$ hours, find: (i) the probability that the component will fail before $t = 400$ hours, (ii) the conditional reliability $R(600 \mid 200)$, i.e. the probability of surviving an additional 400 hours given it has already survived 200 hours. **(7 marks)**

### **Model Solution for Question B:**

**(a) Shape Parameter $\beta$ and Hazard Rate:**

The Weibull hazard rate is:

$$h(t) = \frac{\beta}{\eta}\left(\frac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta - 1}$$

This is a **power function** of $t$ with exponent $(\beta - 1)$. The behaviour is:

- **$\beta < 1$** → exponent is negative → $h(t)$ **decreases monotonically** with time. Represents **infant mortality** phase (defects, poor manufacturing, early failures). At $t = 0^+$, $h(t) \to \infty$.
- **$\beta = 1$** → exponent is zero → $h(t) = \frac{1}{\eta}$ is **constant** with time. Represents **useful life** (chance failures, exponential distribution).
- **$\beta > 1$** → exponent is positive → $h(t)$ **increases monotonically** with time. Represents **wear-out** phase (ageing, fatigue, corrosion). At $t = 0^+$, $h(t) = 0$.

**Sketch (described verbally for the answer sheet):**
- For $\beta < 1$: A hyperbolic curve starting very high and decreasing asymptotically towards zero.
- For $\beta = 1$: A horizontal straight line at $h = 1/\eta$.
- For $\beta > 1$: A curve starting at zero and rising — for $\beta = 2$ it is a straight line, for $\beta > 2$ it is convex upward. **(7 marks — labelled sketch + written explanation)**

**(b) Numerical Computations:**

Given: $\beta = 1.5$, $\eta = 800$ hours.

**(i) Probability of failure before $t = 400$ hours:**

$$F(400) = 1 - e^{-(400/800)^{1.5}} = 1 - e^{-(0.5)^{1.5}}$$

Computing $(0.5)^{1.5} = 0.5 \times \sqrt{0.5} = 0.5 \times 0.7071 = 0.3536$:

$$F(400) = 1 - e^{-0.3536} = 1 - 0.7022 = 0.2978$$

So $P(T < 400) \approx 0.2978$ or **29.78%**.

**(ii) Conditional Reliability $R(600 \mid 200)$:**

The conditional reliability is defined as:

$$R(600 \mid 200) = \frac{R(600)}{R(200)}$$

Compute $R(600)$:

$$R(600) = e^{-(600/800)^{1.5}} = e^{-(0.75)^{1.5}}$$

$(0.75)^{1.5} = 0.75 \times \sqrt{0.75} = 0.75 \times 0.8660 = 0.6495$:

$$R(600) = e^{-0.6495} = 0.5222$$

Compute $R(200)$:

$$R(200) = e^{-(200/800)^{1.5}} = e^{-(0.25)^{1.5}}$$

$(0.25)^{1.5} = 0.25 \times 0.5 = 0.125$:

$$R(200) = e^{-0.125} = 0.8825$$

Therefore:

$$R(600 \mid 200) = \frac{0.5222}{0.8825} = 0.5918$$

So the probability of surviving an additional 400 hours, given survival of the first 200 hours, is approximately **0.5918 or 59.18%**. **(7 marks — full step-by-step work)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ⚠️

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Mixing up $\beta$ and $\eta$**: Students often confuse shape and scale parameters. Remember: $\beta$ is **dimensionless**, $\eta$ has **units of time**.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the exponential term in PDF**: When differentiating $F(t) = 1 - e^{-(t/\eta)^{\beta}}$, many students drop the $e^{-(t/\eta)^{\beta}}$ factor — full marks are lost.
>
> 3. **Incorrect Gamma function evaluation**: $\Gamma(1.5) = \frac{\sqrt{\pi}}{2}$ is memorised incorrectly as $\sqrt{\pi}$. Practice the recurrence $\Gamma(n+1) = n\,\Gamma(n)$.
>
> 4. **Skipping the chain rule in derivation**: Examiners expect explicit use of chain rule — partial marks are awarded for each differentiation step.
>
> 5. **Median formula errors**: Median is $\eta (\ln 2)^{1/\beta}$, NOT $\eta \cdot \ln 2$. This is a frequent error.
>
> 6. **Units mismatch in MTTF**: Always multiply $\eta$ (in hours) by the dimensionless Gamma value to get MTTF in **hours**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — Weibull Distribution**
>
> ✅ **CDF**: $F(t) = 1 - e^{-(t/\eta)^{\beta}}$ — the master equation
>
> ✅ **Reliability**: $R(t) = e^{-(t/\eta)^{\beta}}$
>
> ✅ **PDF**: $f(t) = \frac{\beta}{\eta}\left(\frac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta-1} e^{-(t/\eta)^{\beta}}$
>
> ✅ **Hazard rate**: $h(t) = \frac{\beta}{\eta}\left(\frac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta-1}$ — a power function of $t$
>
> ✅ **MTTF**: $\eta \cdot \Gamma(1 + 1/\beta)$ — always involves the Gamma function
>
> ✅ **Special case 1**: $\beta = 1$ → Exponential, constant hazard, MTTF = $\eta$
>
> ✅ **Special case 2**: $\beta = 2$ → Rayleigh, linear hazard $h(t) = 2t/\eta^{2}$
>
> ✅ **Characteristic life**: At $t = \eta$, $F(\eta) = 0.632$ → **63.2% have failed**
>
> ✅ **Hazard rate trend**: $\beta < 1$ decreasing | $\beta = 1$ constant | $\beta > 1$ increasing
>
> ✅ **Conditional reliability**: $R(t_2 \mid t_1) = R(t_2)/R(t_1)$ for $t_2 > t_1$
>
> ✅ **Median life**: $t_{med} = \eta(\ln 2)^{1/\beta}$
>
> ✅ **Applications**: Bearings, capacitors, ICs, wind energy, biomedical implants, aerospace components
>
> ✅ **Key Gamma values**: $\Gamma(1) = 1$, $\Gamma(1.5) = \sqrt{\pi}/2 \approx 0.886$, $\Gamma(2) = 1$
>
> ✅ **Weibull is the only distribution** that can model all three regions of the bathtub curve
>
> ✅ **Always show full derivation steps** — KTU awards step-marks, not just final answers

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Normal Distribution
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET632 - RELIABILITY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Normal Distribution

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Normal Distribution** (also called the **Gaussian Distribution**) is a continuous probability distribution that is symmetric about its mean and is fully characterized by two parameters — the arithmetic mean (**μ**) and the standard deviation (**σ**). In the context of **Reliability Engineering**, it is the most widely employed analytical model for describing the random variation of failure times, component lifetimes, wear-out failures, dimensional tolerances, and manufacturing process parameters.

The **Probability Density Function (PDF)** of a normally distributed random variable $X$ is mathematically expressed as:

$$
f(x) = \frac{1}{\sigma \sqrt{2\pi}} \exp\left(-\frac{(x-\mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right), \quad -\infty < x < \infty
$$

where:
- $\mu$ = **Mean** of the distribution (location parameter)
- $\sigma$ = **Standard Deviation** of the distribution (scale parameter, $\sigma > 0$)
- $\sigma^2$ = **Variance**
- $\pi \approx 3.14159$ and $e \approx 2.71828$ are universal mathematical constants

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Normal Distribution is the foundational continuous model upon which statistical reliability theory, the *log-normal*, *exponential*, and *Weibull* approximations, and Six-Sigma quality methodologies are mathematically constructed. Mastery of this topic is mandatory for solving KTU Part B numerical problems.

> [!NOTE]
> **Reliability Context:** When the failure mechanism of a component is dominated by *random environmental stresses* (combined effect of many small, independent, additive factors), the Central Limit Theorem guarantees that the time-to-failure $T$ follows a Normal Distribution. This is the classic "**Random Failure Regime**" in the Bathtub Curve.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Bullseye Target" Intuition

Imagine you are an archer shooting arrows at a circular target. The **center of the bullseye** represents the **mean ($\mu$)** — the most likely value. 

- If your aim is **stable but slightly off-target**, the arrows cluster near the bullseye: this is a **small $\sigma$** (high reliability, low scatter).
- If your aim is **erratic**, the arrows spread widely across the target: this is a **large $\sigma$** (low reliability, high scatter).

The Normal Distribution essentially plots *"How likely is each possible value of $X$?"* — the closer to the mean, the higher the bell-shaped curve; the farther away, the lower the tails extend (but never actually touch zero on the horizontal axis).

## 1.3 Physical & Statistical Constants

The following constants and metrics are standard across all KTU-level reliability problems:

- **Mean ($\mu$):** $\mu = E[X]$, the expected value of the random variable
- **Median & Mode:** Both equal $\mu$ due to perfect symmetry
- **Variance:** $\sigma^2 = E[(X-\mu)^2]$
- **Standard Deviation:** $\sigma = \sqrt{\sigma^2}$
- **Skewness:** $\gamma_1 = 0$ (perfectly symmetric)
- **Kurtosis (Excess):** $\gamma_2 = 0$ (mesokurtic — "normal-tailed")
- **Total Area Under Curve:** $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(x)\,dx = 1$ (axiom of probability)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why the Normal Distribution is Central to Reliability

The Normal Distribution is the **cornerstone of statistical reliability** for three deep mathematical reasons:

1. **Central Limit Theorem (CLT):** The sum (or average) of a *large number of independent, identically distributed* random variables — regardless of their individual distributions — tends toward a Normal Distribution. Since most engineering failure phenomena result from the cumulative effect of many microscopic degradation processes, their aggregate effect is approximately Gaussian.
2. **Maximum Entropy Property:** Among all continuous distributions with a specified mean $\mu$ and variance $\sigma^2$, the Normal Distribution has the **highest entropy** — meaning it makes the *fewest assumptions* about the data. This makes it the "safest default" model.
3. **Mathematical Tractability:** Its symmetry, smooth derivatives, and the linear transformation property $Z = (X-\mu)/\sigma$ allow closed-form analytical solutions, which is essential for KTU exam computations.

## 2.2 The Standard Normal Variable

To eliminate dependence on $\mu$ and $\sigma$, every Normal random variable is standardized into the **Standard Normal Variable** $Z$ with mean 0 and standard deviation 1:

$$
Z = \frac{X - \mu}{\sigma} \sim \mathcal{N}(0, 1)
$$

The PDF of $Z$ simplifies to:

$$
\phi(z) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} \exp\left(-\frac{z^2}{2}\right), \quad -\infty < z < \infty
$$

The Cumulative Distribution Function (CDF) is:

$$
\Phi(z) = \int_{-\infty}^{z} \phi(t)\,dt = \int_{-\infty}^{z} \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} \exp\left(-\frac{t^2}{2}\right)\,dt
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reliability Connection:** The **Reliability Function** $R(t)$ for a normally distributed lifetime is given by:
> $$R(t) = P(X > t) = 1 - \Phi\!\left(\frac{t - \mu}{\sigma}\right)$$
> The **Unreliability (CDF)** is $F(t) = \Phi\!\left(\frac{t-\mu}{\sigma}\right)$, and the **Hazard Rate** is $h(t) = f(t)/R(t)$.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **S.No.** | **Formula / Property** | **Mathematical Expression** | **Significance in Reliability** |
|:---------:|:-----------------------|:----------------------------|:-------------------------------|
| 1 | PDF of Normal Distribution | $f(x) = \dfrac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}}\exp\!\left(-\dfrac{(x-\mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)$ | Probability density of failure time $X$ |
| 2 | Standard Normal PDF | $\phi(z) = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}}\exp\!\left(-\dfrac{z^2}{2}\right)$ | Tabulated value for standard $Z$ |
| 3 | CDF of Standard Normal | $\Phi(z) = \displaystyle\int_{-\infty}^{z}\phi(t)\,dt$ | Cumulative probability up to $z$ |
| 4 | Standardization Transformation | $z = \dfrac{x - \mu}{\sigma}$ | Converts $X$ to standard form |
| 5 | Reliability Function | $R(t) = 1 - \Phi\!\left(\dfrac{t-\mu}{\sigma}\right)$ | Probability of *survival* beyond time $t$ |
| 6 | Unreliability Function (CDF) | $F(t) = \Phi\!\left(\dfrac{t-\mu}{\sigma}\right)$ | Probability of *failure* by time $t$ |
| 7 | Hazard Rate | $h(t) = \dfrac{f(t)}{R(t)}$ | Instantaneous failure rate |
| 8 | Mean | $E[X] = \mu$ | Expected lifetime |
| 9 | Variance | $\text{Var}(X) = \sigma^2$ | Spread of lifetimes |
| 10 | Symmetry Property | $\Phi(-z) = 1 - \Phi(z)$ | Used in KTU numerical problems |
| 11 | Empirical (68-95-99.7) Rule | $P(\mu \pm \sigma) = 0.6827$ | Quick mental math for $1\sigma$ |
| 12 | Empirical Rule for $2\sigma$ | $P(\mu \pm 2\sigma) = 0.9545$ | $95.45\%$ of observations |
| 13 | Empirical Rule for $3\sigma$ | $P(\mu \pm 3\sigma) = 0.9973$ | $99.73\%$ of observations (Six Sigma) |
| 14 | Mean Time To Failure (MTTF) | $\text{MTTF} = \mu$ | For Normal distribution |
| 15 | Inflection Points | $x = \mu \pm \sigma$ | Points where curve changes curvature |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Mechanical Engineering:** Modeling wear-out failure times of bearings, shafts, and gears due to fatigue.
- **Civil Engineering:** Concrete compressive strength variability, dimensional tolerances of structural members.
- **Electronics:** Semiconductor parameter variation, signal noise distributions in communication channels.
- **Quality Control:** Six-Sigma manufacturing (target: 3.4 defects per million opportunities, corresponding to $4.5\sigma$ from mean).
- **Aerospace:** Structural load distributions, fatigue crack propagation life modeling.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Normal Distribution PDF

The Normal Distribution PDF can be derived elegantly from three fundamental axioms.

**Starting Axiom 1 — Symmetry:** The PDF must be symmetric about $x = \mu$, so it depends only on $(x-\mu)^2$.

**Starting Axiom 2 — Normalization:** The total probability must equal 1:
$$\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(x)\,dx = 1$$

**Starting Axiom 3 — Mode at Mean:** The maximum of $f(x)$ occurs at $x = \mu$, so $f'(\mu) = 0$.

Assume the PDF has the form $f(x) = A \cdot g\!\left((x-\mu)^2\right)$ for some constant $A$ and function $g$.

**Step 1:** Take the natural logarithm to simplify differentiation:
$$\ln f(x) = \ln A + \ln g\!\left((x-\mu)^2\right)$$

**Step 2:** Differentiate both sides with respect to $x$:
$$\frac{f'(x)}{f(x)} = \frac{g'((x-\mu)^2) \cdot 2(x-\mu)}{g((x-\mu)^2)}$$

**Step 3:** Apply the simplest physically meaningful assumption — that the relative rate of change of $f$ is *linear* in $(x-\mu)$. This means:
$$\frac{f'(x)}{f(x)} = -k(x-\mu)$$
for some positive constant $k$ (the negative sign ensures $f$ decreases as we move away from the peak).

**Step 4:** Integrate both sides:
$$\int \frac{f'(x)}{f(x)}\,dx = \int -k(x-\mu)\,dx$$
$$\ln f(x) = -\frac{k}{2}(x-\mu)^2 + C$$

**Step 5:** Exponentiate:
$$f(x) = e^C \cdot \exp\!\left(-\frac{k(x-\mu)^2}{2}\right)$$

Let $A = e^C$ and $\sigma^2 = 1/k$, giving:
$$f(x) = A \cdot \exp\!\left(-\frac{(x-\mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)$$

**Step 6:** Apply the normalization condition (Axiom 2) to find $A$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} A \cdot \exp\!\left(-\frac{(x-\mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right) dx &= 1 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

Let $u = (x-\mu)/(\sigma\sqrt{2})$, then $dx = \sigma\sqrt{2}\,du$, and the integral becomes:

$$
\begin{aligned}
A \cdot \sigma\sqrt{2} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} e^{-u^2}\,du &= 1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7:** Use the famous **Gaussian integral** (proven via polar coordinates):
$$\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} e^{-u^2}\,du = \sqrt{\pi}$$

**Step 8:** Substitute back to solve for $A$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
A \cdot \sigma\sqrt{2} \cdot \sqrt{\pi} &= 1 \\
A &= \frac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 9:** Final PDF expression:

$$
\boxed{\;f(x) = \frac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{(x-\mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)\;}
$$

## 3.2 Derivation of Mean (Expected Value)

By definition, $E[X] = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x \cdot f(x)\,dx$.

**Step 1:** Substitute the PDF and let $z = (x-\mu)/\sigma$, so $x = \mu + \sigma z$ and $dx = \sigma\,dz$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E[X] &= \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} (\mu + \sigma z) \cdot \frac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{z^2}{2}\right) \sigma\,dz \\
&= \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} (\mu + \sigma z) e^{-z^2/2}\,dz \\
&= \frac{\mu}{\sqrt{2\pi}}\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} e^{-z^2/2}\,dz + \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{2\pi}}\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} z\,e^{-z^2/2}\,dz
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** The first integral equals $\sqrt{2\pi}$ (Gaussian integral). The second integral is zero because the integrand $z\,e^{-z^2/2}$ is an **odd function** integrated over symmetric limits.

$$
\begin{aligned}
E[X] &= \frac{\mu}{\sqrt{2\pi}} \cdot \sqrt{2\pi} + 0 = \mu
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore, $\boxed{E[X] = \mu}$.

## 3.3 Derivation of Variance

$\text{Var}(X) = E[(X-\mu)^2] = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty}(x-\mu)^2 f(x)\,dx$.

**Step 1:** Substitute $z = (x-\mu)/\sigma$, so $(x-\mu)^2 = \sigma^2 z^2$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Var}(X) &= \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \sigma^2 z^2 \cdot \frac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}} e^{-z^2/2} \sigma\,dz \\
&= \frac{\sigma^2}{\sqrt{2\pi}} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} z^2 e^{-z^2/2}\,dz
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Integrate by parts with $u = z$, $dv = z\,e^{-z^2/2}\,dz$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} z^2 e^{-z^2/2}\,dz &= \left[-z e^{-z^2/2}\right]_{-\infty}^{\infty} + \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} e^{-z^2/2}\,dz \\
&= 0 + \sqrt{2\pi}
\end{aligned}
$$

(The boundary term vanishes because $e^{-z^2/2} \to 0$ faster than $z \to \infty$.)

**Step 3:** Substitute back:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Var}(X) &= \frac{\sigma^2}{\sqrt{2\pi}} \cdot \sqrt{2\pi} = \sigma^2
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore, $\boxed{\text{Var}(X) = \sigma^2}$.

## 3.4 Inflection Points — Bell Shape Analysis

To find where the bell curve changes concavity, set $f''(x) = 0$. Using $u = (x-\mu)/\sigma$, the second derivative of the PDF is proportional to:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{d^2}{du^2}\left(e^{-u^2/2}\right) &= \frac{d}{du}(-u\,e^{-u^2/2}) \\
&= -e^{-u^2/2} + u^2 e^{-u^2/2} \\
&= e^{-u^2/2}(u^2 - 1)
\end{aligned}
$$

Setting this equal to zero gives $u^2 = 1$, i.e., $u = \pm 1$, which corresponds to:

$$
\boxed{\;x_{\text{inflection}} = \mu \pm \sigma\;}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** The points $\mu \pm \sigma$ are the **"shoulders"** of the bell curve. In reliability engineering, these are the points of maximum hazard rate curvature for wear-out failures.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Normal Distribution PDF — Annotated Bell Curve

```mermaid
graph TB
    A["Mean mu - Location of Peak Maximum Density"] --> B["Standard Normal PDF phi of z equals 1 over sqrt 2 pi times exp of minus z squared over 2"]
    C["Variance sigma squared - Controls Width Spread"] --> B
    D["Total Area Under Curve equals 1 - Probability Axiom"] --> B
    E["Inflection Points at mu plus sigma and mu minus sigma - Shoulders of Bell"] --> B
    F["Skewness equals 0 - Perfect Symmetry About mu"] --> B
    G["Empirical Rule 68-95-99.7 - Three Sigma Coverage"] --> B
    H["Reliability Function R of t equals 1 minus Phi of z - Area to Right of t"] --> B
    I["Hazard Rate h of t equals f of t divided by R of t - Instantaneous Failure Rate"] --> B
    B --> J["OUTPUT Applications: Reliability Analysis, Quality Control, Six Sigma, Tolerance Design"]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Reliability Computation Flow

```mermaid
graph TB
    INPUT["INPUT: Random Variable X - Failure Time or Measured Parameter"] --> COMPUTE_MEAN["STEP 1: Compute Mean mu - Expected Value E of X"]
    INPUT --> COMPUTE_SD["STEP 2: Compute Standard Deviation sigma - Measure of Spread"]
    COMPUTE_MEAN --> STANDARDIZE["STEP 3: Standardize Variable z equals X minus mu over sigma"]
    COMPUTE_SD --> STANDARDIZE
    STANDARDIZE --> LOOKUP["STEP 4: Look up Phi of z in Standard Normal Tables"]
    LOOKUP --> RELIABILITY["STEP 5: Compute Reliability R of t equals 1 minus Phi of z"]
    LOOKUP --> UNRELIABILITY["STEP 6: Compute Unreliability F of t equals Phi of z"]
    RELIABILITY --> HAZARD["STEP 7: Compute Hazard Rate h of t equals f of t over R of t"]
    UNRELIABILITY --> HAZARD
    HAZARD --> DECISION["OUTPUT DECISION: Component Acceptance or Rejection Threshold"]
```

    D["Region 3: mu minus 2 sigma to mu minus sigma - Probability equals 0.1359"] --> B

    G["Region 5: mu to mu plus sigma - Probability equals 0.3413 - High Density Region"] --> F
    H["Region 6: mu plus sigma to mu plus 2 sigma - Probability equals 0.1359"] --> I["Right Tail Failure Region"]

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]`

**Define Normal Distribution. State any four of its important properties.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Normal Distribution** is a continuous probability distribution characterized by a symmetric, bell-shaped curve, defined by the PDF:

$$
f(x) = \frac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{(x-\mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)
$$

**Four Important Properties:**

1. **Symmetry:** The distribution is perfectly symmetric about the mean $\mu$, i.e., $f(\mu - a) = f(\mu + a)$ for all $a$.

2. **Mean = Median = Mode:** All three measures of central tendency coincide at $\mu$.

3. **Total Area = 1:** $\displaystyle\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(x)\,dx = 1$, satisfying the probability axiom.

4. **Empirical Rule (68-95-99.7):** Approximately $68.27\%$ of observations lie within $\mu \pm \sigma$, $95.45\%$ within $\mu \pm 2\sigma$, and $99.73\%$ within $\mu \pm 3\sigma$.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Sample Paper]`

**Explain the concept of Standard Normal Variable. How is it used in reliability computations?**

**Model Answer:**

The **Standard Normal Variable** $Z$ is obtained by linearly transforming a general Normal random variable $X \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^2)$ into one with mean 0 and standard deviation 1:

$$
Z = \frac{X - \mu}{\sigma} \sim \mathcal{N}(0, 1)
$$

**Use in Reliability Computations:**

By standardizing, we can use a **single universal Standard Normal Table** to find probabilities for *any* Normal distribution, regardless of its specific $\mu$ and $\sigma$. The reliability function becomes:

$$
R(t) = P(X > t) = 1 - \Phi\!\left(\frac{t-\mu}{\sigma}\right)
$$

where $\Phi(z)$ is the standard normal CDF read directly from tables. This allows engineers to compute survival probabilities, hazard rates, and tolerance limits for components with different lifetime distributions using one common reference table.

---

## PART B QUESTIONS (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice Pattern)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** The lifetimes (in hours) of a certain type of electronic component are normally distributed with mean $\mu = 1000$ hours and standard deviation $\sigma = 100$ hours. **(7 Marks)**

**(i)** Find the probability that a randomly selected component lasts between 850 and 1150 hours.

**(ii)** Find the reliability of the component at $t = 900$ hours, i.e., $R(900)$.

**(b)** For the same component, the manufacturer guarantees replacement if failure occurs before $L$ hours. If the desired replacement probability is only $5\%$, determine the guaranteed life $L$. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for Part (a):

**Step 1 — Standardize the boundaries:**

For $x_1 = 850$:
$$z_1 = \frac{850 - 1000}{100} = \frac{-150}{100} = -1.50$$

For $x_2 = 1150$:
$$z_2 = \frac{1150 - 1000}{100} = \frac{150}{100} = +1.50$$

**Step 2 — Compute required probability:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(850 < X < 1150) &= P(-1.50 < Z < 1.50) \\
&= \Phi(1.50) - \Phi(-1.50) \\
&= \Phi(1.50) - [1 - \Phi(1.50)] \\
&= 2\Phi(1.50) - 1
\end{aligned}
$$

From standard normal tables: $\Phi(1.50) = 0.9332$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(850 < X < 1150) &= 2(0.9332) - 1 = 1.8664 - 1 = 0.8664
\end{aligned}
$$

**Answer (i):** The probability is $\boxed{0.8664}$ or $86.64\%$.

**Step 3 — Compute reliability at $t = 900$:**

Standardize: $z = (900 - 1000)/100 = -1.00$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
R(900) &= P(X > 900) = 1 - \Phi(-1.00) \\
&= 1 - [1 - \Phi(1.00)] \\
&= \Phi(1.00) \\
&= 0.8413
\end{aligned}
$$

**Answer (ii):** $\boxed{R(900) = 0.8413}$ or $84.13\%$.

#### Model Solution for Part (b):

**Step 1 — Set up the equation:** We need $P(X < L) = 0.05$ (replacement probability).

**Step 2 — Standardize:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(X < L) &= \Phi\!\left(\frac{L - 1000}{100}\right) = 0.05
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Find the corresponding $z$-value:** Since $\Phi(z) = 0.05$ lies in the left tail, we use the symmetry property:

$$\Phi(z) = 0.05 \implies z = -1.645$$

(From inverse standard normal tables.)

**Step 4 — Solve for $L$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{L - 1000}{100} &= -1.645 \\
L - 1000 &= -164.5 \\
L &= 835.5 \text{ hours}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Answer:** $\boxed{L \approx 835.5 \text{ hours}}$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout #1:** Students frequently forget to apply the symmetry property $\Phi(-z) = 1 - \Phi(z)$ when computing $P(X < \text{something})$ for values *below* the mean. They look up $\Phi(1.645) = 0.95$ and incorrectly report it as the answer, instead of recognizing that the 5th percentile corresponds to $z = -1.645$. **Always draw the bell curve first** to identify the correct tail.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

**(a)** Derive the **Probability Density Function (PDF)** of the Normal Distribution starting from the assumption that the logarithmic derivative $\dfrac{f'(x)}{f(x)} = -k(x-\mu)$. Show all intermediate steps clearly. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A mechanical shaft has a diameter normally distributed with mean $\mu = 50$ mm and standard deviation $\sigma = 0.5$ mm. The design specification requires the diameter to be between $49$ mm and $51$ mm. **(7 Marks)**

**(i)** Calculate the probability that a randomly produced shaft meets the specification.

**(ii)** If the production process must achieve $99.73\%$ conformance (the $3\sigma$ quality level), what is the maximum allowable standard deviation $\sigma$?

#### Model Solution for Part (a):

**Step 1 — Set up the differential equation:**

Given:
$$\frac{f'(x)}{f(x)} = -k(x-\mu)$$

**Step 2 — Integrate both sides:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\int \frac{f'(x)}{f(x)}\,dx &= \int -k(x-\mu)\,dx \\
\ln f(x) &= -\frac{k}{2}(x-\mu)^2 + C
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Exponentiate:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f(x) &= e^C \cdot \exp\!\left(-\frac{k(x-\mu)^2}{2}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

Let $A = e^C$ and substitute $\sigma^2 = 1/k$:

$$
f(x) = A \cdot \exp\!\left(-\frac{(x-\mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)
$$

**Step 4 — Apply normalization** $\displaystyle\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(x)\,dx = 1$:

Substitute $u = (x-\mu)/(\sigma\sqrt{2})$, so $dx = \sigma\sqrt{2}\,du$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
A \cdot \sigma\sqrt{2} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} e^{-u^2}\,du &= 1
\end{aligned}
$$

Using the Gaussian integral $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} e^{-u^2}\,du = \sqrt{\pi}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
A \cdot \sigma\sqrt{2} \cdot \sqrt{\pi} &= 1 \\
A &= \frac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Final PDF:**

$$
\boxed{\;f(x) = \frac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{(x-\mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)\;}
$$

#### Model Solution for Part (b):

**Step 1 — Standardize the specification limits:**

For $x_1 = 49$ mm:
$$z_1 = \frac{49 - 50}{0.5} = -2.00$$

For $x_2 = 51$ mm:
$$z_2 = \frac{51 - 50}{0.5} = +2.00$$

**Step 2 — Compute conformance probability:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(49 < X < 51) &= \Phi(2.00) - \Phi(-2.00) \\
&= 2\Phi(2.00) - 1 \\
&= 2(0.9772) - 1 \\
&= 0.9544
\end{aligned}
$$

**Answer (i):** Conformance probability = $\boxed{0.9544}$ or $95.44\%$.

**Step 3 — For $99.73\%$ conformance (the empirical $3\sigma$ rule):**

By the empirical rule, $99.73\%$ coverage corresponds to $z = \pm 3$. For the specification limits $49$ mm and $51$ mm:

$$
\begin{aligned}
z &= \frac{51 - 50}{\sigma} = 3 \\
\sigma &= \frac{1}{3} \approx 0.333 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Answer (ii):** $\boxed{\sigma_{\max} \approx 0.333 \text{ mm}}$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout #2:** When asked to "derive" the PDF (not just state it), students lose marks by **skipping the normalization step**. The constant $A$ must be explicitly evaluated using the Gaussian integral — it is *not* acceptable to just write the final formula. Also, do **not confuse** the standard deviation $\sigma$ with the variable of integration $x$ — maintain clear notation throughout.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Exam Preparation**

- **Definition:** Normal Distribution = Symmetric bell curve with PDF $f(x) = \frac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{(x-\mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)$.

- **Two Parameters:** Mean $\mu$ (location) and Standard Deviation $\sigma$ (spread). Knowing both fully specifies the distribution.

- **Standardization:** Always convert to $Z = (X-\mu)/\sigma$ before using normal tables. KTU exam tables are typically for $\Phi(z)$ only.

- **Key Symmetry Identity:** $\Phi(-z) = 1 - \Phi(z)$. *Memorize this* — it appears in nearly every KTU numerical problem.

- **Reliability Function:** $R(t) = 1 - \Phi\!\left(\frac{t-\mu}{\sigma}\right)$ — represents area to the *right* of $t$.

- **Unreliability:** $F(t) = \Phi\!\left(\frac{t-\mu}{\sigma}\right)$ — area to the *left* of $t$.

- **Mean Time To Failure (MTTF):** For Normal distribution, MTTF $= \mu$ exactly.

- **Inflection Points:** Located at $x = \mu \pm \sigma$ — these are the "shoulders" where curvature changes.

- **Empirical Rule (must memorize):**
  - $\mu \pm 1\sigma \Rightarrow 68.27\%$
  - $\mu \pm 2\sigma \Rightarrow 95.45\%$
  - $\mu \pm 3\sigma \Rightarrow 99.73\%$

- **Common $z$-values to remember:**
  - $\Phi(1.00) = 0.8413$
  - $\Phi(1.50) = 0.9332$
  - $\Phi(1.645) = 0.95$ (95th percentile)
  - $\Phi(2.00) = 0.9772$
  - $\Phi(2.576) = 0.995$ (99th percentile)
  - $\Phi(3.00) = 0.9987$

- **Pitfall to Avoid:** When asked to compute $P(X > a)$ for $a < \mu$, students often forget that this is a *large* probability (close to 1), not a small one. Always sketch the bell curve and shade the relevant region before plugging into the formula.

- **Derivation Trick:** For "derive the PDF" questions, start with the logarithmic derivative assumption $\frac{f'(x)}{f(x)} = -k(x-\mu)$ — this is the **standard expected approach** in KTU marking schemes.

- **Engineering Application Focus:** Always frame answers in reliability terms — talk about *component lifetime*, *failure probability*, *hazard rate*, and *survival probability* rather than abstract statistics.

- **Symmetry Property:** If $X \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^2)$, then $aX + b \sim \mathcal{N}(a\mu + b, a^2\sigma^2)$ — this linear transformation property is frequently tested.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Lognormal Distribution
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET632 - RELIABILITY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Lognormal Distribution

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Lognormal Distribution (KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition):**
> A continuous random variable $X$ is said to follow a **Lognormal Distribution** with parameters $\mu$ and $\sigma^2$ (where $\mu \in \mathbb{R}$ and $\sigma > 0$) if the natural logarithm of $X$, i.e., $Y = \ln(X)$, follows a Normal (Gaussian) Distribution with mean $\mu$ and variance $\sigma^2$. This is mathematically expressed as:
> $$X \sim \text{Lognormal}(\mu, \sigma^2) \iff \ln(X) = Y \sim N(\mu, \sigma^2)$$

**Domain Constraint:** $X$ must always be strictly positive, i.e., $X > 0$, because the natural logarithm of a non-positive number is undefined in the real number system.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Cumulative Multiplication Effect":**
> Imagine you start a small business with **₹100** of capital. Each day, your money either grows by a small random **percentage** (say, multiplied by a normally distributed factor) or shrinks. After $N$ days, your final wealth is the **product** of $N$ random multiplicative factors. When you take the logarithm of this final wealth, the product magically transforms into a **sum** of normally distributed variables — and by the **Central Limit Theorem**, that sum is normally distributed. Therefore, the *original* wealth follows a **Lognormal Distribution**.

This explains why the lognormal is the *natural* distribution for quantities that arise from multiplicative random processes — exactly the kind of process that drives **fatigue crack growth**, **corrosion penetration**, and **creep rupture** in materials science.

### 1.3 Engineering Significance in Reliability

- **Skewed Failure Data:** Unlike the exponential or normal distribution, the lognormal is **right-skewed (positively skewed)**, making it ideal for modeling time-to-failure data that has a long tail on the right side.
- **Non-Negative Random Variables:** It guarantees positive values, which is physically meaningful for time, length, strength, etc.
- **Inferior to Weibull in some cases, superior in others** — particularly for **early failures** and **wear-out** phenomena in mechanical systems.

**Standard Metrics (KTU Convention):**
- The two parameters $\mu$ (mean of $\ln X$) and $\sigma$ (standard deviation of $\ln X$) are denoted as the **location parameter** and **shape parameter** respectively.
- $\mu$ is dimensionless (it is in log-units), and $\sigma$ is also dimensionless.
- The unit of $X$ itself depends on the physical quantity being modeled (e.g., hours, cycles, micrometers).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Conditions for Applicability (When to Use Lognormal)

A dataset is well-modeled by the lognormal distribution when **all** of the following are satisfied:

1. The random variable $X$ is **strictly positive** ($X > 0$).
2. The natural logarithm of $X$, i.e., $\ln(X)$, is **normally distributed** (verified via probability plots).
3. The underlying physical mechanism is driven by **multiplicative (not additive)** random effects.
4. The data is **right-skewed** with a long upper tail.
5. The **hazard rate is non-monotonic** — it first increases, reaches a maximum, and then decreases. This is a **uniquely identifying signature** of the lognormal distribution.

### 2.2 Why the Lognormal Arises Naturally — The Mathematical "Why"

> [!NOTE]
> **Central Limit Theorem for Multiplicative Processes:**
> If $X = e^{Y_1} \cdot e^{Y_2} \cdot e^{Y_3} \cdots e^{Y_n}$, then $\ln(X) = Y_1 + Y_2 + \cdots + Y_n$. If each $Y_i$ is independent and identically distributed (or satisfies Lindeberg/Feller conditions), then by the **Central Limit Theorem**, $\ln(X) \sim N(n\mu_Y, n\sigma_Y^2)$ for large $n$. This is the foundational reason why lognormal appears so often in nature and engineering.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All key formulas for the Lognormal Distribution (Examination-Critical)**

| **Property** | **Formula** | **Remarks** |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Notation** | $X \sim \text{LN}(\mu, \sigma^2)$ | $\mu$ = location, $\sigma$ = shape |
| **PDF** | $f(x) = \dfrac{1}{x \sigma \sqrt{2\pi}} \exp\left(-\dfrac{(\ln x - \mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)$, for $x > 0$ | Probability Density Function |
| **CDF** | $F(x) = \Phi\left(\dfrac{\ln x - \mu}{\sigma}\right)$ | $\Phi(\cdot)$ = Standard Normal CDF |
| **Reliability Function** | $R(t) = 1 - F(t) = \Phi\left(-\dfrac{\ln t - \mu}{\sigma}\right) = \Phi\left(\dfrac{\mu - \ln t}{\sigma}\right)$ | Survivor Function |
| **Mean (Expected Value)** | $E(X) = \exp\left(\mu + \dfrac{\sigma^2}{2}\right)$ | Arithmetic mean of $X$ |
| **Median** | $\text{Median}(X) = e^{\mu}$ | 50th percentile — always equals $e^\mu$ |
| **Mode** | $\text{Mode}(X) = \exp\left(\mu - \sigma^2\right)$ | Most probable value (peak of PDF) |
| **Variance** | $\text{Var}(X) = \left[\exp(\sigma^2) - 1\right] \cdot \exp(2\mu + \sigma^2)$ | Dispersion measure |
| **Standard Deviation** | $\text{SD}(X) = E(X) \cdot \sqrt{\exp(\sigma^2) - 1}$ | In original units of $X$ |
| **$k$-th Moment** | $E(X^k) = \exp\left(k\mu + \dfrac{k^2 \sigma^2}{2}\right)$ | Moment Generating Identity |
| **Failure Rate (Hazard)** | $h(t) = \dfrac{f(t)}{R(t)}$ | Non-monotonic (unimodal) |
| **MTTF** | $\text{MTTF} = \exp\left(\mu + \dfrac{\sigma^2}{2}\right)$ | Same as mean |
| **Skewness** | $\gamma_1 = \left[\exp(\sigma^2) - 1\right]^{1/2} \left[\exp(\sigma^2) + 2\right]$ | Always positive (right-skewed) |
| **Coefficient of Variation** | $\text{CV} = \sqrt{\exp(\sigma^2) - 1}$ | Dimensionless |

**Real-World Engineering Utility:**
- **Aerospace:** Fatigue life of aircraft components (cycles to crack initiation).
- **Civil Engineering:** Concrete compressive strength, soil bearing capacity.
- **Electronics:** Time-to-failure of semiconductor devices under electromigration.
- **Manufacturing:** Surface roughness, particle size distributions in powders.
- **Maintenance Engineering:** Repair times of complex equipment.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the PDF Using the Transformation Technique

**Setup:** Let $Y = \ln(X) \sim N(\mu, \sigma^2)$. We want to find the PDF of $X = e^Y$.

**Step 1: Write the PDF of $Y$.**
The PDF of a Normal random variable $Y \sim N(\mu, \sigma^2)$ is:
$$f_Y(y) = \frac{1}{\sigma \sqrt{2\pi}} \exp\left(-\frac{(y - \mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right), \quad -\infty < y < \infty$$

**Step 2: Define the monotonic transformation.**
Let $X = g(Y) = e^Y$. Since the exponential function is strictly monotonically increasing on the entire real line, the inverse transformation is:
$$Y = g^{-1}(X) = \ln(X), \quad \text{with } X > 0$$

**Step 3: Compute the Jacobian of the transformation.**
The Jacobian is the absolute value of the derivative of the inverse transformation with respect to $X$:
$$\left| \frac{dY}{dX} \right| = \left| \frac{d}{dX} \ln(X) \right| = \left| \frac{1}{X} \right| = \frac{1}{X}$$

**Step 4: Apply the change-of-variable formula.**
For a monotonic transformation, the PDF of $X$ is given by:
$$f_X(x) = f_Y(y) \cdot \left| \frac{dy}{dx} \right|$$

Substituting $y = \ln(x)$ and the Jacobian:
$$f_X(x) = \frac{1}{\sigma \sqrt{2\pi}} \exp\left(-\frac{(\ln x - \mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right) \cdot \frac{1}{x}$$

**Step 5: Final compact form (Lognormal PDF).**
$$\boxed{f_X(x) = \frac{1}{x \sigma \sqrt{2\pi}} \exp\left(-\frac{(\ln x - \mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right), \quad x > 0}$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the Mean $E(X)$

**Step 1: Set up the expectation integral.**
By definition of expected value for a continuous positive random variable:
$$E(X) = \int_{0}^{\infty} x \cdot f_X(x) \, dx$$

**Step 2: Substitute the PDF.**
$$E(X) = \int_{0}^{\infty} x \cdot \frac{1}{x \sigma \sqrt{2\pi}} \exp\left(-\frac{(\ln x - \mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right) dx$$

**Step 3: Simplify by canceling $x$ in numerator and denominator.**
$$E(X) = \int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{1}{\sigma \sqrt{2\pi}} \exp\left(-\frac{(\ln x - \mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right) dx$$

**Step 4: Use the substitution $y = \ln x$, so $x = e^y$ and $dx = e^y \, dy$.**
$$E(X) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \frac{1}{\sigma \sqrt{2\pi}} \exp\left(-\frac{(y - \mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right) \cdot e^y \, dy$$

**Step 5: Split the exponential $e^y$ and combine with the squared term in the exponent.**
$$E(X) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \frac{1}{\sigma \sqrt{2\pi}} \exp\left(-\frac{(y - \mu)^2}{2\sigma^2} + y\right) dy$$

**Step 6: Complete the square in the exponent.**
$$-\frac{(y - \mu)^2}{2\sigma^2} + y = -\frac{1}{2\sigma^2}\left[(y - \mu)^2 - 2\sigma^2 y\right]$$

Expanding $(y - \mu)^2 = y^2 - 2\mu y + \mu^2$:
$$= -\frac{1}{2\sigma^2}\left[y^2 - 2\mu y + \mu^2 - 2\sigma^2 y\right]$$
$$= -\frac{1}{2\sigma^2}\left[y^2 - 2y(\mu + \sigma^2) + \mu^2\right]$$
$$= -\frac{1}{2\sigma^2}\left[(y - (\mu + \sigma^2))^2 - (\mu + \sigma^2)^2 + \mu^2\right]$$

Expanding the constant terms:
$$-(\mu + \sigma^2)^2 + \mu^2 = -\mu^2 - 2\mu\sigma^2 - \sigma^4 + \mu^2 = -2\mu\sigma^2 - \sigma^4$$

Therefore, the exponent becomes:
$$-\frac{(y - (\mu + \sigma^2))^2}{2\sigma^2} + \mu + \frac{\sigma^2}{2}$$

**Step 7: Recognize the kernel as a normal PDF with shifted mean.**
$$E(X) = \exp\left(\mu + \frac{\sigma^2}{2}\right) \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \frac{1}{\sigma \sqrt{2\pi}} \exp\left(-\frac{(y - (\mu + \sigma^2))^2}{2\sigma^2}\right) dy$$

The integral is **identically 1** because it is the total area under a Normal PDF.

**Step 8: Final result.**
$$\boxed{E(X) = \exp\left(\mu + \frac{\sigma^2}{2}\right)}$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation of the Median and Mode

**Median Derivation:**
The median $m$ satisfies $F(m) = 0.5$:
$$F(m) = \Phi\left(\frac{\ln m - \mu}{\sigma}\right) = 0.5$$
$$\frac{\ln m - \mu}{\sigma} = \Phi^{-1}(0.5) = 0$$
$$\ln m = \mu \implies \boxed{m = e^{\mu}}$$

**Mode Derivation:**
The mode is the value of $x$ that maximizes $f_X(x)$. Taking the derivative and setting it to zero:
$$\frac{d}{dx} \ln f_X(x) = \frac{d}{dx}\left[-\ln(x) - \ln(\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}) - \frac{(\ln x - \mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right] = 0$$

$$-\frac{1}{x} - \frac{(\ln x - \mu)}{x \sigma^2} = 0$$

Multiplying by $x$:
$$-1 - \frac{\ln x - \mu}{\sigma^2} = 0 \implies \ln x - \mu = -\sigma^2 \implies \boxed{\text{Mode} = e^{\mu - \sigma^2}}$$

**Note the ordering:** Mode < Median < Mean (always, for lognormal).

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison: Lognormal vs. Other Distributions

| **Property** | **Exponential** | **Weibull** | **Normal** | **Lognormal** |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| Domain | $t \ge 0$ | $t \ge 0$ | $-\infty < t < \infty$ | $t > 0$ |
| Hazard $h(t)$ | Constant | Monotonic (Inc/Dec/Const) | — | **Non-monotonic (Unimodal)** |
| Skewness | Positive (2) | Positive/Negative/Zero | Zero | Always Positive |
| Use in Reliability | Random failures | General | Not used directly | **Wear-out, fatigue** |
| Parameters | 1 | 2 ($\alpha, \beta$) | 2 ($\mu, \sigma$) | 2 ($\mu, \sigma$) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flow Diagram: Conceptual Origin of the Lognormal Distribution

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Real World Phenomenon] --> B{Is the underlying process ADDITIVE or MULTIPLICATIVE?}
    B -- Additive --> C[Central Limit Theorem applies directly to X]
    C --> D[X follows NORMAL Distribution N mu sigma squared]
    B -- Multiplicative --> E[Take natural logarithm: Y equals ln X]
    E --> F[ln X is now an additive sum of small random effects]
    F --> G[Apply Central Limit Theorem to ln X]
    G --> H[Y follows NORMAL Distribution N mu sigma squared]
    H --> I[X follows LOGNORMAL Distribution LN mu sigma squared]
    D --> J[Engineering Application: Random errors, Measurement noise]
    I --> K[Engineering Application: Fatigue life, Corrosion depth, Repair time]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Functional Architecture of Lognormal Reliability Modeling

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[Input Stage]
        A1[Failure Time Data t1, t2, ..., tn] --> A2[Apply log transformation: yi equals ln ti]
    end
    subgraph PARAM[Parameter Estimation]
        A2 --> B1[Compute sample mean: mu hat equals mean of yi]
        A2 --> B2[Compute sample std dev: sigma hat equals std of yi]
    end
    subgraph MODEL[Model Functions]
        B1 --> C1[PDF: f of t]
        B2 --> C1
        B1 --> C2[CDF: F of t equals Phi of ln t minus mu over sigma]
        B2 --> C2
        C2 --> C3[Reliability: R of t equals 1 minus F of t]
        C1 --> C4[Hazard Rate: h of t equals f of t over R of t]
        C3 --> C4
    end
    subgraph OUTPUT[Reliability Outputs]
        C1 --> D1[MTTF equals exp of mu plus sigma squared over 2]
        C3 --> D2[R at time t0]
        C4 --> D3[h at time t0]
    end
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Shape Behavior of the Lognormal PDF

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Lognormal PDF shape depends on sigma] --> B[Small sigma less than 0.25]
    A --> C[Medium sigma between 0.25 and 1]
    A --> D[Large sigma greater than 1]
    B --> B1[Nearly symmetric, resembles Normal distribution]
    C --> C1[Moderately right skewed, single peak]
    D --> D1[Highly right skewed, long heavy tail]
    B1 --> E[Mode approximately equals Median approximately equals Mean]
    C1 --> F[Mode less than Median less than Mean]
    D1 --> G[Mode much less than Median, Mean pulled far right by tail]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### **Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)**

#### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define Lognormal distribution. State any four of its properties.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The Lognormal distribution is a continuous probability distribution of a random variable $X > 0$ such that $\ln(X) \sim N(\mu, \sigma^2)$.

**Four properties (½ mark each):**
1. The PDF is $f(x) = \dfrac{1}{x\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}} \exp\left(-\dfrac{(\ln x - \mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)$ for $x > 0$.
2. Mean: $E(X) = \exp\left(\mu + \dfrac{\sigma^2}{2}\right)$.
3. Median: $e^\mu$.
4. Variance: $\left[\exp(\sigma^2) - 1\right] \exp(2\mu + \sigma^2)$.
5. The distribution is always **positively skewed**.
6. The hazard function is **non-monotonic** (unimodal).

---

#### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Explain the hazard rate behavior of the lognormal distribution. Why is it unique?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The hazard rate of the lognormal distribution, $h(t) = f(t)/R(t)$, is **non-monotonic** — it increases initially, reaches a **maximum value**, and then **decreases** asymptotically to zero. This behavior is unique because:
- For the **exponential distribution**, $h(t)$ is constant.
- For the **Weibull distribution**, $h(t)$ is monotonic (either always increasing or always decreasing).
- The lognormal hazard is **unimodal**, making it the model of choice for systems that experience a **dominant wear-out mechanism** without a memoryless phase.

---

### **Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)**

#### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Derive the PDF of the Lognormal distribution from first principles using the transformation technique, clearly stating all assumptions and showing the Jacobian calculation. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**
- Assume $Y = \ln(X) \sim N(\mu, \sigma^2)$ with PDF $f_Y(y) = \dfrac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}} \exp\left(-\dfrac{(y-\mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)$. **(1 mark)**
- Apply the monotonic transformation $X = e^Y \implies Y = \ln X$, with domain $X > 0$. **(1 mark)**
- Compute the Jacobian: $\left| \dfrac{dY}{dX} \right| = \left| \dfrac{1}{X} \right| = \dfrac{1}{X}$ for $X > 0$. **(2 marks)**
- Apply the change-of-variables formula: $f_X(x) = f_Y(\ln x) \cdot \left| \dfrac{d(\ln x)}{dx} \right|$. **(1 mark)**
- Substitute to get: $f_X(x) = \dfrac{1}{x\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}} \exp\left(-\dfrac{(\ln x - \mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)$, for $x > 0$. **(2 marks)**

**(b)** The time-to-failure (in hours) of a certain type of bearing follows a Lognormal distribution with $\mu = 8.0$ and $\sigma = 0.5$. Compute: **(i)** the MTTF, **(ii)** the median life, and **(iii)** the reliability at $t = 3000$ hours. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**(i) MTTF** — Use the formula $E(X) = \exp\left(\mu + \sigma^2/2\right)$: **(2 marks)**
$$\text{MTTF} = \exp\left(8.0 + \frac{0.5^2}{2}\right) = \exp(8.0 + 0.125) = \exp(8.125)$$
$$\text{MTTF} \approx 3382.2 \text{ hours}$$

**(ii) Median life** — Use the formula $m = e^\mu$: **(2 marks)**
$$\text{Median} = e^{8.0} \approx 2980.96 \text{ hours}$$

**(iii) Reliability at $t = 3000$ hours** — Use $R(t) = \Phi\left(\dfrac{\mu - \ln t}{\sigma}\right)$: **(3 marks)**
$$R(3000) = \Phi\left(\frac{8.0 - \ln(3000)}{0.5}\right)$$
$$\ln(3000) \approx 8.0064$$
$$R(3000) = \Phi\left(\frac{8.0 - 8.0064}{0.5}\right) = \Phi\left(\frac{-0.0064}{0.5}\right) = \Phi(-0.0128)$$
$$R(3000) \approx 1 - \Phi(0.0128) \approx 1 - 0.5051 \approx 0.4949$$

So $R(3000) \approx 0.495$ or about **49.5%**.

---

#### **Question B** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** State and explain the relationship between the Normal and Lognormal distributions. Show mathematically that the mean of a Lognormal distribution is $\exp(\mu + \sigma^2/2)$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**
- **Relationship (2 marks):** If $X \sim \text{LN}(\mu, \sigma^2)$, then $Y = \ln(X) \sim N(\mu, \sigma^2)$. Conversely, if $Y \sim N(\mu, \sigma^2)$, then $X = e^Y \sim \text{LN}(\mu, \sigma^2)$. The Normal distribution governs the logarithm of the Lognormal variable.
- **Mean Derivation (5 marks):** Using the definition $E(X) = \int_0^\infty x \cdot f_X(x) \, dx$, and substituting the Lognormal PDF, with substitution $y = \ln x$, $dy = dx/x$, the integral reduces to:
$$E(X) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \exp(y) \cdot \frac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}} \exp\left(-\frac{(y-\mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right) dy$$
After completing the square in the exponent, the integral evaluates to $\exp(\mu + \sigma^2/2)$. Therefore, $E(X) = \exp(\mu + \sigma^2/2)$. **(Detailed algebraic steps similar to Section 3.2 above should be shown for full marks.)**

**(b)** For a certain component, the failure time follows a Lognormal distribution with $\mu = 6$ and $\sigma = 1.2$. Find: **(i)** the probability that the component fails before $t = 1000$ hours, **(ii)** the variance of the failure time, and **(iii)** the mode. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**(i) Probability of failure before $t = 1000$** — Use $F(t) = \Phi\left(\dfrac{\ln t - \mu}{\sigma}\right)$: **(3 marks)**
$$F(1000) = \Phi\left(\frac{\ln(1000) - 6}{1.2}\right) = \Phi\left(\frac{6.9078 - 6}{1.2}\right) = \Phi\left(\frac{0.9078}{1.2}\right)$$
$$F(1000) = \Phi(0.7565) \approx 0.7753$$

So $P(T < 1000) \approx 0.7753$ or about **77.5%**.

**(ii) Variance** — Use $\text{Var}(X) = \left[\exp(\sigma^2) - 1\right] \cdot \exp(2\mu + \sigma^2)$: **(2 marks)**
$$\sigma^2 = 1.44, \quad 2\mu + \sigma^2 = 12 + 1.44 = 13.44$$
$$\text{Var}(X) = [e^{1.44} - 1] \cdot e^{13.44} = [4.2207 - 1] \cdot 685,194 \approx 3.2207 \times 685{,}194$$
$$\text{Var}(X) \approx 2.207 \times 10^6 \text{ (hours)}^2$$

**(iii) Mode** — Use Mode $= e^{\mu - \sigma^2}$: **(2 marks)**
$$\text{Mode} = e^{6 - 1.44} = e^{4.56} \approx 95.58 \text{ hours}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Parameter confusion trap:** Many students confuse $\mu$ and $\sigma$ as the mean and standard deviation of $X$ — they are the mean and standard deviation of $\ln(X)$. The mean of $X$ is $\exp(\mu + \sigma^2/2)$, NOT $\mu$. **Loses 2 marks.**
> 2. **Domain violation:** Writing the PDF for $x \le 0$ will cost full marks. Always state $x > 0$ explicitly.
> 3. **Skewness sign:** The lognormal is **always** right-skewed (positive skewness). Stating "symmetric" is wrong.
> 4. **Order statistic:** Students must remember Mode < Median < Mean for lognormal — examiners frequently test this.
> 5. **Transformation direction:** When given data, always take $\ln$ first, then check for normality using probability plots or Shapiro-Wilk — never directly fit on the raw data.

---

### **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember** ✅

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist**

- **Definition:** $X \sim \text{LN}(\mu, \sigma^2)$ iff $\ln(X) \sim N(\mu, \sigma^2)$; valid only for $X > 0$.
- **PDF:** $f(x) = \dfrac{1}{x\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}} \exp\left(-\dfrac{(\ln x - \mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)$, $x > 0$.
- **CDF:** $F(x) = \Phi\left(\dfrac{\ln x - \mu}{\sigma}\right)$ — direct link to standard normal table.
- **Reliability:** $R(t) = 1 - \Phi\left(\dfrac{\ln t - \mu}{\sigma}\right) = \Phi\left(\dfrac{\mu - \ln t}{\sigma}\right)$.
- **Mean:** $E(X) = \exp\left(\mu + \dfrac{\sigma^2}{2}\right)$ — always greater than the median.
- **Median:** $e^\mu$ — equals the 50th percentile exactly.
- **Mode:** $\exp(\mu - \sigma^2)$ — always less than the median.
- **Variance:** $\left[\exp(\sigma^2) - 1\right] \exp(2\mu + \sigma^2)$.
- **Ordering:** $\text{Mode} < \text{Median} < \text{Mean}$ — invariant property.
- **Skewness:** Always positive (right-skewed), increasing with $\sigma$.
- **Hazard:** Non-monotonic (unimodal) — uniquely identifies lognormal.
- **MTTF:** Equals the mean, $\exp(\mu + \sigma^2/2)$.
- **Origin:** Arises from multiplicative random processes via the Central Limit Theorem.
- **Applications:** Fatigue life, corrosion depth, repair times, semiconductor failures, biological growth.
- **Quick Check:** If $\ln(\text{data})$ passes a normality test → use lognormal.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Serial Configuration
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEIET632 - RELIABILITY ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Serial Configuration # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In Reliability Engineering, a **Serial Configuration** (also called a **Series System**) is a system arrangement in which all components are connected in a sequence such that the successful operation of the **entire system** requires the successful operation of **every individual component**. The system fails if, and only if, **at least one** component fails. The weakest link in the chain dictates the overall system reliability. Mathematically, if a system consists of $n$ independent components with reliabilities $R_1, R_2, R_3, \ldots, R_n$, then the system reliability is given by: $$R_{system} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} R_i$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The term "serial" in reliability engineering refers strictly to the *functional/dependency relationship* between components, NOT the physical/electrical series wiring of a circuit. Two resistors in electrical parallel are functionally in series if both must work for the system to succeed. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuition **Real-World Analogy — The Old-Style Christmas Light String:** Imagine a string of 50 old-fashioned Christmas lights connected one after another. If even a single bulb burns out, the entire string goes dark. The string of lights is a perfect example of a serial system in reliability terms. You do not get any light unless **all 50 bulbs** are working simultaneously. **Engineering Analogy — The Rocket Launch Sequence:** Consider a space launch requiring three sequential events: (1) Main engine ignition succeeds, (2) Stage 1 separation succeeds, (3) Stage 2 ignition succeeds. If any one step fails, the entire mission fails. This is a series configuration from a mission-reliability perspective. > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight for Students:** In a series configuration, the overall system reliability is **always less than or equal to the reliability of the weakest (lowest-reliability) component**. Mathematically: $R_{system} \le \min(R_1, R_2, \ldots, R_n)$. The system can never be more reliable than its weakest link. ## 1.3 Standard Reliability Metrics in Serial Systems - **System Reliability, $R_s(t)$:** Probability that the system performs its intended function at time $t$. - **Component Reliability, $R_i(t)$:** Probability that component $i$ functions at time $t$. - **Component Failure Rate / Hazard Rate, $\lambda_i$:** Rate at which component $i$ fails, assuming exponential distribution. - **System Failure Rate, $\lambda_s$:** Equivalent hazard rate of the entire system. - **Mean Time To Failure (MTTF):** Expected time until first system failure. - **Unreliability / Probability of Failure, $F_s(t) = 1 - R_s(t)$.** # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Logic of a Series System A system is classified as a **series configuration** when it satisfies the following logical conditions: - The system performs its intended function **ONLY IF** every component performs its intended function. - The failure of **any single component** causes the entire system to fail. - The components are **statistically independent**, meaning the failure of one component does not influence the survival probability of the others. - The system state function is the **intersection** (AND logic) of all component states: $$\phi(X) = X_1 \cap X_2 \cap X_3 \cap \ldots \cap X_n$$ ### Step-by-Step Logical Derivation of the Master Formula 1. **Define component survival events:** Let $E_i$ denote the event that component $i$ survives at time $t$. Thus, $P(E_i) = R_i(t)$. 2. **Define system survival event:** The system survives if and only if all components survive: $E_s = E_1 \cap E_2 \cap \ldots \cap E_n$. 3. **Apply the multiplication rule for independent events:** $P(E_s) = P(E_1) \cdot P(E_2) \cdots P(E_n)$. 4. **Substitute the reliabilities:** This yields $R_s(t) = R_1(t) \cdot R_2(t) \cdots R_n(t)$. ## 2.2 Special Case: Exponential Failure Distribution When each component follows an **exponential failure distribution** (the most common KTU assumption), the component reliability is given by $R_i(t) = e^{-\lambda_i t}$. Substituting this into the master formula yields: $$R_s(t) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} e^{-\lambda_i t} = e^{-\left(\sum_{i=1}^{n} \lambda_i\right) t}$$ This leads to the **fundamental series-system theorem for exponential components:** $$\boxed{\lambda_s = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \lambda_i = \lambda_1 + \lambda_2 + \lambda_3 + \ldots + \lambda_n}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical KTU Concept — Hazard Rate Addition:** In a series system with exponentially distributed component lifetimes, the failure rates of the individual components **add up** to give the system failure rate. This is a direct consequence of the memoryless property of the exponential distribution. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | **S. No.** | **Quantity** | **Formula** | **Boundary Conditions** | **Units** | |:---:|:---|:---|:---|:---:| | 1 | System Reliability (General) | $R_s = \prod_{i=1}^{n} R_i$ | All $R_i \in [0, 1]$ | Dimensionless | | 2 | System Unreliability | $F_s = 1 - R_s$ | $F_s \in [0, 1]$ | Dimensionless | | 3 | Component Reliability (Exponential) | $R_i(t) = e^{-\lambda_i t}$ | $t \ge 0, \lambda_i \ge 0$ | Dimensionless | | 4 | System Reliability (Exponential) | $R_s(t) = e^{-\lambda_s t}$ | $t \ge 0$ | Dimensionless | | 5 | Equivalent System Failure Rate | $\lambda_s = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \lambda_i$ | Exponential case only | failures/hour | | 6 | MTTF (Exponential case) | $\text{MTTF}_s = \dfrac{1}{\lambda_s} = \dfrac{1}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} \lambda_i}$ | Exponential case only | hours | | 7 | Lower Bound on System Reliability | $R_s \le \min_i(R_i)$ | Always true | Dimensionless | | 8 | MTTF (General / Non-Exponential) | $\text{MTTF}_s = \int_{0}^{\infty} R_s(t) \, dt$ | Riemann-integrable case | hours | ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility Series configuration analysis is foundational in: - **Aerospace systems:** Rocket stages, flight control surfaces, redundant sensor chains. - **Nuclear power plants:** Safety shutdown systems where all relays must trip correctly. - **Computer networks:** A pipeline of routers — failure of any router disconnects the link. - **Manufacturing lines:** Sequential operations in a production line (drilling → milling → assembly). - **Medical devices:** Patient monitoring — failure of any sensor can be life-threatening. > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Design Implication:** Because system reliability is the **product** of component reliabilities, adding a low-reliability component to a series system dramatically degrades the overall reliability. This is why **redundancy** (parallel configurations) is added to critical components. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: System Reliability from Independent Component Reliabilities **Given:** A system has $n$ components connected in series. Component $i$ has reliability $R_i$. All components are statistically independent. **To Derive:** The expression for $R_s$ (system reliability). ### Step 1 — Define the survival events. Let $S_i$ be the event that component $i$ functions successfully. The probability of this event is $P(S_i) = R_i$. The system functions only if all $n$ components function: $$S_{system} = S_1 \cap S_2 \cap S_3 \cap \ldots \cap S_n$$ ### Step 2 — Apply the multiplication rule for independent events. For statistically independent events, the probability of the intersection equals the product of the individual probabilities: $$P(S_{system}) = P(S_1) \cdot P(S_2) \cdot P(S_3) \cdots P(S_n)$$ ### Step 3 — Substitute component reliabilities. Replacing $P(S_i)$ with $R_i$: $$R_s = R_1 \cdot R_2 \cdot R_3 \cdots R_n = \prod_{i=1}^{n} R_i$$ ### Step 4 — Express as a product of exponentials. For exponential distribution, $R_i = e^{-\lambda_i t}$: $$R_s = \prod_{i=1}^{n} e^{-\lambda_i t}$$ ### Step 5 — Simplify using exponent addition rules. $$\boxed{R_s(t) = \exp\left[-\left(\sum_{i=1}^{n} \lambda_i\right) t\right] = e^{-\lambda_s t}}$$ ## 3.2 Derivation 2: MTTF of a Series System (Exponential Case) **Given:** A series system with equivalent failure rate $\lambda_s$. **To Derive:** The Mean Time To Failure (MTTF) of the system. ### Step 1 — State the MTTF definition. MTTF is the expected value of the time-to-failure random variable $T$: $$\text{MTTF} = E[T] = \int_{0}^{\infty} t \cdot f(t) \, dt$$ where $f(t)$ is the probability density function of failure. ### Step 2 — Use the alternate integral form via the reliability function. A standard result in reliability theory states: $$\text{MTTF} = \int_{0}^{\infty} R(t) \, dt$$ This can be derived by integrating the density function by parts. ### Step 3 — Substitute the exponential reliability function. For our series system, $R_s(t) = e^{-\lambda_s t}$: $$\text{MTTF}_s = \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-\lambda_s t} \, dt$$ ### Step 4 — Evaluate the integral. $$\text{MTTF}_s = \left[ \frac{-e^{-\lambda_s t}}{\lambda_s} \right]_{0}^{\infty} = \frac{0 - (-1)}{\lambda_s} = \frac{1}{\lambda_s}$$ ### Step 5 — Expand $\lambda_s$ in terms of component failure rates. $$\boxed{\text{MTTF}_s = \frac{1}{\lambda_1 + \lambda_2 + \lambda_3 + \ldots + \lambda_n}}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Crucial Observation:** The MTTF of a series system is **always less than or equal to the smallest individual component MTTF**. This is a direct consequence of the addition of hazard rates in the denominator. ## 3.3 Worked Example (Numerical Demonstration) **Problem Statement:** A system consists of 3 components connected in series. Their reliabilities over a 1000-hour mission are $R_1 = 0.95$, $R_2 = 0.92$, $R_3 = 0.98$. Assuming constant failure rates, find: (a) The system reliability over the 1000-hour mission. (b) The system unreliability. (c) The MTTF of the system, given failure rates $\lambda_1 = 5 \times 10^{-5}$/hour, $\lambda_2 = 8 \times 10^{-5}$/hour, $\lambda_3 = 2 \times 10^{-5}$/hour. ### Part (a) — System Reliability Using $R_s = R_1 \cdot R_2 \cdot R_3$: $$R_s = 0.95 \times 0.92 \times 0.98$$ Step-by-step multiplication: - $0.95 \times 0.92 = 0.874$ - $0.874 \times 0.98 = 0.85652$ $$\boxed{R_s \approx 0.8565}$$ ### Part (b) — System Unreliability $$F_s = 1 - R_s = 1 - 0.85652 = 0.14348$$ $$\boxed{F_s \approx 0.1435 \text{ (i.e., 14.35\% probability of failure)}}$$ ### Part (c) — MTTF Add the failure rates: $$\lambda_s = 5 \times 10^{-5} + 8 \times 10^{-5} + 2 \times 10^{-5} = 15 \times 10^{-5} \text{ per hour}$$ $$\text{MTTF}_s = \frac{1}{\lambda_s} = \frac{1}{15 \times 10^{-5}} = \frac{10^5}{15} \approx 6666.67 \text{ hours}$$ $$\boxed{\text{MTTF}_s \approx 6666.67 \text{ hours}}$$ > [!WARNING] > **Common Student Mistake:** Confusing the system MTTF (≈6666.67 hours) with the mission time (1000 hours). MTTF is a *lifetime metric*, not a *mission reliability* metric. For mission reliability, use the exponential formula $R_s(1000) = e^{-\lambda_s \cdot 1000}$ directly. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram of a Generic Series Configuration The following Mermaid block diagram represents a series system with $n$ functional blocks connected in a strictly sequential dependency. Each block must succeed for the system to function. ```mermaid graph LR inputA[System Input] --> compA[Component 1
Reliability R1] compA --> compB[Component 2
Reliability R2] compB --> compC[Component 3
Reliability R3] compC --> compD[Component n
Reliability Rn] compD --> outputA[System Output] failA{Failure of
ANY component} -.-> sysfailA[System Failure] compA -.-> failA compB -.-> failA compC -.-> failA compD -.-> failA ``` ## 4.2 Functional Architecture Flow of Series Reliability Computation The following flow chart depicts the algorithm/logic a KTU examiner expects you to follow when solving a series configuration problem. ```mermaid graph TD startA[Start: Series Reliability Problem] --> stepA1[Identify number of components n] stepA1 --> stepA2{Components have
exponential lifetimes?} stepA2 -->|Yes| stepA3[Collect failure rates lambda_i] stepA2 -->|No| stepA4[Collect reliabilities R_i directly] stepA3 --> stepA5[Compute lambda_s = sum of lambda_i] stepA5 --> stepA6[Compute R_s = exp of minus lambda_s times t] stepA4 --> stepA7[Compute R_s = product of all R_i] stepA6 --> stepA8[Compute MTTF_s = 1 divided by lambda_s] stepA7 --> stepA9[Report final R_s and F_s] stepA8 --> stepA9 stepA9 --> endA[End: Final answer reported] ``` ## 4.3 Truth Table / State Matrix for a 3-Component Series System The following table (rendered as a Mermaid-style logic depiction) shows the system state as a function of individual component states. **1 = Working, 0 = Failed**. The system works **ONLY** when all components are 1. | Component 1 | Component 2 | Component 3 | System State | |:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:| | 1 | 1 | 1 | **WORKING** | | 1 | 1 | 0 | FAILED | | 1 | 0 | 1 | FAILED | | 1 | 0 | 0 | FAILED | | 0 | 1 | 1 | FAILED | | 0 | 1 | 0 | FAILED | | 0 | 0 | 1 | FAILED | | 0 | 0 | 0 | FAILED | > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Insight:** Out of $2^3 = 8$ possible state combinations, only **1** combination results in a working system. The probability of being in that single working state is $R_1 \cdot R_2 \cdot R_3$, which directly motivates the multiplication formula for series systems. ## 4.4 Series System Reliability Curve (Conceptual Schematic) ```mermaid graph LR axisA[Time t = 0
R_s = 1] --> curveA[Exponential Decay
R_s decreases with time] curveA --> axisB[Time t increases
R_s approaches 0] axisA --> lambdaEq[Slope governed by lambda_s = sum of lambda_i] lambdaEq --> axisB ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Interpretation:** The reliability curve of a series system with exponential components is itself an exponentially decaying curve, with the decay rate being the **sum** of individual component decay rates. The faster the components fail individually, the steeper the system reliability decay. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: Conceptual Definition **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 1]** **Q:** Define a *series configuration* in a reliability block diagram. State the formula for the reliability of a series system comprising $n$ independent components. **Model Answer (3 Marks):** A series configuration is a system arrangement in which all components must function for the system to function. The failure of any single component causes the entire system to fail. The reliability of a series system with $n$ independent components is given by: $$R_s = \prod_{i=1}^{n} R_i = R_1 \cdot R_2 \cdot R_3 \cdots R_n$$ If the components follow exponential distributions, the system reliability simplifies to $R_s(t) = e^{-\lambda_s t}$, where $\lambda_s = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \lambda_i$. --- ### Question 2: Direct Short-Answer **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 1]** **Q:** A series system has two components with reliabilities $R_1 = 0.9$ and $R_2 = 0.8$. Find the system reliability and unreliability. **Model Answer (3 Marks):** System reliability: $$R_s = R_1 \times R_2 = 0.9 \times 0.8 = 0.72$$ System unreliability: $$F_s = 1 - R_s = 1 - 0.72 = 0.28$$ Hence, the system has a 72% probability of success and 28% probability of failure over the specified mission time. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice ### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Dec 2024 Style]** **Q:** A system consists of **three components in series**. The failure rates of the components are: - $\lambda_1 = 0.001$ failures/hour - $\lambda_2 = 0.002$ failures/hour - $\lambda_3 = 0.003$ failures/hour Calculate the following: **(a)** The equivalent failure rate of the system and the MTTF of the system. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** The reliability of the system for a mission time of $t = 100$ hours. Also determine the unreliability. **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution: **Part (a) — Equivalent Failure Rate and MTTF (7 Marks)** Step 1: For a series system with exponential component lifetimes, the equivalent system failure rate is the sum of the individual failure rates: $$\lambda_s = \lambda_1 + \lambda_2 + \lambda_3$$ $$\lambda_s = 0.001 + 0.002 + 0.003 = 0.006 \text{ failures/hour}$$ Step 2: The MTTF of the system is the reciprocal of the equivalent failure rate: $$\text{MTTF}_s = \frac{1}{\lambda_s} = \frac{1}{0.006} = 166.67 \text{ hours}$$ $$\boxed{\lambda_s = 0.006 \text{ failures/hour}, \quad \text{MTTF}_s = 166.67 \text{ hours}}$$ **Part (b) — System Reliability and Unreliability for t = 100 hours (7 Marks)** Step 1: Apply the exponential reliability formula for the system: $$R_s(100) = e^{-\lambda_s \cdot t} = e^{-0.006 \times 100} = e^{-0.6}$$ Step 2: Evaluate the numerical value: $$e^{-0.6} \approx 0.5488$$ Step 3: Compute the unreliability: $$F_s(100) = 1 - R_s(100) = 1 - 0.5488 = 0.4512$$ $$\boxed{R_s(100) \approx 0.5488, \quad F_s(100) \approx 0.4512}$$ --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Dec 2024 Style]** **Q:** A series system has **four components** with reliabilities $R_1 = 0.95$, $R_2 = 0.90$, $R_3 = 0.85$, and $R_4 = 0.99$ for a 500-hour mission. Calculate: **(a)** The system reliability and unreliability for the 500-hour mission. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** The MTTF of each component assuming exponential distribution, and the MTTF of the entire system. **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution: **Part (a) — System Reliability and Unreliability (7 Marks)** Step 1: The system reliability for a series system is the product of individual reliabilities: $$R_s = R_1 \cdot R_2 \cdot R_3 \cdot R_4$$ Step 2: Perform the multiplication step-by-step: - $R_1 \cdot R_2 = 0.95 \times 0.90 = 0.855$ - $R_3 \cdot R_4 = 0.85 \times 0.99 = 0.8415$ - $R_s = 0.855 \times 0.8415 = 0.7195$ Step 3: Compute unreliability: $$F_s = 1 - R_s = 1 - 0.7195 = 0.2805$$ $$\boxed{R_s \approx 0.7195, \quad F_s \approx 0.2805}$$ **Part (b) — Component MTTFs and System MTTF (7 Marks)** Step 1: For exponential distribution, $R_i = e^{-\lambda_i t}$, so $\lambda_i = -\frac{\ln(R_i)}{t}$ with $t = 500$ hours. - $\lambda_1 = -\frac{\ln(0.95)}{500} = -\frac{-0.0513}{500} = 1.026 \times 10^{-4}$/hour - $\lambda_2 = -\frac{\ln(0.90)}{500} = -\frac{-0.1054}{500} = 2.107 \times 10^{-4}$/hour - $\lambda_3 = -\frac{\ln(0.85)}{500} = -\frac{-0.1625}{500} = 3.250 \times 10^{-4}$/hour - $\lambda_4 = -\frac{\ln(0.99)}{500} = -\frac{-0.01005}{500} = 2.010 \times 10^{-5}$/hour Step 2: Sum them for the system failure rate: $$\lambda_s = 1.026 \times 10^{-4} + 2.107 \times 10^{-4} + 3.250 \times 10^{-4} + 0.201 \times 10^{-4}$$ $$\lambda_s = 6.584 \times 10^{-4} \text{ failures/hour}$$ Step 3: Compute system MTTF: $$\text{MTTF}_s = \frac{1}{\lambda_s} = \frac{1}{6.584 \times 10^{-4}} \approx 1518.84 \text{ hours}$$ $$\boxed{\text{MTTF}_s \approx 1518.84 \text{ hours}}$$ --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Formula Misuse:** Students often write $R_s = 1 - \prod(1-R_i)$, which is the *parallel* system formula. In a **series** system, you multiply the reliabilities directly — you do NOT subtract them from 1. > 2. **Unit Errors:** Forgetting to express $\lambda$ in **per hour** (or per the time unit of the mission) leads to massive numerical errors. Always check units. > 3. **Logarithm Sign Errors:** When computing $\lambda_i$ from $R_i$, students often drop the negative sign, leading to a *negative* failure rate — physically impossible. > 4. **Independence Assumption:** The formula $R_s = \prod R_i$ holds only if components are **statistically independent**. If components share a common-cause failure mode, a conditional probability correction is needed (covered in higher modules). ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Series Configuration:** All components must work; failure of any one fails the system. - **Master Formula:** $R_s = \prod_{i=1}^{n} R_i$ — applies only to **statistically independent** components. - **Exponential Special Case:** $R_s(t) = e^{-\lambda_s t}$, where $\lambda_s = \sum \lambda_i$. - **MTTF of Series (Exponential):** $\text{MTTF}_s = \dfrac{1}{\lambda_s}$. - **Inequality Rule:** $R_s \le \min(R_1, R_2, \ldots, R_n)$ — the system is never more reliable than its weakest component. - **Unreliability:** $F_s = 1 - R_s$. - **Mission Reliability vs MTTF:** MTTF is a lifetime metric; mission reliability is computed at a specific time $t$. - **Hazard Rate Addition:** A defining property of series systems with exponential components. - **Independence is Critical:** Always check the independence assumption before applying the product formula. - **KTU 2024 Expectation:** Be ready to derive formulas, solve numerical problems with multiple sub-parts, and identify when the series assumption applies.

topics.title: Parallel Configuration
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET632 - RELIABILITY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Parallel Configuration

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In Reliability Engineering, a **Parallel Configuration** (also called a **Redundant Configuration** or **Active Parallel System**) is a system arrangement in which **two or more components are connected such that the system continues to function as long as at least one component is operating successfully**. The system fails only when **ALL** components in the parallel group have failed simultaneously.

Mathematically, the unreliability of the parallel system is the **intersection** of the unreliabilities of all individual components. For *n* independent components arranged in parallel:

$$Q_s(t) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} Q_i(t)$$

where $Q_s(t)$ is the system unreliability and $Q_i(t) = 1 - R_i(t)$ is the unreliability of the $i^{th}$ component. The corresponding **system reliability** is:

$$R_s(t) = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n} \left[1 - R_i(t)\right]$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Parallel configuration is the *fundamental building block* of redundancy theory. The keyword in any KTU question is *"system fails ONLY when all components fail"* — this single phrase distinguishes parallel from series configuration.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a commercial passenger aircraft equipped with **four engines**. During flight, the aircraft can continue to operate safely — and even complete its journey — if **one, two, or even three engines** fail, provided at least **one engine** keeps running. This is parallel redundancy in action: each engine is an independent "path" to mission success, and the aircraft (the system) survives as long as any single path remains intact.

A simpler household example is the **parallel wiring of multiple tube-lights in a hall**. If one tube burns out, the others still illuminate the room. The hall becomes dark *only* when every tube has failed.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Summary:** Parallel configuration = "Backup Plan." Series configuration = "Weakest Link." In KTU problems, look for the phrase *"the system works if at least one component works"* to instantly identify parallel topology.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics Introduced
