The key performance indicators for a parallel configuration, expressed in standard reliability units, are:

- **System Reliability, $R_s(t)$** — dimensionless probability, $0 \leq R_s \leq 1$.
- **System Unreliability, $Q_s(t)$** — dimensionless probability.
- **Failure Rate (Hazard Rate), $\lambda$** — failures per unit time (typically per hour or per million hours).
- **Mean Time To Failure (MTTF)** or **Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF)** — measured in hours.
- **System Mission Time, $t$** — operational duration under analysis.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Reasoning

For a parallel configuration of *n* independent components, the operational logic can be decomposed into the following logical stages:

- **Step 1 — Define Component States:** Each component $i$ is in one of two mutually exclusive states at time $t$: *Operational* (probability $R_i$) or *Failed* (probability $Q_i = 1 - R_i$).
- **Step 2 — System Success Condition:** The system succeeds when *at least one* component succeeds. Equivalently, the system fails only when *all* $n$ components fail simultaneously.
- **Step 3 — Apply Independence:** Because components are statistically independent, the joint probability of all $n$ components failing is the **product** of individual failure probabilities.
- **Step 4 — Compute System Unreliability:** $Q_s(t) = Q_1 \cdot Q_2 \cdot Q_3 \cdots Q_n$.
- **Step 5 — Compute System Reliability:** By the complement rule, $R_s(t) = 1 - Q_s(t)$.

### 2.1.1 Special Case — Identical Components

When all *n* components have equal reliability $R$ and equal failure rate $\lambda$ (exponential distribution), the formula collapses elegantly:

$$R_s(t) = 1 - (1 - R)^n$$

And substituting the exponential law $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$:

$$R_s(t) = 1 - (1 - e^{-\lambda t})^n$$

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **single most important page** for KTU exam preparation on Parallel Configuration. Memorize every row.

| **#** | **Parameter** | **General Formula** | **Identical Components ($n$ identical units)** | **Units / Boundary** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | System Unreliability | $Q_s = \prod_{i=1}^{n} Q_i$ | $Q_s = (1-R)^n$ | Dimensionless, $0 \leq Q_s \leq 1$ |
| 2 | System Reliability | $R_s = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n}(1-R_i)$ | $R_s = 1 - (1-R)^n$ | Dimensionless, $0 \leq R_s \leq 1$ |
| 3 | Reliability (Exponential) | $R_s = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n}(1-e^{-\lambda_i t})$ | $R_s = 1 - (1-e^{-\lambda t})^n$ | Function of time $t$ |
| 4 | System Failure Rate (Equivalent) | $\lambda_s = -\dfrac{\ln R_s(t)}{t}$ | $\lambda_s = -\dfrac{\ln\left[1-(1-e^{-\lambda t})^n\right]}{t}$ | Failures per hour |
| 5 | MTTF / MTBF (Non-Repairable) | $\displaystyle\int_0^\infty R_s(t)\,dt$ | $\displaystyle\sum_{k=1}^{n} \frac{(-1)^{k+1}}{k\lambda}\binom{n}{k}$ | Hours |
| 6 | MTTF for $n=2$, equal $\lambda$ | — | $\dfrac{3}{2\lambda}$ | Hours |
| 7 | MTTF for $n=3$, equal $\lambda$ | — | $\dfrac{11}{6\lambda}$ | Hours |
| 8 | MTTF General Series | $\displaystyle\sum_{k=1}^{n} \frac{(-1)^{k+1}}{k\lambda}\binom{n}{k}$ | Same | Hours |

### 2.2.1 Boundary Behaviour (Critical for KTU Numericals)

- As $n \to \infty$ with $\lambda t > 0$: $R_s(t) \to 1$, but **never reaches 1** — parallel redundancy is *asymptotically perfect* but not absolutely so.
- At $t = 0$: $R_s(0) = 1$ (system starts fully healthy).
- At $t \to \infty$: $R_s(t) \to 0$ (all components eventually fail).

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Parallel configurations are deployed wherever **mission-critical continuity** outweighs the cost penalty of redundancy:

- **Aerospace:** Multi-engine aircraft, redundant flight computers (triplex or quadruplex).
- **Power Systems:** Backup diesel generators in hospitals; parallel UPS modules in data centers.
- **Hydraulics:** Dual hydraulic pumps in heavy machinery.
- **Networking:** Multiple ISP links for guaranteed connectivity.
- **Nuclear Reactors:** Redundant coolant pumps and shutdown rods.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Insight:** Adding redundant components in parallel *always* improves reliability. However, the *marginal* improvement diminishes with each additional unit — a fact known as the *diminishing returns of redundancy* — and is a frequent KTU conceptual question.

## 2.4 Important Distinction: Active vs. Standby Parallel

- **Active (Hot) Parallel:** All components operate simultaneously; all share the load.
- **Standby (Cold/Warm) Redundancy:** Backup components are switched in only after primary failure. This belongs to a *separate* KTU module — do **not** confuse the two in exam answers.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — General Parallel System Reliability

**Starting Premise:** A parallel system of *n* independent components succeeds if **at least one** component succeeds.

Let $A_i$ be the event that component $i$ is operational at time $t$. Then:

$$\text{System Success} = A_1 \cup A_2 \cup \cdots \cup A_n$$

By De Morgan's Law, the complement (system failure) is:

$$\text{System Failure} = \overline{A_1} \cup \overline{A_2} \cup \cdots \cup \overline{A_n} = \overline{A_1} \cap \overline{A_2} \cap \cdots \cap \overline{A_n}$$

That is, the system fails when **every** component has failed. Since components fail independently:

$$Q_s(t) = P(\overline{A_1} \cap \overline{A_2} \cap \cdots \cap \overline{A_n}) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(\overline{A_i}) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} Q_i(t)$$

Finally, applying the complement rule $R_s = 1 - Q_s$:

$$\boxed{R_s(t) = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n} \left[1 - R_i(t)\right]}$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Reliability for *n* Identical Exponential Components

Assume all $n$ components have identical failure rate $\lambda$. Then:

$$R_i(t) = e^{-\lambda t} \quad \text{for all } i$$

Substituting into the general formula:

$$R_s(t) = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n}\left[1 - e^{-\lambda t}\right] = 1 - (1 - e^{-\lambda t})^n$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Mean Time To Failure (MTTF) for Identical Exponential Components

By definition:

$$\text{MTTF} = \int_0^\infty R_s(t)\,dt = \int_0^\infty \left[1 - (1 - e^{-\lambda t})^n\right] dt$$

Apply the binomial expansion $(1 - e^{-\lambda t})^n = \displaystyle\sum_{k=0}^{n} \binom{n}{k}(-1)^k e^{-k\lambda t}$:

$$\text{MTTF} = \int_0^\infty \left[1 - \sum_{k=0}^{n} \binom{n}{k}(-1)^k e^{-k\lambda t}\right] dt$$

The $k=0$ term of the sum equals $1$, cancelling the leading $1$:

$$\text{MTTF} = \int_0^\infty \sum_{k=1}^{n} \binom{n}{k}(-1)^{k+1} e^{-k\lambda t}\,dt$$

Interchanging integration and summation (uniform convergence guaranteed):

$$\text{MTTF} = \sum_{k=1}^{n} (-1)^{k+1}\binom{n}{k}\int_0^\infty e^{-k\lambda t}\,dt$$

Each integral evaluates to $\dfrac{1}{k\lambda}$:

$$\boxed{\text{MTTF} = \sum_{k=1}^{n} \frac{(-1)^{k+1}}{k\lambda}\binom{n}{k}}$$

## 3.4 Numerical Verification — Worked Examples

### 3.4.1 Example A: Two Identical Components in Parallel

**Problem:** Two identical components, each with reliability $R = 0.9$ and constant failure rate $\lambda = 0.001$ per hour, are connected in parallel. Compute (i) system reliability at $t = 100$ hours, (ii) MTTF.

**Solution:**

**Step (i) — Compute component reliability at $t=100$ h:**

$$R(100) = e^{-\lambda t} = e^{-(0.001)(100)} = e^{-0.1} = 0.9048$$

**Step (ii) — Compute system reliability:**

$$R_s(100) = 1 - (1 - 0.9048)^2 = 1 - (0.0952)^2 = 1 - 0.00906 = 0.9909$$

So the parallel system reliability (99.09%) is significantly higher than a single component (90.48%).

**Step (iii) — Compute MTTF using the derived formula for $n=2$:**

$$\text{MTTF} = \frac{1}{\lambda}\left[2 - \frac{1}{2}\right] = \frac{3}{2\lambda} = \frac{3}{2 \times 0.001} = 1500 \text{ hours}$$

A single component would have MTTF $= 1/\lambda = 1000$ hours — so parallel redundancy **boosts MTTF by 50%**.

### 3.4.2 Example B: Three Non-Identical Components in Parallel

**Problem:** Three components with reliabilities $R_1 = 0.95$, $R_2 = 0.90$, $R_3 = 0.85$ are connected in parallel. Compute system reliability.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute individual unreliabilities:**

$$Q_1 = 1 - 0.95 = 0.05, \quad Q_2 = 1 - 0.90 = 0.10, \quad Q_3 = 1 - 0.85 = 0.15$$

**Step 2 — Apply the parallel unreliability formula:**

$$Q_s = Q_1 \cdot Q_2 \cdot Q_3 = 0.05 \times 0.10 \times 0.15 = 0.00075$$

**Step 3 — Compute system reliability:**

$$R_s = 1 - 0.00075 = 0.99925$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** Even with one component as low as 85% reliable, the three-unit parallel system achieves **99.925% reliability**. This dramatic uplift is precisely why KTU examiners love asking parallel problems.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Active Parallel Configuration

The block diagram below shows *n* components arranged in parallel between a common input bus and a common output bus. Current/flow/signal can travel through **any** single path; hence the system survives all single and multiple faults except total exhaustion.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inNode[Input Source] --> busA[Common Input Bus]
    busA --> compA[Component A]
    busA --> compB[Component B]
    busA --> compC[Component C]
    busA --> compD[Component n]
    compA --> busB[Common Output Bus]
    compB --> busB
    compC --> busB
    compD --> busB
    busB --> outNode[System Output]
```

## 4.2 Reliability Block Diagram — Symbolic Form

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    start((S)) --> p1((1))
    p1 --> r1[R1]
    r1 --> m1((2))
    p1 --> r2[R2]
    r2 --> m1
    p1 --> r3[R3]
    r3 --> m1
    p1 --> rn[Rn]
    rn --> m1
    m1 --> end1((T))
```

**Reading the diagram:** Path exists from start node **S** to terminal node **T** through **any** of the branches $R_1, R_2, R_3, \ldots, R_n$. The system is operational if **at least one** of the parallel branches is intact.

## 4.3 Failure Logic Tree — When Does the Parallel System Fail?

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    root{System Failed?}
    root -- No --> sysok[System Operational]
    root -- Yes --> all1{All n Failed?}
    all1 -- Yes --> sysfail[System Failure Confirmed]
    all1 -- No --> sysok
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Visual Tip:** When drawing a parallel system in your answer script, always show a *common input node* on the left, *branches fanning out* in the middle, and a *common output node* on the right. Examiners award full method marks when this convention is followed, even if arithmetic slips occur.

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    src[Power Source] --> ctrl{Parallel Switch Matrix}
    ctrl --> cA[Path A: Comp 1 + Comp 2]
    ctrl --> cB[Path B: Comp 3]
    ctrl --> cC[Path C: Comp n]
    cA --> monitor[Health Monitor]
    cB --> monitor
    cC --> monitor
    monitor --> load[System Load]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**  
> *"Define parallel configuration in reliability engineering. How does it differ from a series configuration?"*

**Model Answer (Concise, ~80 words):**

A parallel configuration is a redundant arrangement in which two or more components are connected so that the system continues to operate as long as **at least one** component functions. The system fails only when **all** components fail. In contrast, a series configuration requires **every** component to function for system success; failure of **any single** component causes system failure. Parallel improves reliability through redundancy, while series is governed by the *weakest-link* principle.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**  
> *"A system has three components in parallel with reliabilities 0.9, 0.8 and 0.7. Calculate the system reliability."*

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Compute unreliabilities:**

$$Q_1 = 0.1, \quad Q_2 = 0.2, \quad Q_3 = 0.3$$

**Step 2 — Apply parallel unreliability formula:**

$$Q_s = 0.1 \times 0.2 \times 0.3 = 0.006$$

**Step 3 — Compute system reliability:**

$$R_s = 1 - 0.006 = 0.994$$

> **Final Answer:** $\boxed{R_s = 0.994}$

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question Choice A (14 Marks)
> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**  
> *(a)* Derive the expression for the reliability of *n* identical components connected in parallel, each with constant failure rate $\lambda$, for a mission time $t$. *(7 marks)*  
> *(b)* A parallel system consists of three identical components, each with $\lambda = 0.002$ failures/hour. Compute: (i) the reliability of the system at $t = 200$ hours, (ii) the MTTF of the system. *(7 marks)*

#### Solution to A(a) — Derivation (7 Marks)

**Statement of Configuration:**  
Consider *n* independent, identical components, each with constant failure rate $\lambda$. For exponential distribution, the reliability of a single component is:

$$R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$$

**Definition of System Success:**  
The parallel system operates successfully if **at least one** component is operational at time $t$. The system fails only when **all** $n$ components have failed.

**Probability of Single Component Failure:**

$$Q(t) = 1 - R(t) = 1 - e^{-\lambda t}$$

**Probability of All Components Failing (System Unreliability):**  
Because failures are statistically independent:

$$Q_s(t) = [Q(t)]^n = (1 - e^{-\lambda t})^n$$

**System Reliability (by complement):**

$$\boxed{R_s(t) = 1 - (1 - e^{-\lambda t})^n}$$

This is the required expression. ∎

#### Solution to A(b) — Numerical Computation (7 Marks)

**Given:** $n = 3$, $\lambda = 0.002$ per hour, $t = 200$ hours.

**Part (i) — System Reliability at $t = 200$ h:**

**Step 1 — Compute single-component reliability:**

$$R(200) = e^{-0.002 \times 200} = e^{-0.4} = 0.6703$$

**Step 2 — Compute single-component unreliability:**

$$Q(200) = 1 - 0.6703 = 0.3297$$

**Step 3 — Apply parallel reliability formula:**

$$R_s(200) = 1 - (0.3297)^3 = 1 - 0.03583 = 0.9642$$

> **Answer (i):** $\boxed{R_s(200) \approx 0.9642}$

**Part (ii) — MTTF of the System:**

For $n = 3$ identical exponential components, the MTTF formula simplifies to:

$$\text{MTTF} = \frac{1}{\lambda}\left[3 - \frac{3}{2} + \frac{1}{3}\right] = \frac{1}{\lambda}\left[\frac{18 - 9 + 2}{6}\right] = \frac{11}{6\lambda}$$

Substituting $\lambda = 0.002$:

$$\text{MTTF} = \frac{11}{6 \times 0.002} = \frac{11}{0.012} = 916.67 \text{ hours}$$

> **Answer (ii):** $\boxed{\text{MTTF} \approx 916.67 \text{ hours}}$

For comparison, a single component would have MTTF $= 1/\lambda = 500$ hours. So **parallel redundancy boosts MTTF by ~83%** in this configuration.

---

### Question Choice B (14 Marks)
> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**  
> *(a)* Explain with a neat block diagram the concept of parallel redundancy. Discuss how the unreliability of the system is computed. *(7 marks)*  
> *(b)* A system has two pumps arranged in parallel for a water supply network. Each pump has a constant failure rate of $0.005$ per hour. Determine the probability that the system survives 100 hours. If the mission time is increased to 500 hours, what is the new system reliability? Comment on the result. *(7 marks)*

#### Solution to B(a) — Conceptual Explanation (7 Marks)

**Block Diagram Description:**

A parallel redundant system of *n* components is represented by drawing *n* horizontal branches between two common nodes (left node = input, right node = output). The system is operational if **at least one** of the branches remains connected.

**Concept of Unreliability Computation:**

Let $Q_i$ be the unreliability of component $i$, i.e., the probability that component $i$ has failed by time $t$. Since components fail independently, the probability that *all* $n* components have failed simultaneously is the product:

$$Q_s(t) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} Q_i(t)$$

The system reliability is then obtained by the complement rule:

$$R_s(t) = 1 - Q_s(t) = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n} [1 - R_i(t)]$$

**Key Insight:** In parallel configuration, the *unreliabilities multiply*, while in series configuration, the *reliabilities multiply*. This is the cornerstone difference between the two topologies.

**Diagrammatic Sketch (to be drawn in answer book):**

```
       ┌──[ Comp 1 ]──┐
S ─────┼──[ Comp 2 ]──┼───── T
       └──[ Comp n ]──┘
```

System succeeds if any one of $n$ paths from S to T is intact.

#### Solution to B(b) — Numerical Computation (7 Marks)

**Given:** Two pumps in parallel ($n = 2$), $\lambda = 0.005$ failures/hour.

**Part (i) — System Reliability at $t = 100$ hours:**

**Step 1 — Single-pump reliability at 100 h:**

$$R_1(100) = e^{-0.005 \times 100} = e^{-0.5} = 0.6065$$

**Step 2 — Single-pump unreliability:**

$$Q_1(100) = 1 - 0.6065 = 0.3935$$

**Step 3 — System unreliability (both pumps fail):**

$$Q_s(100) = (0.3935)^2 = 0.1548$$

**Step 4 — System reliability:**

$$R_s(100) = 1 - 0.1548 = 0.8452$$

> **Answer (i):** $\boxed{R_s(100) \approx 0.8452}$

**Part (ii) — System Reliability at $t = 500$ hours:**

**Step 1 — Single-pump reliability at 500 h:**

$$R_1(500) = e^{-0.005 \times 500} = e^{-2.5} = 0.0821$$

**Step 2 — Single-pump unreliability:**

$$Q_1(500) = 1 - 0.0821 = 0.9179$$

**Step 3 — System unreliability:**

$$Q_s(500) = (0.9179)^2 = 0.8425$$

**Step 4 — System reliability:**

$$R_s(500) = 1 - 0.8425 = 0.1575$$

> **Answer (ii):** $\boxed{R_s(500) \approx 0.1575}$

**Comment:** As mission time increases from 100 h to 500 h, the system reliability drops sharply from 84.52% to 15.75%. This demonstrates that **parallel redundancy cannot indefinitely sustain system operation** — beyond a certain mission time, the unreliability of individual components compounds, and the parallel advantage erodes. For very long missions, parallel redundancy must be supplemented by maintainability, repair, or replacement strategies.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Commonly Lose Marks in Parallel Configuration Problems:**
> 
> 1. **Series vs. Parallel Confusion (–2 to –4 marks):** The most frequent error is applying $R_s = \prod R_i$ (series formula) to a parallel problem. Always re-read the problem statement: *"system works if at least one works"* → parallel; *"system works only if all work"* → series.
> 
> 2. **Forgetting the Complement (–2 marks):** Many students compute $Q_s$ correctly but forget to subtract from 1 to get $R_s$. Always end with the final $R_s = 1 - Q_s$ step.
> 
> 3. **Missing the Independence Assumption (–1 to –2 marks):** The parallel formulas $R_s = 1 - \prod(1-R_i)$ and $Q_s = \prod Q_i$ require **statistical independence** of component failures. State this explicitly in your derivation. If components share a common-cause failure mode, the formula is invalid.
> 
> 4. **Units in MTTF (–1 mark):** Always state MTTF in **hours** (or appropriate time unit). A bare numerical answer without units loses a mark.
> 
> 5. **Mermaid/Sketch Quality (–1 mark):** A parallel block diagram without distinct branches (drawn like a series) signals weak conceptual understanding. Draw clear parallel branches between two common nodes.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Parallel Configuration**

- **Definition:** System succeeds if **at least one** of *n* parallel components works; system fails only when **all** components fail.
- **Core Formula (General):** $R_s(t) = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n}\left[1 - R_i(t)\right]$
- **Core Formula (Identical):** $R_s(t) = 1 - (1 - e^{-\lambda t})^n$
- **Unreliability Formula:** $Q_s(t) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} Q_i(t)$ — *unreliabilities multiply in parallel.*
- **MTTF (Identical Exponential):** $\displaystyle\text{MTTF} = \sum_{k=1}^{n} \frac{(-1)^{k+1}}{k\lambda}\binom{n}{k}$
- **Special Cases:** MTTF for $n=2$ is $\dfrac{3}{2\lambda}$; for $n=3$ is $\dfrac{11}{6\lambda}$.
- **Boundary Conditions:** $R_s(0) = 1$; $R_s(\infty) = 0$; $R_s$ *strictly increases* as $n$ increases.
- **Independence Assumption:** Component failures must be statistically independent.
- **Active vs. Standby:** Parallel = Active (all units powered); Standby = passive redundancy (separate KTU topic).
- **Engineering Use Cases:** Aircraft engines, hospital backup power, network links, redundant pumps.
- **Common Mistake:** Mixing series and parallel formulas — always read the problem twice before writing.
- **Key Intuition:** Parallel is *redundancy*; series is *weakest link*. Both appear frequently in KTU Part B (14-mark) questions and form the foundation for **complex / hybrid systems** in later modules.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Combined Series Parallel Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEIET632 - RELIABILITY ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Combined Series Parallel Systems ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition > [!NOTE] > **Combined Series-Parallel System:** A complex reliability configuration in which subsystems are connected in a mixed arrangement such that some components are arranged in **series** with one or more **parallel** sub-blocks (or vice versa). The system reliability is evaluated by progressively reducing the block diagram into an equivalent single block using series and parallel reduction rules. A combined series–parallel system is neither a pure series system nor a pure parallel system. It is a **hierarchical reliability network** in which the overall system functions successfully **only if there exists at least one continuous working path from input to output** through the reliability block diagram (RBD). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Module 1 explicitly trains students to *derive the reliability expression* of combined configurations by *systematic block reduction* — this is a high-frequency 14-mark question in KTU ESE papers. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition) Imagine a **car's electrical ignition system** powering the engine: - The **battery** and **ignition coil** are in **series** — both must work for spark to occur. - However, the **car has two spark plugs per cylinder wired in parallel** (redundancy) — even if one fails, the other fires. - The (battery–coil) series chain is then in **series** with the parallel spark-plug block. This is a **combined series-parallel** arrangement — the entire system works if the series chain delivers current AND at least one spark plug fires. This is exactly what reliability engineers model for ignition, power grids, computer motherboards, and aircraft control systems. ### 1.3 Key Reliability Metrics | Metric | Symbol | Unit | Definition | |---|---|---|---| | Reliability | $R(t)$ | dimensionless (0–1) | Probability the component/system performs its intended function for time $t$ under stated conditions | | Unreliability | $F(t) = 1 - R(t)$ | dimensionless | Probability of failure | | Failure Rate | $\lambda$ | failures/hour | Rate at which components fail | | MTTF | $\bar{t}$ | hours | Mean Time To Failure | | System Reliability | $R_s$ | dimensionless | Reliability of the complete assembled network | > [!NOTE] > Throughout this module, components are assumed to be **independent**, **non-repairable** during the mission time, and operating with **constant failure rate** $\lambda$. The exponential reliability model $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ is the standard assumption for KTU problems. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Fundamental Reduction Rules A combined series-parallel system is solved by **iterative application of two rules**: **Rule 1 — Series Reduction:** If blocks $A$ and $B$ are connected in series (both must work for the path to function): $$R_{series} = R_A \cdot R_B$$ For $n$ components in series: $$R_{series} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} R_i = R_1 \cdot R_2 \cdots R_n$$ **Rule 2 — Parallel Reduction:** If blocks $A$ and $B$ are connected in parallel (at least one must work): $$R_{parallel} = 1 - (1 - R_A)(1 - R_B) = R_A + R_B - R_A R_B$$ For $n$ identical components in parallel each with reliability $R$: $$R_{parallel} = 1 - (1 - R)^n$$ ### 2.2 The Reduction Strategy (Why & How) - **Why:** A complex network with loops and nested structures cannot be evaluated with a single closed-form expression. Reduction breaks it into manageable two-block operations. - **How:** Identify the *innermost* series or parallel sub-block, replace it with its equivalent reliability, redraw the simplified RBD, and repeat until only one block remains. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Exam Rule:** If a network contains a **bridge (non-series-parallel) structure**, simple reduction fails. Module 1 *only* covers **true series-parallel** configurations. The delta-wye or conditional probability method is for Module 2. ### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet | # | Configuration | Reliability Formula | Key Condition | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Single component | $R_s = R$ | — | | 2 | $n$ in series | $R_s = \prod_{i=1}^{n} R_i$ | All must work | | 3 | 2 in parallel | $R_s = R_1 + R_2 - R_1 R_2$ | At least one works | | 4 | $n$ in parallel | $R_s = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n}(1 - R_i)$ | At least one works | | 5 | $n$ identical in parallel | $R_s = 1 - (1 - R)^n$ | All $R_i = R$ | | 6 | Series of $m$ parallel blocks | $R_s = \prod_{j=1}^{m}\left[1 - \prod_{i \in B_j}(1 - R_i)\right]$ | Outer series, inner parallel | | 7 | Parallel of $m$ series blocks | $R_s = 1 - \prod_{j=1}^{m}\left[1 - \prod_{i \in B_j} R_i\right]$ | Outer parallel, inner series | | 8 | Exponential form | $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ | Constant failure rate | | 9 | Series MTTF (identical) | $\bar{t}_s = \dfrac{\bar{t}}{n}$ | For $n$ identical components | | 10 | Parallel MTTF (2 identical) | $\bar{t}_p = \bar{t} + \dfrac{\bar{t}}{2} = \dfrac{3\bar{t}}{2}$ | For 2 identical components | > [!CAUTION] > In the table above, all vertical bars in equations use LaTeX `\vert` or are written as products/parentheses — **never** use the markdown-breaking raw `$\vert x \vert$` inside a table cell. ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility Combined series-parallel reliability models are foundational in: - **Aerospace:** Avionics buses with redundant channels feeding critical flight computers. - **Nuclear Power Plants:** Multiple safety-grade pumps in parallel, arranged in series with the reactor cooling loop. - **Data Centers:** Server racks with dual power supplies (parallel) connected to UPS units (series). - **Automotive:** Brake-by-wire systems with redundant ECUs. - **Telecommunications:** Mesh base-station backhauls with primary and standby routers. > [!NOTE] > The reduction method taught here scales directly into **Fault Tree Analysis (FTA)** and **Reliability Block Diagrams (RBD)** used in ISO 26262 (automotive), DO-178C (aerospace), and IEC 61508 (industrial). ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Series System Reliability **Given:** $n$ independent components with reliabilities $R_1, R_2, \ldots, R_n$ connected in series. **The Series Assumption:** The system functions **only if every** component functions. Hence, system success is the **intersection** of all component success events. $$A_s = A_1 \cap A_2 \cap \cdots \cap A_n$$ By the **multiplicative law of probability for independent events**: $$R_s = P(A_s) = P(A_1) \cdot P(A_2) \cdots P(A_n)$$ $$\boxed{R_s = \prod_{i=1}^{n} R_i}$$ For constant failure rate $\lambda_i$ and mission time $t$: $$R_i(t) = e^{-\lambda_i t}$$ $$R_s(t) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} e^{-\lambda_i t} = e^{-\left(\sum_{i=1}^{n} \lambda_i\right) t}$$ Hence equivalent failure rate $\lambda_s = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \lambda_i$ and **MTTF**: $$\bar{t}_s = \frac{1}{\lambda_s} = \frac{1}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} \lambda_i}$$ ### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Parallel System Reliability **Given:** $n$ independent components in parallel; system works if **at least one** works. System fails **only if all** fail. The failure event is: $$\bar{A}_s = \bar{A}_1 \cap \bar{A}_2 \cap \cdots \cap \bar{A}_n$$ By independence: $$P(\bar{A}_s) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(\bar{A}_i) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} (1 - R_i)$$ Therefore: $$R_s = 1 - P(\bar{A}_s)$$ $$\boxed{R_s = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n}(1 - R_i)}$$ For $n$ **identical** components each with reliability $R$: $$R_s = 1 - (1 - R)^n$$ **MTTF for 2 identical components in parallel** (with $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$): $$R_p(t) = 1 - (1 - e^{-\lambda t})^2 = 2e^{-\lambda t} - e^{-2\lambda t}$$ $$\bar{t}_p = \int_{0}^{\infty} R_p(t)\, dt = \int_{0}^{\infty}(2e^{-\lambda t} - e^{-2\lambda t})\, dt$$ $$= 2 \cdot \frac{1}{\lambda} - \frac{1}{2\lambda} = \frac{2}{\lambda} - \frac{1}{2\lambda}$$ $$\boxed{\bar{t}_p = \frac{3}{2\lambda} = \frac{3\bar{t}}{2}}$$ ### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (Series-Parallel Reduction) **Problem Statement (KTU Style):** A system consists of **Block A** in series with a **parallel combination of B and C**, which is then in series with **Block D**. Given $R_A = 0.95$, $R_B = 0.90$, $R_C = 0.85$, $R_D = 0.92$. Calculate the system reliability. **Solution (Step-by-Step Reduction):** **Step 1 — Reduce the inner parallel block (B ∥ C):** $$R_{BC} = 1 - (1 - R_B)(1 - R_C)$$ $$= 1 - (1 - 0.90)(1 - 0.85)$$ $$= 1 - (0.10)(0.15)$$ $$= 1 - 0.0150 = 0.9850$$ **Step 2 — Reduced RBD is now: A in series with (BC) in series with D.** **Step 3 — Multiply all three in series:** $$R_s = R_A \cdot R_{BC} \cdot R_D$$ $$= (0.95)(0.9850)(0.92)$$ $$= (0.95 \times 0.9850) \times 0.92$$ $$= 0.93575 \times 0.92$$ $$\boxed{R_s = 0.86089 \approx 0.8609 \;(\text{or } 86.09\%)}$$ ### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix | Configuration | Reliability Direction | MTTF Behavior | Failure Probability | |---|---|---|---| | Series | Always **lower** than the lowest $R_i$ | Decreases sharply with $n$ | $F_s = 1 - \prod R_i$ (additive trend) | | Parallel | Always **higher** than the highest $R_i$ | Increases with redundancy | $F_p = \prod(1 - R_i)$ (multiplicative reduction) | | Combined | Bounded by topology | Topology-dependent | Computed via reduction order | ### 3.5 Second Worked Example (Outer Parallel, Inner Series) **Problem:** A system has two **identical parallel channels**. Each channel contains 3 components in series with reliabilities $R_1 = 0.9$, $R_2 = 0.8$, $R_3 = 0.95$. The system works if at least one channel works. Find $R_s$. **Step 1 — Reliability of one channel (series of 3):** $$R_{ch} = R_1 \cdot R_2 \cdot R_3 = 0.9 \times 0.8 \times 0.95$$ $$= 0.72 \times 0.95 = 0.684$$ **Step 2 — Two identical channels in parallel:** $$R_s = 1 - (1 - R_{ch})^2 = 1 - (1 - 0.684)^2$$ $$= 1 - (0.316)^2$$ $$= 1 - 0.09986$$ $$\boxed{R_s = 0.9001 \approx 90.01\%}$$ **Engineering Insight:** Despite each channel having only 68.4% reliability, having two such channels in parallel boosts the system reliability to over 90% — this is the **power of redundancy**. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Series-Parallel Topology ```mermaid graph LR IN[System Input] --> A[Block A
R equals 0.95] A --> B[Block B
R equals 0.90] A --> C[Block C
R equals 0.85] B --> D[Block D
R equals 0.92] C --> D D --> OUT[System Output] style A fill:#FFD580,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style B fill:#A8D5BA,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style C fill:#A8D5BA,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style D fill:#FFD580,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style IN fill:#E0E0E0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style OUT fill:#E0E0E0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px ``` > [!NOTE] > The yellow blocks (A, D) are in **series** with the parallel green sub-block (B ∥ C). This is the topology analyzed in Section 3.3. ### 4.2 Mermaid Reduction Flow Diagram ```mermaid graph TD S1[Stage 1: Original RBD
A in series with B parallel C in series with D] --> S2[Stage 2: Reduce Parallel Sub-block B parallel C
Compute R_BC equals 1 minus 1 minus R_B times 1 minus R_C] S2 --> S3[Stage 3: Simplified RBD
Three series blocks A BC and D] S3 --> S4[Stage 4: Final Series Reduction
R_s equals R_A times R_BC times R_D] S4 --> S5[Stage 5: Final Answer
R_s equals 0.8609] style S1 fill:#FFE5B4,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style S2 fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style S3 fill:#FFE5B4,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style S4 fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style S5 fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px ``` ### 4.3 Mermaid Decision Tree for Reduction Strategy ```mermaid graph TD Q1{Is the network
purely series?} -->|Yes| A1[Apply series formula
R_s equals product of R_i] Q1 -->|No| Q2{Is the network
purely parallel?} Q2 -->|Yes| A2[Apply parallel formula
R_s equals 1 minus product of 1 minus R_i] Q2 -->|No| Q3{Are there series
chains inside a
parallel wrapper?} Q3 -->|Yes| A3[Reduce each inner series chain first
then combine as parallel] Q3 -->|No| Q4{Are there parallel
pairs inside a
series wrapper?} Q4 -->|Yes| A4[Reduce each inner parallel pair first
then combine as series] Q4 -->|No| A5[Non-series-parallel Bridge
Use delta-wye or Bayes method
Module 2 topic] style Q1 fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style Q2 fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style Q3 fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style Q4 fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style A1 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style A2 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style A3 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style A4 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style A5 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** *Define a combined series-parallel system. Why is the block reduction method used in reliability analysis?* **Model Answer (3 Marks):** A combined series-parallel system is one in which subsystems are arranged in a mixed configuration — some blocks in series with parallel sub-blocks, or vice versa — and cannot be classified as purely series or purely parallel. The **block reduction method** is used because: 1. (1 mark) A single closed-form formula cannot directly evaluate such networks. 2. (1 mark) Reduction breaks the network into simpler two-block operations (series or parallel). 3. (1 mark) It allows systematic, error-free computation of overall system reliability $R_s$ by progressive simplification until a single equivalent block remains. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** *Write the general reliability expression for (i) two components in series and (ii) two components in parallel.* **Model Answer (3 Marks):** (i) **Two components in series** (both must work): $$R_s = R_1 \cdot R_2 \quad \text{(1 mark)}$$ (ii) **Two components in parallel** (at least one must work): $$R_s = 1 - (1 - R_1)(1 - R_2) = R_1 + R_2 - R_1 R_2 \quad \text{(1 mark)}$$ **Distinction** (1 mark): In series, reliabilities multiply — giving a value **lower** than either component. In parallel, failure probabilities multiply — giving a system reliability **higher** than the best individual component. --- ### Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Pattern with Internal Choice) --- **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — July 2024]** **(a)** Derive the reliability expression for a system consisting of **two identical components in parallel**, each with reliability $R$ and constant failure rate $\lambda$. Hence obtain the **MTTF** of the parallel configuration. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A system has the following RBD: Component A is in **series** with a parallel combination of (B and C), and this entire block is in **series** with component D. Given $R_A = 0.96$, $R_B = 0.92$, $R_C = 0.88$, $R_D = 0.90$. Compute the **system reliability** and the **probability of failure** for a mission time of 1000 hours, assuming each component has $\lambda = 0.0001$/hr. **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution to Question A **Part (a) — Derivation (7 Marks):** Let the two identical components have reliability $R = R_1 = R_2$. The system works if **at least one** component works. System failure occurs only if **both** fail: $$P(\text{failure}) = (1 - R)(1 - R) = (1 - R)^2$$ Therefore, system reliability: $$R_p = 1 - (1 - R)^2 = 2R - R^2 \quad \text{(2 marks)}$$ With exponential reliability $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$: $$R_p(t) = 1 - (1 - e^{-\lambda t})^2 = 2e^{-\lambda t} - e^{-2\lambda t} \quad \text{(1 mark)}$$ The MTTF is: $$\bar{t}_p = \int_{0}^{\infty} R_p(t)\, dt$$ $$= \int_{0}^{\infty} \left(2e^{-\lambda t} - e^{-2\lambda t}\right) dt \quad \text{(1 mark)}$$ $$= 2 \cdot \frac{1}{\lambda} - \frac{1}{2\lambda} \quad \text{(1 mark)}$$ $$\boxed{\bar{t}_p = \frac{3}{2\lambda} = \frac{3\bar{t}}{2}} \quad \text{(1 mark for final expression)}$$ **Physical meaning** (1 mark): Two identical components in parallel increase MTTF by **50%** over a single component — a dramatic improvement justifying redundancy investment in critical systems. --- **Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 Marks):** **Step 1:** Compute mission reliability of each component for $t = 1000$ hours, $\lambda = 0.0001$/hr: $$R(t) = e^{-\lambda t} = e^{-0.0001 \times 1000} = e^{-0.1}$$ $$e^{-0.1} \approx 0.9048 \quad \text{(1 mark)}$$ Hence the reliability values **scale** — but the problem gives direct $R$ values, so we interpret them as the mission-time reliabilities: $$R_A = 0.96, \quad R_B = 0.92, \quad R_C = 0.88, \quad R_D = 0.90$$ **Step 2:** Reduce the parallel sub-block (B ∥ C): $$R_{BC} = 1 - (1 - R_B)(1 - R_C)$$ $$= 1 - (1 - 0.92)(1 - 0.88)$$ $$= 1 - (0.08)(0.12)$$ $$= 1 - 0.0096 = 0.9904 \quad \text{(2 marks)}$$ **Step 3:** Reduce the outer series chain A — (BC) — D: $$R_s = R_A \cdot R_{BC} \cdot R_D$$ $$= (0.96)(0.9904)(0.90) \quad \text{(2 marks)}$$ $$= 0.95078 \times 0.90$$ $$\boxed{R_s = 0.8557 \;(\text{or } 85.57\%)} \quad \text{(1 mark for final answer)}$$ **Step 4:** Probability of failure: $$F_s = 1 - R_s = 1 - 0.8557$$ $$\boxed{F_s = 0.1443 \;(\text{or } 14.43\%)} \quad \text{(1 mark)}$$ --- **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 — Alternative Pattern]** **(a)** A system has **three components in parallel**, each with reliability 0.80. Calculate: (i) the system reliability, (ii) the unreliability, and (iii) compare the result with a series system of three identical components each with $R = 0.80$. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A complex system consists of **two parallel channels**, where each channel contains **two components in series** with reliabilities 0.9 and 0.85. If the system requires **at least one channel** to function, calculate the system reliability. **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution to Question B **Part (a) — Three Identical Components (7 Marks):** **(i) Parallel system reliability** (at least one works): $$R_p = 1 - (1 - R)^n = 1 - (1 - 0.80)^3$$ $$= 1 - (0.20)^3 = 1 - 0.008$$ $$\boxed{R_p = 0.992} \quad \text{(2 marks)}$$ **(ii) Unreliability:** $$F_p = 1 - R_p = 1 - 0.992$$ $$\boxed{F_p = 0.008} \quad \text{(1 mark)}$$ **(iii) Comparison with series system of three identical components:** $$R_{series} = R^3 = (0.80)^3 = 0.512 \quad \text{(2 marks)}$$ **Comparison Table** (2 marks): | Configuration | Reliability | Unreliability | |---|---|---| | 3 in parallel | 0.992 | 0.008 | | 3 in series | 0.512 | 0.488 | **Conclusion:** Parallel configuration offers **99.2%** reliability versus **51.2%** for series — a **~94% relative improvement** — demonstrating why redundancy is critical for high-reliability systems. --- **Part (b) — Two Parallel Channels with Series Inner (7 Marks):** **Step 1:** Reliability of one channel (two components in series): $$R_{ch} = R_1 \cdot R_2 = 0.9 \times 0.85 = 0.765 \quad \text{(2 marks)}$$ **Step 2:** Two identical channels in parallel: $$R_s = 1 - (1 - R_{ch})^2$$ $$= 1 - (1 - 0.765)^2$$ $$= 1 - (0.235)^2$$ $$= 1 - 0.055225$$ $$\boxed{R_s = 0.9448 \;(\text{or } 94.48\%)} \quad \text{(3 marks)}$$ **Step 3:** Engineering interpretation (2 marks): Even though each channel has only **76.5%** reliability, the parallel-redundant architecture boosts the overall system reliability to **94.48%** — an increase of nearly 18 percentage points. This is the *quantitative justification* for using dual-redundant signal paths in safety-critical systems like aircraft flight controls and medical ventilators. --- ### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Combined Series-Parallel Problems:** > > 1. **Order-of-reduction error** (–2 to –4 marks): Students often apply the parallel formula to a series block or vice versa. **Always reduce the innermost sub-block first.** > > 2. **Forgetting the failure probability product in parallel** (–2 marks): Writing $R_p = R_1 + R_2$ without the $-R_1 R_2$ correction term. This is the **#1 mistake** in KTU valuation reports. > > 3. **Confusing identical vs. non-identical** formulas (–1 mark): Use $1 - (1-R)^n$ **only when all $R_i$ are equal**. Otherwise use the general $1 - \prod(1 - R_i)$. > > 4. **Skipping the redraw step** (–1 mark): Examiners award marks for showing the simplified RBD after each reduction stage. Always redraw. > > 5. **Not stating assumptions** (–1 mark): Explicitly state *independent components, constant failure rate, non-repairable during mission*. > > 6. **Unit confusion with $\lambda$ and MTTF** (–1 mark): State units clearly. $\lambda$ is in **failures per hour**; MTTF is in **hours**. --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Combined Series-Parallel Systems** > > - [x] A **combined series-parallel system** contains both series and parallel sub-blocks. > - [x] **Series rule:** $R_s = \prod R_i$ (all must work). > - [x] **Parallel rule:** $R_s = 1 - \prod(1 - R_i)$ (at least one must work). > - [x] For $n$ identical parallel components: $R_s = 1 - (1-R)^n$. > - [x] For 2 identical parallel components: $\bar{t}_p = \dfrac{3\bar{t}}{2}$. > - [x] For $n$ identical series components: $\bar{t}_s = \dfrac{\bar{t}}{n}$. > - [x] **Reduction order matters:** Always reduce the innermost sub-block first. > - [x] **Exponential model:** $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ — used when constant $\lambda$ is given. > - [x] **Bridge (non-series-parallel) networks are NOT in Module 1** — they require conditional probability or delta-wye transformation (Module 2). > - [x] **Independence assumption** must always be stated explicitly in the solution. > - [x] **Redraw the simplified RBD** after every reduction — KTU examiners reward this with marks. > - [x] Series reliability is **always less** than the lowest component reliability. > - [x] Parallel reliability is **always greater** than the highest component reliability. > - [x] Real-world: redundancy (parallel) boosts reliability, but every additional component adds cost, weight, and failure-mode complexity.

topics.title: K-out-of-M Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET632 - RELIABILITY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** K-out-of-M Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**
> A **K-out-of-M system** (denoted as K/M or KooM system) is a redundant configuration consisting of **M identical components**, out of which the system functions successfully **if and only if at least K components are in the operating state**. Failure of the system occurs only when fewer than K components are functioning — i.e., when (M − K + 1) or more components have failed.

Mathematically, the system reliability $R_{K/M}$ is a function of:
- **M** = Total number of components in the system
- **K** = Minimum number of components required for successful operation
- **R(t)** = Reliability of each identical, independent component at time *t*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy: The Boeing 737 Twin-Engine Aircraft**
> Consider a commercial aircraft with **M = 4 engines** (quad-jet configuration). Airworthiness regulations mandate that the aircraft can safely complete a flight if **at least K = 2 engines** are operational. This is a classic **2-out-of-4** redundant system. One engine failing is acceptable; even two may fail — but if a third fails, the system (aircraft mission) fails.

### 1.3 Special Boundary Cases (Crucial for KTU Board Exam)

| Boundary Case | Configuration | Equivalent System |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| **K = 1** | 1-out-of-M | **Parallel System** (all components must fail) |
| **K = M** | M-out-of-M | **Series System** (any single failure = system down) |
| **K = 2, M = 3** | 2-out-of-3 | **TMR** (Triple Modular Redundancy — majority voting) |
| **K = (M+1)/2** | Majority logic | **Majority Voting System** |

## 1.4 Key Underlying Assumptions

For the standard K-out-of-M mathematical model to apply:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Assumptions Checklist (High-Frequency KTU Question)**
> 1. All **M** components are **statistically independent** of each other.
> 2. All **M** components are **identical**, with the same reliability $R_i(t) = R(t)$.
> 3. Components are **non-repairable** during the mission time (for instantaneous reliability).
> 4. Components have **constant hazard rate** $\lambda$ (if exponential failure distribution is assumed).
> 5. The system is **s-state** (s = 2): each component is either fully working or completely failed.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Logical Derivation Framework

The K-out-of-M system fails when the number of working components falls below K.

**Step 1 — Define State:** Let $X$ be the random variable representing the number of working components at time *t*.

**Step 2 — Identify Working Components:** Since each component works independently with probability $R$ and fails with probability $Q = 1 - R$, the number of working components follows a **Binomial distribution** with parameters $(M, R)$.

$$P(X = i) = \binom{M}{i} R^i (1-R)^{M-i}$$

**Step 3 — Apply Success Criterion:** The system functions if $X \geq K$. Hence, by the **law of total probability**:

$$R_{K/M} = P(X \geq K) = \sum_{i=K}^{M} \binom{M}{i} R^i (1-R)^{M-i}$$

**Step 4 — Equivalent Failure Complement:** Using the complement rule (often useful in numerical problems):

$$R_{K/M} = 1 - \sum_{i=0}^{K-1} \binom{M}{i} R^i (1-R)^{M-i}$$

## 2.2 The Why Behind the Formula

- **Why binomial?** Each component has only two outcomes (success/failure) and they are independent — the textbook conditions for a Bernoulli trial repeated M times.
- **Why summation from i = K?** We sum the probabilities of ALL favorable states where at least K components survive.
- **Why identical components?** It drastically simplifies analysis. For non-identical components, a **product-sum expansion** or inclusion-exclusion principle must be used.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Print & Stick on Wall)

> [!NOTE]
> **Master Formula Table — Memorize Before Exam**

| # | Formula | Description | Application |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | $R_{K/M} = \sum_{i=K}^{M} \binom{M}{i} R^i (1-R)^{M-i}$ | General K-out-of-M system reliability | All problems |
| 2 | $R_{1/M} = 1 - (1-R)^M$ | Parallel system (K=1) | Redundancy |
| 3 | $R_{M/M} = R^M$ | Series system (K=M) | No redundancy |
| 4 | $R_{2/3} = 3R^2 - 2R^3$ | TMR system | Majority voting |
| 5 | $R_{2/4} = 6R^2 - 8R^3 + 3R^4$ | 2-out-of-4 system | Aircraft engines |
| 6 | $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ | Component reliability (exponential) | Time-dependent analysis |
| 7 | $R_{K/M}(t) = \sum_{i=K}^{M} \binom{M}{i} e^{-i\lambda t}(1 - e^{-\lambda t})^{M-i}$ | Time-dependent K-out-of-M system | Mission reliability |
| 8 | $\text{MTTF}_{K/M} = \int_0^{\infty} R_{K/M}(t)\, dt$ | System mean time to failure | Repairable/non-repairable |
| 9 | $\text{MTTF}_{K/M} = \sum_{i=K}^{M} \frac{1}{i\lambda}$ | MTTF (exponential components) | High-yield shortcut |
| 10 | $f_{K/M}(t) = -\dfrac{d}{dt}R_{K/M}(t)$ | System failure density | Hazard analysis |

## 2.4 Engineering Real-World Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industrial Applications (Frequently asked in viva & interviews)**
> - **Aerospace:** Multi-engine aircraft (e.g., 3-out-of-4 engines on a C-17 Globemaster)
> - **Nuclear Power:** Multiple redundant coolant pumps (2-out-of-3, 3-out-of-4)
> - **Digital Electronics:** TMR (Triple Modular Redundant) processors for spacecraft
> - **Data Centers:** RAID-5 storage needs (M-1)/M disks working for data access
> - **Voting Logic:** Decision-making circuits where majority rule prevails
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Master Formula

We start with the assumption that the M components are **independent** and **identical**, each with reliability $R$ and unreliability $Q = 1 - R$.

### Step 1: Probability that exactly *i* components work out of M

For a specific combination of *i* working and (M−i) failed components, the probability is:

$$P(\text{exactly } i \text{ work}) = R^i \cdot Q^{M-i}$$

But there are $\binom{M}{i}$ ways to choose which *i* out of M components work. Therefore:

$$P_i = \binom{M}{i} R^i (1-R)^{M-i}$$

### Step 2: Sum over all favorable states (i ≥ K)

The system works if at least K components work. We sum over all values from K to M:

$$R_{K/M} = P(i = K) + P(i = K+1) + \cdots + P(i = M)$$

$$R_{K/M} = \sum_{i=K}^{M} \binom{M}{i} R^i (1-R)^{M-i}$$

### Step 3: Time-dependent form using exponential law

If each component has constant failure rate $\lambda$, then $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$. Substituting:

$$R_{K/M}(t) = \sum_{i=K}^{M} \binom{M}{i} e^{-i\lambda t} (1 - e^{-\lambda t})^{M-i}$$

This is the **mission reliability** — probability the system survives a mission of duration *t*.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 1: 2-out-of-3 TMR System

**Problem:** A TMR (Triple Modular Redundancy) system has three identical processors, each with reliability $R = 0.95$. The system works if at least 2 of 3 processors work. Find system reliability.

**Solution:**

Using formula 4 from the cheat sheet: $R_{2/3} = 3R^2 - 2R^3$

Let us derive it from scratch using the master formula:

$$R_{2/3} = \sum_{i=2}^{3} \binom{3}{i} R^i (1-R)^{3-i}$$

$$R_{2/3} = \binom{3}{2} R^2 (1-R)^{1} + \binom{3}{3} R^3 (1-R)^{0}$$

$$R_{2/3} = 3 R^2 (1-R) + 1 \cdot R^3 \cdot 1$$

$$R_{2/3} = 3R^2 - 3R^3 + R^3$$

$$R_{2/3} = 3R^2 - 2R^3$$

Now substituting $R = 0.95$:

$$R_{2/3} = 3(0.95)^2 - 2(0.95)^3$$

$$R_{2/3} = 3(0.9025) - 2(0.857375)$$

$$R_{2/3} = 2.7075 - 1.714750$$

$$\boxed{R_{2/3} = 0.99275}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** Notice that the TMR system (99.275%) is MORE reliable than a single processor (95%), demonstrating the value of redundancy. Compare this with a parallel 3-processor system ($1 - (0.05)^3 = 0.999875$) — parallel is more reliable but TMR is more economical.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 2: 2-out-of-4 System with Time-Dependent Analysis

**Problem:** A 2-out-of-4 system has identical components, each with constant failure rate $\lambda = 0.001$ failures/hour. Find:
- (a) System reliability at $t = 100$ hours.
- (b) System MTTF.

**Solution (a) — Mission Reliability:**

Component reliability at t = 100 hours:

$$R(100) = e^{-0.001 \times 100} = e^{-0.1} = 0.904837$$

$$Q = 1 - R = 0.095163$$

Applying master formula:

$$R_{2/4} = \sum_{i=2}^{4} \binom{4}{i} R^i Q^{4-i}$$

Breaking down each term:

$$R_{2/4} = \binom{4}{2} R^2 Q^2 + \binom{4}{3} R^3 Q^1 + \binom{4}{4} R^4 Q^0$$

$$R_{2/4} = 6 R^2 Q^2 + 4 R^3 Q + R^4$$

Substituting numerical values:

$$R^2 = 0.904837^2 = 0.818731$$

$$R^3 = 0.818731 \times 0.904837 = 0.740818$$

$$R^4 = 0.740818 \times 0.904837 = 0.670320$$

$$Q^2 = 0.095163^2 = 0.009056$$

$$Q^1 = 0.095163$$

Now plugging into each term:

$$6 \times 0.818731 \times 0.009056 = 6 \times 0.007416 = 0.044494$$

$$4 \times 0.740818 \times 0.095163 = 4 \times 0.070506 = 0.282024$$

$$1 \times 0.670320 = 0.670320$$

Summing all terms:

$$\boxed{R_{2/4}(100) = 0.044494 + 0.282024 + 0.670320 = 0.996838}$$

**Solution (b) — MTTF Using the Shortcut Formula:**

For exponential components:

$$\text{MTTF}_{K/M} = \sum_{i=K}^{M} \frac{1}{i\lambda}$$

$$\text{MTTF}_{2/4} = \frac{1}{2\lambda} + \frac{1}{3\lambda} + \frac{1}{4\lambda}$$

$$\text{MTTF}_{2/4} = \frac{1}{2(0.001)} + \frac{1}{3(0.001)} + \frac{1}{4(0.001)}$$

$$\text{MTTF}_{2/4} = 500 + 333.33 + 250$$

$$\boxed{\text{MTTF}_{2/4} = 1083.33 \text{ hours}}$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation of the MTTF Shortcut Formula

Starting from the integral definition:

$$\text{MTTF}_{K/M} = \int_0^{\infty} R_{K/M}(t)\, dt = \int_0^{\infty} \sum_{i=K}^{M} \binom{M}{i} e^{-i\lambda t}(1-e^{-\lambda t})^{M-i}\, dt$$

Interchanging summation and integral (valid by linearity):

$$\text{MTTF}_{K/M} = \sum_{i=K}^{M} \binom{M}{i} \int_0^{\infty} e^{-i\lambda t}(1-e^{-\lambda t})^{M-i}\, dt$$

Expanding the term $(1-e^{-\lambda t})^{M-i}$ via binomial theorem:

$$(1-e^{-\lambda t})^{M-i} = \sum_{j=0}^{M-i} \binom{M-i}{j} (-1)^j e^{-j\lambda t}$$

After integration and simplification (standard result — see KTU Module 1 reference material), we obtain:

$$\text{MTTF}_{K/M} = \sum_{i=K}^{M} \frac{1}{i\lambda}$$

---

## 3.5 Comparison Matrix for Special K Values (Numerical)

Assume M = 4, each component has $R = 0.9$:

| K Value | Configuration | Formula Used | System Reliability | Engineering Meaning |
|:---:|:---:|:---|:---:|:---|
| K = 1 | 1-out-of-4 (Parallel) | $1 - 0.1^4$ | **0.9999** | Ultra-reliable (need all 4 to fail) |
| K = 2 | 2-out-of-4 | $6(0.9)^2(0.1)^2 + 4(0.9)^3(0.1) + (0.9)^4$ | **0.9963** | Aircraft quad-engine |
| K = 3 | 3-out-of-4 | $4(0.9)^3(0.1) + (0.9)^4$ | **0.9477** | High-reliability series |
| K = 4 | 4-out-of-4 (Series) | $(0.9)^4$ | **0.6561** | No redundancy |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a K-out-of-M System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    IN[System Input / Mission Demand] --> MUX{Working Components Counter}
    MUX --> C1[Component 1\nR = 0.95]
    MUX --> C2[Component 2\nR = 0.95]
    MUX --> C3[Component 3\nR = 0.95]
    MUX --> C4[Component M\nidentical parallel branch]
    C1 --> COUNT[Counting Logic Block]
    C2 --> COUNT
    C3 --> COUNT
    C4 --> COUNT
    COUNT --> COMP{N >= K Threshold}
    COMP -->|Yes| OUT1[SYSTEM OPERATIONAL\nOutput Available]
    COMP -->|No| OUT2[SYSTEM FAILED\nNo Output]
    OUT1 --> READY[Mission Success R_K_M greater than R_component]
    OUT2 --> DOWN[Reliability Drop Event]
```

## 4.2 Reliability Block Diagram (RBD) for a 2-out-of-4 System

```mermaid
graph LR
    SRC[Source / Power Input] --> A[Component 1]
    SRC --> B[Component 2]
    SRC --> C[Component 3]
    SRC --> D[Component 4]
    A --> MERGE{Any 2 of 4 Paths Valid}
    B --> MERGE
    C --> MERGE
    D --> MERGE
    MERGE -->|At least 2 alive| SNK[Load / Output Delivered]
```

## 4.3 Decision Tree: When to Use Which K-out-of-M Variant

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Engineering Problem: Need Redundancy?] --> Q1{Is mission critical?}
    Q1 -->|No| SER[Use Series K = M system\nLow cost, low reliability]
    Q1 -->|Yes| Q2{What is cost vs safety tradeoff?}
    Q2 -->|Maximize safety| PAR[Use Parallel K = 1 system\nHighest reliability, highest cost]
    Q2 -->|Balanced| Q3{Number of redundant units?}
    Q3 -->|3 units| TMR[Use 2-out-of-3 TMR system\nSpacecraft and digital voting]
    Q3 -->|4 units| QUAD[Use 2-out-of-4 system\nAircraft quad engine]
    Q3 -->|5 units| PENTA[Use 3-out-of-5 system\nMajority voting 5oo5]
    SER --> DONE[Solution Identified]
    PAR --> DONE
    TMR --> DONE
    QUAD --> DONE
    PENTA --> DONE
```

## 4.4 State Transition Diagram for a 2-out-of-3 System

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> S3: Initial state 3 working
    S3: S3 = 3 components working\nSystem OK
    S2: S2 = 2 components working\nSystem OK
    S1: S1 = 1 component working\nSystem FAILED
    S0: S0 = 0 components working\nSystem FAILED
    S3 --> S2: 1 component fails rate 3 lambda
    S2 --> S1: 1 more fails rate 2 lambda
    S2 --> S3: Repair optional
    S1 --> S0: Final failure rate lambda
    S0 --> [*]: System Down Event
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the State Diagram:** The system fails only after transitioning from S3 → S2 → S1. The total failure path requires (M − K + 1) = 2 sequential component failures to cross the system-failure threshold.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define a K-out-of-M system. State the conditions under which the binomial formula applies for K-out-of-M reliability analysis.**

**Model Answer:**

A K-out-of-M system is a redundant configuration comprising M identical components such that the system functions successfully if and only if at least K components are in the working state.

The system reliability is given by:

$$R_{K/M} = \sum_{i=K}^{M} \binom{M}{i} R^i (1-R)^{M-i}$$

**Conditions for binomial formula applicability:**
1. All M components must be **statistically independent**.
2. All components must be **identical** with same reliability R.
3. Each component must be in a **binary state** — either working or failed.
4. The system must be **non-repairable** during the mission.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**For a K-out-of-M system, identify what the configurations K = 1 and K = M reduce to. Justify.**

**Model Answer:**

- **Case K = 1:** Reduces to a **parallel system**. The system functions if at least 1 component works — meaning the system fails only when all M components fail.

$$R_{1/M} = 1 - (1-R)^M$$

- **Case K = M:** Reduces to a **series system**. The system functions only if all M components work.

$$R_{M/M} = R^M$$

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A — Option 1 [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

**(a)** Derive the general reliability expression for a K-out-of-M system with identical independent components, each having reliability R.

**(b)** A 2-out-of-4 redundant system has identical components, each with reliability 0.9. Calculate:
- (i) System reliability $R_{2/4}$
- (ii) System unreliability
- (iii) Probability that exactly 3 components are working

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** [Refer Section 3.1 derivation of the master formula — full derivation with binomial setup, summation, and final expression — for 7 marks.]

**(b) (i) — System Reliability:**

Using formula:

$$R_{2/4} = \sum_{i=2}^{4} \binom{4}{i} (0.9)^i (0.1)^{4-i}$$

Breaking into three terms:

**Term 1** (i = 2):
$$\binom{4}{2} (0.9)^2 (0.1)^2 = 6 \times 0.81 \times 0.01 = 0.0486$$

**Term 2** (i = 3):
$$\binom{4}{3} (0.9)^3 (0.1)^1 = 4 \times 0.729 \times 0.1 = 0.2916$$

**Term 3** (i = 4):
$$\binom{4}{4} (0.9)^4 (0.1)^0 = 1 \times 0.6561 \times 1 = 0.6561$$

Adding:

$$\boxed{R_{2/4} = 0.0486 + 0.2916 + 0.6561 = 0.9963}$$

**(b) (ii) — System Unreliability:**

$$\boxed{F_{2/4} = 1 - R_{2/4} = 1 - 0.9963 = 0.0037}$$

**(b) (iii) — Probability exactly 3 components working:**

$$P(X = 3) = \binom{4}{3} (0.9)^3 (0.1)^1 = 4 \times 0.729 \times 0.1$$

$$\boxed{P(X = 3) = 0.2916}$$

---

### Question B — Option 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**(a)** With the help of a suitable diagram, explain the K-out-of-M system concept. Reduce the general expression for the special cases K = 1 and K = M.

**(b)** A 2-out-of-3 system has components with exponential failure distribution, each with failure rate $\lambda = 0.0005$ per hour. Calculate:
- (i) System reliability at t = 1000 hours
- (ii) System MTTF

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** [Refer to the formal definition in Section 1.1, the block diagrams in Section 4.2, and the special case reductions: K = 1 → parallel, K = M → series, with formulas — for 7 marks.]

**(b) (i) — System Reliability at t = 1000 hours:**

Component reliability:

$$R(1000) = e^{-0.0005 \times 1000} = e^{-0.5} = 0.6065$$

$$Q = 1 - R = 0.3935$$

Apply master formula for 2-out-of-3:

$$R_{2/3} = \sum_{i=2}^{3} \binom{3}{i} R^i Q^{3-i}$$

**Term 1** (i = 2):
$$\binom{3}{2} R^2 Q^1 = 3 \times (0.6065)^2 \times 0.3935$$

$$3 \times 0.367837 \times 0.3935 = 3 \times 0.144759 = 0.434277$$

**Term 2** (i = 3):
$$\binom{3}{3} R^3 Q^0 = 1 \times (0.6065)^3 = 0.223130$$

Adding:

$$\boxed{R_{2/3}(1000) = 0.434277 + 0.223130 = 0.6574}$$

**(b) (ii) — System MTTF:**

Using the MTTF shortcut:

$$\text{MTTF}_{2/3} = \sum_{i=2}^{3} \frac{1}{i\lambda}$$

$$\text{MTTF}_{2/3} = \frac{1}{2\lambda} + \frac{1}{3\lambda}$$

$$\text{MTTF}_{2/3} = \frac{1}{2(0.0005)} + \frac{1}{3(0.0005)}$$

$$\text{MTTF}_{2/3} = 1000 + 666.67$$

$$\boxed{\text{MTTF}_{2/3} = 1666.67 \text{ hours}}$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Easy Marks**
> 1. **Summation Index Error:** Writing $\sum_{i=K}^{M}$ when the system needs at least K, vs $\sum_{i=0}^{K-1}$ for system failure. Many students mix these up.
> 2. **Forgetting the Binomial Coefficient:** Writing $R^i (1-R)^{M-i}$ without $\binom{M}{i}$ — costs 1 to 2 marks.
> 3. **Special Case Confusion:** Writing $R = R^M$ for parallel or $R = 1 - (1-R)^M$ for series. Memorize the boundary cases.
> 4. **MTTF Formula Misuse:** Applying $\text{MTTF} = 1/\lambda$ (single component) to K-out-of-M — must use the **sum formula** $\sum_{i=K}^{M} \frac{1}{i\lambda}$.
> 5. **Unit Inconsistency:** If $\lambda$ is per hour, MTTF is in hours. If per year, then years. Match units throughout.
> 6. **Sign Error in Exponentials:** $e^{-\lambda t}$ vs $e^{\lambda t}$ — negative exponent is correct (decay, not growth).
> 7. **Independent Assumption Not Stated:** In KTU exams, always explicitly write the assumption "components are statistically independent and identical" to earn the assumption marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Last-Minute Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Flashcard — K-out-of-M Systems**

- ✅ **Definition:** System works if at least K out of M components work.
- ✅ **Master Formula:** $R_{K/M} = \sum_{i=K}^{M} \binom{M}{i} R^i Q^{M-i}$, where $Q = 1-R$.
- ✅ **Parallel Special Case (K=1):** $R_{1/M} = 1 - Q^M$.
- ✅ **Series Special Case (K=M):** $R_{M/M} = R^M$.
- ✅ **TMR (2-out-of-3):** $R_{2/3} = 3R^2 - 2R^3$.
- ✅ **2-out-of-4:** $R_{2/4} = 6R^2Q^2 + 4R^3Q + R^4$.
- ✅ **Time-Dependent Reliability:** $R_{K/M}(t) = \sum_{i=K}^{M} \binom{M}{i} e^{-i\lambda t}(1 - e^{-\lambda t})^{M-i}$.
- ✅ **MTTF Shortcut (Exponential Components):** $\text{MTTF}_{K/M} = \sum_{i=K}^{M} \dfrac{1}{i\lambda}$.
- ✅ **Assumptions Checklist:** Independent + Identical + Binary state + Non-repairable.
- ✅ **Real-World Examples:** Aircraft engines (2-out-of-4), spacecraft TMR (2-out-of-3), nuclear pumps.
- ✅ **High-Reliability Benefit:** $R_{K/M} > R$ always (system more reliable than its weakest unit).
- ✅ **System Failure Condition:** Occurs when $(M - K + 1)$ or more components fail.
- ✅ **Mermaid RBD Tip:** In exam diagrams, draw M parallel paths from source to load with a "K of M" logic gate at the merger point.
- ✅ **Examiner's Pet Question:** "Prove that K-out-of-M system reliability is greater than individual component reliability." (Use binomial expansion and show $R_{K/M} = R^M + (\text{positive terms}) > R^M \geq R$).

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Tip:** Always state the value of $\binom{M}{K-1}$ correctly when computing system failure probability $F_{K/M} = \sum_{i=0}^{K-1} \binom{M}{i} R^i Q^{M-i}$. For 2-out-of-4, $K-1 = 1$, so failure terms are only $i = 0$ and $i = 1$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Component vs. Unit Redundancy
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET632 - RELIABILITY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Component vs. Unit Redundancy

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal KTU 2024 Definition

**Redundancy** in a system refers to the existence of more than one means of performing a given function. In KTU's Reliability Engineering framework (Module 2), redundancy is classified by the **granularity level** at which duplication is applied to the system architecture:

- **Component Redundancy (Active Parallel Redundancy at Component Level):** A single critical component within a system is replaced (or augmented) by **two or more identical components connected in parallel**, such that failure of one does not cause system failure. The redundancy exists at the lowest functional level.

- **Unit Redundancy (Redundancy at Subsystem / Unit Level):** The **entire functional unit** (containing several components in series) is duplicated one or more times, and the parallel combination of these *complete* units forms the redundant configuration.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** In KTU Module 2, this topic is a *direct application* of the series/parallel reliability formulas. Students must be able to (i) model both configurations, (ii) compute their system reliabilities, and (iii) quantitatively compare them to justify which strategy yields higher reliability per redundant element added.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a **family car trip from Kerala to Delhi**:

| Analogy | Component Redundancy | Unit Redundancy |
|---|---|---|
| Car Concept | Each critical function (brakes, steering, engine) has a backup *built into the same car* | A second *entire car* is rented as a backup |
| Example | Twin brake circuits in modern cars (one fails → other still works) | Two cars travel together; if one breaks down, the family switches |
| Cost Profile | Cheaper (one car, just extra parts) | Costlier (two cars, full duplication) |
| Outcome | More reliable, less weight, more efficient | Less efficient, heavy, expensive |

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Takeaway:** Component redundancy adds backups *where the failure risk is*, while unit redundancy duplicates the *entire* system — including parts that may already be very reliable. Hence, for the same number of added elements, **component redundancy almost always yields higher system reliability**.

### 1.3 Active vs. Standby — The Other Axis

Both component and unit redundancy may be implemented in two modes:

- **Active (Parallel / Hot) Redundancy:** All redundant elements are energized and operating simultaneously.
- **Standby (Cold) Redundancy:** Redundant elements are *off* and switched in only after a primary element fails. Requires a **perfect / imperfect switch** with reliability $R_{sw}$.

For Module 2 questions, the dominant formulation uses **active parallel redundancy** unless explicitly stated otherwise.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Architecture

#### (a) Component Redundancy Architecture
A series system of $n$ critical components is taken. For component $i$, instead of a single element, $m_i$ identical elements are wired in **parallel**. The system functions if *at least one element in every parallel group functions*.

#### (b) Unit Redundancy Architecture
The original unit is a series chain of $n$ components. Its reliability is computed first as $R_u = \prod_{i=1}^{n} R_i$. Then the **whole unit** is replicated $k$ times, giving a total of $(k+1)$ units in parallel.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic

**Step 1 — Identify the series chain.**
Every non-redundant system begins as a series of components, so the baseline (no-redundancy) reliability is:

$$R_{\text{series}} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} R_i$$

**Step 2 — Apply redundancy at the chosen level.**

- *Component level:* Substitute each $R_i$ with $R_{p,i} = 1 - (1 - R_i)^{m_i}$, where $m_i$ is the number of parallel elements at position $i$.
- *Unit level:* Compute $R_u$ first, then compute $R_{\text{system}} = 1 - (1 - R_u)^{k+1}$, where $(k+1)$ is the total number of parallel units.

**Step 3 — Compare for the *same number of added elements*.**
Count total elements in both designs and confirm which gives higher $R_s$.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Configuration | Reliability Formula | Variables / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Series system (no redundancy) | $R_s = \prod_{i=1}^{n} R_i$ | $n$ = number of components |
| Parallel of $m$ identical components (active) | $R_p = 1 - (1 - R)^{m}$ | Assumes independence |
| **Component Redundancy** in a series chain | $R_s = \prod_{i=1}^{n} \left[1 - (1 - R_i)^{m_i}\right]$ | Redundancy placed at chosen positions |
| Single unit reliability | $R_u = \prod_{i=1}^{n} R_i$ | Series model of one unit |
| **Unit Redundancy** with $k$ spares | $R_{\text{sys}} = 1 - (1 - R_u)^{k+1}$ | $(k+1)$ parallel units |
| $k$-out-of-$n$ system (identical comp.) | $R = \sum_{j=k}^{n} \binom{n}{j} R^{j} (1-R)^{n-j}$ | Binomial sum |
| Standby redundancy (perfect switch) | $R_{\text{standby}} = 1 - \prod_{j=1}^{m} (1 - R_j)$ | $R_j$ = reliability of $j$-th unit *when activated* |
| Switch reliability impact (standby) | $R_{\text{eff}} = R_{sw} \cdot R_{\text{standby}}$ | Multiplicative penalty for non-perfect switch |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Notation Rule for KTU Valuation:** Always define every symbol before using it. Examiners *deduct marks* when a student writes $R = 1 - (1-R)^{m+1}$ without specifying that $(m+1)$ is the total unit count.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace & Aircraft Systems** (e.g., Boeing 787): Critical avionics use **component-level** triple/quadruple redundancy inside line-replaceable units to minimise weight and maximise fault tolerance.
- **Data Centre Power**: Entire **uninterruptible power supplies (UPS units)** are duplicated at the *unit level* — easier to swap and isolate.
- **Automotive Brakes**: Twin hydraulic circuits = pure **component redundancy**.
- **Power Grid Substations**: A redundant feeder line = **unit redundancy** of the whole distribution string.

The choice is governed by **economics, weight, maintainability, and fault-isolation capability** — not just reliability alone.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Component Redundancy Formula

Consider a system with $n$ critical components in series. At the $i^{\text{th}}$ position, we install $m_i$ identical components, each with reliability $R_i$, all wired in **active parallel**.

**Step 1 — Probability that *all* $m_i$ parallel components at position $i$ fail:**

$$P(\text{all fail at } i) = (1 - R_i)^{m_i}$$

(We multiply $(1 - R_i)$ for each independent failure.)

**Step 2 — Probability that *at least one* component at position $i$ works (parallel group survives):**

$$R_{p,i} = 1 - P(\text{all fail at } i) = 1 - (1 - R_i)^{m_i}$$

**Step 3 — Since the parallel groups are in series, all must survive:**

$$R_s = \prod_{i=1}^{n} R_{p,i} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} \left[ 1 - (1 - R_i)^{m_i} \right]$$

This is the **Component Redundancy** equation, valid for active (hot) parallel configuration.

### 3.2 Derivation of the Unit Redundancy Formula

**Step 1 — A single unit contains $n$ components in series.** Its reliability is:

$$R_u = \prod_{i=1}^{n} R_i$$

**Step 2 — We place $k$ additional identical units in parallel with the original**, giving $(k+1)$ units total. By the same parallel-OR logic as before:

$$P(\text{all } k+1 \text{ units fail}) = (1 - R_u)^{k+1}$$

**Step 3 — System reliability (at least one unit works):**

$$R_{\text{sys}} = 1 - (1 - R_u)^{k+1}$$

This is the **Unit Redundancy** equation.

### 3.3 Exhaustive Worked Comparison Example (A KTU Favourite)

**Problem Setup:** A system has 3 components in series, each with $R = 0.9$. We are allowed to add exactly **one extra element** to the system. Compare the two redundancy philosophies.

---

#### (A) Component Redundancy Strategy

Place one extra identical component **in parallel with each** of the 3 components (this uses 3 extras, so for a fair comparison we will first use only 1 extra at the weakest stage, then 1 extra at each stage):

**Sub-case A1 — One extra on the single weakest stage:**

$$R_{p,1} = 1 - (1 - 0.9)^2 = 1 - 0.01 = 0.99$$

$$R_s^{(A1)} = 0.99 \times 0.9 \times 0.9 = 0.8019$$

**Sub-case A2 — One extra on *each* of the 3 stages:**

$$R_s^{(A2)} = \left[1 - (0.1)^2\right]^3 = (0.99)^3 = 0.970299$$

---

#### (B) Unit Redundancy Strategy

The original 3-component unit has:

$$R_u = 0.9^3 = 0.729$$

**Sub-case B1 — One extra entire unit (2 units in parallel):**

$$R_{\text{sys}}^{(B1)} = 1 - (1 - 0.729)^2 = 1 - (0.271)^2 = 1 - 0.073441 = 0.926559$$

**Sub-case B2 — Two extra units (3 units in parallel):**

$$R_{\text{sys}}^{(B2)} = 1 - (0.271)^3 = 1 - 0.019902 = 0.980098$$

---

#### (C) Comparative Analytical Matrix

| Strategy | Components Used | $R_{\text{system}}$ | $\Delta R$ over baseline | Verdict |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Baseline (no redundancy) | 3 | 0.729000 | — | Reference |
| Component Redundancy (1 extra, weakest stage) | 4 | 0.801900 | +0.0729 | Better than baseline |
| Component Redundancy (1 extra, all stages) | 6 | 0.970299 | +0.2413 | **Best for fewer extras** |
| Unit Redundancy (1 extra unit) | 6 | 0.926559 | +0.1976 | Good but inferior |
| Unit Redundancy (2 extra units) | 9 | 0.980098 | +0.2511 | Most expensive |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight — The Key Trade-off:** With the *same hardware budget* (6 components used), **component redundancy gives $R = 0.970299$** while **unit redundancy gives $R = 0.926559$**. This $\approx 4.4\%$ absolute gain is the central pedagogical point of the topic.

### 3.4 Analytical Reasoning — *Why* Component Redundancy Wins

Let $R_i$ be the reliability of component $i$ and $\epsilon_i = 1 - R_i$ be its unreliability. The series system fails when *any* component fails, so unreliability is dominated by the *weakest* (largest $\epsilon_i$) components.

- **Component redundancy** surgically reinforces the high-$\epsilon_i$ links.
- **Unit redundancy** duplicates the *whole chain*, including low-$\epsilon_i$ links whose backup adds little reliability benefit.

Mathematically, for the same total number of added elements $N$:

$$1 - (1 - \epsilon_u)^{k+1} \;\le\; \prod_{i=1}^{n} \left[ 1 - \epsilon_i^{m_i} \right]$$

with equality only when every $R_i$ is identical — a rare real-world case.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Component Redundancy

```mermaid
graph LR
    In[Input] --> G1A[Comp 1: A]
    In --> G1B[Comp 1: A backup]
    G1A --> G2A[Comp 2: B]
    G1B --> G2B[Comp 2: B backup]
    G2A --> G3A[Comp 3: C]
    G2B --> G3B[Comp 3: C backup]
    G3A --> Out[Output]
    G3B --> Out
    classDef primary fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#1F77B4,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef backup fill:#FFF4E6,stroke:#FF7F0E,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef io fill:#E8F8E8,stroke:#2CA02C,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    class In,Out io
    class G1A,G2A,G3A primary
    class G1B,G2B,G3B backup
```

**Interpretation:** Each series stage has a primary (blue) and a backup (orange) element. Failure of a primary does not break the path because the backup carries the signal/load.

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Unit Redundancy

```mermaid
graph TB
    In[Input] --> S1
    In --> S2
    subgraph S1[Unit 1: 3 series components]
        S1A[Comp A1] --> S1B[Comp B1] --> S1C[Comp C1]
    end
    subgraph S2[Unit 2: 3 series components]
        S2A[Comp A2] --> S2B[Comp B2] --> S2C[Comp C2]
    end
    S1 --> Out[Output]
    S2 --> Out
    classDef unit fill:#FDEDEC,stroke:#C0392B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef io fill:#E8F8E8,stroke:#2CA02C,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    class S1A,S1B,S1C,S2A,S2B,S2C unit
    class In,Out io
```

**Interpretation:** Two *complete* units, each containing its own A, B, C components, operate in parallel. The full unit is the redundant element, not individual components.

### 4.3 Side-by-Side Comparison Flow

```mermaid
graph TB
    Start([System with n=3 components, R=0.9]) --> Q{Choose granularity}
    Q -->|At component| CR[Component Redundancy]
    Q -->|At unit level| UR[Unit Redundancy]
    CR --> CR1[Add parallel element to stage i]
    CR1 --> CR2[R_s = product of 1 - 1 - R_i to power m_i]
    CR2 --> CRR[R_s equals 0.970299 for 6 elements]
    UR --> UR1[Duplicate entire unit]
    UR1 --> UR2[R_sys = 1 - 1 - R_u to power k+1]
    UR2 --> URR[R_sys equals 0.926559 for 6 elements]
    CRR --> Win[Component Redundancy WINS]
    URR --> Win
    Win --> End([Decision: prefer component redundancy for same budget])
    classDef winNode fill:#D5F5E3,stroke:#1E8449,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
    classDef choiceNode fill:#FEF9E7,stroke:#B7950B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    class Win,End winNode
    class Q,CR,UR choiceNode
```

### 4.4 Engineering Trade-off Block (Fallback Functional Map)

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Decision Driver] --> B1[Reliability Target]
    A --> B2[Cost Budget]
    A --> B3[Weight / Volume]
    A --> B4[Maintenance Access]
    B1 --> C1{High target AND mixed components}
    C1 -->|Yes| D1[Choose Component Redundancy]
    C1 -->|No| C2{Identical, mobile units, swap-friendly}
    C2 -->|Yes| D2[Choose Unit Redundancy]
    C2 -->|No| D3[Hybrid: component for criticals, unit for full subassembly]
    classDef decision fill:#F4ECF7,stroke:#7D3C98,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef action fill:#D6EAF8,stroke:#2874A6,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    class A,C1,C2 decision
    class D1,D2,D3,B1,B2,B3,B4 action
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
Define *component redundancy* and *unit redundancy*. State **one** key advantage of component redundancy over unit redundancy.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Component Redundancy (1.5 marks):** Component redundancy is a reliability-improvement technique in which two or more identical elements are connected in parallel at the *component level* of a system, so that the failure of a single element does not cause system failure.
- **Unit Redundancy (1 mark):** Unit redundancy is a configuration in which *entire functional units* (each containing several components) are duplicated and connected in parallel.
- **Advantage (0.5 mark):** For the same number of redundant elements, component redundancy yields **higher system reliability** than unit redundancy, because it reinforces only the high-unreliability components rather than duplicating the entire chain (including already-reliable components).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supple. Set B]**
For an active parallel group of **three identical components** each with reliability $R = 0.95$, compute the group reliability.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

$$R_p = 1 - (1 - R)^{3} = 1 - (0.05)^{3} = 1 - 0.000125 = 0.999875$$

The parallel combination has reliability **0.999875** (or **99.9875 %**).

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation Tip:** Always show the substitution of $(1-R)$ explicitly. Writing $1 - 0.05^3$ without parentheses loses a mark.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice Provided)

> **KTU Pattern:** Each Part B question has sub-parts (a) and (b) of 7 marks each. Two alternative questions are provided; the student answers **one**.

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 marks)** — Component Redundancy Focus

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Modified Past Paper]**

A system consists of **3 components in series** with reliabilities $R_1 = 0.90$, $R_2 = 0.85$, and $R_3 = 0.80$.
