**(a)** Calculate the system reliability *without* any redundancy. **(3 marks)**

**(b)** Now apply **component-level redundancy** by adding one extra identical element in parallel to **each** of the three components. Compute the new system reliability. Comment on the improvement. **(7 marks)**

**(c)** If you are allowed only **one extra component** in the entire system, on which stage should it be placed to maximise reliability? Justify with a calculation. **(4 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Baseline system reliability (3 marks):**

$$R_{\text{base}} = R_1 \cdot R_2 \cdot R_3 = 0.90 \times 0.85 \times 0.80 = 0.6120$$

**(b) Component-level redundancy on every stage (7 marks):**

Reliability of each parallel pair:

$$R_{p,1} = 1 - (1 - 0.90)^2 = 1 - 0.01 = 0.99$$

$$R_{p,2} = 1 - (1 - 0.85)^2 = 1 - 0.0225 = 0.9775$$

$$R_{p,3} = 1 - (1 - 0.80)^2 = 1 - 0.04 = 0.96$$

New system reliability:

$$R_s^{(\text{comp})} = 0.99 \times 0.9775 \times 0.96 = 0.9293 \; (\text{approx.})$$

**Numerical verification of the multiplication:**

$$0.99 \times 0.9775 = 0.967725$$

$$0.967725 \times 0.96 = 0.929016$$

So $R_s^{(\text{comp})} \approx 0.9290$. Reliability improved from $0.612$ to $0.929$, a gain of $\approx +0.317$ absolute.

**(c) Single-extra placement (4 marks):**

The component with the *highest unreliability* is the weakest link. Unreliabilities: $\epsilon_1 = 0.10$, $\epsilon_2 = 0.15$, $\epsilon_3 = 0.20$. So **Component 3** is weakest.

Placing the single extra in parallel with Component 3:

$$R_s = 0.90 \times 0.85 \times 0.96 = 0.7344$$

Compare with placing it on Component 2:

$$R_s = 0.90 \times 0.9775 \times 0.80 = 0.7038$$

Placing on Component 1:

$$R_s = 0.99 \times 0.85 \times 0.80 = 0.6732$$

**Highest is $R_s = 0.7344$** with the extra on Component 3. Hence, the **weakest component** should be reinforced.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall:** Many students place the extra on the *most reliable* component (intuitively thinking it must be "backed up"). Always reinforce the *weakest* link. Examiners specifically look for this reasoning.

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 marks)** — Unit Redundancy Focus & Comparison

**[KTU University Exam – June 2023, Retest]**

Consider the same 3-component series system with $R_1 = 0.90$, $R_2 = 0.85$, $R_3 = 0.80$.

**(a)** Compute the reliability of **one** complete unit. Then, by **duplicating the entire unit** (one spare), compute the system reliability. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare this *unit-redundancy* result with the *component-redundancy* result from Question A. Which is more efficient and why? **(4 marks)**

**(c)** State **two** situations in real engineering where unit redundancy is preferred over component redundancy despite its lower mathematical efficiency. **(3 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Unit redundancy calculation (7 marks):**

Reliability of one unit (3 components in series):

$$R_u = 0.90 \times 0.85 \times 0.80 = 0.6120$$

With 1 spare unit (2 units in parallel):

$$R_{\text{unit}} = 1 - (1 - R_u)^{2} = 1 - (0.388)^{2} = 1 - 0.150544 = 0.849456$$

So $R_{\text{unit}} \approx 0.8495$.

**(b) Comparison (4 marks):**

| Configuration | Extra elements used | $R_s$ | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|
| Component redundancy (1 extra on each stage) | 3 extras | 0.9290 | Reinforces every stage |
| Unit redundancy (1 spare unit) | 3 extras (3 components in spare) | 0.8495 | Duplicates entire chain |

**Verdict:** Component redundancy gives higher reliability ($0.9290 > 0.8495$) for the **same hardware budget of 3 extra components**. It is more efficient because it targets the high-unreliability components, whereas unit redundancy wastes capacity on the already-reliable Component 1 ($R=0.90$).

**(c) Real engineering situations favouring unit redundancy (3 marks):**

1. **Modular / Line-Replaceable Systems (e.g., aircraft avionics LRUs, data-centre servers):** Swapping a whole unit is faster and safer than desoldering and replacing a tiny SMD component in the field.
2. **Hazardous or Hard-to-Access Environments (e.g., space satellites, sub-sea installations):** A failed component cannot be individually repaired, so a full functional unit must be the redundant element to allow complete switchover.
3. **Isolated or Independent Subsystems (e.g., independent power feeder lines, parallel cooling pumps):** Each unit already operates semi-independently, making whole-unit duplication a natural fit.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Read Before Writing!**
> 1. **Don't confuse 'parallel' for 'series'** when drawing or describing the redundant group. If you say "two components in parallel" but draw them in series, you lose 2 marks outright.
> 2. **Don't forget to convert reliability to unreliability** before squaring or cubing. Many students write $1 - 0.9^{2}$ instead of $1 - (1-0.9)^{2}$. The bracket position is the most commonly lost mark.
> 3. **Always state the assumption** of *statistical independence* of failures. KTU examiners deduct up to 1 mark if this is missing.
> 4. **For unit redundancy, compute $R_u$ *first***. Jumping straight to $1-(1-R)^{2}$ without finding $R_u$ is treated as an incomplete solution.
> 5. **Switch reliability** matters only in *standby* redundancy. Do not multiply by $R_{sw}$ when the question says "active parallel."

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Component redundancy** = parallel elements at the **component level**; formula $R_s = \prod_{i=1}^{n} [1 - (1 - R_i)^{m_i}]$.
- [x] **Unit redundancy** = parallel **whole units**; formula $R_{\text{sys}} = 1 - (1 - R_u)^{k+1}$, with $R_u = \prod R_i$.
- [x] For the **same number of added elements**, **component redundancy always yields ≥ system reliability** compared to unit redundancy, with strict inequality when component reliabilities differ.
- [x] When placing a *single* redundant component, reinforce the **weakest** (highest unreliability) link.
- [x] **Active / Hot redundancy:** all elements energised → standard $1-(1-R)^m$ formula.
- [x] **Standby / Cold redundancy:** spares are off, switched in on failure → requires switch reliability $R_{sw}$.
- [x] **Perfect switch assumption** is the default in KTU problems unless stated otherwise.
- [x] All formulas assume **statistical independence** of component failures.
- [x] **k-out-of-n** is a special redundancy pattern — works if at least $k$ of $n$ work.
- [x] Real engineering choice balances **reliability gain vs. cost, weight, and maintainability** — pure math is only one input.
- [x] KTU expected answer length: Part A ≈ 50 words, Part B sub-part ≈ 100–150 words plus equations.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Weakest-Link Technique
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET632 - RELIABILITY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Weakest-Link Technique

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Weakest-Link Technique (WLT):** A reliability modeling approach in which the overall reliability of a system or structure is governed by the single most failure-prone (weakest) element within it. The system is said to *fail as soon as the weakest component fails*, making the weakest component the controlling factor of the entire system's performance.

In mathematical terms, for a system composed of $n$ components with individual reliabilities $R_1, R_2, \ldots, R_n$, the **weakest-link reliability** is approximated as:

$$R_{\text{system}} \;=\; \min\{R_1, R_2, \ldots, R_n\}$$

This contrasts with the **series-system reliability model** where $R_s = \prod_{i=1}^{n} R_i$. The weakest-link technique is therefore the *upper-bound approximation* of a series system, valid when one component dominates the failure behaviour.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Chain Puzzle:** Imagine a hanging decorative chain made of 10 links of varying quality. The chain can only support the weight of the chandelier as long as *every* link holds. The moment the **weakest (thinnest / rusted) link** snaps, the entire chain fails — even if the other 9 links are perfectly intact. The weakest link literally *governs* the chain's load-bearing capacity. This is the essence of the weakest-link principle in reliability engineering.

Other familiar real-world instances include:
- A **rope** failing at its thinnest fibre strand.
- A **series electrical fuse chain** blowing at the lowest-rated fuse.
- A **fibre-optic bundle** breaking at the most defective fibre.
- **Brittle ceramic components** fracturing at the largest flaw (a cornerstone of fracture mechanics — Griffith's theory).

### Standard Metrics Used

- **System Reliability:** $R_s$ — dimensionless, $0 \le R_s \le 1$.
- **Component Reliability:** $R_i$ — dimensionless.
- **Weibull Shape Parameter:** $\beta$ — dimensionless, indicates failure mode (β < 1: infant mortality, β = 1: random, β > 1: wear-out).
- **Weibull Scale Parameter:** $\eta$ — has units of time, cycles, or stress (depending on application).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### Step-by-Step Logic Breakdown

**Step 1 — Identify the Series Configuration.**
The weakest-link model is structurally identical to a *series reliability network* — every component must survive for the system to survive.

**Step 2 — Compute Individual Reliabilities.**
Evaluate the reliability $R_i(t)$ of each component $i$ at mission time $t$. For time-independent problems, the reliabilities are taken at the design stress or load level.

**Step 3 — Determine the System Reliability.**
Two equivalent formulations exist:
- **Exact Series Formula:** $R_s = \prod_{i=1}^{n} R_i$
- **Weakest-Link Approximation:** $R_s = \min\{R_1, R_2, \ldots, R_n\}$

**Step 4 — Apply Weibull Statistics (for time-dependent / strength-based problems).**
The Weibull distribution is the canonical weakest-link distribution because its cumulative distribution function (CDF) is built on the *minimum of extreme-value statistics*. For a load $L$ on a structure with $n$ flaw sites:

$$F(L) \;=\; 1 - \exp\!\left[-\left(\frac{L}{\eta}\right)^{\beta}\right]$$

where $\eta$ and $\beta$ are the Weibull scale and shape parameters respectively.

**Step 5 — Combine Multiple Weakest-Link Sub-Systems.**
When several independent weakest-link sub-systems are connected in series at the *system* level, their Weibull parameters combine as:

$$\eta_{\text{sys}} \;=\; \left(\sum_{i=1}^{n} \eta_i^{-\beta_i}\right)^{-1/\beta}$$

valid only when all sub-systems share the same shape parameter $\beta_i = \beta$.

### KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Expression | Meaning / Use | Boundary Conditions |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $R_s = \prod_{i=1}^{n} R_i$ | Series system exact reliability | Independent components |
| 2 | $R_s = \min\{R_i\}$ | Weakest-link reliability (upper bound) | One dominant component |
| 3 | $F(t) = 1 - \exp[-(t/\eta)^{\beta}]$ | Weibull CDF (weakest-link form) | $\beta > 0$, $t \geq 0$ |
| 4 | $R(t) = \exp[-(t/\eta)^{\beta}]$ | Weibull reliability function | Same as above |
| 5 | $\lambda(t) = (\beta / \eta)(t/\eta)^{\beta - 1}$ | Weibull hazard rate | Useful for identifying failure mode |
| 6 | $\eta_{\text{sys}} = (\sum \eta_i^{-\beta})^{-1/\beta}$ | Combined weakest-link scale parameter | Common $\beta$ across sub-systems |
| 7 | $f(t) = (\beta / \eta)(t/\eta)^{\beta-1}\exp[-(t/\eta)^{\beta}]$ | Weibull PDF | For MLE parameter estimation |
| 8 | $\mu = \eta \,\Gamma(1 + 1/\beta)$ | Mean life (MTTF) of Weibull | $\Gamma$ is the gamma function |

> [!TIP]
> **Board Exam Heuristic:** Whenever a problem mentions *failure strength*, *breaking load*, *fracture*, or *dielectric breakdown* — **immediately invoke the Weibull weakest-link distribution**. It is the strongest "default model" in KTU board questions on this topic.

### Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Pressure Vessel & Piping Design (ASME Code):** Section VIII divisional rules implicitly use weakest-link assumptions when computing allowable stresses from material test coupons.
- **Ceramic & Brittle-Material Reliability:** Used to predict fracture probability from flaw-size distributions.
- **Aerospace Composites:** Fibre-bundle strength modelled using Daniels' weakest-link theory.
- **Dielectric Breakdown in Capacitors & Insulators:** Time-to-breakdown is a classic Weibull weakest-link problem.
- **Civil Engineering — Tensile Cables & Suspension Bridges:** Cable strength equals strength of the weakest segment.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### Derivation A — Weakest-Link as the Upper Bound of a Series System

**Given:** A system of $n$ independent components in series, each with reliability $R_i$ where $0 \le R_i \le 1$.

**To Prove:** $R_s \le R_{\min} = \min\{R_i\}$, i.e., the series-system reliability is **less than or equal to** the reliability of the weakest component.

**Step 1.** Write the exact series reliability:

$$R_s \;=\; \prod_{i=1}^{n} R_i$$

**Step 2.** Without loss of generality, assume $R_1 \le R_2 \le \ldots \le R_n$, so $R_{\min} = R_1$.

**Step 3.** Since every $R_i \le 1$ for $i \ge 2$, the product is bounded:

$$R_s \;=\; R_1 \cdot \prod_{i=2}^{n} R_i \;\le\; R_1 \cdot 1^{n-1} \;=\; R_1 \;=\; R_{\min}$$

**Step 4.** Therefore, the weakest-link approximation $R_s \approx R_{\min}$ becomes *exact* in the limiting case where all components other than the weakest have $R_i \approx 1$ — a useful conservative engineering assumption.

**Conclusion:** The weakest-link model is a **safe, conservative upper bound** of true series-system reliability, making it invaluable when component reliability data is sparse or uncertain.

---

### Derivation B — Weibull Distribution as the Weakest-Link Limit Distribution

The Weibull distribution is mathematically *derived* from the theory of extremes: the strength (or failure time) of a structure is determined by its **weakest flaw**, which is the minimum of a large population of randomly distributed flaw strengths.

**Step 1.** Consider $n$ independent flaw sites in a brittle structure. Let the $i$-th flaw have failure probability density $g_i(x)$, with CDF $G_i(x)$.

**Step 2.** Define the failure of the *whole structure* as the failure of the *weakest flaw* — the minimum of $n$ independent random variables $X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_n$.

**Step 3.** The CDF of the minimum is:

$$F_{\min}(x) \;=\; 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n}\bigl[1 - G_i(x)\bigr]$$

**Step 4.** For $n$ large and identically distributed flaw strengths with $G_i(x) = G(x)$, apply the asymptotic extreme-value (Gnedenko) limit:

$$F_{\min}(x) \;\approx\; 1 - \exp\!\left[-n \cdot G(x)\right]$$

**Step 5.** Choose $G(x)$ to be of the power-law form (the *Weibull postulate* for flaw growth): $G(x) = (x/x_0)^{\beta}$.

**Step 6.** Substitute:

$$F_{\min}(x) \;=\; 1 - \exp\!\left[-n\!\left(\frac{x}{x_0}\right)^{\beta}\right]$$

**Step 7.** Re-parameterize by defining $\eta = x_0 \cdot n^{-1/\beta}$ (the Weibull scale parameter):

$$F(x) \;=\; 1 - \exp\!\left[-\left(\frac{x}{\eta}\right)^{\beta}\right]$$

This is the **Weibull CDF**, demonstrating its origin as a weakest-link extreme-value distribution.

---

### Derivation C — Combining Multiple Weakest-Link Sub-Systems

**Given:** Two independent weakest-link sub-systems A and B with Weibull parameters $(\eta_A, \beta)$ and $(\eta_B, \beta)$, connected in series.

**Step 1.** Sub-system A reliability: $R_A(t) = \exp[-(t/\eta_A)^{\beta}]$.

**Step 2.** Sub-system B reliability: $R_B(t) = \exp[-(t/\eta_B)^{\beta}]$.

**Step 3.** Combined series reliability:

$$R_{\text{sys}}(t) \;=\; R_A(t) \cdot R_B(t) \;=\; \exp\!\left[-\left(\frac{t}{\eta_A}\right)^{\beta} - \left(\frac{t}{\eta_B}\right)^{\beta}\right]$$

**Step 4.** Factor the exponent as:

$$R_{\text{sys}}(t) \;=\; \exp\!\left[-t^{\beta}\!\left(\eta_A^{-\beta} + \eta_B^{-\beta}\right)\right]$$

**Step 5.** To re-express in standard Weibull form $R(t) = \exp[-(t/\eta_{\text{sys}})^{\beta}]$, equate:

$$\eta_{\text{sys}}^{-\beta} \;=\; \eta_A^{-\beta} + \eta_B^{-\beta}$$

**Step 6.** Solve for $\eta_{\text{sys}}$:

$$\boxed{\;\eta_{\text{sys}} \;=\; \left(\eta_A^{-\beta} + \eta_B^{-\beta}\right)^{-1/\beta}\;}$$

**Step 7.** Generalising to $n$ sub-systems:

$$\eta_{\text{sys}} \;=\; \left(\sum_{i=1}^{n} \eta_i^{-\beta}\right)^{-1/\beta}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** This formula is *the* most tested derivation in KTU 2024 scheme exams for this topic. Memorise the negative-beta summation form — examiners routinely give "verify" or "derive" questions on this exact expression.

---

### Worked Numerical Example (Analytical Matrix)

**Problem:** A weakest-link structure consists of 3 components with reliabilities $R_1 = 0.95$, $R_2 = 0.90$, $R_3 = 0.99$ at mission time $t = 100$ hours. Compute (i) exact series reliability, (ii) weakest-link reliability, (iii) percentage error of the approximation.

| Quantity | Computation | Result |
|---|---|---|
| Exact $R_s$ | $0.95 \times 0.90 \times 0.99$ | **0.84645** |
| Weakest-link $R_s$ | $\min\{0.95, 0.90, 0.99\}$ | **0.90** |
| Absolute error | $\vert 0.90 - 0.84645 \vert$ | **0.05355** |
| % Error | $(0.05355 / 0.84645) \times 100$ | **≈ 6.33 %** |

**Interpretation:** The weakest-link model **over-estimates** reliability by ~6.33 %. In safety-critical design, this conservatism is acceptable and often desirable (i.e., the actual system is *worse* than predicted — a safe-side error for design margin).

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### Diagram A — Conceptual Chain-of-Links Schematic (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["LOAD INPUT"] --> B["LINK 1 - Strongest"]
    B --> C["LINK 2 - Average"]
    C --> D["LINK 3 - Weakest Link"]
    D --> E["LINK 4 - Strong"]
    E --> F["LINK 5 - Average"]
    F --> G["LOAD OUTPUT - Failure Here"]
    G -.->|System Fails| H["BROKEN CHAIN"]
    D -.->|Causes Failure| H

    styleA fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    styleB fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333
    styleC fill:#FFA500,stroke:#333
    styleD fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000,stroke-width:3px
    styleE fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333
    styleF fill:#FFA500,stroke:#333
    styleG fill:#D3D3D3,stroke:#333,stroke-dasharray: 5 5
    styleH fill:#8B0000,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

**Reading the diagram:** The highlighted red block (Link 3) is the *controlling weak element*. Although Links 1, 2, 4, 5 have differing individual reliabilities, only the weakest link determines the chain's overall behaviour. Dashed arrows indicate *causation paths* to system failure.

---

### Diagram B — Flowchart of the Weakest-Link Reliability Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1["START - Identify Series System"] --> S2["List Components and their R_i"]
    S2 --> S3{"All R_i Available?"}
    S3 -- Yes --> S4["Compute R_min = min of all R_i"]
    S3 -- No --> S5["Use Weibull Parameters eta_i and beta_i"]
    S4 --> S6["R_system approx R_min"]
    S5 --> S7["Compute eta_sys from summation formula"]
    S7 --> S8["Apply R_sys t = exp - t by eta_sys ^ beta"]
    S6 --> S9["Compare with exact product for error"]
    S8 --> S9
    S9 --> S10["END - Report R_system and Confidence"]

    styleS1 fill:#4682B4,color:#fff
    styleS3 fill:#FFD700
    styleS5 fill:#FF8C00
    styleS6 fill:#32CD32
    styleS8 fill:#32CD32
    styleS10 fill:#4682B4,color:#fff
```

---

### Diagram C — Failure Probability vs Load Curve (Weibull Weakest-Link Behaviour)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L["LOAD APPLIED"] --> M["FLAW SITE 1"]
    L --> N["FLAW SITE 2"]
    L --> O["FLAW SITE 3"]
    L --> P["FLAW SITE n"]
    M --> Q["MIN OF FLAW STRENGTHS"]
    N --> Q
    O --> Q
    P --> Q
    Q --> R["WEIBULL CDF F L = 1 - exp - L by eta ^ beta"]
    R --> T["FAILURE PROBABILITY CURVE"]

    styleL fill:#1E90FF,color:#fff
    styleQ fill:#FF4500,color:#fff
    styleR fill:#9370DB,color:#fff
    styleT fill:#006400,color:#fff
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`**
Define the **Weakest-Link Technique** in reliability engineering. State two real-world systems where it is applicable.

**Model Answer:**
The Weakest-Link Technique is a reliability modelling approach in which the failure of an entire system is determined by the failure of its single weakest (least reliable) component. The system reliability is approximated as $R_s \approx \min\{R_i\}$, the minimum of all component reliabilities.

**Two real-world applications:**
1. **Brittle ceramic components** — fracture occurs at the largest flaw (Griffith's theory).
2. **Series-connected electrical fuses** — circuit breaks at the lowest-rated fuse.

*Target CO: CO2 | Bloom Level: Remember (L1) | Marks: 3*

---

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`**
Why is the **Weibull distribution** considered the canonical distribution for weakest-link reliability problems?

**Model Answer:**
The Weibull distribution arises naturally as the asymptotic extreme-value distribution of the *minimum* of a large population of independent random flaw strengths. Its CDF, $F(x) = 1 - \exp[-(x/\eta)^{\beta}]$, is mathematically derived from the probability that the weakest flaw fails first. This makes Weibull uniquely suited to model (a) time-to-failure of brittle materials, (b) strength of fibre bundles, and (c) dielectric breakdown of insulators — all classic weakest-link scenarios.

*Target CO: CO2 | Bloom Level: Understand (L2) | Marks: 3*

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]`**

**(a)** [7 Marks] A weakest-link structure consists of three components in series with reliabilities $R_1 = 0.92$, $R_2 = 0.85$, $R_3 = 0.97$ over a mission time of 500 hours. Calculate the **exact series reliability**, the **weakest-link approximation**, and the **percentage error**. Comment on whether the approximation is conservative.

**(b)** [7 Marks] Derive the formula for combining two weakest-link sub-systems with Weibull parameters $(\eta_1, \beta)$ and $(\eta_2, \beta)$ into a single equivalent weakest-link system with combined scale parameter $\eta_{\text{sys}}$.

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** 
Exact series reliability: $R_s = 0.92 \times 0.85 \times 0.97 = 0.75874$.

Weakest-link reliability: $R_{\min} = \min\{0.92, 0.85, 0.97\} = 0.85$.

Absolute error: $\vert 0.85 - 0.75874 \vert = 0.09126$.

Percentage error: $(0.09126 / 0.75874) \times 100 \approx 12.03\%$.

**Comment:** The weakest-link approximation *over-estimates* reliability, which means the actual system is *worse* than predicted. This is **conservative (safe-side)** for design, because engineers build in extra safety margin.

**(b)** Refer to **Derivation C** in Section 3 of these notes. The combined scale parameter is:

$$\eta_{\text{sys}} = \left(\eta_1^{-\beta} + \eta_2^{-\beta}\right)^{-1/\beta}$$

The derivation proceeds through five steps: (1) write individual reliabilities, (2) multiply them (series combination), (3) factor the exponent, (4) equate to standard Weibull form, (5) solve for $\eta_{\text{sys}}$.

*Target CO: CO2, CO3 | Bloom Level: Apply (L3) + Create (L6) | Marks: 14*

---

#### **Question B — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`**

**(a)** [7 Marks] Explain with a real-world analogy why the weakest-link model is the natural choice for predicting the failure strength of brittle materials such as ceramics and concrete. Mention the role of the Weibull modulus $\beta$.

**(b)** [7 Marks] A pressure vessel has two critical welded joints with Weibull parameters $(\eta_1 = 8000 \text{ cycles}, \beta = 2.5)$ and $(\eta_2 = 6500 \text{ cycles}, \beta = 2.5)$ under cyclic loading. Compute the equivalent scale parameter $\eta_{\text{sys}}$ of the combined weakest-link system and the reliability at $t = 2000$ cycles.

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** Brittle materials fail at the *largest flaw* (micro-crack, void, or inclusion). The strength of the entire component is therefore governed by its **most severe flaw** — a direct weakest-link scenario. Real-world analogy: a sheet of glass breaks at the single deepest scratch, even though the rest of the sheet is intact.

The **Weibull modulus $\beta$** quantifies the scatter in flaw strengths:
- High $\beta$ (≈ 10–20): narrow strength distribution, predictable behaviour.
- Low $\beta$ (≈ 2–5): wide strength scatter, highly variable failure loads.

For ceramics, $\beta$ typically lies between 5 and 15.

**(b)** Using the combined weakest-link formula:

$$\eta_{\text{sys}}^{-\beta} = \eta_1^{-\beta} + \eta_2^{-\beta} = (8000)^{-2.5} + (6500)^{-2.5}$$

Compute each term:
- $(8000)^{-2.5} = 1 / (8000^{2.5}) = 1 / (8000^2 \times \sqrt{8000}) = 1 / (6.4 \times 10^7 \times 89.44) \approx 1.747 \times 10^{-12}$.
- $(6500)^{-2.5} = 1 / (6500^2 \times \sqrt{6500}) = 1 / (4.225 \times 10^7 \times 80.62) \approx 2.937 \times 10^{-12}$.

Sum: $1.747 \times 10^{-12} + 2.937 \times 10^{-12} = 4.684 \times 10^{-12}$.

Then:

$$\eta_{\text{sys}} = (4.684 \times 10^{-12})^{-1/2.5} = (4.684 \times 10^{-12})^{-0.4}$$

Computing:

$$\eta_{\text{sys}} = \exp\!\left[\frac{-\ln(4.684 \times 10^{-12})}{2.5}\right] = \exp\!\left[\frac{26.69}{2.5}\right] \approx \exp(10.676) \approx 43510 \text{ cycles}$$

**Reliability at $t = 2000$ cycles:**

$$R(2000) = \exp\!\left[-\left(\frac{2000}{43510}\right)^{2.5}\right] = \exp\!\left[-(0.04596)^{2.5}\right]$$

Compute $(0.04596)^{2.5}$: First $(0.04596)^2 = 0.002112$, then $\times \sqrt{0.04596} = \times 0.2144$, giving $0.0004529$.

$$R(2000) = e^{-0.0004529} \approx 0.99955 \;\;\text{or}\;\; 99.955\%$$

*Target CO: CO2, CO3, CO5 | Bloom Level: Apply (L3) + Analyse (L4) | Marks: 14*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Formula Mis-application (–2 marks):** Students often use $\eta_{\text{sys}} = \eta_1 + \eta_2$ (additive) instead of the correct inverse-beta summation. This is the single biggest deduction in board exams.
> 2. **Ignoring Shape Parameter Mismatch (–1 to –2 marks):** The combined weakest-link formula $\eta_{\text{sys}} = (\sum \eta_i^{-\beta})^{-1/\beta}$ is **only valid** when all sub-systems share the same $\beta$. If they differ, the system is no longer a pure Weibull distribution, and a numerical/computational approach must be adopted.
> 3. **Confusing Weakest-Link with Series Reliability (–1 mark):** The weakest-link is an *approximation* of a series system, not a separate system architecture. State this distinction clearly in the answer.
> 4. **Units in Numerical Answers (–0.5 mark):** Always report $\eta$ with its physical units (cycles, hours, MPa, etc.) — not just the numerical value.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (KTU 2024 Scheme)**

- ⭐ **Core Concept:** Weakest-link technique states that the system reliability equals the reliability of the *least reliable* component: $R_s = \min\{R_i\}$.
- ⭐ **Mathematical Origin:** Derived as the upper-bound approximation of a series-system reliability, $R_s = \prod R_i$.
- ⭐ **Weibull Connection:** Weibull distribution is the *canonical* weakest-link distribution — it is the asymptotic extreme-value distribution of the minimum of random flaw strengths.
- ⭐ **Key Formula:** $\eta_{\text{sys}} = (\sum_{i=1}^{n} \eta_i^{-\beta})^{-1/\beta}$ — valid only with common shape parameter $\beta$.
- ⭐ **Mean Life (MTTF) for Weibull:** $\mu = \eta \,\Gamma(1 + 1/\beta)$ — useful for comparing alternative designs.
- ⭐ **Real-World Domains:** Brittle fracture, fibre bundles, pressure vessels, dielectric breakdown, civil cables, ceramic components, electronic interconnects.
- ⭐ **Design Conservatism:** Weakest-link approximation *over-estimates* reliability → safe-side error → acceptable in design.
- ⭐ **Shape Parameter $\beta$:** $\beta < 1$ (infant mortality), $\beta = 1$ (random / exponential), $\beta > 1$ (wear-out).
- ⭐ **Common Mistake to Avoid:** Never use additive combination $\eta_1 + \eta_2$; always use the inverse-beta summation.
- ⭐ **Exam Pattern (KTU 2024):** Expect a 14-mark derivation question on the combined weakest-link formula, and a 3-mark definition/analogy question in Part A.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Mixed Redundancy
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET632 - RELIABILITY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Mixed Redundancy

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mixed Redundancy** is a hybrid reliability configuration in which a system simultaneously employs **Active (Parallel) Redundancy** and **Standby Redundancy** to achieve a target level of operational reliability. In this architecture, *n* identical units are available to perform the function, of which *m* units operate in the *active (hot)* mode at any given instant, while the remaining *s = n − m* units remain in a *standby* state (cold, warm, or hot) and are brought into service by a *switching/changeover device* only when an active unit fails.

The system is considered to have failed when the number of working units falls below a pre-specified threshold *k* (where *k ≤ m*), or equivalently, when the total number of failed components exceeds the redundancy reserve *n − k*.

Mathematically, for a system requiring *k*-out-of-*n* operation:

$$n = m + s$$

where:
- **m** = Number of actively loaded (energised) components
- **s** = Number of standby components (cold/warm/hot)
- **k** = Minimum number of components required for successful system operation
- **n** = Total population of components (active + standby)

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Power Grid Backup Model

Imagine the electrical grid powering a hospital's cardiac unit. Two transformers (T1, T2) are energised in parallel and share the load actively. A third transformer (T3) sits idle on standby, pre-cooled but instantly switchable via an automatic transfer switch (ATS). The moment T1 or T2 fails (detected by voltage/current sensors), the ATS energises T3 within milliseconds.

This is **not pure parallel** (where T3 would also be running) and **not pure standby** (where T1, T2 would not be parallel). It is *mixed*: an **active sub-system + a standby reserve** working together.

> [!NOTE]
> Mixed redundancy is the workhorse configuration of mission-critical systems such as aircraft flight-control computers, nuclear reactor protection channels, satellite transponders, and industrial servo drives — wherever both *instantaneous coverage* (parallel) and *reserve capacity* (standby) are simultaneously required.

### 1.3 Why Mixed Redundancy is Superior

| Property | Pure Parallel (Active) | Pure Standby | **Mixed** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Switch-over delay | None | Present | **Minimal (only on standby path)** |
| Load sharing | Yes | No | **Yes (on active part)** |
| Wear-out exposure | All units | Only active | **Reduced for standby** |
| Reliability gain | Moderate | High | **Highest** |
| Complexity | Low | High | **Moderate–High** |

### 1.4 Classification of Mixed Redundancy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Based on the **loading state of the standby units**, mixed redundancy is classified into three canonical forms in the KTU 2024 Reliability Engineering syllabus:

1. **Mixed Configuration with Cold Standby** — Standby units are unpowered, de-energised, and experience zero operational stress; failure rate of standby ≈ 0 until activation.
2. **Mixed Configuration with Warm Standby** — Standby units are lightly loaded (e.g., heaters on, no full load); they carry a reduced but non-zero failure rate λ_w < λ_a (active failure rate).
3. **Mixed Configuration with Hot Standby** — Standby units are fully energised at full rated load but carry no functional output; their failure rate equals the active rate λ_h = λ_a.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operating Logic of a Mixed Redundancy Block

Consider the canonical mixed block: **m** active units in parallel + **s** standby units, with the system failing if fewer than **k** units function. The logical flow is:

- **Step 1 — Steady State:** All *m* active units share the load. The *s* standby units are in their designated state (cold/warm/hot). No failures → system healthy.
- **Step 2 — First Failure Detection:** A failure sensor or watchdog detects that one active unit has degraded or stopped. A control signal is dispatched to the changeover switch.
- **Step 3 — Switchover:** The failed active unit is isolated (possibly after a brief overlap), and one standby unit is brought online by the switching mechanism. The new active unit may require a *warm-up time* τ_s (relevant for warm/cold standby).
- **Step 4 — Reconfiguration:** The system continues with the remaining active units + the newly commissioned standby. The cycle repeats until standby units are exhausted.
- **Step 5 — System Failure:** System failure occurs when active units ≤ *k − 1* and no standby units remain.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet (Exam-Oriented Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below assume **identical components** with exponential time-to-failure distribution. For non-identical components, the formulas must be expanded with individual reliabilities R_i(t).

| # | Configuration | Reliability Formula R_sys(t) | Boundary Conditions |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | n active in parallel, all identical, system needs ≥ 1 | $R_{sys}(t) = 1 - (1 - e^{-\lambda t})^{n}$ | Identical λ, perfect switching |
| 2 | 1 active + s cold standby (system needs 1) | $R_{sys}(t) = e^{-\lambda t} \cdot \sum_{i=0}^{s} \dfrac{(\lambda t)^{i}}{i!}$ | Cold standby, instantaneous switching, λ_standby = 0 |
| 3 | 1 active + s warm standby | $R_{sys}(t) = e^{-\lambda t} \cdot \sum_{i=0}^{s} \dfrac{(\lambda_{w} t)^{i}}{i!}$ | Warm standby with failure rate λ_w |
| 4 | m active parallel + s cold standby, system needs k = m | $R_{sys}(t) = \sum_{j=0}^{s} \binom{m+s-j}{m} \, p^{\,m} q^{\,s-j} \cdot \text{(success prob.)}$ | Requires Poisson analysis (derived in §3) |
| 5 | Mixed with imperfect switch (reliability R_sw) | $R_{sys}(t) = R_{sw} \cdot \left[ e^{-\lambda t} \cdot \sum_{i=0}^{s} \dfrac{(\lambda t)^{i}}{i!} \right]$ | Multiplicative penalty on switch |
| 6 | Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF) of mixed 1+s cold standby | $\text{MTBF} = \dfrac{1}{\lambda} \cdot (s+1)$ | Direct from E[T] of Erlang(s+1, λ) |
| 7 | Hazard rate of mixed system | $h_{sys}(t) = \dfrac{\lambda \cdot (\lambda t)^{s}}{s! \cdot \sum_{i=0}^{s} (\lambda t)^{i}/i!}$ | Time-dependent hazard |
| 8 | Cold standby gain factor (vs. single unit) | $G = \dfrac{R_{mixed}}{R_{single}} = \sum_{i=0}^{s} \dfrac{(\lambda t)^{i}}{i!}$ | Always ≥ 1 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Notation used: **p = R(t) = e^{−λt}** = reliability of a single component, **q = 1 − p** = unreliability, **λ** = constant failure rate (failures/hour), **t** = mission time in hours.

### 2.3 Engineering Significance

Mixed redundancy is the **dominant design philosophy** in any application where:

- **Load sharing is mandatory** (parallel active units divide current/power/heat dissipation).
- **Burst-failure resilience** is mandatory (the system must survive multiple sequential failures, not just one).
- **Standby units must remain pristine** for late-life failures (cold/warm standby protects from wear-out during storage).
- **Mission duration is long** (satellite missions of 5–15 years — pure parallel would over-stress all units; pure standby would leave load capacity under-utilised).

Real-world deployments:

- **Boeing 777 Fly-By-Wire** — 3 active + 1 cold standby flight computers (k = 2 out of 4).
- **SpaceX Falcon 9 avionics** — dual-string active with cross-strapped standby.
- **Nuclear reactor scram system** — 2-of-4 mixed logic with hot standby channels.
- **Data centre UPS** — N+1 redundant UPS modules with active load sharing + cold standby for the (N+1)-th unit.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation A — Reliability of a 1-Active + s-Cold-Standby Mixed System

**Problem Setup:** A single active unit is required for system operation. There are *s* identical standby units in cold standby (zero failure rate while inactive). The switch is perfect (R_sw = 1) and instantaneous. Each active unit has constant failure rate λ.

**Logic:** The system survives the interval [0, t] if and only if the **total number of failures occurring during [0, t]** does **not exceed s**. This is because every failure of an active unit consumes one standby unit, and the system fails only when standby reserves are exhausted.

Since component failures follow a Poisson process with rate λ, the probability of exactly *i* failures in time *t* is given by the Poisson probability mass function:

$$P(N(t) = i) = \frac{(\lambda t)^{i} \cdot e^{-\lambda t}}{i!}$$

The system reliability is therefore the sum of probabilities of surviving 0, 1, 2, ..., s failures:

$$R_{sys}(t) = \sum_{i=0}^{s} P(N(t) = i)$$

Substituting the Poisson PMF into the summation:

$$R_{sys}(t) = \sum_{i=0}^{s} \frac{(\lambda t)^{i} \cdot e^{-\lambda t}}{i!}$$

**Step-by-Step Algebraic Simplification:**

$$\begin{aligned}
R_{sys}(t) &= \sum_{i=0}^{s} \frac{(\lambda t)^{i} \cdot e^{-\lambda t}}{i!} \\[6pt]
&= e^{-\lambda t} \cdot \sum_{i=0}^{s} \frac{(\lambda t)^{i}}{i!} \\[6pt]
&= e^{-\lambda t} \cdot \left[ 1 + (\lambda t) + \frac{(\lambda t)^{2}}{2!} + \frac{(\lambda t)^{3}}{3!} + \cdots + \frac{(\lambda t)^{s}}{s!} \right]
\end{aligned}$$

**Final Compact Result:**

$$\boxed{\,R_{sys}(t) = e^{-\lambda t} \cdot \sum_{i=0}^{s} \frac{(\lambda t)^{i}}{i!}\,}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **regularised upper incomplete gamma function** $\Gamma(s+1, \lambda t)/s!$, also known as the **Erlang distribution CDF** of order $s+1$.

---

### 3.2 Derivation B — Mean Time To Failure (MTTF) of the Same System

The MTTF is the expected value of the failure time T, given that the system survives up to s failures. Equivalently, T is the time of the (s+1)-th failure, which follows an Erlang(s+1, λ) distribution:

$$f_{T}(t) = \frac{\lambda \cdot (\lambda t)^{s} \cdot e^{-\lambda t}}{s!}$$

Therefore:

$$\text{MTTF} = \int_{0}^{\infty} t \cdot f_{T}(t) \, dt = \int_{0}^{\infty} t \cdot \frac{\lambda \cdot (\lambda t)^{s} \cdot e^{-\lambda t}}{s!} \, dt$$

Using the standard identity for the k-th moment of an Erlang distribution:

$$\int_{0}^{\infty} t^{k} \cdot \frac{\lambda^{k+1} \cdot t^{k} \cdot e^{-\lambda t}}{k!} \, dt = \frac{k+1}{\lambda}$$

For our case (k = s, but we need the first moment k = 1):

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{MTTF} &= \int_{0}^{\infty} t \cdot \frac{\lambda^{s+1} \cdot t^{s} \cdot e^{-\lambda t}}{s!} \, dt \\[6pt]
&= \frac{(s+1)}{\lambda}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{\,\text{MTTF} = \frac{s+1}{\lambda}\,}$$

**Verification for s = 0 (no standby):** MTTF = 1/λ ✓ (single component)
**Verification for s = 1 (one cold standby):** MTTF = 2/λ ✓ (well-known result)

---

### 3.3 Derivation C — Mixed Redundancy with Imperfect Switching

**Problem Setup:** Now suppose the changeover switch has reliability R_sw(t) < 1. This models the real-world reality that the switch itself can fail (relay contacts weld, semiconductor driver fails, software hangs, etc.).

**Logic:** For the system to recover from a failure of the active unit, two events must both succeed:
1. The standby unit must be functional (probability R_stby).
2. The switch must successfully route the standby into the active path (probability R_sw).

Since switchover must occur every time an active unit fails, the reliability of the mixed system is the **product** of:
- The reliability of the Poisson-conditioned event (standby sufficient), and
- The probability that the switch works for every required switchover.

For a single switchover (system with 1 active + 1 standby):

$$R_{sys}(t) = R_{sw} \cdot e^{-\lambda t} \cdot \left[ 1 + \lambda t \right]$$

For a system with 1 active + s standby (s required switchovers in the worst case):

$$R_{sys}(t) = R_{sw}^{\,s} \cdot e^{-\lambda t} \cdot \sum_{i=0}^{s} \frac{(\lambda t)^{i}}{i!}$$

If R_sw is constant (memoryless switch), the simplification is:

$$\boxed{\,R_{sys}(t) = R_{sw}^{\,s} \cdot e^{-\lambda t} \cdot \sum_{i=0}^{s} \frac{(\lambda t)^{i}}{i!}\,}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is a **multiplicative penalty** — even R_sw = 0.99 can drastically reduce system reliability when s is large, because the exponent s amplifies the switch unreliability.

---

### 3.4 Worked Analytical Example (KTU 14-Mark Pattern)

**Problem:** A critical industrial controller requires at least **2 working units out of 4** for safe operation. Two units operate in active parallel mode (sharing the load). Two units are in cold standby with a perfect automatic switch. Each unit has constant failure rate λ = 0.0005 per hour.

**Find:** (a) The system reliability R_sys(1000 hr). (b) The MTTF. (c) Compare with a pure 2-out-of-4 parallel configuration.

**Given:**
- m = 2 active units
- s = 2 cold standby units
- k = 2 (minimum required working)
- λ = 0.0005 /hr
- t = 1000 hr

**Part (a) — System Reliability:**

First, compute the reliability of a single component at t = 1000 hr:

$$p = e^{-\lambda t} = e^{-0.0005 \times 1000} = e^{-0.5} = 0.6065$$

The unreliability:

$$q = 1 - p = 1 - 0.6065 = 0.3935$$

Now compute the product λt:

$$\lambda t = 0.5$$

Apply the mixed redundancy formula (with cold standby and perfect switch):

$$R_{sys}(t) = e^{-\lambda t} \cdot \sum_{i=0}^{2} \frac{(\lambda t)^{i}}{i!}$$

Compute each term of the summation:

$$\begin{aligned}
i = 0: \quad & \frac{(0.5)^{0}}{0!} = 1 \\[4pt]
i = 1: \quad & \frac{(0.5)^{1}}{1!} = 0.5 \\[4pt]
i = 2: \quad & \frac{(0.5)^{2}}{2!} = \frac{0.25}{2} = 0.125
\end{aligned}$$

Sum the three terms:

$$\sum_{i=0}^{2} \frac{(0.5)^{i}}{i!} = 1 + 0.5 + 0.125 = 1.625$$

Multiply by the exponential factor:

$$R_{sys}(1000) = e^{-0.5} \times 1.625 = 0.6065 \times 1.625 = 0.9856$$

**Part (b) — MTTF:**

$$\text{MTTF} = \frac{s+1}{\lambda} = \frac{2+1}{0.0005} = \frac{3}{0.0005} = 6000 \text{ hours}$$

**Part (c) — Comparison with Pure 2-out-of-4 Parallel:**

For pure parallel k-out-of-n with k = 2, n = 4:

$$R_{parallel}(t) = \sum_{j=2}^{4} \binom{4}{j} \, p^{\,j} \, q^{\,4-j}$$

Compute each term:

$$\begin{aligned}
j = 2: \quad & \binom{4}{2} \cdot (0.6065)^{2} \cdot (0.3935)^{2} = 6 \cdot 0.3678 \cdot 0.1548 = 0.3417 \\[4pt]
j = 3: \quad & \binom{4}{3} \cdot (0.6065)^{3} \cdot (0.3935)^{1} = 4 \cdot 0.2231 \cdot 0.3935 = 0.3512 \\[4pt]
j = 4: \quad & \binom{4}{4} \cdot (0.6065)^{4} \cdot (0.3935)^{0} = 1 \cdot 0.1353 \cdot 1 = 0.1353
\end{aligned}$$

Sum the three terms:

$$R_{parallel}(1000) = 0.3417 + 0.3512 + 0.1353 = 0.8282$$

**Comparison Table:**

| Configuration | R(1000 hr) | MTTF (hr) |
|---|---|---|
| Mixed (2 active + 2 cold standby) | **0.9856** | **6000** |
| Pure 2-out-of-4 parallel | 0.8282 | (computed via integral) |

**Inference:** The mixed configuration yields a **~18.9 % reliability improvement** over the pure parallel arrangement at t = 1000 hr, because the cold standby units are not subject to wear-out stress during the storage interval.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Mixed Redundancy System

The diagram below captures the load-flow, failure-detection, and switchover logic of a canonical *m*-active + *s*-standby mixed redundancy block.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    src["LOAD SOURCE"] --> busA["ACTIVE BUS"]
    busA --> u1["UNIT 1 ACTIVE"]
    busA --> u2["UNIT 2 ACTIVE"]
    busA --> uM["UNIT M ACTIVE"]
    u1 --> sensor["FAILURE DETECTOR AND WATCHDOG"]
    u2 --> sensor
    uM --> sensor
    sensor --> ctrl["SWITCHOVER CONTROLLER"]
    ctrl --> sw["CHANGE OVER SWITCH"]
    ctrl -.command.-> sw
    s1["STANDBY 1 COLD WARM HOT"] --> sw
    s2["STANDBY 2 COLD WARM HOT"] --> sw
    sS["STANDBY S COLD WARM HOT"] --> sw
    sw --> out["SYSTEM OUTPUT TO LOAD"]
    sensor --> cnt["STANDBY COUNTER AND RESERVE TRACKER"]
    cnt --> ctrl
```

### 4.2 State Transition Diagram — Mixed Redundancy State Machine

States are indexed by (active units working, standby units remaining). Transitions occur upon active-unit failure.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> S_M_S : INITIAL STATE
    S_M_S : STATE M ACTIVE AND S STANDBY
    S_M_S --> S_M_minus_1_S_minus_1 : ACTIVE UNIT FAILS
    S_M_minus_1_S_minus_1 --> S_M_minus_2_S_minus_2 : ACTIVE UNIT FAILS
    S_M_minus_2_S_minus_2 --> S_2_0 : ACTIVE UNIT FAILS
    S_2_0 : STATE 2 ACTIVE AND 0 STANDBY
    S_2_0 --> S_1_0 : ACTIVE UNIT FAILS
    S_1_0 : STATE 1 ACTIVE AND 0 STANDBY CRITICAL
    S_1_0 --> F_SYS : FINAL ACTIVE UNIT FAILS
    F_SYS : SYSTEM FAILURE ABSORBING STATE
    F_SYS --> [*]
```

### 4.3 Reliability Build-up Comparison (Conceptual)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["SINGLE UNIT R equals e to power minus lambda t"] --> B["ADD COLD STANDBY"]
    B --> C["RELIABILITY GROWS AS POEPPER LIKE"]
    C --> D["ADD ACTIVE PARALLEL M STRINGS"]
    D --> E["HYBRID MIXED REDUNDANCY"]
    E --> F["MAXIMUM R_sys GAIN WITH MINIMUM WEAR"]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Question:** *Define mixed redundancy. How does it differ from pure active parallel redundancy and pure standby redundancy?*

**Model Answer:**
Mixed redundancy is a system configuration in which some components operate in active parallel mode while the remaining components are kept as standby (cold/warm/hot) reserves, switched in only upon failure of an active component.

It differs from:
- **Pure Active Parallel Redundancy:** All redundant units are energised and share the load from t = 0. There is no standby and no switchover.
- **Pure Standby Redundancy:** Only one unit operates initially; all others are standby. There is no load sharing.

Mixed redundancy combines the *load-sharing benefit of parallel* with the *failure-reserve benefit of standby*, yielding superior reliability with reduced wear on the standby units. **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Question:** *State the reliability expression for a mixed redundancy system with one active unit and 's' cold standby units, assuming perfect switching.*

**Model Answer:**

$$R_{sys}(t) = e^{-\lambda t} \cdot \sum_{i=0}^{s} \frac{(\lambda t)^{i}}{i!}$$

This expression is the complement of the Poisson CDF and equals the probability that at most *s* failures occur in time *t*. **(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students frequently forget the exponential factor **e^(−λt)** in the mixed formula. It MUST be included.
> - When the switch is imperfect, the penalty **R_sw^s** is multiplicative — do NOT add it as an additive term.
> - For m parallel active units, do NOT blindly use the 1-active formula; expand using the full binomial/Poisson analysis.
> - Always specify whether standby is cold, warm, or hot; each has a different λ.
> - MTTF is **(s+1)/λ ONLY for 1-active + s-cold-standby** — do not apply this to multi-active configurations.

---

#### Question A — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam – Model Paper]

**(a) [7 Marks]** A communication transponder system consists of **2 active transmitters operating in parallel** and **1 cold standby transmitter**. All transmitters are identical with constant failure rate λ = 0.002 per hour. The changeover switch is perfect. Calculate:
- (i) The system reliability at t = 500 hours.
- (ii) The MTTF of the system.

**(b) [7 Marks]** If the switch reliability is R_sw = 0.95, recalculate the system reliability at t = 500 hours and comment on the impact of imperfect switching.

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a)(i) — System Reliability with Perfect Switch:**

Given: λ = 0.002/hr, t = 500 hr, s = 1 cold standby.

$$\lambda t = 0.002 \times 500 = 1.0$$

For this 2-active + 1-cold-standby mixed system, the system fails only when **both active units fail AND the standby fails to be brought in** OR when **more than 2 failures occur in 500 hours**.

The Poisson-based formula (for the load-share path):

$$R_{sys}(t) = e^{-\lambda t} \cdot \left[ 1 + \lambda t + \frac{(\lambda t)^{2}}{2} \right]$$

Wait — for the 2-active case with 1 cold standby, the system can tolerate up to 2 failures (both actives fail sequentially, with the standby substituting after the first failure). Therefore s = 2 effectively when the standby is shared.

Computing:

$$\begin{aligned}
e^{-\lambda t} &= e^{-1.0} = 0.3679 \\[6pt]
\sum_{i=0}^{2} \frac{(\lambda t)^{i}}{i!} &= 1 + 1.0 + \frac{1.0}{2} = 2.5 \\[6pt]
R_{sys}(500) &= 0.3679 \times 2.5 = 0.9198
\end{aligned}$$

**Part (a)(ii) — MTTF:**

For the underlying Erlang process of order 3:

$$\text{MTTF} = \frac{3}{0.002} = 1500 \text{ hours}$$

**Part (b) — With Imperfect Switch R_sw = 0.95:**

Now apply the multiplicative switch penalty. Since the system requires up to 2 switchovers in the worst-case path:

$$R_{sys}^{imperfect}(t) = R_{sw}^{2} \cdot e^{-\lambda t} \cdot \sum_{i=0}^{2} \frac{(\lambda t)^{i}}{i!}$$

Computing the switch penalty:

$$R_{sw}^{2} = (0.95)^{2} = 0.9025$$

Final result:

$$R_{sys}^{imperfect}(500) = 0.9025 \times 0.9198 = 0.8301$$

**Comment:** The reliability dropped from 0.9198 to 0.8301 — a **9.75 % decrease** caused solely by switch unreliability. This demonstrates the critical importance of switch quality in mixed redundancy design. **(14 marks complete)**

---

#### Question B — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam – Model Paper — Alternative Choice]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the concept of **mixed redundancy** with a real-world example. Distinguish between cold, warm, and hot standby in the mixed configuration.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A navigation system on a satellite consists of **3 active inertial measurement units (IMUs)** operating in parallel, with **1 hot standby IMU**. Each IMU has failure rate λ = 0.0001/hr. The system requires at least **3 working units** for full mission success. Mission duration is 10,000 hours. Compute the probability of mission success.

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Concept Explanation:**

(Already covered in Section 1; summarised here for marks.)

- **Mixed redundancy** combines active parallel and standby redundancy.
- **Cold Standby:** Inactive, unpowered, λ_standby = 0.
- **Warm Standby:** Lightly powered, λ_w < λ_active.
- **Hot Standby:** Fully powered but isolated, λ_h = λ_active.

Real-world example: Aircraft autopilot with 3 active computers + 1 hot standby; the standby continuously runs diagnostics but does not control the aircraft until activated. **(7 marks)**

**Part (b) — Satellite Navigation System:**

Given:
- m = 3 active units
- s = 1 hot standby
- k = 3 required working units
- λ = 0.0001/hr
- t = 10,000 hr

Compute λt:

$$\lambda t = 0.0001 \times 10{,}000 = 1.0$$

Since the standby is *hot*, its failure rate equals the active failure rate. The mixed redundancy formula for s = 1 cold standby (we have hot, so the same math applies because λ is the same):

$$R_{sys}(t) = e^{-\lambda t} \cdot \sum_{i=0}^{1} \frac{(\lambda t)^{i}}{i!}$$

Compute each term:

$$\begin{aligned}
e^{-\lambda t} &= e^{-1.0} = 0.3679 \\[6pt]
\sum_{i=0}^{1} \frac{(\lambda t)^{i}}{i!} &= 1 + 1.0 = 2.0
\end{aligned}$$

System reliability:

$$R_{sys}(10{,}000) = 0.3679 \times 2.0 = 0.7358$$

Probability of mission success ≈ **73.58 %**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note:** Because the standby is HOT (carrying full λ), its survival probability is the same as any active unit. The formula reduces to the Erlang-2 survival function, which is why the answer is 0.7358. If the standby were cold (λ_stby = 0), the reliability would be much higher (≈ 0.9197). This illustrates why hot standby is less effective than cold standby for long missions. **(7 marks complete)**

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!NOTE]
> **Important Things to Remember (KTU 2024 Module 2 — Mixed Redundancy):**

- **Definition:** Mixed redundancy = Active Parallel + Standby, working together. Components total = n = m + s.
- **Key Formula (most-tested):**
  $$R_{sys}(t) = e^{-\lambda t} \cdot \sum_{i=0}^{s} \frac{(\lambda t)^{i}}{i!}$$
- **MTTF:** $(s+1)/\lambda$ — valid ONLY for 1-active + s-cold-standby.
- **Three Standby Modes:** Cold (λ_stby = 0), Warm (λ_w < λ_active), Hot (λ_h = λ_active).
- **Imperfect Switch Penalty:** Multiply by $R_{sw}^{\,s}$ — the exponent equals the number of required switchovers.
- **Hazard Rate Formula:**
  $$h_{sys}(t) = \frac{\lambda \cdot (\lambda t)^{s}}{s! \cdot \sum_{i=0}^{s} (\lambda t)^{i}/i!}$$
  This is **time-dependent**, increasing with t (wear-out signature).
- **Engineering Edge:** Mixed redundancy is preferred over pure parallel when standby wear-out matters (satellites, aerospace, nuclear).
- **Common Mistake 1:** Forgetting $e^{-\lambda t}$ factor. ❌
- **Common Mistake 2:** Using MTTF = (s+1)/λ for multi-active configurations. ❌
- **Common Mistake 3:** Adding the switch penalty instead of multiplying. ❌
- **Common Mistake 4:** Confusing mixed redundancy with r-out-of-n parallel. They are related but not identical — mixed has a *switching mechanism*.
- **Examiner Tip:** Always draw the **block diagram + state diagram** in Part B answers — it earns 2 free marks and demonstrates conceptual clarity.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Standby Redundancy
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET632 - RELIABILITY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Standby Redundancy

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Standby Redundancy** is a configuration in which one or more components (called *standby units* or *backup units*) remain *inactive* (de-energized or in a low-stress idle state) during normal operation and are *switched into service* only upon the failure of the *active (online) unit*. The transition from idle to active is mediated by a **fault-detection and switching mechanism** (often called the *sensor* and *switch*).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (Paraphrased):**
> "Standby redundancy is a redundant configuration where the redundant component is kept inoperative (standby mode) until the active component fails, at which point the switch activates the standby unit. The reliability of the switch itself is a critical parameter."

---

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are driving a long highway trip with **only one spare tire** in your trunk:

- The **active tire** (the one currently rolling) does the entire job. It is under continuous stress, heat, wear, and friction — its hazard rate is constantly increasing.
- The **spare tire** sits silently in the trunk. It is not rotating, not heating up, and not accumulating wear. Its hazard rate is effectively **zero** during the trip.
- The moment the active tire goes flat (fails), you stop the car, and your **jack + wrench (the switch)** puts the spare into operation.
- If your jack breaks (switch failure), you cannot replace the tire, even though the spare is perfectly fine.

This analogy captures the three pillars of standby redundancy:
1. The **active unit** operates at full stress.
2. The **standby unit** has near-zero degradation until needed.
3. The **switch** is a single point of vulnerability.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why is this different from Active (Parallel) Redundancy?**
> In *parallel redundancy*, both units are powered and operating simultaneously, sharing the load. In *standby redundancy*, only one unit operates at a time, and the backup waits silently. This makes standby systems potentially **more reliable** (because the backup does not age), but **riskier** (because the switch can fail).

---

### 1.3 Physical / Engineering Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Unit | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Failure rate (active) | $\lambda$ | **failures/hour** | Usually assumed **constant** (exponential distribution) for KTU problems |
| Failure rate (standby, cold) | $\lambda_s$ | failures/hour | For *cold standby*, $\lambda_s = 0$ |
| Switch reliability | $R_{sw}$ | dimensionless (0 to 1) | Probability the switch successfully activates the standby |
| Mission time | $t$ | hours | Duration the system must operate |
| System reliability | $R_s(t)$ | dimensionless (0 to 1) | Probability the system survives up to time $t$ |
| Mean Time To Failure | **MTTF** | hours | Expected lifetime of the redundant system |

> [!TIP]
> **Cold vs Warm vs Hot Standby** — A Key Distinction:
> - **Cold Standby:** Standby unit is unpowered, $\lambda_s = 0$. Maximum theoretical reliability.
> - **Warm Standby:** Standby unit is lightly powered, $0 < \lambda_s < \lambda$.
> - **Hot Standby:** Standby unit is fully powered but not carrying load, $\lambda_s = \lambda$. This effectively becomes identical to *active parallel redundancy*.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Structural Components of a Standby System

A generic **2-unit standby redundant system** contains four conceptual blocks:

1. **Active Unit (A)** — Carries the full load during normal operation. Has failure rate $\lambda_a$.
2. **Standby Unit (B)** — Kept idle. Has failure rate $\lambda_s$ (often zero for cold standby).
3. **Switch / Sensor (SW)** — Monitors unit A. Has reliability $R_{sw}$.
4. **Load / Mission** — Receives output from whichever unit is currently operational.

The system fails **only if**:
- Unit A fails **AND**
- (Switch fails to activate B **OR** Unit B fails at the moment of activation).

---

### 2.2 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step

**Step 1 — Initialization (t = 0):** Unit A is online. Unit B is in standby. Switch is in "monitoring" mode.

**Step 2 — Steady Operation (0 < t < t₁):** Unit A operates at hazard rate $\lambda_a$. Unit B remains unaffected. Probability that A survives to time $t$:
$$R_A(t) = e^{-\lambda_a t}$$

**Step 3 — Failure of A (t = t₁):** A fails. The sensor detects this failure and triggers the switch.

**Step 4 — Switching Event:**
- With probability $R_{sw}$: Switch successfully connects B to the load. B now operates for the *remaining mission time* $(t - t_1)$ at hazard rate $\lambda_b$.
- With probability $(1 - R_{sw})$: Switch fails. The system collapses, even if B is healthy.

**Step 5 — Survival of B:** Given that B is switched in at time $t_1$, its survival probability for the remaining time $(t - t_1)$ is:
$$R_B(t - t_1) = e^{-\lambda_b (t - t_1)}$$

**Step 6 — System Survival:** The system survives up to $t$ if A survives to $t_1$ **AND** switch works **AND** B survives from $t_1$ to $t$.

---

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Configuration | System Reliability $R_s(t)$ | MTTF | Key Assumptions |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **2 identical units, perfect switch, cold standby** | $R_s(t) = e^{-\lambda t}(1 + \lambda t)$ | $\dfrac{2}{\lambda}$ | $\lambda_a = \lambda_b = \lambda$, $R_{sw}=1$, cold ($\lambda_s=0$) |
| 2 | **2 identical units, imperfect switch, cold standby** | $R_s(t) = R_{sw} \cdot e^{-\lambda t}(1 + \lambda t)$ | $R_{sw} \cdot \dfrac{2}{\lambda}$ | Same as above but $R_{sw} < 1$ |
| 3 | **2 identical units, perfect switch, warm standby** | $R_s(t) = \dfrac{\lambda e^{-\lambda_s t} - \lambda_s e^{-\lambda t}}{\lambda - \lambda_s}$ | $\dfrac{1}{\lambda} + \dfrac{1}{\lambda_b}$ | Both rates non-zero, $R_{sw}=1$ |
| 4 | **n identical units, perfect switch, cold standby** | $R_s(t) = e^{-\lambda t} \displaystyle\sum_{k=0}^{n-1} \dfrac{(\lambda t)^k}{k!}$ | $\dfrac{n}{\lambda}$ | Cold standby, perfect switching |
| 5 | **2 different units (λ₁, λ₂), perfect switch, cold standby** | $R_s(t) = \dfrac{\lambda_2 e^{-\lambda_1 t} - \lambda_1 e^{-\lambda_2 t}}{\lambda_2 - \lambda_1}$ | $\dfrac{1}{\lambda_1} + \dfrac{1}{\lambda_2}$ | $\lambda_1 \neq \lambda_2$, cold standby |
| 6 | **2 identical units, imperfect switch, exponential switch failure** | $R_s(t) = \dfrac{\lambda_a \cdot e^{-\lambda_{sw} t} - \lambda_{sw} \cdot e^{-\lambda_a t}}{\lambda_a - \lambda_{sw}}$ (when switch failure rate $\lambda_{sw}$ used) | Depends | More detailed model |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master Rule for KTU 2024 Exams:**
> Whenever you see "standby redundancy" in a numerical problem, the first thing to check is:
> 1. Is the switch **perfect** ($R_{sw} = 1$) or **imperfect**?
> 2. Is the standby **cold** ($\lambda_s = 0$) or **warm**?
> 3. Are the units **identical** ($\lambda_1 = \lambda_2 = \lambda$) or **different**?

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Domain | Why Standby is Used |
|---|---|
| **Aircraft Twin-Engine Systems** | Auxiliary Power Unit (APU) sits in standby; both main engines are active, but APU provides cold backup |
| **Uninterruptible Power Supplies (UPS)** | Battery sits idle until mains power fails |
| **Telecommunications Backbone** | Spare fiber optic line on standby; optical switch activates on primary failure |
| **Nuclear Reactor Safety** | Emergency Cooling Systems (ECS) sit in cold standby; activated on primary coolant failure |
| **Spacecraft Electronics** | Redundant processors held in standby to maximize mission duration (since standby doesn't age) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Standby redundancy is preferred when **load cannot be shared** (e.g., a single high-power transmitter cannot be split across two smaller units). It is also preferred when the **standby degradation rate is non-zero** during operation (e.g., electrolytic capacitors in a hot environment).

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Reliability of 2-Unit Cold Standby with Perfect Switch

This is the **most frequently asked derivation** in KTU exams. We derive it from first principles using conditional probability.

**Given:**
- Two identical components, each with constant failure rate $\lambda$.
- Cold standby ($\lambda_s = 0$).
- Perfect switch ($R_{sw} = 1$).
- Mission time $t$.

**Method — Conditioning on the failure time of unit A:**

Let $T_A$ = time to failure of unit A. Let $T_B$ = time to failure of unit B (measured from when it is activated).

The system survives the mission if **at least one** of the following events occurs:
- **Event E₁:** Unit A does not fail during $[0, t]$. (B is never needed.)
- **Event E₂:** Unit A fails at some time $t_1 \in (0, t)$, the switch activates B, and B survives the remaining time $(t - t_1)$.

By the **law of total probability** and **conditional independence**:

$$R_s(t) = P(\text{E}_1) + P(\text{E}_2)$$

**Compute P(E₁):**
Since unit A operates for the full duration $t$ at rate $\lambda$:
$$P(\text{E}_1) = e^{-\lambda t}$$

**Compute P(E₂):**
Unit A fails at some instant $t_1 \in (0, t)$, and B survives the remaining time. We integrate over all possible $t_1$:

$$P(\text{E}_2) = \int_{0}^{t} \left[\underbrace{f_A(t_1)}_{\text{density of A's failure}} \cdot \underbrace{P(B \text{ survives } t - t_1 \vert B \text{ activated at } t_1)}_{\text{conditional survival of B}}\right] dt_1$$

For exponential distribution, $f_A(t_1) = \lambda e^{-\lambda t_1}$. Since B is cold and then operates at rate $\lambda$ for duration $(t - t_1)$:
$$P(B \text{ survives } t - t_1) = e^{-\lambda(t - t_1)}$$

Therefore:
$$P(\text{E}_2) = \int_{0}^{t} \lambda e^{-\lambda t_1} \cdot e^{-\lambda(t - t_1)} \, dt_1$$

Let's simplify the integrand:
$$\lambda e^{-\lambda t_1} \cdot e^{-\lambda(t - t_1)} = \lambda e^{-\lambda t_1} \cdot e^{-\lambda t} \cdot e^{\lambda t_1} = \lambda e^{-\lambda t}$$

The $t_1$ terms cancel — this is a beautiful property of exponential distributions! Now integrate:

$$P(\text{E}_2) = \int_{0}^{t} \lambda e^{-\lambda t} \, dt_1 = \lambda e^{-\lambda t} \cdot [t_1]_0^t = \lambda t \cdot e^{-\lambda t}$$

**Combine:**
$$R_s(t) = e^{-\lambda t} + \lambda t \cdot e^{-\lambda t}$$

$$\boxed{R_s(t) = e^{-\lambda t}(1 + \lambda t)}$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation: MTTF for 2-Unit Cold Standby with Perfect Switch

The Mean Time To Failure is the **integral of reliability** from 0 to ∞:

$$\text{MTTF} = \int_{0}^{\infty} R_s(t) \, dt$$

Substituting our result:

$$\text{MTTF} = \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-\lambda t}(1 + \lambda t) \, dt$$

Split into two integrals:

$$\text{MTTF} = \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-\lambda t} \, dt + \lambda \int_{0}^{\infty} t \cdot e^{-\lambda t} \, dt$$

**First integral** (standard exponential integral):

$$\int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-\lambda t} \, dt = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}$$

**Second integral** (using $\int_0^\infty t e^{-at} dt = \frac{1}{a^2}$):

$$\int_{0}^{\infty} t \cdot e^{-\lambda t} \, dt = \dfrac{1}{\lambda^2}$$

Therefore:

$$\text{MTTF} = \dfrac{1}{\lambda} + \lambda \cdot \dfrac{1}{\lambda^2} = \dfrac{1}{\lambda} + \dfrac{1}{\lambda}$$

$$\boxed{\text{MTTF} = \dfrac{2}{\lambda}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Comparison with single unit:** A single component has MTTF $= \frac{1}{\lambda}$. The 2-unit standby system has MTTF $= \frac{2}{\lambda}$. This is exactly **double the lifetime** — a dramatic improvement achieved by adding just one cheap standby unit!

---

### 3.3 Derivation: n-Unit Cold Standby (Perfect Switch) by Induction

**Claim:** For $n$ identical units in cold standby with perfect switching:

$$R_s(t) = e^{-\lambda t} \sum_{k=0}^{n-1} \dfrac{(\lambda t)^k}{k!}$$

**Base case (n=1):** A single unit has $R_s(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$, which matches the formula (empty sum from $k=0$ to $0$ is just $1$).

**Base case (n=2):** We already derived $R_s(t) = e^{-\lambda t}(1 + \lambda t)$, which matches $e^{-\lambda t}\left[\frac{(\lambda t)^0}{0!} + \frac{(\lambda t)^1}{1!}\right]$. ✓

**Inductive step:** Assume the formula holds for $n$ units. For $(n+1)$ units, we add one more standby unit. The system reduces to a 2-unit problem where:
- The "first unit" is the entire $n$-unit subsystem, with effective reliability $R_n(t)$.
- The "second unit" is unit $(n+1)$, identical, with reliability $e^{-\lambda t}$.

By the 2-unit formula structure:
$$R_{n+1}(t) = R_n(t) + \int_{0}^{t} f_n(t_1) \cdot e^{-\lambda(t - t_1)} \, dt_1$$

where $f_n(t_1)$ is the failure density of the $n$-unit subsystem. By the inductive hypothesis and standard series manipulations, this reduces to:

$$R_{n+1}(t) = e^{-\lambda t} \sum_{k=0}^{n} \dfrac{(\lambda t)^k}{k!} \quad \blacksquare$$

---

### 3.4 Derivation: Imperfect Switch Case (Constant $R_{sw}$)

**Given:** Same as before, but switch reliability is $R_{sw} \in (0, 1)$.

**Reasoning:** When unit A fails at time $t_1$:
- With probability $R_{sw}$, B is switched in. B then has survival probability $e^{-\lambda(t - t_1)}$.
- With probability $(1 - R_{sw})$, the system fails immediately. No credit is given to B.

Therefore, the conditional survival of B given A's failure at $t_1$ is:
$$R_{sw} \cdot e^{-\lambda(t - t_1)}$$

Substituting into the integral:
$$P(\text{E}_2) = \int_{0}^{t} \lambda e^{-\lambda t_1} \cdot R_{sw} \cdot e^{-\lambda(t - t_1)} \, dt_1 = R_{sw} \cdot \lambda t \cdot e^{-\lambda t}$$

$$\boxed{R_s(t) = R_{sw} \cdot e^{-\lambda t}(1 + \lambda t)}$$

$$\boxed{\text{MTTF} = \dfrac{2 R_{sw}}{\lambda}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students sometimes write $R_s(t) = e^{-\lambda t}(1 + R_{sw} \lambda t)$ which is **incorrect**. The switch reliability factor multiplies the *entire* redundant term, not just the $\lambda t$ piece.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Worked Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A system has one active unit with $\lambda = 0.001$ failures/hour, and one cold standby unit with the same failure rate when active. The switch reliability is 0.95. Find (i) the system reliability for a 1000-hour mission, and (ii) the system MTTF.

**Solution:**

**(i) Reliability calculation:**

Apply the imperfect-switch formula:
$$R_s(t) = R_{sw} \cdot e^{-\lambda t}(1 + \lambda t)$$

Compute $\lambda t = 0.001 \times 1000 = 1$.

Therefore:
$$R_s(1000) = 0.95 \cdot e^{-1}(1 + 1) = 0.95 \cdot e^{-1} \cdot 2$$

Compute $e^{-1} \approx 0.3679$:
$$R_s(1000) = 0.95 \cdot 0.3679 \cdot 2 = 0.95 \cdot 0.7358 \approx 0.699$$

Compare with a single unit: $R(1000) = e^{-1} \approx 0.368$.

**Conclusion:** Standby redundancy improved reliability from **36.8%** to **69.9%** — nearly doubled.

**(ii) MTTF calculation:**

$$\text{MTTF} = \dfrac{2 R_{sw}}{\lambda} = \dfrac{2 \times 0.95}{0.001} = 1900 \text{ hours}$$

Compare with a single unit: $\text{MTTF} = \frac{1}{\lambda} = 1000$ hours. Again, **nearly doubled**.

---

### 3.6 Comparative Analytical Matrix

| Configuration | $R_s(t)$ at $\lambda t = 1$ | MTTF (hours, $\lambda=0.001$) |
|---|---|---|
| Single unit | $0.368$ | $1000$ |
| Active parallel (both running) | $2 e^{-\lambda t} - e^{-2\lambda t} = 0.600$ | $\frac{3}{2\lambda} = 1500$ |
| 2-unit cold standby (perfect switch) | $e^{-1} \cdot 2 = 0.736$ | $\frac{2}{\lambda} = 2000$ |
| 2-unit cold standby (switch $R_{sw}=0.95$) | $0.95 \cdot 0.736 = 0.699$ | $1900$ |
| 3-unit cold standby (perfect switch) | $e^{-1}(1 + 1 + 0.5) = 0.920$ | $\frac{3}{\lambda} = 3000$ |

> [!TIP]
> **Generalization observed:** For $n$-unit cold standby with perfect switch, MTTF scales linearly as $\frac{n}{\lambda}$. This is far superior to active parallel redundancy, where MTTF scales as $\sum_{k=1}^{n} \frac{1}{k\lambda}$ (harmonic growth).

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture of a 2-Unit Standby System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    node1([MISSION START t=0]) --> node2[Active Unit A lambdaA]
    node2 --> node3{Unit A operational?}
    node3 -- YES --> node4[Load Receives Output from A]
    node4 --> node5{Time less than t?}
    node5 -- YES --> node2
    node3 -- NO --> node6[Sensor Detects Failure of A]
    node6 --> node7{Switch works Rsw?}
    node7 -- YES --> node8[Standby Unit B Activated lambdaB]
    node8 --> node9[Load Receives Output from B]
    node9 --> node10{Time less than t?}
    node10 -- YES --> node9
    node10 -- NO --> node11([SYSTEM SURVIVES MISSION])
    node7 -- NO --> node12([SYSTEM FAILURE AT SWITCH])
    node5 -- NO --> node11
```

### 4.2 Reliability Block Diagram (Logical View)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeA[Active Unit A] --> nodeSW[Switch SW]
    nodeSW --> nodeL[Load]
    nodeB[Standby Unit B] --> nodeSW
    nodeSW -.activates upon A failure.-> nodeB
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The standby unit B is **not in the signal path** during normal operation. It enters the circuit only after the switch toggles upon detection of A's failure.

### 4.3 State Transition Diagram (Markov Model)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    state1[STATE 1: Both operational A active B standby]
    state2[STATE 2: A failed B active]
    state3[STATE 3: Both failed or switch failed ABSORBING]
    state1 --> state2 : lambda A switch OK
    state1 --> state3 : lambda A switch fail
    state2 --> state3 : lambda B
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the Markov Diagram:**
> - **State 1 (initial):** Both A and B are good. A is carrying load. Transition out occurs when A fails.
> - **State 2:** A has failed, B is now active. System still functioning. Transition out occurs when B fails.
> - **State 3 (absorbing):** System has failed. No transitions out.
> - The transition from State 1 → State 3 captures **switch failure**.

### 4.4 Comparative Schematic: Standby vs Active Parallel

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SB[STANDBY REDUNDANCY]
        sA[Unit A] --> sLoad[Load]
        sB[Unit B idle] -.switch on A failure.-> sLoad
        sSW[Switch] -.monitors A.-> sA
    end
    subgraph AP[ACTIVE PARALLEL REDUNDANCY]
        pA[Unit A] --> pLoad[Load]
        pB[Unit B] --> pLoad
    end
```

| Feature | Standby | Active Parallel |
|---|---|---|
| Power consumption (nominal) | Lower (only one unit powered) | Higher (both units powered) |
| Switching device required | Yes | No |
| Effect of switch failure | Catastrophic | Not applicable |
| Component aging | Only active unit ages | Both units age equally |
| MTTF for 2 identical exponential units | $\frac{2}{\lambda}$ | $\frac{3}{2\lambda}$ |
| Typical use case | Backup power, emergency systems | Load sharing, fault tolerance |
