<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Define *double failure* in the context of redundant systems. How is it different from a *common-mode failure*?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A *double failure* is a reliability event in which **two independent components must fail** (typically within a defined mission window) to cause system failure. It is the characteristic failure mode of **1-out-of-2 or 2-out-of-3 redundant architectures**. By contrast, a *common-mode failure* originates from a **single root cause** (e.g., a shared power supply, design flaw, or external shock) that disables multiple components **simultaneously**, irrespective of redundancy. Hence redundancy protects against double failures but is *ineffective* against common-mode failures unless **diversity** (different makes/principles) is built in. *(1 mark — definition, 1 mark — contrast, 1 mark — example)*

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**State any three differences between *active* and *standby* redundancy.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| # | Active (Parallel) | Standby |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | All units operate from t = 0 | Standby unit dormant until switch |
| 2 | No switch required → simpler | Switch + failure detector mandatory |
| 3 | Lower MTTF gain (1.5/λ for 2 units) | Higher MTTF gain (2/λ for 2 units, perfect switch) |
| 4 | Immune to switch failure | Degrades sharply with imperfect Rₛ |
| 5 | Higher power consumption (all units energised) | Lower power consumption |

*(1 mark per valid contrasting point, max 3)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** Common mark-loss zones in this topic:
> 1. **Forgetting the assumption of constant failure rate λ** — KTU expects the phrase *"assuming constant failure rate (exponential distribution)"* written explicitly (1 mark).
> 2. **Writing the parallel reliability as R₁ × R₂** — this is the *series* formula and is the single most common error. Always write R = 1 − (1−R₁)(1−R₂).
> 3. **Not drawing the RBD or state diagram** — KTU awards 2 marks for a clean figure alone.
> 4. **Forgetting the MTTF units** — write "hours" or "h" explicitly.
> 5. **In standby problems, ignoring the switch reliability Rₛ** when the question mentions an imperfect switch.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** A critical control system uses **two identical pumps in active parallel**, each having constant failure rate **λ = 0.0008 per hour**. Compute:
  (i) The system reliability for a **mission time of 500 hours**.
  (ii) The **MTTF** of the system.
  (iii) The **probability that exactly one pump fails** in 500 hours. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** With the same two pumps, design a **standby configuration** assuming a switching device with reliability **Rₛ = 0.95**. Compute the system reliability for the same 500-hour mission and **comment on which configuration is better**. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution — Part A(a)

**Step 1 — Component reliability at t = 500 h** *(1 mark)*

$$R_1 = R_2 = e^{-0.0008 \times 500} = e^{-0.4} = 0.6703$$

**Step 2 — System reliability (active parallel)** *(2 marks)*

$$R_{sys} = 1 - (1-R_1)(1-R_2) = 1 - (0.3297)^2 = 1 - 0.1087 = 0.8913$$

**Step 3 — MTTF of 2-parallel system** *(2 marks)*

$$MTTF = \frac{3}{2\lambda} = \frac{3}{2 \times 0.0008} = 1875 \text{ hours}$$

**Step 4 — Probability that exactly one pump fails in 500 h** *(2 marks)*

$$P(\text{exactly one fails}) = \binom{2}{1} R_1(1-R_2) = 2 \times 0.6703 \times 0.3297 = 0.4420$$

**Final Answers (a):** R_sys = **0.8913**, MTTF = **1875 h**, P(exactly one fail) = **0.4420**.

#### Model Solution — Part A(b)

**Step 1 — Standby reliability with imperfect switch** *(3 marks)*

$$R_{st,imp}(t) = e^{-\lambda t}\big[\,1 + R_s \cdot \lambda t\,\big]$$

Substituting λ = 0.0008/h, t = 500 h, Rₛ = 0.95:

$$R_{st,imp} = e^{-0.4}\big[1 + 0.95 \times 0.0008 \times 500\big]$$

$$R_{st,imp} = 0.6703 \times \big[1 + 0.38\big] = 0.6703 \times 1.38 = 0.9250$$

**Step 2 — Comparison with active parallel** *(2 marks)*

| Configuration | R(500 h) |
|---|---|
| Active parallel (from part a) | 0.8913 |
| Standby (Rₛ = 0.95) | **0.9250** |

**Step 3 — Comment** *(2 marks)*

The **standby configuration is superior** in this case because Rₛ = 0.95 is high enough that the standby's *theoretical* advantage (no wear-out of dormant unit) more than compensates for the slight switch imperfection. If Rₛ had been ≤ 0.5, active parallel would be preferable.

**Final Answer (b):** R_standby = **0.9250**, and standby is the better configuration for this mission.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice — **[KTU University Exam — July 2023]**

**(a)** Derive, step by step, the **MTTF of a 2-component active parallel system** with constant failure rate λ. Show that the MTTF is **3/(2λ)**. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A **3-out-of-4:G system** has identical components with R(t) = e^(−λt) and λ = 0.002/h. Find the system reliability and MTTF for a **mission of 200 hours**. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution — Part B(a)

This is the **canonical KTU derivation** (full 7-mark layout below).

**Step 1 — State the assumption** *(1 mark)*
Assume constant failure rate λ → exponential distribution: R₁(t) = R₂(t) = e^(−λt).

**Step 2 — Write the unreliability of each component** *(1 mark)*

$$Q_1 = Q_2 = 1 - e^{-\lambda t}$$

**Step 3 — System fails only on double failure (independent events)** *(1 mark)*

$$Q_{sys} = Q_1 \cdot Q_2 = (1 - e^{-\lambda t})^2$$

**Step 4 — Expand and find R_sys** *(1 mark)*

$$Q_{sys} = 1 - 2e^{-\lambda t} + e^{-2\lambda t}$$
$$R_{sys} = 2e^{-\lambda t} - e^{-2\lambda t}$$

**Step 5 — MTTF integral setup** *(1 mark)*

$$MTTF = \int_{0}^{\infty} R_{sys}(t)\, dt = \int_{0}^{\infty}\left(2e^{-\lambda t} - e^{-2\lambda t}\right) dt$$

**Step 6 — Evaluate term-by-term** *(1 mark)*

$$MTTF = 2 \cdot \frac{1}{\lambda} - \frac{1}{2\lambda} = \frac{3}{2\lambda}$$

**Step 7 — State the boxed result with interpretation** *(1 mark)*

$$\boxed{\,MTTF_{\text{2-parallel}} = \dfrac{3}{2\lambda} = 1.5 \times MTTF_{\text{single}}\,}$$

The system is **1.5× more reliable in the MTTF sense** than a single component, despite doubling the hardware.

#### Model Solution — Part B(b)

**Step 1 — Component reliability at t = 200 h** *(1 mark)*

$$R = e^{-0.002 \times 200} = e^{-0.4} = 0.6703$$

**Step 2 — Apply the k-out-of-n formula** *(3 marks)*

For a 3-out-of-4:G system:

$$R_{sys} = \sum_{i=3}^{4} \binom{4}{i} R^i (1-R)^{4-i}$$

$$R_{sys} = \binom{4}{3} R^3 (1-R) + \binom{4}{4} R^4$$

**Step 3 — Compute each term** *(1 mark)*

$$R^3 = 0.6703^3 = 0.3011, \quad R^4 = 0.6703^4 = 0.2019$$
$$1 - R = 0.3297$$

**Step 4 — Substitute** *(1 mark)*

$$R_{sys} = 4 \times 0.3011 \times 0.3297 + 1 \times 0.2019 = 0.3970 + 0.2019 = 0.5989$$

**Step 5 — MTTF computation** *(1 mark)*
The MTTF of a k-out-of-n:G system with identical exponential components can be evaluated using the standard integral:

$$MTTF = \int_0^\infty R_{sys}(t)\, dt$$

Substituting R(t) = e^(−λt), with λ = 0.002/h, and using binomial expansion:

$$MTTF = \frac{1}{\lambda} \sum_{i=3}^{4} \frac{1}{i} = \frac{1}{0.002}\left(\frac{1}{3} + \frac{1}{4}\right) = 500 \times 0.5833 = 291.67 \text{ h}$$

**Final Answers (b):** R_sys(200 h) = **0.5989**, MTTF ≈ **291.7 hours**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory Aid for KTU:** For a *k-out-of-n:G* system with identical exponential components, the MTTF is
> $$MTTF = \frac{1}{\lambda}\left(\frac{1}{k} + \frac{1}{k+1} + \cdots + \frac{1}{n}\right)$$
> For our case (k=3, n=4) → $\frac{1}{\lambda}\left(\frac{1}{3}+\frac{1}{4}\right)$ — directly gives the answer.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Summary — Read this 30 minutes before the exam.**

* **Double failure** = the system-failure event in 1-out-of-2 redundancy, requiring **two** independent component failures.
* **Active parallel** of *n* identical components: $R = 1 - (1-R)^n$ and MTTF = $\frac{1}{\lambda}\sum_{k=1}^{n}\frac{1}{k}$ — harmonic series.
* **Standby (1+1, perfect switch)**: $R = e^{-\lambda t}(1 + \lambda t)$, MTTF = $2/\lambda$.
* **Standby with imperfect switch (Rₛ)**: $R = e^{-\lambda t}\big[1 + R_s \lambda t\big]$, MTTF = $\frac{1}{\lambda}(1+R_s)$.
* **Critical switching threshold**: Standby beats active parallel only when $R_s > 0.5$.
* **k-out-of-n:G** MTTF shortcut: $\frac{1}{\lambda}\sum_{i=k}^{n}\frac{1}{i}$.
* **Common-mode failure** defeats redundancy — must use *diversity*.
* **Coverage factor (c)** reduces the MTTF of standby further: $MTTF = \frac{1}{\lambda}\big(c + (1-c)(1+R_s)\big)$.
* Always **state the assumption** *"constant failure rate, independent components, exponential distribution"* at the top of every solution (1 free mark in KTU ESE).
* Always **draw the RBD / state diagram** before the algebra (2 free marks).
* Always **carry units** (hours) in MTTF/MTBF answers.
* Hot-standby (warm) vs Cold-standby: warm units have a *small dormant failure rate λ_d*; cold has λ_d = 0.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Markov Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET632 - RELIABILITY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Markov Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Markov Analysis** is a stochastic modeling technique used in reliability engineering to evaluate the time-dependent behavior of repairable systems whose future state depends *only* on the present state and not on the sequence of past events that led to it. This "memoryless" property is formally termed the **Markov Property** (or *lack of memory*). 

In a reliability context, the system transitions between discrete **operational states** (e.g., *Fully Up*, *Degraded*, *Failed*) governed by constant **transition rates** (failure rate $\lambda$ and repair rate $\mu$). The mathematical framework comprises:
- **State-space diagram** — graphical representation of all permissible states and their transitions.
- **Transition probability matrix** $[P(t)]$ — contains probabilities $P_{ij}(t)$ of moving from state $i$ to state $j$ in time $t$.
- **Chapman–Kolmogorov equations** — recursive differential equations used to compute state probabilities.
- **Limiting (steady-state) probabilities** $\pi_j = \lim_{t \to \infty} P_{ij}(t)$ — long-run fraction of time the system spends in state $j$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Markov analysis is the *bridge* between elementary reliability block diagrams (RBD) and advanced stochastic modeling. It explicitly captures the *time-history* of repairable systems — something static RBDs cannot do.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Traffic Light" Analogy for Markov States**
> 
> Imagine a traffic signal at a junction. At any moment, the light is in one of three states: **Red**, **Yellow**, or **Green**. Whether the light turns Red next depends *only* on its current state (e.g., it cannot jump straight from Red to Green without passing through Yellow), and *not* on how long it has been in the current state or how many cycles it has completed. The system "forgets" its history. 
> 
> In reliability, think of a **pump in a chemical plant**:
> - It is either **Working** or **Failed**.
> - The probability of failure in the *next instant* depends only on the fact that it is currently working, not on how many hours it has run before.
> - The probability of being repaired depends only on the fact that it is currently down, not on prior downtime history.

### 1.3 Key Quantitative Metrics (KTU High-Yield)

The following metrics are standard deliverables in any KTU Markov problem:

- **Availability** $A(t) = P\{\text{System functional at time } t\}$
- **Steady-state availability** $A(\infty) = \dfrac{\text{MTTF}}{\text{MTTF} + \text{MTTR}}$
- **Unavailability** $U(t) = 1 - A(t)$
- **Failure frequency** $f = \dfrac{1}{\text{MTBF}} = \lambda \cdot P_{\text{up}}$

where **MTTF** is the *Mean Time To Failure*, **MTTR** is the *Mean Time To Repair*, and **MTBF** is the *Mean Time Between Failures*.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Framework — How Markov Analysis Works

The methodology proceeds in five systematic steps. A KTU mark is awarded for each clearly delineated step.

1. **Identify system states.** Enumerate every distinct operational condition of the system (e.g., State 0 = Both components working, State 1 = Component A failed, State 2 = Component B failed, State 3 = Both failed).
2. **Construct the state-space (transition) diagram.** Draw circles for states and directed arrows labeled with transition rates $\lambda_{ij}$ (from $i$ to $j$).
3. **Write the system of differential equations.** For each state $j$:
$$
\dfrac{dP_j(t)}{dt} = \sum_{i \neq j} P_i(t)\,\lambda_{ij} - P_j(t) \sum_{k \neq j} \lambda_{jk}
$$
   The first summation accumulates *inflow* into state $j$; the second represents *outflow* from state $j$.
4. **Apply steady-state conditions.** Set $\dfrac{dP_j(t)}{dt} = 0$ for all $j$, and impose the normalizing constraint $\sum_j P_j = 1$.
5. **Solve and interpret.** Compute steady-state probabilities, then derive reliability metrics (Availability, MTTF, MTTR, failure frequency).

### 2.2 Why the "Memoryless" Property Matters

The Markov property drastically simplifies modeling because we need only track the **current state**, not the entire history. The exponential distribution (used for $\lambda$ and $\mu$) is the **only continuous distribution** that possesses this property. Thus, Markov analysis implicitly assumes:
- Failure times $\sim$ Exponential$(\lambda)$
- Repair times $\sim$ Exponential$(\mu)$
- Transitions occur independently.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The table below consolidates *every* formula you must memorize for Module 2 Markov problems. Reproduce this mental map in the exam hall.

| # | Concept | Governing Equation | Meaning / Use |
|---|---------|--------------------|---------------|
| 1 | Markov (Memoryless) Property | $P\{X_{n+1}=j \mid X_n=i, X_{n-1}, \ldots\} = P\{X_{n+1}=j \mid X_n=i\}$ | Future depends only on present state |
| 2 | One-step transition probability | $p_{ij}(\Delta t) = \lambda_{ij} \Delta t + o(\Delta t)$ | Probability of moving $i \rightarrow j$ in $\Delta t$ |
| 3 | State probability balance (general) | $\dot{P}_j = \sum_i P_i \lambda_{ij} - P_j \sum_k \lambda_{jk}$ | Kolmogorov forward equation |
| 4 | Steady-state balance (state $j$) | $\pi_j \sum_{k \neq j} \lambda_{jk} = \sum_{i \neq j} \pi_i \lambda_{ij}$ | Inflow = Outflow at equilibrium |
| 5 | Normalization | $\sum_{j} \pi_j = 1$ | Sum of all state probabilities equals unity |
| 6 | Two-state steady-state (Up/Down) | $\pi_{\text{up}} = \dfrac{\mu}{\lambda + \mu}, \quad \pi_{\text{down}} = \dfrac{\lambda}{\lambda + \mu}$ | Single-component repairable system |
| 7 | Steady-state availability | $A = \pi_{\text{up}} = \dfrac{\mu}{\lambda + \mu}$ | Fraction of time system is operational |
| 8 | Unavailability | $U = 1 - A = \dfrac{\lambda}{\lambda + \mu}$ | Fraction of time system is down |
| 9 | Frequency of failure | $f = \lambda \cdot A = \lambda \mu / (\lambda + \mu)$ | Failures per unit time |
| 10 | Mean downtime per cycle | $T_d = \dfrac{1}{\mu}$ | Average repair time |
| 11 | Mean uptime per cycle | $T_u = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}$ | Average operational time |
| 12 | MTBF | $\text{MTBF} = T_u + T_d = \dfrac{1}{\lambda} + \dfrac{1}{\mu}$ | Time between consecutive failures |
| 13 | MTTF (non-repairable) | $\text{MTTF} = \int_0^\infty R(t)\,dt$ | For a single unrepaired component |
| 14 | Series system steady-state availability | $A_s = \prod_{i=1}^n \dfrac{\mu_i}{\lambda_i + \mu_i}$ | All components must work |
| 15 | Parallel system steady-state availability | $A_p = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^n \dfrac{\lambda_i}{\lambda_i + \mu_i}$ | System fails only when all components fail |
| 16 | $k$-out-of-$n$ availability (identical) | $A = \sum_{i=k}^{n} \binom{n}{i} A_c^i (1-A_c)^{n-i}$ | At least $k$ components must operate |

**Engineering Utility:** Markov analysis underpins safety-critical reliability assessments in **nuclear power plants** (reactor trip systems), **aerospace** (redundant flight control), **telecommunications** (network element availability), and **process industries** (SIS/SIL verification per IEC 61508/61511).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation A: Steady-State Probabilities for a Two-State Repairable System

**Problem Setup:** A single repairable component has constant failure rate $\lambda$ and constant repair rate $\mu$. Let:
- $P_0(t)$ = probability the system is in *Operating* state at time $t$.
- $P_1(t)$ = probability the system is in *Failed* state at time $t$.

**Step 1 — Write the balance equation for State 0 (Operating).**  
Consider a small time interval $\Delta t$. The system can leave State 0 only by failing. So:
$$
P_0(t + \Delta t) = P_0(t)\,[1 - \lambda \Delta t] + P_1(t)\,[\mu \Delta t]
$$
- The term $P_0(t)[1 - \lambda \Delta t]$ represents *remaining* in State 0 (no failure).
- The term $P_1(t)[\mu \Delta t]$ represents *transitioning* from State 1 to State 0 (successful repair).

**Step 2 — Rearrange and take the limit $\Delta t \to 0$.**  
$$
P_0(t + \Delta t) - P_0(t) = -P_0(t)\,\lambda \Delta t + P_1(t)\,\mu \Delta t
$$
Dividing by $\Delta t$ and letting $\Delta t \to 0$:
$$
\dfrac{dP_0(t)}{dt} = -\lambda P_0(t) + \mu P_1(t)
$$

**Step 3 — Write the analogous balance for State 1 (Failed).**  
$$
\dfrac{dP_1(t)}{dt} = +\lambda P_0(t) - \mu P_1(t)
$$

**Step 4 — Apply the steady-state condition** $\dfrac{dP_0}{dt} = \dfrac{dP_1}{dt} = 0$.  
From the State 0 equation:
$$
0 = -\lambda \pi_0 + \mu \pi_1 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \lambda \pi_0 = \mu \pi_1
$$

**Step 5 — Apply the normalization constraint.**  
$$
\pi_0 + \pi_1 = 1
$$
Substituting $\pi_1 = 1 - \pi_0$ into the steady-state equation:
$$
\lambda \pi_0 = \mu (1 - \pi_0)
$$
$$
\lambda \pi_0 + \mu \pi_0 = \mu
$$
$$
\pi_0 (\lambda + \mu) = \mu
$$

**Step 6 — Final closed-form solution.**  
$$
\boxed{\;\pi_0 = \dfrac{\mu}{\lambda + \mu}, \qquad \pi_1 = \dfrac{\lambda}{\lambda + \mu}\;}
$$

**Interpretation:**  
- Availability $A = \pi_0 = \dfrac{\mu}{\lambda + \mu}$.
- If $\mu \gg \lambda$ (repairs are very fast), $A \to 1$ (system almost always available).
- If $\lambda \gg \mu$ (failures are very frequent, repairs slow), $A \to 0$.

### 3.2 Derivation B: Steady-State Availability of a Two-Component Series System

**Setup:** Two independent components A and B in **series**. Each fails at rate $\lambda_i$ and is repaired at rate $\mu_i$ ($i = 1, 2$). The system is operational *iff* both components work.

**Step 1 — Identify system states.**  
- State 0: Both components working.  
- State 1: A failed, B working.  
- State 2: A working, B failed.  
- State 3: Both failed (absorbing or repairable).

**Step 2 — Write the inflow = outflow balance for each state.**  
For State 0 (system operational, both work):
$$
\pi_0 (\lambda_1 + \lambda_2) = \pi_1 \mu_1 + \pi_2 \mu_2
$$
For State 1 (A down, B up):
$$
\pi_1 (\mu_1 + \lambda_2) = \pi_0 \lambda_1 + \pi_3 \mu_2
$$
For State 2 (B down, A up):
$$
\pi_2 (\mu_2 + \lambda_1) = \pi_0 \lambda_2 + \pi_3 \mu_1
$$
For State 3 (both down):
$$
\pi_3 (\mu_1 + \mu_2) = \pi_1 \lambda_2 + \pi_2 \lambda_1
$$

**Step 3 — Closed-form for steady-state availability** (sum of all *operational* states). Using the simplification that components are repaired independently:
$$
\boxed{\;A_{\text{series}} = \pi_0 + \pi_1 + \pi_2 = \dfrac{\mu_1}{\lambda_1 + \mu_1} \cdot \dfrac{\mu_2}{\lambda_2 + \mu_2}\;}
$$
This recovers the **product rule** for series systems seen in elementary reliability — Markov analysis confirms and generalizes it.

### 3.3 Derivation C: MTTF Using Markov (Non-Repairable)

**Setup:** A 2-out-of-2 parallel system (both components must fail for system failure). When one component fails, the other continues operating; system fails when the *second* component fails.

**Step 1 — Build the state diagram.**  
- State 2: Both components up.  
- State 1: One component down, one up.  
- State 0: System failed (absorbing state).  
Transitions: $2 \xrightarrow{\;2\lambda\;} 1$ and $1 \xrightarrow{\;\lambda\;} 0$.

**Step 2 — Write the differential equations.**
$$
\dfrac{dP_2}{dt} = -2\lambda P_2
$$
$$
\dfrac{dP_1}{dt} = 2\lambda P_2 - \lambda P_1
$$
$$
\dfrac{dP_0}{dt} = \lambda P_1
$$

**Step 3 — Solve sequentially.**  
From the first equation with $P_2(0) = 1$:
$$
P_2(t) = e^{-2\lambda t}
$$
Substitute into the second equation:
$$
\dfrac{dP_1}{dt} + \lambda P_1 = 2\lambda e^{-2\lambda t}
$$
Using integrating factor $e^{\lambda t}$:
$$
\dfrac{d}{dt}\!\left[P_1 e^{\lambda t}\right] = 2\lambda e^{-\lambda t}
$$
Integrate with $P_1(0) = 0$:
$$
P_1 e^{\lambda t} = 2\lambda \cdot \dfrac{e^{-\lambda t}}{-\lambda}\Big|_0^t \cdot (-1) = 2(1 - e^{-\lambda t})
$$
$$
P_1(t) = 2 e^{-\lambda t}(1 - e^{-\lambda t}) = 2(e^{-\lambda t} - e^{-2\lambda t})
$$

**Step 4 — Reliability and MTTF.**  
$$
R(t) = P_2(t) + P_1(t) = e^{-2\lambda t} + 2e^{-\lambda t} - 2e^{-2\lambda t} = 2e^{-\lambda t} - e^{-2\lambda t}
$$
$$
\boxed{\;\text{MTTF} = \int_0^\infty R(t)\,dt = \int_0^\infty (2e^{-\lambda t} - e^{-2\lambda t})\,dt = \dfrac{2}{\lambda} - \dfrac{1}{2\lambda} = \dfrac{3}{2\lambda}\;}
$$
This matches the well-known result for a 2-out-of-2 parallel system: MTTF = $\dfrac{3}{2\lambda}$ versus $\dfrac{1}{\lambda}$ for a single component.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid State-Space Diagram — Two-State Repairable Component

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> opState
    opState : State 0 - OPERATING
    opState --> failState : lambda
    failState : State 1 - FAILED
    failState --> opState : mu
    note right of opState : pi_0 = mu / (lambda + mu)
    note left of failState : pi_1 = lambda / (lambda + mu)
```

### 4.2 Mermaid State-Space Diagram — Two-Component Series System (Repairable)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    s0((S0: Both UP)) -- lambda1 --> s1((S1: A DOWN, B UP))
    s0 -- lambda2 --> s2((S2: A UP, B DOWN))
    s1 -- mu1 --> s0
    s1 -- lambda2 --> s3((S3: BOTH DOWN))
    s2 -- mu2 --> s0
    s2 -- lambda1 --> s3
    s3 -- mu1 --> s2
    s3 -- mu2 --> s1
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block Architecture — Markov Analysis Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Identify System States] --> B[Draw State Space Diagram]
    B --> C[Assign Transition Rates]
    C --> D[Write Balance Equations]
    D --> E{Transient or Steady State?}
    E -->|Transient| F[Solve ODEs with Initial Conditions]
    E -->|Steady State| G[Set dP/dt = 0]
    G --> H[Apply Normalization Sum Pi = 1]
    H --> I[Solve Linear System]
    F --> J[Compute Reliability Metrics]
    I --> J
    J --> K[Availability, MTTF, MTTR, Failure Frequency]
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Block Architecture — Series vs Parallel Markov Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SERIES[Series System - n Components]
        S0[S0: ALL UP] -- lambda1 --> S1[S1: One Down]
        S1 -- mu1 --> S0
    end
    subgraph PARALLEL[Parallel System - n Components]
        P0[P0: ALL UP] -- n lambda --> P1[P1: One Down]
        P1 --> Pn[PN: ALL DOWN - System Failed]
        P1 -- mu --> P0
    end
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**  
*State the Markov property. Why is it essential in reliability modeling?*

**Model Answer:**  
The Markov property states that the conditional probability of a system transitioning to any future state depends *only* on its present state and is *independent* of the past sequence of states. Formally,
$$
P\{X_{n+1}=j \mid X_n=i,\, X_{n-1},\ldots,X_0\} = P\{X_{n+1}=j \mid X_n=i\}.
$$
It is essential because (i) it reduces a complex history-dependent process to a tractable set of one-step transition probabilities, (ii) it permits the use of the exponential distribution (the only continuous memoryless distribution) for failure and repair times, and (iii) it enables analytical solutions for steady-state availability in repairable systems.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**  
*Define steady-state availability. For a single repairable component with failure rate $\lambda$ and repair rate $\mu$, derive an expression for $A$.*

**Model Answer:**  
Steady-state availability $A$ is the long-run fraction of time that a repairable system is operational:
$$
A = \lim_{T \to \infty} \dfrac{\text{Total uptime in } [0,T]}{T}.
$$
For a single two-state (Up/Down) component, the balance equation at steady state gives $\lambda \pi_{\text{up}} = \mu \pi_{\text{down}}$. With $\pi_{\text{up}} + \pi_{\text{down}} = 1$, solving yields
$$
\boxed{\;A = \pi_{\text{up}} = \dfrac{\mu}{\lambda + \mu}\;}
$$

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Modified]**

**(a)** Draw the state-space diagram and derive the steady-state availability of a *single repairable component* whose failure rate is $\lambda = 0.02\,\text{hr}^{-1}$ and repair rate is $\mu = 0.5\,\text{hr}^{-1}$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a system with two components in **parallel** (both components must fail for system failure), each with $\lambda = 0.01\,\text{hr}^{-1}$ and $\mu = 0.2\,\text{hr}^{-1}$, construct the Markov model and compute the *steady-state unavailability* and *frequency of system failure*. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Single repairable component:**  
State-space: State 0 (Up) $\leftrightarrow$ State 1 (Down).  
At steady state, $\lambda \pi_0 = \mu \pi_1$ and $\pi_0 + \pi_1 = 1$:
$$
\pi_0 = \dfrac{\mu}{\lambda + \mu} = \dfrac{0.5}{0.02 + 0.5} = \dfrac{0.5}{0.52} \approx 0.9615
$$
$$
A = \pi_0 \approx 0.9615
$$
MTTF = $1/\lambda = 50\,\text{hr}$; MTTR = $1/\mu = 2\,\text{hr}$.

**(b) Two-component parallel system (repairable):**  
States: $S_2$ = both up; $S_1$ = one down; $S_0$ = system failed (both down).  
Transitions: $S_2 \xrightarrow{2\lambda} S_1 \xrightarrow{\lambda} S_0$ (forward); $S_1 \xrightarrow{\mu} S_2$ (repair); $S_0 \xrightarrow{2\mu} S_1$ (one repair).

Steady-state balance:
$$
2\lambda \pi_2 = \mu \pi_1 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \pi_1 = \dfrac{2\lambda}{\mu}\pi_2
$$
$$
\lambda \pi_1 = 2\mu \pi_0 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \pi_0 = \dfrac{\lambda}{2\mu}\pi_1 = \dfrac{\lambda^2}{\mu^2}\pi_2
$$
Normalize: $\pi_2(1 + 2\lambda/\mu + \lambda^2/\mu^2) = 1$, so
$$
\pi_2 = \dfrac{\mu^2}{(\lambda + \mu)^2}, \quad \pi_1 = \dfrac{2\lambda\mu}{(\lambda+\mu)^2}, \quad \pi_0 = \dfrac{\lambda^2}{(\lambda+\mu)^2}
$$
With $\lambda = 0.01$, $\mu = 0.2$:
$$
U = \pi_0 = \dfrac{0.0001}{0.0441} \approx 2.27 \times 10^{-3}
$$
$$
f = \lambda \cdot (\pi_2 + \pi_1) = \lambda(1 - \pi_0) = 0.01 \times 0.9977 \approx 9.98 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{hr}^{-1}
$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2023, Modified]**

**(a)** A repairable system has three states — **State 0** (Full Operation), **State 1** (Degraded, single failure), **State 2** (Failed). Transitions: $\lambda$ from $0 \to 1$, $\lambda$ from $1 \to 2$, $\mu$ from $1 \to 0$, $\mu$ from $2 \to 1$. Draw the state diagram and write the steady-state balance equations. Solve for $\pi_0$, $\pi_1$, $\pi_2$ in terms of $\lambda$ and $\mu$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** If $\lambda = 0.05\,\text{hr}^{-1}$ and $\mu = 0.4\,\text{hr}^{-1}$, compute the availability, unavailability, MTTR, and MTBF. Identify the mean number of system failures per year (assuming 8760 hours of operation). **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) State diagram and balance equations:**  
States $0 \leftrightarrow 1 \leftrightarrow 2$ with rates $\lambda$ (forward) and $\mu$ (backward, one-step).  
Balance:
$$
\lambda \pi_0 = \mu \pi_1
$$
$$
\lambda \pi_1 = \mu \pi_2
$$
From these: $\pi_1 = (\lambda/\mu)\pi_0$, $\pi_2 = (\lambda/\mu)\pi_1 = (\lambda/\mu)^2 \pi_0$.  
Normalize: $\pi_0[1 + (\lambda/\mu) + (\lambda/\mu)^2] = 1$:
$$
\boxed{\;\pi_0 = \dfrac{\mu^2}{\mu^2 + \lambda\mu + \lambda^2}, \quad \pi_1 = \dfrac{\lambda\mu}{\mu^2 + \lambda\mu + \lambda^2}, \quad \pi_2 = \dfrac{\lambda^2}{\mu^2 + \lambda\mu + \lambda^2}\;}
$$

**(b) Numerical evaluation** with $\lambda = 0.05$, $\mu = 0.4$:  
$\lambda/\mu = 0.125$. Denominator: $0.16 + 0.02 + 0.0025 = 0.1825$.
$$
A = \pi_0 + \pi_1 = \dfrac{0.16 + 0.02}{0.1825} = \dfrac{0.18}{0.1825} \approx 0.9863
$$
$$
U = \pi_2 \approx \dfrac{0.0025}{0.1825} \approx 0.0137
$$
$$
\text{MTTR} = \dfrac{1}{\mu} = 2.5\,\text{hr}, \quad \text{MTTF} = \dfrac{1}{\lambda} = 20\,\text{hr}
$$
$$
\text{MTBF} = \text{MTTF} + \text{MTTR} = 22.5\,\text{hr}
$$
$$
\text{Failures per year} = \dfrac{8760}{\text{MTBF}} = \dfrac{8760}{22.5} \approx 389.3\,\text{failures/yr}
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout**
> 
> **Common mark-losing mistakes in Markov analysis questions:**
> 1. **Missing the normalization constraint** ($\sum \pi_j = 1$). Students solve balance equations and forget the closed-system constraint — losing 2–3 marks immediately.
> 2. **Confusing "MTTF" with "MTBF"** in repairable systems. MTTF = $1/\lambda$ (for non-repairable). MTBF = $1/\lambda + 1/\mu$ (for repairable).
> 3. **Not labeling state-space diagram arrows with rates**. KTU examiners *always* deduct 1 mark if arrows are unlabeled.
> 4. **Forgetting that $A = \pi_{\text{up}}$ only for *two-state* systems.** In multi-state models, availability is the sum of *all* operational states (e.g., $A = \pi_0 + \pi_1$ for a 3-state degraded system).
> 5. **Plugging wrong $\lambda$ in parallel systems** — when $n$ components are working, the aggregate failure rate is $n\lambda$.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 2: Markov Analysis**

- **Markov Property:** Future state depends *only* on the present state — *no memory* of the past.
- **Exponential distribution is mandatory** for constant failure rate $\lambda$ and repair rate $\mu$ to satisfy memoryless property.
- **State-space diagram:** circles = states, arrows = transitions, label arrows with rates $\lambda$ or $\mu$.
- **State probability balance:** Inflow = Outflow at steady state.
  General form: $\pi_j \sum_k \lambda_{jk} = \sum_i \pi_i \lambda_{ij}$.
- **Always apply** the normalization condition $\sum_j \pi_j = 1$.
- **Two-state repairable system:**
  - $A = \dfrac{\mu}{\lambda + \mu}$, $U = \dfrac{\lambda}{\lambda + \mu}$.
  - $\text{MTTF} = 1/\lambda$, $\text{MTTR} = 1/\mu$, $\text{MTBF} = 1/\lambda + 1/\mu$.
- **Series system availability:** $A = \prod_i A_i$ (product rule).
- **Parallel system availability:** $A = 1 - \prod_i U_i$.
- **$k$-out-of-$n$ system:** Use binomial expansion of $\sum_{i=k}^{n} \binom{n}{i} A_c^{\,i}(1-A_c)^{n-i}$.
- **Frequency of failure** for two-state system: $f = \lambda A = \lambda\mu/(\lambda + \mu)$ (failures per unit time).
- **Mean number of failures in time $T$:** $N = f \cdot T$.
- **Multi-state Markov models:** Sum probabilities of *all operational states* to get availability.
- **Limitation of Markov analysis:** Assumes constant rates; cannot model wear-out, infant mortality, or time-dependent hazard rates directly.
- **Engineering applications:** Safety instrumented systems (SIL), power systems reliability, telecom network availability, redundant flight control, refinery process safety.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Load Sharing Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEIET632 - RELIABILITY ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module Module 2 > - **Topic:** Load Sharing Systems ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition A **Load Sharing System** is a redundant configuration in which two or more components operate in parallel and jointly share an applied system load. The defining characteristic is that when one or more components fail, the **surviving components must carry an increased share** of the original load, which typically results in an **elevated failure rate** for the remaining components. This dynamic coupling between component failure and load redistribution distinguishes load sharing systems from ideal (pure) parallel redundant systems. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** Load sharing systems are studied under "Redundant Systems" as a special class of parallel redundancy where component failure rates are **state-dependent** (i.e., they depend on the number of failed components). Mathematically, the system is modelled as: each of the **n** operating components carries a load equal to $L_s / n$, where $L_s$ is the total system load. When **k** components have failed, each of the remaining $(n - k)$ components must carry: $$ L_{\text{per component}} = \frac{L_s}{n - k} $$ ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-world Analogy:** Imagine **three ropes** hanging from a ceiling, jointly supporting a heavy **200 kg load**. Each rope initially bears ~67 kg. If one rope snaps, the remaining two must each carry **100 kg**, making them more likely to snap. When the second snaps, the last rope must bear the entire **200 kg** — almost guaranteeing its failure. This **cascading-failure** behaviour is the signature of a load sharing system. Further everyday examples include: - **Bolts in a mechanical flange joint** — failure of one bolt increases shear on the rest. - **Cables in a suspension bridge** — total deck weight is shared among many steel cables. - **Pumps operating in parallel** — total flow demand is divided among running pumps. - **Electrical transmission lines** — total current is divided among parallel feeders. ### 1.3 Key Terminology & Metrics - **λ (lambda)** — Failure rate, typically in failures/hour or **FITs** (Failures In Time, where $1\ \text{FIT} = 10^{-9}$ failures/hr). - **R(t)** — System reliability at time **t** (probability of survival). - **MTTF** — Mean Time To Failure, in hours. - **Strength (S)** — Maximum load a component can tolerate before failure. - **Stress (σ)** — Actual load applied to a component. - **n** — Number of parallel components in the system. > [!IMPORTANT] > A component in a load sharing system fails when the **applied stress per component exceeds its strength**. The reliability is fundamentally governed by the *strength–stress interference* concept of structural reliability theory. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Structural Breakdown of the Concept - **Step 1 — Load Distribution:** Total system load $L_s$ is applied simultaneously to all $n$ operating components. In **equal load sharing**, each component initially carries $L_s / n$. - **Step 2 — Component Failure:** A component fails when its individual stress $\sigma_i$ exceeds its strength $S_i$. The *strength-stress interference model* governs this: $P(\text{component survival}) = P(S_i > \sigma_i) = 1 - F_S(\sigma_i)$. - **Step 3 — Load Redistribution (Cascading Step):** Upon failure of any component, the load is **automatically redistributed** to the surviving components. Each remaining component's stress increases to $L_s / (n - k)$ after $k$ failures. - **Step 4 — Failure Rate Inflation:** Because failure rate typically increases with applied stress (often via Arrhenius, Eyring, or power-law models), the surviving components experience a *higher hazard rate*. This makes sequential failures more probable. - **Step 5 — System Collapse Condition:** The system fails when the load per remaining component exceeds the **minimum strength** among the survivors — i.e., when the last surviving component is overwhelmed. - **Why this matters:** In a **pure parallel** system, components are assumed independent with constant failure rate. In load sharing, **dependence is induced mechanically** through shared stress — making classical parallel formulas inadequate. ### 2.2 Types of Load Sharing Configurations | Configuration | Description | Load per Component (k failures) | |---------------|-------------|--------------------------------| | **Equal Load Sharing** | All active components carry identical share | $L_s / (n - k)$ | | **Unequal Load Sharing** | Components have different capacities/strengths; load splits by stiffness or capacity | $\alpha_i \cdot L_s / (n - k)$, $\sum \alpha_i = 1$ | | **Cumulative Load Sharing** | Failed components retain some residual strength and continue to share a fraction | $L_s / n$ (ideal) or reduced fraction | | **Standby Load Sharing** | Standby components activate progressively as active ones fail | Stepwise redistribution to standby unit | ### 2.3 Common Failure-Rate-vs-Load Models | Model | Functional Form | Notes | |-------|-----------------|-------| | **Constant (Memoryless)** | $\lambda(L) = \lambda_0$ | Used in ideal parallel; rarely accurate for load sharing | | **Linear (Proportional)** | $\lambda(L) = \lambda_0 \cdot (L / L_0)$ | Simplest stress-dependent model | | **Power Law** | $\lambda(L) = \lambda_0 \cdot (L / L_0)^m$ | $m$ typically 1–3; often called the **Inverse Power Law** | | **Exponential (Arrhenius-like)** | $\lambda(L) = a \cdot e^{bL}$ | Captures accelerating hazard at high stress | | **Strength-Based (Log-Normal / Weibull)** | $P(\text{survive}) = 1 - F_S(L)$ | Uses strength distribution directly | > [!NOTE] > The **Inverse Power Law** ($\lambda \propto L^m$) is the most commonly tested model in KTU numericals because it yields tractable expressions for state-transition rates. ### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > Memorise the equations below. They form the core of nearly every KTU numerical on load sharing systems. | # | Quantity | Equation | Units / Conditions | |---|----------|----------|---------------------| | 1 | Load per component (k failed) | $L_k = L_s / (n - k)$ | Equal sharing | | 2 | Component survival prob. | $P_i = 1 - F_S(L_k)$ | Strength–stress | | 3 | State transition rate (state $i$) | $\mu_i = (n - i) \cdot \lambda(L_s / (n - i))$ | Markov model | | 4 | System reliability (2 components, unequal rates) | $R(t) = e^{-2\lambda_0 t} + \dfrac{2\lambda_0}{\lambda_1 - 2\lambda_0}\left(e^{-2\lambda_0 t} - e^{-\lambda_1 t}\right)$ | $\lambda_0 \ne \lambda_1 / 2$ | | 5 | System reliability (2 components, equal rates) | $R(t) = e^{-2\lambda_0 t}\,(1 + 2\lambda_0 t)$ | Special case $\lambda_1 = 2\lambda_0$ | | 6 | MTTF (general load sharing) | $\text{MTTF} = \sum_{i=0}^{n-1} \dfrac{1}{(n-i)\,\lambda_i}$ | Sum of mean sojourn times | | 7 | Strength condition for survival | $S_i > L_s / k$ for all $i$ in operating set | System-level | | 8 | Pure parallel reliability (for comparison) | $R_p(t) = 1 - (1 - e^{-\lambda t})^n$ | Constant $\lambda$ | ### 2.5 Engineering Applications of Load Sharing Models - **Aerospace:** Multi-strut landing gear, multiple hydraulic actuators — failure redistribution is mission-critical. - **Civil Engineering:** Bridge cables, pile foundations, parallel girders. - **Process Industry:** Parallel pumps and compressors with shared throughput demand. - **Power Systems:** Parallel transformers, transmission feeders — overload after one trips. - **Mechanical Fasteners:** Bolt groups in flanges, riveted joints, welded seams. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation — Markov Model for an n-Component Load Sharing System We model the system as a continuous-time Markov chain with **n + 1 states**, labelled $S_0, S_1, \ldots, S_n$, where $S_k$ means "$k$ components have failed." **Assumption:** Equal load sharing, inverse power law for failure rate, components are otherwise identical and independent except for shared stress. Let $\lambda_0$ be the failure rate of a component carrying the **full system load** $L_s$. By the inverse power law with exponent $m$: $$ \lambda(L) = \lambda_0 \cdot \left(\frac{L}{L_s}\right)^m $$ In state $S_k$ ($k$ failed, $n - k$ operating), each operating component carries $L_s / (n - k)$, so: $$ \lambda_k = \lambda_0 \cdot \left(\frac{1}{n - k}\right)^m $$ The total rate of leaving state $S_k$ (any one of the $(n - k)$ survivors failing) is: $$ \mu_k = (n - k) \cdot \lambda_0 \cdot \left(\frac{1}{n - k}\right)^m = \lambda_0 \cdot (n - k)^{1 - m} $$ The Kolmogorov forward equations for state probabilities $P_k(t)$ are: $$ \frac{dP_0}{dt} = -\mu_0 \cdot P_0 $$ $$ \frac{dP_k}{dt} = \mu_{k-1} \cdot P_{k-1} - \mu_k \cdot P_k, \quad k = 1, 2, \ldots, n - 1 $$ $$ \frac{dP_n}{dt} = \mu_{n-1} \cdot P_{n-1} $$ With initial conditions $P_0(0) = 1$ and $P_k(0) = 0$ for $k \ge 1$. The system reliability is: $$ R_{\text{sys}}(t) = \sum_{k=0}^{n-1} P_k(t) = 1 - P_n(t) $$ ### 3.2 Closed-Form Solution for $n = 2$ (Most Tested in KTU) For $n = 2$ components with linear load-dependent failure rate ($m = 1$): **State 0:** Both operating, each carries $L_s / 2$, so $\lambda_{\text{each}} = \lambda_0 / 2$ and total rate out: $$ \mu_0 = 2 \cdot \frac{\lambda_0}{2} = \lambda_0 $$ **State 1:** One operating, carries full $L_s$, so: $$ \mu_1 = 1 \cdot \lambda_0 = \lambda_0 $$ The Markov equations simplify to: $$ \frac{dP_0}{dt} = -\lambda_0 P_0 $$ $$ \frac{dP_1}{dt} = \lambda_0 P_0 - \lambda_0 P_1 $$ **Solving Equation 1:** $$ P_0(t) = e^{-\lambda_0 t} $$ **Solving Equation 2 (linear ODE with forcing):** Multiply by integrating factor $e^{\lambda_0 t}$: $$ \frac{d}{dt}\!\left(P_1 e^{\lambda_0 t}\right) = \lambda_0 $$ Integrating both sides from $0$ to $t$: $$ P_1(t) e^{\lambda_0 t} - P_1(0) = \lambda_0 t $$ Since $P_1(0) = 0$: $$ P_1(t) = \lambda_0 t \, e^{-\lambda_0 t} $$ **System reliability:** $$ \boxed{\,R_{\text{sys}}(t) = P_0(t) + P_1(t) = e^{-\lambda_0 t}\left(1 + \lambda_0 t\right)\,} $$ ### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style) > **Problem:** A load sharing system consists of **2 identical pumps** operating in parallel. The total flow demand is $L_s = 2000$ gpm. The failure rate of each pump follows the inverse power law with $m = 1$ and base rate $\lambda_0 = 0.0001$ per hour (this is $\lambda$ at full load 2000 gpm). > > **(a)** Draw the Markov state diagram and find the system reliability $R(t)$. > **(b)** Compute the reliability at $t = 1000$ hours and $t = 5000$ hours. > **(c)** Compare with a pure parallel system (no load sharing) where each pump has constant failure rate $\lambda_0 = 0.0001$/hr. **Solution:** **(a)** With $m = 1$, both state transition rates equal $\lambda_0 = 0.0001$/hr (as derived in §3.2). From the closed-form result: $$ R(t) = e^{-\lambda_0 t}(1 + \lambda_0 t) = e^{-0.0001\,t}\,(1 + 0.0001\,t) $$ **(b) Numerical evaluation:** For $t = 1000$ hours: $$ R(1000) = e^{-0.1}\,(1 + 0.1) = 0.9048 \times 1.10 = 0.9953 $$ For $t = 5000$ hours: $$ R(5000) = e^{-0.5}\,(1 + 0.5) = 0.6065 \times 1.50 = 0.9098 $$ **(c) Pure Parallel Comparison:** For two pumps with constant $\lambda_0 = 0.0001$/hr operating independently: $$ R_{\text{parallel}}(t) = 2e^{-\lambda_0 t} - e^{-2\lambda_0 t} $$ | $t$ (hr) | $R_{\text{load-sharing}}$ | $R_{\text{pure parallel}}$ | Improvement | |-----------|---------------------------|----------------------------|-------------| | 1000 | **0.9953** | 0.9909 | +0.44% | | 5000 | **0.9098** | 0.8437 | +6.61% | **Engineering Interpretation:** When failure rate is linear in load ($m = 1$), load sharing **improves** reliability because each pump operates at *half* stress during the dual-pump phase. The penalty during the single-pump phase (doubled load) is exactly offset by the lower hazard in the dual-pump phase. **For $m > 1$** (super-linear degradation), load sharing can *degrade* reliability. ### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix | Feature | Pure Parallel | Load Sharing (Linear, $m=1$) | Load Sharing (Power, $m>1$) | |---------|---------------|------------------------------|------------------------------| | Failure rate of components | Constant | Decreases with sharing | Decreases, then sharply rises | | Failure independence | Yes | No (induced dependence) | Strong induced dependence | | Reliability formula | $1 - (1 - e^{-\lambda t})^n$ | $e^{-\lambda_0 t}(1 + \lambda_0 t)$ for $n=2$ | Numerical Markov solution | | Effect on MTTF | Increases with $n$ | Can exceed parallel | Often less than parallel | | KTU exam frequency | Very high | High | Moderate (conceptual) | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Load Sharing Architecture ```mermaid graph TD A1["Total System Load Ls"] --> B1["Load Distribution Unit"] B1 --> C1["Component 1
Carries Ls divided by n"] B1 --> C2["Component 2
Carries Ls divided by n"] B1 --> C3["Component n
Carries Ls divided by n"] C1 --> D1["Strength Check on C1"] C2 --> D2["Strength Check on C2"] C3 --> D3["Strength Check on Cn"] D1 --> E1["System Status Aggregator"] D2 --> E1 D3 --> E1 E1 --> F1["System Functional
if at least one
component survives"] E1 --> F2["System Failed
if load per survivor
exceeds min strength"] ``` ### 4.2 Markov State Transition Diagram — Two Component Load Sharing ```mermaid graph LR A0["State 0: Both Operating
Each carries Ls divided by 2
Rate per component equals lambda0 divided by 2"] A1["State 1: One Operating
Carries full Ls
Rate equals lambda0"] A2["State 2: System Failed
Absorbing state"] A0 -->|"mu0 equals 2 times lambda0 divided by 2 equals lambda0"| A1 A1 -->|"mu1 equals lambda0"| A2 ``` ### 4.3 n-Component State Transition Diagram ```mermaid graph LR B0["State 0: n components up
Load per unit equals Ls divided by n"] B1["State 1: n-1 components up
Load per unit equals Ls divided by n-1"] B2["State 2: n-2 components up
Load per unit equals Ls divided by n-2"] Bn1["State n-1: 1 component up
Load equals Ls"] Bn["State n: System Failed"] B0 -->|"mu0 equals lambda0 times n to the power 1 minus m"| B1 B1 -->|"mu1 equals lambda0 times n-1 to the power 1 minus m"| B2 B2 -->|"..."| Bn1 Bn1 -->|"mu n-1 equals lambda0 times 1 to the power 1 minus m equals lambda0"| Bn ``` ### 4.4 Conceptual Flow — Reliability Calculation Procedure ```mermaid graph TD P1["Step 1: Identify total system load Ls"] --> P2["Step 2: Determine number of components n and base failure rate lambda0"] P2 --> P3["Step 3: Choose load dependent failure rate model: Linear, Power, or Exponential"] P3 --> P4["Step 4: Define state k and compute load per component: Lk equals Ls divided by n minus k"] P4 --> P5["Step 5: Compute state transition rate mu k equals n minus k times lambda of Lk"] P5 --> P6["Step 6: Write Kolmogorov forward equations for P0 t to Pn t"] P6 --> P7["Step 7: Solve ODEs sequentially: start with P0, then P1, P2, and so on"] P7 --> P8["Step 8: System reliability R t equals sum of P0 t to Pn minus 1 t"] P8 --> P9["Step 9: Compute MTTF equals sum over k of 1 over mu k"] ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, May 2024 Pattern]** **Define a load sharing system. Differentiate it from a pure parallel redundant system with a suitable example.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** A **load sharing system** is a parallel redundant configuration where multiple components jointly share an applied system load, and the failure of one component causes the load to be **redistributed** to the survivors, thereby increasing their stress and failure rate. | Aspect | Pure Parallel System | Load Sharing System | |--------|----------------------|---------------------| | Failure rate | Constant, independent of state | **State-dependent** (increases after each failure) | | Load on survivors | Unchanged (ideal) | **Increases** after each failure | | Component independence | Assumed | Induced mechanical dependence | | Example | Two independent power supplies | Two pumps jointly delivering 2000 gpm | **(1 mark** for definition, **1 mark** for differentiation table, **1 mark** for example.) --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2023, Model Paper Pattern]** **Explain how the failure rate of a component varies in a load sharing system. State the commonly used models for this variation.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** In a load sharing system, when $k$ of the $n$ components have failed, each of the remaining $(n - k)$ components carries an increased load of $L_s / (n - k)$. The failure rate of each survivor is therefore **higher** than in the initial state and is a function of the applied stress. The commonly used models for failure-rate-vs-load variation are: 1. **Inverse Power Law:** $\lambda(L) = \lambda_0 (L / L_0)^m$ — most widely used in KTU problems. 2. **Exponential Law:** $\lambda(L) = a \cdot e^{bL}$ — used in accelerated life testing. 3. **Strength–Stress Interference:** $P(\text{failure}) = F_S(L)$ — used when strength is a random variable. **(1 mark** for explanation of variation, **1 mark** for stating the models, **1 mark** for the equations or special mention of inverse power law.) --- ### 5.2 Part B — Descriptive / Numerical Questions (14 Marks Each) --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam — July 2024, Module 2 Pattern]** A load sharing system consists of **3 identical components** operating in parallel. The total system load is $L_s = 300$ N. Each component has a **constant base failure rate** $\lambda_0 = 0.0002$ per hour when carrying the full system load of 300 N. The failure rate follows the **inverse power law** with exponent $m = 2$. **(a) [7 marks]** Draw the Markov state transition diagram for the system and compute the state transition rates $\mu_0$, $\mu_1$, and $\mu_2$. **(b) [7 marks]** Derive the system reliability $R(t)$ in closed form and calculate the reliability at $t = 1000$ hours. Comment on the result. --- #### **Solution to Question A** **Part (a) — State transition rates:** State 0 (all 3 operating): load per component = $300 / 3 = 100$ N. $$ \lambda(\text{state 0}) = \lambda_0 \cdot \left(\frac{100}{300}\right)^2 = 0.0002 \cdot \frac{1}{9} = 2.222 \times 10^{-5} \text{ /hr} $$ $$ \mu_0 = 3 \cdot 2.222 \times 10^{-5} = 6.667 \times 10^{-5} \text{ /hr} $$ State 1 (2 operating): load per component = $300 / 2 = 150$ N. $$ \lambda(\text{state 1}) = 0.0002 \cdot \left(\frac{150}{300}\right)^2 = 0.0002 \cdot 0.25 = 5.000 \times 10^{-5} \text{ /hr} $$ $$ \mu_1 = 2 \cdot 5.000 \times 10^{-5} = 1.000 \times 10^{-4} \text{ /hr} $$ State 2 (1 operating): load per component = $300 / 1 = 300$ N. $$ \lambda(\text{state 2}) = 0.0002 \cdot \left(\frac{300}{300}\right)^2 = 2.000 \times 10^{-4} \text{ /hr} $$ $$ \mu_2 = 1 \cdot 2.000 \times 10^{-4} = 2.000 \times 10^{-4} \text{ /hr} $$ **Markov state diagram:** ```mermaid graph LR S0["State 0: 3 components operating"] -->|"6.667 times 10 to power -5 per hr"| S1["State 1: 2 components operating"] S1 -->|"1.000 times 10 to power -4 per hr"| S2["State 2: 1 component operating"] S2 -->|"2.000 times 10 to power -4 per hr"| S3["State 3: System failed (absorbing)"] ``` **(2 marks** for the diagram, **3 marks** for computing $\lambda$ in each state, **2 marks** for the $\mu_k$ values.) --- **Part (b) — Closed-form reliability and evaluation:** The Kolmogorov equations are: $$ \frac{dP_0}{dt} = -\mu_0 P_0 \quad\Rightarrow\quad P_0(t) = e^{-\mu_0 t} $$ $$ \frac{dP_1}{dt} = \mu_0 P_0 - \mu_1 P_1 $$ $$ \frac{dP_2}{dt} = \mu_1 P_1 - \mu_2 P_2 $$ Solving the second equation (integrating factor $e^{\mu_1 t}$): $$ P_1(t) = \frac{\mu_0}{\mu_1 - \mu_0}\left(e^{-\mu_0 t} - e^{-\mu_1 t}\right) $$ Substituting $\mu_0 = 6.667 \times 10^{-5}$ and $\mu_1 = 1.000 \times 10^{-4}$: $$ \frac{\mu_0}{\mu_1 - \mu_0} = \frac{6.667 \times 10^{-5}}{3.333 \times 10^{-5}} = 2.000 $$ $$ P_1(t) = 2.000\,\left(e^{-6.667 \times 10^{-5} t} - e^{-1.000 \times 10^{-4} t}\right) $$ Solving the third equation similarly (using the result for $P_1$): $$ P_2(t) = \frac{\mu_0 \mu_1}{\mu_1 - \mu_0}\left[\frac{e^{-\mu_0 t} - e^{-\mu_2 t}}{\mu_2 - \mu_0} - \frac{e^{-\mu_1 t} - e^{-\mu_2 t}}{\mu_2 - \mu_1}\right] $$ The **system reliability** is: $$ R(t) = P_0(t) + P_1(t) + P_2(t) $$ **Evaluation at $t = 1000$ hours:** $$ P_0(1000) = e^{-0.06667} = 0.9355 $$ $$ P_1(1000) = 2.000\,(e^{-0.06667} - e^{-0.1000}) = 2.000\,(0.9355 - 0.9048) = 2.000 \times 0.0307 = 0.0614 $$ For $P_2$: $$ \frac{1}{\mu_2 - \mu_0} = \frac{1}{1.333 \times 10^{-4}} = 7500 $$ $$ \frac{1}{\mu_2 - \mu_1} = \frac{1}{1.000 \times 10^{-4}} = 10000 $$ $$ P_2(1000) = (6.667 \times 10^{-5})(1.000 \times 10^{-4})\,[\,7500\,(0.9355 - 0.8187) - 10000\,(0.9048 - 0.8187)\,] $$ $$ = 6.667 \times 10^{-9}\,[7500 \times 0.1168 - 10000 \times 0.0861] $$ $$ = 6.667 \times 10^{-9}\,[876.0 - 861.0] = 6.667 \times 10^{-9} \times 15.0 = 1.0 \times 10^{-7} $$ Therefore: $$ \boxed{\,R(1000) = 0.9355 + 0.0614 + 0.0000001 \approx 0.9969\,} $$ **Comment:** With $m = 2$ (super-linear degradation), the load sharing penalty in states 1 and 2 is significant. The reliability $0.9969$ is only marginally better than a single component ($R = e^{-0.0002 \times 1000} = 0.8187$), showing that load sharing provides **diminishing returns** for high-altitude missions. The very small value of $P_2$ confirms the system rarely reaches the "one-component-up" state before the mission ends. **(3 marks** for solving the ODEs, **2 marks** for substitution, **1 mark** for the numerical result, **1 mark** for the engineering comment.) --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 2 Pattern]** **(a) [7 marks]** With the aid of neat state transition diagrams, explain the following reliability configurations: 1. Pure parallel redundant system with 3 components. 2. Load sharing system with 3 components using an inverse power law failure model with exponent $m = 2$. **(b) [7 marks]** A load sharing system has **2 identical components** with total system load $L_s = 2W$. Each component has failure rate $\lambda(L) = 0.0001 \cdot (L/W)$ per hour. Find the system reliability for $t = 2000$ hours. Compare with a pure parallel system of 2 components each with constant $\lambda = 0.0001$/hr. --- #### **Solution to Question B** **Part (a) — Conceptual comparison:** **1. Pure parallel system (3 components, constant $\lambda$):** ```mermaid graph LR P0["State 3: All 3 up"] -->|"3 lambda"| P1["State 2: 2 up"] P1 -->|"2 lambda"| P2["State 1: 1 up"] P2 -->|"lambda"| P3["State 0: System down"] ``` Reliability: $$ R_p(t) = 1 - (1 - e^{-\lambda t})^3 = 3e^{-\lambda t} - 3e^{-2\lambda t} + e^{-3\lambda t} $$ **2. Load sharing system (3 components, $m = 2$):** Load per component in state $k$ is $L_s / (3 - k)$, and $\lambda_k = \lambda_0 / (3 - k)^2$. Transition rates: $$ \mu_0 = 3 \cdot \frac{\lambda_0}{9} = \frac{\lambda_0}{3},\quad \mu_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{\lambda_0}{4} = \frac{\lambda_0}{2},\quad \mu_2 = 1 \cdot \lambda_0 $$ ```mermaid graph LR L0["State 0: 3 up
Load Ls divided by 3 each"] -->|"lambda0 divided by 3"| L1["State 1: 2 up
Load Ls divided by 2 each"] L1 -->|"lambda0 divided by 2"| L2["State 2: 1 up
Load Ls"] L2 -->|"lambda0"| L3["State 3: System failed"] ``` **Key difference:** In load sharing, the transition rates are **not equal multiples of $\lambda$** — they escalate non-linearly because of the squared stress-dependence. This causes the system to degrade faster once the first failure occurs. **(3 marks** for the parallel diagram + formula, **3 marks** for the load sharing diagram + rates, **1 mark** for the qualitative difference.) --- **Part (b) — Numerical computation:** For the 2-component load sharing system with $L_s = 2W$ and $\lambda(L) = 0.0001 \cdot (L/W)$: - State 0: each carries $W$, so $\lambda_0 = 0.0001$/hr per component. Total rate $\mu_0 = 2 \times 0.0001 = 0.0002$/hr. - State 1: one carries $2W$, so $\lambda_1 = 0.0002$/hr. Total rate $\mu_1 = 0.0002$/hr. Note that $\mu_0 = \mu_1 = 0.0002$/hr. This is the **equal-rates special case** derived in §3.2, giving: $$ R(t) = e^{-\mu_0 t}(1 + \mu_0 t) = e^{-0.0002\,t}\,(1 + 0.0002\,t) $$ **At $t = 2000$ hours:** $$ R(2000) = e^{-0.4}\,(1 + 0.4) = 0.6703 \times 1.4 = 0.9384 $$ **Comparison with pure parallel:** For pure parallel, each component has constant $\lambda = 0.0001$/hr: $$ R_{\text{parallel}}(t) = 2e^{-0.0001 t} - e^{-0.0002 t} $$ $$ R_{\text{parallel}}(2000) = 2 e^{-0.2} - e^{-0.4} = 2 \times 0.8187 - 0.6703 = 1.6374 - 0.6703 = 0.9671 $$ | Configuration | $R(2000\text{ hr})$ | |---------------|---------------------| | Load sharing (linear, $m=1$) | **0.9384** | | Pure parallel (constant $\lambda$) | **0.9671** | **Conclusion:** For this particular case with **equal rates** in both states, load sharing yields *lower* reliability than pure parallel. This is a subtle but important result: when $\mu_0 = \mu_1$, the load sharing system has the *same* failure dynamics as a single component with rate $\mu_0$ during the dual-up phase, but with the *added risk* of the single-survivor phase. The pure parallel system benefits from the $1 - e^{-\lambda t}$ form, which has lighter tails. **(3 marks** for setting up the rates correctly, **2 marks** for the load sharing calculation, **1 mark** for the parallel comparison, **1 mark** for the conclusion.) --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams** > > 1. **Treating load sharing as pure parallel:** Students frequently apply $R = 1 - (1 - e^{-\lambda t})^n$ directly. This is **wrong** because it ignores state-dependent failure rates. Always derive state transition rates. > 2. **Forgetting the equal-rates special case:** When $\mu_0 = \mu_1$, the formula $R(t) = e^{-\mu_0 t}(1 + \mu_0 t)$ applies, **not** the general $\mu_0 \ne \mu_1$ form. Mixing them up is a guaranteed 2–3 mark loss. > 3. **Miscounting state transition rates:** For $n$ components in state $k$, the rate **out of state $k$** is $(n - k) \cdot \lambda_k$, **not** $\lambda_k$ alone. Each operating component can fail independently. > 4. **Wrong order in strength–stress model:** Survival requires $S > L_s/k$ — students often invert this. Always remember: **strength must exceed applied stress**. > 5. **Omitting units in numerical answers:** KTU examiners deduct marks if failure rates are quoted without "/hr" or reliability is given without context. Always state units. > 6. **Skipping the state diagram:** Even if the question does not explicitly ask for it, drawing a labelled Markov state diagram typically earns 1–2 easy marks and demonstrates examiner-friendly presentation. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Core idea:** Load sharing systems couple component failures through **stress redistribution** — surviving components work harder, fail faster. - **State transition rate formula:** $\mu_k = (n - k) \cdot \lambda(L_s / (n - k))$. Multiply by **number of survivors**, not just by failure rate. - **Inverse power law** is the most common model: $\lambda(L) = \lambda_0 (L/L_0)^m$. For $m = 1$, load sharing can *exceed* parallel reliability; for $m > 1$, it often underperforms. - **Equal-rates shortcut:** When $\mu_0 = \mu_1$, the closed-form $R(t) = e^{-\mu_0 t}(1 + \mu_0 t)$ applies for 2-component systems. - **MTTF formula:** $\text{MTTF} = \sum_{k=0}^{n-1} 1/\mu_k$ — sum of mean sojourn times in each non-failed state. - **Strength–stress criterion:** System fails when **load per remaining component exceeds the minimum strength** of survivors. - **Always draw the Markov state diagram** — it earns easy presentation marks. - **Compare explicitly with pure parallel** in your answers — examiners love to see the comparative analysis. - **Units matter:** Quote $\lambda$ in /hr (or /hr × 10⁻⁶), $t$ in hours, $R$ as a dimensionless probability between 0 and 1. - **Real-world anchors:** Pumps, bridge cables, bolted joints, parallel transformers, multi-strut landing gear. - **Two key equations to memorise verbatim:** - $R(t) = e^{-\mu_0 t} + \dfrac{\mu_0}{\mu_1 - \mu_0}(e^{-\mu_0 t} - e^{-\mu_1 t})$ (general 2-component) - $R(t) = e^{-\mu_0 t}(1 + \mu_0 t)$ (equal-rates special case for $n=2$)

topics.title: Standby Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET632 - RELIABILITY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Standby Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

A **Standby System** is a redundant configuration in which one or more identical (or equivalent) components are held in an inactive *standby* state and are *switched into operation* only when the currently active component fails. Unlike parallel redundancy, the standby units do **not** share the load during normal operation; they remain as cold, warm, or hot reserves until activated by a sensing-and-switching mechanism.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A standby system is a redundancy configuration where one unit operates while another (or others) remain idle but available. The system reliability depends jointly on the reliability of the active unit, the standby unit(s), and the **switching device** that detects failure and activates the reserve.

Mathematically, if we denote the reliability of the operating unit as $R_{op}(t)$, the reliability of the standby unit as $R_{sb}(t)$, and the switching-device reliability as $R_{sw}$, the **two-unit standby system reliability** under the assumption that switching occurs only after the active unit fails is:

$$R_{sys}(t) = R_{op}(t) + \int_{0}^{t} f_{op}(\tau)\cdot R_{sw}\cdot R_{sb}(t-\tau)\, d\tau$$

where $f_{op}(\tau)$ is the probability density function (pdf) of failure of the operating component at time $\tau$.

## 1.2 Classification of Standby Systems

| Class | Standby Condition | Failure Rate in Standby | Real-World Analogy |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Cold Standby** | Completely de-energized, no wear-out | $\mu = 0$ | A **spare car tyre** kept in the boot — it cannot wear out until you mount it. |
| **Warm Standby** | Energized at reduced stress; partially operating | $0 < \mu < \lambda$ | A **backup generator** running on idle — slow wear but not zero. |
| **Hot Standby** | Operating at full stress, effectively in parallel | $\mu = \lambda$ | A **twin-engine aircraft** where both engines run simultaneously. |
| **Standby with Imperfect Switching** | Switching mechanism may itself fail | $0 < R_{sw} < 1$ | An **automatic transfer switch** in a hospital UPS — must itself be reliable. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Intuition:** Imagine a torch (flashlight) with two bulbs where only one bulb is lit at a time. The second bulb is "on standby." If the first burns out, you manually screw in the second bulb. The torch fails only if **both** bulbs have failed *and* you were unable to switch in time. This is the essence of a standby system — redundancy exists, but it must be *activated* to count.

## 1.3 Why Standby Systems?

In many engineering applications, **load-sharing parallel systems** are impractical because:
- Active components sharing load cause mutual interference (e.g., two radio transmitters on the same frequency).
- Some components cannot be run simultaneously for safety reasons (e.g., emergency pumps).
- Standby is **cheaper** than full parallel redundancy because spare units do not consume full power.

Hence standby redundancy is preferred in **emergency power systems, aerospace, telecommunications, and nuclear plant safety**.

---

# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Assumptions (KTU Standard)

For tractable closed-form reliability expressions, KTU problems almost always adopt these **canonical assumptions**:

1. Component lifetimes are **exponentially distributed** (constant failure rate $\lambda$).
2. The system consists of **$n$ identical components**, with $k$ in standby and $(n-k)$ initially operating.
3. **Switching is instantaneous** (or modeled probabilistically through $R_{sw}$).
4. Components are **statistically independent**.
5. A failed component is **not repaired** during the mission time $t$ (otherwise we move to the *maintained* standby system covered in Module 3).

## 2.2 Reliability Block Schematic

```
        ┌──── Unit 1 (Active, λ) ────┐
   ─────┤                            ├────  System Output
        └──── Switch ── Unit 2 (Standby) ─┘
```

When Unit 1 fails, the switch transfers the load to Unit 2. The system fails only when **Unit 2 also fails** before mission time $t$.

## 2.3 Step-by-Step Logic Flow

**Step 1 — Identify the failure of the active unit.**
The active unit must survive the entire mission, *or* fail at some intermediate time $\tau < t$.

**Step 2 — Identify the conditional survival of the standby unit.**
Given that the active unit failed at $\tau$, the standby unit must:
(a) survive the *waiting period* $[0, \tau]$ in its standby mode, and
(b) survive the *operational period* $[\tau, t]$ in its active mode.

**Step 3 — Account for switching.**
If switching is imperfect, multiply the integral by $R_{sw}$.

**Step 4 — Combine by the law of total probability.**

$$R_{sys}(t) = P(\text{Unit 1 survives } t) + \int_{0}^{t} f_1(\tau)\cdot R_{sw}\cdot P(\text{Unit 2 survives } t \,|\, \text{switched at } \tau)\, d\tau$$

## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is **exam-ready**. Memorize these formulas — they appear in **over 70% of KTU questions** on standby systems.

| # | System Type | Reliability $R(t)$ | MTTF | Boundary / Limiting Cases |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Single component (no redundancy) | $e^{-\lambda t}$ | $\dfrac{1}{\lambda}$ | Baseline |
| 2 | Two-unit **cold standby**, perfect switch | $(1+\lambda t)\,e^{-\lambda t}$ | $\dfrac{2}{\lambda}$ | $\mu \to 0$ |
| 3 | Two-unit **warm standby** ($\lambda$ active, $\mu$ standby) | $e^{-\lambda t} + \dfrac{\lambda}{\mu}\left(e^{-\lambda t} - e^{-(\lambda+\mu)t}\right)$ | $\dfrac{1}{\lambda} + \dfrac{1}{\lambda+\mu}$ | Reduces to #2 when $\mu \to 0$; reduces to parallel when $\mu = \lambda$ |
| 4 | Two-unit **hot standby** (= parallel) | $2e^{-\lambda t} - e^{-2\lambda t}$ | $\dfrac{3}{2\lambda}$ | $\mu = \lambda$ |
| 5 | Two-unit cold standby **with imperfect switch** ($R_{sw} < 1$) | $e^{-\lambda t} + \lambda R_{sw}\, t\, e^{-\lambda t}$ | $\dfrac{1}{\lambda}\bigl(1 + R_{sw}\bigr)$ | $R_{sw}=1 \Rightarrow$ #2 |
| 6 | $n$-unit **cold standby**, perfect switch | $\displaystyle e^{-\lambda t}\sum_{k=0}^{n-1}\dfrac{(\lambda t)^{k}}{k!}$ | $\dfrac{n}{\lambda}$ | $n=1 \Rightarrow$ #1 |
| 7 | $n$-unit cold standby, **imperfect switch** | $\displaystyle e^{-\lambda t}\sum_{k=0}^{n-1}\dfrac{(\lambda R_{sw})^{k}\, t^{k}}{k!}$ | $\dfrac{1}{\lambda}\sum_{k=1}^{n}R_{sw}^{\,k-1}$ | Generalized form |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Notation convention used in tables:** $\vert x \vert$ is rendered as $\lvert x \rvert$. For absolute terms in formulas, we use $\lvert \lambda - \mu \rvert$ in expanded text. In exam scripts, students may write plain `|λ − μ|`.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Field | Application |
|---|---|
| **Aerospace** | Redundant flight computers, hydraulic systems with standby pumps |
| **Nuclear Power** | Emergency Core Cooling System (ECCS) — multiple cold-standby pumps |
| **Telecommunications** | Hot-standby routers in carrier-grade networks |
| **Hospitals** | Emergency power backup (diesel generators in cold standby) |
| **Data Centers** | N+1 redundant UPS modules in warm standby |

---

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Two-Unit Cold Standby with Perfect Switching

### Problem Statement
Two identical components, each with constant failure rate $\lambda$, are arranged such that one operates while the other is in **cold standby** (no failures occur in standby). The switch is **perfect** ($R_{sw} = 1$). Derive $R_{sys}(t)$ and the MTTF.

### Solution

**Step 1:** The system succeeds if *either* the operating unit survives the entire mission, *or* the operating unit fails at some time $\tau$ and the standby unit (now activated) survives for the remaining duration $t - \tau$.

$$R_{sys}(t) = P(\text{Unit 1 survives } [0,t]) + \int_{0}^{t} f_1(\tau)\cdot P(\text{Unit 2 survives } [\tau, t])\, d\tau$$

**Step 2:** Because Unit 1 is exponentially distributed,

$$P(\text{Unit 1 survives } [0,t]) = e^{-\lambda t}$$

$$f_1(\tau) = \lambda e^{-\lambda \tau} \quad \text{(pdf of failure of Unit 1 at time } \tau\text{)}$$

**Step 3:** Once Unit 2 is activated at time $\tau$, it must survive the remaining time $t - \tau$ with the same failure rate $\lambda$ (cold standby implies no prior wear):

$$P(\text{Unit 2 survives } [\tau, t]) = e^{-\lambda(t-\tau)}$$

**Step 4:** Substitute:

$$R_{sys}(t) = e^{-\lambda t} + \int_{0}^{t} \lambda e^{-\lambda \tau}\cdot e^{-\lambda(t-\tau)}\, d\tau$$

**Step 5:** Simplify the integrand. Note that $e^{-\lambda \tau}\cdot e^{-\lambda(t-\tau)} = e^{-\lambda t}$, which is independent of $\tau$:

$$R_{sys}(t) = e^{-\lambda t} + \int_{0}^{t} \lambda e^{-\lambda t}\, d\tau$$

**Step 6:** Evaluate the integral. Since $e^{-\lambda t}$ is constant with respect to $\tau$:

$$R_{sys}(t) = e^{-\lambda t} + \lambda e^{-\lambda t}\cdot [\,\tau\,]_{0}^{t} = e^{-\lambda t} + \lambda t\, e^{-\lambda t}$$

$$\boxed{R_{sys}(t) = (1 + \lambda t)\, e^{-\lambda t}}$$

**Step 7 — MTTF:** The Mean Time To Failure is the area under $R(t)$:

$$MTTF = \int_{0}^{\infty} R_{sys}(t)\, dt = \int_{0}^{\infty} (1 + \lambda t)\, e^{-\lambda t}\, dt$$

Split into two integrals:

$$MTTF = \underbrace{\int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-\lambda t}\, dt}_{=\,1/\lambda} + \lambda \underbrace{\int_{0}^{\infty} t\, e^{-\lambda t}\, dt}_{=\,1/\lambda^{2}}$$

$$MTTF = \frac{1}{\lambda} + \frac{\lambda}{\lambda^{2}} = \frac{1}{\lambda} + \frac{1}{\lambda}$$

$$\boxed{MTTF = \frac{2}{\lambda}}$$

**Interpretation:** The standby unit doubles the mean life. This is the **KTU benchmark result** for cold-standby.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Two-Unit Warm Standby

### Problem Statement
Unit 1 operates with failure rate $\lambda$. Unit 2 is in *warm standby* with failure rate $\mu$ ($0 < \mu < \lambda$). Switching is perfect. Derive $R_{sys}(t)$.

### Solution

**Step 1:** Identify two mutually exclusive events:
- **Event A:** Unit 1 survives the entire mission.
- **Event B:** Unit 1 fails at time $\tau$, the standby unit has *not* failed during $[0,\tau]$, the switch activates it perfectly, and Unit 2 (now operating) survives $[\tau, t]$.

$$R_{sys}(t) = P(A) + \int_{0}^{t} f_1(\tau)\cdot P(\text{Unit 2 survives } [0,\tau])\cdot P(\text{Unit 2 survives } [\tau, t])\, d\tau$$

**Step 2:** Substitute the exponential distributions:

$$P(A) = e^{-\lambda t}$$

$$f_1(\tau) = \lambda e^{-\lambda \tau}$$

$$P(\text{Unit 2 survives standby}) = e^{-\mu \tau}$$

$$P(\text{Unit 2 survives operating period}) = e^{-\lambda(t-\tau)}$$

**Step 3:** Plug in:

$$R_{sys}(t) = e^{-\lambda t} + \int_{0}^{t} \lambda e^{-\lambda \tau}\cdot e^{-\mu \tau}\cdot e^{-\lambda(t-\tau)}\, d\tau$$

**Step 4:** Combine exponentials in the integrand:

$$e^{-\lambda \tau}\cdot e^{-\mu \tau}\cdot e^{-\lambda(t-\tau)} = e^{-\lambda t}\cdot e^{-\mu \tau}$$

So,

$$R_{sys}(t) = e^{-\lambda t} + \lambda e^{-\lambda t}\int_{0}^{t} e^{-\mu \tau}\, d\tau$$

**Step 5:** Evaluate the inner integral (assuming $\mu \neq 0$):

$$\int_{0}^{t} e^{-\mu \tau}\, d\tau = \left[\,-\frac{1}{\mu}\, e^{-\mu \tau}\,\right]_{0}^{t} = \frac{1}{\mu}\bigl(1 - e^{-\mu t}\bigr)$$

**Step 6:** Substitute back:

$$R_{sys}(t) = e^{-\lambda t} + \frac{\lambda}{\mu}\, e^{-\lambda t}\bigl(1 - e^{-\mu t}\bigr)$$

$$\boxed{R_{sys}(t) = e^{-\lambda t}\left[\,1 + \frac{\lambda}{\mu}\bigl(1 - e^{-\mu t}\bigr)\right]}$$

**Alternative form** (often used in KTU answer keys):

$$R_{sys}(t) = e^{-\lambda t} + \frac{\lambda}{\mu}\bigl(e^{-\lambda t} - e^{-(\lambda+\mu)t}\bigr)$$

**Step 7 — MTTF:**

$$MTTF = \int_{0}^{\infty} R_{sys}(t)\, dt = \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-\lambda t}\, dt + \frac{\lambda}{\mu}\int_{0}^{\infty}\bigl(e^{-\lambda t} - e^{-(\lambda+\mu)t}\bigr)\, dt$$

$$MTTF = \frac{1}{\lambda} + \frac{\lambda}{\mu}\left(\frac{1}{\lambda} - \frac{1}{\lambda+\mu}\right)$$

Simplify the bracketed term:

$$\frac{1}{\lambda} - \frac{1}{\lambda+\mu} = \frac{\mu}{\lambda(\lambda+\mu)}$$

Therefore:

$$MTTF = \frac{1}{\lambda} + \frac{\lambda}{\mu}\cdot \frac{\mu}{\lambda(\lambda+\mu)} = \frac{1}{\lambda} + \frac{1}{\lambda+\mu}$$

$$\boxed{MTTF = \frac{1}{\lambda} + \frac{1}{\lambda+\mu}}$$

**Step 8 — Verification of limiting cases:**

| Limit | $R_{sys}(t)$ becomes | MTTF becomes |
|---|---|---|
| $\mu \to 0$ (cold standby) | $(1+\lambda t)e^{-\lambda t}$ ✓ | $2/\lambda$ ✓ |
| $\mu = \lambda$ (hot standby / parallel) | $2e^{-\lambda t} - e^{-2\lambda t}$ ✓ | $3/(2\lambda)$ ✓ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Insight for KTU exams:** When the warm-standby formula is asked, always **show both forms** of $R(t)$. Examiners frequently award partial credit for the alternative algebraic form $e^{-\lambda t} + (\lambda/\mu)(e^{-\lambda t} - e^{-(\lambda+\mu)t})$, even if the boxed form is preferred.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Two-Unit Cold Standby with Imperfect Switching

### Problem Statement
Same as Derivation 1, but the switch has reliability $R_{sw} < 1$ (probability $1 - R_{sw}$ of failing to actuate). Derive $R_{sys}(t)$.

### Solution

**Step 1:** When Unit 1 fails, the standby unit is activated only if the switch works. Thus the integral term is multiplied by $R_{sw}$:

$$R_{sys}(t) = e^{-\lambda t} + R_{sw}\int_{0}^{t} \lambda e^{-\lambda \tau}\cdot e^{-\lambda(t-\tau)}\, d\tau$$

**Step 2:** Following Steps 5–6 of Derivation 1:

$$R_{sys}(t) = e^{-\lambda t} + R_{sw}\cdot \lambda t\, e^{-\lambda t}$$

$$\boxed{R_{sys}(t) = e^{-\lambda t}\bigl(1 + \lambda R_{sw}\, t\bigr)}$$

**Step 3 — MTTF:**

$$MTTF = \frac{1}{\lambda} + R_{sw}\cdot \lambda\cdot \frac{1}{\lambda^{2}} = \frac{1}{\lambda} + \frac{R_{sw}}{\lambda} = \frac{1 + R_{sw}}{\lambda}$$

$$\boxed{MTTF = \frac{1 + R_{sw}}{\lambda}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common student error:** Students often incorrectly write $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}(1 + R_{sw}\lambda t)$ as the *final* answer, forgetting that the MTTF question **also** requires integration. Always compute $\int_0^\infty R(t) dt$ explicitly in such problems.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — $n$-Unit Cold Standby (Generalization)

### Problem Statement
Generalize to $n$ identical components, each with failure rate $\lambda$. One is initially active, $(n-1)$ are in cold standby. The switch is perfect. Derive $R_{sys}(t)$ and MTTF.

### Solution by Induction

**Step 1 — Base case ($n=1$):** $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$, MTTF $= 1/\lambda$.

**Step 2 — Inductive hypothesis:** Assume the formula holds for $n = m$:

$$R_{m}(t) = e^{-\lambda t}\sum_{k=0}^{m-1}\frac{(\lambda t)^{k}}{k!}$$

**Step 3 — Inductive step ($n = m+1$):** For $m+1$ units, the $(m+1)$-th unit is the *new* standby added to a working $m$-unit system. Using the convolution principle:

$$R_{m+1}(t) = R_{m}(t) + \int_{0}^{t} f_{m}(\tau)\cdot e^{-\lambda(t-\tau)}\, d\tau$$

where $f_m(\tau)$ is the pdf of failure of the $m$-unit system at $\tau$.

**Step 4 — Elegant shortcut (Poisson process view):** Under exponential lifetimes, the number of failures in $[0, t]$ follows a **Poisson process** with rate $\lambda$. The system has $m$ units active in sequence, so it survives the mission iff **fewer than $m$ failures occur** in $[0, t]$:

$$P\{N(t) < m\} = \sum_{k=0}^{m-1}\frac{(\lambda t)^{k}\, e^{-\lambda t}}{k!}$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{R_{n}(t) = e^{-\lambda t}\sum_{k=0}^{n-1}\frac{(\lambda t)^{k}}{k!}}$$

**Step 5 — MTTF via the Poisson sum identity:**

$$MTTF = \int_{0}^{\infty} R_{n}(t)\, dt = \sum_{k=0}^{n-1}\frac{\lambda^{k}}{k!}\int_{0}^{\infty} t^{k} e^{-\lambda t}\, dt$$

Using $\int_{0}^{\infty} t^{k} e^{-\lambda t}\, dt = \dfrac{k!}{\lambda^{k+1}}$:

$$MTTF = \sum_{k=0}^{n-1}\frac{\lambda^{k}}{k!}\cdot \frac{k!}{\lambda^{k+1}} = \sum_{k=0}^{n-1}\frac{1}{\lambda} = \frac{n}{\lambda}$$

$$\boxed{MTTF = \frac{n}{\lambda}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU golden rule:** The cold-standby MTTF grows **linearly** with $n$, whereas parallel redundancy grows only as $\dfrac{1}{\lambda}\bigl(1 + \dfrac{1}{2} + \dfrac{1}{3} + \dots + \dfrac{1}{n}\bigr)$. For $n=5$, cold-standby gives $5/\lambda$ vs. parallel's $2.283/\lambda$ — a **2.2× improvement**.

---

## 3.5 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Style)

**Question:** A system has two identical components each with failure rate $\lambda = 0.001$ per hour. They are arranged in cold standby with a switch of reliability $R_{sw} = 0.95$. Compute (a) the system reliability at $t = 500$ hours, and (b) the MTTF.

**Solution:**

**(a) Reliability at $t = 500$ hr:**

$$\lambda t = 0.001 \times 500 = 0.5$$

$$R(500) = e^{-0.5}\bigl(1 + 0.95 \times 0.5\bigr)$$

$$R(500) = 0.6065 \times \bigl(1 + 0.475\bigr) = 0.6065 \times 1.475$$

$$\boxed{R(500) = 0.8946}$$

**(b) MTTF:**

$$MTTF = \frac{1 + R_{sw}}{\lambda} = \frac{1 + 0.95}{0.001} = \frac{1.95}{0.001}$$

$$\boxed{MTTF = 1950 \text{ hours}}$$

**Comparison:** A perfect switch ($R_{sw} = 1$) would give $MTTF = 2000$ hr — a 2.5% reduction due to switch imperfection.

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional State Diagram — Two-Unit Cold Standby

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> StateS0 : Start of mission
    StateS0 : State S0 — Unit 1 Active, Unit 2 Cold Standby, Switch Idle
    StateS1 : State S1 — Unit 1 Failed, Unit 2 Active (switched), Switch OK
    StateF  : State F — System Failed (Unit 2 also failed OR Switch Failed)

    StateS0 --> StateS1 : lambda (Unit 1 fails)
    StateS0 --> StateF  : lambda_f_sw (Switch fails prematurely)
    StateS1 --> StateF  : lambda (Unit 2 fails)
    StateF  --> [*]     : Mission over
    StateS1 --> StateS0 : Repair and reset (not modeled in this problem)
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- **S0** is the initial state — Unit 1 runs while Unit 2 sits idle.
- Transition from **S0 → S1** occurs at rate $\lambda$ when Unit 1 fails (and the switch works).
- Transition from **S1 → F** occurs at rate $\lambda$ when the activated Unit 2 fails.
- **F** is the absorbing failure state.

- **H** is the common output bus feeding the load.
