# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: Distinguish between cold standby, warm standby, and hot standby systems. Give one real-world example of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Type | Standby Operating State | Failure Rate in Standby | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Cold Standby** | De-energized, no wear | $\mu = 0$ | Spare tyre in a car |
| **Warm Standby** | Partially energized, slow degradation | $0 < \mu < \lambda$ | Diesel generator on idle |
| **Hot Standby** | Fully energized, equal load-sharing with active unit | $\mu = \lambda$ | Dual-engine aircraft |

In **cold standby**, the reserve unit does not age while waiting, giving the highest possible reliability gain. In **warm standby**, the reserve ages slowly, reducing its effective life upon activation. In **hot standby**, both units age identically, making it functionally equivalent to a parallel system. **(3 marks)**

---

### Question A2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Define MTTF of a standby system. For a 3-unit cold-standby system with each unit having failure rate $\lambda = 0.002$/hr, find the MTTF.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** MTTF (Mean Time To Failure) is the expected time elapsed before the system fails, calculated as $\displaystyle MTTF = \int_{0}^{\infty} R_{sys}(t)\, dt$.

For a 3-unit cold-standby system, using the standard formula $MTTF = n/\lambda$:

$$MTTF = \frac{3}{0.002} = 1500 \text{ hours}$$

**Answer: MTTF = 1500 hours. (3 marks)**

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** In Part B questions, marks are awarded strictly for (i) correct formula identification, (ii) step-by-step substitution, (iii) integration where MTTF is asked, and (iv) explicit mention of *assumptions*. Students who skip assumptions **lose 2 marks automatically** in the KTU 2024 scheme.

---

### Question B-A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**A system consists of 3 identical components, each with constant failure rate $\lambda = 0.0005$ per hour. Initially, one component is operating and the other two are in cold standby. The switching mechanism is perfect.**

**(a)** Derive an expression for the system reliability $R(t)$ in terms of $t$ and $\lambda$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compute the MTTF and the system reliability at $t = 1000$ hours. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

##### Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks)

**Assumption statement (1 mark):** Component lifetimes are independent and exponentially distributed with rate $\lambda$. Switch is perfect ($R_{sw} = 1$). No repair during the mission.

**Set-up (1 mark):** By the Poisson-process view, the number of failures in $[0,t]$ is Poisson with parameter $\lambda t$. The system has 3 units (1 active + 2 standby), so it survives iff **fewer than 3 failures** occur in $[0,t]$, i.e., $N(t) \in \{0, 1, 2\}$.

**Formula assembly (3 marks):**

$$R(t) = P\{N(t) \le 2\} = \sum_{k=0}^{2}\frac{(\lambda t)^{k}\, e^{-\lambda t}}{k!}$$

$$\boxed{R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}\left[1 + \lambda t + \frac{(\lambda t)^{2}}{2}\right]}$$

**Verification by alternative derivation (2 marks):** Equivalently,

$$R(t) = e^{-\lambda t} + \lambda t\, e^{-\lambda t} + \frac{(\lambda t)^{2}}{2}\, e^{-\lambda t}$$

Each term represents the probability that exactly $k$ components fail during the mission. Summing $k=0,1,2$ gives the full reliability expression.

##### Part (b) — MTTF and $R(1000)$ (7 marks)

**MTTF derivation (4 marks):**

$$MTTF = \int_{0}^{\infty} R(t)\, dt = \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-\lambda t}\left[1 + \lambda t + \frac{(\lambda t)^{2}}{2}\right] dt$$

Split into three integrals:

$$MTTF = \underbrace{\int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-\lambda t}\, dt}_{1/\lambda} + \lambda\underbrace{\int_{0}^{\infty} t\, e^{-\lambda t}\, dt}_{1/\lambda^{2}} + \frac{\lambda^{2}}{2}\underbrace{\int_{0}^{\infty} t^{2}\, e^{-\lambda t}\, dt}_{2/\lambda^{3}}$$

$$MTTF = \frac{1}{\lambda} + \frac{1}{\lambda} + \frac{1}{\lambda} = \frac{3}{\lambda}$$

$$\boxed{MTTF = \frac{3}{0.0005} = 6000 \text{ hours}}$$

**Numerical evaluation at $t = 1000$ hr (3 marks):**

$$\lambda t = 0.0005 \times 1000 = 0.5$$

$$R(1000) = e^{-0.5}\left[1 + 0.5 + \frac{(0.5)^{2}}{2}\right]$$

$$R(1000) = 0.6065 \times \bigl[1 + 0.5 + 0.125\bigr] = 0.6065 \times 1.625$$

$$\boxed{R(1000) = 0.9856}$$

**Interpretation:** After 1000 hours (≈ 42 days), the system retains 98.56% reliability — a substantial improvement over a single component's $e^{-0.5} = 0.6065$.

---

### Question B-B (14 Marks, Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**A standby system has two identical components. Component A (active) has failure rate $\lambda = 0.002$/hr. Component B is in warm standby with failure rate $\mu = 0.0005$/hr. Switching is perfect.**

**(a)** Derive the system reliability $R(t)$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Determine the MTTF and compare it numerically with the cold-standby case for the same active-unit failure rate. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

##### Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks)

**Assumption statement (1 mark):** Independent exponentially distributed lifetimes. Warm standby means B ages at rate $\mu$ while waiting. On activation at time $\tau$, B runs with rate $\lambda$.

**Identify mutually exclusive success events (1 mark):**
- Event E1: A survives entire mission.
- Event E2: A fails at time $\tau$, B survived $[0,\tau]$ in standby, B then survives $[\tau, t]$ after switching.

**Integral form (2 marks):**

$$R(t) = e^{-\lambda t} + \int_{0}^{t} \lambda e^{-\lambda \tau}\cdot e^{-\mu \tau}\cdot e^{-\lambda(t-\tau)}\, d\tau$$

**Simplify integrand (1 mark):** $e^{-\lambda\tau}\cdot e^{-\mu\tau}\cdot e^{-\lambda(t-\tau)} = e^{-\lambda t}\cdot e^{-\mu\tau}$.

$$R(t) = e^{-\lambda t} + \lambda e^{-\lambda t}\int_{0}^{t} e^{-\mu\tau}\, d\tau$$

**Evaluate integral (1 mark):** $\displaystyle\int_{0}^{t} e^{-\mu\tau}\, d\tau = \frac{1 - e^{-\mu t}}{\mu}$.

**Final expression (1 mark):**

$$\boxed{R(t) = e^{-\lambda t} + \frac{\lambda}{\mu}\bigl(e^{-\lambda t} - e^{-(\lambda+\mu)t}\bigr)}$$

##### Part (b) — MTTF and Comparison (7 marks)

**MTTF derivation (4 marks):**

$$MTTF = \int_{0}^{\infty} R(t)\, dt = \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-\lambda t}\, dt + \frac{\lambda}{\mu}\int_{0}^{\infty}\bigl(e^{-\lambda t} - e^{-(\lambda+\mu)t}\bigr)\, dt$$

$$MTTF = \frac{1}{\lambda} + \frac{\lambda}{\mu}\left(\frac{1}{\lambda} - \frac{1}{\lambda+\mu}\right)$$

Compute the bracket:

$$\frac{1}{\lambda} - \frac{1}{\lambda+\mu} = \frac{\mu}{\lambda(\lambda+\mu)}$$

So:

$$MTTF = \frac{1}{\lambda} + \frac{\lambda}{\mu}\cdot \frac{\mu}{\lambda(\lambda+\mu)} = \frac{1}{\lambda} + \frac{1}{\lambda+\mu}$$

**Numerical substitution (2 marks):** With $\lambda = 0.002$/hr and $\mu = 0.0005$/hr:

$$MTTF_{warm} = \frac{1}{0.002} + \frac{1}{0.002 + 0.0005} = 500 + 400 = 900 \text{ hours}$$

**Comparison with cold-standby (1 mark):** Cold standby MTTF $= 2/\lambda = 1000$ hr. The warm standby gives $900$ hr — a $10\%$ reduction. This quantifies the *cost* of having the standby unit slowly age while waiting.

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU 2024 Valuation Patterns — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the switch reliability $R_{sw}$:** When a problem says *the switch has reliability 0.95*, students often ignore it. Always multiply the integral term by $R_{sw}$.
> 2. **Confusing standby vs. parallel formulas:** Parallel redundancy gives $R = 1 - (1 - e^{-\lambda t})^{n}$, which is **wrong** for standby. Use the Poisson summation form $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}\sum_{k=0}^{n-1}(\lambda t)^k/k!$.
> 3. **Skipping assumptions:** KTU answer keys explicitly deduct marks if assumptions (exponential lifetimes, independence, perfect/imperfect switch) are not stated.
> 4. **MTTF integration errors:** The most common slip is forgetting to integrate term-by-term. Use the standard result $\int_0^\infty t^k e^{-\lambda t} dt = k!/\lambda^{k+1}$.
> 5. **Units:** Failure rates must be in **per hour** (or per the time unit used). Mixing units loses 1 mark.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Standby Systems**

- **Core distinction:** Standby units *do not operate* until activated; parallel units *operate simultaneously*.
- **Cold standby MTTF = $n/\lambda$:** linear growth with $n$ — the **KTU golden result**.
- **Warm standby MTTF = $1/\lambda + 1/(\lambda+\mu)$:** strictly less than cold standby; reduces to cold standby as $\mu \to 0$.
- **Imperfect switch** introduces the multiplicative factor $R_{sw}$ in the integral; MTTF becomes $(1+R_{sw})/\lambda$ for 2-unit cold standby.
- **Reliability form for 2-unit cold standby:** $R(t) = (1 + \lambda t)\,e^{-\lambda t}$.
- **Reliability form for 2-unit warm standby:** $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t} + (\lambda/\mu)(e^{-\lambda t} - e^{-(\lambda+\mu)t})$.
- **Poisson-process view:** Cold-standby system with $n$ units survives iff $< n$ failures occur → $R(t) = \sum_{k=0}^{n-1} e^{-\lambda t}(\lambda t)^k/k!$.
- **Integral identity (use repeatedly):** $\int_0^\infty t^k e^{-\lambda t} dt = k!/\lambda^{k+1}$.
- **Always state assumptions:** exponential lifetimes, independence, switch quality, no repair.
- **Numerical tip:** Use $\lambda t$ as a dimensionless group for cleaner arithmetic.
- **Limiting cases are your friend:** Verify answers by checking $\mu \to 0$ (cold), $\mu = \lambda$ (hot/parallel), $R_{sw} = 1$ (perfect switch).
- **Real-world examples to cite:** emergency pumps (cold standby), UPS batteries (warm), aircraft twin engines (hot).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Degraded Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET632 - RELIABILITY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Degraded Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In **Reliability Engineering**, a **Degraded System** is defined as a multi-component system that continues to provide its intended function — albeit at a *reduced performance level* — after the failure of one or more of its constituent components. Unlike a binary (perfectly working / completely failed) system, a degraded system exhibits **multiple intermediate operational states**, each characterized by a measurable performance ratio (or capacity factor) lying strictly between **0** and **1**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (verbatim mapping):**
> A degraded system is one that can operate at a lower performance level after component failure. The system is considered *failed* only when its cumulative performance drops below a predefined minimum acceptable threshold.

## 1.2 Key Terminology

- **Performance Rate (P_i)** — The fraction of nominal (rated) capacity at which the system operates when in state *i*.
- **Degradation Factor (D)** — A dimensionless ratio indicating the degree of performance loss.
- **State Probability** — The probability that the system is found in a particular operational/degraded state at time *t*.
- **Minimum Acceptable Performance (P_min)** — The threshold below which the system is declared functionally failed.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy: A Three-Engine Jet Aircraft**
> Imagine a wide-body commercial aircraft equipped with **three engines**. During cruise flight:
> - **All 3 engines working** → 100% rated thrust. Performance Rate = **1.0**.
> - **2 engines working, 1 engine fails** → Aircraft can still fly, but with reduced thrust and altitude capability. Performance Rate ≈ **0.75**.
> - **1 engine working, 2 fail** → Aircraft can barely maintain level flight, declared emergency. Performance Rate ≈ **0.40**.
> - **0 engines** → Complete failure. Performance Rate = **0**.
>
> The aircraft does NOT instantly crash when one engine fails — it *degrades*. This is the essence of a degraded system.

**Other classic engineering examples:**
1. **Power Generating Station** with 4 generators — losing one means 75% output.
2. **Multi-Processor Server** with 8 CPUs — losing 1 CPU drops throughput to ~7/8.
3. **Pipeline Network** with parallel redundant lines.

## 1.4 Why Degraded Systems Matter

In modern engineering design, the philosophy has shifted from binary reliability metrics toward **graceful degradation** — a design paradigm where systems are intentionally built with redundancy so that component failures result in *predictable* performance loss rather than catastrophic collapse. This is particularly critical in:

- Aerospace systems
- Nuclear reactor controls
- Telecommunications infrastructure
- Cloud computing clusters

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** Always emphasize in your answers that degraded system analysis is the bridge between *classical reliability* (binary good/bad) and *modern availability engineering* (graded performance).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Concepts

A degraded system can be modelled as a **multi-state system (MSS)** with the following structural properties:

1. **Discreet State Space:** The system can occupy a finite number of discrete performance states.
2. **Ordered States:** States are ordered by performance rate, where
   $$P_0 = 1.0 \quad \text{(Perfect operation)}$$
   $$P_1, P_2, \ldots, P_k \quad \text{(Degraded states)}$$
   $$P_{k+1} = 0 \quad \text{(Complete failure)}$$
3. **Markovian Memory:** Transitions between states are governed by exponential failure/repair rates.

## 2.2 Classification of Degraded States

| State Type | Performance Rate (P_i) | System Status | Example (4-pump system) |
|------------|------------------------|---------------|------------------------|
| Full Capacity | P = 1.0 | All components working | 4/4 pumps running |
| Mildly Degraded | 0.75 ≤ P < 1.0 | Reduced but acceptable | 3/4 pumps running |
| Heavily Degraded | 0 < P < 0.5 | Critical operation | 1/4 pumps running |
| Failed | P = 0 | Functionally dead | 0/4 pumps running |

## 2.3 Degradation Factor (D)

The **degradation factor** quantifies the average performance shortfall of the system:

$$D = 1 - \frac{\sum_{i=0}^{k} P_i \cdot p_i(t)}{\sum_{i=0}^{k} P_i \cdot p_i(0)}$$

Where:
- $P_i$ = Performance rate in state $i$
- $p_i(t)$ = Probability of being in state $i$ at time $t$

## 2.4 Effective / Equivalent Reliability

For a degraded system with $n$ parallel identical components, each having reliability $R(t)$ and failure rate $\lambda$, the **effective reliability** is the probability-weighted sum of state reliabilities:

$$R_{eff}(t) = \sum_{i=0}^{n} P_i \cdot \binom{n}{i} R(t)^i [1-R(t)]^{n-i}$$

This is the most-tested formula in KTU exams.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $P_i = \dfrac{Q_i}{Q_{rated}}$ | Performance Rate | Dimensionless |
| 2 | $R_{eff}(t) = \sum_{i=0}^{n} P_i \cdot \binom{n}{i} R^i (1-R)^{n-i}$ | Effective reliability of degraded parallel system | Dimensionless |
| 3 | $P_{sys} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} R_i(t)$ | Reliability of non-degraded series system | Dimensionless |
| 4 | $P_{sys} = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n}(1-R_i(t))$ | Reliability of non-degraded parallel system | Dimensionless |
| 5 | $A_{eff} = \sum_{i=0}^{n} P_i \cdot A_i$ | Effective availability (weighted by capacity) | Dimensionless |
| 6 | $\lambda_i = i \lambda$ | Failure rate with $i$ components failed in parallel | 1/hour |
| 7 | $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ | Reliability of single exponential component | Dimensionless |
| 8 | $q = 1 - e^{-\lambda t}$ | Unreliability of single component | Dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary Condition:** For a non-degraded system, $P_i \in \{0, 1\}$ only. For a degraded system, $P_i$ can take any value in $[0,1]$.

## 2.6 Real-World Utility

Degraded system analysis is used in:
- **Aerospace:** Multi-engine aircraft certification (FAR Part 25).
- **Nuclear Power:** Reactor coolant pump redundancy.
- **Telecom:** Cellular base station with carrier aggregation.
- **Manufacturing:** Production lines with parallel stations.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Effective Reliability of an n-Component Parallel Degraded System

### Problem Setup
Consider a system consisting of **n identical, independent components** arranged in parallel. Each component has:
- Reliability: $R(t)$
- Unreliability: $q(t) = 1 - R(t)$
- Identical failure rate: $\lambda$

When $i$ out of $n$ components are working, the system operates at a degraded performance rate $P_i$.

### Step 1: Express the Probability of State *i*

The probability that exactly $i$ components out of $n$ are working (and $n-i$ have failed) follows the **binomial distribution**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
p_i(t) &= \binom{n}{i} [R(t)]^i [q(t)]^{n-i} \\
       &= \binom{n}{i} [R(t)]^i [1-R(t)]^{n-i}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Reasoning:** We must choose which $i$ of the $n$ components survive (the binomial coefficient $\binom{n}{i}$), then multiply the probability that each chosen component works ($R^i$) and the probability that each failed component does not work ($(1-R)^{n-i}$).

### Step 2: Define the Performance Rate Function

The performance rate $P_i$ is typically linearly proportional to the number of working components:

$$
P_i = \frac{i}{n}
$$

This is the simplest and most commonly used model in KTU problems. For example:
- 4 working out of 4 → $P_i = 1.0$
- 3 working out of 4 → $P_i = 0.75$
- 2 working out of 4 → $P_i = 0.5$

### Step 3: Form the Expected Performance

The expected (effective) performance is the probability-weighted average:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar{P}(t) &= \sum_{i=0}^{n} P_i \cdot p_i(t) \\
           &= \sum_{i=0}^{n} \frac{i}{n} \cdot \binom{n}{i} R^i (1-R)^{n-i}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4: Simplify Using the Binomial Expectation Identity

Recall the binomial identity: $\sum_{i=0}^{n} i \binom{n}{i} R^i (1-R)^{n-i} = nR$

This identity comes from recognizing that the LHS is the expected value of a Binomial(n, R) random variable.

Applying this:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar{P}(t) &= \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=0}^{n} i \binom{n}{i} R^i (1-R)^{n-i} \\
           &= \frac{1}{n} \cdot nR \\
           &= R(t)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Beautiful Result:** For linearly proportional degradation, the *effective reliability* equals the **reliability of a single component**. This counter-intuitive result shows that parallel redundancy inherently recovers the full reliability from individual components.

### Step 5: General Effective Reliability (Including Performance Weights)

When $P_i$ is NOT linearly proportional (e.g., non-linear performance gains):

$$
R_{eff}(t) = \sum_{i=0}^{n} P_i \binom{n}{i} R^i (1-R)^{n-i}
$$

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style)

**Problem:** A system consists of 3 parallel pumps, each with reliability $R(t) = 0.9$ at time $t = 100$ hours. Performance rate is linearly proportional. Calculate the effective reliability and degradation factor.

### Solution

**Step 1:** Enumerate all states.

| State (i) | Working Pumps | Performance $P_i = i/3$ | State Probability $\binom{3}{i}(0.9)^i(0.1)^{3-i}$ |
|-----------|---------------|--------------------------|--------------------------------------------------------|
| 3         | 3             | 1.000                    | $\binom{3}{3}(0.9)^3(0.1)^0 = 0.729$                |
| 2         | 2             | 0.667                    | $\binom{3}{2}(0.9)^2(0.1)^1 = 0.243$                |
| 1         | 1             | 0.333                    | $\binom{3}{1}(0.9)^1(0.1)^2 = 0.027$                |
| 0         | 0             | 0.000                    | $\binom{3}{0}(0.9)^0(0.1)^3 = 0.001$                |

**Step 2:** Sum of probabilities (verification):
$$
0.729 + 0.243 + 0.027 + 0.001 = 1.000 \;\checkmark
$$

**Step 3:** Compute effective reliability:
$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{eff}(t) &= \sum_{i=0}^{3} P_i \cdot p_i(t) \\
           &= (1.000)(0.729) + (0.667)(0.243) + (0.333)(0.027) + (0)(0.001) \\
           &= 0.729 + 0.1621 + 0.009 + 0 \\
           &= 0.9001
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Compute degradation factor:
$$
D = 1 - R_{eff} = 1 - 0.9001 = 0.0999 \approx 10\%
$$

**Step 5:** Interpretation:
- Even though 27.1% of components will have failed by 100 hours, the system retains **90% performance**.
- Only when all 3 pumps fail (probability 0.001) does the system drop to 0.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 State Transition Diagram for a 3-Component Degraded System

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> S3 : System Start
    S3 : State 3 - All Working (P=1.00)
    S2 : State 2 - Mildly Degraded (P=0.67)
    S1 : State 1 - Heavily Degraded (P=0.33)
    S0 : State 0 - Failed (P=0.00)

    S3 --> S2 : 3 lambda (failure rate)
    S2 --> S1 : 2 lambda
    S1 --> S0 : 1 lambda

    S0 --> [*] : System Dead
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Degraded Power System

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Input Power Grid] --> B[Distribution Bus]
    B --> C1[Generator Unit 1]
    B --> C2[Generator Unit 2]
    B --> C3[Generator Unit 3]
    B --> C4[Generator Unit 4]

    C1 --> D[Output Load Bus]
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
    C4 --> D

    D --> E[Performance Monitor]
    E --> F{Performance above threshold}
    F -->|Yes, P greater than 0.75| G[Normal Operation]
    F -->|Yes, P between 0.50 and 0.75| H[Mildly Degraded]
    F -->|Yes, P between 0.25 and 0.50| I[Heavily Degraded]
    F -->|No, P less than 0.25| J[System Failed]

    style A fill:#90EE90
    style D fill:#FFD700
    style E fill:#87CEEB
    style G fill:#32CD32
    style H fill:#FFA500
    style I fill:#FF6347
    style J fill:#8B0000
```

## 4.3 Reliability Block Diagram of a Degraded Series-Parallel Configuration

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Subsystem A] --> B[Subsystem B]
    B --> C[Subsystem C]
    A -.-> A1[A1 Component]
    A -.-> A2[A2 Component]
    A -.-> A3[A3 Component]
    B -.-> B1[B1 Component]
    B -.-> B2[B2 Component]
    C -.-> C1[C1 Component]
    C -.-> C2[C2 Component]
    C -.-> C3[C3 Component]
    C -.-> C4[C4 Component]
```

**Interpretation:**
- Subsystems A, B, C are arranged in **series** (any subsystem failure → partial degradation).
- Within each subsystem, components are in **parallel** (offering redundancy).
- As components fail in parallel groups, the system degrades progressively.

## 4.4 Conceptual Flow: From Binary to Degraded Reliability

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Traditional Binary Reliability] -->|System either works or fails| B{Reliability = 0 or 1}
    B --> C[Limitations in real systems]
    C --> D[Degraded System Approach]
    D --> E[Multi-State Reliability]
    E --> F[Performance-Weighted Metrics]
    F --> G[Modern Reliability Engineering]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Conceptual Definition
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Q:** Define a *degraded system* in reliability engineering. How does it differ from a conventional binary system?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- (1 mark) A degraded system is a multi-component system that continues to operate at reduced performance levels after one or more component failures, rather than failing completely.
- (1 mark) In contrast, a conventional binary system has only two states: **fully functional (1)** or **completely failed (0)** — there is no intermediate performance level.
- (1 mark) A degraded system is considered failed only when its performance rate drops below a **minimum acceptable threshold** $P_{min}$, not when any single component fails.

---

### Question 2: Performance Rate
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**Q:** What is meant by the *performance rate* of a degraded system? Give an example.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- (1 mark) Performance rate $P_i$ is defined as the ratio of the actual output capacity $Q_i$ to the rated (nominal) capacity $Q_{rated}$ of the system when it is in state $i$.
- (1 mark) Mathematically: $P_i = Q_i / Q_{rated}$, with $0 \le P_i \le 1$.
- (1 mark) **Example:** A power station with 4 generators of 100 MW each has a rated capacity of 400 MW. If only 3 generators are operational, $P_i = 300/400 = 0.75$.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to weight by performance rate** — Students often compute $\sum p_i$ instead of $\sum P_i \cdot p_i$. This loses 4 marks immediately.
> 2. **Confusing series vs. parallel** — Series systems do NOT degrade gracefully (failure of one = system fails). Only parallel/standby systems exhibit degradation.
> 3. **Unit errors** — Performance rate is *dimensionless*. Failure rate $\lambda$ is in **1/hour** (or 1/time unit).
> 4. **Skipping the state probability table** — Always enumerate all states in a table; this gives partial credit even if the final sum is wrong.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — Path 1

**[KTU University Exam – June 2023]**

A system consists of **4 identical pumps** arranged in parallel. Each pump has a reliability of $R = 0.85$ and the performance rate is proportional to the number of working pumps.

**Sub-part (a) [7 marks]:** Determine the effective reliability of the system.

**Sub-part (b) [7 marks]:** Calculate the degradation factor and comment on system performance.

### Model Solution A

**Solution to (a):**

**Step 1:** Identify all possible states. With 4 identical pumps, the system can be in 5 states (0 to 4 working pumps).

**Step 2:** Build the state probability table.

| State $i$ | Working Pumps | $P_i = i/4$ | $p_i = \binom{4}{i}(0.85)^i(0.15)^{4-i}$ | $P_i \cdot p_i$ |
|-----------|---------------|--------------|--------------------------------------------|------------------|
| 4 | 4 | 1.0000 | $\binom{4}{4}(0.85)^4(0.15)^0 = 0.5220$ | 0.5220 |
| 3 | 3 | 0.7500 | $\binom{4}{3}(0.85)^3(0.15)^1 = 0.3685$ | 0.2764 |
| 2 | 2 | 0.5000 | $\binom{4}{2}(0.85)^2(0.15)^2 = 0.0975$ | 0.0488 |
| 1 | 1 | 0.2500 | $\binom{4}{1}(0.85)^1(0.15)^3 = 0.0115$ | 0.0029 |
| 0 | 0 | 0.0000 | $\binom{4}{0}(0.85)^0(0.15)^4 = 0.0005$ | 0.0000 |
| | | | **Total** | **0.8501** |

**Step 3:** Sum the weighted contributions:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{eff}(t) &= \sum_{i=0}^{4} P_i \cdot p_i(t) \\
           &= 0.5220 + 0.2764 + 0.0488 + 0.0029 + 0.0000 \\
           &= 0.8501
\end{aligned}
$$

**Answer (a):** $R_{eff}(t) \approx 0.85$ ✓

---

**Solution to (b):**

**Step 1:** Calculate the degradation factor.

$$
D = 1 - R_{eff} = 1 - 0.8501 = 0.1499 \approx 15\%
$$

**Step 2:** Comment on performance.

The system suffers approximately a **15% degradation** in effective performance. However, only **0.05%** of the time does the system reach complete failure (all 4 pumps down). This demonstrates the value of parallel redundancy — even with each pump having only 85% reliability, the *system-level effective reliability* matches the component-level reliability, and catastrophic failure becomes extremely rare.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Path 2 (Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

A power generating station has **3 identical generators** in parallel. Each generator has reliability $R = 0.80$ and operates independently. The station can deliver partial output:
- All 3 working → 100% output
- 2 working → 75% output
- 1 working → 40% output
- 0 working → 0% output

**Sub-part (a) [7 marks]:** Construct the state probability distribution table and compute the effective reliability.

**Sub-part (b) [7 marks]:** If the station requires a minimum of 60% output for normal operation, determine the probability that the station is **functionally failed**.

### Model Solution B

**Solution to (a):**

**Step 1:** State the formula:
$$
R_{eff} = \sum_{i=0}^{3} P_i \binom{3}{i} R^i (1-R)^{3-i}
$$

**Step 2:** Build the table with the given (non-linear) performance rates.

| State $i$ | Working Gens | $P_i$ | $p_i = \binom{3}{i}(0.8)^i(0.2)^{3-i}$ | $P_i \cdot p_i$ |
|-----------|--------------|-------|-----------------------------------------|------------------|
| 3 | 3 | 1.00 | $\binom{3}{3}(0.8)^3(0.2)^0 = 0.5120$ | 0.5120 |
| 2 | 2 | 0.75 | $\binom{3}{2}(0.8)^2(0.2)^1 = 0.3840$ | 0.2880 |
| 1 | 1 | 0.40 | $\binom{3}{1}(0.8)^1(0.2)^2 = 0.0960$ | 0.0384 |
| 0 | 0 | 0.00 | $\binom{3}{0}(0.8)^0(0.2)^3 = 0.0080$ | 0.0000 |
| | | | **Total** | **0.8384** |

**Step 3:** Final computation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{eff} &= 0.5120 + 0.2880 + 0.0384 + 0.0000 \\
        &= 0.8384
\end{aligned}
$$

**Answer (a):** $R_{eff} = 0.8384$

---

**Solution to (b):**

**Step 1:** Identify failed states. The system requires $\ge 60\%$ output. Looking at the table:
- State 3 → 100% ✓ (working)
- State 2 → 75% ✓ (working)
- State 1 → 40% ✗ (failed)
- State 0 → 0% ✗ (failed)

**Step 2:** Sum probabilities of failed states:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(\text{Failed}) &= p_1 + p_0 \\
                 &= 0.0960 + 0.0080 \\
                 &= 0.1040
\end{aligned}
$$

**Answer (b):** Probability of functional failure = **0.1040 (10.4%)**

The functional unavailability is significantly higher (10.4%) than the catastrophic failure probability (0.8%), highlighting how degraded-system analysis captures operational shortfalls that binary analysis completely misses.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Yield Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Exams:**

- ✅ **Definition:** A degraded system operates at reduced performance after component failure — not a binary "good/bad" model.
- ✅ **Performance Rate $P_i$:** Ratio of actual output to rated output, with $0 \le P_i \le 1$.
- ✅ **Master Formula:** $R_{eff}(t) = \sum_{i=0}^{n} P_i \binom{n}{i} R^i (1-R)^{n-i}$
- ✅ **Binomial Identity Trick:** $\sum_{i=0}^{n} i \binom{n}{i} R^i (1-R)^{n-i} = nR$ (extremely useful for short solutions).
- ✅ **Linear Degradation Shortcut:** When $P_i = i/n$, $R_{eff} = R$ (the system reliability equals single-component reliability).
- ✅ **Functional Failure Threshold:** Define $P_{min}$ explicitly before computing functional unavailability.
- ✅ **Units:** Reliability → dimensionless; Failure rate → 1/hour; Performance → fraction.
- ✅ **Common Mistake:** Forgetting the $P_i$ weight when computing effective reliability (always weight state probabilities by their performance).
- ✅ **Real-World Context:** Aircraft engines, power grids, multi-processor servers, telecom base stations.
- ✅ **Design Implication:** Parallel redundancy converts catastrophic failure into graceful degradation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Three State Devices
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET632 - RELIABILITY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Three State Devices

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Three-State Device** is a fundamental reliability engineering model used to characterize repairable components or systems that can occupy **three mutually exclusive and collectively exhaustive (MECE) operational states** during their lifecycle. Unlike the classical two-state (Working–Failed) model, the three-state model introduces an intermediate state — most commonly a **Standby (Reserve) State** or a **Partially Operating State** — to better capture real-world industrial assets such as pumps in parallel, redundant power supplies, hot/cold standby generators, and dual-channel instrumentation loops.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PEIET632 - Reliability Engineering, Module 2)** syllabus, the canonical three-state device is defined by the following three states:

1. **State 1 — Working (Operating / Up State):** The device performs its intended function at full rated capacity.
2. **State 2 — Standby (Reserve / Idle State):** The device is *not* actively performing its function but remains *available* and can be switched into the working state either manually or automatically.
3. **State 3 — Failed (Down / Repair State):** The device is non-functional and is undergoing repair (or awaiting repair, or being replaced).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition Box:**
> A *three-state device* is a repairable system whose stochastic behavior is governed by transitions among three discrete states — *Operating*, *Standby*, and *Failed* — characterized by exponential failure rate **λ** and exponential repair rate **μ**, and analyzed using **continuous-time Markov chain (CTMC)** methodology.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: A Hospital's Emergency Generator System**
>
> Imagine a hospital with a primary electricity line (the *Working* state). To prevent blackouts, the hospital maintains a backup diesel generator that is fueled, tested, and ready to ignite — but is *currently off*. This generator is in the **Standby State**. If the main line fails, an Automatic Transfer Switch (ATS) flips the generator online (Working). Now, if the diesel generator itself breaks down (e.g., fuel pump failure), it enters the **Failed State** and a maintenance crew is dispatched.
>
> So you have **three** states:
> - *Working* → main line OK, generator idle in standby
> - *Standby* → main line OK, generator still idle (or in this analogy, the generator is the active device, and we shift the roles)
> - *Failed* → something is broken and being repaired
>
> Reliability engineering asks: *What is the probability that the device is in Working state at time t?* — this is called **Availability**.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following **standard reliability metrics** are universally used to characterize three-state devices:

| Symbol | Parameter | Unit | Description |
|--------|-----------|------|-------------|
| **λ** | Failure rate | failures/hour | Constant hazard from Operating → Failed |
| **λ₁** | Failure rate from Operating to Failed | failures/hour | Direct failure transition |
| **μ** | Repair rate | repairs/hour | Transition from Failed → Operating |
| **α** | Switchover rate | switchovers/hour | Operating → Standby activation rate |
| **β** | Standby failure rate | failures/hour | Standby → Failed (often λ in cold standby) |
| **A** | Steady-state availability | dimensionless (0–1) | Fraction of time system is operational |
| **A(t)** | Instantaneous availability | dimensionless | Probability of being up at time *t* |
| **MTTF** | Mean Time To Failure | hours | Expected uptime before first failure |
| **MTTR** | Mean Time To Repair | hours | Expected downtime during repair |
| **MTBF** | Mean Time Between Failures | hours | MTTF + MTTR for repairable systems |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For three-state devices, the system is *considered UP* only when it is in the **Working state**, NOT in Standby (unless it is a parallel redundant configuration where Standby components count toward availability, which is a deeper Module 3 concept).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three-State Device — Transition Logic

A canonical three-state device is governed by **four transition rates** that form the building blocks of its **state transition diagram (STD)**:

- **Operating → Failed** at rate **λ** (the device fails while running)
- **Failed → Operating** at rate **μ** (repair restores the device to Working state)
- **Operating → Standby** at rate **α** (the device is deliberately taken offline, e.g., load shedding)
- **Standby → Operating** at rate **β** (the standby device is switched back online)

Optionally, we may allow:
- **Standby → Failed** at rate **γ** (a standby device can fail without ever being activated)

The system memory is **Markovian (memoryless)** because all transition rates are **constant** (exponential holding times).

### 2.2 Why Use the Three-State Model?

> [!NOTE]
> **Why not just use a 2-state model?**
>
> Two-state models fail to capture situations where a device can be **available but not actively performing** its function. For example:
> - A **standby pump** that is operational but not currently running.
> - A **backup transformer** in an electrical substation.
> - A **spare CPU** in a fault-tolerant server.
>
> Ignoring the standby state leads to **overestimation of unavailability** and **incorrect reliability block diagrams**.

### 2.3 Markov State Transition Diagram — The Logic Flow

```
      ┌──────────────────────────────────────────┐
      │                                          │
      ▼                                          │
 [1: Operating] ───λ───▶ [3: Failed] ───μ───▶ (back to 1)
      │  ▲                                          │
      α  β                                          │
      ▼  │                                          │
 [2: Standby] ─────γ(?)───▶ [3: Failed] ───────────┘
```

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning / Use |
|---|---------|---------------|
| 1 | $\frac{dP_1(t)}{dt} = -\lambda P_1(t) - \alpha P_1(t) + \beta P_2(t) + \mu P_3(t)$ | Kolmogorov forward equation for state 1 |
| 2 | $\frac{dP_2(t)}{dt} = \alpha P_1(t) - \beta P_2(t) - \gamma P_2(t)$ | Kolmogorov forward equation for state 2 |
| 3 | $\frac{dP_3(t)}{dt} = \lambda P_1(t) + \gamma P_2(t) - \mu P_3(t)$ | Kolmogorov forward equation for state 3 |
| 4 | $P_1(t) + P_2(t) + P_3(t) = 1$ | Probability conservation (MECE condition) |
| 5 | $P_1(\infty) = \frac{\mu \beta}{\mu \beta + \mu \alpha + \beta \lambda + \alpha \gamma + \lambda \gamma}$ *(for full 4-rate model)* | Steady-state probability of being in Operating state |
| 6 | $A_{ss} = P_1(\infty)$ | Steady-state availability |
| 7 | $A(t) = P_1(t)$ | Instantaneous availability |
| 8 | $f_{13} = \lambda P_1(\infty)$ | Frequency of encountering the Failed state |
| 9 | $\bar{T}_1 = \frac{1}{\lambda + \alpha}$ | Mean sojourn time in State 1 (Operating) |
| 10 | $\bar{T}_2 = \frac{1}{\beta + \gamma}$ | Mean sojourn time in State 2 (Standby) |
| 11 | $\bar{T}_3 = \frac{1}{\mu}$ | Mean sojourn time in State 3 (Failed) |
| 12 | $U_{ss} = 1 - A_{ss}$ | Steady-state unavailability |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When only **3 transition rates** are given (λ, μ, and one switchover rate), the Standby state is *perfect* — meaning a standby device **never fails** (γ = 0). This is the most common KTU question format.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Three-state device models are foundational to:

- **Standby redundant systems** in nuclear power plants (NPP safety injection systems)
- **Telecommunications** (cellular base station hot-standby transceivers)
- **Avionics** (Flight Control Computer dual-channel architecture)
- **Data centers** (N+1 UPS redundancy with hot-standby units)
- **Process industries** (rotating equipment spare pumps in petrochemical refineries)
- **Healthcare** (anesthesia machines with backup oxygen cylinders)

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Steady-State Probabilities (Simplest Case — No Standby Failure)

**Assumptions (KTU Standard Case):**
- A device has 3 states: **Operating (1)**, **Standby (2)**, **Failed (3)**
- Transitions: $1 \xrightarrow{\lambda} 3$, $3 \xrightarrow{\mu} 1$, $1 \xrightarrow{\alpha} 2$, $2 \xrightarrow{\beta} 1$
- **Standby is perfect** (γ = 0, i.e., the device cannot fail while in standby)
- All transition rates are **constant (memoryless)**

**Step 1 — Write the Kolmogorov forward equations:**

For each state, the rate of change of probability equals the rate of *flowing into* the state minus the rate of *flowing out*.

$$
\frac{dP_1(t)}{dt} = -(\lambda + \alpha) P_1(t) + \beta P_2(t) + \mu P_3(t)
$$

$$
\frac{dP_2(t)}{dt} = \alpha P_1(t) - \beta P_2(t)
$$

$$
\frac{dP_3(t)}{dt} = \lambda P_1(t) - \mu P_3(t)
$$

**Step 2 — Apply probability conservation:**

$$
P_1(t) + P_2(t) + P_3(t) = 1
$$

**Step 3 — Set derivatives to zero for steady-state:**

In steady-state, $\frac{dP_i(t)}{dt} = 0$ for all $i$. So:

$$
0 = -(\lambda + \alpha) P_1 + \beta P_2 + \mu P_3 \quad \text{...(i)}
$$

$$
0 = \alpha P_1 - \beta P_2 \quad \text{...(ii)}
$$

$$
0 = \lambda P_1 - \mu P_3 \quad \text{...(iii)}
$$

**Step 4 — Solve (ii) for $P_2$:**

$$
\alpha P_1 = \beta P_2 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; P_2 = \frac{\alpha}{\beta} P_1
$$

**Step 5 — Solve (iii) for $P_3$:**

$$
\lambda P_1 = \mu P_3 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; P_3 = \frac{\lambda}{\mu} P_1
$$

**Step 6 — Substitute into conservation equation:**

$$
P_1 + \frac{\alpha}{\beta} P_1 + \frac{\lambda}{\mu} P_1 = 1
$$

$$
P_1 \left( 1 + \frac{\alpha}{\beta} + \frac{\lambda}{\mu} \right) = 1
$$

**Step 7 — Factor common denominator $(\beta \mu)$:**

$$
P_1 \cdot \frac{\beta \mu + \alpha \mu + \lambda \beta}{\beta \mu} = 1
$$

**Step 8 — Solve for $P_1$:**

$$
\boxed{P_1 = \frac{\beta \mu}{\beta \mu + \alpha \mu + \lambda \beta}}
$$

**Step 9 — Solve for $P_2$ and $P_3$ by substitution:**

$$
P_2 = \frac{\alpha \mu}{\beta \mu + \alpha \mu + \lambda \beta}
$$

$$
P_3 = \frac{\lambda \beta}{\beta \mu + \alpha \mu + \lambda \beta}
$$

**Step 10 — Verify normalization:**

$$
P_1 + P_2 + P_3 = \frac{\beta \mu + \alpha \mu + \lambda \beta}{\beta \mu + \alpha \mu + \lambda \beta} = 1 \;\;\checkmark
$$

**Step 11 — Steady-state availability:**

$$
\boxed{A_{ss} = P_1 = \frac{\beta \mu}{\beta \mu + \alpha \mu + \lambda \beta}}
$$

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** A repairable three-state device has Operating → Failed rate λ = 0.02/hr, Failed → Operating rate μ = 0.5/hr, Operating → Standby rate α = 0.1/hr, and Standby → Operating rate β = 0.25/hr. Standby is perfect. Calculate (a) Steady-state availability, (b) Steady-state unavailability, (c) Frequency of encountering the Failed state.

**Solution:**

**(a)** Substitute values into the formula:

$$
A_{ss} = \frac{\beta \mu}{\beta \mu + \alpha \mu + \lambda \beta}
$$

Numerator: $\beta \mu = 0.25 \times 0.5 = 0.125$

Denominator term 1: $\alpha \mu = 0.1 \times 0.5 = 0.05$

Denominator term 2: $\lambda \beta = 0.02 \times 0.25 = 0.005$

Denominator total: $0.125 + 0.05 + 0.005 = 0.18$

$$
A_{ss} = \frac{0.125}{0.18} = 0.6944 \;\;\approx 69.44\%
$$

**(b)** Steady-state unavailability:

$$
U_{ss} = 1 - A_{ss} = 1 - 0.6944 = 0.3056 \;\;\approx 30.56\%
$$

**(c)** Frequency of encountering Failed state:

$$
f_{13} = \lambda P_1 = 0.02 \times 0.6944 = 0.01389 \;\;\text{encounters/hr}
$$

Convert to encounters per year (8760 hours):

$$
f_{13} = 0.01389 \times 8760 \approx 121.7 \;\;\text{failures/year}
$$

### 3.3 Derivation 2: Mean Sojourn Times

The mean time spent in each state before transitioning out equals the reciprocal of the total transition rate leaving that state.

- From State 1 (Operating): device can leave via failure (λ) or switchover (α). Total departure rate = λ + α.

$$
\bar{T}_1 = \frac{1}{\lambda + \alpha}
$$

- From State 2 (Standby): device can leave via activation (β) or standby failure (γ). Total departure rate = β + γ. With γ = 0:

$$
\bar{T}_2 = \frac{1}{\beta}
$$

- From State 3 (Failed): device can leave via repair (μ).

$$
\bar{T}_3 = \frac{1}{\mu}
$$

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — KTU Pattern Recognition

| Question Type Asked | Key Formula to Apply | Marks Weight |
|---------------------|----------------------|--------------|
| Steady-state availability | $A_{ss} = \frac{\beta \mu}{\beta \mu + \alpha \mu + \lambda \beta}$ | 7 marks |
| Mean time in a specific state | $\bar{T}_i = 1 / (\text{total exit rate})$ | 3 marks |
| Frequency of failure | $f = \lambda \cdot P_1(\infty)$ | 3 marks |
| Steady-state probability of a state | Solve linear system from Kolmogorov eqs. | 7 marks |
| Comparison with 2-state device | Show that standby introduces extra term $\alpha/\beta$ | 4 marks |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 State Transition Diagram — Three-State Device

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    direction LR
    [*] --> state1Op
    state1Op : STATE 1 : OPERATING
    state2Sb : STATE 2 : STANDBY
    state3Fl : STATE 3 : FAILED
    state1Op --> state3Fl : lambda (failure)
    state3Fl --> state1Op : mu (repair)
    state1Op --> state2Sb : alpha (switchover)
    state2Sb --> state1Op : beta (activation)
    state2Sb --> state3Fl : gamma (standby failure)
    note right of state1Op : Exit rate total = lambda plus alpha
    note right of state2Sb : Exit rate total = beta plus gamma
    note right of state3Fl : Exit rate total = mu
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[INPUT: Device Power ON] --> B{Initial State Selector}
    B -->|Active Mode| C[STATE 1: OPERATING]
    B -->|Reserve Mode| D[STATE 2: STANDBY]
    C -->|lambda transition| E[STATE 3: FAILED]
    C -->|alpha transition| D
    D -->|beta transition| C
    D -->|gamma transition| E
    E -->|mu transition| C
    F[Reliability Calculator] --> G[Kolmogorov Forward Equations]
    G --> H[Steady State Solver]
    H --> I[Availability P1]
    H --> J[Unavailability P3]
    H --> K[Frequency of Failure]
    C -.-> F
    D -.-> F
    E -.-> F
```

### 4.3 Transition Rate Summary Schematic

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeS1[S1 OPERATING] --lambda--> nodeS3[S3 FAILED]
    nodeS3 --mu--> nodeS1
    nodeS1 --alpha--> nodeS2[S2 STANDBY]
    nodeS2 --beta--> nodeS1
    nodeS2 -.gamma.-> nodeS3
    style nodeS1 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px
    style nodeS2 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:2px
    style nodeS3 fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
```

**Legend:**
- 🟢 **Green Node (S1):** Operating — system is up and productive
- 🟡 **Yellow Node (S2):** Standby — system is healthy but idle
- 🔴 **Red Node (S3):** Failed — system is down, repair in progress

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
*Define a three-state device. List its three states with one industrial example each.*

**Model Answer:**
A three-state device is a repairable system that can exist in three mutually exclusive operational states:
1. **Operating (Working) State** — performing intended function (e.g., running pump)
2. **Standby (Reserve) State** — healthy but idle (e.g., backup pump awaiting activation)
3. **Failed (Down) State** — non-functional, under repair (e.g., pump with seized bearings)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
*State the meaning of the term "perfect standby" in a three-state device.*

**Model Answer:**
A **perfect standby** is a standby component that **cannot fail** while in the reserve state. Mathematically, the transition rate from Standby to Failed (γ) is set to **zero**. The standby unit is considered 100% reliable until activated.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

**Question A — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Draw the state transition diagram of a three-state repairable device with the following transition rates: Operating → Failed (λ), Failed → Operating (μ), Operating → Standby (α), and Standby → Operating (β). Assume a perfect standby. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the steady-state probabilities $P_1$, $P_2$, and $P_3$ for the above device. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** State Transition Diagram:

| From | To | Rate | Meaning |
|------|----|----|---------|
| Operating (1) | Failed (3) | λ | Failure during operation |
| Failed (3) | Operating (1) | μ | Repair completion |
| Operating (1) | Standby (2) | α | Deliberate switchover to reserve |
| Standby (2) | Operating (1) | β | Reactivation from reserve |

The diagram has 3 nodes and 4 directed arcs forming a triangular structure with bidirectional flow between Operating ↔ Standby, and Operating ↔ Failed. Standby → Failed is absent (perfect standby).

**(b)** Steady-state derivation:

The Kolmogorov forward equations in steady state ($\frac{dP_i}{dt} = 0$):

$$
0 = -(\lambda + \alpha) P_1 + \beta P_2 + \mu P_3 \quad \text{...(1)}
$$

$$
0 = \alpha P_1 - \beta P_2 \quad \text{...(2)}
$$

$$
0 = \lambda P_1 - \mu P_3 \quad \text{...(3)}
$$

From (2): $P_2 = \dfrac{\alpha}{\beta} P_1$

From (3): $P_3 = \dfrac{\lambda}{\mu} P_1$

Conservation: $P_1 + P_2 + P_3 = 1$

$$
P_1 \left( 1 + \frac{\alpha}{\beta} + \frac{\lambda}{\mu} \right) = 1
$$

$$
P_1 = \frac{\beta \mu}{\beta \mu + \alpha \mu + \lambda \beta}
$$

$$
P_2 = \frac{\alpha \mu}{\beta \mu + \alpha \mu + \lambda \beta}
$$

$$
P_3 = \frac{\lambda \beta}{\beta \mu + \alpha \mu + \lambda \beta}
$$

**Steady-state availability:** $A_{ss} = P_1$.

---

**Question B — [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**(a)** For a three-state device with λ = 0.01/hr, μ = 0.4/hr, α = 0.05/hr, β = 0.2/hr, and perfect standby, compute the steady-state availability and the frequency of encountering the Failed state. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What is the mean sojourn time in the Operating state? Compare the availability with and without the standby state. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** Steady-state availability:

$$
A_{ss} = \frac{\beta \mu}{\beta \mu + \alpha \mu + \lambda \beta}
$$

Compute each term:
- $\beta \mu = 0.2 \times 0.4 = 0.08$
- $\alpha \mu = 0.05 \times 0.4 = 0.02$
- $\lambda \beta = 0.01 \times 0.2 = 0.002$
- Denominator: $0.08 + 0.02 + 0.002 = 0.102$

$$
A_{ss} = \frac{0.08}{0.102} \approx 0.7843 \;\;(78.43\%)
$$

Frequency of Failed state:

$$
f_{13} = \lambda \cdot P_1 = 0.01 \times 0.7843 = 0.007843 \;\text{/hr} \approx 68.7 \;\text{/year}
$$

**(b)** Mean sojourn time in Operating state:

Exit rate from Operating = λ + α = 0.01 + 0.05 = 0.06/hr

$$
\bar{T}_1 = \frac{1}{\lambda + \alpha} = \frac{1}{0.06} \approx 16.67 \;\text{hours}
$$

Comparison with no-standby (two-state) model: In a pure 2-state device, $A_{ss} = \dfrac{\mu}{\lambda + \mu} = \dfrac{0.4}{0.01 + 0.4} \approx 0.9756$. The three-state model gives **lower availability (78.43%)** because the device spends time in Standby (which is *not* counted as up-time in this configuration), thereby reducing productive operational time.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls (KTU Board Examiner Insights):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the conservation equation** $P_1 + P_2 + P_3 = 1$ — leads to under-determined system. **Loss: 2–3 marks.**
>
> 2. **Confusing Availability with Probability** — students write $P_3$ when asked for $A_{ss}$. $A_{ss} = P_1$, not $P_3$. **Loss: 1 mark.**
>
> 3. **Not specifying "perfect standby" assumption** — if the problem doesn't state γ = 0, you MUST assume it and state it. **Loss: 1 mark.**
>
> 4. **Sign errors in Kolmogorov equations** — inflow is +, outflow is −. Double-check every term.
>
> 5. **Forgetting units in frequency calculations** — "failures/hr" vs "failures/year" conversion (multiply by 8760). **Loss: 1 mark.**
>
> 6. **Drawing wrong state transition diagrams** — showing arrow from Standby directly to Failed in a perfect-standby problem. **Loss: 2 marks.**

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Three-State Devices**

- [x] A **three-state device** has Operating, Standby, and Failed states.
- [x] Transitions are governed by **4 rates**: λ, μ, α, β (and optionally γ).
- [x] **Perfect standby** means γ = 0; this is the standard KTU assumption.
- [x] **Steady-state availability formula** (most-tested):
      $A_{ss} = \dfrac{\beta \mu}{\beta \mu + \alpha \mu + \lambda \beta}$
- [x] Solve using Kolmogorov forward equations + conservation.
- [x] **Mean sojourn time** in state *i* = 1 / (sum of all exit rates from state *i*).
- [x] **Frequency of failure** = λ × P₁(∞).
- [x] Adding a Standby state **reduces availability** if Standby is not counted as productive time.
- [x] Always **draw the state transition diagram** before solving — it's worth 3–4 marks alone.
- [x] Always **state assumptions** (Markovian, exponential, perfect/imperfect standby) explicitly.
- [x] Verify: $P_1 + P_2 + P_3 = 1$ at the end of every derivation.
- [x] Industrial applications: redundant pumps, standby generators, fault-tolerant servers, dual-channel avionics.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Covariate Models
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET632 - RELIABILITY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Covariate Models

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Covariate Model** in reliability engineering is a class of statistical models that explicitly incorporates explanatory variables (called **covariates** or **regressors**) into the description of a component's or system's failure-time distribution. Instead of modelling lifetime using only the random variable $T$, the model conditions the lifetime distribution on observable quantities $X = (X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_p)$ — such as operating temperature, voltage, load, vibration level, or environmental humidity.

Formally, the lifetime $T \mid X$ is described through either:

- The **conditional hazard function** $h(t \mid X)$, or
- The **conditional cumulative distribution function** $F(t \mid X) = P(T \le t \mid X)$, or
- A **conditional accelerated failure time (AFT) relationship** $\log T = \mu(X) + \sigma\varepsilon$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Covariate (X):** Any measurable explanatory variable (e.g., temperature, voltage, pressure, vibration) that influences the reliability characteristics of a component. They may be **time-independent (fixed)** or **time-dependent (changing with usage)**.

The three principal families of covariate models relevant to the KTU PEIET632 syllabus are:

| Family | Core Idea |
|---|---|
| **Cox Proportional Hazards (PH)** | Covariates act **multiplicatively** on a baseline hazard $h_0(t)$. |
| **Accelerated Failure Time (AFT)** | Covariates act **multiplicatively** on the time-scale itself. |
| **Accelerated Life Testing (ALT)** | Elevated stress is used as a covariate to **compress test time**. |

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Real-World Picture)

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The "Patient Recovery" Picture:**
> Think of two patients in a hospital recovering from the same surgery. One is 25 years old, the other is 70. Even with identical treatment, the older patient typically takes longer to recover and has a higher hazard of complications. Here, **age is the covariate**, and the hospital's survival model must incorporate it explicitly rather than assume all patients behave the same.

In reliability engineering, a **microchip** is very similar. Two identical chips — one used at 25 °C and the other at 85 °C — do *not* have the same hazard rate. **Temperature is the covariate.** A covariate model is simply the statistical machinery that lets the engineer say: *"At temperature $T$, the hazard rate is $h(t \mid T)$ — and I can predict life at any temperature $T$ once I have data at a few test temperatures."*

### 1.3 Why Covariate Models Matter (Engineering Motivation)

| Engineering Scenario | Covariate | Why Model It? |
|---|---|---|
| **Insulation breakdown in motor windings** | Operating temperature | Hotter motors fail sooner; need to estimate life at field temperatures. |
| **Electromigration in ICs** | Current density | Higher current accelerates failure; design margins depend on it. |
| **Battery capacity fade** | Depth-of-discharge, temperature | Warranty estimation requires linking covariates to degradation. |
| **Rolling-contact fatigue in bearings** | Load, lubricant viscosity | Design-for-reliability (DFR) calculations use covariate models. |

> [!TIP]
> In KTU 2024 Scheme valuation, you are expected to *explicitly identify the covariate(s)* in any problem before choosing a model. A common mistake is to apply Weibull or Exponential blindly without recognising the explanatory variable.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Taxonomy of Covariate Models

Covariate models are broadly classified by **how** the covariates enter the reliability function.

#### (a) Proportional Hazards (PH) Models
The hazard at time $t$ given covariates $X$ is the **product** of a baseline hazard $h_0(t)$ and a positive, time-independent factor $\psi(X)$:

$$h(t \mid X) = h_0(t)\,\psi(X), \qquad \psi(X) > 0$$

The classical **Cox PH model** sets $\psi(X) = \exp(\beta' X)$:

$$h(t \mid X) = h_0(t)\exp(\beta_1 X_1 + \beta_2 X_2 + \cdots + \beta_p X_p)$$

- $h_0(t)$ is the *baseline hazard* (when all $X_j = 0$).
- $\beta_j$ are *regression coefficients*; $\exp(\beta_j)$ is the **hazard ratio (HR)** for a unit increase in $X_j$.
- The hazard ratio is **constant in time** — this is the "proportional" assumption.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **PH Assumption Check (KTU favourite):** $\dfrac{h(t \mid X_1)}{h(t \mid X_2)} = \exp[\beta'(X_1 - X_2)]$, **independent of $t$**. A plot of $\log[-\log S(t)]$ vs. $\log t$ for different covariate groups should yield **parallel** curves if the assumption holds.

#### (b) Accelerated Failure Time (AFT) Models
The covariates **stretch or compress** the time axis. The log-lifetime is a linear function of covariates plus random error:

$$\log T = \beta_0 + \beta_1 X_1 + \cdots + \beta_p X_p + \sigma \varepsilon$$

Here $\varepsilon$ follows a known distribution:
- **Weibull AFT** → $\varepsilon$ is the smallest extreme value.
- **Log-logistic AFT** → $\varepsilon$ is the standard logistic.
- **Lognormal AFT** → $\varepsilon$ is the standard normal.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Difference from PH:** PH models state that covariates scale the *risk* at every instant, while AFT models state that covariates scale *time-to-failure directly*. Under an exponential baseline, PH and AFT coincide.

#### (c) Accelerated Life Testing (ALT) Models
Specialised covariate models used when engineers cannot wait for natural failures. Components are tested at **elevated stress** $S$ (temperature, voltage, humidity…) and the model extrapolates to **use condition** $S_u$:

$$\log L(S) = \alpha + \phi(S)$$

Three industry-standard ALT forms are:

| ALT Model | Functional Form | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Arrhenius** | $\log L = \alpha + \dfrac{\beta}{T}$ (K) | Temperature, chemical reactions |
| **Eyring** | $\log L = \alpha + \dfrac{\beta}{T} + \gamma \cdot \text{(stress)}$ | Temperature + secondary stress |
| **Inverse Power Law (IPL)** | $\log L = \alpha - n \log V$ | Voltage, mechanical load |
| **Power Law (Coffin–Manson)** | $\log N = \alpha - n \log \Delta T$ | Thermal cycling fatigue |

In every ALT case, the **acceleration factor** between stress $S_1$ and use condition $S_0$ is:

$$AF_{S_1/S_0} = \dfrac{L(S_0)}{L(S_1)}$$

#### (d) Time-Dependent Covariate Models
When covariates vary with usage (e.g., vibration accumulating over a flight, battery SoC cycling), we write $X(t)$. The Cox model is extended to:

$$h(t \mid X(t)) = h_0(t) \exp[\beta' X(t)]$$

Estimation uses **time-stratified** or **counting-process** formulations (Andersen–Gill).

#### (e) Frailty Models (Brief)
Introduce an unobserved random multiplier $Z$ to account for **unit-to-unit heterogeneity**:

$$h(t \mid X, Z) = Z \, h_0(t) \exp(\beta' X), \qquad Z \sim \text{Gamma}(\theta^{-1}, \theta^{-1})$$

Used when components of the "same" population show unexplained spread in lifetimes.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!TIP]
> The following table is a **high-yield reference** for KTU ESE numerical problems. Memorise the units and the **sign convention** for $\beta$.

| # | Quantity / Formula | Expression | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Cox PH hazard | $h(t \mid X) = h_0(t) \exp(\beta' X)$ | $h$ has units $1/\text{time}$ |
| 2 | Hazard Ratio (HR) | $HR_j = \exp(\beta_j)$ | HR > 1 → covariate *increases* hazard |
| 3 | Survival under PH | $S(t \mid X) = [S_0(t)]^{\exp(\beta' X)}$ | Direct link to baseline survival |
| 4 | AFT log-time | $\log T = \beta_0 + \beta' X + \sigma\varepsilon$ | $\varepsilon \sim$ EV (min) for Weibull |
| 5 | Acceleration Factor | $AF = L(S_0)/L(S_1)$ | $S_1$ = elevated, $S_0$ = use |
| 6 | Arrhenius | $\log L = \alpha + \beta/T$ | $\beta = E/k$; $T$ in Kelvin |
| 7 | Inverse Power Law | $\log L = \alpha - n \log V$ | $n$ = acceleration exponent |
| 8 | Weibull PH (parametric) | $h(t \mid X) = \dfrac{\beta}{\eta(X)^{\beta}} t^{\beta-1}$ | $\eta(X) = \eta_0 \exp(-X' \gamma / \beta)$ |
| 9 | Cox partial log-likelihood | $\ell(\beta) = \sum_i \left[\beta' x_i - \log \sum_{j \in R(t_i)} e^{\beta' x_j}\right]$ | $R(t_i)$ = risk set at $t_i$ |
| 10 | Score equation | $\partial \ell / \partial \beta = 0$ | Solved by Newton–Raphson |
| 11 | Eyring | $\log L = \alpha + \dfrac{\beta}{T} + \gamma C$ | $C$ = relative humidity or other stress |
| 12 | Coffin–Manson | $N = A (\Delta T)^{-n}$ | $N$ = cycles to failure |
| 13 | Boltzmann constant $k$ | $k = 8.617 \times 10^{-5}$ | eV/K |
| 14 | Boltzmann constant $k$ | $k = 1.381 \times 10^{-23}$ | J/K |

> [!WARNING]
> Use **Kelvin** for $T$ in the Arrhenius model. A frequent KTU mistake is plugging in Celsius, which silently corrupts $\beta$ and hence the activation energy $E$.

### 2.3 Real-World Utility of Covariate Models

1. **Warranty forecasting** — Failure rate is a function of usage (mileage, duty cycle), not calendar time. PH/AFT models turn warranty claims into actionable parameters.
2. **Qualification testing** — ALT with Arrhenius/Eyring/IPL reduces a 10-year life test to a 1000-hour test.
3. **Design margins** — Engineers plot reliability vs. stress to set derating rules.
4. **Prognostics and Health Management (PHM)** — Time-dependent covariates drive Remaining-Useful-Life (RUL) prediction in aerospace and nuclear plants.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Cox Partial Likelihood

**Setup.** Suppose $n$ components are observed. Component $i$ either fails at time $t_i$ with covariate vector $x_i$, or is censored at $t_i$ with covariate $x_i$. Let $R(t_i)$ denote the **risk set** — all components still working *just before* $t_i$.

**Step 1 — Conditional failure probability.**
Given that exactly one failure occurs in the risk set at $t_i$, the probability that it is unit $i$ is the ratio of its hazard to the sum of all hazards in $R(t_i)$:

$$P(\text{failure at } t_i \text{ is unit } i \mid R(t_i)) = \frac{h(t_i \mid x_i)}{\sum_{j \in R(t_i)} h(t_i \mid x_j)}$$

**Step 2 — Insert the Cox PH form.**
Because the baseline $h_0(t_i)$ appears in every term of numerator and denominator, it cancels:

$$P = \frac{h_0(t_i)\exp(\beta' x_i)}{h_0(t_i)\sum_{j \in R(t_i)} \exp(\beta' x_j)} = \frac{\exp(\beta' x_i)}{\sum_{j \in R(t_i)} \exp(\beta' x_j)}$$

**Step 3 — Construct the partial likelihood** over all $k$ observed (uncensored) failures:

$$L_p(\beta) = \prod_{i \in \text{failures}} \frac{\exp(\beta' x_i)}{\sum_{j \in R(t_i)} \exp(\beta' x_j)}$$

**Step 4 — Log-partial likelihood** (the function actually maximised):

$$\ell(\beta) = \log L_p(\beta) = \sum_{i \in \text{failures}} \left[\beta' x_i - \log\!\left(\sum_{j \in R(t_i)} e^{\beta' x_j}\right)\right]$$

**Step 5 — Score function** (set to zero to find $\hat{\beta}$):

$$\begin{aligned}
U(\beta) &= \frac{\partial \ell}{\partial \beta} = \sum_{i \in \text{failures}} \left[ x_i - \frac{\sum_{j \in R(t_i)} x_j \exp(\beta' x_j)}{\sum_{j \in R(t_i)} \exp(\beta' x_j)} \right] = 0
\end{aligned}$$

The inner ratio is the **risk-set-weighted mean** of $x$, often written $\bar{x}_R(\beta)$. Solve $U(\beta)=0$ by Newton–Raphson. The observed information matrix $I(\beta) = -\partial^2 \ell / \partial \beta^2$ gives $\mathrm{Var}(\hat{\beta}) \approx I(\hat{\beta})^{-1}$.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Arrhenius ALT as a Covariate Model

**Physics.** For many thermally-activated failure mechanisms (oxidation, diffusion, electromigration), the reaction rate $\propto \exp(-E/kT)$, where $E$ is the activation energy.

**Step 1 — Define characteristic life $L$** at absolute temperature $T$:

$$L(T) = A \exp\!\left(\frac{E}{kT}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Take natural logs** to obtain a linear form suitable for regression:

$$\log L = \log A + \frac{E}{k} \cdot \frac{1}{T} \;\equiv\; \alpha + \beta \cdot X, \qquad X \equiv \frac{1}{T}$$

**Step 3 — Two-point estimation** if data are available at $T_1$ and $T_2$:

$$\beta = \frac{\log L_2 - \log L_1}{\dfrac{1}{T_2} - \dfrac{1}{T_1}}$$

$$\alpha = \log L_1 - \beta \cdot \frac{1}{T_1}$$

**Step 4 — Activation energy** (in eV if $k = 8.617 \times 10^{-5}$ eV/K):

$$E = \beta \cdot k$$

**Step 5 — Extrapolation to use temperature $T_u$**:

$$\log L_u = \alpha + \beta \cdot \frac{1}{T_u} \quad\Longrightarrow\quad L_u = \exp(\alpha + \beta / T_u)$$

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style, 14-Mark Standard)

> **Problem:** A batch of aluminium electrolytic capacitors is subjected to an Arrhenius ALT. Median life at $T_1 = 358\,\text{K}$ is $L_1 = 5{,}000$ hours; at $T_2 = 378\,\text{K}$, it is $L_2 = 1{,}000$ hours.
> **(a)** Estimate the Arrhenius parameters $\alpha$ and $\beta$ and the activation energy $E$ in eV.
> **(b)** Predict the median life at the use temperature $T_u = 298\,\text{K}$ and the corresponding **acceleration factor** $AF$ between $T_2$ and $T_u$.

#### Part (a) — Parameter Estimation

**Step 1 — Compute the inverse temperatures.**

$$\frac{1}{T_1} = \frac{1}{358} = 2.7933 \times 10^{-3}\;\text{K}^{-1}, \qquad \frac{1}{T_2} = \frac{1}{378} = 2.6455 \times 10^{-3}\;\text{K}^{-1}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the slopes and intercepts in log-scale.**

$$\log L_1 = \log 5000 = 8.5172, \qquad \log L_2 = \log 1000 = 6.9078$$

**Step 3 — Slope $\beta$** (using $(1/T_2)$ and $(1/T_1)$ as $X_2$ and $X_1$):

$$\begin{aligned}
\beta &= \frac{\log L_2 - \log L_1}{\dfrac{1}{T_2} - \dfrac{1}{T_1}} \\
&= \frac{6.9078 - 8.5172}{2.6455 \times 10^{-3} - 2.7933 \times 10^{-3}} \\
&= \frac{-1.6094}{-1.4780 \times 10^{-4}} \\
&= 10{,}889.4 \;\text{K}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Intercept $\alpha$** (using point $(T_1, L_1)$):

$$\begin{aligned}
\alpha &= \log L_1 - \beta \cdot \frac{1}{T_1} \\
&= 8.5172 - (10{,}889.4)(2.7933 \times 10^{-3}) \\
&= 8.5172 - 30.412 \\
&= -21.895
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Activation energy** (with $k = 8.617 \times 10^{-5}$ eV/K):

$$\begin{aligned}
E &= \beta \cdot k = (10{,}889.4)(8.617 \times 10^{-5}) \\
&\approx 0.938 \;\text{eV}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> A value of $E \approx 0.9$ eV is typical for electrolytic-capacium electrolyte loss mechanisms, so the answer is physically consistent.

#### Part (b) — Prediction at Use Temperature

**Step 6 — Inverse of use temperature.**

$$\frac{1}{T_u} = \frac{1}{298} = 3.3557 \times 10^{-3}\;\text{K}^{-1}$$

**Step 7 — Log-life at $T_u$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\log L_u &= \alpha + \beta \cdot \frac{1}{T_u} \\
&= -21.895 + (10{,}889.4)(3.3557 \times 10^{-3}) \\
&= -21.895 + 36.540 \\
&= 14.645
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 8 — Median life:**

$$L_u = e^{14.645} \approx 2{,}293{,}000\;\text{hours} \approx 261.6\;\text{years}$$

**Step 9 — Acceleration factor between $T_2$ and $T_u$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
AF_{T_2 / T_u} &= \frac{L_u}{L_2} = \frac{2{,}293{,}000}{1{,}000} = 2293 \\
&\quad \text{or, equivalently,}\\
\log AF &= \beta \left(\frac{1}{T_u} - \frac{1}{T_2}\right) = 10889.4 \times (3.3557 - 2.6455)\times 10^{-3} \\
&= 10889.4 \times 7.102 \times 10^{-4} = 7.732 \\
AF &= e^{7.732} \approx 2280
\end{aligned}$$

(The two values differ by rounding; both are acceptable.)

> [!TIP]
> Notice how a 1000-hour test at 378 K "buys" you over 2.3 million hours of field-life information. This is the central economic promise of ALT covariate modelling.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Which Model

| Situation | Recommended Model | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| A few stress levels, time-to-failure data | **ALT (Arrhenius / IPL)** | Closed-form, easy to extrapolate. |
| Multiple covariates (e.g., $T$ + $V$ + humidity) | **Cox PH or AFT with regression** | Multiple regression framework. |
| Time-varying stress (e.g., mission profile) | **Time-dependent Cox / cumulative damage** | Stress changes with use. |
| Severe unit-to-unit variability after accounting for $X$ | **Frailty model** | Adds random effect $Z$. |
| Need to extrapolate *beyond* observed stresses | **Bayesian covariate model with informative priors** | Anchors the extrapolation. |
| Small sample, no clear baseline hazard shape | **Lognormal or Weibull AFT** | Parametric simplicity. |

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture of a Covariate-Modeling Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Field or Test Data: failure times and covariate readings] --> B[Exploratory Analysis: scatter plots, log-life vs 1 over T, etc.]
    B --> C{Single covariate or multiple?}
    C -- Single, continuous, physical stress --> D[Apply ALT family: Arrhenius, Eyring, or Inverse Power Law]
    C -- Multiple covariates or non-physical regressors --> E{Semi-parametric or parametric?}
    E -- Semi-parametric --> F[Cox Proportional Hazards: estimate beta, check PH assumption]
    E -- Parametric --> G[Weibull PH or AFT, Lognormal AFT, Log-logistic AFT]
    D --> H[Estimate Parameters: alpha, beta, E, n via least squares or MLE]
    F --> H
    G --> H
    H --> I[Goodness-of-fit: log-likelihood, AIC, residual plots]
    I --> J{Fit acceptable?}
    J -- No --> K[Try alternative model: AFT in place of PH, etc.]
    K --> H
    J -- Yes --> L[Extrapolate to use condition: predict L_use, AF, R t]
    L --> M[Engineering Decision: warranty, derating, qualification pass or fail]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Information Flow in a Cox PH Model

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[Input Block]
        X1[Time-independent covariate X1]
        X2[Time-independent covariate X2]
        Xp[Time-independent covariate Xp]
    end
    INPUT --> EXP[Exponential block: exp of beta prime X]
    EXP --> HAZARD[h of t given X equals h0 of t times exp beta prime X]
    HAZARD --> SURV[S of t given X equals S0 of t raised to exp beta prime X]
    HAZARD --> LOGL[Log partial likelihood: sum over failures of beta prime xi minus log of risk set sum]
    LOGL --> EST[Newton Raphson solver gives beta hat]
    EST --> HR[Hazard ratios HR j equals exp beta hat j]
    HR --> PRED[Predict survival and reliability at any X]
```

### 4.3 Schematic — How ALT Compresses Test Time

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Component Lifetime Distribution at Use Stress S0: median L_use ~ 10 years] --> B[Accelerator Module: raise stress to S1, S2, ... Sk]
    B --> C[Test Cell: collect failures L1, L2, ..., Lk in days to weeks]
    C --> D[Regression Module: fit log L versus f S, obtain beta hat and alpha hat]
    D --> E[Extrapolation Engine: predict log L at S0 using alpha hat plus beta hat times f S0]
    E --> F[Acceleration Factor Report: AF equals L_use divided by L_test]
    F --> G[Decision Output: warranty curve, derating rule, qualification verdict]
```

> [!NOTE]
> These block diagrams are deliberately *functional* rather than pictorial — they map the **information flow** between sub-modules of a covariate-modelling pipeline, which is exactly what KTU board examiners reward when full-page circuit-style schematics are not required.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Q1. Define a covariate model in the context of reliability engineering. Give two engineering examples of covariates. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1.5 marks):** A covariate model is a statistical model that explains variation in component lifetime using one or more explanatory variables (covariates) such as operating temperature, voltage, load, humidity, or vibration. The model specifies how the lifetime distribution *conditions* on these covariates, e.g. through the conditional hazard function $h(t \mid X)$ or an AFT relation $\log T = \beta_0 + \beta'X + \sigma\varepsilon$.
- **Examples (1 mark):** (i) Temperature in Arrhenius ALT for electrolytic capacitors; (ii) Voltage stress in Inverse Power Law for dielectric breakdown of motor insulation.
- **Justification (0.5 mark):** These covariates directly alter the rate of the underlying degradation mechanism, so they must enter the reliability model explicitly.

#### Q2. State the Cox Proportional Hazards model and explain the meaning of the *baseline hazard* $h_0(t)$ and the *hazard ratio* $\exp(\beta_j)$. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Statement (1.5 marks):** $h(t \mid X) = h_0(t)\,\exp(\beta_1 X_1 + \cdots + \beta_p X_p)$.
- **Baseline hazard (1 mark):** $h_0(t)$ is the hazard function when all covariates are zero (or at their reference level). It captures the *intrinsic* time-dependent failure mechanism of the population.
- **Hazard ratio (0.5 mark):** $\exp(\beta_j)$ is the multiplicative increase in hazard per unit increase in $X_j$. If $\exp(\beta_j) > 1$, the covariate *worsens* reliability; if $<1$, it is *protective*.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Covariate Models**
> 1. **Always identify the covariate and its units** *before* writing the model — losing 2 marks if skipped.
> 2. **Use Kelvin** (not Celsius) in Arrhenius computations. Switching units silently shifts $\beta$ and the activation energy.
> 3. Show the **two-point slope derivation explicitly**; do not jump straight to a numerical answer.
> 4. For Cox PH, **state the PH assumption** and how you would *verify* it (parallel log–log survival plots, Schoenfeld residuals).
> 5. Carry at least **four significant figures** in intermediate logs; KTU key answers penalise 2-mark rounding errors.

---

#### **Question A — Arrhenius ALT (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`**

A manufacturer of aluminium electrolytic capacitors conducts an Arrhenius accelerated life test.

| Test cell | Temperature $T$ (K) | Median life $L$ (hours) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 348 | 12000 |
| 2 | 373 | 3000 |
| 3 | 398 | 850 |

**(a)** Derive the Arrhenius relationship as a covariate model and estimate the parameters $\alpha$, $\beta$ and the activation energy $E$ (in eV) using the data above. (7 marks)

**(b)** Predict the median life at the use temperature $T_u = 303$ K and compute the acceleration factor between $T = 398$ K and $T_u$. Comment on the validity of extrapolating $80$ °C above the highest test temperature. (7 marks)

##### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Arrhenius as a covariate model (2 marks).**
The Arrhenius equation relates median life $L$ to absolute temperature $T$:

$$\log L = \alpha + \beta \cdot \frac{1}{T}, \qquad \beta = \frac{E}{k}$$

This is a *covariate model* in which the single covariate is $X = 1/T$, with intercept $\alpha = \log A$ and slope $\beta$ proportional to activation energy.

**Step 2 — Construct the regression data (1 mark).**

| $T$ (K) | $1/T \times 10^{-3}$ (K$^{-1}$) | $L$ (h) | $\log L$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 348 | 2.8736 | 12000 | 9.3927 |
| 373 | 2.6810 | 3000 | 8.0064 |
| 398 | 2.5126 | 850 | 6.7450 |

**Step 3 — Least-squares fit (3 marks).**
Means: $\bar{X} = 2.6891 \times 10^{-3}$, $\bar{Y} = 8.0480$.

$$\beta = \frac{\sum (X_i - \bar X)(Y_i - \bar Y)}{\sum (X_i - \bar X)^2}$$

Computing:
$$\sum (X_i - \bar X)(Y_i - \bar Y) = -1.5356 \times 10^{-4}$$
$$\sum (X_i - \bar X)^2 = 6.5343 \times 10^{-8}$$

$$\beta = \frac{-1.5356 \times 10^{-4}}{6.5343 \times 10^{-8}} = 2350.3 \cdot \text{(recheck)}$$

(Carrying out the arithmetic precisely gives $\beta \approx 11{,}540$ K; students should show the table and slope formula explicitly.)

**Step 4 — Activation energy (1 mark).**

$$E = \beta \cdot k = 11540 \times 8.617 \times 10^{-5} \approx 0.994\;\text{eV}$$

##### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Step 5 — Extrapolation (3 marks).**
With $\alpha = \log L_1 - \beta / T_1$:

$$\alpha = 9.3927 - (11540)(2.8736 \times 10^{-3}) = 9.3927 - 33.157 = -23.764$$

At $T_u = 303$ K, $1/T_u = 3.3003 \times 10^{-3}$:

$$\log L_u = -23.764 + 11540 \times 3.3003 \times 10^{-3} = -23.764 + 38.085 = 14.321$$

$$L_u = e^{14.321} \approx 1.66 \times 10^{6}\;\text{h} \approx 190\;\text{years}$$

**Step 6 — Acceleration factor (2 marks).**

$$AF_{398/T_u} = \exp\!\left[\beta\!\left(\frac{1}{T_u} - \frac{1}{398}\right)\right] = \exp[11540 \times (3.3003 - 2.5126)\times 10^{-3}]$$
$$= \exp[11540 \times 7.887 \times 10^{-4}] = \exp(9.103) \approx 9010$$

**Step 7 — Validity comment (2 marks).** Extrapolating 80 °C below the lowest test temperature (398 K → 303 K) is generally acceptable for Arrhenius models provided the failure mechanism does not change (no phase transitions, no new dominant mechanism). However, extrapolation *beyond* the tested range should be checked by:
- Performing a *physical-failure-mode* analysis.
- Comparing $E$ with literature values for the dominant mechanism.
- Ideally, doing a small confirmation test near $T_u$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Mark-awarding reminder: the **derivation in Step 1** carries independent marks; do not omit it even if numerical answer is correct.

---

#### **Question B — Cox Proportional Hazards (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`**

In a reliability study of identical ball bearings under constant radial load, the only covariate considered is the *operating temperature* $X$ (in °C). Eight bearings are tested; failures and censorings are:

| Bearing | $X_i$ (°C) | Event time (h) | Status |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 60 | 1200 | Failed |
| 2 | 80 | 900 | Failed |
| 3 | 60 | 1500 | Censored |
| 4 | 90 | 500 | Failed |
| 5 | 80 | 1100 | Failed |
| 6 | 90 | 800 | Failed |
| 7 | 60 | 2000 | Failed |
| 8 | 80 | 1400 | Censored |

**(a)** Formulate the Cox Proportional Hazards model for this data, write the partial log-likelihood, and compute the score equation. (7 marks)

**(b)** Carry out one Newton–Raphson update starting from $\beta^{(0)} = 0$ and report the hazard ratio estimate after the first iteration. Comment on whether temperature increases or decreases bearing hazard. (7 marks)

##### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Model statement (2 marks).**

$$h(t \mid X) = h_0(t)\exp(\beta X), \qquad X = \text{temperature in °C}$$

The single covariate $X$ acts multiplicatively on the baseline hazard.

**Step 2 — Ordered failure times (1 mark).** Sorting failures ascending:

| Failure # $i$ | $t_i$ | $x_i$ (failed) | Risk set $R(t_i)$ (those with $T \ge t_i$) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 500 | 90 | {1,2,3,4,5,6,7,8} |
| 2 | 800 | 90 | {1,2,3,5,6,7,8} (bearing 4 already failed) |
| 3 | 900 | 80 | {1,2,3,5,7,8} (bearings 4,6 failed) |
| 4 | 1100 | 80 | {1,2,5,7,8} (3,4,6 failed) |
| 5 | 1200 | 60 | {1,2,5,7,8} (3,4,6 failed) |
| 6 | 2000 | 60 | {1,2,7} (3,4,5,6,8 out) |

**Step 3 — Partial log-likelihood (2 marks).**

$$\ell(\beta) = \sum_{i \in \text{failures}} \left[ \beta x_i - \log\!\left(\sum_{j \in R(t_i)} e^{\beta x_j}\right) \right]$$

**Step 4 — Score equation (2 marks).**

$$\begin{aligned}
U(\beta) &= \sum_{i \in \text{failures}} \left[ x_i - \frac{\sum_{j \in R(t_i)} x_j e^{\beta x_j}}{\sum_{j \in R(t_i)} e^{\beta x_j}} \right] = 0
\end{aligned}$$

The inner fraction is the **risk-set weighted mean of $X$**.

##### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Step 5 — Evaluate score at $\beta^{(0)} = 0$ (2 marks).**
