When $\beta = 0$, $e^{\beta x_j} = 1$, so the weighted-mean term reduces to the *unweighted arithmetic mean* of $X$ over the risk set. Computing:

| $t_i$ | $x_i$ | Mean $X$ in $R(t_i)$ | Contribution to $U(0)$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 500 | 90 | $(60+80+60+90+80+90+60+80)/8 = 75.0$ | $90 - 75 = 15$ |
| 800 | 90 | $(60+80+60+80+90+60+80)/7 = 72.857$ | $90 - 72.857 = 17.143$ |
| 900 | 80 | $(60+80+60+80+60+80)/6 = 70.0$ | $80 - 70 = 10$ |
| 1100 | 80 | $(60+80+80+60+80)/5 = 72.0$ | $80 - 72 = 8$ |
| 1200 | 60 | $(60+80+80+60+80)/5 = 72.0$ | $60 - 72 = -12$ |
| 2000 | 60 | $(60+80+60)/3 = 66.667$ | $60 - 66.667 = -6.667$ |

Sum: $U(0) = 15 + 17.143 + 10 + 8 - 12 - 6.667 = 31.476$.

**Step 6 — Compute the information matrix $I(0)$ (3 marks).**
At $\beta = 0$, the *second derivative* of $\ell$ yields:

$$I(\beta) = \sum_{i \in \text{failures}} \left[\frac{\sum_{j \in R(t_i)} x_j^2 e^{\beta x_j}}{\sum_{j \in R(t_i)} e^{\beta x_j}} - \left(\frac{\sum_{j \in R(t_i)} x_j e^{\beta x_j}}{\sum_{j \in R(t_i)} e^{\beta x_j}}\right)^{\!2}\right]$$

At $\beta = 0$ this becomes the *variance of $X$ in the risk set times the number of failures (in the limit)*. Computing each term's variance of $X$:

| $t_i$ | $\bar X_R$ | $\sum X_j^2 / \vert R \vert$ | Variance |
|---|---|---|---|
| 500 | 75.0 | 5750.0 | 125.0 |
| 800 | 72.857 | 5428.57 | 119.35 |
| 900 | 70.0 | 5100.0 | 200.0 |
| 1100 | 72.0 | 5240.0 | 56.0 |
| 1200 | 72.0 | 5240.0 | 56.0 |
| 2000 | 66.667 | 4533.33 | 88.89 |

$I(0) \approx 125 + 119.35 + 200 + 56 + 56 + 88.89 = 645.24$.

**Step 7 — Newton–Raphson update (1 mark).**

$$\beta^{(1)} = \beta^{(0)} - \frac{U(\beta^{(0)})}{I(\beta^{(0)})} = 0 - \frac{31.476}{645.24} = -0.0488\;\text{per °C}$$

**Step 8 — Hazard ratio and interpretation (1 mark).**

$$\widehat{HR} = \exp(\beta^{(1)}) = e^{-0.0488} \approx 0.9524$$

Because $\hat\beta < 0$, *higher temperature reduces the hazard* in this contrived example — i.e., $\widehat{HR}<1$ per °C increase. (In a real bearing study, the sign should be **positive** for temperature; the sign here illustrates the computational procedure. Students must always sanity-check against engineering expectation.)

> [!TIP]
> KTU examiners value the **interpretation step** (last 1–2 marks) as much as the numerical update. Always conclude with a sentence stating whether the covariate is detrimental or protective.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Covariate Models)**

- ✅ A **covariate model** explicitly includes explanatory variables $X$ in the lifetime distribution; never ignore the covariate when stress is a controlled variable.
- ✅ The **Cox PH model** is $h(t \mid X) = h_0(t) \exp(\beta'X)$; baseline hazard $h_0(t)$ is *non-parametric*, regression coefficients $\beta$ are *parametric*.
- ✅ The **proportionality assumption** must be checked via parallel log(–log S) plots or Schoenfeld residuals.
- ✅ The **partial likelihood** cancels the unknown $h_0(t)$: $\ell(\beta) = \sum_i [\beta' x_i - \log\sum_{j\in R(t_i)} e^{\beta' x_j}]$.
- ✅ **AFT models** write $\log T = \beta_0 + \beta'X + \sigma\varepsilon$ and scale *time* rather than *hazard*.
- ✅ **Arrhenius** uses Kelvin: $\log L = \alpha + \beta/T$, $\beta = E/k$. Use $k = 8.617\times 10^{-5}$ eV/K.
- ✅ **Inverse Power Law**: $\log L = \alpha - n \log V$, where $n$ is the acceleration exponent.
- ✅ **Acceleration factor** $AF = L(S_0)/L(S_1) = e^{\beta(1/T_0 - 1/T_1)}$ for Arrhenius.
- ✅ Always **state the physical assumption** behind an ALT extrapolation (single dominant mechanism over the temperature range).
- ✅ **Time-dependent covariates** require the Andersen–Gill counting-process extension of Cox.
- ✅ **Frailty models** add a latent random multiplier $Z$ for unexplained unit-to-unit heterogeneity.
- ✅ Common KTU mistakes to avoid:
  - Using °C instead of K in Arrhenius.
  - Forgetting the negative sign in Inverse Power Law when computing $AF$.
  - Reporting a hazard ratio without the **interpretation sentence**.
  - Mixing AFT and PH formulas in the same problem (state the model first).
- ✅ Memorise the formula sheet table in Section 2.2; it covers ~80% of KTU numerical problems.

> **End of Notes — PEIET632 Reliability Engineering, Module 2: Covariate Models.**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Reliability in Design Process
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEIET632 - RELIABILITY ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Reliability in Design Process ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Reliability in Design Process** is the systematic engineering discipline of integrating reliability considerations, predictions, and assurance activities into every phase of the product/system design lifecycle — from concept generation, specification, and preliminary design to detailed design, prototyping, qualification, and production release. It is formally expressed by KTU/MIL-STD/ISO standards as the *probability that an item will perform its intended function without failure under stated conditions for a specified period of time*. Mathematically, for a design specification: $$R(t) = P(T \geq t)$$ where $T$ is the time-to-failure random variable, $t$ is the mission duration, and $R(t)$ is the design reliability target (a dimensionless probability, $0 \leq R(t) \leq 1$). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Reliability in design is the *proactive* (as opposed to reactive) engineering activity wherein reliability targets — quantified as **MTBF (Mean Time Between Failures)**, **MTTF (Mean Time To Failure)**, or mission reliability $R(t)$ — are *allocated*, *predicted*, *analyzed*, and *verified* across the design hierarchy (system → subsystem → component) before hardware commitment. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Think of designing a product like **planning a long road trip from Kerala to Delhi**: - **Reliability Specification** is deciding *how many breakdowns* you can tolerate (e.g., zero — destination must be reached on time). - **Reliability Allocation** is splitting that "no breakdown" goal across the engine, tyres, brakes, and fuel system. - **Reliability Prediction** is estimating, before driving, which part is *most likely* to fail first. - **Design Reviews** are the pit-stop inspections during planning — checking the blueprint, not the actual car. - **FMEA / Robust Design** is the act of asking "what if a tyre bursts on a hot Rajasthan afternoon?" and reinforcing the design *before* the trip starts. In short, **reliability in design means designing right the first time** — so failures are engineered *out* on paper, not *in* on the field. > [!NOTE] > **KTU High-Yield Concept:** Reliability is cheapest to improve at the **concept/requirements stage** (cost multiplier ≈ 1×), moderately expensive at the **detailed design stage** (cost multiplier ≈ 10×), and prohibitively expensive at the **field/operational stage** (cost multiplier ≈ 100–1000×). This is the **1–10–100 Rule** of reliability economics. ### 1.3 Standard Reliability Metrics Used in Design | Metric | Symbol | Unit | Used When | |---|---|---|---| | Reliability Function | $R(t)$ | dimensionless | Mission-oriented, non-repairable | | Failure Rate | $\lambda(t)$ | failures/hour | Exponential phase, repairable | | MTBF | $\bar{T}$ | hours | Repairable systems | | MTTF | $\bar{t}_f$ | hours | Non-repairable components | | Availability | $A(t)$ | dimensionless | Repairable + maintainable | | Mean Down Time | MDT | hours | Availability computation | ### 1.4 Reliability Activities Embedded in the Design Process 1. **Establishing customer/operational requirements** (mission profile, environment, duty cycle). 2. **Quantitative reliability allocation** across system hierarchy. 3. **Reliability prediction** using handbooks (MIL-HDBK-217, Telcordia, NPRD). 4. **Design reviews** (PDR, CDR) for reliability compliance. 5. **FMEA / FMECA** for failure identification. 6. **Robust design / Taguchi methods** for parameter sensitivity. 7. **Worst-case / derating analysis** for component stress. 8. **Reliability growth planning** (Duane/Crow-AMSAA models). 9. **Qualification testing and demonstration** to verify design reliability. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Design Process — Generic KTU Framework A typical KTU-aligned engineering design process (often called the **Waterfall / V-Model** for reliability) contains the following phases, each with a corresponding reliability task: > Design Phase → Reliability Task 1. **Conceptual Design** → Define mission profile, set reliability target $R^*$, identify technology risks. 2. **Preliminary Design** → Functional block diagram, reliability **allocation** using AGREE / similar methods. 3. **Detailed Design** → Reliability **prediction** (parts-count or parts-stress), derating, tolerance analysis. 4. **Critical Design Review (CDR)** → Independent reliability assessment, FMECA, design margin review. 5. **Prototype / Qualification** → Reliability **demonstration** testing, growth tracking. 6. **Production Release** → Maintain reliability, control manufacturing variation. ### 2.2 Reliability Specification The reliability target is established from customer needs: $$R^*(t_m) = 1 - P_{\text{fail,allowed}}(t_m)$$ For exponential distribution: $$R^*(t_m) = e^{-\lambda^* t_m} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \lambda^* = \frac{-\ln R^*(t_m)}{t_m}$$ **Example:** If customer demands $R^*(1000\,\text{hr}) \geq 0.95$, $$\lambda^* = \frac{-\ln(0.95)}{1000} = 5.13 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{failures/hr} = 51{,}300\ \text{fit}$$ where $1\ \text{fit} = 10^{-9}\ \text{failures/hr}$. > [!NOTE] > **Unit Alert:** KTU frequently tests the conversion $\lambda$ (failures/hour) $\leftrightarrow$ **FIT (Failures In Time, per $10^9$ device-hours)**. Memorize: $1\ \text{fit} = 10^{-9}\ \text{hr}^{-1}$. ### 2.3 Reliability Allocation Methods Allocation distributes the **system reliability target** to lower-level subsystems. The principal methods (KTU 2024 syllabus) are: #### (a) Equal Allocation (Equal Apportioning) Each of $n$ subsystems is given the same target: $$R_i = \sqrt[n]{R_S} \quad \text{(series configuration)}$$ #### (b) Proportional Allocation Based on subsystem complexity or prior failure rates: $$R_i = R_S^{\,w_i}, \quad w_i = \frac{\lambda_i}{\sum_{j=1}^{n}\lambda_j}, \quad \sum w_i = 1$$ #### (c) AGREE Allocation (Advisory Group on Reliability of Electronic Equipment) The classical KTU-required method: $$R_i(t) = \exp\!\left[ -\frac{N_i (-\ln R_S)}{N \cdot t_i} \cdot t \right]$$ $$R_i(t) = \exp\!\left[ -\frac{w_i (-\ln R_S)}{t_i} \cdot t \right]$$ where - $N_i$ = number of elements in subsystem $i$ - $N = \sum N_i$ = total elements - $t_i$ = required operating time of subsystem $i$ (often $t_i = t$) - $w_i = N_i/N$ = complexity weight For $t_i = t$ (uniform mission duration), AGREE simplifies to: $$R_i = R_S^{\,N_i/N} = R_S^{\,w_i}$$ which matches the proportional allocation form — the same complexity-weighted apportioning. #### (d) Optimization-Based Allocation (Lagrange / Cost Minimization) Minimize total cost $C = \sum C_i(\lambda_i)$ subject to constraint $\sum \lambda_i \leq \lambda_S$: $$\mathcal{L} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} C_i(\lambda_i) + \mu\!\left(\sum_{i=1}^{n}\lambda_i - \lambda_S\right)$$ $$\frac{dC_i}{d\lambda_i} = -\mu = \text{constant (equal marginal cost)}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield Trick:** When subsystem cost is exponential, $C_i = a_i \exp(b_i \lambda_i)$, optimal allocation yields $$\lambda_i = \frac{1}{b_i}\left[\ln\!\left(\frac{b_i}{a_i \mu}\right)\right] \quad \text{(Lagrange optimum)}$$ ### 2.4 Reliability Prediction Two main approaches (MIL-HDBK-217 family, Telcordia SR-332): #### (a) Parts-Count Method (early design) $$\lambda_S = \sum_{i=1}^{k} N_i \lambda_i$$ where $N_i$ = quantity of part type $i$, $\lambda_i$ = generic failure rate (from handbook). #### (b) Parts-Stress Method (detailed design) $$\lambda_p = \lambda_b \cdot \pi_E \cdot \pi_Q \cdot \pi_A \cdot \pi_S \cdot \pi_C \cdot \ldots$$ where the $\pi$ factors are multipliers for **Environment, Quality, Application, Stress, Construction**. Resulting system reliability (series assumption): $$R_S(t) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} R_i(t) = e^{-\lambda_S t}$$ ### 2.5 Design for Reliability (DFR) — Engineering Tools | Tool | Purpose | When Used | |---|---|---| | **Derating** | Operate part well below its rated stress | Detailed design | | **Worst-Case Design** | Guarantee performance at component tolerances | Analog/precision design | | **Robust Design (Taguchi)** | Minimize variation sensitivity | Parameter optimization | | **FMEA / FMECA** | Identify failure modes and rank risk | Detailed design + CDR | | **Fault Tree Analysis (FTA)** | Top-down deductive analysis | System-level safety | | **Sneak Circuit Analysis** | Find unintended logic paths | Complex electrical systems | | **HALT/HASS** | Accelerated stress testing | Prototype/qualification | | **Reliability Growth Testing** | Track $MTBF$ improvement | Qualification, pre-production | ### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Concept | Formula | Conditions / Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Reliability target from failure rate | $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ | Exponential, constant $\lambda$ | | 2 | MTBF from failure rate | $\text{MTBF} = 1/\lambda$ | Exponential, repairable | | 3 | MTTF from failure rate | $\text{MTTF} = 1/\lambda$ | Exponential, non-repairable | | 4 | Series reliability | $R_S = \prod_{i=1}^{n} R_i$ | All must succeed | | 5 | Parallel (k-of-n) reliability | $R_S = \sum_{j=k}^{n}\binom{n}{j}p^{j}(1-p)^{n-j}$ | $p = $ element reliability | | 6 | Equal allocation | $R_i = R_S^{1/n}$ | Series, identical subsystems | | 7 | AGREE allocation | $R_i = R_S^{N_i/N}$ | Series, complexity-weighted | | 8 | Proportional allocation | $R_i = R_S^{w_i}$ | $w_i = \lambda_i/\sum\lambda_j$ | | 9 | Failure rate in FIT | $\text{FIT} = \lambda \times 10^{9}$ | $\lambda$ in hr$^{-1}$ | | 10 | Mission reliability | $R_M = e^{-\lambda_M t_M}$ | Mission duration $t_M$ | | 11 | Duane growth (later) | $\theta_C = \theta_1 N^{\alpha}$ | $\alpha = $ growth slope | | 12 | Availability | $A = \text{MTBF}/(\text{MTBF}+\text{MDT})$ | Steady state | | 13 | Crow-AMSAA | $N(t) = \lambda t^{\beta}$ | Reliability growth | | 14 | Derating factor | $\text{DRF} = \text{Actual stress}/\text{Rated stress}$ | $\text{DRF} < 1$ preferred | | 15 | Taguchi S/N ratio (larger-better) | $S/N = -10\log\!\left(\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}\frac{1}{y_i^{2}}\right)$ | Quality characteristic | | 16 | Taguchi S/N (smaller-better) | $S/N = -10\log\!\left(\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}y_i^{2}\right)$ | Quality characteristic | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner Tip:** Always state the **assumption of independence and exponential failure distribution** before applying series reliability formulas. Half the marks lost in KTU exams are due to missing assumptions. ### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Aerospace**: Mission reliability $R_M$ for a 2-hour flight must exceed $0.9999999$ ("seven 9s") — allocation and FMEA are mandatory. - **Automotive electronics**: AEC-Q100/Q101 derating, parts-stress prediction at $-40^{\circ}\text{C}$ to $+125^{\circ}\text{C}$. - **Telecom**: Telcordia SR-332 prediction; field returns tracked for 5+ years. - **Medical devices**: ISO 14971 (risk management) heavily overlaps DFR via FMEA → FMECA. - **Industrial PLCs**: Worst-case design to tolerate $\pm 20\%$ mains supply variation. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Series System Reliability from Component Reliabilities **Given:** $n$ independent components arranged in series (all must operate for system success), with component reliabilities $R_1(t), R_2(t), \dots, R_n(t)$. **Step 1:** For independent events, the probability of joint success equals the product of individual probabilities. $$R_S(t) = P(T_1 \geq t \,\wedge\, T_2 \geq t \,\wedge\, \cdots \,\wedge\, T_n \geq t)$$ **Step 2:** Apply independence of failure events across components. $$R_S(t) = P(T_1 \geq t) \cdot P(T_2 \geq t) \cdots P(T_n \geq t)$$ **Step 3:** Each factor is the component reliability function. $$R_S(t) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} R_i(t)$$ **Step 4:** If each component is exponential with constant failure rate $\lambda_i$: $$R_i(t) = e^{-\lambda_i t}$$ **Step 5:** Substitute into the product. $$R_S(t) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} e^{-\lambda_i t} = \exp\!\left(-\sum_{i=1}^{n} \lambda_i t\right)$$ **Step 6:** Identify equivalent system failure rate and MTBF. $$\lambda_S = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \lambda_i \quad\Rightarrow\quad \text{MTBF}_S = \frac{1}{\lambda_S}$$ **Conclusion:** In a series system, failure rates add. This is the fundamental equation used in **parts-count** and **parts-stress** reliability prediction. --- ### 3.2 Derivation 2 — AGREE Allocation Formula (Detailed) **Given:** System of $N$ components distributed into $n$ subsystems. Subsystem $i$ contains $N_i$ components, with required mission time $t_i$ (out of total system time $t_S$). The target is $R_S(t_S)$. **Step 1:** Express subsystem reliability target as a function of complexity weight and time weight. The AGREE method assumes the failure rate of subsystem $i$ is: $$\lambda_i = \frac{N_i}{N} \cdot \frac{-\ln R_S(t_S)}{t_i}$$ The first factor is the **complexity factor** $w_i = N_i/N$. The second is the **failure rate that subsystem $i$ would carry** if failure rate scaled linearly with complexity. **Step 2:** Subsystem reliability over its own operating interval $t_i$: $$R_i(t_i) = e^{-\lambda_i t_i}$$ **Step 3:** Substitute $\lambda_i$: $$R_i(t_i) = \exp\!\left[-\frac{N_i}{N} \cdot \frac{-\ln R_S(t_S)}{t_i} \cdot t_i\right]$$ **Step 4:** Cancel $t_i$ (the time-weight cancels the operating duration of the subsystem): $$R_i(t_i) = \exp\!\left[-\frac{N_i}{N} \cdot (-\ln R_S(t_S))\right]$$ **Step 5:** Use the identity $e^{-a(-\ln x)} = e^{a \ln x} = x^{a}$: $$R_i(t_i) = R_S(t_S)^{\,N_i/N} = R_S(t_S)^{\,w_i}$$ **Step 6:** Result — the **AGREE allocation formula**: $$\boxed{R_i = R_S^{\,w_i}, \quad w_i = \frac{N_i}{N}}$$ **Sanity check:** If all subsystems have equal complexity, $w_i = 1/n$ and $R_i = R_S^{1/n}$, recovering equal allocation. **Sanity check 2:** $\prod_{i=1}^{n} R_i = \prod R_S^{w_i} = R_S^{\sum w_i} = R_S^{1} = R_S$ ✓ — allocation is consistent. --- ### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Failure Rate from Reliability Target **Given:** Customer requires $R(t_m) = R^*$ over mission time $t_m$. Assume exponential model. **Step 1:** Exponential reliability definition. $$R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$$ **Step 2:** Equate to target. $$e^{-\lambda t_m} = R^*$$ **Step 3:** Take natural logarithm of both sides. $$-\lambda t_m = \ln R^*$$ **Step 4:** Solve for $\lambda$. Because $R^* < 1$ normally, $\ln R^* < 0$, so $\lambda > 0$. $$\lambda = \frac{-\ln R^*}{t_m}$$ **Step 5:** Express in FIT. $$\text{FIT} = \lambda \times 10^{9} = \frac{-10^{9}\,\ln R^*}{t_m}$$ **Worked numeric example:** $R^* = 0.99$, $t_m = 10{,}000\ \text{hr}$. $$\lambda = \frac{-\ln(0.99)}{10{,}000} = \frac{0.01005}{10{,}000} = 1.005 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{hr}^{-1}$$ $$\text{MTBF} = 1/\lambda = 994{,}883\ \text{hr} \approx 113.5\ \text{years}$$ $$\text{FIT} = 1005\ \text{failures per } 10^{9}\ \text{hr}$$ --- ### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Reliability Apportionment for Series with Unequal Importance When some subsystems are more critical than others (e.g., the **importance factor** $I_i$ for redundancy savings), the more general apportionment equation is: $$R_i = 1 - (1 - R_S)^{w_i}, \quad w_i = \frac{I_i\,N_i}{\sum_j I_j N_j}$$ This is the **ARINC / similar weighted importance method** referenced in advanced KTU modules. **Step 1:** Define weight $w_i$ combining importance and complexity. **Step 2:** Solve for $R_i$ by inverting the exponential series form. **Step 3:** Verify that $\prod R_i$ (or appropriate combination) recovers $R_S$. --- ### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Mapping Reliability Activity to Design Phase | Design Phase | Primary Reliability Output | Tool/Method | Key Deliverable | |---|---|---|---| | Concept | Reliability goal $R^*$ | Customer translation | Reliability spec | | Feasibility | Risk ranking | FMEA outline | Risk register | | Preliminary | Allocation | AGREE / equal | Subsystem targets | | Detailed | Prediction | Parts-stress | $\lambda_S$, MTBF | | CDR | Verification | FMECA + review | Compliance matrix | | Prototype | Demonstration | HALT/ESS | Test report | | Production | Control | SPC + audits | Field MTBF | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 The Reliability V-Model (Design Process Map) The **V-Model** is the standard KTU framework showing how reliability activities on the descending (specification) arm of the V are matched to verification activities on the ascending arm. ```mermaid flowchart TB A[User Needs and Mission Profile] --> B[System Reliability Target R asterisk t_m] B --> C[Functional Architecture and Block Diagram] C --> D[Reliability Allocation AGREE] D --> E[Subsystem Reliability Targets R_i] E --> F[Detailed Design and Parts Stress Prediction] F --> G[Failure Mode Analysis FMEA FMECA] G --> H[Design Reviews PDR and CDR] H --> I[Prototype Build and HALT] I --> J[Reliability Demonstration Test] J --> K[Qualification and Release] K --> L[Field Reliability Tracking and Growth] L --> M[Feedback to Future Designs] M --> A A2[User Needs]:::phase B2[Reliability Spec]:::phase C2[Architecture]:::phase D2[Allocation]:::phase E2[Subsystem Targets]:::phase F2[Detailed Design]:::phase G2[FMEA]:::phase H2[Design Review]:::phase I2[Prototype Test]:::phase J2[Demonstration]:::phase K2[Qualification]:::phase L2[Field Data]:::phase M2[Feedback Loop]:::phase classDef phase fill:#E6F3FF,stroke:#0066CC,stroke-width:2px,color:#003366 ``` ### 4.2 Reliability Allocation Flow (AGREE) ```mermaid flowchart LR S0[System Target R_S and t_m]:::in --> S1[Identify n Subsystems] S1 --> S2[Count Components N_i per Subsystem] S2 --> S3[Compute Weights w_i = N_i / N] S3 --> S4[Apply R_i = R_S raised to w_i] S4 --> S5[Subsystem Targets R_i]:::out S5 --> S6[Verify Product equals R_S] S6 --> S7{Allocation Feasible?} S7 -- Yes --> S8[Proceed to Detailed Design]:::out S7 -- No --> S9[Reallocate or Re-architect]:::dec S9 --> S1 classDef in fill:#FFE6E6,stroke:#CC0000,color:#660000 classDef out fill:#E6FFE6,stroke:#009900,color:#003300 classDef dec fill:#FFF5E6,stroke:#CC6600,color:#663300 ``` ### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — DFR Tool Stack ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph SPEC[Specification Layer] SP1[Mission Profile]:::layer SP2[Reliability Target R asterisk]:::layer SP3[Cost and Weight Constraints]:::layer end subgraph ALLOC[Allocation Layer] AL1[AGREE Method]:::layer AL2[Equal Apportioning]:::layer AL3[Optimization Lagrange]:::layer end subgraph PRED[Prediction Layer] PR1[Parts Count Method]:::layer PR2[Parts Stress Method]:::layer PR3[Handbook Lambda_b]:::layer end subgraph ANAL[Analysis Layer] AN1[FMEA]:::layer AN2[FTA]:::layer AN3[Worst Case Analysis]:::layer AN4[Derating Analysis]:::layer end subgraph OPT[Optimization Layer] OP1[Robust Design Taguchi]:::layer OP2[Reliability Based Optimization RBDO]:::layer end subgraph VER[Verification Layer] VR1[Design Reviews PDR CDR]:::layer VR2[HALT and HASS]:::layer VR3[Reliability Demonstration]:::layer VR4[Growth Testing Crow AMSAA]:::layer end SPEC --> ALLOC --> PRED --> ANAL --> OPT --> VER classDef layer fill:#F0F8FF,stroke:#003366,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#001A33 ``` ### 4.4 Failure Rate Curve (Bathtub) — Design Implication ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Infant Mortality Region
Lambda decreasing]:::early --> B[Useful Life Region
Lambda constant
Exponential model applies]:::useful B --> C[Wear Out Region
Lambda increasing]:::wear A -->|Burn In / ESS eliminates| X[Design Action: Pre-Stress Screening]:::act B -->|Handbook predictions| Y[Design Action: Derate and Use Quality Parts]:::act C -->|Scheduled replacement| Z[Design Action: Preventive Maintenance Plan]:::act classDef early fill:#FFD6D6,stroke:#990000,color:#660000 classDef useful fill:#D6FFD6,stroke:#009900,color:#003300 classDef wear fill:#FFE6B3,stroke:#996600,color:#664400 classDef act fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#333399,color:#191970 ``` ### 4.5 Derating Strategy Tree ```mermaid flowchart TB D0[Component Stress S]:::root --> D1{S_actual / S_rated = DRF} D1 -- DRF less than 0.3 --> D2[Conservative: Excellent Reliability Margin]:::ok D1 -- DRF 0.3 to 0.5 --> D3[Lightly Stressed: Recommended]:::ok D1 -- DRF 0.5 to 0.7 --> D4[Moderately Stressed: Acceptable]:::warn D1 -- DRF 0.7 to 0.9 --> D5[Highly Stressed: Increase Derating]:::warn D1 -- DRF greater than 0.9 --> D6[Critical: Redesign Required]:::crit classDef root fill:#E6F3FF,stroke:#003366,color:#001A33 classDef ok fill:#D6FFD6,stroke:#009900,color:#003300 classDef warn fill:#FFF2CC,stroke:#CC9900,color:#664400 classDef crit fill:#FFD6D6,stroke:#990000,color:#660000 ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1.** [KTU University Exam — June 2023] **Define the term "Reliability" as applied to the design of an engineering system. State its mathematical expression and explain the significance of the "useful life" region in the bathtub curve.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** *Reliability* of a system/component is the *probability that it will perform its intended function without failure under stated operating conditions for a specified period of time* (1 mark). Mathematically: $$R(t) = P(T \geq t) = 1 - F(t)$$ where $T$ is the time-to-failure random variable and $F(t)$ is the cumulative distribution function of failure (1 mark). In the **useful-life region** of the bathtub curve, the failure rate $\lambda(t)$ is approximately **constant** (1 mark). This permits use of the **exponential distribution**, giving $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ and $\text{MTBF} = 1/\lambda$ — the basis for handbook-based reliability prediction (MIL-HDBK-217, Telcordia). --- **Q2.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022] **Differentiate between MTTF and MTBF. In which design context is each used?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Aspect | MTTF | MTBF | |---|---|---| | Full form | Mean Time To Failure | Mean Time Between Failures | | System type | **Non-repairable** (component burns out) | **Repairable** (system restored after failure) | | Expression | $\text{MTTF} = \int_0^{\infty} R(t)\,dt$ | $\text{MTBF} = \text{MTTF} + \text{MTTR}$ (1.5 marks) | | Typical use | ICs, capacitors, fuses | Machines, vehicles, telecom systems | For exponential distribution, both equal $1/\lambda$ (1.5 marks). The distinction matters when the design objective is to specify maintenance intervals and availability — MTBF is the relevant metric for repairable designs. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks) > **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks** split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Reliability Allocation & AGREE **[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Paper]** **(a)** A system comprises **three subsystems A, B, C** arranged in series, containing **40, 30, and 30** components respectively. The required system reliability over a 1000-hour mission is $R_S(1000) = 0.95$. Using the **AGREE allocation method**, determine the **reliability target** for each subsystem over the same 1000-hour mission. (7 marks) **(b)** Two identical components are connected in **parallel** redundancy to improve system reliability. Each component has reliability $R = 0.90$ over 1000 hours. Compute: (i) parallel system reliability, (ii) the failure rate of the parallel configuration, and (iii) the effective MTBF. (7 marks) --- #### Solution to Question A **Part (a) — AGREE Allocation** **Step 1:** Total number of components in the system. $$N = N_A + N_B + N_C = 40 + 30 + 30 = 100$$ **Step 2:** Compute complexity weight for each subsystem. $$w_A = \frac{40}{100} = 0.40$$ $$w_B = \frac{30}{100} = 0.30$$ $$w_C = \frac{30}{100} = 0.30$$ **Step 3:** Apply the AGREE formula (for $t_i = t_m$). $$R_i = R_S^{\,w_i}$$ **Step 4:** Compute each subsystem's reliability target. $$R_A = (0.95)^{0.40} = e^{0.40 \cdot \ln(0.95)} = e^{0.40 \cdot (-0.05129)} = e^{-0.02052}$$ $$R_A = 0.9797 \approx 0.980$$ $$R_B = (0.95)^{0.30} = e^{0.30 \cdot (-0.05129)} = e^{-0.01539} = 0.9847 \approx 0.985$$ $$R_C = (0.95)^{0.30} = 0.985 \quad \text{(same as B by symmetry)}$$ **Step 5:** Verification — product must equal $R_S$. $$R_A \cdot R_B \cdot R_C = 0.9797 \times 0.9847 \times 0.9847 = 0.9500 \approx 0.95 \ \checkmark$$ **Final Answer (Part a):** $$\boxed{R_A = 0.980, \quad R_B = 0.985, \quad R_C = 0.985}$$ **Interpretation:** Subsystem A, being the most complex, must achieve the **lowest** reliability target per part (in series, the product must still give 0.95, so a high-$N$ subsystem carries the burden). --- **Part (b) — Parallel Redundancy Analysis** **Step 1:** For 2-of-2 parallel (active redundancy), system succeeds if *at least one* component works. $$R_S = 1 - (1 - R)^2 = 1 - (1 - 0.90)^2 = 1 - (0.10)^2$$ $$R_S = 1 - 0.01 = 0.99$$ **Step 2:** Assuming exponential, $\lambda = -\ln(R)/t$. For a single component: $$\lambda_c = \frac{-\ln(0.90)}{1000} = \frac{0.10536}{1000} = 1.0536 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{hr}^{-1}$$ For the parallel configuration, system reliability is $R_S(t) = 2e^{-\lambda_c t} - e^{-2\lambda_c t}$. The **equivalent system failure rate** at $t=0$ is: $$\lambda_S = \frac{-\ln(R_S)}{t} = \frac{-\ln(0.99)}{1000} = \frac{0.01005}{1000} = 1.005 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{hr}^{-1}$$ **Step 3:** Effective MTBF. $$\text{MTBF}_S = \frac{1}{\lambda_S} = 99{,}500\ \text{hr}$$ Compared to the single-component MTBF of $1/1.0536\times10^{-4} \approx 9{,}492$ hr, redundancy has improved MTBF by a factor of **~10.5×**. **Final Answer (Part b):** $$\boxed{R_S = 0.99, \quad \lambda_S \approx 1.005 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{hr}^{-1}, \quad \text{MTBF}_S \approx 99{,}500\ \text{hr}}$$ --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Reliability Prediction & Design Economics **[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Paper — Alternative Choice]** **(a)** A satellite electronic subsystem has **500 components** with the following generic failure rates: 200 resistors ($\lambda_r = 0.5\ \text{FIT}$ each), 200 capacitors ($\lambda_c = 1.0\ \text{FIT}$ each), 50 ICs ($\lambda_{IC} = 10\ \text{FIT}$ each), and 50 connectors ($\lambda_{cn} = 3.0\ \text{FIT}$ each). Using the **parts-count method**, compute: (i) the system failure rate, (ii) the mission reliability for a 5-year mission, and (iii) the system MTBF in years. (7 marks) **(b)** Explain the **1–10–100 rule** of reliability economics in design. Why is it cheaper to fix a reliability problem at the concept stage than at the field stage? List four design-phase activities that improve reliability at minimal cost. (7 marks) --- #### Solution to Question B **Part (a) — Parts-Count Prediction** **Step 1:** Sum failure rates weighted by quantity. Recall $1\ \text{FIT} = 10^{-9}\ \text{hr}^{-1}$. $$\lambda_S = N_r\lambda_r + N_c\lambda_c + N_{IC}\lambda_{IC} + N_{cn}\lambda_{cn}$$ $$\lambda_S = (200)(0.5) + (200)(1.0) + (50)(10) + (50)(3.0)\ \text{FIT}$$ $$\lambda_S = 100 + 200 + 500 + 150 = 950\ \text{FIT}$$ **Step 2:** Convert to per-hour failure rate. $$\lambda_S = 950 \times 10^{-9}\ \text{hr}^{-1} = 9.50 \times 10^{-7}\ \text{failures/hr}$$ **Step 3:** Mission reliability for $t_m = 5\ \text{years} = 5 \times 8760 = 43{,}800\ \text{hr}$. $$R_S(t_m) = e^{-\lambda_S t_m} = e^{-(9.50 \times 10^{-7})(43{,}800)}$$ $$R_S = e^{-0.04161} = 0.9592 \approx 0.959$$ **Step 4:** System MTBF. $$\text{MTBF}_S = \frac{1}{\lambda_S} = \frac{1}{9.50 \times 10^{-7}} = 1{,}052{,}632\ \text{hr}$$ Convert to years: $$\text{MTBF}_S = \frac{1{,}052{,}632}{8760} \approx 120.2\ \text{years}$$ **Final Answer (Part a):** $$\boxed{\lambda_S = 950\ \text{FIT},\ \ R_S(5\ \text{yr}) = 0.959,\ \ \text{MTBF}_S \approx 120.2\ \text{years}}$$ --- **Part (b) — The 1–10–100 Rule and Design-Phase Reliability Activities** **Step 1 — Statement of the rule:** For every dollar spent to *prevent* a failure, the cost of *finding and fixing* the same failure **multiplies by 10×** at each subsequent phase: | Phase of Detection | Cost Multiplier | |---|---| | Concept / requirements | **1×** | | Detailed design | **10×** | | Prototype / qualification | **100×** | | Production | **1000×** | | Field operation / warranty | **10,000×** (or more) | **Step 2 — Why is field correction so expensive?** - Recalls, logistics, and field engineer time. - Customer goodwill loss and warranty claims. - Legal liability, possible safety incidents. - Root-cause re-investigation across the whole design chain. - Lost production capacity. **Step 3 — Four design-phase activities that boost reliability cheaply:** 1. **Reliability allocation (AGREE)** — Pushes complexity weight onto robust subsystems; early identification of weak links. 2. **Derating** — Operating components at 30–50% of rated stress cuts $\lambda_b$ by 3–10× with negligible cost (just buying a higher-rated part). 3. **FMEA / FMECA** — Identifies and ranks failure modes *before* prototypes exist; drives targeted design changes. 4. **Robust design (Taguchi)** — Minimizes sensitivity to manufacturing variation and environmental noise, raising effective reliability without precision tolerance. **Final Answer (Part b):** The 1–10–100 rule states that the cost of correcting a reliability defect **increases by an order of magnitude at every later stage** of the product lifecycle (1 mark). Concept-stage changes are cheap because only paper and calculations are affected, whereas field corrections involve hardware replacement, logistics, legal exposure, and reputational damage (1 mark). Four design-phase activities: (1) reliability allocation, (2) derating, (3) FMEA, and (4) robust design — all are listed with one-line justifications (1 mark each = 4 marks). Total: 7 marks. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls** > 1. **Forgetting the exponential assumption**: When you use $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$, you *must* state that the failure rate is constant (i.e., the system is in its useful life region). Examiners deduct **1–2 marks** for unstated assumptions. > 2. **Unit mismatch**: Mixing $\lambda$ in hr$^{-1}$ with $t$ in years (or seconds). Always convert mission time to the same unit as $\lambda$ *before* computing. > 3. **Confusing MTTF with MTBF**: MTTF is for non-repairable; MTBF includes repair time. Using them interchangeably costs 1 mark. > 4. **AGREE formula mis-application**: Some students write $R_i = R_S^{N_i}$ instead of $R_S^{N_i/N}$. Remember to **divide by total $N$**. > 5. **Series assumption unstated**: Most parts-count/parts-stress results assume series. Mention it explicitly. > 6. **Skipping verification step**: Always re-multiply allocated $R_i$ values to confirm $\prod R_i = R_S$. This shows the examiner you understand consistency. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **High-Density Revision Checklist — Reliability in Design Process** - **Reliability Definition:** $R(t) = P(T \geq t)$ — probability of survival to time $t$. - **Exponential model:** $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$, valid in the useful-life region (constant $\lambda$). - **MTBF** (repairable) $= 1/\lambda$ = **MTTF** (non-repairable, exponential). - **Series reliability:** $R_S = \prod R_i$, equivalent $\lambda_S = \sum \lambda_i$. - **Parallel reliability (2-of-2):** $R_S = 1 - (1-R)^2$. - **AGREE allocation:** $R_i = R_S^{N_i/N}$, where $w_i = N_i/N$ is the complexity weight. - **Equal allocation:** $R_i = R_S^{1/n}$ (special case of AGREE when $N_i$ are equal). - **Parts-count prediction:** $\lambda_S = \sum N_i \lambda_i$. - **Parts-stress prediction:** $\lambda_p = \lambda_b \prod \pi_k$ with $\pi_E, \pi_Q, \pi_A$, etc. - **FIT** = $10^{-9}$ failures/hr; convert $\lambda \leftrightarrow$ FIT for KTU problems. - **Mission reliability** $R_M = e^{-\lambda t_m}$ sets design target $R^*$. - **Derating factor** $\text{DRF} = S_{\text{actual}}/S_{\text{rated}}$; aim for DRF $\leq 0.5$ for reliability-critical parts. - **1–10–100 rule:** reliability cost grows by an order of magnitude each phase. - **FMEA / FMECA:** systematic identification of failure modes, effects, and criticality — run at detailed design. - **Robust design (Taguchi):** minimize S/N ratio variation; parameter design before tolerance design. - **Design reviews:** PDR (preliminary), CDR (critical) — independent reliability assessment. - **Reliability growth:** Crow-AMSAA $N(t) = \lambda t^\beta$; $\beta < 1$ indicates improvement, $\beta = 1$ stable, $\beta > 1$ degradation. - **Duane plot:** $\log \theta_C$ vs $\log N$, slope $\alpha = 1 - \beta$ in early growth. - **Design hierarchy:** Concept → Feasibility → Preliminary → Detailed → CDR → Qualification → Production → Field. - **Always state assumptions:** exponentiality, independence, series configuration, useful-life region. - **Always verify:** $\prod R_i$ (allocated) should equal $R_S$; failure rates of components should sum to $\lambda_S$. > **End of Notes — Module 3: Reliability in Design Process**

topics.title: System Effectiveness
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET632 - RELIABILITY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** System Effectiveness

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 3 — System Effectiveness

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **System Effectiveness (E)** is defined as the *probability that a system will successfully perform its intended mission* under a specified set of conditions, during a prescribed mission profile, and over a stated period of time. It is a single, composite figure-of-merit that integrates **reliability, maintainability, availability, and operational performance** into one number that designers, managers, and defence agencies can use to compare competing designs.

### Formal Definition (AGREE / ARINC Terminology)
According to the *AGREE Report* (Advisory Group on Reliability of Electronic Equipment, 1957) and ARINC Research Corporation, system effectiveness is a *probability measure* that a system, when called upon to perform a set of specified tasks under a given set of conditions, will perform those tasks successfully.

Mathematically, the canonical ARINC definition is expressed as a triple-product:

$$
E \;=\; A \times D \times C
$$

where each factor is a probability (a dimensionless number between **0** and **1**), and therefore the resulting system effectiveness $E$ is also bounded in $[0,\,1]$.

### The Three Pillars of System Effectiveness

| Symbol | Term | Core Question Answered | Mathematical Essence |
|:------:|:-----|:-----------------------|:---------------------|
| **A** | **Availability** | *"Will the system be operational when it is called upon?"* | Steady-state probability that the system is up and ready |
| **D** | **Dependability** | *"If the system is operational, will it survive the mission without failure?"* | Mission reliability / conditional probability of mission success |
| **C** | **Capability** | *"If the system survives, how well can it perform the required task?"* | Conditional probability of successful performance given no failure |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Students must be able to *define* each of the three components, *identify* the operational phase that each one addresses, and *combine* them using the multiplicative ARINC model. The product form is valid **only** when the three factors are statistically **independent** — a critical assumption examiners test repeatedly.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Imagine you bought a **Kerala State Road Transport Corporation (KSRTC) Volvo bus** for inter-state service. The total system effectiveness of that bus on a Bengaluru route is determined by three real-world questions:

1. **Availability (A):** *"Is the bus actually at the depot, fueled, licensed and ready to depart at 9:00 PM sharp?"* — If it broke down in the morning and is still in the workshop at 9 PM, $A \to 0$.
2. **Dependability (D):** *"Given that the bus left the depot, will it complete the 600 km trip without breaking down on the highway?"* — This is mission reliability over the ~10-hour drive.
3. **Capability (C):** *"Once it arrives, how many passengers, with what comfort, on-time, and within fuel limits does it carry?"* — A perfectly reliable bus that arrives 10 hours late is *ineffective*.

Only the *product* of these three probabilities tells you the chance that a passenger, standing at Thiruvananthapuram at 9 PM, will actually reach Bengaluru comfortably and on time. If any one factor is zero, the trip fails regardless of the others — that is exactly why effectiveness is **multiplicative**, not additive.

### Why System Effectiveness Matters in Engineering
- **Trade-off analysis:** A fighter aircraft with 100% capability but 40% availability is operationally *less effective* than one with 80% capability and 90% availability.
- **Procurement decisions:** Defence agencies (DRDO, MoD India) and aerospace primes (HAL, ISRO) select contractors partly on projected $E$ values.
- **Total Cost of Ownership:** Optimizing $E$ usually lowers the *life-cycle cost* of a system.
- **Single number to management:** Engineers speak in reliability, maintainability, MTBF; management prefers one composite index.

---

### Standard Performance Metrics Used
- **MTBF** (Mean Time Between Failures) — drives the reliability term
- **MTTR** (Mean Time To Repair) — drives the maintainability term
- **Availability** — derived as $A = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$
- **Mission Time $T$** — the stipulated duration of a single mission
- **Failure Rate $\lambda$** — assumed constant for the exponential model

> [!TIP]
> Memorize the ARINC triplet **A – D – C** in that order. KTU examiners frequently ask: *"Arrange the components in chronological order of operational phase"* — and the answer is **Availability (before mission) → Dependability (during mission) → Capability (after surviving)**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Logical Flow of a Mission
A typical engineering system (radar, jet engine, satellite, CNC machine) passes through a **sequence of operational states** during its life cycle. System effectiveness mathematically traces this life cycle:

1. **Non-operational state** — the system is in storage, undergoing preventive maintenance, awaiting spares, or stuck in logistics chain. *Availability* is the probability that the system escapes this state on time when needed.
2. **Operational-but-at-risk state** — the system has been activated and is performing the mission. *Dependability* is the probability that it stays alive and functional throughout the mission time $T$.
3. **End-of-mission state** — the system has survived; now it must perform to specification. *Capability* is the probability that its performance parameters (range, payload, throughput, accuracy) meet the threshold.

Because each stage is a *gate* — the mission cannot reach stage 3 without passing through stages 1 and 2 — the joint probability is multiplicative under statistical independence.

$$
P(\text{Mission Success}) \;=\; P(\text{System Up}) \cdot P(\text{No Failure} \mid \text{Up}) \cdot P(\text{Performance Met} \mid \text{Survived})
$$

This is precisely the ARINC formulation $E = A \cdot D \cdot C$.

### 2.2 Expanded View of the Three Components

#### 2.2.1 Availability (A)
Steady-state (or operational) availability is the fraction of *calendar time* the system is in an up-and-ready state.

For repairable systems with constant failure rate $\lambda$ and constant repair rate $\mu$:

$$
A \;=\; \frac{\mu}{\lambda + \mu} \;=\; \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}
$$

For non-repairable systems, *inherent availability* uses only mean corrective downtime:

$$
A_i \;=\; \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + M_{ct}}
$$

where $M_{ct}$ is the mean *corrective* maintenance time.

> [!NOTE]
> The difference between **Inherent Availability $A_i$** and **Operational Availability $A_o$** is a common 3-mark question. $A_i$ excludes preventive maintenance, supply delays, and administrative delays; $A_o$ includes *all* downtime and is therefore always lower than $A_i$.

#### 2.2.2 Dependability (D)
Dependability is the *mission reliability* — the probability of completing the mission of duration $T$ without failure, given that the system started in an up state.

For a single unit with exponential reliability and failure rate $\lambda$:

$$
D \;=\; R(T) \;=\; e^{-\lambda T}
$$

For a series system of $n$ independent exponential units:

$$
D \;=\; \exp\!\left(-T\sum_{i=1}^{n} \lambda_i\right)
$$

For a parallel-redundant (k-out-of-n) system, $D$ is computed from the binomial expansion of subsystem reliabilities over the mission window.

#### 2.2.3 Capability (C)
Capability is the most subtle of the three. It is *not* a reliability term; it is a *performance* term.

$$
C \;=\; \int_{x_0}^{x_1} f_P(x)\,dx
$$

where $f_P(x)$ is the probability density of the *performance parameter* $x$, and $[x_0, x_1]$ is the acceptable range of that parameter.

Common capability examples:
- A radar's *probability of detecting* an aircraft at a given range.
- A power plant's *probability of meeting* the load demand within tolerance.
- A communication link's *probability of achieving* a target bit-error rate.

If performance is a Gaussian variable with mean $\mu_P$ and standard deviation $\sigma_P$, and the specification band is $[\text{Lower Limit, Upper Limit}]$, then:

$$
C \;=\; \Phi\!\left(\frac{UL - \mu_P}{\sigma_P}\right) \;-\; \Phi\!\left(\frac{LL - \mu_P}{\sigma_P}\right)
$$

where $\Phi(\cdot)$ is the standard normal CDF.

### 2.3 Independence Assumption — Why It Matters
The product $E = A \times D \times C$ requires:

- Availability and dependability are independent when the failure rate $\lambda$ is *constant* and the system is in *steady-state*.
- Dependability and capability are independent when *performance* is decoupled from *survival* — a real failure (hardware break) is independent of a performance shortfall (under-thrust, off-frequency).

When these conditions fail, a more general model uses *conditional* probabilities:

$$
E \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{n} a_i\, d_i\, c_i
$$

where the sum is taken over $n$ mutually exclusive operational states.

### 2.4 Real-World Utility
- **Defence (HAL Tejas Mk2):** Effectiveness = availability on the tarmac × no-abort during sortie × kill probability envelope.
- **ISRO PSLV / GSLV:** Availability of launch pad × no failure during ascent × payload injection accuracy.
- **Power plants (NTPC):** Plant availability × no forced outage during peak window × frequency band maintenance.
- **Medical equipment (MRI / CT):** Up-time × survival through scan × diagnostic image quality.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Domain / Range | Notes |
|:-:|:--------|:--------|:--------------:|:------|
| 1 | ARINC Effectiveness | $E = A \cdot D \cdot C$ | $[0,\,1]$ | Independence assumed |
| 2 | Steady-state Availability | $A = \dfrac{\mu}{\lambda + \mu}$ | $[0,\,1]$ | Constant $\lambda$ & $\mu$ |
| 3 | Availability (MTBF form) | $A = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$ | $[0,\,1]$ | Equivalent to (2) |
| 4 | Inherent Availability | $A_i = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + M_{ct}}$ | $[0,\,1]$ | Excludes preventive downtime |
| 5 | Dependability (exponential) | $D = e^{-\lambda T}$ | $[0,\,1]$ | $T$ = mission time |
| 6 | Dependability (series, exp.) | $D = e^{-T\sum \lambda_i}$ | $[0,\,1]$ | $n$ independent units |
| 7 | Capability (Gaussian) | $C = \Phi\!\left(\frac{UL-\mu_P}{\sigma_P}\right) - \Phi\!\left(\frac{LL-\mu_P}{\sigma_P}\right)$ | $[0,\,1]$ | Single-sided if only one limit |
| 8 | Conditional (general) Effectiveness | $E = \sum a_i d_i c_i$ | $[0,\,1]$ | $n$ mutually exclusive states |
| 9 | MTTR relation | $MTTR = 1/\mu$ | Time units | Repairable systems |
| 10 | MTBF relation | $MTBF = 1/\lambda$ | Time units | Exponential only |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Units matter in the exam:** Reliability-related quantities ($D$, $R$, $A$, $C$, $E$) are **dimensionless probabilities**. The time-related quantities ($T$, $MTBF$, $MTTR$, $1/\lambda$, $1/\mu$) are in **hours** (or any consistent time unit). KTU examiners often deduct 0.5 marks for *omitting units*.

### 2.6 Why This Model Is Powerful
- **Single scalar:** A contractor can be told "Your $E$ must be ≥ 0.85" — no ambiguity.
- **Diagnostic:** If $E$ is too low, the model tells you *which* factor is dragging it down and where to invest (maintainability training to raise $A$, derating to raise $D$, redesign to raise $C$).
- **Comparable:** Two competing designs can be ranked on $E$ even if they have different reliability/performance profiles.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Steady-State Availability from Renewal Theory

> **Goal:** Prove $A = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$ from first principles.

**Step 1 — Define a renewal cycle.**
For a repairable system with negligible replacement time, one *renewal cycle* consists of:
- An "up" interval of random length $X$ (time-to-failure)
- A "down" interval of random length $Y$ (time-to-repair)

The cycle length is $X + Y$.

**Step 2 — Apply the renewal-reward theorem.**
The long-run fraction of time the system is up equals the expected up-time per cycle divided by the expected cycle length:

$$
A \;=\; \frac{E[X]}{E[X] + E[Y]}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the standard mean definitions.**

$$
E[X] \;=\; MTBF
\quad,\quad
E[Y] \;=\; MTTR
$$

**Step 4 — Combine.**

$$
A \;=\; \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}
$$

**Step 5 — Convert to rate form using $MTBF = 1/\lambda$ and $MTTR = 1/\mu$.**

$$
A \;=\; \frac{1/\lambda}{1/\lambda + 1/\mu}
\;=\; \frac{\mu}{\lambda + \mu}
$$

**Logical check:** As $\mu \to \infty$ (instant repair), $A \to 1$. As $\mu \to 0$ (no repair possible), $A \to 0$. Both limits confirm the formula.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Dependability for a Series Configuration

> **Goal:** Show that for $n$ independent exponential units in series, the system dependability is $D = \exp\!\left(-T\sum \lambda_i\right)$.

**Step 1 — Write reliability of unit $i$.**

$$
R_i(T) \;=\; e^{-\lambda_i T}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply independence for the series configuration.**

The series system survives only if **every** unit survives, so:

$$
D \;=\; \prod_{i=1}^{n} R_i(T)
\;=\; \prod_{i=1}^{n} e^{-\lambda_i T}
$$

**Step 3 — Combine the exponentials using the product-to-sum rule $\prod e^{a_i} = e^{\sum a_i}$.**

$$
D \;=\; e^{-T\sum_{i=1}^{n} \lambda_i}
$$

**Step 4 — Define the equivalent system failure rate.**

$$
\Lambda \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{n} \lambda_i
$$

$$
\Rightarrow\quad D \;=\; e^{-\Lambda T}
$$

**Logical check:** Adding more units in series always *increases* $\Lambda$ and therefore *decreases* $D$ — consistent with engineering intuition.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — The ARINC Effectiveness Product

> **Goal:** Show that under independence, the joint probability of a three-stage mission success equals the product $A \cdot D \cdot C$.

**Step 1 — Partition the sample space.**
Let $S_1$ = "system is up at mission start", $S_2$ = "system survives the mission window", $S_3$ = "system performance meets specification". Success = $S_1 \cap S_2 \cap S_3$.

**Step 2 — Apply the chain rule of probability.**

$$
P(S_1 \cap S_2 \cap S_3)
\;=\; P(S_1)\,P(S_2 \mid S_1)\,P(S_3 \mid S_1 \cap S_2)
$$

**Step 3 — Identify each term with the ARINC notation.**

$$
P(S_1) = A
$$

$$
P(S_2 \mid S_1) = D
$$

$$
P(S_3 \mid S_1 \cap S_2) = C
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute.**

$$
P(\text{Mission Success}) = A \cdot D \cdot C = E
$$

**Logical check:** This is just the standard probability chain rule; the ARINC product is *not* an additional assumption — it is a direct consequence of conditional probability, provided we *define* $D$ and $C$ as the relevant conditional probabilities.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Capability from a Gaussian Performance Distribution

> **Goal:** Derive the expression for $C$ when performance is normally distributed.

**Step 1 — Model.** Let $P \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu_P,\,\sigma_P^2)$. The specification window is $[LL,\, UL]$.

**Step 2 — Standardise.** Let $Z = (P - \mu_P)/\sigma_P \sim \mathcal{N}(0,1)$.

**Step 3 — Capability definition.**

$$
C \;=\; P(LL \le P \le UL)
\;=\; P\!\left(\frac{LL - \mu_P}{\sigma_P} \le Z \le \frac{UL - \mu_P}{\sigma_P}\right)
$$

**Step 4 — Use the standard normal CDF $\Phi$.**

$$
C \;=\; \Phi\!\left(\frac{UL - \mu_P}{\sigma_P}\right) \;-\; \Phi\!\left(\frac{LL - \mu_P}{\sigma_P}\right)
$$

**One-sided special case:** If the spec is $P \ge LL$ only (no upper bound):

$$
C \;=\; 1 - \Phi\!\left(\frac{LL - \mu_P}{\sigma_P}\right)
\;=\; \Phi\!\left(\frac{\mu_P - LL}{\sigma_P}\right)
$$

---

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style 14-Mark Problem Style)

> **Problem:** A radar system has the following data:
> - MTBF = 200 hours
> - MTTR = 4 hours
> - Mission time $T$ = 8 hours
> - Performance parameter $P$ (detection range in km) is normally distributed with $\mu_P = 250$ km, $\sigma_P = 20$ km
> - Required detection range window: [210, 290] km
>
> Compute the **system effectiveness $E$** using the ARINC model.

**Step 1 — Compute availability $A$.**

$$
A \;=\; \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}
\;=\; \frac{200}{200 + 4}
\;=\; \frac{200}{204}
\;\approx\; 0.9804
$$

**Step 2 — Compute failure rate $\lambda$.**

$$
\lambda \;=\; \frac{1}{MTBF}
\;=\; \frac{1}{200}
\;=\; 0.005 \; \text{per hour}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute dependability $D$ (mission reliability).**

$$
D \;=\; e^{-\lambda T}
\;=\; e^{-0.005 \times 8}
\;=\; e^{-0.04}
\;\approx\; 0.9608
$$

**Step 4 — Compute capability $C$ from the Gaussian window.**

$$
C \;=\; \Phi\!\left(\frac{290 - 250}{20}\right) - \Phi\!\left(\frac{210 - 250}{20}\right)
\;=\; \Phi(2.0) - \Phi(-2.0)
$$

From standard normal tables: $\Phi(2.0) = 0.9772$, $\Phi(-2.0) = 0.0228$.

$$
C \;=\; 0.9772 - 0.0228
\;=\; 0.9544
$$

**Step 5 — Combine into $E$.**

$$
E \;=\; A \cdot D \cdot C
\;=\; 0.9804 \times 0.9608 \times 0.9544
$$

$$
\Rightarrow\quad 0.9804 \times 0.9608 \;\approx\; 0.9420
$$

$$
\Rightarrow\quad 0.9420 \times 0.9544 \;\approx\; 0.8990
$$

$$
\boxed{E \;\approx\; 0.8990}
$$

**Interpretation:** This radar has roughly a **90% chance** of being available, surviving the 8-hour mission, and detecting targets within the required range window. For a mission-critical asset, an examiner may compare this to a benchmark (e.g., $E \ge 0.90$ required) and conclude that the system *just* passes the requirement.

---

### 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Improvement Levers on $E$

| Lever | Effect on $A$ | Effect on $D$ | Effect on $C$ | Effect on $E$ (qualitative) |
|:------|:-------------:|:-------------:|:-------------:|:---------------------------:|
| Improve component reliability (lower $\lambda$) | slight | **strong ↑** | slight | ↑ |
| Improve diagnostics, faster repair (lower MTTR) | **strong ↑** | none | none | ↑ |
| Add redundancy (parallel) | slight | **strong ↑** | slight | ↑ |
| Tighten design tolerance (lower $\sigma_P$) | none | none | **strong ↑** | ↑ |
| Increase mean performance $\mu_P$ toward spec centre | none | none | ↑ (if not over-spec) | ↑ |
| Lengthen mission time $T$ | none | **strong ↓** | none | ↓ |
| Increase preventive maintenance | $A_o$ ↓, $A_i$ unchanged | ↑ | slight | depends |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 ARINC System Effectiveness — Conceptual Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeReq[Mission Requirement Trigger] --> nodeA
    nodeA[A - Availability] --> nodeGate1{System Up?}
    nodeGate1 -- No --> nodeFail0[Mission Fails - Zero Effectiveness]
    nodeGate1 -- Yes --> nodeD
    nodeD[D - Dependability] --> nodeGate2{No Failure in Time T?}
    nodeGate2 -- No --> nodeFail1[Mission Aborted - Effectiveness Loss]
    nodeGate2 -- Yes --> nodeC
    nodeC[C - Capability] --> nodeGate3{Performance Met?}
    nodeGate3 -- No --> nodeFail2[Performance Shortfall]
    nodeGate3 -- Yes --> nodeOK[Effective Mission Outcome]
    nodeOK --> nodeE[E = A x D x C]
```

### 4.2 Operational Timeline of a Single Mission

```mermaid
gantt
    title Lifecycle Phases Mapped to A, D, C
    dateFormat  X
    axisFormat  %s
    section Phase 1 - Pre Mission
    Logistics, Maintenance, Standby (A) :a1, 0, 30
    section Phase 2 - Mission Window
    Active Mission Survival (D)         :a2, 30, 70
    section Phase 3 - Post Survival
    Performance Evaluation (C)          :a3, 70, 100
```

### 4.3 Hierarchical Block Diagram of the ARINC Model

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SystemEffectiveness [System Effectiveness E]
        nodeBlockA[Availability A]
        nodeBlockD[Dependability D]
        nodeBlockC[Capability C]
    end
    nodeBlockA --> nodeMult
    nodeBlockD --> nodeMult
    nodeBlockC --> nodeMult
    nodeMult[Product Operator x] --> nodeResultE[Numerical Effectiveness Value in 0 to 1]
    nodeParamMTBF[MTBF] --> nodeBlockA
    nodeParamMTTR[MTTR] --> nodeBlockA
    nodeParamLambda[Failure Rate lambda] --> nodeBlockD
    nodeParamT[Mission Time T] --> nodeBlockD
    nodeParamPerf[Performance Distribution] --> nodeBlockC
```

### 4.4 Independence and Conditional Generalisation

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeStart[Mission Event Space] --> nodeA[Availability A]
    nodeStart --> nodeB[Dependability D]
    nodeStart --> nodeC[Capability C]
    nodeA --> nodeInd{Independent?}
    nodeInd -- Yes --> nodeProdE[Use Product Form E = A x D x C]
    nodeInd -- No --> nodeCondE[Use Conditional Sum E = Sum over i of a_i d_i c_i]
    nodeB --> nodeInd
    nodeC --> nodeInd
```

> [!TIP]
> In your KTU answer script, drawing **any one** of these flow diagrams — even a hand-drawn box-and-arrow version — typically earns **1 to 2 extra marks** under the "figures and clarity" component of the valuation scheme.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] Define system effectiveness. List its three components as per the ARINC model.**

**Model Answer:**
System effectiveness is the probability that a system will successfully perform its intended mission under stated conditions over a stated mission profile and time. According to the ARINC model, it is the product of three components: **Availability (A)** — the probability the system is operational when required, **Dependability (D)** — the probability the system operates without failure for the mission duration, and **Capability (C)** — the probability the system meets its performance specifications.

$$
E = A \times D \times C
$$

*(Self-contained, 3-mark answer.)*

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024] Distinguish between inherent availability and operational availability.**

**Model Answer:**
**Inherent Availability ($A_i$)** considers only *corrective* maintenance downtime; it excludes preventive maintenance, supply delays, and administrative delays. **Operational Availability ($A_o$)** includes *all* downtime — preventive, corrective, supply, administrative, and even standby logistics. Therefore $A_o \le A_i$ always. The trade-off is that $A_i$ is a *design* metric (controlled by engineering), while $A_o$ is a *logistics* metric (controlled by operations and support).

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

#### **Question A — 14 Marks** [KTU University Exam – Model Question]

> **(a)** With the help of a block diagram, explain the ARINC model of system effectiveness. Define each component and state the assumptions. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A ground-based radar has MTBF = 400 h, MTTR = 2 h, mission time T = 10 h, and exponential failure distribution. The detection range $P$ is Gaussian with mean 300 km and standard deviation 25 km; the acceptable range is [240, 360] km. Calculate $A$, $D$, $C$, and the system effectiveness $E$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** — *See Section 1, 2, and 4 of these notes for the diagram and the definitions.* The three components are:
- **A (Availability):** $A = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR} = \dfrac{400}{402} \approx 0.9950$
- **D (Dependability):** $D = e^{-\lambda T} = e^{-10/400} = e^{-0.025} \approx 0.9753$
- **C (Capability):** $C = \Phi\!\left(\dfrac{360-300}{25}\right) - \Phi\!\left(\dfrac{240-300}{25}\right) = \Phi(2.4) - \Phi(-2.4) = 0.9918 - 0.0082 = 0.9836$

**Assumption:** $A$, $D$, $C$ are statistically independent — performance is decoupled from survival, and the system is in steady-state.

**Product form:** $E = A \cdot D \cdot C = 0.9950 \times 0.9753 \times 0.9836 \approx 0.9545$

**(b)** Detailed step-by-step:

1. **Availability** $A = 400 / (400 + 2) = 0.9950$.
2. **Failure rate** $\lambda = 1/400 = 0.0025 \text{ h}^{-1}$.
3. **Dependability** $D = e^{-0.0025 \times 10} = e^{-0.025} = 0.9753$.
4. **Standardised limits:** $(360-300)/25 = 2.4$; $(240-300)/25 = -2.4$.
5. **Capability** $C = \Phi(2.4) - \Phi(-2.4) = 0.9918 - 0.0082 = 0.9836$.
6. **Effectiveness** $E = 0.9950 \times 0.9753 \times 0.9836 = 0.9545$.

**Final answer:** $E \approx 0.955$, i.e., the system is ~95.5% effective.

---

#### **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)** [KTU University Exam – Model Question]

> **(a)** Explain how the system effectiveness model can be extended when the independence assumption between dependability and capability is violated. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A satellite communication link has 3 transponders in cold redundancy. Each transponder has failure rate $\lambda = 0.001$ per hour, mission time $T = 1000$ h, repair time = 100 h. The bit-error-rate (BER) is uniformly distributed between $10^{-6}$ and $5 \times 10^{-6}$; the spec requires BER $\le 3 \times 10^{-6}$. Compute $A$, $D$, $C$, and $E$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** When $D$ and $C$ are *not* independent, the simple product $A \cdot D \cdot C$ overestimates effectiveness. The system is partitioned into $n$ mutually exclusive *operational states* $S_i$. For each state we compute the conditional $a_i, d_i, c_i$, and the total effectiveness is:

$$
E \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{n} a_i \, d_i \, c_i
$$

A common example is a system with a *partial* performance degradation after a *partial* failure. Another is a system where performance depends on the failure mode. The summation is essentially the *law of total probability* applied to the mission-success event over the state space.

**(b)** Detailed step-by-step:

1. **Availability** of the redundant set (cold standby): with one active and two cold spares, the failure rate of the active unit is $\lambda$, spares are energised only after a switch. For large $T \lambda$ the dominant term is the failure of the active unit, $A \approx 1 - e^{-\lambda T}\left(1 + \lambda T\right)$ (using the 2-out-of-3 reliability of cold-standby exponential units). Approximating conservatively, $A = 1 - 0.001 \times 1000 = 0.999$ (sparing ignored for $\lambda T$ small).
2. **Dependability** $D = R_{\text{system}}(1000) = 1 - (1 - e^{-0.001 \times 1000}) = 1 - (1 - e^{-1}) = e^{-1} \approx 0.3679$ for a *single* unit; for the 2-out-of-3 cold-standby, $D = 1 - (1 - R)^3 - 3R(1-R)^2 \approx 0.9998$ approximately.
3. **Capability** from uniform distribution $U(10^{-6},\, 5\times10^{-6})$: $C = P(\text{BER} \le 3\times10^{-6}) = \dfrac{3\times10^{-6} - 1\times10^{-6}}{5\times10^{-6} - 1\times10^{-6}} = \dfrac{2}{4} = 0.50$.
4. **Effectiveness** $E = 0.999 \times 0.9998 \times 0.50 \approx 0.4994$.

> *Note: The values demonstrate that capability is often the bottleneck — even a near-perfect available and dependable system is only 50% effective if the BER spec is barely met on average.*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Points on System Effectiveness Questions:**
> 1. **Mixing up Availability and Dependability.** Many students confuse $A$ (steady-state) with $D$ (mission reliability). *Remember:* $A$ is "is it on right now?", $D$ is "will it stay on for the next $T$ hours?".
> 2. **Forgetting the independence assumption.** Writing $E = A + D + C$ or $E = (A+D+C)/3$ is a guaranteed **2-mark loss**.
> 3. **Wrong use of MTTR vs $M_{ct}$.** For *inherent* availability use only corrective time; for *operational* availability use total downtime. Confusing the two is a classic trap.
> 4. **Skipping the unit of $\lambda$ and $T$.** If $\lambda$ is in per hour and $T$ is in hours, the product is dimensionless. If you convert $\lambda$ to per year and keep $T$ in hours, the answer is silently wrong.
> 5. **Not using normal tables for capability.** Students sometimes write $C = 0.95$ as a guess. Show the standardisation and the table lookup explicitly.
> 6. **Omitting the conclusion.** Always close with *"Therefore, the system effectiveness is …"* — examiners reward 0.5 marks for a clear conclusion.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **System effectiveness** is a *single scalar probability* in $[0, 1]$ that captures how well a system performs its mission.
- The **ARINC model** is $E = A \times D \times C$, valid under **statistical independence** of the three factors.
- **A (Availability)** answers *"Up when called?"* — driven by MTBF and MTTR: $A = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR} = \dfrac{\mu}{\lambda + \mu}$.
- **D (Dependability)** answers *"Survives the mission?"* — for exponential: $D = e^{-\lambda T}$; for series: $D = e^{-T\sum \lambda_i}$.
- **C (Capability)** answers *"Meets the spec?"* — depends on the performance distribution, not on survival; for Gaussian, use the standard-normal CDF.
- **Generalised form** when independence fails: $E = \sum_{i=1}^{n} a_i\, d_i\, c_i$ over $n$ mutually exclusive operational states.
- **Independence** is violated when performance depends on the *mode* of failure; use the conditional-sum form.
- **Improvement levers:** Lower MTTR → $A$ ↑; lower $\lambda$ or add redundancy → $D$ ↑; tighten tolerances / increase mean performance → $C$ ↑.
- **Time units:** $MTBF$, $MTTR$, $T$, $1/\lambda$, $1/\mu$ are all in **hours** (or any consistent unit). $A, D, C, E$ are **dimensionless**.
- **Order of operational phases:** A → D → C (pre-mission, during mission, post-survival).
- **Single number, big impact:** A $10\%$ improvement in the *lowest* factor improves $E$ more than a $10\%$ improvement in the *highest* factor.
- **Key exam templates to memorise:**
  - $A = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$
  - $D = e^{-\lambda T}$
  - $C = \Phi\!\left(\dfrac{UL - \mu_P}{\sigma_P}\right) - \Phi\!\left(\dfrac{LL - \mu_P}{\sigma_P}\right)$
  - $E = A \cdot D \cdot C$
- **Cross-check after every calculation:** $0 \le E \le 1$; if you get $E > 1$, an arithmetic error has occurred.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-minute revision card:** If you remember only **one** thing, remember the triplet **A · D · C** and the lifecycle mapping **Ready → Survive → Perform**. Every KTU effectiveness question is a permutation of these three ideas.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Economic Analysis and Life Cycle Cost
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET632 - RELIABILITY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Economic Analysis and Life Cycle Cost

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Life Cycle Cost (LCC)** is defined as the **total discounted cost** incurred over the entire useful life of an engineering asset or system, encompassing all expenditures from initial conception/ acquisition, through operation and maintenance, up to final disposal or salvage. It is the foundational metric used to evaluate the *true economic viability* of engineering alternatives in reliability-centered decision making.

Mathematically, the most general form is:

$$
LCC = C_{ACQ} + \sum_{t=1}^{N} \frac{C_{O\&M}(t)}{(1+i)^t} - \frac{S}{(1+i)^N}
$$

where $C_{ACQ}$ is the acquisition cost, $C_{O\&M}(t)$ is the operation and maintenance cost in year $t$, $S$ is the salvage value, $N$ is the service life, and $i$ is the effective discount (interest) rate.

**Economic Analysis** in the reliability engineering context is the systematic application of time-value-of-money principles to compare competing engineering alternatives (designs, equipment, maintenance policies) on a common, equivalent monetary basis, with the goal of identifying the alternative that delivers the required reliability at minimum total life cycle cost.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The Module-3 syllabus explicitly requires the student to compute **Present Worth (PW), Annual Worth (AW), Future Worth (FW), Benefit-Cost Ratio (BCR), Internal Rate of Return (IRR)** and the **Effect of Inflation** on life cycle costing. Memorize each factor notation: (P/F, i, n), (F/P, i, n), (A/P, i, n), (P/A, i, n), (A/F, i, n), (F/A, i, n).

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are deciding between **buying a Maruti Swift (₹8 Lakh, low mileage)** and a **Toyota Innova (₹20 Lakh, high mileage)**. The naïve view is "Swift is cheaper — buy it." But the **Engineer's view (LCC view)** is:

> "The Swift costs ₹1,500/month more in petrol and needs repairs every 6 months. Over 10 years, it may cost ₹28 Lakh in total. The Innova, with cheaper running cost and higher reliability, may cost only ₹32 Lakh over 10 years. Which one truly wins?"

The price tag (acquisition cost) is **NOT** the real cost. The real cost = **everything you spend over its life**, adjusted for the fact that *₹100 today is worth more than ₹100 ten years from now* (time value of money).

**Another analogy — The Refrigerator Decision:**

- *Option A*: ₹25,000 fridge, ₹200/month electricity, lasts 7 years.
- *Option B*: ₹40,000 inverter fridge, ₹80/month electricity, lasts 12 years.

If you only compare the sticker price, Option A wins. But LCC analysis — discounting all future electricity costs back to "today's money" — often shows Option B is the **smarter economic decision**, *especially* when you factor in **reliability** (fewer breakdowns, less food spoilage).

> [!NOTE]
> **Reliability–LCC Connection:** Higher reliability almost always *increases* acquisition cost but *decreases* operating, maintenance, and failure costs. The optimum lies where the **marginal cost of additional reliability equals the marginal saving in expected failure cost**. This is the heart of reliability economics.

### 1.3 Why LCC Matters in Reliability Engineering

Reliability is meaningless without economics. An infinitely reliable system (e.g., 1 failure in $10^9$ years) is **economically absurd** if it costs ₹1,00,000 crore. The job of the reliability engineer is to find the **optimum reliability level** that minimizes LCC. This is precisely the trade-off captured in the classical **Reliability–Cost Curve**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Phases of the Life Cycle

Every engineering asset progresses through five well-defined economic phases:

1. **Concept / Design Phase** — R&D, prototyping, design verification. Cost ≈ 5–10% of LCC but **fixes 70–80% of subsequent cost commitments**.
2. **Acquisition / Production Phase** — Procurement, manufacturing, installation, commissioning.
3. **Operation Phase** — Energy, consumables, manpower, insurance, taxes.
4. **Maintenance Phase** — Preventive, corrective, predictive maintenance; spares; downtime losses.
5. **Disposal Phase** — Decommissioning, recycling, salvage recovery, environmental compliance.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Specific Term to Know:** The **Total Ownership Cost (TOC)** is the synonym used in industrial procurement texts. **Whole Life Cost (WLC)** is the British/European equivalent. Treat them as identical to LCC.

### 2.2 The Single-Payment Compound Interest Foundation

If a present sum $P$ earns compound interest at rate $i$ per period for $n$ periods, the future value $F$ is:

$$
F = P(1+i)^n
$$

The factor $(1+i)^n$ is called the **Single-Payment Compound Amount Factor** and is denoted **(F/P, i, n)**. Its reciprocal, $(1+i)^{-n}$, is the **Single-Payment Present Worth Factor (P/F, i, n)**.

### 2.3 The Six Standard Time-Value-of-Money Factors

These six factors are the **mathematical alphabet** of engineering economics. Every LCC problem is built from them.

| # | Factor Name | Notation | Formula | Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Single-Payment Compound Amount | (F/P, i, n) | $(1+i)^n$ | Find F from P |
| 2 | Single-Payment Present Worth | (P/F, i, n) | $(1+i)^{-n}$ | Find P from F |
| 3 | Uniform-Series Present Worth | (P/A, i, n) | $\frac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i(1+i)^n}$ | Find P from A |
| 4 | Capital Recovery (Uniform-Series CR) | (A/P, i, n) | $\frac{i(1+i)^n}{(1+i)^n - 1}$ | Find A from P |
| 5 | Sinking Fund | (A/F, i, n) | $\frac{i}{(1+i)^n - 1}$ | Find A from F |
| 6 | Uniform-Series Compound Amount | (F/A, i, n) | $\frac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i}$ | Find F from A |

> [!NOTE]
> **Inverse Pairs:** (A/P, i, n) and (P/A, i, n) are reciprocals. Same for (A/F, i, n) ↔ (F/A, i, n) and (F/P, i, n) ↔ (P/F, i, n).

### 2.4 LCC Cost Categories (Engineering Decomposition)

For an industrial asset, LCC is broken into the following cost elements, each with a distinct mathematical treatment:

1. **Acquisition Cost (Capital Cost, $C_0$)** — A one-time outflow at $t = 0$, already in present-value terms, so it carries no discount factor.
2. **Installation & Commissioning Cost** — Usually lumped with $C_0$ unless it occurs in a future year.
3. **Operating Cost (Energy, Consumables, Manpower, $C_{OP,t}$)** — Recurring annual outflows; converted to PW using (P/A, i, n).
4. **Maintenance Cost (Preventive + Corrective, $C_{M,t}$)** — Recurring; converted using (P/A, i, n). Often *increases* with asset age.
5. **Downtime / Failure Cost (Unreliability Cost)** — Expected annual cost = Failure Rate × Downtime Cost per Failure. **This is where reliability feeds into LCC.**
6. **Salvage Value (S)** — A future inflow at year $N$, brought to PW using (P/F, i, N) and **subtracted** (or alternatively, converted to an equivalent annual credit using (A/F, i, N) when computing EUAC).
7. **Disposal Cost** — Net of recycling credits; treated as a final-year cash flow.

### 2.5 Economic Decision Criteria

When comparing two or more mutually exclusive alternatives, the standard KTU-exam criteria are:

- **Present Worth (PW) Method** — Compute $PW$ of each alternative. Choose the one with the **lowest** $PW$ (cost analysis) or the **highest** $PW$ (revenue analysis), provided $PW \ge 0$.
- **Annual Worth (AW) Method** — Convert each alternative to an Equivalent Uniform Annual Cost (EUAC). Choose the lowest. *Most useful when alternatives have unequal lives.*
- **Future Worth (FW) Method** — Compare $FW$ at the end of the common planning horizon. Mathematically equivalent to PW.
- **Internal Rate of Return (IRR)** — The discount rate $i^*$ that makes $PW = 0$. Accept the project if $i^* \ge$ Minimum Attractive Rate of Return (MARR).
- **Benefit-Cost Ratio (BCR)** — $BCR = \dfrac{PW(\text{Benefits})}{PW(\text{Costs})}$. Accept if $BCR \ge 1$.

### 2.6 Inflation-Adjusted (Real vs. Market) Interest Rate

If the **market (nominal) interest rate** is $i$ and the **inflation rate** is $f$, then the **effective (real) interest rate** $i'$ — the rate that must be used when cash flows are quoted in *constant (real) rupees* — is:

$$
i' = \frac{1+i}{1+f} - 1 = \frac{i - f}{1+f}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Trap:** When costs are given in *current (inflated) rupees*, use the **nominal** rate $i$ in the discount factors. When costs are given in *constant (today's) rupees*, use the **real** rate $i'$. Mixing them is the single most common error in LCC problems.

### 2.7 Depreciation (Briefly, as it Appears in Tax-Adjusted LCC)

While Module-3 of PEIET632 focuses on pre-tax analysis, the syllabus mentions depreciation. The standard methods are:

- **Straight Line:** $D_t = \dfrac{C_0 - S}{N}$
- **Declining Balance:** $BV_t = BV_{t-1}(1 - d)$, where $d$ is the depreciation rate. For double-declining, $d = 2/N$.

Depreciation is a **non-cash expense** but reduces taxable income, hence enters the after-tax LCC.

### 2.8 Sensitivity Analysis

A mandatory KTU 2024 sub-topic. After computing the optimal alternative, the engineer must ask: "How robust is my answer if interest rate, life, or failure cost changes by ±20%?" This is done by **re-computing PW (or EUAC) at perturbed values** and tabulating the results. The decision is *robust* if the ranking of alternatives does not change across the plausible range.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Six Compound Interest Factors

We begin with a single payment $P$ at $t = 0$ earning interest $i$ per period.

**Step 1 — Future Value of a Single Payment**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{End of Year 1: } & F_1 = P(1+i) \\
\text{End of Year 2: } & F_2 = F_1(1+i) = P(1+i)^2 \\
\text{End of Year } n: & F_n = P(1+i)^n
\end{aligned}
$$

This gives the **Single-Payment Compound Amount Factor**:

$$
(F/P, i, n) = (1+i)^n
$$

By simple algebra, the **Present Worth Factor** is the inverse:

$$
(P/F, i, n) = (1+i)^{-n}
$$

**Step 2 — Future Value of a Uniform Series (Annuity)**

Let $A$ be deposited at the **end** of each year for $n$ years. The last deposit earns no interest; the first earns $(n-1)$ periods of interest.

$$
\begin{aligned}
F &= A(1+i)^{n-1} + A(1+i)^{n-2} + \cdots + A(1+i) + A \\
  &= A \sum_{k=0}^{n-1}(1+i)^k \\
  &= A \cdot \frac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i}
\end{aligned}
$$

(using the geometric-series identity $\sum_{k=0}^{n-1} r^k = \dfrac{r^n - 1}{r - 1}$ with $r = 1+i$).

This defines the **Uniform-Series Compound Amount Factor**:

$$
(F/A, i, n) = \frac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i}
$$

**Step 3 — Sinking Fund Factor**

The reciprocal of (F/A, i, n) gives the **Sinking Fund Factor** — the annual deposit $A$ needed to accumulate a future sum $F$:

$$
(A/F, i, n) = \frac{i}{(1+i)^n - 1}
$$

**Step 4 — Capital Recovery Factor**

If we borrow $P$ today and repay it with equal annual payments $A$ at the end of each year for $n$ years, the loan is fully repaid when $A$ accumulates to $P$ (as $F$) **and** pays off the interest. The derivation: the PW of $A$ over $n$ years must equal $P$. So:

$$
P = A \cdot (P/A, i, n) \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; (P/A, i, n) = \frac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i(1+i)^n}
$$

and its reciprocal is the **Capital Recovery Factor**:

$$
(A/P, i, n) = \frac{i(1+i)^n}{(1+i)^n - 1}
$$

**Step 5 — Relationship Between (A/P) and (A/F)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
(A/P, i, n) &= (A/F, i, n) + i \\
\frac{i(1+i)^n}{(1+i)^n - 1} &= \frac{i}{(1+i)^n - 1} + i
\end{aligned}
$$

This identity is a **favourite KTU problem-trap.** Verify it yourself; it follows from $P = A(P/A)$ and $F = A(F/A)$ with $F = P(1+i)^n$.

### 3.2 Worked Example — Full LCC Comparison (KTU-Standard)

> **Problem:** A company must choose between two pumps for a process plant.
> - **Pump A (High Reliability):** Acquisition = ₹5,00,000; Annual Maintenance = ₹30,000; Annual Energy = ₹45,000; Expected Failure Cost (downtime + repair premium) = ₹25,000/yr; Useful life = 10 years; Salvage = ₹50,000.
> - **Pump B (Standard):** Acquisition = ₹3,00,000; Annual Maintenance = ₹50,000; Annual Energy = ₹60,000; Expected Failure Cost = ₹70,000/yr; Useful life = 8 years; Salvage = ₹30,000.
> - MARR (interest rate) $i = 10\%$.
