> - Both have to be replaced repeatedly; assume the **least common multiple (LCM) of lives = 40 years** for fair comparison.

**Solution Strategy:** Compute PW of LCC over the 40-year analysis horizon.

**Step 1 — Identify the cash flow streams for each pump over 40 years.**

Pump A repeats every 10 years: 4 cycles. Pump B repeats every 8 years: 5 cycles.

**Step 2 — Compute the PW of one cycle of each pump, then scale.**

For Pump A over 10 years:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{A,\text{cycle}} &= 5{,}00{,}000 + (30{,}000 + 45{,}000 + 25{,}000)(P/A, 10\%, 10) \\
&\quad - 50{,}000 \cdot (P/F, 10\%, 10)
\end{aligned}
$$

Look up the factors (or compute):

$$
(P/A, 10\%, 10) = \frac{(1.10)^{10} - 1}{0.10(1.10)^{10}} = \frac{1.5937}{0.2594} = 6.1446
$$

$$
(P/F, 10\%, 10) = (1.10)^{-10} = 0.3855
$$

Substitute:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{A,\text{cycle}} &= 5{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}00{,}000(6.1446) - 50{,}000(0.3855) \\
&= 5{,}00{,}000 + 6{,}14{,}460 - 19{,}275 \\
&= ₹10{,}95{,}185
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Total PW over 40 years for Pump A (using series present-worth factor for repeating cycles).**

Since cycles are identical and back-to-back, the PW of 4 cycles = $C_{A,\text{cycle}} \cdot (P/A, 10\%, 40) / (P/A, 10\%, 10)$. Equivalently, use the "uniform-series of identical blocks" trick:

$$
PW_{A,40} = C_{A,\text{cycle}} \cdot \frac{(P/A, 10\%, 40)}{(P/A, 10\%, 10)}
$$

Compute:

$$
(P/A, 10\%, 40) = \frac{(1.10)^{40} - 1}{0.10(1.10)^{40}} = \frac{44.259 - 1}{4.4259} = \frac{43.259}{4.4259} = 9.779
$$

So:

$$
PW_{A,40} = 10{,}95{,}185 \cdot \frac{9.779}{6.1446} = 10{,}95{,}185 \cdot 1.5916 = ₹17{,}42{,}876
$$

**Step 4 — Pump B over 8 years:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
(P/A, 10\%, 8) &= \frac{(1.10)^8 - 1}{0.10(1.10)^8} = \frac{1.1436 - 1}{0.11436} = 5.3349 \\
(P/F, 10\%, 8) &= (1.10)^{-8} = 0.4665
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{B,\text{cycle}} &= 3{,}00{,}000 + (50{,}000 + 60{,}000 + 70{,}000)(5.3349) - 30{,}000(0.4665) \\
&= 3{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}80{,}000(5.3349) - 13{,}995 \\
&= 3{,}00{,}000 + 9{,}60{,}282 - 13{,}995 \\
&= ₹12{,}46{,}287
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Total PW of Pump B over 40 years (5 cycles):**

$$
(P/A, 10\%, 40) = 9.779,\quad (P/A, 10\%, 8) = 5.3349
$$

$$
PW_{B,40} = 12{,}46{,}287 \cdot \frac{9.779}{5.3349} = 12{,}46{,}287 \cdot 1.8331 = ₹22{,}84{,}532
$$

**Step 6 — Decision:**

$$
PW_{A,40} = ₹17{,}42{,}876 \;\; < \;\; PW_{B,40} = ₹22{,}84{,}532
$$

**Pump A is preferred**, saving approximately **₹5.4 Lakh in PW terms over 40 years**, *despite* the higher acquisition cost. This is the textbook illustration of why **LCC ≠ purchase price**.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Method Selection Cheat Sheet

| Situation | Preferred Method | Why |
|---|---|---|
| Alternatives have **equal lives** | PW or AW | Either is straightforward |
| Alternatives have **unequal lives** | AW (EUAC) or PW over LCM | Handles time mismatch |
| **Single project**, not comparison | PW or IRR | No need for AW |
| Project is a **public/infrastructure** with social benefits | BCR | Captures benefit–cost balance |
| Comparing to a **do-nothing** baseline | Incremental BCR or Incremental IRR | Proper MARR check |
| Cash flows quoted in **today's rupees** with **nominal** rate given | First convert to real rate $i'$ | Avoid inflation bias |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Module-3 Internal-Choice Pattern:** A 14-mark question almost always presents **two alternatives with unequal lives**, requires you to (a) compute LCC of each over a 20–40 year horizon, and (b) recommend with a sensitivity check at $i \pm 2\%$.

### 3.4 Numerical Verification of the (A/P) ≡ (A/F) + i Identity

Let $i = 10\%$, $n = 5$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
(A/P, 10\%, 5) &= \frac{0.10(1.10)^5}{(1.10)^5 - 1} = \frac{0.10 \times 1.6105}{0.6105} = \frac{0.16105}{0.6105} = 0.2638 \\
(A/F, 10\%, 5) &= \frac{0.10}{(1.10)^5 - 1} = \frac{0.10}{0.6105} = 0.1638 \\
(A/F) + i &= 0.1638 + 0.10 = 0.2638 \;\;\checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Life Cycle Cost — Phased Cash Flow Map

```mermaid
graph TD

    A1 --> A2["t = 1 to N-1: Operation"]:::phase

    A3 -.->|Preventive, corrective, downtime| CM["Maintenance Cost CM"]:::cash
    A4 -.->|Recovery or disposal| S["Salvage Value S"]:::cash

    C0 --> LCC["LCC = PW of all outflows - PW of salvage"]:::total

    CM --> LCC
    S --> LCC
```

```mermaid
graph TD

    Q1 -- "Yes" --> Q2["Equal useful lives?"]:::decide
    Q1 -- "Single project only" --> PW1["Use PW or IRR method"]:::method

    Q2 -- "Equal lives" --> M1["Compute PW of LCC for each"]:::method
    Q2 -- "Unequal lives" --> M2["Use LCM of lives OR EUAC"]:::method

    M1 --> S1["Select lowest PW"]:::result
    M2 --> S2["Select lowest EUAC or PW over LCM"]:::result
    PW1 --> S3["Accept if PW >= 0 or IRR >= MARR"]:::result

    S1 --> SENS["Perform Sensitivity Analysis +/- 20%"]:::check
    S2 --> SENS
    S3 --> SENS
    SENS --> DONE["Final Recommendation"]:::result
```

```mermaid

    R["Reliability R t"]:::axis
    CR["Reliability Cost (increases with R)"]:::curve
    CF["Expected Failure Cost (decreases with R)"]:::curve

    R -- "R is low, CF dominates" --> LCCX
    R -- "R is high, CR dominates" --> LCCX

    CF -.->|"Monotonically falling"| LCCX

    OPT["Optimum Reliability R* minimizes LCC"]:::optimum
    LCCX --> OPT
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Style]**

**Q1. Define Life Cycle Cost (LCC). List any four categories of cost included in LCC.**

**Model Answer:**
LCC is the sum of all discounted costs incurred over the entire useful life of an asset, from conception to disposal, expressed in equivalent present-day money.

$$
LCC = C_{ACQ} + \sum_{t=1}^{N} \frac{C_{O\&M}(t)}{(1+i)^t} - \frac{S}{(1+i)^N}
$$

Four categories: (i) **Acquisition / Capital Cost**, (ii) **Installation & Commissioning Cost**, (iii) **Operation & Maintenance Cost** (including energy, labour, spares, downtime), (iv) **Disposal / Decommissioning Cost** (offset by salvage value). *(3 marks)*

---

**Q2. State the relationship between the Capital Recovery Factor (A/P, i, n) and the Sinking Fund Factor (A/F, i, n).**

**Model Answer:**
The Capital Recovery Factor equals the Sinking Fund Factor plus the interest rate:

$$
(A/P, i, n) = (A/F, i, n) + i
$$

This is because repaying a loan of $P$ over $n$ years involves both (a) accumulating the principal via the sinking fund, and (b) paying interest on the outstanding balance each year. *(3 marks)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Question (Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam - December 2024 Expected Pattern]**

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Full LCC Comparison with Unequal Lives

A firm must select between two CNC machines:

- **Machine X:** First cost = ₹12,00,000; Annual operating + maintenance = ₹1,80,000; Life = 8 years; Salvage = ₹1,50,000.
- **Machine Y:** First cost = ₹16,00,000; Annual operating + maintenance = ₹1,20,000; Life = 10 years; Salvage = ₹2,00,000.

MARR = 12% per year. Both machines will be replaced repeatedly. Use a 40-year analysis horizon.

**(a)** Compute the Present Worth of Life Cycle Cost for each machine and recommend the economically superior alternative. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Repeat the analysis if the MARR rises to 15% and perform a sensitivity check. Comment on the robustness of the recommendation. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Q-A:**

**Part (a) — At MARR = 12%**

Step 1: Look up the factors.

$$
(P/A, 12\%, 8) = \frac{(1.12)^8 - 1}{0.12(1.12)^8} = \frac{1.4740}{0.17688} = 4.9676
$$

$$
(P/F, 12\%, 8) = (1.12)^{-8} = 0.4039
$$

$$
(P/A, 12\%, 10) = \frac{(1.12)^{10} - 1}{0.12(1.12)^{10}} = \frac{2.1060}{0.25272} = 8.3332
$$

$$
(P/F, 12\%, 10) = (1.12)^{-10} = 0.3220
$$

$$
(P/A, 12\%, 40) = \frac{(1.12)^{40} - 1}{0.12(1.12)^{40}} = \frac{92.324}{11.079} = 8.3338 \text{ (approx)}
$$

Step 2: One-cycle PW of Machine X (8 years):

$$
\begin{aligned}
PW_{X,\text{cycle}} &= 12{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}80{,}000(4.9676) - 1{,}50{,}000(0.4039) \\
&= 12{,}00{,}000 + 8{,}94{,}168 - 60{,}585 \\
&= ₹20{,}33{,}583
\end{aligned}
$$

Step 3: Total PW of Machine X over 40 years (5 cycles, since 40/8 = 5):

$$
PW_{X,40} = 20{,}33{,}583 \times 5 = ₹1{,}01{,}67{,}915
$$

(equivalently, multiply by $\dfrac{(P/A, 12\%, 40)}{(P/A, 12\%, 8)} = \dfrac{8.3338}{4.9676} = 1.6776 \approx 5/3$, and the cycle PW × 5 = same result).

Step 4: One-cycle PW of Machine Y (10 years):

$$
\begin{aligned}
PW_{Y,\text{cycle}} &= 16{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}20{,}000(8.3332) - 2{,}00{,}000(0.3220) \\
&= 16{,}00{,}000 + 9{,}99{,}984 - 64{,}400 \\
&= ₹25{,}35{,}584
\end{aligned}
$$

Step 5: Total PW of Machine Y over 40 years (4 cycles, since 40/10 = 4):

$$
PW_{Y,40} = 25{,}35{,}584 \times 4 = ₹1{,}01{,}42{,}336
$$

Step 6: Recommendation.

$$
PW_{Y,40} = ₹1{,}01{,}42{,}336 \;\;\text{vs.}\;\; PW_{X,40} = ₹1{,}01{,}67{,}915
$$

**Machine Y is preferred**, saving approximately **₹25,579** in PW terms — a thin margin, signalling that the decision is *sensitive* to assumptions. *(7 marks distributed: 2 for factor values, 3 for cycle PW, 2 for 40-year PW and decision.)*

---

**Part (b) — At MARR = 15%**

Recompute with $i = 15\%$:

$$
(P/A, 15\%, 8) = \frac{(1.15)^8 - 1}{0.15(1.15)^8} = \frac{2.0990}{0.31486} = 4.4873
$$

$$
(P/F, 15\%, 8) = (1.15)^{-8} = 0.3269
$$

$$
(P/A, 15\%, 10) = \frac{(1.15)^{10} - 1}{0.15(1.15)^{10}} = \frac{3.0463}{0.45694} = 5.0188
$$

$$
(P/F, 15\%, 10) = (1.15)^{-10} = 0.2472
$$

Machine X (8 years):

$$
\begin{aligned}
PW_{X,\text{cycle}} &= 12{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}80{,}000(4.4873) - 1{,}50{,}000(0.3269) \\
&= 12{,}00{,}000 + 8{,}07{,}714 - 49{,}035 \\
&= ₹19{,}58{,}679
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
PW_{X,40} = 19{,}58{,}679 \times 5 = ₹97{,}93{,}395
$$

Machine Y (10 years):

$$
\begin{aligned}
PW_{Y,\text{cycle}} &= 16{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}20{,}000(5.0188) - 2{,}00{,}000(0.2472) \\
&= 16{,}00{,}000 + 6{,}02{,}256 - 49{,}440 \\
&= ₹21{,}52{,}816
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
PW_{Y,40} = 21{,}52{,}816 \times 4 = ₹86{,}11{,}264
$$

**At 15%, Machine Y is now preferred by ₹11,82,131** — a much larger gap.

**Sensitivity Conclusion:**
| MARR | PW of X (40 yr) | PW of Y (40 yr) | Preferred |
|---|---|---|---|
| 12% | ₹1,01,67,915 | ₹1,01,42,336 | **Y** (margin ₹0.26 L) |
| 15% | ₹97,93,395 | ₹86,11,264 | **Y** (margin ₹11.82 L) |

**The recommendation is robust** — Machine Y is preferred at both rates. The decision is *more* robust at higher MARR. The very thin margin at 12%, however, indicates that if failure rates or maintenance costs deviate from the assumed values, the ranking could flip. A full sensitivity analysis on operating cost (±25%) should be performed. *(7 marks: 3 for new PW at 15%, 2 for comparison, 2 for sensitivity commentary.)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative (Internal Choice)

A company is evaluating a reliability improvement program for a critical pump. The current annual expected failure cost is ₹8,00,000. A proposed program costs ₹15,00,000 initially and ₹2,00,000 per year, but reduces the expected failure cost to ₹2,50,000 per year. The program lasts 10 years with negligible salvage. MARR = 10%.

**(a)** Using the Benefit–Cost Ratio (BCR) method, determine whether the program should be accepted. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compute the Internal Rate of Return (IRR) of the program and interpret. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Q-B:**

**Part (a) — BCR Method**

Annual benefit from the program:

$$
B = (\text{old failure cost}) - (\text{new failure cost}) = 8{,}00{,}000 - 2{,}50{,}000 = ₹5{,}50{,}000/\text{yr}
$$

Annual cost of the program (excluding the savings it creates):

$$
C_{\text{annual}} = ₹2{,}00{,}000/\text{yr}
$$

Initial cost: $₹15,00,000$ at $t = 0$.

Convert to PW using $i = 10\%$, $n = 10$:

$$
(P/A, 10\%, 10) = 6.1446
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
PW(\text{Benefits}) &= 5{,}50{,}000 \times 6.1446 = ₹33{,}79{,}530 \\
PW(\text{Costs}) &= 15{,}00{,}000 + 2{,}00{,}000 \times 6.1446 = 15{,}00{,}000 + 12{,}28{,}920 = ₹27{,}28{,}920 \\
BCR &= \frac{33{,}79{,}530}{27{,}28{,}920} = 1.238
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $BCR = 1.24 > 1.0$, **the program is economically justified**. Every ₹1 invested yields ₹1.24 in benefits. *(7 marks: 2 for identifying B & C, 2 for PW computation, 2 for ratio, 1 for decision.)*

---

**Part (b) — IRR Method**

Set the net PW to zero and solve for $i^*$:

$$
0 = -15{,}00{,}000 + (5{,}50{,}000 - 2{,}00{,}000)(P/A, i^*, 10)
$$

$$
(P/A, i^*, 10) = \frac{15{,}00{,}000}{3{,}50{,}000} = 4.2857
$$

We need to find $i^*$ such that $\dfrac{(1+i^*)^{10} - 1}{i^*(1+i^*)^{10}} = 4.2857$.

Trial $i = 20\%$: $(P/A, 20\%, 10) = 4.1925$ → too low.
Trial $i = 18\%$: $(P/A, 18\%, 10) = 4.4941$ → too high.
Linear interpolation:

$$
i^* \approx 18\% + (4.4941 - 4.2857) \times \frac{20\% - 18\%}{4.4941 - 4.1925} = 18\% + 0.2084 \times \frac{2}{0.3016} = 18\% + 1.38\% = 19.38\%
$$

More precisely, try $i = 19\%$: $(P/A, 19\%, 10) = 4.3389$ → still high.
Try $i = 19.5\%$: $(P/A, 19.5\%, 10) = 4.2786$ → slightly low.
Interpolate: $i^* \approx 19\% + \dfrac{4.3389 - 4.2857}{4.3389 - 4.2786} \times 0.5\% = 19\% + 0.53 \times 0.5\% = 19.27\%$

**$i^* \approx 19.3\%$.**

Since $i^* = 19.3\% \gg MARR = 10\%$, the program is **strongly justified**. The high IRR confirms the BCR result. The extra 9.3 percentage points of return above MARR is the project's "margin of safety" against estimation errors. *(7 marks: 3 for setting PW = 0, 2 for trials, 2 for interpolation and interpretation.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mark Losers:**
> 1. **Forgetting to bring salvage to present worth** using (P/F, i, N). Many students subtract salvage at face value → loses 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Mixing nominal and real rates** in inflation problems. Always state explicitly which rate is in use, then pick the matching cost stream.
> 3. **Not using the LCM of lives** for unequal-life comparisons. The KTU 2024 scheme explicitly tests this; using only one cycle of each alternative is **wrong**.
> 4. **In IRR problems**, forgetting that if cash flows change sign more than once, multiple IRRs exist (Descartes' rule). The trial-and-interpolation method is acceptable only when the sign changes **once**.
> 5. **Confusing AW (Annual Worth) with Annual Cost.** Annual Worth includes *all* equivalent annual amounts — operating, maintenance, *and* the annualized capital cost via (A/P, i, n).

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (High-Density Revision Checklist)

- **LCC = Acquisition + Σ discounted O&M – discounted Salvage** (over the analysis horizon).
- **Six Standard Factors** — (F/P), (P/F), (F/A), (A/F), (P/A), (A/P). Always quote the factor notation, the rate $i$, and the periods $n$ in your solution.
- **(A/P, i, n) = (A/F, i, n) + i** — a high-yield identity for KTU 2024.
- **Real rate formula:** $i' = \dfrac{1+i}{1+f} - 1 = \dfrac{i - f}{1 + f}$. Use $i'$ with *constant* rupees; use $i$ with *inflated* rupees.
- **Decision rule (cost minimization):** Lowest PW ≡ Lowest AW ≡ Lowest FW (all mathematically equivalent).
- **Unequal lives:** Always compare over the **Least Common Multiple (LCM)** of the lives, or use the **EUAC** method.
- **Expected Failure Cost** = Failure Rate × Downtime Cost per Failure. This is the bridge between *reliability* and *economics*.
- **IRR = discount rate making PW = 0.** Accept if $i^* \geq MARR$.
- **BCR = PW(Benefits) / PW(Costs).** Accept if $BCR \geq 1$.
- **Sensitivity analysis** is mandatory in KTU 2024 14-mark answers — vary MARR by ±2% to ±5% and recompute PW.
- **Depreciation** (straight-line, declining-balance) is non-cash; relevant only for after-tax LCC.
- **Repeatability assumption** for PW over LCM: identical, back-to-back cycles — verify with the problem statement.
- **KTU 2024 Module-3 mnemonics:** "*Plan, Buy, Run, Fix, Junk*" — the five LCC phases.
- **LCC does not equal price** — the most important conceptual takeaway. Reliability costs money, but unreliability costs *more*.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Reliability Allocation - Optimal Allocations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET632 - RELIABILITY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Reliability Allocation - Optimal Allocations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Reliability Allocation** is the inverse process of reliability prediction. Given an overall target system reliability $R_s^*$ (or $R^*_s$) and a set of design constraints (cost, weight, volume, maintainability), reliability allocation distributes the reliability goals among the subsystems, subassemblies, or components in a logical, equitable, and achievable manner.

**Optimal Reliability Allocation** is a special, mathematically rigorous branch of reliability allocation in which the allocation is performed using **mathematical optimization techniques** (such as Lagrange multipliers, Non-Linear Programming (NLP), Integer Programming, or Dynamic Programming) such that a single engineering objective function is satisfied to its extremum (maximum or minimum) while honoring all design constraints.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> Optimal allocation is the procedure of distributing reliability among components to either **maximize the system reliability** for a fixed allowable cost, or to **minimize the total system cost** for a fixed minimum reliability requirement.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are planning a **road trip from Kerala to Delhi** with a strict budget of ₹15,000 (cost constraint) and you must reach before 9:00 PM tomorrow (reliability/time constraint). You must decide how much to spend on fuel, food, tolls, and a contingency buffer.

- **Equal Allocation** would mean: spend ₹3,750 on each (fuel, food, toll, buffer). This is wasteful because fuel matters more than tolls.
- **Proportional Allocation** would mean: spend more on what is most critical (fuel).
- **Optimal Allocation** would mean: dynamically adjust each expense using calculus so that the *marginal benefit per rupee* spent is identical across all categories. The last rupee spent on fuel, food, and tolls each provides the *exact same increase in your "on-time probability"*.

In engineering terms, optimal allocation balances the **marginal cost of reliability improvement** across all subsystems. The point of optimality occurs when:

$$\frac{1}{R_1}\frac{\partial C_1}{\partial R_1} = \frac{1}{R_2}\frac{\partial C_2}{\partial R_2} = \dots = \frac{1}{n}\frac{1}{R_n}\frac{\partial C_n}{\partial R_n} = \lambda$$

This is the **Equal Marginal Principle** of optimization.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metrics Used in KTU Problems:**
> - System reliability target: $R_s^*$ (dimensionless, $0 \le R \le 1$)
> - Subsystem reliability: $R_i$ for $i = 1, 2, \dots, n$
> - Cost of subsystem $i$ as a function of reliability: $C_i(R_i)$
> - Lagrange multiplier: $\lambda$ (also denoted $K$ in many textbooks)
> - Time period of operation: $T$ (hours/cycles)

## 1.3 Why Optimal Allocation is Necessary

Heuristic methods (Equal, AGREE, ARINC) provide only **rule-of-thumb** numbers. They are fast but rarely the most economical. For high-cost, high-consequence systems (aircraft, nuclear plants, satellites), even a 0.1% gain in reliability can save crores of rupees over the operational life. Optimal allocation therefore becomes an **economic necessity**, not just an engineering preference.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two Canonical Optimization Problems

There are two equivalent dual formulations. Let $C_T$ be the total allowable cost, and $R_s^*$ be the minimum system reliability.

**Problem 1 (Cost Minimization):**
Minimize total cost
$$C_T = \sum_{i=1}^{n} C_i(R_i)$$
subject to:
$$\prod_{i=1}^{n} R_i \geq R_s^* \quad \text{and} \quad 0 \le R_i \le 1$$

**Problem 2 (Reliability Maximization):**
Maximize system reliability
$$R_s = \prod_{i=1}^{n} R_i$$
subject to:
$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} C_i(R_i) \leq C_T$$

Both yield identical optimal $R_i^*$ when solved correctly.

## 2.2 Standard Cost-Reliability Function

The most commonly used mathematical form in KTU problems is the **exponential cost-reliability function**:

$$C_i(R_i) = a_i + b_i \cdot \left[\frac{-\ln R_i}{R_i}\right]$$

Where:
- $a_i$ = fixed cost (procurement, design) of subsystem $i$
- $b_i$ = variable cost coefficient of subsystem $i$
- $-\ln R_i / R_i$ is the reliability improvement factor

> [!IMPORTANT]
> An alternative textbook form (used in some KTU numericals) is:
> $$C_i(R_i) = e^{\left(a_i \cdot \frac{1 - R_i}{R_i}\right)}$$
> This is purely exponential, modelling more aggressive cost escalation for high reliability.

## 2.3 The Lagrange Multiplier Method (Derivation in Section 3)

For the **cost-minimization problem** in a **series system**, the Lagrangian is:

$$\mathcal{L}(R_1, R_2, \dots, R_n, \lambda) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} C_i(R_i) - \lambda \left[\ln R_s^* - \sum_{i=1}^{n} \ln R_i\right]$$

Setting $\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial R_i} = 0$ gives the optimality condition:

$$\frac{\partial C_i}{\partial R_i} = \frac{\lambda}{R_i}$$

This is the **Equal Marginal Principle**: at the optimum, the marginal cost of improving each subsystem, weighted by $1/R_i$, is the same constant $\lambda$.

## 2.4 Closed-Form Solution (Exponential Cost Function)

Using $C_i(R_i) = a_i + b_i \cdot \left(\frac{-\ln R_i}{R_i}\right)$, the derivative is:

$$\frac{\partial C_i}{\partial R_i} = b_i \cdot \left(\frac{1}{R_i^2} - \frac{\ln R_i}{R_i^2}\right) = \frac{b_i}{R_i^2}\left(1 - \ln R_i\right)$$

Applying $\frac{\partial C_i}{\partial R_i} = \frac{\lambda}{R_i}$:

$$\frac{b_i (1 - \ln R_i)}{R_i^2} = \frac{\lambda}{R_i}$$

$$b_i (1 - \ln R_i) = \lambda R_i$$

$$b_i - b_i \ln R_i = \lambda R_i$$

This is a **transcendental equation** and generally solved by numerical/iterative methods (Newton-Raphson, trial-and-error, or graphical). However, in KTU exams, the function is often chosen so that the transcendental equation reduces to a closed form.

## 2.5 Special Case: Linear-Log Cost Function

If $C_i(R_i) = a_i + b_i \cdot (-\ln R_i)$, then:

$$\frac{\partial C_i}{\partial R_i} = \frac{b_i}{R_i}$$

Optimality condition becomes:
$$\frac{b_i}{R_i} = \frac{\lambda}{R_i} \implies b_i = \lambda$$

This means **all $b_i$ must equal $\lambda$** — useful only when the $b_i$ are already equal. For unequal $b_i$, an **approximate closed-form** uses the fact that for high $R_i$, $-\ln R_i \approx 1 - R_i$.

## 2.6 Approximate Closed-Form for Series Systems

A widely used engineering approximation (valid for $R_i > 0.9$):

$$R_i^* = 1 - \frac{(1 - R_s^*)}{n \cdot W_i} \cdot \frac{1}{\sum_{j=1}^{n}\frac{1}{W_j}}$$

Where $W_i$ is the **weighting factor** for subsystem $i$ (based on complexity, criticality, etc.). This is essentially a **weighted equal allocation**, not a true optimum, but KTU sometimes asks this as a simpler variant.

## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Equation | Meaning / Use | Typical Marks |
|---|-------------------|---------------|---------------|
| 1 | $R_s = \prod_{i=1}^{n} R_i$ | Series system reliability (assumed unless stated) | 1–2 |
| 2 | $C_T = \sum_{i=1}^{n} C_i(R_i)$ | Total cost of the system | 1 |
| 3 | $C_i(R_i) = a_i + b_i \cdot \left(\frac{-\ln R_i}{R_i}\right)$ | Standard exponential cost-reliability model | 2 |
| 4 | $\frac{\partial C_i}{\partial R_i} = \frac{\lambda}{R_i}$ | Lagrange optimality condition (equal marginal principle) | 3 |
| 5 | $b_i (1 - \ln R_i) = \lambda R_i$ | Transcendental equation for $R_i^*$ with standard model | 4 |
| 6 | $R_i = e^{-\lambda_i T}$ | Reliability-exponential-failure-rate relationship | 2 |
| 7 | $\sum_{i=1}^{n} \lambda_i = -\frac{\ln R_s^*}{T}$ | Allocation constraint (sum of failure rates) | 2 |
| 8 | $C_i(\lambda_i) = a_i + b_i \lambda_i$ | Linear cost-failure-rate form (simpler numericals) | 2 |
| 9 | $\lambda_i = \frac{b_j}{b_i} \cdot \frac{\lambda_j}{R_j} \cdot R_i$ (etc.) | Ratio relationships from optimality | 3 |
| 10 | $L = \sum C_i - \lambda\left(\ln R_s^* - \sum \ln R_i\right)$ | Lagrangian function for cost minimization | 2 |

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

Optimal reliability allocation is used in:
- **Aerospace**: Allocating MTBF to avionics LRUs (Line Replaceable Units) within an aircraft-level MTBF goal.
- **Nuclear Power**: Distributing failure probability targets to safety-critical systems per ASME/ASME-RA-S standards.
- **Automotive**: ISO 26262 ASIL decomposition (Automotive Safety Integrity Level) requires optimal allocation of safety budgets.
- **Telecommunications**: 5G base station hardware design within tight cost-per-board constraints.
- **Defense**: Missile and UAV subsystems where every gram of weight and every rupee of cost is scrutinized.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: The Lagrangian Optimality Condition

**Problem Statement:**
Minimize $C_T = \sum_{i=1}^{n} C_i(R_i)$ subject to the series-system constraint $R_s = \prod_{i=1}^{n} R_i \geq R_s^*$, with $0 \le R_i \le 1$.

**Step 1 — Restrict to the Active Constraint:**
At the optimum, the constraint is active (binding), so:
$$\prod_{i=1}^{n} R_i = R_s^*$$

**Step 2 — Take the Natural Logarithm (to linearize the product):**
$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} \ln R_i = \ln R_s^*$$

**Step 3 — Construct the Lagrangian Function $\mathcal{L}$:**
$$\mathcal{L} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} C_i(R_i) - \lambda \left[\ln R_s^* - \sum_{i=1}^{n} \ln R_i\right]$$

The term $\lambda$ is the **Lagrange multiplier** (also called the *shadow price* of reliability). The negative sign convention is used so that increasing $\lambda$ corresponds to a tighter reliability constraint.

**Step 4 — Compute the Partial Derivative with respect to $R_i$:**
$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial R_i} = \frac{\partial C_i}{\partial R_i} - \lambda \cdot \left(-\frac{1}{R_i}\right) = \frac{\partial C_i}{\partial R_i} + \frac{\lambda}{R_i}$$

**Step 5 — Set the Partial Derivative to Zero (Stationary Point):**
$$\frac{\partial C_i}{\partial R_i} + \frac{\lambda}{R_i} = 0$$

$$\boxed{\frac{\partial C_i}{\partial R_i} = -\frac{\lambda}{R_i}}$$

**Step 6 — Economic Interpretation (Equal Marginal Principle):**
Re-write as:
$$-R_i \cdot \frac{\partial C_i}{\partial R_i} = \lambda = \text{constant for all } i$$

This means: the **marginal cost of reliability improvement per unit of reliability** is the same across all subsystems. If subsystem A has a lower marginal cost, it should be made more reliable first; allocation proceeds until all marginal costs are equalized.

**Step 7 — Apply Boundary Conditions:**
Any $R_i^* > 1$ (mathematically) is set to $1$. Any $R_i^* < R_{i,\min}$ (a physical floor) is set to $R_{i,\min}$. Re-solve the reduced problem.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Closed-Form Solution for Exponential Cost Function

Given $C_i(R_i) = a_i + b_i \cdot \left(\frac{-\ln R_i}{R_i}\right)$:

**Step 1 — Differentiate with respect to $R_i$:**
Using the quotient rule on $f(R_i) = \frac{-\ln R_i}{R_i}$:

$$\frac{df}{dR_i} = \frac{-\frac{1}{R_i} \cdot R_i - (-\ln R_i) \cdot 1}{R_i^2}$$

$$\frac{df}{dR_i} = \frac{-1 + \ln R_i}{R_i^2} = -\frac{(1 - \ln R_i)}{R_i^2}$$

**Step 2 — So the marginal cost is:**
$$\frac{\partial C_i}{\partial R_i} = -\frac{b_i(1 - \ln R_i)}{R_i^2}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute into Optimality Condition:**
$$-\frac{b_i(1 - \ln R_i)}{R_i^2} = -\frac{\lambda}{R_i}$$

**Step 4 — Simplify:**
$$\frac{b_i(1 - \ln R_i)}{R_i^2} = \frac{\lambda}{R_i}$$

$$b_i(1 - \ln R_i) = \lambda R_i$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $\lambda$ in terms of $R_i$:**
$$\lambda = \frac{b_i (1 - \ln R_i)}{R_i}$$

**Step 6 — Apply the System Constraint:**
Use $\prod_{i=1}^{n} R_i = R_s^*$ to back-solve for $\lambda$, then for each $R_i^*$.

> [!NOTE]
> This is a **transcendental equation** in $R_i$. KTU numericals often use a *simplified* model like $C_i(R_i) = a_i - b_i \ln R_i$ (with no division by $R_i$) to obtain a clean closed form.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Simplified Linear-Log Model (Most Common in KTU Numericals)

**Cost model:** $C_i(R_i) = a_i + b_i \cdot (-\ln R_i)$

**Step 1 — Derivative:**
$$\frac{\partial C_i}{\partial R_i} = \frac{b_i}{R_i}$$

**Step 2 — Optimality condition:**
$$\frac{b_i}{R_i} = \frac{\lambda}{R_i} \implies b_i = \lambda$$

**Step 3 — Apparent contradiction:** All $b_i$ must equal $\lambda$. This works **only** when $b_i$ is identical for all subsystems.

**Step 4 — Practical fix (used in KTU):** Express cost in terms of failure rate $\lambda_i$ instead of reliability $R_i$.

Using $R_i = e^{-\lambda_i T}$:
$$C_i(\lambda_i) = a_i + b_i \lambda_i$$

**Step 5 — Lagrangian becomes:**
$$\mathcal{L} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (a_i + b_i \lambda_i) - \mu \left[\sum_{i=1}^{n} \lambda_i + \frac{\ln R_s^*}{T}\right]$$

**Step 6 — Partial derivative with respect to $\lambda_i$:**
$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \lambda_i} = b_i - \mu = 0 \implies \mu = b_i$$

**Step 7 — Conclusion:** If all $b_i$ are equal, allocate equally. If not, this simple model fails and we must use the **exponential model** of Derivation 2.

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Allocation with Weights (Weighted Optimal Allocation)

When subsystems have unequal criticality, complexity, or operational hours, include weights $w_i$:

$$R_s = \prod_{i=1}^{n} R_i^{w_i}$$

Cost-minimization Lagrangian:
$$\mathcal{L} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} C_i(R_i) - \lambda \left[\ln R_s^* - \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \ln R_i\right]$$

Optimality:
$$\frac{\partial C_i}{\partial R_i} = \frac{\lambda w_i}{R_i}$$

This is the **generalized equal marginal principle** — heavily weighted subsystems receive a larger "slice" of the reliability budget.

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Pattern)

**Problem:** A system has 3 subsystems in series. The cost-reliability functions are:
- $C_1(R_1) = 5 + 10 \cdot \left(\frac{-\ln R_1}{R_1}\right)$
- $C_2(R_2) = 8 + 20 \cdot \left(\frac{-\ln R_2}{R_2}\right)$
- $C_3(R_3) = 3 + 15 \cdot \left(\frac{-\ln R_3}{R_3}\right)$

Target: $R_s^* = 0.95$. Find the optimal $R_i^*$ and minimum total cost.

**Step 1 — Marginal costs:**
$$\frac{\partial C_1}{\partial R_1} = -\frac{10(1 - \ln R_1)}{R_1^2}, \quad \frac{\partial C_2}{\partial R_2} = -\frac{20(1 - \ln R_2)}{R_2^2}, \quad \frac{\partial C_3}{\partial R_3} = -\frac{15(1 - \ln R_3)}{R_3^2}$$

**Step 2 — Apply optimality:**
$$\frac{10(1 - \ln R_1)}{R_1} = \frac{20(1 - \ln R_2)}{R_2} = \frac{15(1 - \ln R_3)}{R_3} = \lambda$$

**Step 3 — Use constraint:** $R_1 R_2 R_3 = 0.95$.

**Step 4 — Trial-and-error (since transcendental):** Assume $R_1 = 0.992$, $R_2 = 0.985$, $R_3 = 0.974$.
- Product = $0.992 \times 0.985 \times 0.974 \approx 0.9516$ ✓
- $\frac{10(1 - \ln 0.992)}{0.992} = \frac{10(1 - (-0.00803))}{0.992} = \frac{10(1.00803)}{0.992} \approx 10.16$
- $\frac{20(1 - \ln 0.985)}{0.985} = \frac{20(1 - (-0.01511))}{0.985} = \frac{20(1.01511)}{0.985} \approx 20.61$
- $\frac{15(1 - \ln 0.974)}{0.974} = \frac{15(1 - (-0.02634))}{0.974} = \frac{15(1.02634)}{0.974} \approx 15.81$

**Step 5 — Close but not equal.** Iterate: reduce $R_2$ slightly to 0.983, increase $R_1$ to 0.993.
- Product = $0.993 \times 0.983 \times 0.974 \approx 0.9508$
- New $\lambda$ values: 10.04, 20.32, 15.81 — still not perfectly equal.

**Step 6 — Converged solution (typical KTU answer):**
$$R_1^* \approx 0.994, \quad R_2^* \approx 0.982, \quad R_3^* \approx 0.975$$

**Step 7 — Minimum total cost:**
$$C_1 = 5 + 10 \cdot \left(\frac{-\ln 0.994}{0.994}\right) = 5 + 10 \cdot 0.00605 \approx 5.06$$
$$C_2 = 8 + 20 \cdot \left(\frac{-\ln 0.982}{0.982}\right) = 8 + 20 \cdot 0.01835 \approx 8.37$$
$$C_3 = 3 + 15 \cdot \left(\frac{-\ln 0.975}{0.975}\right) = 3 + 15 \cdot 0.02564 \approx 3.38$$

$$\boxed{C_T^{\min} \approx 16.81 \text{ units}}$$

## 3.6 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Step | Operation | Result |
|------|-----------|--------|
| 1 | Formulate $C_i(R_i)$ | Given in problem |
| 2 | Build Lagrangian $\mathcal{L}$ | $C_T - \lambda(\ln R_s^* - \sum \ln R_i)$ |
| 3 | $\partial \mathcal{L}/\partial R_i = 0$ | $\partial C_i/\partial R_i = \lambda/R_i$ |
| 4 | Solve for $R_i^*$ | Transcendental; use iteration |
| 5 | Apply boundary checks | $R_i \in [R_{i,\min}, 1]$ |
| 6 | Compute minimum cost | Sum the optimal $C_i(R_i^*)$ |
