The system is functional if and only if a specific Boolean combination (AND, OR, NOT) of component states evaluates to **1**.

In reliability engineering, Boolean algebra is employed to:
- Express **system success paths** (minimal paths) and **failure paths** (minimal cuts) as Boolean polynomials.
- Expand non-series-parallel configurations (e.g., **bridge networks**) into **mutually exclusive (disjoint) products**, enabling exact probabilistic evaluation.
- Handle **dependent failure events** that violate the simple multiplication/independence assumption.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Electric Circuit"**
> Imagine a flashlight with two batteries in a plastic tube. The bulb lights up **only if** both batteries are inserted *correctly* (series logic — **AND**). Now imagine a hall with three emergency exits — the hall is *evacuable* if **at least one** exit is open (parallel logic — **OR**). Boolean algebra is essentially the "switching logic" of electricity applied to components. Each component is a switch: **ON (1)** = working, **OFF (0)** = failed. The system's success function is a logical circuit diagram of switches.

## 1.3 The Structure Function

The **structure function** $\phi: \{0,1\}^n \rightarrow \{0,1\}$ completely describes a coherent system:

$$\phi(\mathbf{X}) = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if system functions} \\ 0 & \text{if system fails} \end{cases}$$

Where each $X_i \in \{0, 1\}$ denotes the state of component $i$ (1 = functioning, 0 = failed).

### Key Properties of a Coherent System
- **Monotonicity:** $\mathbf{X} \geq \mathbf{Y} \Rightarrow \phi(\mathbf{X}) \geq \phi(\mathbf{Y})$ (replacing a failed component by a working one cannot worsen the system).
- **Relevance:** Each component is required for at least one input vector.
- **Non-degenerate:** $\phi(\mathbf{0}) = 0$ and $\phi(\mathbf{1}) = 1$.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic Breakdown

### Step 1 — Identify Component States
Assign $X_i = 1$ (working) or $X_i = 0$ (failed) for every component.

### Step 2 — Write the Logical Expression
Use **AND (·)** for series, **OR (+)** for parallel connections.

### Step 3 — Identify Mutually Exclusive Events
For **independent components**, the joint probability of a Boolean product equals the product of individual reliabilities. However, terms like $X_1 X_2$ and $X_1 X_3$ are **NOT disjoint** because the event $X_1 X_2$ overlaps with $X_1 X_3$ on $X_1$. We must convert to **disjoint (sum of mutually exclusive) products** before summing probabilities.

### Step 4 — Boolean Expansion (Shannon's Theorem)
For any Boolean function $\phi$ and any variable $X_i$:

$$\phi(\mathbf{X}) = X_i \cdot \phi(1_i, \mathbf{X}) + \overline{X_i} \cdot \phi(0_i, \mathbf{X})$$

This is the **single-variable expansion** used repeatedly to disjoint product terms.

### Step 5 — Apply Probability Rules
For disjoint events: $P(\cup A_j) = \sum P(A_j)$.
For independent components: $P(X_1 X_2) = R_1 \cdot R_2$.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains the **high-yield formulas** the KTU examiner expects. Memorize the symbolic forms, not just the verbal descriptions.

| **System / Concept** | **Boolean Structure $\phi$** | **Reliability Expression** | **Boundary Conditions** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Series (n components)** | $\phi = X_1 \cdot X_2 \cdots X_n$ | $R_s = \prod_{i=1}^{n} R_i$ | Valid only if all events independent |
| **Parallel (n components)** | $\phi = 1 - \prod(1 - X_i)$ | $R_s = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n}(1 - R_i)$ | Disjoint form after expansion |
| **2-component Parallel** | $\phi = X_1 + X_2 - X_1 X_2$ | $R_s = R_1 + R_2 - R_1 R_2$ | Inclusion–exclusion principle |
| **k-out-of-n (identical)** | At least $k$ of $n$ work | $R_s = \sum_{j=k}^{n} \binom{n}{j} R^j (1-R)^{n-j}$ | Binomial distribution of successes |
| **Minimal Path Set $P_j$** | AND over path $j$ | $R_{P_j} = \prod_{i \in P_j} R_i$ | Boolean product of components in path |
| **Minimal Cut Set $K_j$** | OR over cut $j$ | $Q_{K_j} = \prod_{i \in K_j} (1 - R_i)$ | Boolean sum of component failures |
| **Inclusion–Exclusion (Path)** | $\phi = \bigcup_j P_j$ | $R_s = \sum P(P_j) - \sum P(P_j \cap P_k) + \cdots$ | Exact for path sets |
| **Disjoint Product Rule** | $P(AB) + P(A\overline{B}) = P(A)$ | Conditioning on $X_i$ | Foundation of Shannon expansion |
| **De Morgan's Law** | $\overline{A \cdot B} = \overline{A} + \overline{B}$ | Duality of series ↔ parallel | Use to convert cuts ↔ paths |
| **Shannon Expansion** | $\phi = X\phi_X + \overline{X}\phi_{\overline{X}}$ | $R_s = R_i \cdot R_s(X_i=1) + (1-R_i) \cdot R_s(X_i=0)$ | $\phi_X = \phi(X_i=1)$, $\phi_{\overline{X}} = \phi(X_i=0)$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL TABLE FORMATTING RULE:** In the LaTeX expressions above, the absolute-value / alternation bar has been written using the upright, table-safe symbol `\vert` or `\mid` where it might conflict with markdown cell separators. For example, $R_s \mid_{X_i=1}$ is written as $R_s(X_i=1)$.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace systems** (e.g., Boeing 777 fly-by-wire) — Boolean algebra evaluates redundant flight computer channels.
- **Nuclear plant safety systems** — The Probability Risk Assessment (PRA) uses minimal cut sets identified via Boolean methods.
- **Automotive ECU networks** — Multiple sensors feed control units; Boolean logic determines if a fault code triggers.
- **Power grid reliability** — Bridge topology (3-bus) is the standard textbook non-series-parallel case.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Reliability of a Series System (Boolean Proof)

A series system functions only if **every** component works.

$$\phi_s(\mathbf{X}) = X_1 \cdot X_2 \cdot X_3 \cdots X_n$$

Assuming statistical independence of component states:

$$R_s = P(\phi_s = 1) = P\left(\bigcap_{i=1}^{n}\{X_i = 1\}\right) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(X_i = 1) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} R_i$$

> **Logic:** The system works $\iff$ the intersection of "component $i$ works" events occurs. Independence factorises the joint probability into a product.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Reliability of a 2-Component Parallel System

A parallel system functions if **at least one** component works.

$$\phi_p = X_1 + X_2 - X_1 X_2$$

This is the **inclusion–exclusion identity**. The product $X_1 X_2$ is subtracted because it was counted twice in $X_1 + X_2$.

$$R_p = P(X_1 + X_2 - X_1 X_2) = R_1 + R_2 - R_1 R_2$$

Equivalently, by the **complement rule**:

$$\phi_p = 1 - \overline{X_1} \cdot \overline{X_2}$$

$$R_p = 1 - (1 - R_1)(1 - R_2)$$

Both expressions are algebraically identical. Expand the second to confirm:

$$\begin{aligned}
R_p &= 1 - \big[(1-R_1)(1-R_2)\big] \\
&= 1 - \big[1 - R_1 - R_2 + R_1 R_2\big] \\
&= R_1 + R_2 - R_1 R_2
\end{aligned}$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Bridge Network (The Classic Non-Series-Parallel Case)

A **bridge network** has 5 components $X_1, X_2, X_3, X_4, X_5$ with $X_5$ as the bridge.

**Path sets (all working combinations from Source to Sink):**

$$P_1 = X_1 X_2 \quad (\text{top branch})$$
$$P_2 = X_3 X_4 \quad (\text{bottom branch})$$
$$P_3 = X_1 X_5 X_4 \quad (\text{left-bridge-right})$$
$$P_4 = X_3 X_5 X_2 \quad (\text{right-bridge-left})$$

The structure function is the **union** of all path sets:

$$\phi = P_1 \cup P_2 \cup P_3 \cup P_4$$

### Method A — Disjoint Product Form via Shannon Expansion

Expand with respect to bridge component $X_5$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\phi &= X_5 \cdot \phi(X_5=1) + \overline{X_5} \cdot \phi(X_5=0)
\end{aligned}$$

**Case (i): $X_5 = 1$ (bridge works):**
With $X_5=1$, the network reduces to parallel combination of $(X_1 X_2)$ and $(X_3 X_4)$:

$$\phi_{X_5=1} = X_1 X_2 + X_3 X_4 - X_1 X_2 X_3 X_4$$

**Case (ii): $X_5 = 0$ (bridge fails):**
The network becomes a pure series of two parallel branches:

$$\phi_{X_5=0} = (X_1 + X_3)(X_2 + X_4)$$

Substitute back:

$$\begin{aligned}
\phi &= X_5 \big[X_1 X_2 + X_3 X_4 - X_1 X_2 X_3 X_4\big] \\
&\quad + \overline{X_5}(X_1 + X_3)(X_2 + X_4)
\end{aligned}$$

The terms in the bracket are NOT yet disjoint. Apply Shannon expansion again on $X_1$ inside the bracket:

$$X_1 X_2 = X_1 X_2; \quad \overline{X_1} \cdot 0 = 0 \quad \text{(replacing } X_1 \text{ by } 0)$$

The result (after full expansion) is the **disjoint product form**:

$$\begin{aligned}
\phi &= X_1 X_2 X_5 + \overline{X_1} X_2 X_3 X_4 X_5 + \overline{X_2} X_1 X_3 X_4 X_5 \\
&\quad + \overline{X_1 X_2} \cdot X_3 X_4 \cdot \overline{X_5} + X_1 \overline{X_2} \overline{X_3} X_4 \overline{X_5} + \overline{X_1} X_2 X_3 \overline{X_4} \overline{X_5} \\
&\quad + \overline{X_1} \overline{X_2} X_3 X_4 \overline{X_5} \quad \text{(+ similar disjoint terms)}
\end{aligned}$$

> **Logic:** Every product now contains each variable or its complement exactly once, guaranteeing mutual exclusivity. Probabilities add directly.

The reliability is then:

$$R_s = P(\phi = 1) = \sum P(\text{each disjoint product term})$$

For independent identical components with $R_i = R$:

$$\begin{aligned}
R_s &= R^3 + (1-R)R^3 + (1-R)R^3 + (1-R)^2 R^2 \\
&\quad + R(1-R)^3 + (1-R)^2 R^2(1-R) + (1-R)^3 R^2 \\
&= 2R^3 + 2(1-R)R^3 + 2(1-R)^2 R^2 + (1-R)^2 R^2 + R(1-R)^3
\end{aligned}$$

Simplified closed form (well-known bridge result):

$$R_s = 2R^2 + 2R^3 - 5R^4 + 2R^5$$

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Minimal Cut Set Method (Boolean De Morgan Duality)

The system fails if **any** minimal cut set fails. Cuts of the bridge:

$$K_1 = X_1 X_3 \quad \text{(top-left short)}$$
$$K_2 = X_2 X_4 \quad \text{(bottom-right short)}$$
$$K_3 = X_1 X_5 X_4 \quad \text{(top-bridge-bottom)}$$
$$K_4 = X_2 X_5 X_3 \quad \text{(bottom-bridge-top)}$$

System unreliability (failure) is the **OR** of cut sets:

$$\overline{\phi} = K_1 + K_2 + K_3 + K_4$$

$$Q_s = 1 - R_s = P(K_1 \cup K_2 \cup K_3 \cup K_4)$$

Use inclusion–exclusion on the disjoint cut form. For $R_i = R$:

$$Q_s = 2(1-R)^2 + 2(1-R)^3 - 5(1-R)^4 + 2(1-R)^5$$

Confirm via $R_s + Q_s = 1$ ✓

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Which Method

| **Method** | **Best For** | **Computational Cost** | **Key Assumption** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Inclusion–Exclusion (Path) | Small systems (≤ 6 components) | $2^p - 1$ terms where $p$ = #paths | Independent components |
| Shannon Expansion (Disjoint) | Bridge, ladder, complex | Exponential in $n$ | Independent components |
| Minimal Cut Set Enumeration | Safety-critical, large systems | Polynomial for sparse cuts | Coherent system |
| Factoring (Conditional) | Recursive, modular systems | $O(2^n)$ in worst case | Independence |
| Boolean Truth Table | Pedagogical, ≤ 4 components | $2^n$ rows | Direct enumeration |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Series System — Boolean AND Logic

```mermaid
graph LR
    SRC[Source] --> C1[Comp 1]
    C1 --> C2[Comp 2]
    C2 --> C3[Comp 3]
    C3 --> Cnn[Comp n]
    Cnn --> SNK[Sink]
    classDef node fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#E65100,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    class SRC,C1,C2,C3,Cnn,SNK node
```

> **Logic Interpretation:** The path from `Source` to `Sink` exists **iff** all components $X_1 \land X_2 \land \cdots \land X_n = 1$.

## 4.2 Parallel System — Boolean OR Logic

```mermaid
graph LR
    SRC[Source] --> SPLIT{Split}
    SPLIT --> C1[Comp 1]
    SPLIT --> C2[Comp 2]
    SPLIT --> C3[Comp 3]
    SPLIT --> Cn[Comp n]
    C1 --> MERGE{Merge}
    C2 --> MERGE
    C3 --> MERGE
    Cn --> MERGE
    MERGE --> SNK[Sink]
    classDef node fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    class SRC,SPLIT,C1,C2,C3,Cn,MERGE,SNK node
```

> **Logic Interpretation:** At least one path `Source → Sink` is open when $X_1 \lor X_2 \lor \cdots \lor X_n = 1$.

## 4.3 Bridge Network — The Canonical Non-Series-Parallel Topology

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Source] --> B[Node 1]
    A --> C[Node 2]
    B --> D[Comp 1]
    B --> E[Comp 5 Bridge]
    C --> F[Comp 3]
    C --> G[Comp 5 alt]
    D --> H[Node 3]
    E --> I[Node 4]
    F --> I
    G --> H
    H --> J[Sink]
    I --> J
    classDef source fill:#FFF59D,stroke:#F57F17,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef comp fill:#FFCCBC,stroke:#BF360C,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef sink fill:#B3E5FC,stroke:#01579B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    class A,J source
    class D,E,F,G comp
    class B,C,H,I sink
```

> **Reading the Diagram:** `Source → Comp 1 → Node 3 → Comp 4 → Sink` is one path. `Source → Comp 3 → Node 4 → Comp 2 → Sink` is another. `Comp 5` is the diagonal bridge, creating the **non-series-parallel** structure that Boolean algebra must untangle.

## 4.4 Boolean Expansion (Factoring) Tree

```mermaid
graph TD
    ROOT[phi of X1 X2 X3 X4 X5] --> L1A[X5 equals 1 branch]
    ROOT --> L1B[X5 equals 0 branch]
    L1A --> L2A[phi with X5 on, becomes parallel of X1X2 and X3X4]
    L1A --> L2B[Result 1 plus X5 term]
    L1B --> L2C[phi with X5 off, series of two parallel branches]
    L1B --> L2D[Result 0 plus X5 bar term]
    L2A --> L3A[Shannon expand on X1]
    L2A --> L3B[Shannon expand on X3]
    L2C --> L3C[Expand to product of sums]
    L2C --> L3D[Distribute over X1 X2 X3 X4]
    L3A --> L4A[Disjoint product term 1]
    L3A --> L4B[Disjoint product term 2]
    L3B --> L4C[Disjoint product term 3]
    L3B --> L4D[Disjoint product term 4]
    L3C --> L4E[Disjoint product term 5]
    L3D --> L4F[Disjoint product term 6]
    classDef root fill:#E1BEE7,stroke:#4A148C,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef branch fill:#FFE082,stroke:#FF6F00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef leaf fill:#C5E1A5,stroke:#33691E,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    class ROOT root
    class L1A,L1B,L2A,L2B,L2C,L2D,L3A,L3B,L3C,L3D branch
    class L4A,L4B,L4C,L4D,L4E,L4F leaf
```

> **Interpretation:** The Shannon (factoring) decomposition recursively splits the structure function on each variable, terminating in **disjoint products** whose probabilities add without double-counting.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** Define a *minimal path set* and a *minimal cut set* in Boolean reliability analysis. How are they related? **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Minimal Path Set (MPS):** A set of components that, if all are working, ensures system success. It is *minimal* in the sense that no proper subset is itself a path set. Formally, $P_j = \{i : X_i = 1 \text{ for all } i \in P_j \Rightarrow \phi = 1\}$, with no proper subset having this property. **(1 mark)**
- **Minimal Cut Set (MCS):** A set of components that, if all fail, causes system failure. Minimality: no proper subset causes failure. $K_j = \{i : X_i = 0 \text{ for all } i \in K_j \Rightarrow \phi = 0\}$. **(1 mark)**
- **Duality Relation (De Morgan):** A set $P$ is a minimal path set **iff** its complement is a minimal cut set. $\phi(\mathbf{X}) = 1 \iff \overline{\phi}(\overline{\mathbf{X}}) = 0$ where complements interchange AND/OR. **(1 mark)**

---

### **Q2.** State and explain **Shannon's expansion theorem** as used in Boolean reliability expansion. **[KTU University Exam – May 2023]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Statement:** For any Boolean function $\phi(\mathbf{X})$ and any variable $X_i$:

$$\phi(\mathbf{X}) = X_i \cdot \phi(1_i, \mathbf{X}) + \overline{X_i} \cdot \phi(0_i, \mathbf{X})$$

where $\phi(1_i, \mathbf{X})$ denotes $\phi$ evaluated with $X_i = 1$, and $\phi(0_i, \mathbf{X})$ with $X_i = 0$. **(1.5 marks)**

- **Reliability Interpretation:** Conditioning on $X_i$:

$$R_s = R_i \cdot R_s(X_i=1) + (1-R_i) \cdot R_s(X_i=0)$$

This decomposes the reliability computation into two *mutually exclusive* sub-problems (component works vs. fails), enabling systematic enumeration of disjoint product terms. **(1.5 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Draw the reliability block diagram of a **bridge network** with 5 components. List all minimal path sets and minimal cut sets. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Using **Boolean algebra (Shannon expansion)**, derive the system reliability expression assuming all 5 components are identical with reliability $R$ and statistically independent. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution**

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

**Block Diagram (3 marks):**

```
       X1
   ●──────●─────
   │      │     │
   │     X5     X4
   │      │     │
   ●──────●─────
       X3
```

**Minimal Path Sets (2 marks):**

$$P_1 = X_1 X_2, \quad P_2 = X_3 X_4, \quad P_3 = X_1 X_5 X_4, \quad P_4 = X_3 X_5 X_2$$

**Minimal Cut Sets (2 marks):**

$$K_1 = X_1 X_3, \quad K_2 = X_2 X_4, \quad K_3 = X_1 X_5 X_4, \quad K_4 = X_2 X_5 X_3$$

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

Apply Shannon's expansion on $X_5$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\phi &= X_5 \cdot \phi_{X_5=1} + \overline{X_5} \cdot \phi_{X_5=0}
\end{aligned}$$

With $X_5 = 1$, the network is the parallel combination of $(X_1X_2)$ and $(X_3X_4)$:

$$\phi_{X_5=1} = X_1 X_2 + X_3 X_4 - X_1 X_2 X_3 X_4$$

With $X_5 = 0$, the network reduces to $(X_1+X_3)(X_2+X_4)$:

$$\phi_{X_5=0} = (X_1 + X_3)(X_2 + X_4)$$

Expand the second term using distributive law:

$$\begin{aligned}
(X_1 + X_3)(X_2 + X_4) &= X_1 X_2 + X_1 X_4 + X_3 X_2 + X_3 X_4
\end{aligned}$$

Now apply Shannon's expansion **inside** the $X_5=1$ branch on $X_1$ to obtain disjoint products:

$$X_1 X_2 = X_1 X_2$$
$$X_3 X_4 \to \text{expand on } X_1: \quad X_3 X_4 = X_1 \cdot X_3 X_4 + \overline{X_1} \cdot X_3 X_4$$

Combining all disjoint products (full expansion) yields the system reliability:

$$\begin{aligned}
R_s &= 2R^3 + 2R^2(1-R) + 2R^2(1-R)^2 - R^3(1-R)^2 \\
&\quad + R(1-R)^3 + (1-R)^2 R^2 \\
\end{aligned}$$

Simplifying to the canonical closed form:

$$\boxed{R_s = 2R^2 + 2R^3 - 5R^4 + 2R^5}$$

**Verification:** When $R = 1$: $R_s = 2+2-5+2 = 1$ ✓. When $R = 0$: $R_s = 0$ ✓. When $R = 0.5$: $R_s = 0.5 + 0.25 - 0.3125 + 0.0625 = 0.5$ ✓ (matches known bridge symmetry at $R=0.5$).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*

**(a)** A system consists of **two components in parallel**, each with reliability $0.9$. The system functions if at least one component works. Compute the system reliability using: (i) Inclusion–Exclusion, (ii) Complement method. Show that the two answers are identical. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 2-out-of-3 system has three identical components, each with reliability $R = 0.85$. The system works if **at least 2** of the 3 components work. Determine system reliability using Boolean expansion. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution**

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

**Method (i): Inclusion–Exclusion (3.5 marks)**

The system functions if $X_1 = 1$ OR $X_2 = 1$. By inclusion–exclusion:

$$R_s = P(X_1 \cup X_2) = P(X_1) + P(X_2) - P(X_1 \cap X_2)$$

Assuming independence $P(X_1 \cap X_2) = R_1 R_2$:

$$R_s = 0.9 + 0.9 - (0.9)(0.9) = 1.8 - 0.81 = 0.99$$

**Method (ii): Complement Method (3.5 marks)**

The system fails only if both components fail. Let $Q_i = 1 - R_i$:

$$R_s = 1 - P(\overline{X_1} \cap \overline{X_2}) = 1 - Q_1 Q_2 = 1 - (0.1)(0.1) = 1 - 0.01 = 0.99$$

**Equivalence Proof:** Expand the complement method expression:

$$1 - (1-R_1)(1-R_2) = 1 - 1 + R_1 + R_2 - R_1 R_2 = R_1 + R_2 - R_1 R_2$$

This is algebraically equal to the inclusion–exclusion result. Both yield $R_s = 0.99$. ✓

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

The structure function for a 2-out-of-3 system is the OR of three minimal path sets, each of size 2:

$$\phi = X_1 X_2 + X_1 X_3 + X_2 X_3$$

Apply Boolean expansion on $X_1$ to obtain disjoint products (Shannon):

$$\begin{aligned}
\phi &= X_1 \cdot \phi_{X_1=1} + \overline{X_1} \cdot \phi_{X_1=0} \\
&= X_1(X_2 + X_3) + \overline{X_1}(X_2 X_3) \\
&= X_1 X_2 + X_1 X_3 - X_1 X_2 X_3 + \overline{X_1} X_2 X_3
\end{aligned}$$

Apply Shannon on $X_2$ inside the first two terms to make them disjoint:

$$X_1 X_2 = X_1 X_2, \quad X_1 X_3 = X_1 \overline{X_2} X_3 + X_1 X_2 X_3 \cdot (\text{already covered})$$

Final disjoint form:

$$\phi = X_1 X_2 + X_1 \overline{X_2} X_3 + \overline{X_1} X_2 X_3$$

Probabilities (using $R = 0.85$):

$$R_s = P(X_1 X_2) + P(X_1 \overline{X_2} X_3) + P(\overline{X_1} X_2 X_3)$$

$$\begin{aligned}
R_s &= R^2 + R(1-R)R + (1-R) R \cdot R \\
&= R^2 + 2R^2(1-R) \\
&= R^2[1 + 2(1-R)] \\
&= R^2(3 - 2R)
\end{aligned}$$

Substituting $R = 0.85$:

$$R_s = (0.85)^2 (3 - 1.7) = 0.7225 \times 1.3 = 0.9393$$

**Verification via binomial formula:**

$$R_s = \sum_{j=2}^{3} \binom{3}{j} (0.85)^j (0.15)^{3-j} = 3(0.85)^2(0.15) + (0.85)^3 = 0.3251 + 0.6141 = 0.9393 \quad ✓$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to disjoint products:** Many students write $R_s = R_1R_2 + R_3R_4 + R_1R_5R_4 + \ldots$ and stop. This **double-counts** overlapping path events. KTU examiner deducts 2–3 marks for this. Always apply Shannon expansion to obtain **disjoint** products.
> 2. **Misidentifying bridge structure:** Students often treat a bridge as series-parallel by guessing. Always draw the block diagram first and confirm topology.
> 3. **Confusing path and cut sets:** A minimal path set is a **working** path; a minimal cut set is a **failure** trigger. They are **duals** (De Morgan), not the same thing.
> 4. **Independence assumption silently dropped:** When components share load/environment, they are **dependent**. Boolean algebra still applies, but the probability step changes: $P(X_1 X_2) \neq R_1 R_2$.
> 5. **Sign errors in inclusion–exclusion:** Adding instead of subtracting intersection terms is a classic 1-mark loss.
> 6. **Not verifying the closed form:** A 1-minute sanity check (R=0, R=1, R=0.5 for bridge) catches most algebra errors.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (for last-minute KTU prep)**

- [ ] **Structure function $\phi(\mathbf{X})$** maps component states $\{0,1\}^n$ to system state $\{0,1\}$.
- [ ] **Series** = AND logic; **Parallel** = OR logic; **Bridge** = neither.
- [ ] **Minimal Path Set** = smallest set of components whose joint success guarantees system success.
- [ ] **Minimal Cut Set** = smallest set of components whose joint failure causes system failure.
- [ ] **De Morgan Duality:** A minimal path's complement is a minimal cut.
- [ ] **Shannon Expansion:** $\phi = X_i \phi_{X_i=1} + \overline{X_i} \phi_{X_i=0}$ — the workhorse for disjoint-product conversion.
- [ ] **Independence prerequisite:** All probability factorizations $R_1 R_2 \cdots$ require **statistical independence** of component states.
- [ ] **Bridge formula (5 identical components, independent):** $R_s = 2R^2 + 2R^3 - 5R^4 + 2R^5$.
- [ ] **2-out-of-3 formula (identical):** $R_s = R^2(3 - 2R)$.
- [ ] **Parallel (2 comps):** $R_s = R_1 + R_2 - R_1 R_2 = 1 - (1-R_1)(1-R_2)$.
- [ ] **Series (n comps):** $R_s = \prod R_i$.
- [ ] **Disjoint products are mandatory** before summing probabilities — otherwise double-counting occurs.
- [ ] **Inclusion–Exclusion** for non-disjoint unions: alternate add/subtract intersection terms.
- [ ] **Sanity check boundaries:** $R_s(0)=0$, $R_s(1)=1$, monotonic in each $R_i$.
- [ ] **Coherent system properties:** monotonicity, relevance, non-degeneracy — all satisfied by series-parallel and bridge.
- [ ] **Real-world application:** PRA in nuclear, FMEA in aerospace, fault-tree analysis in automotive.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Definitions and Basic Concepts
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET632 - RELIABILITY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Definitions and Basic Concepts

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to System Reliability

In Reliability Engineering, before we can evaluate or design any system, we must first understand the **fundamental vocabulary** that distinguishes component-level behaviour from system-level behaviour. Module 4 focuses specifically on **System Reliability**, which deals with how individual component reliabilities combine to determine the overall reliability of an engineering system.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **System Reliability** is the probability that a *system*, consisting of one or more *components*, will perform its intended function under stated conditions for a specified period of time.

## 1.2 Formal KTU Syllabus Definitions

The following definitions form the **canonical vocabulary** of this module and are highly repeated in KTU University Examinations.

### Definition 1 — System
A **System** is a collection of *components*, *subsystems*, or *assemblies* arranged in a specific configuration to accomplish a desired function. The boundaries of a system depend on the perspective of the analyst.

- A *component* in one study may itself be a *system* in another.
- Examples: a power plant (system of turbines, boilers, generators), a laptop (system of CPU, RAM, battery, screen).

### Definition 2 — Component
A **Component** is the *smallest functional entity* of a system that is treated as an indivisible unit in the reliability model. The reliability of each component is assumed to be known (either from field data or manufacturer specifications).

### Definition 3 — System Reliability, $R_S(t)$
The probability that the system performs its intended function without failure for a specified mission time $t$, under given operating conditions.

$$R_S(t) = P(\text{System functions successfully over } [0, t])$$

### Definition 4 — System Unreliability / Probability of Failure, $F_S(t)$ or $Q_S(t)$
The probability that the system fails within the mission time $t$.

$$Q_S(t) = 1 - R_S(t)$$

### Definition 5 — Mission Time, $t$
The **specified duration** for which the system is required to perform its function. Common mission times: aircraft flight = 5 h, satellite = 15 yr, smartphone = 3 yr.

## 1.3 Classification of Systems

Engineering systems are classified based on the *manner in which components are interconnected*. The reliability of a system is a **function of both the component reliabilities and the interconnection topology**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Classification Table**
>
> | System Type | Functional Logic | Block Diagram Analogy |
> |---|---|---|
> | **Series System** | All components must work | Components in a single chain |
> | **Parallel System** | At least one path must work | Components side-by-side (redundant) |
> | **Combined (Series-Parallel)** | Mix of series and parallel blocks | Hierarchical interconnection |
> | **Standby (Redundant) System** | Active + inactive spares | Switch-controlled backup |
> | **k-out-of-n System** | At least $k$ of $n$ must work | Voting configuration |

## 1.4 Fundamental Assumptions in System Reliability

The derivations that follow universally rely on the following two **standard assumptions**. A KTU Examiner will often deduct marks if these are not stated.

1. **Independence Assumption**: Components fail *independently* of one another. The failure of one component does not influence the failure of any other.
2. **Binary-State Assumption**: Each component exists in exactly one of two states — *Working* (Success) or *Failed*.

## 1.5 Conceptual Analogy — The Classroom Light Analogy

> [!TIP]
> **Intuition Box — Imagine Your Classroom**
>
> - A classroom has **four LED tubes** in series on one switch. The room is bright *only if* all four tubes work. If *one* tube fails, the room is dark. → This is a **Series System**.
> - Now imagine an **emergency exit light** that has two bulbs wired in parallel. The exit remains visible if *at least one* bulb works. → This is a **Parallel System**.
> - An aeroplane has *two engines* (parallel) and *three hydraulic lines* (series within each engine). → This is a **Combined System**.

The reliability of the classroom, the exit light, or the aeroplane is *not* simply the sum or product of the individual reliabilities — it depends on **how they are connected**.

## 1.6 Why System Reliability Matters

A manufacturer can certify that every component in a machine has a reliability of **0.99**, yet the system may have a reliability of less than **0.5** if the components are arranged in a long series. Conversely, a system with mediocre components can approach near-perfect reliability through **redundancy** (parallel configuration). This trade-off is the **central theme** of Module 4.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Block Diagram Approach

A **Block Diagram Method** is a pictorial representation of a system's logical configuration using rectangular blocks, where:

- Each **block** represents a component (or subsystem).
- A **line connecting** the blocks indicates the system is operational only when the logical condition of that connection is satisfied.
- The block diagram represents the *functional dependence* of the system on its components, **not** the physical layout.

## 2.2 Why Block Diagram Analysis?

A block diagram abstracts away physical details (wires, plumbing, mounting) and exposes the **logical structure of redundancy**. For any given mission time $t$, the block diagram allows the engineer to compute $R_S(t)$ using only:

- The component reliabilities $R_i(t)$.
- Boolean logic of series/parallel combinations.

## 2.3 Step-by-Step Logic of Series Configuration

In a **Series System**, the system functions successfully *only if every component functions successfully*. The failure of *any single* component causes system failure.

**Logical Boolean Expression** (using success indicator $X_i = 1$ for working, $0$ for failed):

$$X_S = X_1 \cdot X_2 \cdot X_3 \cdots X_n$$

Where $X_S = 1$ means the system works. Since components are *independent*:

$$R_S(t) = R_1(t) \cdot R_2(t) \cdot R_3(t) \cdots R_n(t) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} R_i(t)$$

**Key Insight**: A series system is *weaker* than its weakest component. As $n$ increases, $R_S$ decreases monotonically.

## 2.4 Step-by-Step Logic of Parallel Configuration

In a **Parallel System**, the system functions successfully if *at least one* component functions successfully. The system fails *only if all* components fail.

**Logical Boolean Expression**:

$$X_S = 1 - (1 - X_1)(1 - X_2)(1 - X_3) \cdots (1 - X_n)$$

**Reliability Formulation** — Using the complement rule $P(\text{at least one works}) = 1 - P(\text{all fail})$:

$$R_S(t) = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n} \left[1 - R_i(t)\right]$$

**Key Insight**: A parallel system is *stronger* than its strongest component. As $n$ increases, $R_S$ approaches 1.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **single most important reference** for Module 4. Memorise the boundary conditions and units.

| # | Configuration | Reliability Formula | Unreliability Formula | Special Case ($R_i = R$) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Single Component** | $R_S = R_1$ | $Q_S = 1 - R_1$ | $R_S = R$ |
| 2 | **Series** ($n$ components) | $R_S = \prod_{i=1}^{n} R_i$ | $Q_S = 1 - \prod R_i$ | $R_S = R^n$ |
| 3 | **Parallel** (2 components) | $R_S = 1 - (1-R_1)(1-R_2)$ | $Q_S = (1-R_1)(1-R_2)$ | $R_S = 2R - R^2$ |
| 4 | **Parallel** ($n$ components) | $R_S = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n} Q_i$ | $Q_S = \prod_{i=1}^{n} Q_i$ | $R_S = 1 - (1-R)^n$ |
| 5 | **Series-Parallel** (Reduce inner first) | $R_{\text{inner}} = 1 - \prod Q_i$, then multiply | Apply at each level | Case-specific |
| 6 | **Parallel-Series** (Reduce inner first) | $R_{\text{inner}} = \prod R_i$, then apply parallel rule | Case-specific | Case-specific |
| 7 | **Standby (1 active + 1 spare, perfect switch)** | $R_S = R_1 + (1 - R_1)R_2$ | $Q_S = (1 - R_1)(1 - R_2)$ | $R_S = 2R - R^2$ (numeric) |

**Where**:
- $R_i(t)$ = reliability of $i$-th component over mission time $t$
- $Q_i(t) = 1 - R_i(t)$ = unreliability of $i$-th component
- All quantities are **dimensionless** (they are probabilities), ranging in $[0, 1]$.

## 2.6 Boundary Condition Sanity Checks

A reliable KTU student always checks boundary cases:

- If any $R_i = 0$ in **series** → $R_S = 0$ (broken component breaks the system). ✓
- If any $R_i = 1$ in **parallel** → $R_S = 1$ (perfect component makes the system perfect). ✓
- If $R_i = R$ for all $i$ in **series of $n$** → $R_S = R^n \le R$ for $R \le 1$. ✓
- If $R_i = R$ for all $i$ in **parallel of $n$** → $R_S = 1 - (1-R)^n \ge R$ for $R \le 1$. ✓

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Field | Series-Parallel Application |
|---|---|
| **Aerospace** | Twin-engine aircraft (engines parallel) with fuel lines (series) |
| **Nuclear Power** | Three independent cooling loops (parallel), each with pumps and valves (series) |
| **Telecommunications** | Multiple base stations in parallel, each with a chain of signal processors (series) |
| **Automotive** | Braking system: dual-circuit parallel for safety; ECU with cascaded sensors (series) |
| **Process Plants** | Distillation column with reboiler pumps in series; with multiple parallel reboilers for redundancy |

The system-reliability engineer uses these formulas to:
- **Allocate** reliability targets to individual components (reliability apportionment).
- **Decide** the optimal level of redundancy (cost-reliability trade-off).
- **Predict** field failure rates before deployment.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Reliability of a Series System

**Given**: $n$ independent components, each with reliability $R_i(t) = P(\text{Component } i \text{ works over } [0, t])$.

**Step 1**: Define the *system success event* $S$ as the intersection of individual successes:

$$S = \{C_1 \text{ works}\} \cap \{C_2 \text{ works}\} \cap \cdots \cap \{C_n \text{ works}\}$$

**Step 2**: Apply the probability of an intersection of *independent* events. The independence condition $P(A \cap B) = P(A) P(B)$ for all pairs is the key step.

$$R_S(t) = P(S) = P(C_1) \cdot P(C_2) \cdot P(C_3) \cdots P(C_n)$$

**Step 3**: Substitute $P(C_i) = R_i(t)$:

$$R_S(t) = R_1(t) \cdot R_2(t) \cdot R_3(t) \cdots R_n(t)$$

**Step 4**: Express compactly using the product operator:

$$\boxed{R_S(t) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} R_i(t)}$$

**Step 5 — Special Case**: If all $n$ components are *identical* with common reliability $R$:

$$R_S(t) = R^n$$

**Step 6 — Exponential Decay Insight**: If $R_i(t) = e^{-\lambda_i t}$, then:

$$R_S(t) = \exp\!\left(-\sum_{i=1}^{n} \lambda_i t\right) = e^{-\lambda_S t}, \quad \lambda_S = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \lambda_i$$

The **system failure rate equals the sum** of component failure rates in an exponential-failure series system.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Reliability of a Parallel System

**Given**: $n$ independent components in parallel, each with reliability $R_i(t)$.

**Step 1**: The system *fails* only if *every* component fails. Define the system failure event $F$:

$$F = \{C_1 \text{ fails}\} \cap \{C_2 \text{ fails}\} \cap \cdots \cap \{C_n \text{ fails}\}$$

**Step 2**: By De Morgan's law and the complement rule:

$$R_S = 1 - P(F) = 1 - P(\text{all components fail})$$

**Step 3**: Apply independence — the probability that *all* fail is the product of individual failure probabilities:

$$P(F) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} \left[1 - R_i(t)\right] = \prod_{i=1}^{n} Q_i(t)$$

**Step 4**: Substitute back:

$$\boxed{R_S(t) = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n} \left[1 - R_i(t)\right]}$$

**Step 5 — Special Case**: Identical components with reliability $R$:

$$R_S(t) = 1 - (1 - R)^n$$

**Step 6 — Two-Component Expansion**: Setting $n = 2$ and expanding $(1-R_1)(1-R_2) = 1 - R_1 - R_2 + R_1 R_2$:

$$R_S = R_1 + R_2 - R_1 R_2$$

**Step 7 — Sanity Check**: If $R_1 = 0.5$ and $R_2 = 0.5$ in parallel, then $R_S = 0.5 + 0.5 - 0.25 = 0.75$. In series the same components would give $R_S = 0.25$. Parallel is **three times more reliable** — illustrating the **redundancy effect**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Combined Series-Parallel System

**Configuration**: Two parallel sub-blocks, connected in series.

Let subsystem A consist of components 1 and 2 in parallel, and subsystem B consist of components 3 and 4 in parallel. The two subsystems are in series.

**Step 1**: Compute reliability of subsystem A (parallel of 2 components):

$$R_A = 1 - (1 - R_1)(1 - R_2) = R_1 + R_2 - R_1 R_2$$

**Step 2**: Compute reliability of subsystem B (parallel of 2 components):

$$R_B = 1 - (1 - R_3)(1 - R_4) = R_3 + R_4 - R_3 R_4$$

**Step 3**: Compute overall system reliability (series combination of A and B):

$$\boxed{R_S = R_A \cdot R_B}$$

**Step 4 — Numerical Illustration**: With $R_1 = R_2 = R_3 = R_4 = 0.9$:

- $R_A = 1 - (0.1)(0.1) = 1 - 0.01 = 0.99$
- $R_B = 1 - (0.1)(0.1) = 1 - 0.01 = 0.99$
- $R_S = 0.99 \times 0.99 = 0.9801$

If these same four components were arranged purely in **series**, the reliability would be $0.9^4 = 0.6561$. The combined configuration gives nearly **50% higher reliability**.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Reliability Improvement Through Redundancy

This table compares the reliability of $n$ identical components (each with $R = 0.9$) under different configurations.

| Configuration Type | Formula | Value for $n = 2$ | Value for $n = 3$ | Value for $n = 4$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Series of $n$** | $R^n$ | 0.8100 | 0.7290 | 0.6561 |
| **Parallel of $n$** | $1 - (1-R)^n$ | 0.9900 | 0.9990 | 0.9999 |
| **Series of (Parallel of 2)** | $[1-(1-R)^2]^2$ | 0.9801 | — | — |
| **Parallel of (Series of 2)** | $1 - (1-R^2)^2$ | 0.9801 | — | — |

**Observation**: For the same components, the **parallel configuration always yields a higher reliability** than the series configuration. This is the mathematical basis for engineering redundancy.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 4 — Reduction Strategy for Complex Block Diagrams

For a general system with $m$ levels of nesting, the algorithm is:

1. Identify the **innermost (deepest) sub-block** in the block diagram.
2. Replace it with a single equivalent block whose reliability is computed using the appropriate series or parallel formula.
3. Re-draw the simplified block diagram.
4. Repeat steps 1–3 until only a **single block** remains.
5. The reliability of the final block is $R_S$.

> [!NOTE]
> This **bottom-up reduction algorithm** is the standard KTU-prescribed method for solving nested series-parallel networks. Marks are awarded for clearly identifying *which* sub-block is being reduced at each step.

---

## 3.6 Worked Numerical Example — Complete Solution

> **Problem [KTU University Exam Pattern]**: A system consists of three components A, B, C in series. The reliabilities are $R_A = 0.95$, $R_B = 0.90$, $R_C = 0.85$ for a 100-hour mission. Find (i) system reliability, (ii) system unreliability, and (iii) the improvement if B and C are placed in parallel.

**Solution**:

**Part (i) — Series Reliability**:

$$R_S = R_A \times R_B \times R_C = 0.95 \times 0.90 \times 0.85$$

$$R_S = 0.95 \times 0.7650 = 0.72675 \approx 0.727$$

**Part (ii) — System Unreliability**:

$$Q_S = 1 - R_S = 1 - 0.72675 = 0.27325 \approx 0.273$$

**Part (iii) — Improved Configuration**: Now B and C are in parallel; this parallel block is in series with A.

$$R_{BC} = 1 - (1 - R_B)(1 - R_C) = 1 - (0.10)(0.15) = 1 - 0.015 = 0.985$$

$$R_S^{\text{new}} = R_A \times R_{BC} = 0.95 \times 0.985 = 0.93575 \approx 0.936$$

**Improvement in reliability**:

$$\Delta R = 0.93575 - 0.72675 = 0.209$$

The improvement is approximately **20.9 percentage points** purely from the redundant placement of two components.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Series System — Block Diagram

The functional architecture flow for a *series* configuration in which the system success requires the success of every block in sequence.

```mermaid
graph LR
    inputA[Input] --> block1[Component 1]
    block1 --> block2[Component 2]
    block2 --> block3[Component 3]
    block3 --> blockN[Component N]
    blockN --> outputA[Output]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram**: Failure of *any single block* (1, 2, 3, …, or N) interrupts the signal flow from Input to Output. The Output is therefore a logical AND of all component states.

---

## 4.2 Parallel System — Block Diagram

The functional architecture flow for a *parallel* configuration. The system succeeds if *at least one* path remains intact.

```mermaid
graph LR
    inputB[Input] --> splitNode{Split}
    splitNode --> path1[Component 1]
    splitNode --> path2[Component 2]
    splitNode --> path3[Component 3]
    path1 --> joinNode{Join}
    path2 --> joinNode
    path3 --> joinNode
    joinNode --> outputB[Output]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram**: The Split node fans the input into three parallel paths. The Join node merges the outputs. The Output is a logical OR of all component states. The system fails only when *all* three paths are broken simultaneously.

---

## 4.3 Combined Series-Parallel — Block Diagram

Two parallel sub-systems, each composed of two parallel components, connected in series. This is the classic "series of parallel" topology.

```mermaid
graph LR
    inC[Input] --> subA
    subgraph subA[Subsystem A]
        direction LR
        sA1[Component 1] --- jA{Join}
        sA2[Component 2] --- jA
    end
    subA --> subB
    subgraph subB[Subsystem B]
        direction LR
        sB1[Component 3] --- jB{Join}
        sB2[Component 4] --- jB
    end
    subB --> outC[Output]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram**: The reduction algorithm proceeds in two passes. First, reduce each parallel sub-block (A and B) into a single equivalent block. Then multiply the two reduced reliabilities (series rule).

---

## 4.4 Reduction Flowchart — Solving Complex Systems

```mermaid
graph TD
    startA[Start with complex block diagram] --> identA{Identify innermost sub-block}
    identA --> applyA{Series or parallel rule?}
    applyA --> seriesRule[Apply product of reliabilities]
    applyA --> parallelRule[Apply one minus product of unreliabilities]
    seriesRule --> redrawA[Redraw with equivalent block]
    parallelRule --> redrawA
    redrawA --> checkA{Only one block left?}
    checkA -->|No| identA
    checkA -->|Yes| doneA[Report final system reliability]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Operational tip**: The flowchart above is the *exact algorithm* expected in the KTU valuation key for any 14-mark system-reliability problem. Each iteration is worth 1–2 marks in the marking scheme.

---

## 4.5 Logical Equivalence of Parallel and Standby (Perfect Switch)

```mermaid
graph TD
    mainA[Active Component 1] --> switchA{Perfect Switch}
    switchA --> outA[System Output]
    spareA[Spare Component 2] -.-> switchA
    switchA -.->|Activates on failure| outA
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation**: The *spare* is dormant until the active unit fails. A perfect switch transfers load instantly. The reliability of this 1-out-of-2 standby system equals the parallel system numerically, but the underlying physics is fundamentally different (the spare does not age while dormant).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
> Define the following terms with reference to system reliability: (a) System, (b) Component, (c) Mission time. Give one real-world example of each.

**Model Answer**:

(a) **System**: A *system* is a combination of components, sub-systems, or assemblies arranged in a defined configuration to perform a specific function. *Example*: An aircraft engine (system) comprising turbine blades, combustion chamber, fuel injector, and shaft (components).

(b) **Component**: A *component* is the smallest functional unit of a system treated as indivisible in the reliability model. The component is the *atomic element* in the analysis. *Example*: An individual capacitor on a printed circuit board.

(c) **Mission time**: The *mission time* is the duration for which the system is required to perform its intended function. *Example*: 4 hours for a fighter-jet sortie, 15 years for a geostationary satellite.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall**: Students often confuse "component" with "part". A *part* is a physical piece of hardware; a *component* is a *functional* unit in the reliability model. A single physical part may be treated as multiple reliability components (e.g., a chip with two independent logic gates).

---

> **Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
> State the fundamental assumptions used in the block-diagram method of system reliability analysis. Why are these assumptions necessary?

**Model Answer**:

1. **Independence Assumption**: All components fail *independently* of each other. The failure of one component does not influence the failure of any other.
2. **Binary-State Assumption**: Each component exists in exactly one of two states — *Working* (Success) or *Failed* — with no intermediate degraded state.

**Why necessary**:
- Independence allows the use of the product rule $P(A \cap B) = P(A) P(B)$, which is the basis of the series-system reliability formula.
- Binary-state simplifies the analysis by reducing continuous degradation to a Boolean success/failure logic, making the problem tractable for closed-form mathematical evaluation.
- Without these assumptions, closed-form reliability formulas cannot be derived and Monte Carlo simulation is required.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall**: Many students write only the assumption and forget to explain *why* it is needed. Always state both the assumption and the mathematical reason it is required. Marks are split 50:50 between the two.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> **Question A [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]**
>
> **(a) [7 Marks]** A system consists of four components in series with reliabilities $R_1 = 0.92$, $R_2 = 0.88$, $R_3 = 0.95$, $R_4 = 0.90$ for a 500-hour mission. Calculate the system reliability and unreliability. Comment on the result.
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** Two components with reliabilities $R_1 = 0.80$ and $R_2 = 0.70$ are arranged in parallel. Determine the parallel system reliability. Compare this with the reliability of the same two components arranged in series.

### Model Solution to Question A

**Part (a) — Series System Calculation**:

**Step 1**: Apply the series reliability formula:

$$R_S = R_1 \cdot R_2 \cdot R_3 \cdot R_4$$

**Step 2**: Substitute the numerical values:

$$R_S = 0.92 \times 0.88 \times 0.95 \times 0.90$$

**Step 3**: Perform the multiplication stepwise:

$$0.92 \times 0.88 = 0.8096$$

$$0.8096 \times 0.95 = 0.76912$$

$$0.76912 \times 0.90 = 0.69221$$

**Step 4**: State the result:

$$\boxed{R_S \approx 0.6922 \text{ or } 69.22\%}$$

**Step 5**: Compute the system unreliability:

$$Q_S = 1 - R_S = 1 - 0.6922 = 0.3078 \text{ or } 30.78\%$$

**Step 6 — Comment**: Even though every individual component has a reliability above **0.88**, the series arrangement causes the system reliability to drop below **0.70**. This illustrates that *a chain is only as strong as its weakest link*, and series configurations are particularly vulnerable to reliability degradation as the number of components increases.

---

**Part (b) — Parallel vs Series Comparison**:

**Step 1 — Parallel System**: Use the parallel reliability formula for two components:

$$R_{S,\text{parallel}} = 1 - (1 - R_1)(1 - R_2)$$

**Step 2**: Substitute the values:

$$R_{S,\text{parallel}} = 1 - (1 - 0.80)(1 - 0.70) = 1 - (0.20)(0.30) = 1 - 0.06 = 0.94$$

**Step 3 — Series System**: Use the series formula:

$$R_{S,\text{series}} = R_1 \times R_2 = 0.80 \times 0.70 = 0.56$$

**Step 4 — Comparison**:

| Configuration | Reliability | Unreliability |
|---|---|---|
| Series | 0.5600 | 0.4400 |
| Parallel | 0.9400 | 0.0600 |

**Step 5 — Conclusion**: The parallel configuration yields a reliability improvement of:

$$\Delta R = 0.94 - 0.56 = 0.38 \text{ or } 38 \text{ percentage points}$$

The parallel system is **1.68 times more reliable** than the series system for these components. This demonstrates the **drastic reliability enhancement achievable through redundancy**, even when using components of moderate individual reliability.

---

> **Question B [KTU University Exam — Alternative Module Choice]**
>
> **(a) [7 Marks]** A satellite system has a 10-year mission. Three independent redundant transmitters (parallel arrangement) are used to ensure downlink. Each transmitter has an exponential failure rate of $\lambda = 0.02$ per year. Find the system reliability for the 10-year mission. What is the reliability if only two transmitters were used in parallel?
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** A combined series-parallel system consists of components A and B in parallel (forming sub-block X), and components C and D in parallel (forming sub-block Y). Sub-blocks X and Y are in series. Component reliabilities are: $R_A = 0.95$, $R_B = 0.90$, $R_C = 0.92$, $R_D = 0.88$. Determine the overall system reliability.

### Model Solution to Question B

**Part (a) — Transmitter Parallel Redundancy**:

**Step 1**: Compute the reliability of a single transmitter over the 10-year mission using the exponential model:

$$R(t) = e^{-\lambda t} = e^{-0.02 \times 10} = e^{-0.20}$$

**Step 2**: Evaluate the exponential:

$$e^{-0.20} \approx 0.8187$$

**Step 3 — Three Transmitters in Parallel**: Apply the parallel formula with $n = 3$:

$$R_{S,3} = 1 - (1 - R)^3 = 1 - (1 - 0.8187)^3 = 1 - (0.1813)^3$$

**Step 4**: Compute the cube:

$$(0.1813)^3 = 0.00596 \approx 0.0060$$

**Step 5**: Subtract from one:

$$R_{S,3} = 1 - 0.0060 = 0.9940 \approx 0.994$$

**Step 6 — Two Transmitters in Parallel**: Apply the parallel formula with $n = 2$:

$$R_{S,2} = 1 - (1 - R)^2 = 1 - (0.1813)^2 = 1 - 0.0329 = 0.9671$$

**Step 7 — Comparison**:

$$\Delta R = 0.9940 - 0.9671 = 0.0269 \text{ or about } 2.7 \text{ percentage points}$$

**Conclusion**: Adding the *third* transmitter improves the system reliability by approximately **2.7 percentage points** over the two-transmitter configuration, at a cost of one additional transmitter. The decision depends on the cost-reliability trade-off.

---

**Part (b) — Series of Two Parallel Sub-Blocks**:

**Step 1 — Sub-block X (A and B in parallel)**:

$$R_X = 1 - (1 - R_A)(1 - R_B) = 1 - (0.05)(0.10) = 1 - 0.005 = 0.995$$

**Step 2 — Sub-block Y (C and D in parallel)**:

$$R_Y = 1 - (1 - R_C)(1 - R_D) = 1 - (0.08)(0.12) = 1 - 0.0096 = 0.9904$$

**Step 3 — Series Combination of X and Y**:

$$R_S = R_X \times R_Y = 0.995 \times 0.9904$$

**Step 4**: Compute the product:

$$R_S = 0.98545 \approx 0.9855$$

**Step 5 — Verification by Direct Expansion**: This can be cross-checked by writing:

$$R_S = [1 - (1-R_A)(1-R_B)] \times [1 - (1-R_C)(1-R_D)] = 0.995 \times 0.9904 = 0.9855$$

**Conclusion**: Despite the moderate individual component reliabilities (0.88 to 0.95), the redundant combined configuration yields a system reliability of approximately **98.55%**, which would be very difficult to achieve with a pure-series arrangement of the same components.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls in Module 4 Numerical Problems**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to state assumptions (–1 mark)**: Always begin the solution with "Assuming independent and binary-state components…".
> 2. **Confusing series and parallel formulas (–2 to –3 marks)**: Series is *product of reliabilities*; parallel is *one minus product of unreliabilities*. Drawing the block diagram first prevents confusion.
> 3. **Skipping intermediate steps (–1 to –2 marks)**: Show the partial products explicitly. KTU examiners do not award full marks for a single final number without working.
> 4. **Mixing up units**: All probabilities are *dimensionless*; do not write "0.95 units" or "95% reliability" interchangeably without clarity. Stick to one convention per answer.
> 5. **Not identifying the configuration correctly (–3 to –4 marks)**: The biggest mark-loss comes from applying the wrong formula because the student misread the block diagram. Always draw the diagram from the problem statement.
> 6. **Omitting the conclusion or comment (–1 mark)**: For 7-mark sub-questions, always end with a short interpretive comment on the numerical result.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Definitions and Basic Concepts (Module 4)**
>
> - ☐ A **system** is a collection of components; a **component** is the smallest functional unit treated as atomic.
> - ☐ **System reliability** is the probability the system performs its function over the **mission time** $t$.
> - ☐ Two core assumptions: **independence** of component failures and **binary** working/failed states.
> - ☐ The **block diagram** shows logical (not physical) connections between components.
> - ☐ **Series system** works *only if all* components work: $R_S = \prod R_i$.
> - ☐ **Parallel system** works *if at least one* component works: $R_S = 1 - \prod Q_i$.
> - ☐ A **series system is weaker** than its weakest component; a **parallel system is stronger** than its strongest component.
> - ☐ Adding a **redundant** parallel component always increases $R_S$, but with **diminishing returns**.
> - ☐ For $n$ identical components in series, $R_S = R^n$ (decreases rapidly with $n$).
> - ☐ For $n$ identical components in parallel, $R_S = 1 - (1 - R)^n$ (approaches 1 rapidly).
> - ☐ **Reduction algorithm**: solve innermost sub-block first, propagate outward, repeat until one block remains.
> - ☐ **Boundary checks**: if any $R_i = 0$ in series → $R_S = 0$; if any $R_i = 1$ in parallel → $R_S = 1$.
> - ☐ Always state assumptions, draw the block diagram, show intermediate arithmetic, and provide a concluding comment for full marks.
> - ☐ The **exponential failure model** $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ makes series-system failure rates **additive**: $\lambda_S = \sum \lambda_i$.
> - ☐ Standby redundancy with a *perfect switch* gives the same reliability formula as parallel, but the spare does not age while dormant.
> - ☐ The combined (series-of-parallel) topology is the most commonly tested configuration in 14-mark KTU questions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Relationship Between Reliability
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET632 - RELIABILITY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Relationship Between Reliability

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

In the context of **Reliability Engineering (PEIET632)**, the *Relationship Between Reliability* refers to the set of rigorous mathematical and probabilistic interconnections that exist between the fundamental reliability descriptors of any repairable or non-repairable component, system, or process. These descriptors include:

- **Reliability Function** $R(t)$ — Probability of survival up to time $t$
- **Unreliability (CDF)** $F(t)$ — Cumulative probability of failure by time $t$
- **Probability Density Function (PDF)** $f(t)$ — Instantaneous rate of failure
- **Hazard / Failure Rate** $\lambda(t)$ — Conditional instantaneous failure rate for a survivor
- **Mean Time To Failure (MTTF)** — Expected operating life
- **Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF)** — Expected uptime in cyclic operation

In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus, Module 4 explicitly demands that the student derive, interpret, and apply these inter-relationships to solve real-world problems in design, maintenance scheduling, and warranty analysis.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy — "The Bus Journey"**
>
> Imagine you board a long-distance KSRTC bus from Thiruvananthapuram to Kasaragod. Let the total journey time be $T$.
>
> - **Reliability $R(t)$** = "The probability that the bus *reaches Kasaragod without breaking down* within time $t$."
> - **Unreliability $F(t)$** = "The probability that the bus *has already broken down* before reaching Kasaragod by time $t$."
> - Since the bus either reaches or it doesn't: $R(t) + F(t) = 1$.
> - **Failure Density $f(t)$** = "The chance the bus breaks down *during a tiny interval* around time $t$."
> - **Hazard Rate $\lambda(t)$** = "Given that the bus *is still running perfectly at time $t$*, what is the rate at which it is *about to* fail in the next instant?"
> - **MTTF** = "The average total distance/time the bus runs before its first major breakdown."

This single analogy captures the **complete closed loop** of reliability mathematics.

## 1.3 Standard Reliability Metrics (Key Markers)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight — Core KTU Markers**
>
> - **Reliability $R(t)$** — Dimensionless probability, $0 \le R(t) \le 1$
> - **Failure Rate $\lambda(t)$** — Measured in **failures per hour** (or FIT — Failures In Time, $10^9$ device-hours)
> - **MTTF / MTBF** — Measured in **hours**
> - **Mission Time $t$** — Specified operating duration in **hours** or **cycles**
> - **Confidence Level** — $C$ (typically $90\%$, $95\%$, or $99\%$)

---

## 1.4 The Five Foundational Reliability Functions

| # | Function | Symbol | Physical Meaning |
|---|----------|--------|------------------|
| 1 | Reliability | $R(t)$ | Probability of *successful* operation in $(0, t]$ |
| 2 | Unreliability | $F(t)$ | Probability of *failure* in $(0, t]$ |
| 3 | Failure Density | $f(t)$ | Rate of change of $F(t)$ at instant $t$ |
| 4 | Hazard Rate | $\lambda(t)$ | Conditional failure rate, *given survival* to $t$ |
| 5 | Mean Life | $m$ (or $\theta$) | Expected time to first failure |

These five functions are **mathematically interlocked** — given any one (and the model), the other four can be derived. This is the central idea of the topic.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Logical Foundation (Step-by-Step Logic)

### Step 1 — The Exclusivity Axiom
A component is in **one of exactly two states** at any time $t$:
- **Operating (Success)** — with probability $R(t)$
- **Failed** — with probability $F(t)$

Since the sample space is exhaustive and mutually exclusive:

$$R(t) + F(t) = 1$$

This is the **most fundamental reliability identity** and is asked almost every KTU exam cycle.

### Step 2 — Failure Density as a Derivative
The probability that failure occurs in the **infinitesimal window** $[t, t + dt]$ is:

$$P(t \le T \le t + dt) = f(t)\,dt$$

By the definition of the probability density function (PDF):

$$f(t) = \frac{dF(t)}{dt}$$

and equivalently, by the chain rule:

$$f(t) = -\frac{dR(t)}{dt}$$

### Step 3 — Reliability from the Density
Integrating $f(t)$ from $0$ to $t$ gives the CDF:

$$F(t) = \int_0^t f(u)\,du$$

Therefore the reliability is the **complementary probability**:

$$R(t) = 1 - \int_0^t f(u)\,du = \int_t^\infty f(u)\,du$$

### Step 4 — The Hazard Function (Most Important for KTU)
The **Conditional Probability of Failure in $[t, t+dt]$, given survival to $t$** is:

$$\lambda(t)\,dt = \frac{f(t)\,dt}{R(t)}$$

Therefore:

$$\lambda(t) = \frac{f(t)}{R(t)} = \frac{-dR(t)/dt}{R(t)} = -\frac{1}{R(t)} \cdot \frac{dR(t)}{dt}$$

### Step 5 — The Master Exponential Form
Rearranging Step 4 and integrating from $0$ to $t$ (with $R(0) = 1$):

$$\frac{dR(t)}{R(t)} = -\lambda(t)\,dt$$

$$\ln R(t) - \ln R(0) = -\int_0^t \lambda(u)\,du$$

$$\boxed{R(t) = \exp\!\left(-\int_0^t \lambda(u)\,du\right)}$$

This is the **Single Most Important Equation in Reliability Engineering** and carries guaranteed marks in Part B questions.

### Step 6 — Mean Time To Failure (MTTF)
The expected lifetime is the **first moment** of the failure density:

$$\text{MTTF} = E[T] = \int_0^\infty t \cdot f(t)\,dt$$

Using integration by parts (with $\lim_{t\to\infty} t\cdot R(t) = 0$ for a convergent distribution):

$$\boxed{\text{MTTF} = \int_0^\infty R(t)\,dt}$$

This elegant identity — that the *area under the reliability curve equals the MTTF* — is a KTU-favourite.

### Step 7 — The Exponential Special Case
When $\lambda(t) = \lambda = \text{constant}$ (i.e., the component is in its **useful life / random-failure phase**):

$$R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}, \quad F(t) = 1 - e^{-\lambda t}, \quad f(t) = \lambda e^{-\lambda t}$$

$$\text{MTTF} = \frac{1}{\lambda}, \quad \text{MTBF} = \frac{1}{\lambda} + M_r$$

where $M_r$ is the **mean repair time**. For non-repairable systems $M_r = 0$, so MTTF $=$ MTBF.

## 2.2 The Bathtub Curve Connection

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Point**
>
> The hazard rate $\lambda(t)$ is **not constant** throughout a system's life. It traces the famous **Bathtub Curve**:
>
> - **Infant Mortality Phase (I):** $\lambda(t)$ is *decreasing* — burn-in defects dominate
> - **Useful Life Phase (II):** $\lambda(t) \approx \text{constant} = \lambda$ — random failures, modelled as **exponential**
> - **Wear-Out Phase (III):** $\lambda(t)$ is *increasing* — fatigue, corrosion, aging
>
> Reliability $R(t) = \exp\!\left(-\int_0^t \lambda(u)du\right)$ **always decreases monotonically**, but the *rate* of decrease depends on which phase we are in.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat-Sheet Table)

> [!NOTE]
> Use `\vert` for absolute value in the table below to comply with the markdown syntax safeguard.

| # | Relationship | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|--------------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | Complementarity | $R(t) + F(t) = 1$ | Dimensionless |
| 2 | CDF from PDF | $F(t) = \int_0^t f(u)\,du$ | Cumulative probability |
| 3 | Reliability from PDF | $R(t) = \int_t^\infty f(u)\,du$ | Tail integral |
| 4 | Failure Density | $f(t) = dF(t)/dt = -dR(t)/dt$ | Probability per unit time |
| 5 | Hazard Rate | $\lambda(t) = f(t)/R(t)$ | Failures / hour |
| 6 | Master Reliability Eq. | $R(t) = \exp\!\left(-\int_0^t \lambda(u)du\right)$ | Always valid |
| 7 | MTTF (general) | $\text{MTTF} = \int_0^\infty R(t)\,dt$ | Hours |
| 8 | MTTF (exponential) | $\text{MTTF} = 1/\lambda$ | Hours |
| 9 | MTBF (exponential, repairable) | $\text{MTBF} = 1/\lambda + M_r$ | Hours |
| 10 | Exponential $R(t)$ | $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ | Memoryless property |
| 11 | Weibull $R(t)$ | $R(t) = \exp[-(t/\eta)^\beta]$ | Shape $\beta$, scale $\eta$ |
| 12 | Weibull $\lambda(t)$ | $\lambda(t) = (\beta/\eta)(t/\eta)^{\beta-1}$ | Increasing / decreasing |
| 13 | Conditional $R$ | $R(t+t_0 \vert t_0) = R(t+t_0)/R(t_0)$ | Probability given survival |
| 14 | Memoryless (Exponential) | $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ implies $R(t+t_0 \vert t_0) = e^{-\lambda t}$ | Only exponential is memoryless |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace:** Mission reliability for satellites — $R(10\text{ years}) > 0.95$ required.
- **Power Systems:** MTBF of transformers is used to schedule predictive maintenance.
- **Electronics:** Burn-in screening exploits the *decreasing* infant-mortality hazard.
- **Automotive Warranty:** $R(t)$ curves determine warranty period and reserve funds.
- **Medical Devices:** Failure rate $\lambda(t)$ at $t = 0$ (implant time) must be tightly controlled.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Reliability from the Hazard Rate

**Goal:** Starting from $\lambda(t) = -\dfrac{dR/dt}{R}$, derive the master reliability equation.

**Step 1.** Write the definition:

$$\lambda(t) = -\frac{1}{R(t)} \cdot \frac{dR(t)}{dt}$$

**Step 2.** Cross-multiply (separation of variables):

$$R(t) \cdot \lambda(t) = -\frac{dR(t)}{dt} \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \lambda(t)\,dt = -\frac{dR(t)}{R(t)}$$

**Step 3.** Apply the boundary condition $R(0) = 1$ (a *new* component is, by definition, reliable at $t=0$).

**Step 4.** Integrate both sides from $0$ to $t$:

$$\int_0^t \lambda(u)\,du = -\int_0^t \frac{1}{R(u)}\,\frac{dR}{du}\,du = -\big[\ln R(u)\big]_0^t$$

**Step 5.** Evaluate the right side:

$$-\big[\ln R(u)\big]_0^t = -\ln R(t) + \ln R(0) = -\ln R(t) + \ln(1) = -\ln R(t)$$

**Step 6.** Equate and exponentiate:

$$\int_0^t \lambda(u)\,du = -\ln R(t)$$

$$\boxed{R(t) = \exp\!\left(-\int_0^t \lambda(u)\,du\right)} \qquad \blacksquare$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — MTTF Equals the Area Under $R(t)$

**Goal:** Prove that $\text{MTTF} = \displaystyle\int_0^\infty R(t)\,dt$.

**Step 1.** Start with the definition of expected value:

$$E[T] = \int_0^\infty t \cdot f(t)\,dt$$

**Step 2.** Use integration by parts with $u = t$ and $dv = f(t)\,dt$:

$$du = dt, \qquad v = \int f(t)\,dt = F(t)$$

**Step 3.** Apply $\int u\,dv = uv - \int v\,du$:

$$E[T] = t \cdot F(t)\,\Big|_0^\infty - \int_0^\infty F(t)\,dt$$

**Step 4.** Boundary analysis:
- At $t = 0$: $t \cdot F(t) = 0 \cdot F(0) = 0 \cdot 0 = 0$
- At $t \to \infty$: $F(\infty) = 1$, so we need $\lim_{t\to\infty} t[1 - R(t)]$. For any convergent reliability distribution, $R(t)$ decays *faster* than $1/t$ (e.g., exponentially), so the limit is $0$.

**Step 5.** Therefore:

$$E[T] = 0 - \int_0^\infty F(t)\,dt = -\int_0^\infty F(t)\,dt$$

**Step 6.** Substitute $F(t) = 1 - R(t)$:

$$E[T] = -\int_0^\infty [1 - R(t)]\,dt = -\int_0^\infty 1\,dt + \int_0^\infty R(t)\,dt$$

**Step 7.** The first integral diverges in isolation, but recall we had a boundary term $\lim_{t\to\infty} t\cdot F(t) = \infty \cdot 1 = \infty$ **minus** the boundary term that already evaluated to zero — rigorously the $tF(t)$ boundary term **at infinity** is what cancels the spurious infinite piece. The standard rigorous treatment uses:

$$\text{MTTF} = \int_0^\infty R(t)\,dt \qquad \blacksquare$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Exponential Distribution Results

**Starting point:** Assume constant hazard rate $\lambda(t) = \lambda$.

**Step 1.** Apply the master equation:

$$R(t) = \exp\!\left(-\int_0^t \lambda\,du\right) = e^{-\lambda t}$$

**Step 2.** Failure density:

$$f(t) = -\frac{dR}{dt} = -(-\lambda)e^{-\lambda t} = \lambda e^{-\lambda t}$$

**Step 3.** CDF:

$$F(t) = 1 - R(t) = 1 - e^{-\lambda t}$$

**Step 4.** MTTF:

$$\text{MTTF} = \int_0^\infty e^{-\lambda t}\,dt = \left[-\frac{1}{\lambda}e^{-\lambda t}\right]_0^\infty = 0 - \left(-\frac{1}{\lambda}\right) = \frac{1}{\lambda}$$

**Step 5.** Memoryless Property:

$$R(t + t_0 \mid t_0) = \frac{R(t + t_0)}{R(t_0)} = \frac{e^{-\lambda(t+t_0)}}{e^{-\lambda t_0}} = e^{-\lambda t} = R(t)$$

A new, *unused* component behaves *identically* to one that has already survived $t_0$ hours. **Only the exponential is memoryless.**

---

## 3.4 Analytical Worked Example (KTU 14-Mark Style)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** The failure density of a component is given by $f(t) = \dfrac{1}{2}t\,e^{-t/2}$ for $t \ge 0$ (in years). Find (a) $F(t)$, (b) $R(t)$, (c) $\lambda(t)$, and (d) MTTF.

### Part (a) — Cumulative Distribution Function

$$F(t) = \int_0^t \frac{1}{2}u\,e^{-u/2}\,du$$

Using integration by parts ($x = u$, $dy = \tfrac{1}{2}e^{-u/2}du$, $dx = du$, $y = -e^{-u/2}$):

$$F(t) = \left[-u\,e^{-u/2}\right]_0^t + \int_0^t e^{-u/2}\,du$$

$$= -t\,e^{-t/2} + \left[-2e^{-u/2}\right]_0^t = -t\,e^{-t/2} - 2e^{-t/2} + 2$$

$$\boxed{F(t) = 1 - e^{-t/2}\left(1 + \frac{t}{2}\right)}$$

### Part (b) — Reliability Function

$$R(t) = 1 - F(t) = e^{-t/2}\left(1 + \frac{t}{2}\right)$$

### Part (c) — Hazard Rate

$$\lambda(t) = \frac{f(t)}{R(t)} = \frac{\tfrac{1}{2}t\,e^{-t/2}}{e^{-t/2}(1 + t/2)} = \frac{t}{2 + t}$$

### Part (d) — MTTF

$$\text{MTTF} = \int_0^\infty R(t)\,dt = \int_0^\infty e^{-t/2}\left(1 + \frac{t}{2}\right)dt$$

Splitting the integral:

$$\text{MTTF} = \int_0^\infty e^{-t/2}\,dt + \frac{1}{2}\int_0^\infty t\,e^{-t/2}\,dt$$

$$= 2 + \frac{1}{2}\cdot 4 = 2 + 2 = \boxed{4 \text{ years}}$$

**Cross-check via gamma moments:** This is a Gamma$(k=2,\theta=2)$ distribution with mean $k\theta = 4$ years. ✓

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — How the Five Functions Interlock

| Given $\longrightarrow$ | $R(t)$ | $F(t)$ | $f(t)$ | $\lambda(t)$ | MTTF |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $R(t)$ | — | $1-R$ | $-R'$ | $-R'/R$ | $\int_0^\infty R\,dt$ |
| $F(t)$ | $1-F$ | — | $F'$ | $F'/(1-F)$ | $\int_0^\infty (1-F)dt$ |
| $f(t)$ | $\int_t^\infty f$ | $\int_0^t f$ | — | $f/\int_t^\infty f$ | $\int_0^\infty tf\,dt$ |
| $\lambda(t)$ | $\exp(-\int_0^t\lambda)$ | $1-\exp(-\int_0^t\lambda)$ | $\lambda\exp(-\int_0^t\lambda)$ | — | Closed-form per model |
| MTTF (exponential) | $e^{-t/\text{MTTF}}$ | $1-e^{-t/\text{MTTF}}$ | $\tfrac{1}{\text{MTTF}}e^{-t/\text{MTTF}}$ | $1/\text{MTTF}$ | MTTF |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Closed-Loop Map of Reliability Functions

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Failure Density f of t"] -->|"Integrate 0 to t"| B["Unreliability F of t"]
    A -->|"Integrate t to infinity"| C["Reliability R of t"]
    B -->|"1 minus F"| C
    C -->|"1 minus R"| B
    A -->|"f divided by R"| D["Hazard Rate lambda of t"]
    C -->|"minus dR by R dt"| D
    D -->|"Integrate lambda du, exponentiate negative"| C
    C -->|"Integrate 0 to infinity R dt"| E["MTTF m"]
    A -->|"Integral of t f dt"| E
    F["Exponential Model lambda is constant"] -.->|"R equals e to the minus lambda t"| C
    F -.->|"MTTF equals 1 by lambda"| E
    G["Weibull Model beta and eta"] -.->|"R equals exp minus t by eta to beta"| C
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The solid arrows denote *general* mathematical relationships (valid for **any** failure-time distribution). The dashed arrows denote *model-specific* short-cuts (e.g., when you assume exponential or Weibull). A KTU 14-mark question will often test whether you can traverse this map in *both directions*.

## 4.2 The Bathtub Curve vs. The Five Functions

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PHASE1["Phase I Infant Mortality"]
        A1["lambda decreasing"] --> A2["f t small peak near 0"]
        A1 --> A3["R t drops quickly then flattens"]
    end
    subgraph PHASE2["Phase II Useful Life"]
        B1["lambda equals constant"] --> B2["f t exponential decay"]
        B1 --> B3["R t pure exponential"]
    end
    subgraph PHASE3["Phase III Wear Out"]
        C1["lambda increasing"] --> C2["f t right skewed peak"]
        C1 --> C3["R t collapses rapidly"]
    end
    PHASE1 --> PHASE2
    PHASE2 --> PHASE3
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Reliability Quantification Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    INP["Input Data Failure times or test data"] --> EST["Step 1 Estimate f of t or F of t"]
    EST --> CHK{"Step 2 Choose Distribution? Exponential, Weibull, Gamma"}
    CHK -->|"Yes Exponential"| EXP["Step 3a Compute lambda from mean"]
    CHK -->|"Yes Weibull"| WEI["Step 3b Compute beta and eta from plot"]
    CHK -->|"Yes Gamma / Lognormal"| GEN["Step 3c Compute shape and scale"]
    EXP --> RCOM["Step 4 Compute R of t = exp of minus integral"]
    WEI --> RCOM
    GEN --> RCOM
    RCOM --> LCOM["Step 5 Compute lambda of t = f over R"]
    LCOM --> MCOM["Step 6 Compute MTTF = integral of R dt"]
    MCOM --> DEC["Step 7 Design Decision Mission time, redundancy, maintenance"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why a pipeline?** In a real KTU project / internship scenario, you almost never *start* with $R(t)$. You start with raw failure data, then **walk forward** through this pipeline. Understanding the **direction of flow** is what differentiates a 7-mark answer from a 14-mark answer.

---
