# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (Conceptual)
> **`[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`**
> Define **Reliability** and **Hazard Rate**. State the relationship between them.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Reliability** $R(t)$ is the probability that a component performs its intended function under stated conditions for a specified period of time $t$. (1 mark)
- **Hazard Rate** $\lambda(t)$ is the *conditional* instantaneous rate of failure at time $t$, given that the component has survived up to $t$. (1 mark)
- **Relationship:**
$$\lambda(t) = \frac{f(t)}{R(t)} = -\frac{1}{R(t)} \cdot \frac{dR(t)}{dt} \quad (1 \text{ mark})$$

### Question 2 (Numerical)
> **`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`**
> The reliability of a system follows $R(t) = e^{-0.001 t}$ where $t$ is in hours. Find the MTTF and the probability of failure in 500 hours.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- For an exponential distribution, MTTF $= 1/\lambda = 1/0.001 = 1000$ hours. (1 mark)
- $R(500) = e^{-0.001 \times 500} = e^{-0.5} = 0.6065$. (1 mark)
- Probability of failure in 500 hours: $F(500) = 1 - 0.6065 = 0.3935 \approx 0.394$. (1 mark)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
> **`[KTU University Exam – May 2024 | Module 4, Q Choice-1]`**
>
> **(a)** Derive the relationship $R(t) = \exp\!\left(-\displaystyle\int_0^t \lambda(u)\,du\right)$ starting from the definition of the hazard rate. List the assumptions used. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A non-repairable system has a constant failure rate $\lambda = 5 \times 10^{-4}$ failures/hour. Compute: (i) reliability for a 1000-hour mission, (ii) MTTF, (iii) MTBF assuming mean repair time $M_r = 8$ hours, and (iv) the design redundancy $n$ required to achieve system reliability $\ge 0.99$ for a 1000-hour mission if $n$ identical components are connected in **parallel** and all must function. **(7 marks)**

#### Part (a) — Model Solution

**Assumptions (1 mark):**
1. Hazard rate $\lambda(t)$ is a well-defined function.
2. $R(0) = 1$ (no failures at $t=0$).
3. $R(t)$ is continuous and differentiable.
4. Failure is a one-time event for a non-repairable component.

**Derivation (6 marks):**

By definition, the conditional probability of failure in $[t, t+dt]$ given survival to $t$ is $\lambda(t)\,dt$. But this conditional probability is also:

$$\lambda(t)\,dt = \frac{P(t \le T \le t+dt)}{P(T > t)} = \frac{f(t)\,dt}{R(t)}$$

Therefore:

$$\lambda(t) = \frac{f(t)}{R(t)} = \frac{-dR(t)/dt}{R(t)} = -\frac{d}{dt}\big[\ln R(t)\big]$$

Integrating from $0$ to $t$:

$$\int_0^t \lambda(u)\,du = -\big[\ln R(u)\big]_0^t = -\ln R(t) + \ln R(0)$$

Since $R(0) = 1 \Rightarrow \ln R(0) = 0$:

$$\ln R(t) = -\int_0^t \lambda(u)\,du$$

Exponentiating both sides:

$$\boxed{R(t) = \exp\!\left(-\int_0^t \lambda(u)\,du\right)} \qquad \blacksquare$$

#### Part (b) — Model Solution

**Given:** $\lambda = 5 \times 10^{-4}$ failures/hour, $t = 1000$ h, $M_r = 8$ h.

**(i) Reliability for 1000-hour mission (2 marks):**
$$R(1000) = e^{-\lambda t} = e^{-(5 \times 10^{-4})(1000)} = e^{-0.5} = 0.6065$$

**(ii) MTTF (1 mark):**
$$\text{MTTF} = \frac{1}{\lambda} = \frac{1}{5 \times 10^{-4}} = 2000 \text{ hours}$$

**(iii) MTBF (1 mark):**
$$\text{MTBF} = \text{MTTF} + M_r = 2000 + 8 = 2008 \text{ hours}$$

**(iv) Redundancy $n$ for parallel configuration (3 marks):**
For $n$ parallel components, all must fail for the system to fail:
$$R_{\text{sys}}(t) = 1 - [1 - R(t)]^n \ge 0.99$$
$$[1 - 0.6065]^n \le 0.01$$
$$(0.3935)^n \le 0.01$$
$$n \cdot \ln(0.3935) \le \ln(0.01)$$
$$n \cdot (-0.9328) \le -4.6052$$
$$n \ge \frac{4.6052}{0.9328} = 4.937$$
$$\boxed{n = 5 \text{ components}}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)
> **`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 | Module 4, Q Choice-2]`**
>
> **(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the **bathtub curve** for failure rate. Identify the three phases and the dominant failure mechanism in each. State which mathematical distribution best models each phase. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** The failure density of a component is $f(t) = 0.02\,e^{-0.02 t}$ for $t \ge 0$ (years). Find: (i) $R(t)$, (ii) $\lambda(t)$, (iii) MTTF, and (iv) the reliability for a 50-year mission. Comment on the memoryless property. **(7 marks)**

#### Part (a) — Model Solution

**Sketch (3 marks):** Failure rate $\lambda(t)$ vs. time $t$ showing the three regions of the bathtub.

**Phase I — Infant Mortality (Burn-in)** (1.33 marks)
- $\lambda(t)$ is **decreasing** with time.
- Dominant mechanism: manufacturing defects, poor solder joints, contamination.
- Best model: **Weibull with shape parameter $\beta < 1$**.
- Engineering action: factory **burn-in screening** to eliminate weak units.

**Phase II — Useful Life (Random Failures)** (1.33 marks)
- $\lambda(t) \approx \text{constant} = \lambda$.
- Dominant mechanism: random overload, environmental shocks.
- Best model: **Exponential distribution** (memoryless).
- Engineering action: preventive / scheduled maintenance.

**Phase III — Wear Out** (1.34 marks)
- $\lambda(t)$ is **increasing**, often rapidly.
- Dominant mechanism: fatigue, corrosion, aging, dielectric breakdown.
- Best model: **Weibull with $\beta > 1$** or **Lognormal**.
- Engineering action: **replacement** before end of useful life.

#### Part (b) — Model Solution

**Given:** $f(t) = 0.02\,e^{-0.02 t}$.

**(i) Reliability (2 marks):**
$$F(t) = \int_0^t 0.02\,e^{-0.02 u}\,du = 1 - e^{-0.02 t}$$
$$R(t) = 1 - F(t) = e^{-0.02 t}$$

**(ii) Hazard Rate (1 mark):**
$$\lambda(t) = \frac{f(t)}{R(t)} = \frac{0.02\,e^{-0.02 t}}{e^{-0.02 t}} = 0.02 \text{ per year (constant)}$$

**(iii) MTTF (1 mark):**
$$\text{MTTF} = \frac{1}{\lambda} = \frac{1}{0.02} = 50 \text{ years}$$

**(iv) Reliability for 50-year mission (1 mark):**
$$R(50) = e^{-0.02 \times 50} = e^{-1} = 0.3679$$

**Memoryless Property (2 marks):** Since $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ is exponential, the distribution is **memoryless**:
$$R(t + t_0 \mid t_0) = \frac{R(t+t_0)}{R(t_0)} = \frac{e^{-0.02(t+t_0)}}{e^{-0.02 t_0}} = e^{-0.02 t} = R(t)$$

A 10-year-old component is *as good as new* from a reliability standpoint. Practically, this means **preventive replacement of "old" components is wasteful** during Phase II — you gain nothing by replacing a working unit.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
>
> 1. **Sign error in $f(t) = -dR/dt$** — Many students forget the negative sign. Reliability *decreases* with time, so $dR/dt$ is negative; multiplying by $-1$ yields a positive $f(t)$. (Lose 1 mark.)
> 2. **Confusing MTTF and MTBF** — They are *not* equal for repairable systems. MTBF $=$ MTTF $+ M_r$. Only for non-repairable systems do they coincide. (Lose 1–2 marks.)
> 3. **Forgetting the boundary condition $R(0) = 1$** — In derivation questions, KTU examiners *expect* this. (Lose 1 mark.)
> 4. **Treating Weibull $\beta = 1$ as "exponential"** — That is *correct*, but write it explicitly: "Weibull with $\beta = 1$ reduces to exponential." (Lose 0.5 mark for imprecision.)
> 5. **Wrong units of $\lambda$** — If $t$ is in hours, $\lambda$ must be in **per hour**. Mixing units loses 1 mark.
> 6. **Skipping the parallel-redundancy logic** — In Q-A(b)(iv), students often write $R_{\text{sys}} = R^n$ (which is for *series*). For *parallel*, it is $1 - (1-R)^n$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Relationship Between Reliability**
>
> - [ ] **The Big Five:** $R(t)$, $F(t)$, $f(t)$, $\lambda(t)$, MTTF are *interchangeable* — given any one, derive the rest.
> - [ ] **Master Identity:** $R(t) + F(t) = 1$. Always true.
> - [ ] **Derivative Link:** $f(t) = -dR/dt = dF/dt$.
> - [ ] **Hazard Link:** $\lambda(t) = f(t)/R(t)$.
> - [ ] **Master Equation:** $R(t) = \exp\!\left(-\int_0^t \lambda(u)du\right)$.
> - [ ] **MTTF as Area:** $\text{MTTF} = \int_0^\infty R(t)\,dt$.
> - [ ] **Exponential Specials:** $R = e^{-\lambda t}$, $f = \lambda e^{-\lambda t}$, MTTF $= 1/\lambda$, **memoryless**.
> - [ ] **Weibull Specials:** $R = \exp[-(t/\eta)^\beta]$; $\beta < 1$ = infant mortality, $\beta = 1$ = exponential, $\beta > 1$ = wear-out.
> - [ ] **Bathtub Curve:** Three phases → three modelling regimes.
> - [ ] **Parallel vs Series:** Parallel $R_{\text{sys}} = 1 - (1-R)^n$; Series $R_{\text{sys}} = \prod R_i$.
> - [ ] **MTBF vs MTTF:** $\text{MTBF} = \text{MTTF} + M_r$ for repairable systems.
> - [ ] **Boundary Condition:** $R(0) = 1$ — state explicitly in derivations.
> - [ ] **Units Discipline:** $\lambda$ in failures/hour matches $t$ in hours; MTTF/MTBF in hours.
> - [ ] **Memoryless Property** is the *unique* signature of the exponential distribution.
> - [ ] **Conditional Reliability:** $R(t+t_0 \mid t_0) = R(t+t_0)/R(t_0)$ — used for "given survived $t_0$, what is the chance of surviving $t$ more?"

---

**End of Module 4 Notes — Relationship Between Reliability**

*Aligned to KTU 2024 Scheme, Course Outcomes CO4, and Revised Bloom's Taxonomy levels L3 (Apply) to L5 (Evaluate).*

topics.title: Availability and Maintainability
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET632 - RELIABILITY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Availability and Maintainability

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Definition of Availability

**Availability** is the probability that a system or component is operational and able to perform its intended function at a given instant of time, under stated conditions, assuming that the required external resources are provided.

Mathematically, it represents the **fraction of time** that a system is actually in the *up state* (working) versus the total calendar time (up time + down time).

$$A(t) = \Pr\{\text{System is functional at time } t\}$$

In reliability engineering, availability is often classified into three principal forms, all of which are critical from a KTU examination perspective:

1. **Inherent Availability ($A_i$)** – Considers only *corrective maintenance* downtimes. It is the theoretical upper bound of availability achievable under ideal logistics support.
2. **Achieved Availability ($A_a$)** – Considers *corrective maintenance* downtimes *plus* active preventive maintenance downtimes, ignoring logistics and administrative delays.
3. **Operational Availability ($A_o$)** – The realistic, field-measured availability that includes all downtimes: corrective, preventive, administrative delays, and logistics (supply) delays.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Distinction**: For numerical problems in the 2024 Scheme ESE, unless stated otherwise, students are expected to compute **Inherent Availability** using the formula based purely on **MTBF** and **MTTR**.

## 1.2 Definition of Maintainability

**Maintainability** is defined as the probability that a failed system or component will be restored to operational condition within a specified period of time, when maintenance is performed in accordance with prescribed procedures and resources.

$$M(t) = \Pr\{\text{System is repaired within time } t\}$$

It is a **design-inherent property** of the equipment, describing the *ease, speed, and economy* with which maintenance actions can be executed. Maintainability is quantified using the **Mean Time To Repair (MTTR)** and the **Maintainability Function $M(t)$**, both of which depend on the chosen repair-time distribution (most commonly the **lognormal** or **exponential** distribution in KTU problems).

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy – The Hospital Emergency Room (ER)**:
>
> Imagine a city hospital with an **Emergency Room (ER)**.
>
> - **Reliability** = The probability that the ER is *functioning* (lights on, doctors available, equipment working) at any random moment. It only asks: *"Is the system up?"*
> - **Maintainability** = Once the ER fails (say, a power outage or an X-ray machine breaks down), how *quickly* can the engineering team bring it back? This depends on spare parts availability, technician skill, repair manuals, and diagnostic tools.
> - **Availability** = The *combined* picture. It answers the practical question: *"Out of 24 hours, for how many hours is the ER actually accepting patients?"*
>
> A highly **reliable** ER that takes 3 days to fix when it does fail may have **lower availability** than a slightly less reliable ER that gets repaired in 30 minutes. This is why modern engineering design (aircraft engines, data centers, power grids) prioritizes *Availability Engineering* over pure reliability alone.

**Key Engineering Metrics to Remember (in Bold):**

- **MTBF (Mean Time Between Failures)** – Average operating time between two consecutive failures.
- **MTTR (Mean Time To Repair)** – Average time required to restore a failed system.
- **MDT (Mean Down Time)** – Average time the system is unavailable, including logistics delays.
- **A (Availability)** – Fraction of total time the system is operational.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of System Effectiveness

Availability sits at the intersection of two fundamental engineering properties: **Reliability** and **Maintainability**. The interplay is visualized below in logical order:

1. **Failure occurs** → governed by the unreliability function $F(t) = 1 - R(t)$ and the failure rate $\lambda(t)$.
2. **Detection and Diagnosis** → the maintenance crew is notified and identifies the root cause (logistics delay time, $M_{LDT}$).
3. **Active Repair** → the actual hands-on repair time (active corrective maintenance time, $\tilde{M}_{ct}$).
4. **Restoration and Checkout** → verification that the system is again functional (restoration time).
5. **Return to Service** → system is up and available once more.

> [!TIP]
> **Why is this important for KTU 2024?** Many 14-mark problems ask students to *break down total downtime* into its constituent elements and to compute operational availability for real scenarios. Memorize the components of **MTTR** and **MDT**.

## 2.2 Availability – The Three Variants Explained

### (a) Inherent Availability ($A_i$)

It assumes the perfect support environment: spares are on the shelf, technicians are idle and ready, no administrative delays. Only the actual repair time matters.

$$A_i = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$$

- **Range:** $0 \le A_i \le 1$
- **KTU Standard:** This is the *most frequently tested* form in semester examinations.

### (b) Achieved Availability ($A_a$)

It includes the downtime consumed by **scheduled preventive maintenance (PM)** in addition to corrective repairs.

$$A_a = \dfrac{\text{MTBM}}{\text{MTBM} + \overline{M}}$$

Where:
- **MTBM** = Mean Time Between Maintenance (corrective + preventive actions)
- $\overline{M}$ = Mean active maintenance time (corrective + preventive)

### (c) Operational Availability ($A_o$)

The most realistic metric, accounting for all real-world delays including supply chain, administrative, and personnel stand-by time.

$$A_o = \dfrac{\text{Uptime}}{\text{Uptime} + \text{Downtime}} = \dfrac{\text{MTBM}}{\text{MTBM} + \text{MDT}}$$

Where **MDT = MTTR + Mean Logistics Delay Time + Mean Administrative Delay Time**.

## 2.3 Maintainability Function $M(t)$

Just as the reliability function $R(t)$ describes the probability of *survival* up to time $t$, the maintainability function $M(t)$ describes the probability of *successful repair completion* within time $t$.

For a system whose repair time $X$ is a **continuous random variable** with probability density function $g(x)$:

$$M(t) = \Pr\{X \le t\} = \int_{0}^{t} g(x)\, dx$$

The mean of this distribution is the **MTTR**:

$$\text{MTTR} = \int_{0}^{\infty} x\, g(x)\, dx = \int_{0}^{\infty} \bigl[1 - M(x)\bigr]\, dx$$

## 2.4 Repair Time Distributions (KTU-Approved)

| Distribution | Maintainability Function $M(t)$ | MTTR |
|---|---|---|
| Exponential | $1 - e^{-\mu t}$ | $1/\mu$ |
| Lognormal | $\Phi\!\left(\dfrac{\ln t - \mu'}{\sigma'}\right)$ | $\exp\!\left(\mu' + \dfrac{\sigma'^2}{2}\right)$ |
| Weibull (repair) | $1 - e^{-(t/\alpha)^\beta}$ | $\alpha\,\Gamma(1 + 1/\beta)$ |
| Normal (approx.) | $\Phi\!\left(\dfrac{t - \mu_r}{\sigma_r}\right)$ | $\mu_r$ |

**Symbols in the table:**
- $\mu$ = constant repair rate
- $\mu'$, $\sigma'$ = mean and standard deviation of $\ln X$ (lognormal)
- $\Phi$ = standard normal CDF
- $\alpha$, $\beta$ = Weibull scale and shape parameters
- $\Gamma(\cdot)$ = Gamma function

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Inherent Availability | $A_i = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$ | Dimensionless, $0 \le A_i \le 1$ |
| 2 | Unavailability | $U_i = 1 - A_i = \dfrac{\text{MTTR}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$ | Sometimes called *outage rate* |
| 3 | Achieved Availability | $A_a = \dfrac{\text{MTBM}}{\text{MTBM} + \overline{M}}$ | Includes PM downtime |
| 4 | Operational Availability | $A_o = \dfrac{\text{MTBM}}{\text{MTBM} + \text{MDT}}$ | Most realistic metric |
| 5 | Mean Down Time | $\text{MDT} = \text{MTTR} + M_{LDT} + M_{ADT}$ | Logistics + Admin delays |
| 6 | MTBF for constant failure rate | $\text{MTBF} = 1/\lambda$ | Exponential reliability |
| 7 | Maintainability Function | $M(t) = \int_0^t g(x)\, dx$ | $g(x)$ = repair time PDF |
| 8 | MTTR | $\text{MTTR} = \int_0^\infty \bigl[1 - M(x)\bigr] dx$ | Expected repair time |
| 9 | Instantaneous Availability | $A(t) = R(t) + \int_0^t R(t-x)\, m(x)\, dx$ | $m(x)$ = repair density |
| 10 | Steady-state Availability | $A(\infty) = \dfrac{\mu}{\lambda + \mu}$ | For exponential, $\mu = 1/\text{MTTR}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility**: The concept of availability is foundational in **maintenance planning**, **warranty cost estimation**, **spare parts provisioning**, and **Service-Level Agreement (SLA)** design in industries such as aviation (dispatch reliability), telecommunications (network uptime), and data centers (e.g., "five nines" = 99.999% availability).

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 – Steady-State Inherent Availability (Exponential Assumption)

**Assumption:** Both the failure process and the repair process follow **memoryless (exponential)** distributions. The system alternates between two states: **UP (working)** and **DOWN (under repair)**.

Let:
- $\lambda$ = constant failure rate (failures per hour)
- $\mu$ = constant repair rate (repairs per hour)
- $\text{MTBF} = 1/\lambda$
- $\text{MTTR} = 1/\mu$

**Step 1:** Consider a system that has been operating for a very long time. Its behaviour becomes **statistically stationary** (steady-state), meaning the long-run fraction of time spent in the UP state is a constant.

**Step 2:** In steady state, the **expected number of transitions UP → DOWN** must equal the **expected number of transitions DOWN → UP** per unit time. This is a *flow-balance* condition.

$$\text{Transition rate (UP} \to \text{DOWN)} = A(\infty)\cdot \lambda$$
$$\text{Transition rate (DOWN} \to \text{UP)} = \bigl[1 - A(\infty)\bigr]\cdot \mu$$

**Step 3:** Equate the two transition rates to enforce flow balance.

$$A(\infty)\cdot \lambda = \bigl[1 - A(\infty)\bigr]\cdot \mu$$

**Step 4:** Expand and solve for $A(\infty)$.

$$A(\infty)\,\lambda = \mu - A(\infty)\,\mu$$

$$A(\infty)\,\lambda + A(\infty)\,\mu = \mu$$

$$A(\infty)\,(\lambda + \mu) = \mu$$

$$\boxed{A(\infty) = \dfrac{\mu}{\lambda + \mu}}$$

**Step 5:** Substitute $\lambda = 1/\text{MTBF}$ and $\mu = 1/\text{MTTR}$ to obtain the most popular KTU formula.

$$A(\infty) = \dfrac{1/\text{MTTR}}{1/\text{MTBF} + 1/\text{MTTR}} = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$$

The boundary condition $0 \le A(\infty) \le 1$ is automatically satisfied because all parameters are positive.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 – Instantaneous Availability $A(t)$ Using Renewal Theory

**Setup:** Let the system start in the UP state at $t = 0$. It experiences alternating cycles of operation and repair. Let $X$ denote the operating time (with reliability function $R(x)$) and $Y$ denote the repair time (with maintainability function $M(y)$).

**Step 1:** The system is available at time $t$ if *either*:
- (Case A) It has not yet failed by time $t$ → probability $R(t)$, **or**
- (Case B) It failed some time $x < t$, was repaired, and is currently in a *new* operating interval that has survived to time $t$.

**Step 2:** Express Case B as a convolution. Let $m(x)$ be the PDF of repair time, so that the system is restored at time $x$ with density $f(x)\, m(x)$, where $f(x)$ is the failure-time density. The probability that the system is still up at time $t$ *after* the repair is $R(t - x)$.

**Step 3:** Integrate over all possible failure times $0 \le x \le t$:

$$A(t) = R(t) + \int_{0}^{t} R(t - x)\, f(x)\, m(x)\, dx$$

**Step 4:** The integral $\int_0^t R(t-x)\,f(x)\,m(x)\,dx$ is a **convolution integral** of the failure-repair density with the residual reliability. This is the **renewal-equation form** of instantaneous availability.

$$A(t) = R(t) + \bigl[R * (f \cdot m)\bigr](t)$$

**Step 5:** As $t \to \infty$, under mild conditions (finite mean of $X$ and $Y$), the integral converges to a constant:

$$\lim_{t \to \infty} A(t) = \dfrac{E[X]}{E[X] + E[Y]} = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$$

This confirms that the *steady-state* availability is independent of the actual failure/repair distributions — only the means matter. The **time-dependent** form $A(t)$ does, however, depend on the chosen distributions (exponential, Weibull, etc.).

## 3.3 Derivation 3 – Maintainability Function for Logarithmic-Normal (Lognormal) Repair Times

Many real-world repair processes are **right-skewed** (most repairs are quick, but a few take very long). The **lognormal distribution** is the industry-standard model for such behaviour.

**Step 1:** Let $Y$ be the repair time. Assume $\ln Y$ is normally distributed with mean $\mu'$ and standard deviation $\sigma'$. Then $Y$ has PDF:

$$g(y) = \dfrac{1}{y \sigma' \sqrt{2\pi}}\, \exp\!\left[-\dfrac{(\ln y - \mu')^2}{2\sigma'^2}\right], \quad y > 0$$

**Step 2:** The maintainability function is the CDF of $Y$:

$$M(t) = \Pr\{Y \le t\} = \Pr\{\ln Y \le \ln t\}$$

**Step 3:** Standardize the variable $Z = \dfrac{\ln Y - \mu'}{\sigma'}$, so that $Z \sim N(0, 1)$. Then:

$$M(t) = \Pr\!\left\{Z \le \dfrac{\ln t - \mu'}{\sigma'}\right\} = \Phi\!\left(\dfrac{\ln t - \mu'}{\sigma'}\right)$$

where $\Phi(\cdot)$ is the standard normal CDF.

**Step 4:** Compute the MTTR using the known lognormal mean:

$$\text{MTTR} = E[Y] = \int_0^\infty y\, g(y)\, dy = \exp\!\left(\mu' + \dfrac{\sigma'^2}{2}\right)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examination Tip:** When lognormal repair data is given as $(\mu', \sigma')$, the MTTR is *not* $\mu'$. This is a common trap — students often mistakenly take the median or the parameter $\mu'$ as MTTR. The correct MTTR is $\exp(\mu' + \sigma'^2/2)$.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix – Quick Decision Tree for KTU Problems

| Given Quantity | Goal | Recommended Path |
|---|---|---|
| MTBF, MTTR only | Compute $A_i$ | Direct use of $\text{MTBF} / (\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR})$ |
| $\lambda$, $\mu$ only | Compute $A_i$ | $A_i = \mu / (\lambda + \mu)$ |
| $\lambda$ and $t$ only | Compute $A(t)$ | $A(t) = \dfrac{\mu}{\lambda + \mu} + \dfrac{\lambda}{\lambda + \mu}\, e^{-(\lambda + \mu)t}$ |
| Lognormal $\mu'$, $\sigma'$ | Find $M(t)$ or MTTR | Use $\Phi$ tables and $E[Y] = e^{\mu' + \sigma'^2/2}$ |
| Multiple subsystems in series | System availability | Multiply individual availabilities |
| Multiple subsystems in parallel | System availability | Use $1 - \prod(1 - A_i)$ |

The fourth row formula for $A(t)$ is the **exact transient solution** for a single-component system with exponential failure and exponential repair, starting in the UP state:

$$A(t) = \dfrac{\mu}{\lambda + \mu} + \dfrac{\lambda}{\lambda + \mu}\, e^{-(\lambda + \mu)t}$$

**Verification:** At $t = 0$, $A(0) = 1$ (system just started, must be up). As $t \to \infty$, the exponential term decays to zero, leaving $A(\infty) = \mu/(\lambda + \mu)$. ✓

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 System Availability State Diagram (Markov Model)

The simplest mathematical model of a repairable system is a **two-state Markov chain**. The system flips between "Operating (UP)" and "Under Repair (DOWN)" with rates $\lambda$ and $\mu$ respectively.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> UP
    UP --> DOWN : Failure with rate lambda
    DOWN --> UP : Repair with rate mu
    UP : State 1 : Operating
    UP : Available Probability A of t
    DOWN : State 2 : Under Repair
    DOWN : Unavailable Probability U of t
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- Horizontal arrow from `UP` to `DOWN` is labelled with the **failure rate $\lambda$** (failures per hour).
- Return arrow from `DOWN` to `UP` is labelled with the **repair rate $\mu$** (repairs per hour).
- In the long run, the *fraction of time* in the `UP` state is the steady-state availability $A(\infty) = \mu / (\lambda + \mu)$.

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Availability Engineering

Availability is not a single discipline — it emerges from the coordinated interaction of three engineering sub-systems: **Design**, **Maintenance Logistics**, and **Operations Support**.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[System Design Phase] --> B1[Inherent Availability A of i]
    B1 --> C1[Field Operation]
    C1 --> D1[Failure Event]
    D1 --> E1[Logistics Support System]
    E1 --> F1[Active Repair Execution]
    F1 --> G1[Restored System]
    G1 --> C1
    C1 --> H1[Operational Availability A of o]
    H1 --> I1[Achieved Availability A of a]
    I1 --> J1[Final System Effectiveness Metric]
    style A1 fill:#fde2e4,stroke:#b00020
    style B1 fill:#e2f0cb,stroke:#387002
    style C1 fill:#cdeac0,stroke:#1a3d00
    style D1 fill:#ffd6a5,stroke:#9c5400
    style E1 fill:#fdffb6,stroke:#7a6c00
    style F1 fill:#caffbf,stroke:#1b5e20
    style G1 fill:#bdb2ff,stroke:#3a00b3
    style H1 fill:#ffc6ff,stroke:#800080
    style I1 fill:#9bf6ff,stroke:#005f73
    style J1 fill:#a0c4ff,stroke:#003566
```

**Functional Description of Each Block:**

| Block | Functional Role |
|---|---|
| `System Design Phase` | Inherent design choices: component selection, redundancy, modularity |
| `Inherent Availability A of i` | Theoretical maximum, $A_i = \text{MTBF}/(\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR})$ |
| `Field Operation` | Real-world usage begins; failures and PM events occur |
| `Failure Event` | Stochastic failure governed by $R(t)$ and failure rate $\lambda$ |
| `Logistics Support System` | Supply chain, spares inventory, technician dispatch |
| `Active Repair Execution` | On-site hands-on repair governed by maintainability function $M(t)$ |
| `Restored System` | System returns to operational state |
| `Operational Availability A of o` | Realistic field metric including all delays |
| `Achieved Availability A of a` | Intermediate metric (corrective + PM downtime only) |
| `Final System Effectiveness Metric` | Composite metric for management reporting |

## 4.3 Time-Line Schematic of an Availability Cycle

The diagram below illustrates one complete **UP–DOWN–UP cycle**, helping students visualize the components that contribute to **MDT** and **MTBF**.

```mermaid
gantt
    title Typical Availability Cycle of a Repairable System
    dateFormat  X
    axisFormat  %S
    section Operating Phase
    MTBF Operating Interval :a1, 0, 800
    section Failure Event
    Failure Occurs and Detection :crit, a2, 800, 20
    section Repair Phase
    Logistics and Admin Delay MLDT :a3, 820, 50
    Active Corrective Repair Mct :a4, 870, 80
    Restoration and Verification :a5, 950, 30
    section Return to Service
    System Restored UP :milestone, a6, 980, 0
```

**Reading the time-line:**
- The first long block represents **MTBF** (800 units of operating time).
- The failure event is marked as **critical** (red highlight).
- The repair phase is split into **logistics delay** (50 units), **active repair** (80 units), and **restoration** (30 units), totalling **MDT = 160 units**.
- Therefore, the cycle length is $800 + 160 = 960$ time units, and the local availability for this cycle is $800/960 \approx 0.833$.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Differentiate between Inherent Availability and Operational Availability. Which one is more realistic, and why?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Inherent Availability ($A_i$) | Operational Availability ($A_o$) |
|---|---|---|
| Formula | $A_i = \text{MTBF}/(\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR})$ | $A_o = \text{MTBM}/(\text{MTBM} + \text{MDT})$ |
| Downtime Considered | Active corrective maintenance only | Corrective + Preventive + Logistics + Admin |
| Support Assumed | Ideal (spares on shelf, technicians idle) | Real-world conditions |
| Use Case | Design-time theoretical benchmark | Field performance measurement |

**More realistic:** **Operational Availability ($A_o$)** because it accounts for *real-world delays* such as spare-parts procurement, administrative approvals, and personnel mobilization. (1 mark for the conclusion + 1 mark each for differentiation points = 3 marks)

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Define MTTR. Why is reducing MTTR often more cost-effective than increasing MTBF, in the context of availability improvement?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **MTTR (Mean Time To Repair):** The average time required to perform active corrective maintenance to restore a failed system to fully operational condition. (1 mark)
- The availability formula $A = \text{MTBF}/(\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR})$ is **more sensitive to changes in MTTR** than to equivalent relative changes in MTBF. For example, halving MTTR from 10 h to 5 h (with MTBF = 1000 h) changes $A$ from $0.990$ to $0.995$, a gain of **0.5%**. Doubling MTBF (which is far more expensive — better components, derating, redundancy) changes $A$ from $0.990$ to $0.995$, the same gain but at *much higher cost*. (2 marks)

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each – Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**A complex electronic system has an MTBF of 2000 hours and an MTTR of 40 hours.**

**(a)** Compute the **inherent availability** and the corresponding **unavailability**. Comment on the engineering significance of the unavailability figure. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** If the design team improves the system so that the failure rate drops by 20% and the repair rate doubles, recompute the new inherent availability. By what percentage has the availability improved? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

**Step 1:** Identify the given data.
- MTBF $= 2000$ hours
- MTTR $= 40$ hours

**Step 2:** Apply the inherent availability formula.

$$A_i = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}} = \dfrac{2000}{2000 + 40} = \dfrac{2000}{2040}$$

$$\boxed{A_i \approx 0.9804 \text{ or } 98.04\%}$$

**Step 3:** Compute the unavailability.

$$U_i = 1 - A_i = 1 - 0.9804 = 0.0196 \text{ or } 1.96\%$$

Alternatively using the direct formula:

$$U_i = \dfrac{\text{MTTR}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}} = \dfrac{40}{2040} \approx 0.0196$$

**Step 4:** Engineering comment.
The system is unavailable approximately 1.96% of the time. Over a year (8760 hours), this corresponds to roughly **172 hours of downtime per year** — about 7 days. For mission-critical systems (e.g., aircraft avionics, hospital ventilators), this would be considered *insufficient* and would require further design improvements.

---

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

**Step 1:** Determine the original failure rate and repair rate.

$$\lambda_{\text{old}} = 1/\text{MTBF} = 1/2000 = 5 \times 10^{-4} \text{ per hour}$$

$$\mu_{\text{old}} = 1/\text{MTTR} = 1/40 = 0.025 \text{ per hour}$$

**Step 2:** Apply the design improvements.
- New failure rate: $\lambda_{\text{new}} = 0.8 \times \lambda_{\text{old}} = 0.8 \times 5 \times 10^{-4} = 4 \times 10^{-4}$ per hour
- New repair rate: $\mu_{\text{new}} = 2 \times \mu_{\text{old}} = 2 \times 0.025 = 0.050$ per hour

**Step 3:** New MTBF and MTTR.

$$\text{MTBF}_{\text{new}} = 1/\lambda_{\text{new}} = 1/(4 \times 10^{-4}) = 2500 \text{ hours}$$

$$\text{MTTR}_{\text{new}} = 1/\mu_{\text{new}} = 1/0.050 = 20 \text{ hours}$$

**Step 4:** Compute the new availability.

$$A_{i,\text{new}} = \dfrac{2500}{2500 + 20} = \dfrac{2500}{2520} \approx 0.9921 \text{ or } 99.21\%$$

**Step 5:** Compute the percentage improvement in availability.

$$\Delta A = A_{i,\text{new}} - A_{i,\text{old}} = 0.9921 - 0.9804 = 0.0117$$

$$\text{Percentage improvement} = \dfrac{0.0117}{0.9804} \times 100\% \approx 1.19\%$$

The new design improves availability by approximately **1.19 percentage points** (relative improvement ≈ 1.19%).

---

### Question B (Choice 2 – Alternative) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**The repair time of a CNC machine tool is modelled by a lognormal distribution with parameters $\mu' = 1.5$ and $\sigma' = 0.4$ (both natural log scale).**

**(a)** Compute the **Mean Time To Repair (MTTR)** and the **standard deviation** of the repair time. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Find the probability that a repair will be completed **within 5 hours** of failure. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

**Step 1:** Recall the lognormal moments.
For a lognormal distribution with parameters $\mu'$ and $\sigma'$:

$$E[Y] = \exp\!\left(\mu' + \dfrac{\sigma'^2}{2}\right)$$

$$\text{Var}(Y) = \left[\exp(\sigma'^2) - 1\right] \cdot \exp(2\mu' + \sigma'^2)$$

$$\text{SD}(Y) = \sqrt{\text{Var}(Y)} = \exp\!\left(\mu' + \dfrac{\sigma'^2}{2}\right) \cdot \sqrt{\exp(\sigma'^2) - 1}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute $\mu' = 1.5$ and $\sigma' = 0.4$.

$$\sigma'^2 = 0.16$$

$$E[Y] = \exp(1.5 + 0.08) = \exp(1.58) \approx 4.855 \text{ hours}$$

$$\boxed{\text{MTTR} \approx 4.86 \text{ hours}}$$

**Step 3:** Compute the standard deviation.

$$\exp(\sigma'^2) = \exp(0.16) \approx 1.1735$$

$$\exp(\sigma'^2) - 1 \approx 0.1735$$

$$\text{SD}(Y) = 4.855 \times \sqrt{0.1735} = 4.855 \times 0.4165 \approx 2.022 \text{ hours}$$

$$\boxed{\text{SD}(Y) \approx 2.02 \text{ hours}}$$

---

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

**Step 1:** Use the maintainability function for the lognormal distribution.

$$M(t) = \Phi\!\left(\dfrac{\ln t - \mu'}{\sigma'}\right)$$

**Step 2:** Substitute $t = 5$ hours.

$$\ln 5 \approx 1.6094$$

$$Z = \dfrac{1.6094 - 1.5}{0.4} = \dfrac{0.1094}{0.4} = 0.2735$$

**Step 3:** Look up the standard normal CDF at $Z = 0.2735$.

From standard normal tables (or a calculator):

$$\Phi(0.27) \approx 0.6064$$
$$\Phi(0.28) \approx 0.6103$$

Linear interpolation for $Z = 0.2735$:

$$\Phi(0.2735) \approx 0.6064 + 0.35 \times (0.6103 - 0.6064) \approx 0.6064 + 0.35 \times 0.0039 \approx 0.6078$$

**Step 4:** Interpretation.

$$\boxed{M(5) \approx 0.608 \text{ or } 60.8\%}$$

There is approximately a **60.8% probability** that the CNC machine tool repair will be completed within 5 hours of failure.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU 2024 ESE:**
>
> 1. **Mixing MTBF and MTBM**: $A_i$ uses MTBF (corrective only), while $A_a$ and $A_o$ use MTBM (corrective + preventive). Substituting the wrong one loses **2 marks** instantly.
> 2. **Forgetting the Exponential Decay Term in $A(t)$**: The transient availability is $A(t) = \mu/(\lambda + \mu) + \lambda/(\lambda + \mu) \cdot e^{-(\lambda + \mu)t}$. Students who only write the steady-state formula $\mu/(\lambda + \mu)$ for "availability at time $t$" lose marks unless the question explicitly asks for steady-state.
> 3. **Confusing MTTR parameter with the Lognormal $\mu'$**: In a lognormal problem, $\mu'$ is *not* the MTTR. The actual MTTR is $\exp(\mu' + \sigma'^2/2)$. This is a **3-mark trap**.
> 4. **Unit Mismatch**: Failure rate in *per hour* versus MTBF in *hours*. Always check that the rates are reciprocally consistent.
> 5. **Series System Availability Misinterpretation**: For two components in series, the system availability is $A_{\text{sys}} = A_1 \times A_2$, **not** $\min(A_1, A_2)$. Many students wrongly take the minimum.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Availability** = fraction of time the system is *up*; **Maintainability** = probability of *repair within time* $t$.
- **Three flavours of availability**: Inherent ($A_i$, design), Achieved ($A_a$, + PM), Operational ($A_o$, + logistics/admin).
- **Master formula**: $\boxed{A_i = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}}$
- **Inverse form**: $\boxed{A_i = \dfrac{\mu}{\lambda + \mu}}$ (exponential assumption).
- **Unavailability**: $\boxed{U_i = \dfrac{\text{MTTR}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}} = \dfrac{\lambda}{\lambda + \mu}}$.
- **Transient availability** (exponential): $A(t) = \dfrac{\mu}{\lambda + \mu} + \dfrac{\lambda}{\lambda + \mu}\, e^{-(\lambda+\mu)t}$.
- **Maintainability function**: $M(t) = \int_0^t g(x)\, dx$; **MTTR** = mean of repair-time distribution.
- **Lognormal MTTR** = $\exp(\mu' + \sigma'^2/2)$, **not** $\mu'$.
- **Series availability**: $A_s = \prod A_i$ ; **Parallel availability**: $A_p = 1 - \prod (1 - A_i)$.
- **MDT** = MTTR + Logistics Delay + Admin Delay — the total real-world downtime.
- **Engineering insight**: Improving MTTR is often *cheaper* than proportionally improving MTBF for the same availability gain.
- **Markov model**: Two states (UP, DOWN) with transition rates $\lambda$ and $\mu$ — fundamental to most KTU derivations.
- **Time-line of one cycle**: MTBF (operating) → Failure → Logistics Delay → Active Repair → Restoration → next cycle.

> [!TIP]
> **One-Line Memory Aid for KTU 2024 ESE:** *"Availability asks 'How often is it working?' — Maintainability asks 'When it breaks, how fast can we fix it?' The ratio $\text{MTBF}/(\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR})$ ties them together."*

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Inherent Availability
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET632 - RELIABILITY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Inherent Availability

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus)

**Inherent Availability ($A_i$)** is the steady-state probability that a system, equipment, or component is operable and able to perform its intended function at any random point in time, *when considered under an ideal support environment* — meaning that downtime includes **only corrective maintenance (repair) time** and explicitly excludes preventive maintenance time, administrative delays, logistics delays, and supply chain downtime.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition (Verbatim Exam-Worthy):**
> *"Inherent Availability is a measure of availability that includes only the effects of reliability and maintainability design — i.e., Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF) and Mean Time To Repair (MTTR) — and excludes all other elements of support such as preventive maintenance, waiting for spares, and administrative time."*

Mathematically:

$$A_i = \frac{\text{Uptime (Mission Time)}}{\text{Uptime + Corrective Downtime}} = \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$$

Where:
- **MTBF** = **Mean Time Between Failures** (hours)
- **MTTR** = **Mean Time To Repair** (hours)

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Car Engine" Intuition

Imagine you own a **petrol car** that you drive daily to college:

- The engine runs well for an average of **500 hours** between two consecutive breakdowns → this is your **MTBF**.
- Each time it breaks down, the local mechanic takes on average **4 hours** to fix it → this is your **MTTR**.

Now ask yourself: *"Out of every 504 hours, how many hours is the car actually available to drive?"*

The answer is:

$$A_i = \frac{500}{500 + 4} = \frac{500}{504} = 0.9921 = 99.21\%$$

So, **99.21% of the time** your car is usable. That is your *Inherent Availability* — the *best-case* scenario when you assume the mechanic is *always on standby* and *spares are always on the shelf*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU Exams:** Inherent Availability represents the **upper bound** of a system's availability, set entirely by the designer's choices of component quality (reliability) and ease of repair (maintainability). It is the metric engineers use to evaluate the **inherent design** of the equipment — not how it operates in the messy real world.

## 1.3 Why "Inherent"?

The word **"inherent"** means *"built-in"* or *"intrinsic to the design."* It reflects the designer's responsibility — a well-designed product with high MTBF and low MTTR **inherently** has high availability, regardless of how the user maintains it in the field.

## 1.4 The Three-Tier Availability Hierarchy (KTU High-Yield Concept)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examiners frequently ask students to **rank** or **differentiate** the three availability metrics. Memorize the inequality:

$$A_i \geq A_a \geq A_o$$

| Tier | Name | Considers | Excludes |
|:----:|:-----|:----------|:---------|
| **1 (Highest)** | **Inherent Availability ($A_i$)** | MTBF, MTTR (corrective) | PM, logistics, admin |
| **2 (Middle)** | **Achieved Availability ($A_a$)** | MTBF, MTTR, MTTPM (preventive maintenance) | Logistics, admin |
| **3 (Lowest)** | **Operational Availability ($A_o$)** | All downtime (corrective + PM + waiting + admin) | Nothing — real-world |

This hierarchy exists because **each successive metric includes more sources of downtime**, thus producing a smaller (more realistic) availability value.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — Derivation Logic Step-by-Step

The concept of Inherent Availability rests on three foundational pillars:

1. **Reliability Pillar** — Captured by **MTBF (Mean Time Between Failures)**, which characterizes how *rarely* the system fails.
2. **Maintainability Pillar** — Captured by **MTTR (Mean Time To Repair)**, which characterizes how *quickly* the system is restored after failure.
3. **Steady-State Assumption** — The system operates long enough that initial transients die out, and the long-run fraction of time it is "up" stabilizes.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Steady-State" Interpretation:** In the long run, an exponential system cycles between UP and DOWN with average lengths MTBF and MTTR respectively. The fraction of time spent UP is therefore $\frac{MTBF}{MTBF+MTTR}$. This is the *theoretical basis* of the formula and is what students must write in derivations.

## 2.2 Assumptions Underlying Inherent Availability

For the formula to hold valid, the following must be true (this is a **favourite KTU question**):

- The system has an **exponential time-to-failure distribution** (constant failure rate $\lambda$).
- Repair times follow a **general distribution** (only the *mean* MTTR is used).
- The system is **renewing** — after each repair it is "as good as new."
- The system is observed for a **long time** (steady-state regime).
- **No preventive maintenance** is performed during the observation window.
- **Logistic and administrative delays are zero** (ideal support environment).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Boundary Behavior |
|:-:|:--------|:------------|:------------------|
| 1 | $A_i = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$ | Primary Inherent Availability formula | $0 \leq A_i \leq 1$ |
| 2 | $A_i = \dfrac{1}{1 + \frac{MTTR}{MTBF}}$ | Rearranged form emphasizing ratio | If MTTR $\to 0$, $A_i \to 1$ |
| 3 | $A_i = 1 - \dfrac{MTTR}{MTBF + MTTR}$ | Unavailability complement | $U_i = 1 - A_i$ |
| 4 | $U_i = \dfrac{MTTR}{MTBF + MTTR}$ | Inherent Unavailability | $0 \leq U_i \leq 1$ |
| 5 | $A_i = \dfrac{\mu}{\lambda + \mu}$ | Alternate form using rates | $\lambda$ = failure rate, $\mu$ = repair rate |
| 6 | $\lambda = \dfrac{1}{MTBF}$ | Failure rate from MTBF | Units: $\text{failures/hour}$ |
| 7 | $\mu = \dfrac{1}{MTTR}$ | Repair rate from MTTR | Units: $\text{repairs/hour}$ |
| 8 | $A_i + U_i = 1$ | Complementarity law | Always true |
| 9 | $A_i \geq A_a \geq A_o$ | Availability hierarchy | KTU favourite ranking question |

> [!WARNING]
> **Table Notation Convention:** When writing absolute values or conditionals inline within a sentence, use $\vert x \vert$ (LaTeX vertical bar) — **never** a raw keyboard `|` pipe, as this breaks markdown table rendering.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Engineering Domain | Application of $A_i$ |
|:-------------------|:--------------------|
| **Aerospace** | Predicting in-flight mission capability of avionics suites |
| **Nuclear Power** | Demonstrating safety-system readiness to regulators (AERB) |
| **Telecommunications** | SLA design for 99.999% ("five-nines") backbone networks |
| **Defence / Military** | MIL-HDBK-217 reliability specifications use $A_i$ as design target |
| **Manufacturing** | Benchmarking CNC machine tool uptime for Industry 4.0 |
| **Medical Devices** | FDA submission for life-support equipment like ventilators |

## 2.5 Decision-Making Insight for Designers

> [!NOTE]
> **A 50% reduction in MTTR gives a larger availability boost than a 50% increase in MTBF** — *because MTTR is typically orders of magnitude smaller than MTBF*, so its relative weight in the denominator is huge. Designers should target **ease of diagnosis and modular replacement** before chasing exotic, ultra-reliable components.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: From First Principles (Up-Down Cycle Method)

**Goal:** Derive $A_i = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$ from elementary reasoning.

### Step 1 — Define the Observation Window

Let the system be observed over a long total time $T$ during which exactly $N$ failures occur (where $N$ is large).

### Step 2 — Express Total Uptime

Each of the $N$ operating periods has an average duration equal to **MTBF**. So the total operational (up) time is:

$$T_{up} = N \times MTBF$$

### Step 3 — Express Total Downtime

Each of the $N$ repair intervals has an average duration equal to **MTTR**. So the total corrective downtime is:

$$T_{down} = N \times MTTR$$

### Step 4 — Express Total Mission Time

$$T = T_{up} + T_{down} = N \cdot MTBF + N \cdot MTTR$$

Factoring out $N$:

$$T = N \times (MTBF + MTTR)$$

### Step 5 — Apply the Definition of Availability

Availability is the fraction of time the system is *up*:

$$A_i = \frac{T_{up}}{T} = \frac{N \times MTBF}{N \times (MTBF + MTTR)}$$

### Step 6 — Cancel the Common Factor

The integer $N$ cancels out (this is why the formula is independent of how long you observe):

$$A_i = \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$$

$$\blacksquare$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2: From the Rate-Parameter Form

This alternate derivation is required when the problem gives you **failure rate** $\lambda$ and **repair rate** $\mu$ instead of MTBF/MTTR.

### Step 1 — Identify the Reciprocal Relationships

By definition:

$$MTBF = \frac{1}{\lambda} \quad \text{and} \quad MTTR = \frac{1}{\mu}$$

### Step 2 — Substitute into the Primary Formula

$$A_i = \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR} = \frac{\frac{1}{\lambda}}{\frac{1}{\lambda} + \frac{1}{\mu}}$$

### Step 3 — Multiply Numerator and Denominator by $\lambda \mu$

$$A_i = \frac{\frac{1}{\lambda} \cdot \lambda \mu}{\left(\frac{1}{\lambda} + \frac{1}{\mu}\right) \cdot \lambda \mu} = \frac{\mu}{\mu + \lambda}$$

### Step 4 — Write the Final Rate Form

$$A_i = \frac{\mu}{\lambda + \mu}$$

This is symmetrically beautiful: **availability equals the repair rate divided by the sum of failure and repair rates.**

$$\blacksquare$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Limit Case Behavior (Sanity Check)

A common KTU sub-question is: *"What happens to $A_i$ when MTTR approaches 0? When MTBF approaches 0?"*

### Case A — Instantaneous Repair ($MTTR \to 0$)

$$A_i = \lim_{MTTR \to 0} \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR} = \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + 0} = 1$$

**Interpretation:** Perfect availability — the system is never down.

### Case B — Infinitely Frequent Failure ($MTBF \to 0$)

$$A_i = \lim_{MTBF \to 0} \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR} = \frac{0}{0 + MTTR} = 0$$

**Interpretation:** Zero availability — the system is always failing.

### Case C — Equal MTBF and MTTR

$$A_i = \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTBF} = \frac{1}{2} = 0.5$$

**Interpretation:** The system is up exactly half the time — *fair coin flip*.

## 3.4 Numerical Worked Example (KTU 14-Mark Style)

**Problem Statement:** A CNC milling machine in a production line has an MTBF of 800 hours and an MTTR of 10 hours. Compute (a) the inherent availability, (b) the inherent unavailability, and (c) the expected downtime per 1000-hour operating quarter.

### Part (a) — Inherent Availability

$$A_i = \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR} = \frac{800}{800 + 10} = \frac{800}{810}$$

$$A_i = 0.9877 = 98.77\%$$

### Part (b) — Inherent Unavailability

$$U_i = 1 - A_i = 1 - 0.9877 = 0.0123 = 1.23\%$$

### Part (c) — Expected Downtime per 1000 Hours

$$\text{Downtime} = U_i \times T_{quarter} = 0.0123 \times 1000 = 12.3 \text{ hours}$$

This means the machine will be under repair for roughly **12.3 hours** in every 1000-hour production window.

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Scenario | MTBF (hrs) | MTTR (hrs) | $A_i$ | Interpretation |
|:---------|:----------:|:----------:|:-----:|:---------------|
| Excellent design | 9000 | 1 | 0.99989 | "Five-nines" telecom-grade |
| Well-designed | 800 | 10 | 0.9877 | Industrial CNC (above) |
| Average system | 500 | 50 | 0.9091 | Consumer electronics |
| Poor design | 100 | 100 | 0.5000 | Coin-flip reliability |
| Terrible design | 10 | 990 | 0.0100 | Almost always broken |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Inherent Availability Computation

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **logical data flow** of computing Inherent Availability from raw operational data, showing how the design-level inputs (failure and repair events) are aggregated into the final metric.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Operational System in Service] --> B[Failure Event Log]
    A --> C[Repair Completion Log]
    B --> D[Sum of All Operating Times]
    C --> E[Sum of All Downtimes]
    D --> F[MTBF Computation Mean Time Between Failures]
    E --> G[MTTR Computation Mean Time To Repair]
    F --> H[Inherent Availability Formula Numerator]
    G --> I[Inherent Availability Formula Denominator]
    H --> J[Inherent Availability Ai = MTBF / MTBF + MTTR]
    I --> J
    J --> K{Exclude PM Time?}
    K -->|Yes Inherent| L[Ai Reported as Design Benchmark]
    K -->|No Include PM| M[Achieved Availability Aa]
    M --> N{Include Logistics and Admin?}
    N -->|Yes Operational| O[Ao Reported as Field Reality]
    N -->|No Stop| P[Aa Reported]
    L --> Q[Final Ranking Ai greater than Aa greater than Ao]
    P --> Q
    O --> Q
```

## 4.2 Steady-State Up-Down Cycle Diagram

The following diagram visualizes the **renewal process** that mathematically underlies the formula — a system oscillating between UP states of average length MTBF and DOWN (repair) states of average length MTTR.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[System UP Operational] -->|Failure Occurs| S2[System DOWN Under Repair]
    S2 -->|Repair Complete| S1
    S1 -.Average Duration.-> M1[MTBF Hours]
    S2 -.Average Duration.-> M2[MTTR Hours]
    M1 --> R[Ai = MTBF / MTBF + MTTR]
    M2 --> R
```

## 4.3 Three-Tier Availability Hierarchy Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    TOP[System Availability Universe]
    TOP --> L1[Inherent Availability Ai]
    TOP --> L2[Achieved Availability Aa]
    TOP --> L3[Operational Availability Ao]
    L1 --> D1[Inputs: MTBF and MTTR Only]
    L1 --> D2[Excludes: PM Logistics Admin]
    L2 --> D3[Inputs: MTBF MTTR MTTPM]
    L2 --> D4[Excludes: Logistics and Admin]
    L3 --> D5[Inputs: All Downtime Sources]
    L3 --> D6[Includes: Real Field Conditions]
    D1 --> RANK[Highest Ai greater than Aa greater than Ao]
    D3 --> RANK
    D5 --> RANK
```

## 4.4 Design Lever Decision Tree

> [!NOTE]
> This diagram shows the **decision logic** a reliability engineer follows when choosing between improving reliability (MTBF) versus improving maintainability (MTTR) to boost $A_i$.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q[Need to Increase Ai] --> R{Current MTTR and MTBF Ratio?}
    R -->|MTTR much less than MTBF| S1[Target MTTR Reduction First]
    R -->|MTBF and MTTR Comparable| S2[Target Both Levers Equally]
    R -->|MTBF much less than MTTR| S3[Target MTBF Increase via Redundancy]
    S1 --> T1[Modular Design Hot Swaps Better Diagnostics]
    S2 --> T2[Balanced Design Refresh Program]
    S3 --> T3[Component Upgrades Parallel Channels]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

> **Q:** Define *Inherent Availability*. State its mathematical formula and explain why it is called "inherent."

**Model Answer (3 Marks Breakdown):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** Inherent Availability is a steady-state availability measure that considers *only* the corrective maintenance (repair) downtime of a system, computed using the Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF) and the Mean Time To Repair (MTTR). It is the fraction of time, in the long run, that a system is operational under the assumption of an *ideal support environment* (no preventive maintenance, no logistics delays, no administrative delays).

**Formula (1 mark):**

$$A_i = \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$$

**Why "Inherent" (0.5 mark):** The term *inherent* signifies that this availability is *built into* the design of the equipment — it reflects the designer's intrinsic choices of component reliability and ease of repair, and is therefore independent of field support conditions.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

> **Q:** Differentiate between *Inherent Availability* and *Operational Availability*. Why is $A_i$ always greater than or equal to $A_o$?

**Model Answer (3 Marks Breakdown):**

| Aspect | Inherent Availability $A_i$ | Operational Availability $A_o$ |
|:-------|:---------------------------|:------------------------------|
| **Downtime considered** | Corrective maintenance only | All real-world downtime (corrective + PM + logistics + admin) |
| **Support environment** | Ideal | Realistic / field conditions |
| **Use** | Design benchmark | Field performance metric |

**Inequality Justification (1.5 marks):** Since the denominator of $A_o$ is *larger* (it includes strictly more sources of downtime) than the denominator of $A_i$, while the numerator (MTBF) remains the same, it follows that:

$$A_i \geq A_a \geq A_o$$

Equality holds only in the unrealistic case where preventive maintenance, logistics, and administrative times are all zero.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

### Choice A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]`

> **Q (a)** A radar transmitter on a naval vessel has an MTBF of 1200 hours and an MTTR of 6 hours. Compute (i) Inherent Availability, (ii) Inherent Unavailability, and (iii) the expected number of failures in a 6-month deployment of 4000 hours. **(7 marks)**
>
> **Q (b)** With the help of a suitable expression, prove that $A_i = \dfrac{\mu}{\lambda + \mu}$. State and justify the limit cases $MTTR \to 0$ and $MTBF \to 0$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) **(7 marks)**

**Given:** MTBF $= 1200$ hours, MTTR $= 6$ hours, $T = 4000$ hours.

**(i) Inherent Availability (3 marks):**

$$A_i = \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR} = \frac{1200}{1200 + 6} = \frac{1200}{1206}$$

$$A_i = 0.9950 = 99.50\%$$

**(ii) Inherent Unavailability (2 marks):**

$$U_i = 1 - A_i = 1 - 0.9950 = 0.0050 = 0.50\%$$

**(iii) Expected Number of Failures (2 marks):**

The mean number of failures in time $T$ is:

$$E[N] = \frac{T}{MTBF} = \frac{4000}{1200} = 3.333 \text{ failures}$$

So approximately **3 to 4 failures** are expected in the 6-month deployment, with a total expected downtime of:

$$T_{down} = E[N] \times MTTR = 3.333 \times 6 = 20 \text{ hours}$$

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) **(7 marks)**

**Proof that $A_i = \dfrac{\mu}{\lambda + \mu}$ (4 marks):**

Starting from the primary formula:

$$A_i = \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$$

Substituting $MTBF = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}$ and $MTTR = \dfrac{1}{\mu}$:

$$A_i = \frac{\frac{1}{\lambda}}{\frac{1}{\lambda} + \frac{1}{\mu}}$$

Multiplying numerator and denominator by $\lambda \mu$:

$$A_i = \frac{\mu}{\mu + \lambda} \qquad \blacksquare$$

**Limit Case 1 — $MTTR \to 0$ (1.5 marks):**

$$A_i = \lim_{MTTR \to 0} \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR} = \frac{MTBF}{MTBF} = 1$$

**Justification:** If repair is instantaneous, the system is never down, achieving *perfect availability*.

**Limit Case 2 — $MTBF \to 0$ (1.5 marks):**

$$A_i = \lim_{MTBF \to 0} \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR} = \frac{0}{MTTR} = 0$$

**Justification:** If the system fails instantly after every repair, it is never operational, achieving *zero availability*.

---

### Choice B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

> **Q (a)** A server farm's cooling pump has a constant failure rate of $\lambda = 0.0005$ failures per hour and a mean repair time of MTTR $= 8$ hours. Determine (i) the Inherent Availability, (ii) the MTBF, and (iii) the total expected downtime in a 30-day month (720 hours). **(7 marks)**
>
> **Q (b)** Explain the three-tier availability hierarchy: $A_i$, $A_a$, and $A_o$. For each, identify the downtime components included and excluded, and justify why $A_i \geq A_a \geq A_o$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) **(7 marks)**

**Given:** $\lambda = 0.0005$ per hour, MTTR $= 8$ hours, $T = 720$ hours.

**(i) Compute MTBF (1 mark):**

$$MTBF = \frac{1}{\lambda} = \frac{1}{0.0005} = 2000 \text{ hours}$$

**(ii) Compute Inherent Availability (3 marks):**

$$A_i = \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR} = \frac{2000}{2000 + 8} = \frac{2000}{2008}$$

$$A_i = 0.9960 = 99.60\%$$

**Alternate verification using rates (1 mark):**

$$A_i = \frac{\mu}{\lambda + \mu} = \frac{0.125}{0.0005 + 0.125} = \frac{0.125}{0.1255} = 0.9960 \checkmark$$

where $\mu = \dfrac{1}{MTTR} = \dfrac{1}{8} = 0.125$ per hour.

**(iii) Expected Downtime (2 marks):**

$$U_i = 1 - A_i = 0.0040$$

$$\text{Downtime} = U_i \times T = 0.0040 \times 720 = 2.88 \text{ hours}$$

So in a 30-day month, the pump is expected to be down for approximately **2.88 hours**.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) **(7 marks)**

**Three-Tier Hierarchy Explanation:**

**1. Inherent Availability $A_i$ (2.5 marks):**
- **Includes:** Corrective maintenance (repair) downtime only.
- **Excludes:** Preventive maintenance (PM), Mean Logistic Delay Time (MLDT), Mean Administrative Delay Time (MADT).
- **Formula:** $A_i = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$
- **Use:** Design benchmark; quantifies how well the equipment is *engineered*.

**2. Achieved Availability $A_a$ (2.5 marks):**
- **Includes:** Corrective + preventive maintenance downtime.
- **Excludes:** Logistic and administrative delays.
- **Formula:** $A_a = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR + MTTPM}$, where MTTPM = Mean Time To Perform Preventive Maintenance.
- **Use:** Intermediate metric used by maintenance crews.

**3. Operational Availability $A_o$ (1 mark):**
- **Includes:** *All* sources of downtime — corrective, preventive, logistics, administrative.
- **Use:** Realistic field performance; used for SLA reporting.

**Justification of Inequality (1 mark):** The denominators strictly increase in size from $A_i$ to $A_a$ to $A_o$ (more downtime terms added), while the numerator (MTBF) remains the same. Therefore:

$$A_i \geq A_a \geq A_o$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing the three availability metrics** — Examiners frequently award only 1 of 3 marks if you mix up MTTPM, MLDT, and MADT inclusions. *Memorize the table in Section 2.4 cold.*
> 2. **Forgetting units** in numerical answers — Always write "hours" or "failures/hour" explicitly.
> 3. **Skipping the steady-state assumption** in derivations — Examiners deduct 1 mark if you don't mention that the formula is valid only in the *long-run* limit.
> 4. **Writing $|$ for absolute value inside tables** — Use $\vert \cdot \vert$ LaTeX to avoid formatting collapse.
> 5. **Omitting the limit case verification** — Many students derive the rate form but forget the boundary analysis, losing 1.5 marks.
> 6. **Wrong inequality direction** — Some students write $A_o \geq A_i$. Always remember: *more downtime in denominator = smaller availability.*

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Definition:** $A_i$ = steady-state availability under *ideal support* (corrective maintenance only).
- [x] **Primary Formula:** $A_i = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$ — the most-tested expression.
- [x] **Rate Form:** $A_i = \dfrac{\mu}{\lambda + \mu}$ — required when problem gives $\lambda$, $\mu$ directly.
- [x] **Unavailability:** $U_i = 1 - A_i = \dfrac{MTTR}{MTBF + MTTR}$.
- [x] **Hierarchy Rule:** $A_i \geq A_a \geq A_o$ — memorize, do not derive on exam day.
- [x] **Boundaries:** $A_i \to 1$ as $MTTR \to 0$; $A_i \to 0$ as $MTBF \to 0$.
- [x] **Assumptions:** Exponential TTF, renewing system, long-run steady state, no PM, no delays.
- [x] **Design Insight:** Reducing MTTR gives a *bigger* availability boost than increasing MTBF (when MTTR $\ll$ MTBF).
- [x] **Expected Downtime:** $E[\text{Downtime}] = U_i \times T$ — a favourite 2-mark sub-question.
- [x] **Expected Failures:** $E[N] = T / MTBF$ — pairs with downtime computation.
- [x] **KTU Tag Words to Recognize in Questions:** *"inherent," "ideal support," "design benchmark," "excluding preventive maintenance"* — all map to $A_i$.
- [x] **Don't Confuse With:** Achieved Availability ($A_a$, includes PM) and Operational Availability ($A_o$, includes everything).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Achieved Availability
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET632 - RELIABILITY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Achieved Availability

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme Terminology)

**Achieved Availability (A_a)** is a fundamental performance metric in reliability engineering that quantifies the *actual* long-term fraction of time that a repairable system is in an operable state under real-world operating conditions. It is formally defined as the ratio of the system's **Mean Up Time (MUT)** to the sum of its Mean Up Time and **Mean Down Time (MDT)**.

Mathematically, it is expressed as:

$$A_a = \frac{\text{MUT}}{\text{MUT} + \text{MDT}} = \frac{\text{Uptime}}{\text{Uptime} + \text{Downtime}}$$

Unlike **Inherent Availability (A_i)**, which only considers *corrective* maintenance time (MTTR), **Achieved Availability** provides a more pessimistic and realistic figure because it includes the total downtime experienced in actual field operations. This downtime encompasses:

- **Active Repair Time (MTTR):** Time spent actively diagnosing and repairing the failed component.
- **Logistics Delay Time (MLDT):** Waiting time for spare parts, technician arrival, or administrative paperwork.
- **Preventive Maintenance Time (MPMT):** Time consumed by scheduled, routine maintenance activities (overhauls, inspections, calibrations).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Distinction:** Students must clearly differentiate three availability definitions, as they appear frequently in Part A (3-mark) and Part B (14-mark) questions:
> 1. **Inherent Availability (A_i)** — Bench-level / design performance (MTBF + MTTR only).
> 2. **Achieved Availability (A_a)** — Real-world steady-state performance including logistics and preventive maintenance.
> 3. **Operational Availability (A_o)** — Sometimes used interchangeably with A_a, but strictly includes ALL downtime (including administrative/idle delays).

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Real-World Engineering Perspective)

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Hospital Emergency Room" Analogy:**
> Imagine an ambulance service in a city. The **inherent availability** would describe the time the ambulance is *theoretically* functional assuming a mechanic is standing right next to it the moment it breaks down. However, in real life, when an ambulance breaks down on the highway:
> - A tow truck must be called (Logistics Delay).
> - The right spare part must be ordered and shipped (Logistics Delay).
> - The ambulance must undergo scheduled oil changes and brake checks every 10,000 km (Preventive Maintenance).
>
> **Achieved Availability** captures *all* of these real-world downtimes, giving a true picture of how often the ambulance is *actually* available to respond to emergencies. The lower the A_a, the more often the city is without ambulance coverage — a critical metric for a life-safety system.

**Physical / Operational Constants Highlighted:**
- The metric is **dimensionless** (a pure ratio between 0 and 1).
- It is often expressed as a **percentage** (0% to 100%) in industry reports.
- A value of **0.9999 ("Four Nines")** represents only ~52.6 minutes of annual downtime, which is a high-reliability industrial benchmark.

## 1.3 Why Achieved Availability Matters in KTU Examinations

Achieved Availability is critical in **industrial engineering, power systems, aerospace, and manufacturing** because it is the metric that reflects what a customer or end-user *actually experiences*. When designing a new system, engineers strive to maximize A_i (design phase), but maintenance managers work to maximize A_a (operational phase) by reducing logistics and preventive maintenance overheads.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Underlying Logic of Achieved Availability

The derivation of Achieved Availability follows a strict **operational timeline logic**. Consider a repairable system observed over a long operational period $T$:

**Step 1 — Decompose the Total Time:**
The observation window is partitioned into mutually exclusive intervals:

$$T = T_{\text{up}} + T_{\text{down}}$$

**Step 2 — Decompose the Downtime:**
The downtime itself is composed of three practical sub-phases:

$$T_{\text{down}} = T_{\text{repair}} + T_{\text{logistics}} + T_{\text{preventive}}$$

**Step 3 — Average over Many Cycles:**
For $N$ operational cycles (failure-to-failure or scheduled maintenance cycles), we take the expected value:

$$E[T_{\text{up}}] = \text{MUT}, \quad E[T_{\text{repair}}] = \text{MTTR}, \quad E[T_{\text{logistics}}] = \text{MLDT}, \quad E[T_{\text{preventive}}] = \text{MPMT}$$

**Step 4 — Assemble the Ratio:**
The long-run fraction of time the system is operational is the ratio of expected up time to total expected cycle time.

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind the Formula

The reason Achieved Availability is lower than Inherent Availability is intuitively obvious: it adds two real-world penalties:

- **Logistics Delay (MLDT):** The system is *failed* but is *not* being repaired because it is waiting for a part or a technician. From the user's perspective, this is downtime.
- **Preventive Maintenance (MPMT):** The system is perfectly functional, but is *intentionally* taken offline for servicing. From the user's perspective, this is also downtime.

Both are "invisible" in a pure lab measurement (which gives A_i), but both are brutally real on the factory floor (which gives A_a).

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations for KTU 2024 Scheme examinations. Each formula maps directly to a potential 3-mark or 7-mark sub-question.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units / Boundary |
|---|---------|-------------|------------------|
| 1 | $A_a = \dfrac{\text{MUT}}{\text{MUT} + \text{MDT}}$ | Master definition of Achieved Availability | Dimensionless, $0 \le A_a \le 1$ |
| 2 | $A_a = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR} + \text{MLDT} + \text{MPMT}}$ | Engineering form using MTBF | Used when MUT $\approx$ MTBF |
| 3 | $A_i = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$ | Inherent Availability (for comparison) | Always $A_i \ge A_a$ |
| 4 | $\text{MDT} = \text{MTTR} + \text{MLDT} + \text{MPMT}$ | Mean Down Time decomposition | Hours or days |
| 5 | $A_a = 1 - \dfrac{\text{MDT}}{\text{MTBC}}$ | Alternative form where MTBC = mean time between cycles | Useful for scheduled systems |
| 6 | $A_a = 1 - f_{\text{down}}$ | Where $f_{\text{down}}$ is fractional downtime | Used in reliability block diagrams |
| 7 | $\text{U}(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ | Exponential reliability (links MTBF to failure rate) | $\text{MTBF} = 1/\lambda$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL Mermaid Table Note:** The vertical bar `$\vert$` has been replaced with `\mid` or `\dfrac` to avoid breaking markdown table rendering, as per KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 protocol.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Achieved Availability is the **lingua franca** of the following domains:

- **Aerospace Maintenance:** A commercial airliner might have A_i = 0.99999, but an A_a of 0.95 due to mandatory daily inspections (MPMT). The airline schedules routes based on A_a, not A_i.
- **Power Grid Engineering:** Grid operators use A_a to plan reserve margins — a transformer with low A_a forces the utility to keep expensive backup transformers spinning.
- **Manufacturing (OEE):** Achieved Availability is one of the three pillars of **Overall Equipment Effectiveness (OEE)**, alongside Performance and Quality.
- **Defense Systems:** Military readiness reports (D-RAM) are based on A_a, because a tank in the motor pool is "unavailable" regardless of why.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Formal Derivation of Achieved Availability

We start from first principles. Consider a system observed for a long period $T$ undergoing $N$ operational cycles (each cycle ends with a restoration event: either a failure repair or a scheduled PM).

**Step 1 — Total Observation Window:**
The total time $T$ is the sum of up times and down times for all $N$ cycles:

$$T = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \text{U}_i + \sum_{i=1}^{N} \text{D}_i$$

**Step 2 — Expected (Mean) Values:**
For large $N$, by the **Law of Large Numbers**, the sample means converge to expectations:

$$\text{MUT} = E[\text{U}_i] = \lim_{N \to \infty} \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} \text{U}_i$$

$$\text{MDT} = E[\text{D}_i] = \lim_{N \to \infty} \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} \text{D}_i$$

**Step 3 — Downtime Decomposition (Engineering Definition):**
For each cycle, the downtime consists of three independent phases:

$$\text{D}_i = R_i + L_i + P_i$$

Where $R_i$ is repair time, $L_i$ is logistics delay, and $P_i$ is preventive maintenance time. By linearity of expectation:

$$\text{MDT} = E[\text{D}_i] = E[R_i] + E[L_i] + E[P_i] = \text{MTTR} + \text{MLDT} + \text{MPMT}$$

**Step 4 — Limit Definition of Availability:**
Availability is defined as the long-run fraction of time the system is up:

$$A_a = \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{\text{Total Uptime}}{T} = \lim_{N \to \infty} \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} \text{U}_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{N} \text{U}_i + \sum_{i=1}^{N} \text{D}_i}$$

**Step 5 — Divide Numerator and Denominator by N:**

$$A_a = \lim_{N \to \infty} \frac{\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} \text{U}_i}{\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} \text{U}_i + \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} \text{D}_i}$$

**Step 6 — Substitute the Mean Values:**

$$\boxed{A_a = \frac{\text{MUT}}{\text{MUT} + \text{MDT}}}$$

**Step 7 — Apply the Downtime Decomposition:**
Recognizing that MUT is well-approximated by MTBF for systems with negligible standby time, we get the engineering form:

$$A_a = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR} + \text{MLDT} + \text{MPMT}}$$

## 3.2 KTU Analytical Worked Example (Board Exam Style)

> **Problem:** A CNC machining center in a production line has the following observed data over 6 months (4380 hours):
> - Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF) = 500 hours
> - Mean Time To Repair (MTTR) = 4 hours
> - Mean Logistics Delay Time (MLDT) = 6 hours
> - Mean Preventive Maintenance Time (MPMT) = 2 hours
>
> **Calculate:**
> (a) Inherent Availability $A_i$
> (b) Achieved Availability $A_a$
> (c) The annual downtime hours lost to logistics and preventive maintenance
> (d) Comment on the operational significance.

### Solution:

**(a) Inherent Availability:**

$$A_i = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}} = \frac{500}{500 + 4} = \frac{500}{504}$$

$$A_i = 0.99206 \approx 99.21\%$$

**(b) Achieved Availability:**

$$A_a = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR} + \text{MLDT} + \text{MPMT}}$$

$$A_a = \frac{500}{500 + 4 + 6 + 2} = \frac{500}{512}$$

$$A_a = 0.97656 \approx 97.66\%$$

**(c) Annual Downtime Due to Non-Repair Delays:**

First, compute total annual downtime:

$$T_{\text{down, annual}} = 8760 \times (1 - A_a) = 8760 \times (1 - 0.97656)$$

$$T_{\text{down, annual}} = 8760 \times 0.02344 = 205.3 \text{ hours/year}$$

The fraction of this downtime that is NOT active repair is:

$$f_{\text{non-repair}} = \frac{\text{MLDT} + \text{MPMT}}{\text{MTTR} + \text{MLDT} + \text{MPMT}} = \frac{6 + 2}{4 + 6 + 2} = \frac{8}{12} = 0.6667$$

Therefore, downtime due to logistics + PM:

$$T_{\text{non-repair}} = 205.3 \times 0.6667 \approx 136.9 \text{ hours/year}$$

**(d) Operational Significance:**
- The drop from **99.21%** (A_i, design) to **97.66%** (A_a, real) is **1.55 percentage points**, which translates to **~137 hours/year** of avoidable downtime.
- **67% of the total downtime is NOT due to the machine itself failing**, but due to organizational inefficiencies (spares, scheduling).
- **Recommendation:** Invest in faster logistics (on-site spares inventory) and reduce PM frequency, as these are *management levers* that do not require redesigning the machine.

## 3.3 Comparative Analytical Matrix

| Metric | Includes MTTR? | Includes MLDT? | Includes MPMT? | Use Case |
|--------|:---:|:---:|:---:|----------|
| **Inherent Availability (A_i)** | Yes | No | No | Design specification, OEM contracts |
| **Achieved Availability (A_a)** | Yes | Yes | Yes | Maintenance management, real-world reporting |
| **Operational Availability (A_o)** | Yes | Yes | Yes (plus admin) | Military readiness, mission-critical systems |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Achieved Availability

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates how the three downtime components combine to yield the achieved availability, and the comparative relationships among the availability metrics.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[System Observation Window T] --> nodeB{Total Cycle Time}
    nodeB --> nodeC[Uptime Phase]
    nodeB --> nodeD[Downtime Phase]
    
    nodeC --> nodeE[Mean Up Time MUT approx MTBF]
    nodeE --> nodeE1[Includes Operational Performance]
    
    nodeD --> nodeF[Active Repair MTTR]
    nodeD --> nodeG[Logistics Delay MLDT]
    nodeD --> nodeH[Preventive Maintenance MPMT]
    
    nodeF --> nodeF1[Technician on-site repair]
    nodeG --> nodeG1[Spare parts waiting]
    nodeG --> nodeG2[Travel time of crew]
    nodeH --> nodeH1[Scheduled overhauls]
    nodeH --> nodeH2[Calibration and inspection]
    
    nodeE --> nodeI[Achieved Availability Aa]
    nodeF --> nodeI
    nodeG --> nodeI
    nodeH --> nodeI
    
    nodeI --> nodeJ[Used for Maintenance KPIs and OEE]
    
    nodeK[Inherent Availability Ai] --> nodeK1[Design only MTBF plus MTTR]
    nodeI --> nodeL[Comparative Analysis]
    nodeK --> nodeL
    nodeL --> nodeM[Performance Gap Delta Aa to Ai]
```

## 4.2 Timeline Schematic of a Single Operational Cycle

```mermaid
gantt
    title Single Operational Cycle of a Repairable System
    dateFormat  X
    axisFormat  %S
    
    section Uptime
    Operational Mission (MUT)    :active, t1, 0, 500
    
    section Downtime
    Active Repair (MTTR)         :crit, t2, 500, 4
    Logistics Delay (MLDT)       :crit, t3, 504, 6
    Preventive Maintenance (MPMT):crit, t4, 510, 2
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- The system is **operational** for **500 hours** (the MUT/MTBF).
- A failure occurs, triggering a **4-hour repair**.
- After repair, before the *next* failure cycle begins, there is a **6-hour logistics delay** and a **2-hour preventive maintenance window**.
- The Achieved Availability for this single cycle is:

$$A_a = \frac{500}{500 + 4 + 6 + 2} = \frac{500}{512} = 97.66\%$$

## 4.3 Comparative Hierarchy of Availability Metrics

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA[Availability Family] --> nodeB[Inherent Ai]
    nodeA --> nodeC[Achieved Aa]
    nodeA --> nodeD[Operational Ao]
    
    nodeB --> nodeB1[Strictest Lab Measure]
    nodeC --> nodeC1[Realistic Field Measure]
    nodeD --> nodeD1[Most Conservative Measure]
    
    nodeB1 --> nodeB2[MTBF over MTBF plus MTTR]
    nodeC1 --> nodeC2[MTBF over MTBF plus MTTR plus MLDT plus MPMT]
    nodeD1 --> nodeD2[Aa plus administrative idle time]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1:** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define Achieved Availability. How does it differ from Inherent Availability?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1.5 marks):** Achieved Availability ($A_a$) is the long-run fraction of time a repairable system is in an operable state under real-world operating conditions, considering active repair, logistics delay, and preventive maintenance.
  $$A_a = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR} + \text{MLDT} + \text{MPMT}}$$
- **Difference (1.5 marks):** Inherent Availability ($A_i$) is a *theoretical* measure considering only corrective maintenance (MTTR) — used during design. Achieved Availability is the *actual* real-world measure including logistics and preventive maintenance, and is therefore always **lower** than $A_i$.

---

### **Question 2:** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is meant by Mean Logistics Delay Time (MLDT) and Mean Preventive Maintenance Time (MPMT) in the context of Achieved Availability?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **MLDT (1.5 marks):** The mean time the system is in a failed state but is *not* being actively repaired, due to waiting for spare parts, technician arrival, or administrative clearances.
- **MPMT (1.5 marks):** The mean time the system is intentionally taken offline for scheduled servicing — overhauls, inspections, calibrations, and routine replacements — even though the system has not failed.
- Both are added to MTTR in the denominator of $A_a$, lowering the availability value.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

> **KTU Pattern:** The ESE Part B provides a choice between **Question A** and **Question B** within the same module. Both options below are independent and complete.

---

### **OPTION A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Define the following terms with reference to repairable system availability: (7 marks)
- (i) Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF)
- (ii) Mean Time To Repair (MTTR)
- (iii) Mean Logistics Delay Time (MLDT)
- (iv) Mean Preventive Maintenance Time (MPMT)

**(b)** A diesel generator set in a hospital has the following data:
- MTBF = 800 hours
- MTTR = 6 hours
- MLDT = 14 hours
- MPMT = 4 hours

Calculate: (7 marks)
