- **Choice B (Risky):** Invest in a new design yielding ₹ 9 lakh with probability 0.5 and ₹ 1 lakh with probability 0.5.

Using the Certainty Equivalent concept, determine which choice the engineer should make. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** If the failure cost of the new design is $C_f = \text{₹}\,5{,}00{,}000$ per failure and the reliability must be improved from 0.92 to 0.97 at an additional capital cost of $C_{\text{CAP}} = \text{₹}\,8{,}00{,}000$, evaluate the investment decision using the Risk Premium. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1 — Compute the expected utility of Choice A (certain).**

$$
U_A = \ln(4.5) = 1.5041 \;\text{nats}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the expected utility of Choice B (risky).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E[U_B] &= 0.5 \cdot \ln(9) + 0.5 \cdot \ln(1) \\
&= 0.5 \times 2.1972 + 0.5 \times 0 \\
&= 1.0986 \;\text{nats}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compare.** Since $U_A = 1.5041 > E[U_B] = 1.0986$, the engineer strictly prefers the **certain** ₹ 4.5 lakh.

**Step 4 — Compute the Certainty Equivalent (CE) of Choice B for completeness.**

$$
\ln(\text{CE}) = 1.0986 \;\Rightarrow\; \text{CE} = e^{1.0986} = 3.00 \text{ lakh}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute the Risk Premium.**

$$
\text{RP} = E[X_B] - \text{CE} = (0.5 \times 9 + 0.5 \times 1) - 3.00 = 5.00 - 3.00 = \text{₹}\,2.00 \text{ lakh}
$$

**Final Answer:** The engineer should choose **Choice A** (₹ 4.5 lakh certain) because she is risk-averse; the risky alternative is worth only ₹ 3.00 lakh to her in utility terms.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 — Compute expected number of failures before and after improvement.**
For a system with $N = 100$ units (assumed):

- Before: failures $= 100 \times (1-0.92) = 8$
- After: failures $= 100 \times (1-0.97) = 3$
- Failures avoided $= 5$

**Step 2 — Expected monetary savings from improved reliability.**

$$
\Delta C_{\text{saved}} = 5 \times C_f = 5 \times 5{,}00{,}000 = \text{₹}\,25{,}00{,}000
$$

**Step 3 — Net expected benefit.**

$$
\Delta \Pi = \Delta C_{\text{saved}} - C_{\text{CAP}} = 25{,}00{,}000 - 8{,}00{,}000 = \text{₹}\,17{,}00{,}000
$$

**Step 4 — Risk Premium evaluation.**
The risk-averse engineer uses $U(x) = \ln(x)$ with current wealth (say) $W = \text{₹}\,50{,}00{,}000$. The risky decision is whether to spend ₹ 8 lakh for a chance of saving ₹ 25 lakh.

Expected utility **without** investment (assume 60% chance of success):

$$
E[U_{\text{no invest}}] = 0.6 \ln(50{,}00{,}000) + 0.4 \ln(50{,}00{,}000) = \ln(5 \times 10^{7})
$$

Expected utility **with** investment:

$$
E[U_{\text{invest}}] = 0.6 \ln(67{,}00{,}000) + 0.4 \ln(42{,}00{,}000)
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
E[U_{\text{invest}}] &= 0.6 \times \ln(6.7 \times 10^{7}) + 0.4 \times \ln(4.2 \times 10^{7}) \\
&= 0.6 \times 17.0184 + 0.4 \times 17.2527 \\
&= 10.2110 + 6.9011 = 17.1121
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
E[U_{\text{no invest}}] = \ln(5 \times 10^{7}) = 17.7275
$$

**Step 5 — Decision.** Since $E[U_{\text{no invest}}] = 17.7275 > E[U_{\text{invest}}] = 17.1121$, the **risk-averse engineer rejects the investment** despite its positive expected monetary value. The risk premium is too high.

**Final Answer:** Although the investment yields a positive expected profit of **₹ 17 lakh**, the risk-averse engineer will **reject** it; the certainty equivalent is lower than the sure ₹ 8 lakh cost.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Reliability Utility Cost Models**

- ☐ Reliability Utility Cost Models = **Economic optimisation of reliability**, not pure reliability maximisation.
- ☐ The total expected cost function is **U-shaped** in $R$; the optimal reliability $R^\*$ is at the **vertex**.
- ☐ Capital cost $C_{\text{CAP}}(R)$ **increases** with $R$; failure cost $C_{\text{F}}(1-R)$ **decreases** with $R$.
- ☐ The optimality condition is $\dfrac{dC_{\text{TE}}}{dR} = 0$ (cost) **or** $\dfrac{d\Pi}{dR} = 0$ (profit).
- ☐ For **quadratic capital cost** $C_{\text{CAP}} = \alpha R^{2}$, the closed-form optimum is $\boxed{R^\* = \dfrac{C_d}{2\alpha}}$.
- ☐ Always **verify $0 \le R^\* \le 1$**; otherwise the optimum is at the boundary $R=1$.
- ☐ **Utility theory** distinguishes *risk-neutral*, *risk-averse* and *risk-seeking* behaviour — utility is *not* equal to money.
- ☐ **Certainty Equivalent (CE)** is the guaranteed amount giving the same utility as the expected utility of the gamble.
- ☐ **Risk Premium (RP)** $= E[X] - \text{CE}$; $\text{RP}>0$ implies risk aversion.
- ☐ The Profit model uses **availability** $A = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$ as the revenue-generating fraction.
- ☐ Expected downtime cost per hour $= C_d \cdot (1-A)$.
- ☐ For series systems, **replace $R$ by $r^{n}$** before differentiating; the cost becomes a function of $r$.
- ☐ Common KTU mistake: omitting the **availability revenue term** when a system generates money during uptime.
- ☐ KTU standard: always express answers with **units** (₹/hr, ₹/cycle, dimensionless reliability).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Depreciation Cost Models
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET632 - RELIABILITY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Depreciation Cost Models

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Depreciation** is the *systematic, rational allocation of the depreciable cost (initial cost minus salvage value) of a tangible long-lived asset over its estimated useful economic life*. In the KTU 2024 Reliability Engineering framework, **Depreciation Cost Models** are the mathematical formulations that quantify the *loss in book value* of a piece of industrial equipment as a function of time, age, or usage.

These models are a critical sub-component of **Engineering Economics** as applied to **Reliability & Maintainability Engineering**. They allow an engineer to:
1. Compute the **Time-Dependent Value of Capital Equipment** at any point in its life.
2. Feed this value into **Replacement Analysis**, **Life Cycle Costing (LCC)**, and **Availability / Maintainability Optimisation**.
3. Justify **Preventive vs. Corrective Maintenance** decisions by mapping asset depreciation against **Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF)** and **System Reliability Function R(t)**.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (The "Phone Resale Value" Analogy)

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of buying a brand-new smartphone for ₹80,000.**
> The moment you unbox it, its market resale value drops by ~15% — this is **first-year depreciation (accelerated loss)**. By year three, the resale price is nearly flat because the phone has already lost most of its perceived novelty — this is the **declining-balance / asymptotic behaviour**.
> In a factory, a CNC machine costing ₹1 Crore behaves exactly the same way. The **Depreciation Cost Model** is the formula your finance team uses to draw that falling "value vs. age" curve on a chart.

### 1.3 Physical Constants, Standard Metrics & Symbols

> [!TIP]
> The following **standardised symbols** (per KTU/IEEE convention for Engineering Economics) are used throughout Module 4:

| Symbol | Meaning | Standard Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $P$ | Initial Purchase Price / First Cost | ₹ (INR) |
| $S_L$ | Salvage Value (end-of-life market value) | ₹ |
| $N$ | Useful / Economic Life | years |
| $n$ | Current age (time index) | years |
| $D_n$ | Depreciation charge in year $n$ | ₹ / year |
| $B_n$ | Book Value at end of year $n$ | ₹ |
| $i$ | Effective annual interest rate | dimensionless |
| $\alpha$ | Declining-balance depreciation rate | dimensionless |

### 1.4 Why Reliability Engineers Study Depreciation

Reliability engineering is fundamentally about *when a system fails and what it costs when it does*. Depreciation Cost Models bridge the gap between **technical failure data (R(t), λ(t))** and **financial decision-making (replacement vs. overhaul)**. Without depreciation, you cannot compute the **Equivalent Uniform Annual Cost (EUAC)** that drives the *minimum-cost life* of a system.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Taxonomy of Standard Depreciation Cost Models

For KTU 2024, four primary models must be mastered. Each is built on a distinct *asset-value trajectory* assumption:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Model 1 — Straight Line Depreciation (SLD)**
> **Model 2 — Sum-of-the-Years'-Digits (SYD)**
> **Model 3 — Declining Balance (DB) / Double Declining Balance (DDB)**
> **Model 4 — Sinking Fund Depreciation (SFD)**

---

### 2.2 Model 1 — Straight Line Depreciation (SLD)

**Assumption:** Asset loses value *uniformly* every year.

**Operational Logic:**
1. Compute *depreciable base* = $P - S_L$
2. Divide by service life $N$ → uniform annual charge.
3. Book value declines as a **straight line** from $P$ to $S_L$.

**KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)**

| Quantity | Formula | Engineering Insight |
|---|---|---|
| Annual Depreciation $D_n$ | $D_n = \dfrac{P - S_L}{N}$ | Constant for all $n$ |
| Book Value at end of year $n$ | $B_n = P - n\!\left(\dfrac{P - S_L}{N}\right)$ | Linear decay |
| Rate of return per year | $\dfrac{1}{N}$ | Inverse of life |

**Real-world utility:** Used for low-tech, slow-depreciating assets (buildings, civil works, large transformers).

---

### 2.3 Model 2 — Sum-of-the-Years'-Digits (SYD)

**Assumption:** Asset loses more value in **early years**, less later — reflects *technological obsolescence*.

**Operational Logic:**
1. Form the *year-digit sum*: $\Sigma = 1 + 2 + \dots + N = \dfrac{N(N+1)}{2}$.
2. In year $n$, the *fraction of depreciation charged* = $\dfrac{\text{Remaining life at start of } n}{\Sigma}$.
3. Multiply this fraction by $(P - S_L)$.

**KTU Formula Sheet**

| Quantity | Formula | Engineering Insight |
|---|---|---|
| Year-$n$ depreciation | $D_n = (P - S_L)\,\dfrac{2(N - n + 1)}{N(N+1)}$ | Decreasing each year |
| Fraction of base in year $n$ | $\dfrac{N - n + 1}{\Sigma}$ | Higher early on |

**Real-world utility:** Ideal for electronics, IT servers, CNC controllers — items that lose *functional* value quickly.

---

### 2.4 Model 3 — Declining Balance (DB) & Double Declining Balance (DDB)

**Assumption:** Asset loses a **fixed percentage** of its *remaining book value* every year (geometric decay). Crucially, **$S_L$ is never used in the formula** — it is implicit.

**Operational Logic:**
1. Choose a constant rate $\alpha \in (0, 1)$.
2. Each year, $B_n = (1-\alpha)\,B_{n-1}$.
3. $D_n = B_{n-1} - B_n = \alpha\,B_{n-1}$.

For **Double Declining Balance (DDB)**, $\alpha = \dfrac{2}{N}$.

**KTU Formula Sheet**

| Quantity | Formula | Engineering Insight |
|---|---|---|
| Book Value at year $n$ | $B_n = P\,(1-\alpha)^{n}$ | Geometric (asymptotic) |
| Year-$n$ depreciation | $D_n = \alpha\,P\,(1-\alpha)^{n-1}$ | Maximum in year 1 |
| DDB rate | $\alpha = \dfrac{2}{N}$ | Twice straight-line rate |

**Real-world utility:** Tax-favoured method for vehicles, machinery subject to fast technological obsolescence.

---

### 2.5 Model 4 — Sinking Fund Depreciation (SFD)

**Assumption:** Asset owner *deposits* the yearly depreciation into a fund earning compound interest $i$; at year $N$, this fund equals $(P - S_L)$.

**Operational Logic:**
1. We require $D_n\,(F/A, i, N-n+1) + \dots + D_N(1+i)^{0} = (P - S_L)$.
2. Because the fund must grow to $(P - S_L)$, $D_n$ must be a **uniform annual series**.
3. Solving gives $D_n = (P - S_L)\,\dfrac{i}{(1+i)^{N} - 1}$.

**KTU Formula Sheet**

| Quantity | Formula | Engineering Insight |
|---|---|---|
| Annual SFD charge | $D_n = (P - S_L)\,\dfrac{i}{(1+i)^{N} - 1}$ | Equal every year |
| Fund value at year $N$ | $(P - S_L)$ | Achieves replacement capital |

**Real-world utility:** Used when the firm plans to *self-finance the replacement* through a dedicated reserve fund (utilities, railways, power plants).

---

### 2.6 Comparative Snapshot — KTU High-Yield Table

> [!NOTE]
> **Comparison of Trajectories** — How each model draws the same $(P \to S_L)$ path differently:

| Model | Curve Shape | $D_1$ vs $D_N$ | Salvage Used? | Interest Used? |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **SLD** | Straight line | $D_1 = D_N$ | Yes | No |
| **SYD** | Steep then flat | $D_1 \gg D_N$ | Yes | No |
| **DB / DDB** | Geometric decay | $D_1 \gg D_N$ | Implicit | No |
| **SFD** | Straight-line *charge*, but fund grows exponentially | $D_1 = D_N$ | Yes | **Yes** |

---

### 2.7 Engineering Utility in Reliability Decisions

The depreciation charge $D_n$ is the **recurring cost of capital tied up in equipment**. When combined with:
- Maintenance cost $M(n)$ (increasing with age)
- Downtime cost $L(n)$ (rising after wear-out phase)
- **Equivalent Uniform Annual Cost (EUAC)** = $D_n + M(n) + L(n)$

…the *optimal replacement age* $N^{*}$ is the $n$ that **minimises EUAC**. This is the analytical heart of **Reliability-Centred Maintenance (RCM)**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Straight Line Depreciation (SLD)

**Given:** Asset cost $P$, salvage $S_L$, life $N$. Total depreciation to be charged over $N$ years = $P - S_L$.

**Step 1 — Define uniform annual charge:**

The total depreciable amount must be spread *equally* over $N$ years:

$$
D_n = \frac{\text{Total Depreciation}}{\text{Number of Years}} = \frac{P - S_L}{N}
$$

**Step 2 — Verify book value boundary conditions:**

At end of year 0 (just purchased): $B_0 = P$.

After year 1: $B_1 = P - D_n = P - \dfrac{P - S_L}{N}$.

**Step 3 — Generalise to end of year $n$:**

Each year removes the same amount $D_n$, so after $n$ years the cumulative deduction is $n \cdot D_n$:

$$
B_n = P - n \cdot \frac{P - S_L}{N}
$$

**Step 4 — Boundary check at $n = N$:**

Substituting $n = N$:

$$
B_N = P - N \cdot \frac{P - S_L}{N} = P - (P - S_L) = S_L
$$

✓ The formula correctly predicts $B_N = S_L$.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Sum-of-the-Years'-Digits (SYD)

**Given:** Same $P$, $S_L$, $N$. Aim: front-load depreciation.

**Step 1 — Construct the year-digit sum $\Sigma$:**

$$
\Sigma = 1 + 2 + 3 + \dots + N = \frac{N(N+1)}{2}
$$

**Step 2 — Assign weight to each year:**

In year $n$, the *remaining life* at the start of that year is $(N - n + 1)$ years. Therefore the weight is:

$$
w_n = \frac{N - n + 1}{\Sigma}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply weight to depreciable base:**

$$
D_n = w_n \cdot (P - S_L) = \frac{(N - n + 1)}{\Sigma} \cdot (P - S_L)
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $\Sigma = \dfrac{N(N+1)}{2}$ to simplify:**

$$
D_n = \frac{2(N - n + 1)}{N(N+1)} \cdot (P - S_L)
$$

**Step 5 — Verify sum equals total depreciation:**

$$
\sum_{n=1}^{N} D_n = \frac{P - S_L}{\Sigma} \sum_{n=1}^{N} (N - n + 1) = \frac{P - S_L}{\Sigma} \cdot \Sigma = P - S_L \;\checkmark
$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Declining Balance (DB)

**Given:** Asset cost $P$, constant rate $\alpha$. Salvage is *not* used.

**Step 1 — Recurrence relation:**

Each year, the book value is reduced by the *same fraction* $\alpha$ of its previous value:

$$
B_n = B_{n-1} - \alpha\,B_{n-1} = (1-\alpha)\,B_{n-1}
$$

**Step 2 — Iterate from $B_0 = P$:**

$$
B_1 = (1-\alpha)P
$$
$$
B_2 = (1-\alpha)^2 P
$$
$$
\boxed{B_n = P\,(1-\alpha)^{n}}
$$

**Step 3 — Annual depreciation:**

$$
D_n = B_{n-1} - B_n = P(1-\alpha)^{n-1} - P(1-\alpha)^{n} = \alpha P (1-\alpha)^{n-1}
$$

**Step 4 — DDB specialisation:**

For DDB, $\alpha$ is set to *twice* the straight-line rate:

$$
\alpha_{DDB} = \frac{2}{N}
$$

**Step 5 — Verify $D_n$ series is a geometric series:**

$$
\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} D_n = \alpha P \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} (1-\alpha)^{n-1} = \alpha P \cdot \frac{1}{1 - (1-\alpha)} = P
$$

✓ Total depreciation equals full purchase price $P$ as $n \to \infty$, justifying why DB never reaches $S_L$ exactly.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Sinking Fund Depreciation (SFD)

**Given:** $P$, $S_L$, $N$, annual interest rate $i$.

**Step 1 — Define goal of the sinking fund:**

A *uniform annual deposit* $D_n$ is made at the end of each year into an account earning interest $i$. At end of year $N$, the fund must equal $(P - S_L)$ to fund replacement.

**Step 2 — Future value of an annuity:**

The future value (at year $N$) of a uniform series $D_n$ deposited at year-end is:

$$
FV = D_n \cdot \frac{(1+i)^{N} - 1}{i}
$$

**Step 3 — Set FV equal to the replacement target:**

$$
D_n \cdot \frac{(1+i)^{N} - 1}{i} = P - S_L
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $D_n$:**

$$
\boxed{D_n = (P - S_L) \cdot \frac{i}{(1+i)^{N} - 1}}
$$

**Step 5 — Verify against SLD limit:**

As $i \to 0$, using L'Hôpital or first-order Taylor expansion of $(1+i)^{N} \approx 1 + Ni$:

$$
D_n \to (P - S_L) \cdot \frac{i}{Ni} = \frac{P - S_L}{N}
$$

✓ SFD gracefully reduces to SLD when there is no interest.

---

### 3.5 Worked Example — Mixed Application

> **Question:** A CNC machine costs **₹25,00,000**, has a salvage value of **₹5,00,000**, and a useful life of **10 years**. Compare the *book value at end of year 4* under all four methods. Use $i = 8\%$ for SFD.

#### Solution:

**Step 1 — Common values:**
- $P = 2{,}500{,}000$
- $S_L = 500{,}000$
- $P - S_L = 2{,}000{,}000$
- $N = 10$
- For DDB: $\alpha = 2/10 = 0.20$

**Step 2 — SLD:**
$D_n = 2{,}000{,}000 / 10 = 2{,}00{,}000$ per year.

$$
B_4 = P - 4 \cdot \frac{P - S_L}{N} = 2{,}500{,}000 - 4 \cdot 2{,}00{,}000 = \boxed{17{,}00{,}000}
$$

**Step 3 — SYD:**

$\Sigma = \dfrac{10 \cdot 11}{2} = 55$.

$$
D_1 + D_2 + D_3 + D_4 = \frac{2{,}000{,}000}{55}(10 + 9 + 8 + 7) = \frac{2{,}000{,}000 \cdot 34}{55}
$$

$$
B_4 = 2{,}500{,}000 - 1{,}236{,}363.64 = \boxed{12{,}63{,}636.36}
$$

**Step 4 — DDB:**

$B_4 = P(1-\alpha)^4 = 2{,}500{,}000 \cdot (0.8)^4 = 2{,}500{,}000 \cdot 0.4096 = \boxed{10{,}24{,}000}$

**Step 5 — SFD:**

$D_n = 2{,}000{,}000 \cdot \dfrac{0.08}{(1.08)^{10} - 1} = 2{,}000{,}000 \cdot \dfrac{0.08}{1.15892} \approx 1{,}38{,}032.34$

$$
B_4 = 2{,}500{,}000 - 4 \cdot 1{,}38{,}032.34 = \boxed{19{,}47{,}870.64}
$$

#### Comparative Table

| Method | $B_4$ (₹) | Trajectory Shape |
|---|---|---|
| **SFD** | 19,47,870.64 | Slowest decay (interest earnings offset) |
| **SLD** | 17,00,000.00 | Linear |
| **SYD** | 12,63,636.36 | Front-loaded decay |
| **DDB** | 10,24,000.00 | Fastest decay |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering interpretation:** In reliability-replacement decisions, **DDB** *severely understates* the asset's true service value early on — leading to *premature replacement*. **SFD** best aligns book value with *economic replacement capacity*. **SLD** is the simplest and most defensible for audit purposes.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — The Position of Depreciation in Reliability Engineering Economics

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Equipment in Service] --> B[Operational Use Phase]
    B --> C[Wear and Aging over Time]
    C --> D[Failure Rate lambda t Increases]
    B --> E[Depreciation Cost Models]
    E --> F[Book Value B_n Computation]
    F --> G[Annual Capital Charge D_n]
    D --> H[Equivalent Uniform Annual Cost EUAC]
    G --> H
    I[Maintenance Cost M n] --> H
    J[Downtime Cost L n] --> H
    H --> K[Optimal Replacement Age N star]
    K --> L[Capital Replacement Decision]
    L --> A
```

### 4.2 Comparison Flowchart — Choosing the Right Depreciation Model

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Start: Asset with Cost P, Life N, Salvage S_L] --> Q{Is asset front-loaded obsolescence?}
    Q -->|Yes, e.g. IT/electronics| R[Use SYD]
    Q -->|No| S{Is salvage value explicitly tracked?}
    S -->|No, only tax-shield needed| T[Use Declining Balance or DDB]
    S -->|Yes| U{Does firm maintain a replacement reserve fund?}
    U -->|Yes, fund earns interest i| V[Use Sinking Fund SFD]
    U -->|No, simple accounting| W[Use Straight Line SLD]
    R --> X[Apply Formula from Cheat Sheet]
    T --> X
    V --> X
    W --> X
    X --> Y[Compute B_n and D_n for required n]
    Y --> Z[Integrate with EUAC for Reliability Decision]
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Mapping Depreciation Models to Book Value Trajectories

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Linear_Family
        SLD_Traj[SLD: B_n linear]
        SFD_Traj[SFD: B_n linear charge, exponential fund]
    end
    subgraph Accelerated_Family
        SYD_Traj[SYD: B_n steep early, flat late]
        DDB_Traj[DDB: B_n geometric asymptote]
    end
    P0[Initial Cost P] --> SLD_Traj
    P0 --> SFD_Traj
    P0 --> SYD_Traj
    P0 --> DDB_Traj
    SLD_Traj --> SL_Final[Salvage S_L at year N]
    SFD_Traj --> SL_Final
    SYD_Traj --> SYD_Approach[Approaches S_L steeply]
    DDB_Traj --> DDB_Asymptote[Asymptotic to S_L]
```

### 4.4 Reliability + Depreciation Decision Loop

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Asset Age n] --> B[Compute Depreciation D_n]
    A --> C[Compute Maintenance M n]
    A --> D[Compute Downtime L n]
    B --> E[EUAC n]
    C --> E
    D --> E
    E --> F{Min EUAC reached?}
    F -->|No, still declining| G[Continue Operation, increment n]
    G --> A
    F -->|Yes| H[Replace Asset at age N star]
    H --> I[Acquire New Asset with new P and S_L]
    I --> A
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
Define **Straight Line Depreciation (SLD)**. Derive an expression for the book value at the end of year $n$.

**Model Answer:**
> Straight Line Depreciation assumes that the depreciable cost of an asset is charged *equally* over its useful life. If $P$ is the initial cost, $S_L$ the salvage value and $N$ the life in years, the annual depreciation charge is:
> 
> $$D_n = \frac{P - S_L}{N}$$
> 
> Hence the book value at the end of year $n$ is:
> 
> $$B_n = P - n \cdot \frac{P - S_L}{N}$$
> 
> At $n = N$, $B_N = S_L$, satisfying the boundary condition. SLD is widely used in reliability costing because of its simplicity and audit-defensibility.

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
List any **three differences** between **Straight Line Depreciation** and **Sinking Fund Depreciation**.

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Straight Line | Sinking Fund |
|---|---|---|
| Interest considered? | **No** | **Yes** — at rate $i$ |
| Annual charge | $D_n = \dfrac{P-S_L}{N}$ | $D_n = (P-S_L)\,\dfrac{i}{(1+i)^{N}-1}$ |
| Salvage handling | Direct subtraction | Replacement-fund target |
| Underlying assumption | Uniform value loss | Self-financed replacement |

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - In **SYD**, students *frequently* forget that the numerator is $(N - n + 1)$, not $(N - n)$. Off-by-one errors cost **1 mark minimum**.
> - In **DDB**, students often substitute $\alpha = 1/N$ instead of $2/N$. This converts DDB into *plain DB*, and the full 3-mark final step is forfeited.
> - In **SFD**, do **not** confuse the annuity factor $\dfrac{i}{(1+i)^{N}-1}$ with the present-worth factor $\dfrac{1}{(1+i)^{N}-1}$. Examiner penalises ~2 marks for this.
> - Always write the **boundary check** $B_N = S_L$ (SLD) or asymptotic check (DDB). It demonstrates *complete* mastery.

---

#### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** A pump costs **₹8,00,000** with a salvage value of **₹2,00,000** and a useful life of **6 years**. Compute the *Sum-of-the-Years'-Digits depreciation schedule* and the *book value* at the end of each year. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** What is **Double Declining Balance Depreciation**? For the same pump, calculate the *book value at the end of year 5* using DDB. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) SYD Schedule**

$\Sigma = \dfrac{6 \cdot 7}{2} = 21$, $P - S_L = 6{,}00{,}000$.

| Year $n$ | Weight $\dfrac{7-n}{\Sigma}$ | $D_n$ (₹) | $B_n$ (₹) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 6/21 | 1,71,428.57 | 6,28,571.43 |
| 2 | 5/21 | 1,42,857.14 | 4,85,714.29 |
| 3 | 4/21 | 1,14,285.71 | 3,71,428.57 |
| 4 | 3/21 | 85,714.29 | 2,85,714.29 |
| 5 | 2/21 | 57,142.86 | 2,28,571.43 |
| 6 | 1/21 | 28,571.43 | 2,00,000.00 |

Sum check: total $D_n = 6{,}00{,}000$ ✓. Final book value $= S_L$ ✓.

**(b) DDB Analysis**

Definition: DDB charges a constant rate $\alpha = 2/N$ on the *remaining* book value. Book value decays geometrically: $B_n = P(1 - 2/N)^{n}$.

For our pump: $\alpha = 2/6 = 1/3 \approx 0.3333$.

$$
B_5 = 8{,}00{,}000 \cdot (1 - 1/3)^{5} = 8{,}00{,}000 \cdot (2/3)^{5}
$$

$$
(2/3)^{5} = \frac{32}{243} \approx 0.1317
$$

$$
B_5 = 8{,}00{,}000 \times 0.1317 = \boxed{1{,}05{,}328.99}
$$

> Note: DDB does not guarantee $B_N = S_L$; here $B_6 = ₹70,219.33$, which is *below* the ₹2,00,000 salvage. In practice, the firm switches to straight-line in the final years to match salvage.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam — Sessional Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the **Sinking Fund Depreciation** concept. A boiler costs **₹20,00,000** with a salvage of **₹4,00,000**, life **8 years**, and interest rate **10% p.a.** Find the *annual SFD charge* and the *book value at the end of year 3*. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **Declining Balance Depreciation** formula step by step. Why is the salvage value *not* explicitly used in this model? **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) SFD Solution**

Formula:
$$
D_n = (P - S_L)\,\frac{i}{(1+i)^{N}-1}
$$

Substituting $P - S_L = 16{,}00{,}000$, $i = 0.10$, $N = 8$:

$$
(1.10)^{8} = 2.14358881 \Rightarrow (1.10)^{8} - 1 = 1.14358881
$$

$$
D_n = 16{,}00{,}000 \times \frac{0.10}{1.14358881} = 16{,}00{,}000 \times 0.08744 = \boxed{1{,}39{,}904.59}
$$

Book value at end of year 3:
$$
B_3 = 20{,}00{,}000 - 3 \times 1{,}39{,}904.59 = 20{,}00{,}000 - 4{,}19{,}713.77 = \boxed{15{,}80{,}286.23}
$$

The fund at year 8 would have grown to $1{,}39{,}904.59 \times (F/A, 10\%, 8) = 1{,}39{,}904.59 \times 11.4359 = 16{,}00{,}000$ ✓ (matches $P - S_L$).

**(b) Declining Balance Derivation**

Step 1: Assume each year, a fixed fraction $\alpha$ of *current book value* is depreciated.
Step 2: $B_n = B_{n-1}(1-\alpha)$ → by recursion, $B_n = P(1-\alpha)^n$.
Step 3: $D_n = B_{n-1} - B_n = \alpha P (1-\alpha)^{n-1}$.

**Why salvage is not used:** The DB formula lets the asset *asymptotically* approach zero value. Summing the infinite geometric series of $D_n$ gives exactly $P$. Therefore the *natural* end-of-life value is zero, and the *actual* salvage $S_L$ is implicitly recovered by **switching to straight-line depreciation** in the final years when $B_n$ falls below $S_L$.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Depreciation Cost Models**

- ✅ **SLD**: Simplest; $D_n$ uniform; $B_n$ linear; salvage used directly.
- ✅ **SYD**: Front-loaded; weights are $(N, N-1, \dots, 1)$; sum of digits = $N(N+1)/2$.
- ✅ **DB / DDB**: Geometric decay; **salvage NOT in formula**; DDB uses $\alpha = 2/N$; book value approaches zero asymptotically.
- ✅ **SFD**: Uniform annual charge, but underlying fund grows at interest rate $i$; $D_n = (P - S_L)\cdot\dfrac{i}{(1+i)^{N}-1}$.
- ✅ **Boundary check**: Always verify $B_N = S_L$ for SLD, SYD, SFD. For DB, verify asymptotic sum equals $P$.
- ✅ **EUAC = $D_n + M(n) + L(n)$**: Minimising this gives the *minimum-cost replacement age* — the *core* reliability-economics decision.
- ✅ **Acceleration rule**: DDB > SYD > SLD > SFD in terms of early-year depreciation magnitude (for typical $N > 2$).
- ✅ **Common exam trap**: Off-by-one errors in SYD weights — use $(N - n + 1)$, not $(N - n)$.
- ✅ **Common exam trap**: DDB requires switch to straight-line once $B_n$ < $S_L$.
- ✅ **SFD ↔ SLD limit**: As $i \to 0$, SFD reduces to SLD.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Board Mantra:** *The choice of depreciation model is itself a reliability decision — it determines the perceived economic life of the asset and therefore influences the replacement strategy that governs long-term system availability.*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Availability Cost Model for Parallel Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET632 - RELIABILITY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Availability Cost Model for Parallel Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Parallel System (Reliability Engineering Context):**
A parallel configuration is a system topology in which two or more components, sub-systems, or units are arranged such that the **system performs its intended function as long as at least one of the components is operational**. Failure of the system occurs only when *all* parallel units have failed simultaneously. This is fundamentally different from a series configuration, where the failure of any single component causes system failure.

**Availability Cost Model:**
The Availability Cost Model is a quantitative engineering framework that integrates the **steady-state availability** of a parallel (redundant) system with the **economic consequences** of its operation. It expresses the total expected life-cycle cost of a system as the algebraic sum of:
1. The **acquisition (capital) cost** of the redundant units,
2. The **operating and maintenance cost**, and
3. The **expected downtime cost** (loss-of-function penalty) which is directly proportional to the system unavailability.

The model is used to determine the **economically optimal level of redundancy** — i.e., the number of parallel units that minimises total cost while satisfying a target availability requirement.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):**
> The availability cost model for parallel systems belongs to the *economic analysis of redundancy* domain. It bridges the mathematical reliability theory (Module 1–3) with engineering economics, and is a recurring 14-mark and competitive 3-mark question in KTU ESE.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

**Real-World Analogy — The Hospital Emergency Power System:**

Imagine a hospital that requires **uninterrupted electrical power** for its ICU and operation theatres. To ensure this, the hospital installs **three diesel generators in parallel** rather than one. The hospital is analogous to the *system*, and each generator is a *component*.

- If **any one** generator is running, the hospital has power. The system is **UP**.
- Only when **all three** generators fail (e.g., during a massive flood damaging all fuel pumps) does the hospital lose power. The system is **DOWN**.

Now consider the **cost dimension**:
- **Buying** a second generator costs money (capital cost).
- **Maintaining** it costs money each year.
- **But** if a single generator fails and there is no backup, the hospital loses revenue (and risks patient lives) — this is the *downtime cost*.

The Availability Cost Model asks: *"How many generators should we buy?"* The answer is **not** always "as many as possible" — at some point, the cost of an additional generator exceeds the expected savings from reduced downtime. The model finds that sweet spot.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics and Symbols (Bolded)

- **Availability (A)** — Probability that the system is operational at a random instant of time, dimension: dimensionless, range: $0 \leq A \leq 1$.
- **Unavailability (U)** — Complement of availability, $U = 1 - A$.
- **MTBF** — Mean Time Between Failures, in hours.
- **MTTR** — Mean Time To Repair, in hours.
- **Steady-State Availability**: $A_{ss} = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$
- **Capital Cost per Unit ($C_c$)** — One-time purchase cost per parallel unit.
- **Downtime Cost Rate ($C_d$)** — Cost incurred per unit time of system unavailability (₹/hour or $/hour).
- **Total Cost Function (TC(n))** — Sum of all costs as a function of the number *n* of parallel units.
- **Expected Downtime per Unit Time** = $U_{system} \cdot T$, where *T* is the total operating time.

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU 2024 scheme, Module 4 expects students to recall and apply the **parallel-availability formula** $A_s = 1 - (1-A)^n$ for identical independent components and combine it with a linear cost model to perform optimisation.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Conceptual Foundation of Parallel System Availability

Consider *n* **statistically independent, identical components** arranged in parallel. Let the availability of each component be *A*. The probability that **any single component is unavailable** is $(1 - A)$.

Since the parallel system fails **only when ALL *n* components fail simultaneously**, the system unavailability is the product of individual unavailabilities (by the multiplication rule for independent events):

$$
U_{system} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} (1 - A_i) = (1 - A)^n \quad \text{(for identical components)}
$$

Therefore, the system availability is:

$$
\boxed{A_{system} = 1 - (1 - A)^n}
$$

#### Step-by-step Logic:
- A parallel system is **UP** if at least one component is UP.
- It is **DOWN** only if every single component is DOWN.
- For *n* identical components, the joint probability of all being down is $(1-A) \cdot (1-A) \cdots (1-A) = (1-A)^n$.
- This requires the **independence assumption** — failure and repair of one unit does not affect the others.

### 2.2 Explicit Formulas for Small n (Frequently Asked in KTU)

| Number of Parallel Units (*n*) | System Unavailability $U_s$ | System Availability $A_s$ |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 1 (no redundancy) | $1 - A$ | $A$ |
| 2 | $(1 - A)^2$ | $2A - A^2$ |
| 3 | $(1 - A)^3$ | $3A^2 - 2A^3$ |
| 4 | $(1 - A)^4$ | $1 - (1-A)^4$ |

### 2.3 The Availability Cost Model — Mathematical Formulation

The total cost per unit operating time (e.g., per year) of a parallel-redundant system is the sum of three terms:

$$
\boxed{TC(n) = n \cdot C_c \cdot \alpha + C_m(n) + C_d \cdot U_s(n)}
$$

Where:
- $n \cdot C_c \cdot \alpha$ is the **annualised capital cost** of *n* parallel units. Here $\alpha$ is the capital recovery factor $\alpha = \dfrac{i(1+i)^L}{(1+i)^L - 1}$, *i* = interest rate, *L* = economic life in years.
- $C_m(n)$ is the **annual maintenance cost** of *n* units, often modelled as $\beta \cdot n \cdot C_c$.
- $C_d \cdot U_s(n)$ is the **expected annual downtime cost**, where $C_d$ is the downtime cost per unit time (₹/hour) and $U_s(n)$ is the system unavailability expressed in equivalent hours/year. If operating time $T = 8760$ hours/year, then expected downtime hours/year = $U_s(n) \cdot 8760$.

A simplified but widely-used KTU form is:

$$
\boxed{TC(n) = n \cdot K + D \cdot (1 - A)^n}
$$

Where:
- *K* = annualised cost of installing & maintaining ONE redundant unit,
- *D* = total downtime cost per year if the system has zero availability (i.e., expected loss if completely failed).

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Term | Formula / Expression | Meaning / Units |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Component availability | $A = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$ | Dimensionless, $\in [0,1]$ |
| Component unavailability | $U = 1 - A = \dfrac{MTTR}{MTBF + MTTR}$ | Dimensionless |
| System availability (parallel, *n* identical) | $A_s = 1 - (1-A)^n$ | Dimensionless |
| System availability (parallel, 2 components) | $A_s = 2A - A^2$ | Dimensionless |
| System availability (parallel, 3 components) | $A_s = 3A^2 - 2A^3$ | Dimensionless |
| Steady-state availability (general) | $A_{ss} = \dfrac{\mu}{\lambda + \mu}$ | $\lambda$ = failure rate, $\mu$ = repair rate |
| Total cost (KTU standard form) | $TC(n) = nK + D(1-A)^n$ | Currency / year |
| Optimality condition | $\dfrac{d\,TC(n)}{dn} = K - D(1-A)^n \ln(1-A) = 0$ | First derivative test |
| Optimality condition (discrete) | $K \geq D\left[(1-A)^{n^*} - (1-A)^{n^*+1}\right]$ | Inequality for integer $n^*$ |
| Expected downtime (hours/year) | $8760 \cdot U_s(n)$ | Hours/year |

> [!NOTE]
> The function $TC(n)$ has a **discrete minimum**. KTU examiners expect students to **compute TC(n) for n = 1, 2, 3, …** and pick the value giving minimum total cost. The derivative approach is a refinement for *continuous* *n* and the optimal integer is the nearest integer to that.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace industry**: Triple-Modular Redundancy (TMR) on flight-control computers — selecting between 2-out-of-3 vs. 4-out-of-4 voting involves availability cost trade-offs.
- **Data centres**: Tier-IV data centres use $N+1$ or $2N$ redundancy; the availability cost model helps choose the *N* that balances rack capital expenditure against SLA penalty costs.
- **Power systems**: Substation transformer paralleling — one transformer must carry full load while the other is on standby.
- **Telecommunications**: Base transceiver station (BTS) redundancy planning in mobile networks.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: System Availability for *n* Identical Independent Parallel Components

**Given:**
- *n* identical components, each with availability $A$ and unavailability $U = 1 - A$.
- All components operate **independently** (failure and repair events do not correlate).
- The system is UP if **at least one** component is UP.

**Step 1 — Define the system DOWN event.**
Let $F_i$ be the event that the *i*-th component is failed (DOWN). The system is DOWN if and only if **all** components are failed simultaneously:

$$
E_{DOWN} = F_1 \cap F_2 \cap F_3 \cap \cdots \cap F_n
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the probability multiplication rule for independent events.**
Since the components are statistically independent, the probability of the intersection equals the product of the probabilities:

$$
P(E_{DOWN}) = P(F_1) \cdot P(F_2) \cdot P(F_3) \cdots P(F_n)
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute identical unavailability $U$.**
Because all components are identical, $P(F_i) = U$ for every *i*:

$$
P(E_{DOWN}) = U \cdot U \cdot U \cdots U \quad (n \text{ times})
$$

Therefore:

$$
P(E_{DOWN}) = U^n = (1 - A)^n
$$

**Step 4 — Compute system availability.**
Availability is the probability of the complementary event (system UP):

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_s &= 1 - P(E_{DOWN}) \\
A_s &= 1 - (1 - A)^n
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **canonical KTU parallel-availability formula**.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Two-Component Parallel System Availability (Expanded Form)

Starting from $A_s = 1 - (1 - A)^n$ with $n = 2$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_s &= 1 - (1 - A)^2 \\
&= 1 - \left[1 - 2A + A^2\right] \quad \text{(binomial expansion)} \\
&= 1 - 1 + 2A - A^2 \\
A_s &= 2A - A^2
\end{aligned}
$$

This expansion is useful because KTU frequently asks: *"If each unit has availability 0.95, find the system availability for two units in parallel."* Direct substitution $A_s = 2(0.95) - (0.95)^2 = 1.90 - 0.9025 = 0.9975$ is faster than the compact form.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Three-Component Parallel System Availability

With $n = 3$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_s &= 1 - (1 - A)^3 \\
&= 1 - \left[1 - 3A + 3A^2 - A^3\right] \quad \text{(binomial expansion)} \\
&= 3A - 3A^2 + A^3
\end{aligned}
$$

Wait — the binomial expansion of $(1 - A)^3 = 1 - 3A + 3A^2 - A^3$. Subtracting from 1:

$$
A_s = 1 - (1 - 3A + 3A^2 - A^3) = 3A - 3A^2 + A^3
$$

This can also be written (as in the formula sheet) in the equivalent rearranged form $3A^2 - 2A^3$ **only if** it has been algebraically manipulated; the direct binomial subtraction gives $3A - 3A^2 + A^3$. The Cheat Sheet value $3A^2 - 2A^3$ comes from a different starting expression — let us verify both forms agree by setting $A = 0.9$:

- Form 1: $3(0.9) - 3(0.9)^2 + (0.9)^3 = 2.7 - 2.43 + 0.729 = 0.999$
- Form 2: $3(0.81) - 2(0.729) = 2.43 - 1.458 = 0.972$

These **do not match**, indicating an error in one of the formulas. The correct binomial expansion gives:

$$
\boxed{A_s(n=3) = 3A - 3A^2 + A^3}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Correction note for KTU students:** Some textbooks quote the parallel-system *reliability* polynomial (for non-repairable parallel systems with success probability $R$) as $R_s = 3R^2 - 2R^3$ for $n = 3$ — this is the reliability form derived using inclusion–exclusion on three events. The **availability** form $A_s = 1 - (1-A)^3$ expands to $3A - 3A^2 + A^3$. Students should verify which form is being requested in the exam by checking whether the question asks for reliability or availability.

Let us double-verify numerically for $A = 0.9$:
- $1 - (0.1)^3 = 1 - 0.001 = 0.999$ ✓
- $3(0.9) - 3(0.81) + 0.729 = 2.7 - 2.43 + 0.729 = 0.999$ ✓
- $3(0.81) - 2(0.729) = 2.43 - 1.458 = 0.972$ ✗ (this would be wrong for availability)

The correct expanded form is therefore $3A - 3A^2 + A^3$.

---

### 3.4 Derivation: Optimal Number of Parallel Units (Cost Minimisation)

**Objective:** Find integer $n^*$ that minimises $TC(n) = nK + D(1-A)^n$.

**Step 1 — Consider TC as a function of continuous *n*.**
Treat *n* as a real variable and differentiate with respect to *n*:

$$
\frac{d\,TC}{dn} = K + D \cdot \frac{d}{dn}\left[(1-A)^n\right]
$$

Using the identity $\frac{d}{dx}(b^x) = b^x \ln(b)$:

$$
\frac{d\,TC}{dn} = K + D \cdot (1-A)^n \ln(1-A)
$$

**Step 2 — Set the derivative to zero to find the stationary point.**

$$
K + D \cdot (1-A)^{n^*} \ln(1-A) = 0
$$

Solving for $n^*$:

$$
(1-A)^{n^*} = \frac{-K}{D \cdot \ln(1-A)}
$$

Taking natural logarithm of both sides:

$$
n^* \cdot \ln(1-A) = \ln\!\left(\frac{-K}{D \cdot \ln(1-A)}\right)
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
n^* &= \frac{\ln\!\left(\dfrac{-K}{D \cdot \ln(1-A)}\right)}{\ln(1-A)} \\
&= \frac{\ln\!\left(\dfrac{K}{D \cdot \vert \ln(1-A) \vert}\right)}{\vert \ln(1-A) \vert}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $(1 - A) < 1$, we have $\ln(1-A) < 0$, so $\vert \ln(1-A) \vert = -\ln(1-A)$.

**Step 3 — Verify it is a minimum using the second derivative test.**

$$
\frac{d^2\,TC}{dn^2} = D \cdot (1-A)^n \left[\ln(1-A)\right]^2 > 0
$$

Because $\left[\ln(1-A)\right]^2 > 0$ and $D(1-A)^n > 0$, the second derivative is **positive** — confirming that the stationary point is indeed a **minimum**.

**Step 4 — Round to nearest integer.**
The optimal integer number of parallel units is:

$$
\boxed{n^*_{integer} = \text{round}\!\left(\frac{\ln\!\left(\dfrac{K}{D \cdot \vert \ln(1-A) \vert}\right)}{\vert \ln(1-A) \vert}\right)}
$$

In practice, KTU exam answers use the **discrete-comparison method**: compute $TC(n)$ for $n = 1, 2, 3, \ldots$ and pick the lowest.

---

### 3.5 Worked Example — Full KTU-Style Numerical Problem

**Problem Statement (modelled on KTU ESE 2024 pattern):**
A critical process control system requires uninterrupted operation. Each control unit has MTBF = 2000 hours and MTTR = 10 hours. The capital & maintenance cost per redundant unit is **₹50,000 per year**. If the system is unavailable, the production loss is **₹20,000,000 per year** (assume downtime is fully monetised). Determine the optimal number of parallel units to install.

**Solution:**

**Step A — Compute component availability.**

$$
A = \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR} = \frac{2000}{2000 + 10} = \frac{2000}{2010} \approx 0.995025
$$

Component unavailability:

$$
U = 1 - A = \frac{10}{2010} \approx 0.004975
$$

**Step B — Compute total cost $TC(n) = nK + D \cdot U^n$** with $K = 50{,}000$ and $D = 20{,}000{,}000$.

| $n$ | $U^n$ | $nK$ (₹) | $D \cdot U^n$ (₹) | $TC(n)$ (₹) |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 1 | 0.004975 | 50,000 | 99,500 | 149,500 |
| 2 | $2.475 \times 10^{-5}$ | 100,000 | 495 | 100,495 |
| 3 | $1.231 \times 10^{-7}$ | 150,000 | 2.46 | 150,002 |
| 4 | $6.124 \times 10^{-10}$ | 200,000 | 0.012 | 200,000 |

(Values computed: $U^2 \approx 2.475 \times 10^{-5}$, $U^3 \approx 1.231 \times 10^{-7}$, $U^4 \approx 6.12 \times 10^{-10}$.)

**Step C — Identify optimum.**
The minimum total cost is **₹100,495 at $n^* = 2$**.

**Step D — Verify using continuous-optimality formula.**

$$
n^* = \frac{\ln\!\left(\dfrac{K}{D \cdot \vert \ln(U) \vert}\right)}{\vert \ln(U) \vert}
$$

$$
\ln(U) = \ln(0.004975) \approx -5.304 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \vert \ln(U) \vert = 5.304
$$

$$
\frac{K}{D \cdot \vert \ln(U) \vert} = \frac{50{,}000}{20{,}000{,}000 \cdot 5.304} = \frac{50{,}000}{1.0608 \times 10^{8}} \approx 4.713 \times 10^{-4}
$$

$$
\ln(4.713 \times 10^{-4}) \approx -7.659
$$

$$
n^* = \frac{-7.659}{5.304} \approx 1.444
$$

Nearest integer = **2**, confirming the discrete computation.

**Step E — Compute the optimum availability.**

$$
A_s(n=2) = 1 - (0.004975)^2 = 1 - 2.475 \times 10^{-5} \approx 0.999975
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** Installing **two** parallel control units gives availability ≈ 99.9975% (≈ 11 minutes downtime per year) at the minimum total annualised cost of ₹100,495. Adding a third unit pushes availability higher (≈ 99.99999%) but increases cost to ₹150,002 because the marginal downtime-cost saving (₹2.46) is far smaller than the extra ₹50,000 capital outlay.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram: Parallel Redundant System with Cost Model Overlay

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the parallel-availability cost model architecture, mapping the interaction between parallel components, the availability computation block, the cost computation block, and the optimisation decision block.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A1[Unit 1] --> O1[OR Logic - System Output]
    A2[Unit 2] --> O1
    A3[Unit n - last parallel unit] --> O1
    A1 -.MTBF1, MTTR1.-> CALCA[Availability Calculator Block]
    A2 -.MTBF2, MTTR2.-> CALCA
    A3 -.MTBn, MTTRn.-> CALCA
    CALCA --> AS[System Availability As equals 1 minus 1 minus A to the power n]
    AS --> COSTD[Downtime Cost equals D times 1 minus As]
    CCAP[Capital Cost Block - n times K per year] --> SUMC[Total Cost Summation Block]
    CMAINT[Maintenance Cost Block - beta times n times K] --> SUMC
    COSTD --> SUMC
    SUMC --> TCO[Total Cost Output TC of n]
    TCO --> OPT[Optimization Loop - find n star minimising TC]
    OPT --> DEC[Decision: select n star]
```

### 4.2 Decision Flow: Selecting Optimal Redundancy Level

The next diagram shows the logical decision process an engineer follows when applying the availability cost model.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Step 1: Define component MTBF and MTTR] --> S2[Step 2: Compute component availability A]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3: Identify cost parameters K and D]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4: Tabulate TC of n for n equals 1, 2, 3...]
    S4 --> S5{Step 5: Does TC decrease as n increases?}
    S5 -- Yes --> S6[Step 6: Continue incrementing n]
    S6 --> S7{Step 7: Has TC reached a minimum and started rising?}
    S5 -- No --> S7
    S7 -- Yes --> S8[Step 8: Select n star as optimal redundancy]
    S7 -- No --> S6
```

### 4.3 Cost-vs-Redundancy Behavioural Diagram (Conceptual)

The following Mermaid graph captures the qualitative shape of the total-cost function versus the number of parallel units, with two opposing curves and their sum.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Costs
        C1[Capital Cost Curve - linearly rising with n]
        C2[Downtime Cost Curve - exponentially decaying with n]
        C3[Total Cost TC of n - U-shaped with a minimum at n star]
    end
    C1 --> C3
    C2 --> C3
    C3 -.minimum point.-> MARK[Marker: n star = economically optimal redundancy]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **capital cost curve** rises linearly with *n* (each redundant unit costs *K* per year), while the **downtime cost curve** decays exponentially as $(1-A)^n$ shrinks. Their sum is a convex U-shaped function — the bottom of the U is the economically optimal redundancy level.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1 (3 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question]**
Define *steady-state availability* of a repairable component. Compute the availability of a component whose MTBF = 5000 hours and MTTR = 25 hours. Comment on whether the availability changes if two such components are placed in parallel.

**Model Answer:**

> *Steady-state availability* of a repairable component is the long-run fraction of time that the component is operational, given by $A_{ss} = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$.

$$
A = \frac{5000}{5000 + 25} = \frac{5000}{5025} \approx 0.9950
$$

For two such components in parallel (independent, identical):

$$
A_s = 2A - A^2 = 2(0.9950) - (0.9950)^2 = 1.9900 - 0.990025 = 0.999975
$$

**Comment:** The parallel-system availability (≈ 0.999975) is significantly higher than a single component's availability (0.9950). Even a small redundancy greatly reduces the system's unavailability from 0.005 to 0.000025.

---

#### **Question 2 (3 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Retest Pattern]**
State the parallel-system availability formula for *n* identical components. Why is the independence assumption important in its derivation?

**Model Answer:**

For *n* identical, independent components, each with availability *A*:

$$
A_s = 1 - (1 - A)^n
$$

**Importance of Independence:** The derivation uses the multiplication rule $P(F_1 \cap F_2 \cap \cdots \cap F_n) = \prod P(F_i)$, which holds **only if** the failure events of different components are statistically independent. If components share a common failure mode (e.g., common power supply, common cooling, or environmental cause), a single external event can fail all components simultaneously and the formula overestimates availability. Common-cause failures require **separate modelling** (e.g., the β-factor model) outside the scope of the basic parallel-availability formula.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice 1

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Module 4 Pattern]**
(a) Derive the system availability expression for *n* identical, statistically independent components connected in parallel. **(7 Marks)**

(b) A manufacturing plant uses an automated welding robot. The robot has MTBF = 1500 hours and MTTR = 12 hours. The cost of installing one robot is **₹8,00,000 per year** (annualised). The production loss due to welding-line downtime is **₹1,50,00,000 per year**. Assuming independence, determine the optimal number of parallel robots to install. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution to Part (a):**

**Step 1 — Define component and system events.**
Let $A_i$ be the availability of the *i*-th component. The component is DOWN with probability $U_i = 1 - A_i$.

The parallel system is DOWN only if **all** components are DOWN. Define this event as:

$$
E_{DOWN} = \{ \text{Component 1 is DOWN} \} \cap \{ \text{Component 2 is DOWN} \} \cap \cdots \cap \{ \text{Component } n \text{ is DOWN} \}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the multiplication rule for independent events.**

$$
P(E_{DOWN}) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} U_i = U_1 \cdot U_2 \cdots U_n
$$

**Step 3 — Specialise to identical components.**
If $U_i = U$ for all $i$ (identical components), then $P(E_{DOWN}) = U^n = (1 - A)^n$.

**Step 4 — Compute system availability.**

$$
\boxed{A_s = 1 - (1 - A)^n}
$$

The same derivation generalises trivially to non-identical components:

$$
A_s = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n} (1 - A_i)
$$

**Model Solution to Part (b):**

**Step 1 — Compute component availability.**

$$
A = \frac{1500}{1500 + 12} = \frac{1500}{1512} \approx 0.99206
$$

$$
U = 1 - A = \frac{12}{1512} \approx 0.007937
$$

**Step 2 — Define the cost model.**
With $K = 8{,}00{,}000$ per year per robot and $D = 1{,}50{,}00{,}000$ per year as the full-loss downtime cost:

$$
TC(n) = nK + D \cdot U^n = 8{,}00{,}000\,n + 1{,}50{,}00{,}000 \cdot (0.007937)^n
$$

**Step 3 — Tabulate TC(n) for n = 1, 2, 3, 4.**

| $n$ | $U^n$ | $nK$ (₹) | $D \cdot U^n$ (₹) | $TC(n)$ (₹) |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 1 | 0.007937 | 8,00,000 | 11,90,550 | 19,90,550 |
| 2 | $6.30 \times 10^{-5}$ | 16,00,000 | 9,450 | 16,09,450 |
| 3 | $5.00 \times 10^{-7}$ | 24,00,000 | 75 | 24,00,075 |
| 4 | $3.97 \times 10^{-9}$ | 32,00,000 | 0.60 | 32,00,001 |

**Step 4 — Identify optimum.**
Minimum TC is at $n^* = 2$ with $TC \approx ₹16,09,450$ per year.

**Step 5 — Compute optimal availability.**

$$
A_s(n=2) = 1 - (0.007937)^2 = 1 - 6.30 \times 10^{-5} = 0.999937
$$

**Conclusion:** Install **2 robots in parallel**. The optimal system availability is approximately **99.9937%** (≈ 22 minutes of expected downtime per year).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice 2

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Supplementary Pattern]**
(a) Explain the concept of the *availability cost model* for parallel systems. List and justify the three major cost components in the model. **(7 Marks)**

(b) For a parallel-redundant system with identical components, derive the condition that determines the economically optimal number of parallel units by treating the total cost as a continuous function of *n* and using differential calculus. Verify that the second-order condition confirms a minimum. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution to Part (a):**

**Definition:** The availability cost model for parallel systems is an engineering-economic framework that combines the steady-state availability of a redundant parallel system with the monetary consequences of purchasing, maintaining, and operating that system. The objective is to choose the redundancy level *n* that yields the lowest total annualised cost while satisfying reliability/availability targets.

**Three Major Cost Components:**

1. **Capital Cost (Acquisition Cost):** The annualised purchase and installation cost of the *n* parallel units, modelled as $n \cdot C_c \cdot \alpha$. This is a *monotonically increasing* function of *n* — every additional unit adds the same marginal capital cost. Justification: redundancy cannot be obtained for free.

2. **Operating & Maintenance Cost:** The recurring cost of keeping each unit serviceable — preventive maintenance, spares, operator training, scheduled overhauls. Often modelled as a fixed fraction of capital cost, e.g., $\beta \cdot n \cdot C_c$. Justification: every active or standby unit consumes maintenance resources regardless of whether it has ever failed.

3. **Downtime (Failure) Cost:** The expected monetary loss due to the system being unavailable. Modelled as $C_d \cdot U_s(n) \cdot T$, where $C_d$ is the downtime cost rate (₹/hour) and $U_s(n) \cdot T$ is the expected annual downtime. This is a *monotonically decreasing* function of *n* — adding redundancy reduces unavailability. Justification: in industries such as petrochemicals, e-commerce, or healthcare, system downtime translates directly to lost revenue, contractual penalties, or safety incidents.

The total cost is the sum:

$$
TC(n) = \underbrace{n \cdot K}_{\text{capital + maintenance}} + \underbrace{C_d \cdot T \cdot (1 - A)^n}_{\text{downtime}}
$$

**Model Solution to Part (b):**

**Step 1 — Define total cost function (continuous form).**
Treating *n* as a continuous real variable $\geq 1$:

$$
TC(n) = nK + D(1 - A)^n
$$

where *K* and *D* are constants with $K, D > 0$ and $0 < A < 1$.

**Step 2 — First derivative (first-order necessary condition).**

$$
\frac{d\,TC}{dn} = K + D(1-A)^n \ln(1-A)
$$

Setting $\frac{d\,TC}{dn} = 0$:

$$
K + D(1-A)^{n^*} \ln(1-A) = 0
$$

$$
(1-A)^{n^*} = \frac{-K}{D \ln(1-A)} = \frac{K}{D \cdot \vert \ln(1-A) \vert}
$$

$$
n^* = \frac{\ln\!\left(\dfrac{K}{D \cdot \vert \ln(1-A) \vert}\right)}{\vert \ln(1-A) \vert}
$$

**Step 3 — Second derivative (second-order sufficiency condition).**

$$
\frac{d^2\,TC}{dn^2} = D \cdot (1-A)^n \cdot [\ln(1-A)]^2
$$

Since $D > 0$, $(1-A)^n > 0$, and $[\ln(1-A)]^2 > 0$ for $A \neq 1$, we have:

$$
\frac{d^2\,TC}{dn^2} > 0
$$

A **positive second derivative confirms the stationary point is a strict local minimum**.

**Step 4 — Round to nearest integer.**
The economically optimal integer redundancy is:

$$
n^*_{\text{integer}} = \text{round}(n^*)
$$

In practice, KTU exam answers use **discrete tabulation** (compute $TC(1), TC(2), TC(3), \ldots$ and choose the lowest), which gives the exact integer optimum without approximation.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes where KTU students lose marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing availability with reliability.** Reliability $R(t)$ applies to non-repairable systems; availability $A$ applies to repairable systems. The parallel formula $A_s = 1 - (1-A)^n$ is **availability**, not reliability. For reliability in non-repairable parallel systems, the analogous formula is $R_s = 1 - (1-R)^n$.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the independence assumption.** Students often write the formula without stating that the components must be statistically independent. Examiners deduct 1–2 marks if independence is not explicitly justified.
>
> 3. **Algebra slip on the binomial expansion of $(1 - A)^3$.** The correct expansion is $1 - 3A + 3A^2 - A^3$, giving $A_s = 3A - 3A^2 + A^3$. A frequently confused form $3A^2 - 2A^3$ is the **reliability polynomial** for non-repairable systems and is **incorrect** for availability.
>
> 4. **Forgetting to round *n\*** to the nearest integer. The continuous optimum $n^* = 1.4$ does not mean "1.4 robots" — the answer is 2 units (the nearest integer that minimises TC).
>
> 5. **Using incorrect units of cost.** If $D$ is annual downtime cost in ₹/year and $K$ is annual cost per unit in ₹/year, then $TC(n)$ is in ₹/year. Mixing units (e.g., $D$ in ₹/hour and $T$ in years without conversion) is a frequent arithmetic error.
>
> 6. **Forgetting to state $0 < A < 1$.** A common-cause or perfectly correlated failure mode can make $(1-A)^n$ an under-estimate of unavailability; students who do not flag this lose marks on the "why independence matters" sub-part.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Parallel system fails only when ALL components fail.** This is the single most important conceptual point.
- **Canonical formula:** $A_s = 1 - (1 - A)^n$ for *n* identical, independent components. Memorise this — it appears in nearly every Part B question on Module 4.
- **Two-component form (commonly needed):** $A_s = 2A - A^2$.
- **Three-component form (commonly needed):** $A_s = 3A - 3A^2 + A^3$ (NOT $3A^2 - 2A^3$, which is the reliability polynomial).
- **Component availability from MTBF/MTTR:** $A = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$.
- **Three cost components:** (i) Capital + maintenance (linear in *n*), (ii) Downtime cost (decreasing exponentially with *n*), (iii) Total cost = (i) + (ii).
- **Total cost function (KTU standard form):** $TC(n) = nK + D(1 - A)^n$.
- **Continuous optimum:** $n^* = \dfrac{\ln\!\left(\dfrac{K}{D \cdot \vert \ln(1-A) \vert}\right)}{\vert \ln(1-A) \vert}$, rounded to nearest integer.
- **Second-derivative test:** Always positive → stationary point is a minimum.
- **Discrete method preferred for KTU exams:** Tabulate TC(n) for $n = 1, 2, 3, \ldots$ and pick the minimum.
- **Independence is non-negotiable:** Always state and justify it.
- **Availability vs. reliability:** Do not mix the two; repairable systems use availability, non-repairable use reliability.
- **Engineering insight:** Doubling the redundancy (e.g., 1 → 2 units) gives a much larger availability gain than tripling (2 → 3 units) because unavailability falls as $(1-A)^n$ — the marginal benefit decreases rapidly.
- **Annualisation:** If capital cost is a one-time purchase, annualise it using the capital recovery factor $\alpha = \dfrac{i(1+i)^L}{(1+i)^L - 1}$ before combining with annual operating and downtime costs.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Reliability Management and Reliability Management by Objectives
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET632 - RELIABILITY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Reliability Management and Reliability Management by Objectives

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of Reliability Management (RM)

**Reliability Management** is the structured, top-down administrative discipline that plans, organizes, directs, and controls all organizational resources—human, technical, and financial—to ensure that a product, system, or process performs its intended function under stated conditions, for a stated period of time, with a stated probability of success.

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme** context, it is viewed as an integrated management function that spans the entire **product life cycle**: concept → design → development → production → operation → disposal.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Definition (Syllabus Highlight)**
> Reliability Management = Coordination of **Design Reliability**, **Production Reliability**, and **Use Reliability** to meet customer-specified performance targets, while optimizing the **Life Cycle Cost (LCC)** of the system.

### 1.2 Formal Definition of Reliability Management by Objectives (RMBO)

**Reliability Management by Objectives (RMBO)** is a goal-oriented management technique in which **specific, measurable, achievable, relevant, and time-bound (SMART)** reliability targets are established at every organizational level, and managerial performance is evaluated against the achievement of these quantitative reliability goals.

> [!NOTE]
> **Origin of RMBO**
> RMBO evolved from the classical **Management by Objectives (MBO)** philosophy of Peter Drucker, applied specifically to the reliability discipline. The first formalized industry use was seen in aerospace and defense (US MIL-STD series) and later adopted in automotive (QS-9000, ISO/TS 16949) and telecommunications standards.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

**The Personal Health Analogy:**

Think of **Reliability Management** like the entire medical system of a city — it covers hospitals (production), doctors (design engineers), ambulances (maintenance crews), and health insurance (cost recovery). It exists to keep the "patient" (your machine/factory) healthy.

**Reliability Management by Objectives (RMBO)** is more specific — it is like a **fitness goal-setting program**. You don't just say "stay healthy." You say:

- "I will run **5 km** in **30 minutes** within **3 months**."
- "My body fat will drop to **15%** by **December**."
- "I will measure progress every **week** using a **weighing scale and stopwatch**."

Similarly, RMBO says:
- "This server must have an **uptime of 99.99%** within **2 years**."
- "The MTBF of this compressor will improve from **2,000 hours** to **5,000 hours** by **Q4**."
- "We will measure progress every **month** using **failure logs**."

> [!TIP]
> Just as you cannot run a marathon without training metrics, you cannot run a reliable plant without **quantified reliability objectives**.

### 1.4 Why RM and RMBO Matter in Engineering

Modern systems are **interdependent, complex, and expensive to maintain**. Without structured reliability management:
- Field failures cascade into warranty losses.
- Unreliability costs (downtime, repair, brand damage) spiral.
- Regulatory compliance (ISO 9001, AS9100, Six Sigma) fails.

RMBO converts **reliability from an abstract "good feeling" into a measurable, auditable, and managerially controllable variable**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Pillars of Reliability Management

Reliability management operates across **three distinct but interconnected phases** of a product's life:

**Pillar 1 — Design Reliability (Inherent Reliability)**
This is the reliability built *into* the product during the engineering design phase. It represents the **maximum achievable reliability** of a system, governed by:
- Component selection and derating
- Tolerance design
- Redundancy architecture (active, standby, k-out-of-n)
- Design for Maintainability and Design for Manufacturability (DFM/DFT)

**Pillar 2 — Production Reliability (Achieved Reliability)**
This is the reliability *realized* during manufacturing. It depends on:
- Process capability indices ($C_p$, $C_{pk}$)
- Quality control (SPC, Six Sigma)
- Supplier quality assurance
- Workmanship standards

**Pillar 3 — Use Reliability (Operational Reliability)**
This is the reliability experienced by the *customer* in the field. It depends on:
- Operating environment (temperature, humidity, vibration)
- Maintenance policy (preventive, predictive, corrective)
- Operator skill level
- Logistics support

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** $\text{Operational Reliability} \;\leq\; \text{Achieved Reliability} \;\leq\; \text{Inherent Reliability}$.
> Every phase introduces degradation; reliability management aims to minimize this gap.

### 2.2 Key Elements of a Reliability Management Program

A formal reliability program (as required in KTU Module 4 and standard texts on reliability engineering) consists of the following elements:

- **Reliability Planning**: Defining reliability tasks, schedules, budgets.
- **Organizing for Reliability**: Creating reliability groups, defining roles (Reliability Engineer, RAMS Engineer, Chief Reliability Officer).
- **Reliability Design Reviews**: PDR (Preliminary Design Review), CDR (Critical Design Review).
- **Reliability Prediction & Allocation**: Using MIL-HDBK-217, Telcordia, or NSWC methods.
- **Failure Modes, Effects, and Criticality Analysis (FMECA)**.
- **Reliability Testing**: HALT, HASS, ALT, ESS.
- **Reliability Growth Tracking**: Using Duane Model or AMSAA model.
- **Field Data Collection & FRACAS**: Failure Reporting, Analysis, and Corrective Action System.
- **Maintainability & Availability Analysis**.
- **Reliability Audits and Continuous Improvement**.

### 2.3 The Reliability Management by Objectives (RMBO) Framework — Stepwise Logic

The RMBO methodology follows a structured 6-step framework, conceptually similar to the **PDCA (Plan-Do-Check-Act)** cycle of Deming:

**Step 1 — Define the Reliability Vision and Policy**
Senior management articulates the organization's commitment to reliability (e.g., "Zero unplanned downtime by 2027").

**Step 2 — Establish SMART Reliability Objectives**
Set quantitative targets. Example:
- "Reduce system failure rate to **$\lambda = 0.001$ failures/hour** by **end of FY 2025**."

**Step 3 — Decompose Objectives (Goal Cascading)**
Translate top-level reliability goals into sub-objectives for each department:
- Design team: "Reduce part count by 15%."
- Production team: "Achieve $C_{pk} \geq 1.67$ for critical dimensions."
- Maintenance team: "Reduce MTTR to under 2 hours."

**Step 4 — Develop Action Plans and Allocate Resources**
For each sub-objective, assign responsibility, budget, timeline, and measurable milestones.

**Step 5 — Monitor, Measure, and Review Performance**
Track Key Reliability Indicators (KRIs) such as MTBF, MTTR, Availability, and Warranty Cost Ratio (WCR).

**Step 6 — Take Corrective Action and Recycle**
If objectives are not met, perform root-cause analysis (RCA) and update objectives based on new data.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

The following table summarizes all key mathematical relationships for Module 4 topics. Note that the **vertical bar symbol** for absolute value or conditional probability is written using `\vert` to maintain markdown compatibility.

| Formula / Metric | Mathematical Expression | Units | Meaning / Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Failure Rate** | $\lambda(t) = \dfrac{f(t)}{R(t)}$ | failures/hour | Instantaneous rate of failure; used in reliability prediction |
| **Reliability Function** | $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ (for constant $\lambda$) | dimensionless (probability) | Probability of survival up to time $t$ |
| **Mean Time Between Failures** | $\text{MTBF} = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}$ | hours | Average operating time between successive failures |
| **Mean Time To Repair** | $\text{MTTR} = \dfrac{\text{Total Downtime}}{\text{Number of Failures}}$ | hours | Average time to restore a failed system |
| **Steady-State Availability** | $A = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$ | dimensionless (0 to 1) | Long-run fraction of time system is operational |
| **Inherent Availability** | $A_i = \dfrac{\text{MTBM}}{\text{MTBM} + \text{MADT}}$ | dimensionless | Availability excluding logistics and admin delays |
| **Achieved Availability** | $A_a = \dfrac{\text{Up Time}}{\text{Up Time} + \text{Down Time}}$ | dimensionless | Real-world availability including all delays |
| **Warranty Cost Ratio (WCR)** | $\text{WCR} = \dfrac{C_w}{C_s}$ | dimensionless | Ratio of warranty cost to sales cost; key RMBO KRI |
| **Quality Cost Ratio** | $\text{QCR} = \dfrac{\text{Prevention + Appraisal Cost}}{\text{Internal + External Failure Cost}}$ | dimensionless | Indicates balance of quality spending (target $\geq 1$) |
| **Six Sigma Defect Rate** | $D = 3.4 \text{ DPMO}$ | defects/million | World-class quality benchmark |
| **Goal Achievement Index (RMBO)** | $G = \dfrac{R_{\text{achieved}}}{R_{\text{target}}}$ | dimensionless | If $G \geq 1$, objective met; used to score managers |

### 2.5 Real-World Utility of RM and RMBO in Engineering

- **Aerospace & Defense**: AS9100 mandates reliability management; RMBO drives MTBF targets of $10^5$+ hours.
- **Automotive**: IATF 16949 requires warranty cost reduction programs — direct application of RMBO.
- **Power Generation**: NTPC, KSEB use RMBO to track plant availability (target $> 85\%$).
- **IT/Data Centers**: Tier-IV data centers target availability of 99.995% — strictly an RMBO goal.
- **Manufacturing SMEs in Kerala**: RMBO can reduce rejection rates, a key KTU industrial-application context.

### 2.6 Comparative Matrix: Reliability Management vs. RMBO

| Aspect | Reliability Management (RM) | Reliability Management by Objectives (RMBO) |
|---|---|---|
| Focus | Process-oriented: build a system | Result-oriented: achieve targets |
| Style | Continuous improvement framework | Goal-setting and measurement framework |
| Time Horizon | Long-term, ongoing | Defined cycle (typically annual/quarterly) |
| Evaluation Basis | Process adherence and program maturity | Achievement of quantified targets |
| Top Management Role | Sponsor and policy maker | Sponsor **and** accountability holder |
| Best Suited For | Building reliability culture from scratch | Mature organizations with existing reliability data |

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Relationship Between Reliability and Availability

We begin with the definition of steady-state availability for a repairable system with constant failure rate $\lambda$ and constant repair rate $\mu$.

**Step 1 — Define the Up-Time Probability Density.**
For an exponential failure distribution, the probability that the system survives to time $t$ without failure is:

$$
R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}
$$

**Step 2 — Define the Mean Time to Failure.**
The expected operating duration of a single cycle is:

$$
\text{MTTF} = \int_{0}^{\infty} R(t)\, dt = \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-\lambda t}\, dt
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the Integral.**

$$
\text{MTTF} = \left[ -\dfrac{1}{\lambda} e^{-\lambda t} \right]_{0}^{\infty} = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}
$$

**Step 4 — Define MTTR Analogously.**
For an exponential repair-time distribution with repair rate $\mu$:

$$
\text{MTTR} = \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-\mu t}\, dt = \dfrac{1}{\mu}
$$

**Step 5 — Define MTBF as Sum of MTTF and MTTR (Repairable System).**
For a repairable system that is restored to "as-good-as-new":

$$
\text{MTBF} = \text{MTTF} + \text{MTTR} = \dfrac{1}{\lambda} + \dfrac{1}{\mu}
$$

**Step 6 — Apply the Steady-State Availability Definition.**
In the long run, the fraction of time the system is operational equals the ratio of mean up-time to mean cycle time:

$$
A = \dfrac{\text{MTTF}}{\text{MTTF} + \text{MTTR}} = \dfrac{\dfrac{1}{\lambda}}{\dfrac{1}{\lambda} + \dfrac{1}{\mu}}
$$

**Step 7 — Simplify the Expression.**
Multiply numerator and denominator by $\lambda \mu$:

$$
A = \dfrac{\mu}{\lambda + \mu}
$$

**Step 8 — Practical Engineering Form.**
Since $\text{MTBF} = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}$ and $\text{MTTR} = \dfrac{1}{\mu}$, we may also write:

$$
A = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}
$$

This is the KTU high-yield availability equation used in RMBO target-setting (e.g., "achieve $A = 0.999$").

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Cost-of-Unreliability Function (RMBO Financial Backbone)

Reliability management by objectives often uses cost minimization as its economic rationale. Let $C$ be total cost, decomposed as:

$$
C_{\text{total}} = C_p + C_a + C_f
$$

where:
- $C_p$ = Prevention Cost (training, design reviews, derating)
- $C_a$ = Appraisal Cost (testing, inspection, audits)
- $C_f$ = Failure Cost (warranty, downtime, recall, brand loss)

**Step 1 — Behavior of Cost Components with Reliability.**
Empirically (Feigenbaum's P-A-F model and later Juran):

- $C_p \uparrow$ as reliability target $R$ increases (more design effort).
- $C_a \uparrow$ as $R$ increases initially, then plateaus.
- $C_f \downarrow$ as $R$ increases.

**Step 2 — Define Cost-of-Quality Function (Total).**
The optimum reliability point is where $C_{\text{total}}$ is minimum:

$$
C_{\text{total}}(R) = C_p(R) + C_a(R) + C_f(R)
$$

**Step 3 — Find the Optimal Reliability by Differentiation.**
Setting $\dfrac{dC_{\text{total}}}{dR} = 0$:

$$
\dfrac{dC_p}{dR} + \dfrac{dC_a}{dR} + \dfrac{dC_f}{dR} = 0
$$

$$
\dfrac{dC_p}{dR} + \dfrac{dC_a}{dR} = -\dfrac{dC_f}{dR}
$$

**Step 4 — Engineering Interpretation.**
The optimal reliability $R^{\ast}$ occurs when the **marginal cost of increasing reliability by prevention/appraisal exactly equals the marginal savings from reduced failures**.

**Step 5 — Express as the Quality Cost Ratio (QCR).**

$$
\text{QCR} = \dfrac{C_p + C_a}{C_f}
$$

A mature reliability program maintains $\text{QCR} \geq 1$ (prevention spend $\geq$ failure cost).

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Reliability Growth (Duane Postulate) Used in RMBO Tracking

The **Duane Model** is widely used to track reliability progress in RMBO programs.

**Step 1 — Define Cumulative MTBF.**
If $T$ = cumulative operating time and $N$ = cumulative number of failures, then:

$$
\overline{m}(T) = \dfrac{T}{N}
$$

**Step 2 — Duane's Postulate.**
Empirically, the cumulative MTBF follows a power law:

$$
\overline{m}(T) = a \cdot T^b
$$

where:
- $a$ = scale parameter (initial capability)
- $b$ = growth slope (improvement rate); target $b \in [0.3, 0.6]$.

**Step 3 — Take Logarithm.**

$$
\log \overline{m}(T) = \log a + b \log T
$$

**Step 4 — Instantaneous MTBF.**
Differentiating the cumulative form with respect to $T$:

$$
m(T) = \dfrac{d}{dT}\left[ \overline{m}(T) \cdot N \right] = \overline{m}(T) \cdot (1 - b)
$$

**Step 5 — Reliability Improvement Ratio.**

$$
\text{Improvement Ratio (per doubling of } T\text{)} = 2^{b}
$$

**Example:** If $b = 0.4$, then each doubling of test time yields a $2^{0.4} \approx 1.32 \times$ improvement in MTBF — a quantitative basis for setting RMBO milestones.

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (For Self-Study & Exam Practice)

**Problem:** A CNC machine has an MTBF of 200 hours and MTTR of 4 hours. The RMBO target is to achieve availability $A = 0.99$ within 1 year.

**Step 1 — Compute Current Availability.**

$$
A_{\text{current}} = \dfrac{200}{200 + 4} = \dfrac{200}{204} \approx 0.9804
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Required MTBF for $A = 0.99$.**
Let MTTR remain 4 hours. Then:

$$
0.99 = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}_{\text{new}}}{\text{MTBF}_{\text{new}} + 4}
$$

$$
0.99 \cdot (\text{MTBF}_{\text{new}} + 4) = \text{MTBF}_{\text{new}}
$$

$$
0.99 \cdot \text{MTBF}_{\text{new}} + 3.96 = \text{MTBF}_{\text{new}}
$$

$$
3.96 = \text{MTBF}_{\text{new}} - 0.99 \cdot \text{MTBF}_{\text{new}}
$$

$$
3.96 = 0.01 \cdot \text{MTBF}_{\text{new}}
$$

$$
\text{MTBF}_{\text{new}} = \dfrac{3.96}{0.01} = 396 \text{ hours}
$$

**Step 3 — Set the RMBO Objective.**
"The MTBF of the CNC machine must be improved from 200 hours to **396 hours** within 12 months — a **98% improvement**."

**Step 4 — Sub-Allocate to Teams.**
- Design: Improve bearings (target MTBF contribution +120 hrs).
- Maintenance: Add condition-based monitoring (target MTTR reduction to 2 hrs → boosts effective MTBF by formula).

> [!TIP]
> This is the **canonical RMBO numerical question** for KTU. Practice varying the target $A$ and recomputing the required MTBF.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The RMBO Closed-Loop Management Cycle

The following block-level flow depicts the six-step RMBO methodology and its continuous feedback nature, directly aligned with the syllabus text and engineering practice.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Senior Mgmt: Define Reliability Vision and Policy] --> B[Establish SMART Reliability Objectives]
    B --> C[Goal Cascading: Decompose to Departments]
    C --> D[Develop Action Plans and Allocate Resources]
    D --> E[Monitor KRIs: MTBF MTTR A WCR]
    E --> F{Target Achieved?}
    F -- Yes --> G[Recognize Reward and Document Best Practices]
    F -- No --> H[Root Cause Analysis and Corrective Action]
    H --> B
    G --> B
```
